topics.title: Fundamental Problems - Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Problems - Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Path Planning in Mobile Robotics?

In the canonical taxonomy of **mobile robotics**, the discipline recognizes four "Fundamental Problems" that any autonomous mobile agent must solve simultaneously and continuously:

1. **Perception** (Sensing the environment via sensors)
2. **Localization** (Estimating the robot's pose $\mathbf{q}$)
3. **Path / Motion Planning** (Computing a collision-free trajectory)
4. **Motion Control** (Executing the trajectory on physical actuators)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Path Planning is the computational problem of determining a sequence of valid configurations $\sigma(s) = \{\mathbf{q}(t) \mid t \in [0, T]\}$ in the robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C}$, such that the robot transitions from an initial configuration $\mathbf{q}_{init}$ to a goal configuration $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ while avoiding the configuration-space obstacle region $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$, and typically minimizing a cost functional $J(\sigma)$.*

Mathematically, the path planning problem is posed as a constrained optimization:

$$
\sigma^{*} = \arg\min_{\sigma \in \Sigma_{free}} J(\sigma)
$$

subject to the boundary and collision constraints:

$$
\sigma(0) = \mathbf{q}_{init}, \quad \sigma(T) = \mathbf{q}_{goal}, \quad \sigma(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \quad \forall t \in [0, T]
$$

where the **free space** is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Vocabulary You MUST Memorize:**
> - **Workspace** ($\mathcal{W}$) = The 2D/3D physical world.
> - **Configuration Space** ($\mathcal{C}$) = The mathematical space of all possible robot poses.
> - **C-obstacle** ($\mathcal{C}_{obs}$) = The region of $\mathcal{C}$ that causes a collision.
> - **Free Space** ($\mathcal{C}_{free}$) = The admissible region where the robot can exist safely.
> - **Path** ($\sigma$) vs. **Trajectory** ($\tau$) : A path is purely geometric; a trajectory is parameterized by time.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Intuition: The "Blindfolded Elephant in a Furniture Store"**
>
> Imagine you are blindfolded (no localization feedback) inside a giant furniture store cluttered with obstacles (sofas, tables, walls). You know where the entrance is (start) and where the exit is (goal), but you cannot see anything. Your job is to walk to the exit without bumping into furniture.
>
> - **Configuration space** = a mental "map of possibilities" where every point tells you *where your body could possibly be*.
> - **Path planning** = drawing a continuous line on that mental map from "entrance" to "exit" that never crosses the shaded "collision zones."
> - **C-obstacles** = those shaded zones where, if your body were there, you would crash into a sofa.
>
> A good path planner is the friend who studies the store layout, then hands you a slip of paper with: *"Walk 5 m north, turn 30° east, walk 3 m — done!"* — a recipe that guarantees safety and speed.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in Path Planning

A path planner is judged against four standard engineering metrics, all critical in KTU valuation:

| # | Metric | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Optimality** | $J(\sigma)$ | Total cost of the returned path (length, energy, time). |
| 2 | **Completeness** | Boolean | Does it *guarantee* a solution if one exists? |
| 3 | **Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$ | How long does the algorithm take to compute $\sigma^{*}$? |
| 4 | **Space Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$ | How much memory does it consume? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The metric **"Completeness"** is non-negotiable in KTU viva and Part B answers. *Resolution-completeness* and *probabilistic completeness* are the two flavours most often asked.

## 1.4 Classification of Path Planning Algorithms

The vast literature of path planning is canonically split into **four families**, and you must know all four cold for KTU 2024 ESE:

1. **Graph / Grid Search Methods** — Dijkstra, A*, D*, JPS
2. **Sampling-Based Methods** — PRM, RRT, RRT*, Informed RRT*
3. **Potential Field Methods** — Classical APF, Navigation Functions
4. **Optimization-Based Methods** — Trajectory Optimization, MPC
5. **Geometric / Cell Decomposition Methods** — Trapezoidal, Boustrophedon

This module's notes are anchored in the **first three** because they constitute the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus core.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Path Planning Problem

Every path-planning problem in 2D is a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{P} = (\mathcal{C}_{free}, \, \mathbf{q}_{init}, \, \mathbf{q}_{goal}, \, \mu)
$$

where $\mu : \mathcal{C}_{free} \times \mathcal{C}_{free} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is the **cost function** (often a metric distance). Solving $\mathcal{P}$ means returning a continuous, collision-free curve $\sigma : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ that connects the two endpoints.

### 2.1.1 The Pipeline (5-Stage Decomposition)

The end-to-end flow on any real autonomous vehicle (AGV, AMR, autonomous car) is:

1. **World Modeling** — Obtain a map (occupancy grid, point cloud, or geometric primitives).
2. **C-Space Transformation** — Shrink the robot to a point and inflate the obstacles (Minkowski sum).
3. **Graph / Roadmap Construction** — Discretize the free space into a searchable graph.
4. **Graph Search** — Run Dijkstra / A* / BFS to extract the optimal sequence.
5. **Trajectory Smoothing** — Fit splines or polynomial primitives for execution.

## 2.2 Configuration Space Construction — The Heart of the Theory

The single most important equation in this module is the **Minkowski-sum definition** of the C-obstacle for a translating rigid body:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{\, \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{W}_{obs} \neq \varnothing \,\}
$$

For a **circular robot** of radius $r$ in a polygonal workspace with obstacle $\mathcal{O}$:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{O} \oplus B_r = \{\, \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{p} \in \mathcal{O}, \, \mathbf{v} \in B_r \,\}
$$

where $B_r$ is the disc of radius $r$ centered at the origin. The Minkowski sum "grows" the obstacle by $r$ in every direction, allowing us to treat the robot as a point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Minkowski sum?** It transforms a hard geometric problem (computing collisions of a 2D shape with a polygon) into a simple point-vs-polygon query. This is the standard trick examined in KTU board problems.

## 2.3 Grid-Based Planning: A* and its Family

Grid planning discretizes $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ into cells of side $\Delta$. A node $n \in \mathcal{G}$ is a cell, and edges connect 4-neighbours (or 8-neighbours for diagonal motion).

### 2.3.1 Cost Function

For a node $n$, the **A\* evaluation function** is:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = the actual cost accumulated from $\mathbf{q}_{init}$ to $n$.
- $h(n)$ = the **heuristic estimate** of cost from $n$ to $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$.

### 2.3.2 Admissible Heuristics (Critical for KTU)

A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost $h^{*}(n)$:

$$
h(n) \le h^{*}(n) \quad \forall n \in \mathcal{G}
$$

If $h$ is admissible, A* is guaranteed to return an optimal path. The three canonical admissible heuristics on a 4-/8-connected grid with movement cost $D$ (cardinal) and $D_2$ (diagonal, typically $D\sqrt{2}$) are:

| Heuristic | Formula | Admissible? |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Manhattan (L¹) | $h(n) = D \cdot (\lvert x_g - x_n \rvert + \lvert y_g - y_n \rvert)$ | Yes, 4-connected |
| Euclidean (L²) | $h(n) = D \cdot \sqrt{(x_g - x_n)^2 + (y_g - y_n)^2}$ | Yes, any-angle |
| Chebyshev (L∞) | $h(n) = D \cdot \max(\lvert x_g - x_n \rvert, \lvert y_g - y_n \rvert)$ | Yes, 8-connected (with $D_2 = D$) |
| Diagonal (Octile) | $h(n) = (D_2 - D)\cdot\min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + D\cdot(\Delta x + \Delta y - 2\min(\Delta x, \Delta y))$ | Yes, 8-connected |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In your answer sheets, **never** write `|x|` inside a markdown table — the renderer will break. Use `\lvert x \rvert` instead.

### 2.3.3 Consistency (Monotonicity) Condition

A heuristic is **consistent** (and therefore A* can skip re-opening closed nodes) iff:

$$
h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n') \quad \forall \text{ successors } n' \text{ of } n
$$

If consistent, $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path, which makes A* equivalent to running Dijkstra with a re-weighted cost.

## 2.4 Sampling-Based Planning: RRT & RRT*

When $\mathcal{C}$ is high-dimensional ($\geq 7$ DOF), grids blow up exponentially. **Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT)** sidestep the curse of dimensionality via random sampling.

The RRT iteration is:

$$
\mathbf{q}_{rand} \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{C})
$$

$$
\mathbf{q}_{near} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{T}} \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{rand} \rVert
$$

$$
\mathbf{q}_{new} = \mathbf{q}_{near} + \eta \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q}_{rand} - \mathbf{q}_{near}}{\lVert \mathbf{q}_{rand} - \mathbf{q}_{near} \rVert}
$$

where $\eta$ is the **step size** and $\mathcal{T}$ is the current tree. $\mathbf{q}_{new}$ is added only if the edge $(\mathbf{q}_{near}, \mathbf{q}_{new})$ lies in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**RRT\*** adds two operations:

1. **Rewire** within a ball of radius $r_{\mathcal{T}}$ :

$$
r_{\mathcal{T}} = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}
$$

2. Choose, for each new node, the parent that minimises $g$.

RRT* enjoys **probabilistic completeness** and **asymptotic optimality**: $\lim_{n \to \infty} J(\sigma_n) = J(\sigma^{*})$.

## 2.5 Artificial Potential Field (APF)

A **potential function** $U : \mathcal{C}_{free} \to \mathbb{R}$ attracts the robot to the goal and repels it from obstacles. The control is the negative gradient:

$$
\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}) = - \nabla U(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}_{rep}(\mathbf{q})
$$

A classical choice (Khatib, 1986):

$$
U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \, \lVert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \rVert^{2}
$$

$$
U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{rep}\left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) \le \rho_0 \\
0, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0
\end{cases}
$$

where $\rho(\mathbf{q})$ is the distance from $\mathbf{q}$ to the nearest obstacle, and $\rho_0$ is the influence radius.

> [!WARNING]
> APF suffers from **local minima** (e.g., equidistant from two walls opposite the goal). This is a frequent KTU Part-A question.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Typical Use / Comment |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Free space definition | $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Sets the admissible region. |
| 2 | C-obstacle (Minkowski) | $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{O} \oplus B_r$ | Shrinks robot to a point. |
| 3 | A\* evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Search priority. |
| 4 | Admissibility | $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ | Guarantees optimality. |
| 5 | Consistency | $h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$ | No re-opening of nodes. |
| 6 | Dijkstra | $h(n) \equiv 0$ | A* with zero heuristic. |
| 7 | Greedy Best-First | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Fast but sub-optimal. |
| 8 | RRT step | $\mathbf{q}_{new} = \mathbf{q}_{near} + \eta \hat{\mathbf{d}}$ | Incremental growth. |
| 9 | RRT* rewire radius | $r_n = \gamma (\log n / n)^{1/d}$ | Asymptotic optimality. |
| 10 | Attractive potential | $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} d^2$ | Quadratic pull to goal. |
| 11 | Repulsive potential | $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep}(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)^2$ | Inflated obstacle repulsion. |
| 12 | Configuration dimension | $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 3$ (2D pose), $6$ (3D free-flyer) | DOF count. |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AGVs in warehouses** (Amazon Kiva / Symbotic) → A* on occupancy grids.
- **Autonomous highway driving** → RRT* with kinodynamic constraints.
- **Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)** → Field D* on stereo elevation maps.
- **Drone swarms** → Multi-agent PRM with conflict-resolution.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** → RRT-Connect in 7-DOF configuration space.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Configuration Space Inflation for a Circular Robot

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A circular mobile robot of radius $r$ moves by pure translation in a 2-D workspace. A single polygonal obstacle is given by the convex polygon $\mathcal{O} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ with vertices $(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_k, y_k)$. Derive an explicit equation for the C-obstacle boundary.

### 3.1.2 Derivation (Exhaustive)

We seek all configurations $\mathbf{q} = (x, y)$ such that the robot disc $B_r(\mathbf{q})$ intersects $\mathcal{O}$.

$$
B_r(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \varnothing
$$

This is equivalent to stating that there exist two points $\mathbf{a} \in B_r(\mathbf{q})$ and $\mathbf{b} \in \mathcal{O}$ with $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{b}$.

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{q} + \mathbf{v}, \quad \text{with } \lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert \le r
$$

The set of all such $\mathbf{a}$ is exactly the disc $B_r(\mathbf{q})$. We can therefore rewrite:

$$
\exists\, \mathbf{v} \in B_r(\mathbf{0}) : \mathbf{q} + \mathbf{v} \in \mathcal{O}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\mathbf{q} \in \{\, \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{p} \in \mathcal{O}, \, \mathbf{v} \in B_r(\mathbf{0}) \,\}
$$

Recognising the definition of the **Minkowski sum** of a set and a disc:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{O} \oplus B_r(\mathbf{0})
$$

For a convex polygon $\mathcal{O}$ and a disc of radius $r$, the Minkowski sum is again a convex polygon, obtained by:

1. Computing the **outward normals** $\hat{\mathbf{n}}_i$ of every edge of $\mathcal{O}$.
2. Translating each edge by $r$ along its outward normal.

Hence, for the $i$-th edge of $\mathcal{O}$ with equation $\hat{\mathbf{n}}_i \cdot \mathbf{p} = c_i$, the corresponding edge of $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{n}}_i \cdot \mathbf{q} = c_i + r
$$

And every vertex $(x_i, y_i)$ of $\mathcal{O}$ spawns a **circular arc** of radius $r$ centred at that vertex. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is the formal justification for the engineering trick: *"inflate obstacles by the robot radius, then plan as a point."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Admissibility of the Manhattan Heuristic on a 4-Connected Grid

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
Let $n = (x_n, y_n)$ and $g = (x_g, y_g)$ be two integer grid cells, and suppose the robot may move in only four cardinal directions with uniform cost $D$ per step. Prove that the heuristic

$$
h_{MH}(n) = D \cdot \bigl( \lvert x_g - x_n \rvert + \lvert y_g - y_n \rvert \bigr)
$$

is admissible.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

By definition, admissibility requires $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for every node $n$. The true optimal cost $h^{*}(n)$ is the length of the shortest 4-connected grid walk from $n$ to $g$.

**Step 1 — Lower bound on horizontal travel:** Every walk from $n$ to $g$ must change the $x$-coordinate by exactly $\lvert x_g - x_n \rvert$ net units. Since each cardinal step changes $x$ by at most 1, the walk contains **at least** $\lvert x_g - x_n \rvert$ horizontal moves.

**Step 2 — Lower bound on vertical travel:** By identical reasoning, the walk contains at least $\lvert y_g - y_n \rvert$ vertical moves.

**Step 3 — Total lower bound:** Since each step costs $D$, the total cost is bounded below by:

$$
h^{*}(n) \ge D \cdot \lvert x_g - x_n \rvert + D \cdot \lvert y_g - y_n \rvert = h_{MH}(n)
$$

**Step 4 — Achievability:** A simple walk that first varies $x$ by $\lvert x_g - x_n \rvert$ steps and then $y$ by $\lvert y_g - y_n \rvert$ steps achieves exactly $h_{MH}(n)$ moves. Hence:

$$
h^{*}(n) = D \cdot \lvert x_g - x_n \rvert + D \cdot \lvert y_g - y_n \rvert \quad \text{(for unobstructed grids)}
$$

and on obstructed grids the true cost is $\ge h_{MH}(n)$. Therefore the Manhattan heuristic is admissible for 4-connected grids. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same proof fails on an 8-connected grid because diagonal shortcuts (cost $D\sqrt{2}$) can outperform the sum of two cardinal moves (cost $2D$) when $D\sqrt{2} < 2D$, which is always true. Hence Manhattan is **NOT admissible** on 8-connected grids — a very common KTU viva trap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimality Guarantee of A* with an Admissible Heuristic

### 3.3.1 Setup
Let $\mathcal{G}$ be a finite graph with positive edge costs $c(u, v) > 0$. Suppose A* is run with an admissible heuristic $h$, and let $\sigma^{*}$ be the optimal path. Let $n_{\text{goal}}$ be the goal node.

### 3.3.2 Derivation (Sketch of Standard Proof)

Assume, for contradiction, that A* terminates at a non-optimal node $n$ with $f(n) > J(\sigma^{*})$. Define $n^{*}$ as a node on the optimal path $\sigma^{*}$ that was *not expanded* when A* terminated.

For any such unexpanded node $m$:

$$
f(m) = g(m) + h(m) \le g(m) + h^{*}(m) = J(\text{path through } m)
$$

In particular, choosing $m = n_{\text{goal}}$ on $\sigma^{*}$:

$$
f(n_{\text{goal}}) \le J(\sigma^{*})
$$

Since A* always expands the open node with the smallest $f$, the unexpanded status of $n_{\text{goal}}$ means the algorithm terminated at a node $n$ with $f(n) \le f(n_{\text{goal}})$, i.e.

$$
f(n) \le J(\sigma^{*})
$$

But the algorithm terminates by popping $n$ and concluding it is the goal. If $f(n) \le J(\sigma^{*})$ and $h$ is admissible, then $f(n) = J(\sigma^{*})$, so the returned path is optimal. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the classic *optimality of A\** proof. KTU expects you to reproduce it in long-answer Part B.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Trade-offs

| Algorithm | Optimal? | Complete? | Time | Space | Best Use-Case |
|-----------|----------|-----------|------|-------|---------------|
| BFS | Optimal (uniform cost) | Yes | $\mathcal{O}(\lvert V \rvert + \lvert E \rvert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\lvert V \rvert)$ | Small unweighted grids. |
| Dijkstra | Optimal | Yes | $\mathcal{O}((\lvert V \rvert + \lvert E \rvert)\log \lvert V \rvert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\lvert V \rvert)$ | Weighted grid. |
| A* | Optimal (admissible $h$) | Yes | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, sub-exponential in practice | $\mathcal{O}(\lvert V \rvert)$ | Heuristic-rich grids. |
| Greedy Best-First | Not optimal | No | $\mathcal{O}(\lvert V \rvert \log \lvert V \rvert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\lvert V \rvert)$ | Speed over optimality. |
| RRT | Not optimal | Probabilistic | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | High-DOF, kinodynamic. |
| RRT* | Asymptotically optimal | Probabilistic | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Smooth high-DOF. |
| PRM | Asymptotically optimal | Probabilistic | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ preprocessing | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Multi-query. |
| APF | Not optimal | No (local minima) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per step | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Real-time reactive. |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — A* on a 4×4 Grid

### 3.5.1 Problem
4×4 grid, start $S = (0,0)$, goal $G = (3,3)$, cardinal cost $D = 1$. Obstacle cell $(2,1)$ is impassable. Use Manhattan heuristic.

### 3.5.2 Solution Trace

At each step A* picks the open node with minimum $f = g + h_{MH}$.

| Step | Open set (f-g-h) | Pop | Neighbour updates | Closed |
|------|------------------|-----|-------------------|--------|
| 1 | $\{(0,0):0-0-6\}$ | $(0,0)$ | $(1,0):1-1-5$; $(0,1):1-1-5$ | $\{(0,0)\}$ |
| 2 | $\{(1,0):1-1-5; (0,1):1-1-5\}$ | tie → $(1,0)$ | $(2,0):2-2-4$; $(1,1):3-2-4$ | $\{(0,0),(1,0)\}$ |
| 3 | $\{(0,1):1-1-5; (2,0):2-2-4; (1,1):3-2-4\}$ | $(0,1)$ | $(1,1)$ updated: $g=2 \Rightarrow f=6$ | add $(0,1)$ |
| 4 | $\{(2,0):2-2-4; (1,1):6-2-4\}$ | $(2,0)$ | $(3,0):3-3-3$; $(2,1)$ blocked | add $(2,0)$ |
| 5 | $\{(3,0):3-3-3; (1,1):6-2-4\}$ | $(3,0)$ | $(3,1):4-4-2$ | add $(3,0)$ |
| 6 | $\{(3,1):4-4-2; (1,1):6-2-4\}$ | $(3,1)$ | $(2,1)$ blocked; $(3,2):5-5-1$ | add $(3,1)$ |
| 7 | $\{(3,2):5-5-1; (1,1):6-2-4\}$ | $(3,2)$ | $(2,2):7-6-2$; $(3,3):6-6-0$ | add $(3,2)$ |
| 8 | $\{(3,3):6-6-0; (1,1):6-2-4\}$ | $(3,3)$ | GOAL | done |

**Optimal cost $J(\sigma^{*}) = 6$**, path: $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3)$.

> [!NOTE]
> The trace shows the textbook *parent-pointer update* that KTU examiners love to mark. Write the **Open**, **Closed**, and **g-value** columns explicitly for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Path Planning Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA([START]) --> inputA[Map Input]
    inputA --> wsModel[Workspace Model W]
    wsModel --> cspaceT[Configuration Space Transform]
    cspaceT --> diskCheck[Collision Test Cfree]
    diskCheck --> decide1{Plan Type}
    decide1 -- Grid --> gridPath[A Star or Dijkstra]
    decide1 -- Sampling --> smpPath[RRT or RRT Star]
    decide1 -- Reactive --> potPath[Artificial Potential Field]
    gridPath --> fcost1[Cost Function J]
    smpPath --> fcost1
    potPath --> fcost1
    fcost1 --> trajSmooth[Trajectory Smoothing]
    trajSmooth --> ctrlOut[Motion Controller Output]
    ctrlOut --> endA([END])
```

## 4.2 Configuration Space Concept (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    wsBlock[Workspace W with obstacles O] -- Minkowski Sum with disc Br --> cobsBlock[C-Obstacle region Cobs]
    cobsBlock -- remove from C --> cfreeBlock[Free Configuration Space Cfree]
    cfreeBlock -- contains start qinit and goal qgoal --> searchBlock[Path sigma in Cfree]
    searchBlock -- connects qinit to qgoal --> outBlock[Returned Path sigma star]
    wsBlock:::world
    cobsBlock:::obs
    cfreeBlock:::free
    searchBlock:::search
    outBlock:::final
    classDef world fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef obs fill:#ffadad,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    classDef search fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#ca6702,color:#000
```

## 4.3 A* Search State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Initialize: insert qinit with f = h qinit
    Initialize --> Loop: open set nonempty
    Loop --> ExpandNode: pop node with min f
    ExpandNode --> GoalReached: node equals qgoal
    ExpandNode --> GenerateNeighbours: for each successor nprime
    GenerateNeighbours --> SkipObstacle: nprime in Cobs
    SkipObstacle --> GenerateNeighbours
    GenerateNeighbours --> TentativeG: g via current node
    TentativeG --> UpdateOpen: new g less than existing
    TentativeG --> GenerateNeighbours
    UpdateOpen --> Loop
    GoalReached --> [*]: reconstruct path sigma star
    Loop --> NoSolution: open set empty
    NoSolution --> [*]: failure
```

## 4.4 Path Planning Algorithm Taxonomy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootBlock[Path Planning Algorithms] --> grpA[Graph Search]
    rootBlock --> grpB[Sampling Based]
    rootBlock --> grpC[Reactive]
    rootBlock --> grpD[Optimization Based]
    grpA --> a1[Dijkstra]
    grpA --> a2[A Star]
    grpA --> a3[D Star Lite]
    grpA --> a4[Jump Point Search]
    grpB --> b1[Probabilistic Roadmap]
    grpB --> b2[Rapidly Exploring Random Tree]
    grpB --> b3[RRT Star]
    grpB --> b4[Informed RRT Star]
    grpC --> c1[Artificial Potential Field]
    grpC --> c2[Vector Field Histogram]
    grpC --> c3[Dynamic Window Approach]
    grpD --> d1[Trajectory Optimization]
    grpD --> d2[Model Predictive Control]
    grpD --> d3[CHOMP and STOMP]

    grpA:::cat

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are framed in the exact KTU 2024 ESE template, with a simulated Past-Year Question tag, Bloom's level, and a 100% worked model solution.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBRT522]**
**Differentiate between configuration space and workspace. Why is the configuration space concept central to path planning?** *(Bloom: Understand, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ | Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Dimension | Equals physical world (2D or 3D) | Equals robot's DOF (3 for planar, 6 for 3D free-flyer) |
| Robot size | A geometric body with finite shape | A point (after Minkowski inflation) |
| Obstacles | Physical objects $\mathcal{W}_{obs}$ | Inflated C-obstacles $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ |
| Query type | Body-vs-body intersection | Point-vs-region test |

**Why central:** Path planning reduces a complex geometric collision-avoidance problem (with shapes) into a simple point-navigation problem in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. This abstraction enables the application of generic graph-search and sampling algorithms without re-engineering for every robot geometry. The Minkowski sum gives a clean, mathematically closed-form construction, and the optimality proofs of A*, RRT* etc. all live in $\mathcal{C}$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBRT522]**
**Define the admissibility of a heuristic. Show that the Euclidean distance is an admissible heuristic for any-angle 2D path planning.** *(Bloom: Apply, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true minimum cost $h^{*}(n)$ to the goal:

$$
h(n) \le h^{*}(n), \quad \forall n \in \mathcal{G}
$$

**Proof that Euclidean is admissible:**
The straight line between $n = (x_n, y_n)$ and $g = (x_g, y_g)$ has length:

$$
d_E = \sqrt{(x_g - x_n)^2 + (y_g - y_n)^2}
$$

Any collision-free path from $n$ to $g$ is at least as long as the straight line, because the Euclidean distance is the **infimum** of all rectifiable curves joining the two points (triangle inequality / property of metric spaces). Hence $h_E(n) = d_E \le h^{*}(n)$, satisfying admissibility. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEBRT522]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the configuration space approach to path planning. Show that for a circular robot of radius $r$, the C-obstacle is the Minkowski sum of the physical obstacle and a disc of radius $r$. *(7 marks, Bloom: Apply)*

**(b)** Apply A* algorithm on the following 5×5 grid to find the shortest path from $S = (0,0)$ to $G = (4,4)$. Use the Manhattan distance heuristic. Black cells are obstacles: $(1,2), (2,2), (3,1), (3,3)$. Diagonal moves are NOT allowed; cardinal cost = 1. Show the open and closed lists at every step. *(7 marks, Bloom: Apply)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible robot configurations. For a circular planar robot translating in 2D, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$, and a configuration is a point $\mathbf{q} = (x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Formal definition of C-obstacle:**

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{\, \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{W}_{obs} \neq \varnothing \,\}
$$

where $\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q})$ is the robot footprint placed at $\mathbf{q}$.

**Step 2 — Substituting the circular geometry:** For a disc of radius $r$,

$$
\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) = B_r(\mathbf{q}) = \{\, \mathbf{p} \mid \lVert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{q} \rVert \le r \,\}
$$

The collision condition becomes:

$$
\exists\, \mathbf{p} \in \mathcal{W}_{obs} : \lVert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{q} \rVert \le r
$$

Equivalently, $\mathbf{q}$ lies within distance $r$ of some obstacle point, i.e.

$$
\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{W}_{obs} \oplus B_r(\mathbf{0})
$$

which is the Minkowski sum.

**Step 3 — Geometric construction:** For a convex polygon obstacle, the Minkowski sum with a disc produces a polygon whose every edge is shifted outward by $r$, and whose vertices are rounded with arcs of radius $r$.

**Step 4 — Pipeline diagram (as in Section 4.2 above)**: workspace → Minkowski inflation → $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ → point path search.

*(Examiner note: full-marks answers explicitly state the Minkowski-sum definition and provide a labelled figure.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): A\* Trace on 5×5 Grid**

Heuristic: $h(n) = \lvert x_g - x_n \rvert + \lvert y_g - y_n \rvert$. Cardinal cost $D = 1$. Obstacles: $(1,2), (2,2), (3,1), (3,3)$.

| Step | Pop | Open set (f, g, h) | Closed set |
|------|-----|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | — | $\{(0,0):8,0,8\}$ | $\varnothing$ |
| 2 | $(0,0)$ | $\{(1,0):7,1,6; (0,1):7,1,6\}$ | $\{(0,0)\}$ |
| 3 | $(1,0)$ | $\{(0,1):7,1,6; (2,0):6,2,4; (1,1):8,2,6\}$ | $\{(0,0),(1,0)\}$ |
| 4 | $(0,1)$ | $\{(2,0):6,2,4; (1,1):7,2,5; (0,2):8,2,6\}$ | $+(0,1)$ |
| 5 | $(2,0)$ | $\{(3,0):6,3,3; (1,1):7,2,5; (0,2):8,2,6; (2,1):8,3,5\}$ | $+(2,0)$ |
| 6 | $(3,0)$ | $\{(3,0):6,3,3\;$ (already), then $\{(4,0):7,4,3; (1,1):7,2,5; (0,2):8,2,6; (2,1):8,3,5\}$; $(3,1)$ blocked | $+(3,0)$ |
| 7 | $(1,1)$ | $\{(4,0):7,4,3; (1,2)\;$ blocked; $(2,1):8,3,5; (0,2):8,2,6\}$ | $+(1,1)$ |
| 8 | $(4,0)$ | $\{(4,1):8,5,3; (2,1):8,3,5; (0,2):8,2,6\}$ | $+(4,0)$ |
| 9 | $(4,1)$ | $\{(4,2):9,6,3; (2,1):8,3,5; (0,2):8,2,6\}$; $(3,1)$ blocked | $+(4,1)$ |
| 10 | $(2,1)$ | $\{(0,2):8,2,6; (4,2):9,6,3\}$; $(3,1)$ blocked; $(2,2)$ blocked | $+(2,1)$ |
| 11 | $(0,2)$ | $\{(0,3):9,3,6; (1,2)\;$ blocked; $(4,2):9,6,3\}$ | $+(0,2)$ |
| 12 | $(0,3)$ | $\{(0,4):10,4,6; (1,3):9,4,5; (4,2):9,6,3\}$; $(1,2)$ blocked | $+(0,3)$ |
| 13 | $(1,3)$ | $\{(2,3):9,5,4; (4,2):9,6,3; (0,4):10,4,6\}$; $(2,2)$ blocked | $+(1,3)$ |
| 14 | $(2,3)$ | $\{(3,2):9,6,3; (4,2):9,6,3; (0,4):10,4,6\}$; $(3,3)$ blocked; $(2,2)$ blocked | $+(2,3)$ |
| 15 | $(3,2)$ | $\{(4,2):9,6,3; (0,4):10,4,6\}$; $(3,3)$ blocked | $+(3,2)$ |
| 16 | $(4,2)$ | $\{(4,3):9,7,2; (0,4):10,4,6\}$ | $+(4,2)$ |
| 17 | $(4,3)$ | $\{(4,4):8,8,0; (0,4):10,4,6\}$; $(3,3)$ blocked | $+(4,3)$ |
| 18 | $(4,4)$ | GOAL — terminate | $+(4,4)$ |

**Optimal path:** $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (4,0) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to (4,4)$.
**Optimal cost:** $J(\sigma^{*}) = 8$ steps.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEBRT522 Alternative]**

**(a)** What is the Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) algorithm? Derive the asymptotic complexity of its coverage behaviour and explain the term *probabilistic completeness*. *(7 marks, Bloom: Understand / Apply)*

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot must navigate from $\mathbf{q}_{init} = (0,0,0)$ to $\mathbf{q}_{goal} = (5,5,\pi/2)$ in a 2D plane with $\theta$ as the heading angle. The control inputs are linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$ bounded by $v \in [0, 1]$ m/s and $\omega \in [-1, 1]$ rad/s. Design an Artificial Potential Field and write the equations of motion. Discuss the *local minima problem* and two methods to escape it. *(7 marks, Bloom: Apply / Analyse)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition:** RRT is an incremental sampling-based path planner. It maintains a tree $\mathcal{T} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ rooted at $\mathbf{q}_{init}$. At each iteration, it samples a random configuration $\mathbf{q}_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}$, finds the nearest tree node $\mathbf{q}_{near}$, extends towards $\mathbf{q}_{rand}$ by a step $\eta$, and adds the new node $\mathbf{q}_{new}$ to $\mathcal{T}$ if the edge is collision-free.

**Coverage analysis:** The probability that a node in a Voronoi cell of volume $V_i$ is sampled in $n$ iterations is $1 - (1 - V_i / \mu(\mathcal{C}))^n$. The expected number of nodes in that cell is therefore $n V_i / \mu(\mathcal{C})$, showing that the tree grows **proportionally to the volume of unexplored regions**. This yields the property that RRT rapidly biases its expansion towards the largest unexplored region — hence the name.

**Probabilistic completeness:** RRT is *probabilistically complete*, meaning:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \mathbb{P}[\text{RRT returns a path} \mid \text{a path exists}] = 1
$$

This is weaker than exact completeness but holds for all practical RRT variants. The proof uses the Borel-Cantelli lemma and the fact that the $\eta$-ball coverage is dense in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ as $n \to \infty$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Equations of motion (differential drive):**

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos \theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin \theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

**Attractive potential (point goal in $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$):**

$$
U_{att}(x,y) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \bigl( (x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2 \bigr)
$$

We project the gradient onto the admissible motion directions $(v, \omega)$:

$$
v = -k_v \frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial d} = k_v \, k_{att}\, d, \quad \omega = -k_\omega (\theta - \theta_{goal}) + \text{obstacle terms}
$$

**Repulsive potential** with $\rho_0$ as the influence radius and $\rho(\mathbf{q})$ as the distance to the nearest obstacle:

$$
U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{rep}\, \bigl( \tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \bigr)^2, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\
0, & \rho > \rho_0
\end{cases}
$$

**Local minima problem:** A local minimum occurs at any $\mathbf{q}^{*}$ where $\nabla U_{att} + \nabla U_{rep} = \mathbf{0}$ but $\mathbf{q}^{*} \neq \mathbf{q}_{goal}$. Classic example: robot equidistant from two parallel walls directly opposite the goal.

**Two escape methods:**

1. **Random walk / noise injection:** When $\lVert \mathbf{F} \rVert < \epsilon$ for $T$ consecutive steps, inject a random perturbation $(v_{rand}, \omega_{rand})$ to dislodge the robot.
2. **Navigation function (Koditschek-Rimon):** Replace $U$ with a constructed *navigation function* $U : \mathcal{C}_{free} \to [0,1]$ that has the goal as its unique minimum, guaranteeing no local traps in a sphere-world topology.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the off-by-one in admissibility:** Many students write $h(n) \ge h^{*}(n)$ — this is the *opposite* of admissibility and guarantees a sub-optimal A*. Examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing completeness flavours:** *Resolution-complete* (cell-decomposition) and *probabilistically complete* (RRT) are NOT the same. State explicitly which one your algorithm enjoys.
> 3. **Minkowski sum misuse:** For a *non-circular* robot, the C-obstacle is **not** a simple offset polygon. The full Minkowski sum with the robot's shape is required. Examiners test this with non-circular bodies (L-shape, rectangle).
> 4. **Diagonal-move heuristic mismatch:** Using the Manhattan heuristic on an 8-connected grid *violates admissibility* and silently makes A* sub-optimal. Always pair the heuristic with the connectivity correctly.
> 5. **RRT step-size abuse:** Choosing $\eta$ too large causes frequent rejection; too small causes the tree to grow pathologically slowly. State that $\eta$ should scale with the workspace diameter divided by the desired path resolution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Print & Pin to Wall**
>
> - **Configuration space** $\mathcal{C}$ = space of robot configurations; $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ = Minkowski sum of obstacles with robot shape; $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.
> - **C-obstacle for disc robot** = original obstacle inflated by radius $r$ (Minkowski sum with disc $B_r$).
> - **A\* evaluation:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — search by lowest $f$.
> - **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$. *Consistent:* $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$.
> - **Manhattan admissible only on 4-connected grids**; **Euclidean admissible for any-angle**; **Octile admissible on 8-connected**.
> - **Dijkstra** = A\* with $h \equiv 0$; **Greedy Best-First** = A\* with $g \equiv 0$.
> - **RRT** = sample, nearest, steer, collision-check, add. **RRT\*** = + rewire within $r_n = \gamma (\log n/n)^{1/d}$.
> - **RRT\*** is *probabilistically complete* and *asymptotically optimal*.
> - **APF** = $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$; control = $-\nabla U$. Suffers from **local minima**.
> - **Completeness** flavours: *exact* (rare), *resolution* (cell-decomp), *probabilistic* (RRT).
> - **Optimality** requires admissible (or consistent) heuristic for A\*; RRT* achieves asymptotic optimality.
> - **Workspace** is the physical world; **configuration space** is the planning space.
> - **Path** is geometric, **trajectory** is time-parameterized.
> - **5×5 grid A\*** trace: open list, closed list, $g$-updates, parent pointers — practice 2–3 times before the exam.
> - **Minkowski sum** for non-circular robots = true geometric sum, not a simple offset polygon.
> - **Differential-drive** state is $(x, y, \theta)$; control is $(v, \omega)$; non-holonomic constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ must be respected.
> - **Reactive methods** (APF, VFH, DWA) are real-time but not globally optimal.
> - **Optimization-based** methods (CHOMP, STOMP, MPC) handle dynamics and smoothness.
> - **Standard practical pairs:** AGV → A\* on grid; manipulator arm → RRT\*; Mars rover → Field D\*; drone swarm → multi-agent PRM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** in mobile robotics is the process by which a robot determines its **pose** (position and orientation) within a known or partially known reference frame (map of the environment) using on-board sensor measurements, control inputs, and prior knowledge of the environment. Formally, it is the estimation of the belief state $bel(x_t)$ of the robot's pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ at time $t$, given the history of observations $z_{1:t}$ and control actions $u_{1:t}$.

Mathematically, the localization problem is to compute:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

where $m$ is the environment map, $z_{1:t}$ are sensor measurements up to time $t$, and $u_{1:t}$ are the executed control commands. This is a recursive **Bayesian estimation** problem.

The two primary sub-problems of localization are:

1. **Local Localization (Position Tracking):** The robot's initial pose is known, and small uncertainties are tracked over time. Solved via **Kalman Filter** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.
2. **Global Localization:** The robot's initial pose is unknown. Solved via **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** or **Markov Localization** using particle filters / grid-based methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology to Remember:**
> - **Pose:** $(x, y, \theta)$ — 2D position + heading angle.
> - **Odometry:** Dead-reckoning estimate from wheel encoders.
> - **Belief:** Probability distribution over possible robot poses.
> - **Landmark:** A distinguishable feature in the environment (e.g., door, corner).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine waking up in a **strange hotel room at night** with the lights off. You have a rough idea of the room's layout (the **map**), but you don't know which room you are in (your **pose** is unknown).

- You **feel the wall** with your hand (this is like a **sensor measurement** $z_t$ — noisy, partial).
- You **walk two steps forward** (this is a **control action** $u_t$ — known but somewhat inaccurate).
- Based on the wall's texture and door direction, you **update your guess** of where you are.

This continuous process of **"guess → move → sense → update guess"** is **localization**.

> [!TIP]
> A perfect analogy used by Sebastian Thrun (Stanford): Localization is like **playing "Where's Waldo?"** while blindfolded, where Waldo can also move and the background keeps changing.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $T_s$ (Sampling Period) | **0.02 s to 0.1 s** | Time between sensor updates |
| $\sigma_{odom}$ (Odometry Noise) | **~1–5 % of distance traveled** | Wheel slippage error |
| $\sigma_{sensor}$ (Range Finder) | **±0.01 m to ±0.05 m** | Typical LiDAR accuracy |
| $N_{particles}$ (MCL) | **100 to 10,000** | Number of pose hypotheses |

---

## 1.3 The Three Localization Paradigms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks to **classify** localization techniques. Memorize the three paradigms below:

1. **Continuous (Kalman-based):** Unimodal Gaussian belief.
2. **Grid-based (Histogram/Markov):** Discrete grid decomposition of state space.
3. **Monte Carlo (Particle Filter):** Sample-based representation of belief.

These are visualized in the diagram in **Section 4**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Foundation of Localization

The **belief distribution** $bel(x_t)$ is updated recursively through two fundamental Bayesian steps — the **Prediction Step** and the **Measurement Update Step**. Every KTU question on localization ultimately tests your understanding of these two steps.

### 2.1.1 Prediction Step (Motion Update)

The prediction step computes the prior belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ (belief *before* incorporating the new sensor measurement) using the **total probability theorem** and the **Markov assumption** (the future depends only on the present, not the past):

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

- $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ is the **motion model** — how the pose changes given a control action.
- $bel(x_{t-1})$ is the previous posterior belief.
- The integration is over all possible previous poses.

### 2.1.2 Measurement Update Step (Correction)

After the new sensor reading $z_t$ arrives, the belief is updated using **Bayes' rule**:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

where:
- $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **measurement model** (sensor likelihood) — the probability of observing $z_t$ if the robot were at $x_t$ in map $m$.
- $\eta$ is the **normalizer** ensuring the belief sums to 1:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

---

## 2.2 The Three Algorithmic Families in Depth

### 2.2.1 Kalman Filter Localization (Gaussian)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The belief is a **unimodal Gaussian** $bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.

**Update Equations:**

- **Predict (Mean):**
$$\mu_t^- = \mu_{t-1} + \Delta_t$$
- **Predict (Covariance):**
$$\Sigma_t^- = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$$
- **Innovation (Measurement Residual):**
$$\nu_t = z_t - h(\mu_t^-)$$
- **Kalman Gain:**
$$K_t = \Sigma_t^- H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$
- **Update (Mean):**
$$\mu_t = \mu_t^- + K_t \nu_t$$
- **Update (Covariance):**
$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t^-$$

Where:
- $A_t$ = Jacobian of motion model w.r.t. state.
- $H_t$ = Jacobian of measurement model w.r.t. state.
- $R_t$ = Process (motion) noise covariance.
- $Q_t$ = Measurement noise covariance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **nonlinear** systems (which is the case in 2D mobile robotics), the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes the motion and measurement models around the current estimate using **first-order Taylor series expansion**.

### 2.2.2 Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The belief is represented by a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles) $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^N$.

**Algorithm (Per Time Step):**

1. **Prediction:** Sample new pose for each particle:
$$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$
2. **Update (Weighting):** Compute importance weight:
$$w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$
3. **Normalization:**
$$w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^N w_t^{[j]}}$$
4. **Resampling:** Draw $N$ particles with replacement proportional to their weights (e.g., using **low-variance resampling**).
5. **Pose Estimate:** Weighted mean or MAP:
$$\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^N w_t^{[i]} \cdot x_t^{[i]}$$

### 2.2.3 Markov (Grid-Based) Localization

The state space is decomposed into a **discrete grid**. Each cell stores the probability that the robot occupies that pose. Update uses the discrete version of Bayes' rule:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Computational Complexity:** Markov localization is $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot n_x)$ where $n$ is the number of grid cells and $n_x$ is the number of state dimensions. In 2D, this scales **exponentially** with resolution.

---

## 2.3 Challenges in Localization (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Challenge | Description | Mitigation Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Robot is teleported to an arbitrary new pose; belief is wrong and unimodal. | MCL with random particle injection |
| **Sensor Aliasing / Perceptual Ambiguity** | Different places look identical (e.g., symmetric corridors). | Multi-hypothesis tracking, more features |
| **Dynamic Environments** | People, chairs moving — map is non-stationary. | Robust sensor models, outlier rejection |
| **Localization Failure** | Belief becomes stuck on wrong hypothesis. | Recovery behaviors, random particle injection |
| **Computational Cost** | High-dimensional continuous state spaces. | Particle filter, hierarchical methods |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the KTU ESE — these are the high-yield equations.**

| Symbol | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $bel(x_t)$ | $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$ | Posterior belief over pose |
| $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction step |
| $bel(x_t)$ | $\eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Update step |
| $\eta$ | $1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$ | Normalizer |
| $K_t$ | $\Sigma_t^- H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| $\mu_t$ | $\mu_t^- + K_t (z_t - h(\mu_t^-))$ | EKF mean update |
| $\Sigma_t$ | $(I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t^-$ | EKF covariance update |
| $w_t^{[i]}$ | $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | Particle weight (MCL) |
| $\hat{x}_t$ | $\sum_{i=1}^N w_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]}$ | MCL pose estimate |
| $N_{eff}$ | $1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ | Effective sample size |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Tesla, Waymo use EKF + particle filter hybrids with LiDAR + GPS + IMU fusion.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva):** Monte Carlo localization on pre-built grid maps.
- **Indoor Service Robots (iRobot Roomba):** Particle filter on 2D occupancy grid maps.
- **Drones (UAVs):** EKF-based fusion of GPS, optical flow, and IMU.
- **Underwater ROVs (AUVs):** Particle filter SLAM for GPS-denied environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bayes' Filter Recursion (Foundation of All Localization)

We start with the goal: compute $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$.

**Step 1:** Apply the definition of conditional probability:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Markov assumption** — the current measurement depends only on the current pose and the map (not on past measurements or controls):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Total Probability Theorem** to the second term by conditioning on $x_{t-1}$:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Markov again — current state depends only on previous state and current control:

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, m)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back. Let $bel(x_{t-1}) = p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m)$. Combining:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 6:** Apply Bayes' rule for the measurement update:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

**Result:** This is the foundational Bayes filter recursion. All three algorithms (Kalman, MCL, Grid) are special cases of this recursion under different representations of $bel(x)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: EKF Localization — Linearization of Motion Model

Consider a **unicycle robot** with state $x = (x, y, \theta)^T$ and control $u = (v, \omega)^T$ (linear and angular velocity). The nonlinear motion model is:

$$g(x, u) = \begin{pmatrix} x + v \Delta t \cos\theta \\ y + v \Delta t \sin\theta \\ \theta + \omega \Delta t \end{pmatrix}$$

where $\Delta t$ is the sampling interval.

**Step 1:** Compute the **Jacobian** of $g$ with respect to $x$:

$$G_x = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \Delta t \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & v \Delta t \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **Jacobian** of $g$ with respect to $u$:

$$G_u = \frac{\partial g}{\partial u} = \begin{pmatrix} \Delta t \cos\theta & 0 \\ \Delta t \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Linearized motion update (EKF prediction):

$$\mu_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$
$$\Sigma_t = G_x \Sigma_{t-1} G_x^T + G_u R_t G_u^T$$

Where $R_t$ is the control noise covariance (variance of $v$ and $\omega$ noise).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kalman Gain Derivation

We want to find the optimal $K_t$ that minimizes the **posterior covariance trace** $\text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$.

**Step 1:** Write the updated covariance:

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t^-$$

**Step 2:** Expand:

$$\Sigma_t = \Sigma_t^- - K_t H_t \Sigma_t^- - \Sigma_t^- H_t^T K_t^T + K_t H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T K_t^T$$

**Step 3:** Take the derivative of the scalar cost $J = \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)$ with respect to $K_t$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial K_t} = -2(H_t \Sigma_t^-)^T + 2 K_t H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $K_t$:

$$K_t H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T = (H_t \Sigma_t^-)^T$$
$$K_t = \Sigma_t^- H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T)^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** With measurement noise $Q_t$:

$$K_t = \Sigma_t^- H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When $Q_t \to \infty$ (sensor very noisy), $K_t \to 0$ and the filter trusts prediction more. When $\Sigma_t^- \to 0$ (prediction very confident), $K_t \to 0$ and the filter ignores the measurement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Localization Algorithms

| Property | Kalman / EKF | Grid (Markov) | Monte Carlo (Particle) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Belief Representation** | Unimodal Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Discrete grid cells | $N$ weighted particles |
| **Handles Multi-Modal** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Computational Cost** | $\mathcal{O}(k^{2.4})$ — fast | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ |
| **Memory** | Low (2 numbers) | High (full grid) | Medium (N samples) |
| **Accuracy** | High (unimodal) | Depends on resolution | Tunable via N |
| **Global Localization** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Position Tracking** | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good | ✅ Good |
| **Kidnapped Recovery** | ❌ Fails | ✅ Recovers | ✅ Recovers |
| **Continuous Space** | ✅ Natural | ❌ Discretized | ✅ Natural |
| **Implementation Ease** | Medium | Easy | Medium |

Where $k$ = state dimension, $n$ = number of grid cells, $N$ = number of particles.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Localization Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel x0] --> B[Prediction Step]
    B --> C[Prior Belief bel bar xt]
    C --> D[Acquire Sensor Measurement zt]
    D --> E[Compute Sensor Likelihood p zt given xt m]
    E --> F[Measurement Update via Bayes Rule]
    F --> G[Posterior Belief bel xt]
    G --> H{Estimate Pose hat xt}
    H --> I[Execute Control ut]
    I --> J[Propagate State via Motion Model]
    J --> B
    G --> K{Multimodal Check}
    K -->|Yes| L[Inject Random Particles]
    K -->|No| M[Resample Weighted Particles]
    L --> G
    M --> G
```

## 4.2 Particle Filter State Evolution Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Particle Set X t-1 with Weights] --> A1[Prediction: Sample from Motion Model]
    A1 --> A2[Unweighted Particle Set X t-]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Weights from Sensor Model]
    A3 --> A4[Weighted Particle Set X t]
    A4 --> A5[Resampling Step]
    A5 --> P1
```

## 4.3 The Three Paradigms — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Localization Paradigms] --> A1[Kalman Filter]
    Root --> A2[Grid Markov Localization]
    Root --> A3[Monte Carlo Localization]
    A1 --> B1[Linear Gaussian Models]
    A1 --> B2[EKF for Nonlinear]
    A2 --> C1[Discrete Cell Decomposition]
    A2 --> C2[Histogram Filter]
    A3 --> D1[Particle Filter with Sampling]
    A3 --> D2[Handles Multi-Modal Beliefs]
```

## 4.4 Sensor Fusion Architecture for Localization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IMU[IMU Sensor] --> F1[Kalman Filter Predictor]
    WHEEL[Wheel Encoders] --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Predicted Pose]
    LIDAR[LiDAR] --> F3[Measurement Model]
    CAM[Camera] --> F3
    F3 --> F4[Innovation]
    F2 --> F4
    F4 --> F5[Kalman Gain Multiplication]
    F5 --> F6[Updated Pose Estimate]
    GPS[GPS Receiver] --> F7[Outlier Rejection]
    F7 --> F6
    F6 --> OUT[Final Pose hat x y theta]
```

## 4.5 Belief Evolution Under Different Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initial Unknown Pose] --> G1[Uniform Distribution]
    G1 --> P1[First Sensor Reading]
    P1 --> G2[Multimodal Peaks]
    G2 --> M1[MCL: Particles Concentrate]
    M1 --> M2[MCL: Converges on True Pose]
    G2 --> K1[EKF: Single Gaussian Fit]
    K1 --> K2[EKF: Fails on Multimodal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the localization problem in mobile robotics. Differentiate between local and global localization.**

**Model Answer:**

Localization is the process of estimating a robot's pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ within a known map $m$, given sensor observations $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$. Formally, it computes the belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$.

**Local Localization (Position Tracking):**
- The initial pose is known.
- The robot tracks small deviations from a known starting position.
- Belief remains unimodal and is well-approximated by a Gaussian.
- Solved using **Kalman Filter / EKF**.
- Computationally cheap.

**Global Localization:**
- The initial pose is completely unknown.
- The belief must be initialized as uniform over the entire map.
- The robot must recover its position from scratch, often dealing with multimodal distributions.
- Solved using **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** or **Markov (grid-based) localization**.
- Computationally expensive but robust.

**Key Distinction:** Local localization assumes a known starting position; global localization does not.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the "kidnapped robot problem" and how Monte Carlo Localization addresses it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **kidnapped robot problem** occurs when a properly localized robot is suddenly teleported to a new arbitrary location without being told. The robot's belief is wrong (and overconfidently wrong), so the algorithm must detect this and recover.

**How MCL Addresses It:**
MCL represents the belief as a set of $N$ weighted particles spread across the map.
1. **Detection:** The sensor likelihood of all particles suddenly drops, because the robot's new location is inconsistent with the cluster of particles.
2. **Recovery via Random Particle Injection:** A small fraction (e.g., **5%**) of the particles are randomly re-instantiated uniformly across the map. These random particles may receive high weights when they happen to land near the new true pose.
3. **Convergence:** Over time, the resampling process propagates the correct high-weight particles, and the filter recovers.

> [!NOTE]
> A standard Kalman filter **cannot** recover from kidnapping because its unimodal Gaussian belief cannot represent the sudden shift to a new location.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer EITHER Q(A) OR Q(B) of the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **Bayes Filter recursion** for localization. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat derivations, explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization** algorithm. What are its limitations? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Goal:**
We want to compute $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$.

**Step 2 — Bayes' Rule:**
Apply the definition of conditional probability:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 3 — Markov Assumption 1 (Sensor):**
The current measurement depends only on the current state and the map:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step 4 — Total Probability:**
Expand the second term by conditioning on $x_{t-1}$:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 — Markov Assumption 2 (Motion):**
The current state depends only on the previous state and current control:

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, m)$$

**Step 6 — Combine:**

$$\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \underbrace{\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}_{\overline{bel}(x_t)}}$$

with normalizer:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)} = \frac{1}{\int p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) \, dx_t}$$

**Assumptions used:**
1. **Markov Assumption:** Future is independent of past given the present.
2. **Static World:** The map $m$ is time-invariant.
3. **Known Controls and Observations:** Sensor and control models are known.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**EKF Localization Derivation:**

**Step 1 — State and Models:**
The robot's state is $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$. The motion model $g(x, u)$ and measurement model $h(x, m)$ are **nonlinear**.

**Step 2 — Linearization (Taylor Series Expansion):**
Expand $g(x, u)$ around the current estimate $\mu_{t-1}$:

$$g(x, u) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_x (x - \mu_{t-1}) + \text{higher order terms}$$

where $G_x = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\big|_{x=\mu_{t-1}}$ is the **Jacobian**.

**Step 3 — Prediction Step:**

$$\mu_t^- = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$
$$\Sigma_t^- = G_x \Sigma_{t-1} G_x^T + G_u R_t G_u^T$$

**Step 4 — Measurement Update:**

Innovation: $\nu_t = z_t - h(\mu_t^-, m)$

Kalman Gain: $K_t = \Sigma_t^- H^T (H \Sigma_t^- H^T + Q_t)^{-1}$

Posterior mean: $\mu_t = \mu_t^- + K_t \nu_t$

Posterior covariance: $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H) \Sigma_t^-$

**Step 5 — Pose Output:** $\hat{x}_t = \mu_t$.

**Limitations of EKF Localization:**

1. **Unimodal Assumption:** Cannot represent multi-modal beliefs (e.g., symmetric corridors). Fails at global localization.
2. **Linearization Errors:** The first-order Taylor expansion introduces errors when the system is highly nonlinear.
3. **No Kidnapping Recovery:** A unimodal Gaussian cannot represent a sudden jump in pose.
4. **Computational Cost:** Requires $O(k^{2.4})$ per step due to matrix inversion.
5. **Gaussian Noise Assumption:** Real sensors often have non-Gaussian noise (heavy tails).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian $G_x$:** Students often write $\Sigma_t^- = \Sigma_{t-1} + R_t$, which is **only valid for linear systems**. For EKF, you MUST compute the Jacobian.
> 2. **Confusing $Q_t$ and $R_t$:** $R_t$ is **process (motion)** noise; $Q_t$ is **measurement (sensor)** noise. Many students swap them.
> 3. **Omitting the normalizer $\eta$:** Even in the EKF, the innovation should be properly scaled. A derivation without $\eta$ loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Always explicitly list Markov assumption, static map, and Gaussian noise.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** algorithm with its mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of resampling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has odometry noise standard deviation $\sigma_v = 0.05$ m/s for linear velocity and $\sigma_\omega = 0.02$ rad/s for angular velocity. Compute the covariance of the motion update over a time step $\Delta t = 0.1$ s when the robot moves with $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 0.3$ rad/s at heading $\theta = \pi/4$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) Algorithm:**

MCL represents the posterior belief $bel(x_t)$ as a set of $N$ weighted samples:

$$\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^N$$

Each particle $x_t^{[i]}$ is a hypothesis for the robot's pose, and $w_t^{[i]}$ is its importance weight (sum of weights = 1).

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
For global localization, particles are drawn uniformly from the map. For position tracking, they are sampled from a Gaussian around the initial estimate.

**Step 2 — Prediction (Sampling):**
For each particle, sample a new pose from the motion model with injected noise:

$$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$

This propagates the particles according to the control input $u_t$.

**Step 3 — Update (Importance Weighting):**
Set each particle's weight proportional to the sensor likelihood:

$$w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$

This measures how well each particle's pose explains the sensor measurement $z_t$.

**Step 4 — Normalization:**

$$w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^N w_t^{[j]}}$$

**Step 5 — Resampling:**
Draw $N$ new particles with replacement, where the probability of selecting particle $i$ is proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$. Discard low-weight particles; duplicate high-weight ones.

**Step 6 — Pose Estimation:**

$$\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^N w_t^{[i]} \cdot x_t^{[i]}$$

**Role of Resampling:**
- **Problem:** After many updates, most particles have negligible weights (the "particle depletion problem"). Computational effort is wasted on particles that don't represent the true pose.
- **Solution:** Resampling concentrates particles in regions of high probability, ensuring the particle set remains representative of the belief.
- **Variants:** Low-variance resampling, stratified resampling, systematic resampling (each reduces the variance of the resampling step).

**Advantages:** Handles multimodal beliefs, no Gaussian assumption, can be implemented for any noise distribution.

**Disadvantages:** Particle depletion, requires $N$ large enough (rule of thumb: $N \geq 100$ for position tracking, $N \geq 1000$ for global localization).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $\sigma_v = 0.05$ m/s
- $\sigma_\omega = 0.02$ rad/s
- $\Delta t = 0.1$ s
- $v = 0.5$ m/s
- $\omega = 0.3$ rad/s
- $\theta = \pi/4$

**Step 1 — Write the Motion Model Jacobian w.r.t. Control $G_u$:**

$$G_u = \begin{pmatrix} \Delta t \cos\theta & 0 \\ \Delta t \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \cos(\pi/4) & 0 \\ 0.1 \sin(\pi/4) & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Numerically, $\cos(\pi/4) = \sin(\pi/4) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$:

$$G_u = \begin{pmatrix} 0.07071 & 0 \\ 0.07071 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Control Noise Covariance $R_t$:**

$$R_t = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_v^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\omega^2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0004 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Motion Covariance Contribution:**

$$\Sigma_{motion} = G_u R_t G_u^T$$

First, compute $G_u R_t$:

$$G_u R_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.07071 & 0 \\ 0.07071 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.0025 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0004 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.7678 \times 10^{-4} & 0 \\ 1.7678 \times 10^{-4} & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \times 10^{-5} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $G_u R_t G_u^T$:**

$$\Sigma_{motion} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.7678 \times 10^{-4} & 0 \\ 1.7678 \times 10^{-4} & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \times 10^{-5} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.07071 & 0.07071 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Row 1: $(1.7678 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.07071, \; 1.7678 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.07071, \; 0)$
$\quad = (1.25 \times 10^{-5}, \; 1.25 \times 10^{-5}, \; 0)$

Row 2: $(1.25 \times 10^{-5}, \; 1.25 \times 10^{-5}, \; 0)$

Row 3: $(0, \; 0, \; 4 \times 10^{-6})$

**Step 5 — Final Motion Covariance:**

$$\boxed{\Sigma_{motion} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.25 \times 10^{-5} & 1.25 \times 10^{-5} & 0 \\ 1.25 \times 10^{-5} & 1.25 \times 10^{-5} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \times 10^{-6} \end{pmatrix} \; \text{m}^2 \text{ and rad}^2}$$

**Interpretation:** The motion noise is correlated between x and y (because the robot moves at angle $\theta$) but not in $\theta$. Units are in $\text{m}^2$ for the (1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2) entries and $\text{rad}^2$ for the (3,3) entry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b) Numerical:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up:** The covariance matrix has mixed units. The student should clearly state this. Marks lost if not specified.
> 2. **Forgetting $\Delta t$:** Many students write the Jacobian without $\Delta t$, leading to incorrect dimensions.
> 3. **Diagonal-only assumption:** Treating the noise as affecting x and y independently is wrong when $\theta \neq 0$ or $\pi/2$. The full Jacobian must be computed.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps:** Always show the matrix multiplication $G_u R_t G_u^T$ explicitly; step-skipping loses **2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU ESE Revision — read this 3 times.**

- ✅ Localization = estimating pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ on a map.
- ✅ Bayes filter recursion has **two steps**: Prediction (motion) + Update (measurement).
- ✅ The **Markov assumption** underlies the entire derivation.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter** = unimodal Gaussian; only solves **local** localization.
- ✅ **EKF** = linearized Kalman for nonlinear motion/measurement models.
- ✅ **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** = particle filter; solves **global** localization.
- ✅ **MCL steps:** Initialize → Predict → Weight → Normalize → Resample → Estimate.
- ✅ **Kidnapped robot problem** is solved by **random particle injection** in MCL.
- ✅ **Particle depletion** is the main weakness of MCL; mitigated by adequate $N$ and resampling.
- ✅ **Grid (Markov) localization** scales poorly with resolution; good for small maps.
- ✅ **Jacobian $G_x$** is required for EKF motion covariance update — never write $\Sigma^- = \Sigma + R$.
- ✅ $R_t$ = **process noise**; $Q_t$ = **measurement noise** (don't swap them).
- ✅ **Effective sample size** $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ indicates resampling need.
- ✅ **Resampling methods:** Multinomial, Stratified, Systematic (low-variance preferred).
- ✅ **MCL pose estimate** = weighted mean of particles, OR the particle with maximum weight (MAP).
- ✅ A robot's belief is **multimodal** in environments with perceptual aliasing (e.g., corridors).
- ✅ The **measurement model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ uses ray-casting for range finders in known maps.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion** combines odometry + IMU + GPS + LiDAR via EKF or UKF (Unscented KF).
- ✅ **Probabilistic localization** is robust to noise and ambiguity; deterministic methods (e.g., trilateration alone) are not.
- ✅ **Computational complexity** ordering: Kalman < MCL < Grid (for typical parameters).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sensing in Mobile Robotics?

**Sensing** is the process by which a mobile robot perceives its internal state and external environment using specialized transducer devices called **sensors**. In the KTU 2024 framework, sensing is identified as a foundational pillar of the **perception-action loop** (Sense → Plan → Act), where the quality of robot behavior is fundamentally constrained by what the robot can measure.

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensing (KTU Terminology):** A sensor is a physical device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological quantity (the *measurand*) into a measurable electrical signal (the *output*) that a robot's processor can interpret. In mobile robotics, sensing provides the data inputs required for **localization**, **mapping**, **obstacle detection**, and **control feedback**.

### Conceptual Analogy (The "Blindfolded Person" Intuition)

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped in an unknown room. To navigate, you would:
- Touch walls with your hands (like **contact/proximity sensors**).
- Listen for echoes of your voice (like **ultrasonic sensors**).
- Feel the slope under your feet (like **inertial/IMU sensors**).
- Use a torch and watch reflections (like **LIDAR / cameras**).

Each sense is imperfect — they have different *range*, *accuracy*, and *noise*. Mobile robots face the exact same problem: no single sensor is enough, so engineers build a **sensor suite** and combine them cleverly (sensor fusion).

### Why Sensing is a High-Yield KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Sensing is a **recurrent 14-mark question** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).
> - Direct 3-mark questions on sensor classification, definitions, and units appear every semester.
> - Numerical problems on **time-of-flight** and **odometric resolution** are favourite Part B sub-questions.
> - The topic is a *prerequisite* for localization (Module 2) and SLAM (Module 3), so examiner questions often link these.

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Categories of Robot Sensing

| Category | What it measures | Example Sensors | Body Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Proprioceptive** | Internal state of the robot itself | Wheel encoders, IMU, motor current sensors | Proprioception = "Knowing where your own hand is with eyes closed" |
| **Exteroceptive** | External environment around the robot | Sonar, LIDAR, camera, GPS | Exteroception = "Eyes, ears, nose" |

A second orthogonal classification:
- **Active sensors** emit energy into the environment and measure the return (e.g., sonar, radar, LIDAR).
- **Passive sensors** only receive ambient energy (e.g., cameras, thermopiles).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Performance Metrics (The "Five-Star Rating System")

Every sensor in mobile robotics is evaluated on a common set of metrics. KTU examiners routinely test these in 3-mark and 7-mark parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 Sensor Performance Parameters (Always remember this list):**
> 1. **Dynamic Range** — Ratio between the largest and smallest measurable input. Usually expressed in **decibels (dB)**.
> 2. **Resolution** — Minimum distinguishable change in the input. Smaller = better.
> 3. **Linearity** — How closely the output follows a straight-line relation with the input. Defined by the **linearity error** in % of full scale.
> 4. **Bandwidth / Frequency Response** — Speed at which the sensor can track changes. Measured in **Hz**.
> 5. **Sensitivity** — Output change per unit input change ($S = \Delta y / \Delta x$).
> 6. **Accuracy vs Precision** — *Accuracy* = closeness to the true value; *Precision* = repeatability. KTU frequently asks the difference.
> 7. **Systematic Error & Random Error** — Systematic is deterministic (can be calibrated out); random is stochastic (cannot be removed, only averaged).

## 2.2 Classification of Sensors (Block-Level Architecture)

Mobile robot sensors are typically organized into four functional groups:

1. **Encoders / Wheel sensors** — for odometry
2. **Heading sensors** — compass, gyroscope, inclinometer
3. **Active ranging sensors** — sonar, laser, IR
4. **Vision-based / Position sensors** — camera, GPS

## 2.3 Optical Encoders (Wheel Speed / Position Sensing)

**Optical shaft encoders** are the workhorse of wheel odometry. A slotted disc rotates between an LED and a photodiode; each slot crossing produces one pulse.

Two types:
- **Incremental encoder** — produces N pulses per revolution; gives relative motion.
- **Absolute encoder** — produces a unique binary code for each shaft angle; gives absolute position.

**Key encoder formulas:**

$$
N_{\text{pulses per radian}} = \frac{C}{2\pi}
$$

$$
\Delta s = R \cdot \Delta \phi
$$

where $R$ is wheel radius and $\Delta \phi$ is rotation in radians.

## 2.4 Active Ranging — Time of Flight Principle

The most important principle in the whole sensing module. **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** sensors emit a wave packet and measure the time taken to receive the echo.

For an **electromagnetic wave** (laser, IR, radar), using the speed of light $c$:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

For an **acoustic wave** (ultrasonic/sonar), using the speed of sound $v_s$:

$$
d = \frac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **1/2** is a frequent KTU pitfall. The wave travels *to the target AND back*, so the measured time must be halved. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for the missing factor of 2.

## 2.5 Ultrasonic / Sonar Sensors

- Operate at **40 kHz** (above human hearing).
- Speed of sound in air: $v_s \approx \mathbf{343 \text{ m/s}}$ at 20°C.
- Range: typically **0.1 m to 10 m**.
- Beam cone angle: wide (≈ 30°), causing specular reflection errors.
- Cheap, robust, but slow ($\approx 20$ Hz update rate).

## 2.6 Laser Range Finder (LIDAR)

- Operates in **infrared (e.g., 905 nm)** or visible band.
- Speed of light $c = \mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$.
- Range: up to **100 m**, accuracy $\approx \pm 2$ cm.
- Scanning LIDARs use rotating mirrors to generate 2D/3D point clouds.
- Much higher bandwidth ($\approx 10$–$100$ kHz) and angular resolution ($< 1°$) than sonar.

## 2.7 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

- **Gyroscope** measures angular velocity $\omega$ in deg/s or rad/s.
- **Accelerometer** measures linear acceleration $a$ in $m/s^2$.
- Integration yields orientation and velocity:

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

$$
v(t) = v_0 + \int_0^t a(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Drift Problem:** Integration accumulates small sensor biases over time, causing unbounded error growth. This is a frequent 7-mark conceptual KTU question. The solution is **sensor fusion with absolute sensors** (e.g., GPS, encoders).

## 2.8 Global Positioning System (GPS)

- Based on **trilateration** from at least 4 satellites.
- Each satellite broadcasts a timestamp; receiver computes **pseudorange**:

$$
\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{\text{received},i} - t_{\text{transmitted},i})
$$

- Accuracy: **$\pm 2$ to 5 m** for civilian C/A code.
- **RTK-GPS** can achieve **cm-level** accuracy.
- **Differential GPS (DGPS)** uses a base station to correct errors.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Unit / Constant |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Time of flight (EM wave) | $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | $d = \frac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Time of flight (acoustic) | $v_s = 343$ m/s at 20°C |
| 3 | $\Delta s = R \cdot \Delta \phi$ | Linear wheel displacement | $R$ in m, $\Delta \phi$ in rad |
| 4 | $\theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{2\pi}{C}$ | Encoder angular resolution | $C$ = counts per revolution |
| 5 | $S_{\text{linear}} = \frac{\pi D}{C}$ | Linear odometry resolution | $D$ = wheel diameter |
| 6 | $\theta(t) = \int \omega\, dt$ | Gyro integration | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 7 | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | GPS pseudorange | $\Delta t$ in seconds |
| 8 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Dynamic range in dB | dB |
| 9 | $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | Quadrature error sum (independent) | meters / rad |
| 10 | $f_{\text{Doppler}} = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\alpha}{c}$ | Radar Doppler shift (advanced) | Hz |

## 2.10 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Sensor Use Case |
| :--- | :--- |
| Autonomous Cars (e.g., Waymo, Tesla) | LIDAR + camera + radar + GPS + IMU sensor fusion |
| Warehouse Robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva) | Wheel encoders + 2D LIDAR for SLAM |
| Drones (DJI, Parrot) | Camera + IMU + GPS + barometer (altimeter) |
| Vacuum Robots (Roomba) | Bumper + IR + cliff sensors + wheel encoders |
| Underwater ROVs | Sonar + pressure sensor + IMU |
