# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Odometry Resolution of a Wheel Encoder

> This is one of the highest-yield derivations in the entire module. It is almost guaranteed to appear as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Problem Setup:** A wheel of diameter $D$ is fitted with an incremental optical encoder that produces $C$ counts per full revolution. Derive the linear distance the robot moves per encoder count.

**Step 1 — Relate wheel rotation to linear distance:**

When a wheel of diameter $D$ makes exactly **one full revolution**, the linear distance traveled equals the circumference:

$$
s_{\text{1 rev}} = \pi D
$$

**Step 2 — Find the linear distance per single count:**

If $C$ counts are produced in one revolution, the distance per count is obtained by dividing the circumference by the number of counts:

$$
s_{\text{per count}} = \frac{\pi D}{C}
$$

This quantity is the **odometric resolution** of the wheel encoder. To improve resolution (smaller distance per count), one must either reduce $D$ or increase $C$.

**Step 3 — General expression for distance traveled in $N$ counts:**

$$
s = N \cdot \frac{\pi D}{C}
$$

> **Engineering sanity check:** If $D = 0.2$ m and $C = 500$ counts/rev, then $s_{\text{per count}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.2}{500} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$ m $= 1.26$ mm. This is a typical resolution for a small mobile robot.

**Step 4 — Angular resolution per count:**

The angular rotation corresponding to one count is:

$$
\theta_{\text{per count}} = \frac{2\pi}{C} \quad \text{radians}
$$

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the *unit* explicitly (radians vs degrees). $1 \text{ count} = \frac{360°}{C}$ in degrees. Missing the unit is a 1-mark deduction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time of Flight Distance (Electromagnetic Wave)

> A laser range finder sends a pulse, which returns after $\Delta t = 33$ ns. Find the distance to the obstacle.

**Step 1 — Identify the wave type and speed:**

The wave is electromagnetic (light), so we use the speed of light $c$:

$$
c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the round-trip principle:**

The light travels the distance $d$ to the target, then the same distance $d$ back to the sensor. The total distance traveled by the photon is $2d$. Therefore:

$$
2d = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
d = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8} \ \mathrm{m/s}) \times (33 \times 10^{-9} \ \mathrm{s})}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{2.998 \times 33 \times 10^{-1}}{2} \ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
d = \frac{98.934 \times 10^{-1}}{2} \ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
d = 4.9467 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance of the 1/2 factor:** For a one-way time of flight, the pulse would travel $c \cdot t = 9.89$ m, but the obstacle is only **half** that distance, i.e., **4.95 m**. The half is non-negotiable in KTU marking.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential Drive Odometry Update

> Derive the incremental pose update for a differential-drive robot moving between two encoder readings.

**Setup:** A differential-drive robot has a wheelbase $b$ (distance between two drive wheels) and wheel radius $R$. The left and right encoders report rotations $\Delta \phi_l$ and $\Delta \phi_r$ between time steps.

**Step 1 — Compute individual wheel displacements:**

$$
\Delta s_l = R \cdot \Delta \phi_l
$$

$$
\Delta s_r = R \cdot \Delta \phi_r
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear translation of the robot's center:**

The translation is the average of both wheel motions:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_l + \Delta s_r}{2} = \frac{R(\Delta \phi_l + \Delta \phi_r)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the rotation about the robot's center:**

The robot pivots about the midpoint of the wheelbase. The rotation angle is the difference of the wheel motions divided by the wheelbase:

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b} = \frac{R(\Delta \phi_r - \Delta \phi_l)}{b}
$$

**Step 4 — Update the global pose $(x, y, \theta)$:**

Assuming small $\Delta \theta$ and an instantaneous center of rotation at the midpoint:

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2})
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2})
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** The midpoint rule ($\theta_k + \Delta \theta/2$) gives a more accurate integration than using $\theta_k$. Examiners reward students who mention this explicitly.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — GPS Position via 2D Trilateration

**Setup:** Three satellites at known positions $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$ are visible. Pseudoranges $\rho_i$ are measured.

**Step 1 — Range equation for each satellite:**

For a 2D plane, the distance from the unknown robot position $(x, y)$ to satellite $i$ is:

$$
\rho_i^2 = (x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Linearize by subtracting satellite 1 from satellites 2 and 3:**

Subtracting the equation for $i=1$ from those of $i=2$ and $i=3$ eliminates the quadratic term:

$$
\rho_2^2 - \rho_1^2 = (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2) - 2x(x_2 - x_1) - 2y(y_2 - y_1)
$$

$$
\rho_3^2 - \rho_1^2 = (x_3^2 - x_1^2) + (y_3^2 - y_1^2) - 2x(x_3 - x_1) - 2y(y_3 - y_1)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the 2x2 linear system:**

This can be expressed in matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} (x_2 - x_1) & (y_2 - y_1) \\ (x_3 - x_1) & (y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2) - (\rho_2^2 - \rho_1^2) \\ (x_3^2 - x_1^2) + (y_3^2 - y_1^2) - (\rho_3^2 - \rho_1^2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In 3D, **four satellites** are required to additionally solve for the receiver clock bias. The KTU question often simplifies to a 2D problem with 3 satellites.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection table (commonly asked as 7-mark "Compare any 3 sensors" question):**

| Property | Wheel Encoder | Sonar | LIDAR | Camera | GPS | IMU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type | Proprioceptive, Passive | Exteroceptive, Active | Exteroceptive, Active | Exteroceptive, Passive | Exteroceptive, Passive | Proprioceptive, Passive |
| Range | N/A (internal) | 0.1 – 10 m | 0.1 – 100 m | 0.1 – 50 m | Global (sky view) | N/A (internal) |
| Accuracy | ±0.5 mm/count | ±1 – 5 cm | ±1 – 2 cm | ±pixels → m | ±2 – 5 m | Drift 1°/min |
| Update Rate | Very high (kHz) | Low (20 Hz) | High (10 – 100 kHz) | Medium (30 – 60 Hz) | Low (1 – 10 Hz) | Very high (kHz) |
| Cost | Very Low | Very Low | High | Low – Medium | High | Medium |
| Robust to light | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (fails in dark/glare) | Yes | Yes |
| Robust to weather | Yes | Poor in rain | Cloud-limited | Poor in fog | Poor indoors | Yes |
| Failure mode | Slip on wet floor | Specular reflection | Sun interference | Lighting changes | Indoor failure | Drift |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Perception-Action Loop in Mobile Robots

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envA[Environment] -->|emits/stimuli| sensorA[Sensors]
    sensorA -->|raw data| procA[Signal Processing]
    procA -->|features| fusionA[Sensor Fusion]
    fusionA -->|perception state| planA[Planning / Localization]
    planA -->|motion commands| ctrlA[Controller]
    ctrlA -->|actuator signals| actA[Actuators / Motors]
    actA -->|physical motion| envA
    fbEnc[Wheel Encoders] -->|proprioceptive feedback| procA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The outer loop is the *external* perception-action cycle. The bottom line is the *proprioceptive* internal feedback. This is the classic closed-loop control model used throughout mobile robotics.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Sensor Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Robot Sensors]
    rootA --> propA[Proprioceptive]
    rootA --> exterA[Exteroceptive]
    propA --> encA[Wheel Encoders]
    propA --> imuA[IMU Gyroscope]
    propA --> accA[IMU Accelerometer]
    propA --> curA[Motor Current Sensors]
    exterA --> rangA[Ranging Sensors]
    exterA --> visA[Vision Sensors]
    exterA --> posA[Position Sensors]
    rangA --> sonA[Ultrasonic Sonar]
    rangA --> lidaA[Laser LIDAR]
    rangA --> irA[Infrared Ranger]
    visA --> camA[Monocular Camera]
    visA --> sterA[Stereo Camera]
    posA --> gpsA[GPS]
    posA --> magA[Magnetic Compass]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Time-of-Flight Operation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TX as Transmitter
    participant Obj as Target
    participant RX as Receiver
    participant CPU as Robot CPU

    CPU->>TX: Trigger pulse
    TX->>Obj: Emit wave packet (light/sound)
    Note over TX,Obj: Wave travels distance d
    Obj->>RX: Reflected wave returns
    Note over RX,Obj: Wave travels distance d back
    RX->>CPU: Echo detection timestamp
    CPU->>CPU: Compute dt and distance d = c*dt/2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Odometry Pipeline for Differential Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    encL[Left Encoder Pulses] -->|count N_L| calcA[Encoder to Angle]
    encR[Right Encoder Pulses] -->|count N_R| calcA
    calcA -->|phi_L, phi_R| calcB[Linear Displacement]
    calcB -->|s_L, s_R| calcC[Robot Frame Update]
    calcC -->|ds, dtheta| poseA[Global Pose Integration]
    poseA -->|x, y, theta| mapA[Robot Map / Plot]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — GPS Trilateration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sat1[SAT 1 at x1, y1, z1] -->|broadcast time t1| recvA[GPS Receiver]
    sat2[SAT 2 at x2, y2, z2] -->|broadcast time t2| recvA
    sat3[SAT 3 at x3, y3, z3] -->|broadcast time t3| recvA
    sat4[SAT 4 at x4, y4, z4] -->|broadcast time t4| recvA
    recvA -->|pseudoranges rho_i| calcD[Trilateration Solver]
    calcD -->|linearized equations| matA[Matrix Inversion 4x4]
    matA -->|solution vector| poseB[Robot Position x, y, z, t_bias]
```

> **Why 4 satellites?** Three satellites give a 2D position (x, y) and the receiver clock bias is solved implicitly with the **4th** satellite. A 3D fix needs 4 satellites minimum; 4 gives the 3D position + time.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a sensor. Differentiate between active and passive sensors.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A sensor is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (measurand) into a measurable electrical signal that a robot can interpret.
>
> | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | Emits energy (light, sound, RF) into the environment | Only receives ambient energy |
> | Does not depend on external illumination | Often depends on external illumination |
> | Example: Sonar, LIDAR, Radar | Example: Camera, thermopile, microphone |
> | Can measure distance directly via ToF | Cannot measure range directly (2D image only) |

### Question 2: What is odometry? List its main error sources.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Odometry** is the use of data from motion sensors (typically wheel encoders) to estimate change in robot position over time. It is a relative localization method.
>
> **Main error sources:**
> 1. Wheel slip on the floor
> 2. Uneven wheel diameter (manufacturing tolerance, wear)
> 3. Wheel misalignment (caster wheels)
> 4. Finite encoder resolution / quantization
> 5. Uneven floor surface and bumps
>
> Errors are **systematic** (cumulative) and grow unboundedly with distance traveled.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Wheel Encoders & Odometry

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **optical incremental encoder**. Derive the linear odometry resolution formula. (7 marks)

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel diameter $D = 0.30$ m, wheelbase $b = 0.50$ m, and uses encoders with $C = 1000$ counts/revolution. If the left and right encoders register $\Delta N_l = 520$ counts and $\Delta N_r = 580$ counts over a sampling interval, compute:
- (i) The linear displacement of each wheel.
- (ii) The translation and rotation of the robot.
- (iii) The new pose if the starting pose is $(0, 0, 0^\circ)$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Optical Incremental Encoder — Construction and Working (7 marks)**

A disc with $N$ fine radial slots is mounted on the encoder shaft. A light source (LED) is placed on one side of the disc, and a photodiode on the other. As the shaft rotates, slots alternately allow and block light, producing a train of electrical pulses. By counting pulses, the angular displacement is obtained.

A second photodiode offset by **90° (a quadrature channel)** is used to determine the **direction of rotation**. Two square waves in quadrature are produced (A and B channels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **quadrature encoding** allows the *count* and *direction* of motion to be resolved. KTU examiners specifically check for the quadrature explanation.

**Linear odometry resolution derivation** (as done in Section 3.1):
One revolution = circumference $\pi D$, gives $C$ counts.
Resolution = $\pi D / C$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D = 0.30$ m, $b = 0.50$ m, $C = 1000$ counts/rev, $\Delta N_l = 520$, $\Delta N_r = 580$.

**Step 1 — Compute wheel rotations in radians:**

$$
\Delta \phi_l = 2\pi \cdot \frac{520}{1000} = 1.04\pi \ \mathrm{rad} \approx 3.267 \ \mathrm{rad}
$$

$$
\Delta \phi_r = 2\pi \cdot \frac{580}{1000} = 1.16\pi \ \mathrm{rad} \approx 3.644 \ \mathrm{rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute wheel linear displacements ($R = D/2 = 0.15$ m):**

$$
\Delta s_l = R \cdot \Delta \phi_l = 0.15 \times 3.267 = 0.4901 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
\Delta s_r = R \cdot \Delta \phi_r = 0.15 \times 3.644 = 0.5466 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute robot translation $\Delta s$ and rotation $\Delta \theta$:**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_l + \Delta s_r}{2} = \frac{0.4901 + 0.5466}{2} = 0.5184 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b} = \frac{0.5466 - 0.4901}{0.50} = 0.1131 \ \mathrm{rad} \approx 6.48^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Compute new pose using midpoint rule:**

Starting pose: $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0^\circ)$.

$$
x_1 = x_0 + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_0 + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) = 0 + 0.5184 \cdot \cos(3.24^\circ) = 0.5184 \times 0.9984 = 0.5176 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
y_1 = y_0 + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_0 + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) = 0 + 0.5184 \cdot \sin(3.24^\circ) = 0.5184 \times 0.0565 = 0.0293 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \Delta \theta = 0 + 6.48^\circ = 6.48^\circ
$$

**Final pose:** $(0.518 \ \mathrm{m}, \ 0.029 \ \mathrm{m}, \ 6.48^\circ)$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Active Ranging & Time of Flight

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the principle of Time-of-Flight (ToF), explain the working of an **ultrasonic range sensor** and a **laser range finder**. Compare their performance. (7 marks)

**(b)** An autonomous car is fitted with a 2D scanning LIDAR. Each laser pulse returns with a delay of $0.50 \,\mu s$. The LIDAR scans a 90° arc in front of the car at $1^\circ$ angular resolution. Compute:
- (i) The distance to the detected object in front.
- (ii) The number of laser pulses in the 90° scan.
- (iii) The total scan time if the pulse repetition rate is 10 kHz. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Time-of-Flight Active Ranging (7 marks)**

**Ultrasonic sensor (sonar):**
A piezoelectric transducer emits a **40 kHz** acoustic pulse. The pulse travels at the speed of sound ($v_s \approx 343$ m/s), reflects off an object, and returns. The time delay $\Delta t$ is measured. The distance is computed as $d = v_s \Delta t / 2$.

**Laser range finder (LIDAR):**
A laser diode (typically **905 nm IR**) emits a short light pulse at $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s. The echo is detected by an avalanche photodiode, and $\Delta t$ is measured with picosecond precision. The distance is $d = c \Delta t / 2$.

**Comparison table:**

| Parameter | Sonar | LIDAR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wave speed | 343 m/s | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Range | 0.1 – 10 m | 0.1 – 100 m |
| Beam width | Wide (≈ 30°) | Narrow (≈ 0.25°) |
| Accuracy | ±1 – 5 cm | ±1 – 2 cm |
| Cost | Very low | High |
| Failure modes | Specular reflection, slow speed | Sun interference, eye safety |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\Delta t = 0.50 \,\mu s = 0.50 \times 10^{-6}$ s, scan arc $= 90^\circ$, angular resolution $= 1^\circ$, pulse rate $= 10$ kHz.

**(i) Distance to object:**

For EM wave: $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^{8} \times 0.50 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{149.9 \times 10^{2}}{2} = \frac{14990}{2} \times 10^{-1} = 74.95 \ \mathrm{m}
$$

**Result:** $d \approx 75$ m (car is at 75 m from the obstacle).

**(ii) Number of pulses in 90° scan:**

$$
N = \frac{90^\circ}{1^\circ/\text{pulse}} = 90 \ \text{pulses}
$$

**(iii) Total scan time:**

Pulse repetition period $T = 1/f = 1/10000$ Hz $= 100 \,\mu s$ per pulse.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T = 90 \times 100 \,\mu s = 9000 \,\mu s = 9 \ \mathrm{ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 9 ms scan time implies the LIDAR can refresh the **full 90° scan 111 times per second** (1/0.009 ≈ 111 Hz). This is *much faster* than a sonar ($\approx 20$ Hz) — a key engineering reason why LIDAR is preferred in autonomous vehicles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Deductions to Avoid):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the ToF formula → **1 mark lost** (this is the #1 most-deducted error).
> 2. **Mixing up speed of light vs speed of sound** in ToF questions → 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Using $\theta_k$ instead of $\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2$** in odometry pose update → KTU examiner deducts 0.5 – 1 mark for "imprecise integration".
> 4. **Confusing proprioceptive vs exteroceptive** (sonar is exteroceptive; encoders are proprioceptive) → full mark lost on classification.
> 5. **Ignoring units** in encoder resolution (radians vs degrees) → 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Wrong base for log in dynamic range** (use $20\log_{10}$ for amplitude ratio, $10\log_{10}$ for power) → 1 mark.
> 7. **Omitting the quadrature channel explanation** in encoder questions → 1 – 2 marks lost (examiners always test this).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Sensing (KTU Module 1):**
> - [x] A **sensor** converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal.
> - [x] **Proprioceptive** = internal state (encoders, IMU). **Exteroceptive** = external world (sonar, LIDAR, camera, GPS).
> - [x] **Active** sensors emit energy (LIDAR, sonar). **Passive** sensors only receive (camera, magnetometer).
> - [x] The **7 key performance parameters** of any sensor are dynamic range, resolution, linearity, bandwidth, sensitivity, accuracy, precision.
> - [x] **Accuracy** = closeness to true value; **Precision** = repeatability. (Ask KTU examiners: "Can a sensor be precise but inaccurate?" — Yes, if it is biased.)
> - [x] **Optical encoder** uses a slotted disc and a photodiode; produces $C$ counts per revolution.
> - [x] **Quadrature encoding** uses two photodiodes 90° apart to detect direction.
> - [x] Linear odometry resolution $= \pi D / C$ (meters per count).
> - [x] **Time-of-Flight** distance $= c \Delta t / 2$ for EM waves; $v_s \Delta t / 2$ for acoustic.
> - [x] Speed of light $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s; speed of sound $v_s = 343$ m/s at 20°C.
> - [x] **Sonar** operates at **40 kHz**, has wide beam (30°), range 0.1 – 10 m.
> - [x] **LIDAR** has narrow beam (< 1°), range up to 100 m, accuracy $\pm 1$ cm, refresh rate > 10 Hz.
> - [x] **IMU** = gyroscope + accelerometer. **Drift** is the unbounded error growth due to integration of sensor bias.
> - [x] **GPS** uses **trilateration** from 4 satellites (in 3D) to solve (x, y, z, clock bias).
> - [x] Civilian GPS accuracy: $\pm 2$ – 5 m. RTK-GPS gives cm-level.
> - [x] **Differential drive odometry** updates: $\Delta s = R(\Delta\phi_l + \Delta\phi_r)/2$ and $\Delta\theta = R(\Delta\phi_r - \Delta\phi_l)/b$.
> - [x] Pose update uses the **midpoint rule** for higher accuracy.
> - [x] **Sensor fusion** (e.g., Kalman filter) is required to combine complementary sensors and bound odometric drift.
> - [x] No single sensor is sufficient for real-world mobile robots — always use a **sensor suite**.
> - [x] Common KTU questions ask: "Compare sonar and LIDAR" / "Derive odometry formula" / "Explain GPS trilateration" / "Explain the perception-action loop".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Mapping in Mobile Robotics?

**Mapping** is the process by which a mobile robot constructs a spatial representation of its environment using sensor measurements collected while it moves through that environment. It is the fundamental problem of transforming raw streams of noisy, partial, and incremental sensor data (typically from **Laser Range Finders (LRF)**, **Sonar**, **RGB-D cameras**, or **LiDAR**) into a coherent, globally consistent model of the surroundings.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus, mapping is treated as a **probabilistic estimation problem** where the robot must infer the posterior probability over the map given its trajectory and observations. The map $m$ is built incrementally from observations $z_{1:t}$ and odometry readings $u_{1:t}$ according to Bayes' theorem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEBRT522 – Module 1):** Mapping covers *Occupancy Grid Maps*, *Metric vs. Topological Representations*, *Probabilistic Mapping using Bayes' Rule*, the *Inverse Sensor Model*, and the *Log-Odds Update Formulation*. These are the bread-and-butter topics for the 14-mark ESE questions.

### 1.2 Map Representations — The Three Families

| Representation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Metric (Grid) Map** | Discretizes the world into cells, each holding a probability of being occupied | Indoor navigation, SLAM |
| **Topological Map** | Represents the environment as a graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) | High-level planning, floor abstraction |
| **Hybrid Map** | Combines metric grids locally with topological links globally | Large-scale service robots |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Blind Cartographer

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded and placed in an unfamiliar house. You hold a **walking cane** in front of you (your range sensor). With every tap of the cane, you learn whether the cell in front of you is *empty* or *occupied*. As you shuffle forward, you mentally "draw" a floor plan — but every measurement is noisy, so a single tap could lie. You keep a *belief* about each tile: "I am 70% sure this tile has a wall." A robot does the **exact same thing** with sonar or laser returns — it updates its *belief* about every cell in a grid, one observation at a time. This belief is precisely the **occupancy probability**.

## 1.4 Why is Mapping Hard?

A robot performing mapping must overcome four intrinsic challenges:

1. **Perceptual uncertainty** — sensors are noisy and return false positives/negatives.
2. **Motion uncertainty** — wheel slippage and drift corrupt odometry.
3. **Correspondence problem** — it is non-trivial to know *which* map cell an observation refers to.
4. **Real-time constraints** — updates must occur at the sensor's sampling rate (often **20–40 Hz** for LiDAR).

The probabilistic approach (Bayes filter on grids) elegantly addresses all four by maintaining a *belief distribution* over the map rather than a single deterministic estimate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Occupancy Grid Mapping — The Core Idea

The environment is discretized into a fine 2-D grid of $N = W \times H$ cells $m_i$, where $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$. Each cell is assumed to be **independent** of every other cell (this is the *independence assumption* of the occupancy grid framework, originally proposed by Moravec & Elfes, 1985). The map posterior then factorizes:

$$p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

Here, $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ denotes whether cell $i$ is *free* (0) or *occupied* (1), $z_{1:t}$ are the time-ordered sensor measurements, and $x_{1:t}$ is the robot's pose trajectory.

## 2.2 The Bayesian Update — Operational Logic

The mapping algorithm updates each cell's occupancy *only* when a measurement passes through it. The discrete Bayes update for a single cell is:

$$p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

Because the denominator is a normalizing constant, we work with the **odds form** and apply the **log-odds trick** to convert the recursive multiplication into a recursive addition (critical for numerical stability on millions of cells).

## 2.3 The Log-Odds Representation (KTU High-Yield!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **log-odds** formulation is the single most important formula in this module. It is asked almost every cycle in the KTU ESE.

The log-odds (or *logit*) of a cell is:

$$l_i = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$$

The recursive update becomes a *sum*:

$$l_{i,t} = l_{i,t-1} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds (zero for the standard 0.5 prior). After every update, the occupancy probability is recovered by the **inverse logit**:

$$p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$$

The term $\log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}$ is the **inverse sensor model** value, which assigns a positive number if the measurement *suggests* occupancy and a negative number if it suggests free space.

## 2.4 The Inverse Sensor Model

For a **range-bearing sensor** (e.g., a laser), the inverse sensor model returns a value $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ for every cell along the ray. A standard piecewise definition is:

$$
p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) =
\begin{cases}
0.0 & \text{if } d_i < d_{\min} \\
0.5 & \text{if } d_{\min} \le d_i < z_t - \tfrac{\Delta}{2} \\
1.0 & \text{if } z_t - \tfrac{\Delta}{2} \le d_i \le z_t + \tfrac{\Delta}{2} \\
0.5 & \text{if } z_t + \tfrac{\Delta}{2} < d_i \le z_{\max} \\
0.0 & \text{if } d_i > z_{\max}
\end{cases}
$$

where $d_i$ is the perpendicular distance from cell $i$ to the sensor ray, $z_t$ is the measured range, $\Delta$ is the obstacle thickness constant, and $z_{\max}$ is the sensor's maximum reliable range.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Map posterior | $p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | $\prod_{i=1}^{N} p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | Dimensionless probability in $[0,1]$ |
| Bayes update | $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ | $\frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$ | Recursive per cell |
| Log-odds | $l_i$ | $\log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$ | Real number $\in (-\infty, +\infty)$ |
| Log-odds update | $l_{i,t}$ | $l_{i,t-1} + \text{inv\_sensor}(m_i, z_t, x_t) - l_0$ | Additive recursion |
| Inverse logit | $p(m_i)$ | $1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$ | Recovers probability |
| Occupancy threshold | $p_{\text{occ}}$ | $0.5$ (log-odds $= 0$) | Decision boundary |
| Grid resolution | $r$ | $0.05$ m to $0.30$ m | $r = 0.05$ m typical for indoor |
| Sensor range | $z_{\max}$ | $30$ m (LiDAR) | $z_{\max} = 5$ m (sonar) |
| Map size | $N$ | $W \times H$ | $W = \frac{L_x}{r}$, $H = \frac{L_y}{r}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Occupancy grid maps are the **de facto standard** for indoor mobile robots (e.g., **ROS Navigation Stack**, **TurtleBot**, **iRobot Create**, **Boston Dynamics Stretch**). They feed directly into:
- **A\*** and **D\*** path planners,
- **Dijkstra** global planners,
- **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** local obstacle avoidance,
- **AMCL** (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) for re-localization.

Topological maps are used in warehouse logistics (e.g., **Amazon Kiva robots**) where the world is already structured into aisles and nodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Log-Odds Update

We begin with the recursive Bayes update for a single grid cell, given the **conditional independence** of measurements and the **independence assumption** between cells.

**Step 1 — Write Bayes' Theorem**

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the *Odds* Ratio**

Define the odds of cell $i$ being occupied as:

$$
o_i \;=\; \frac{p(m_i)}{p(\neg m_i)} \;=\; \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

The odds ratio updates *multiplicatively* under Bayes' rule, which is the key insight.

**Step 3 — Apply the Logarithm**

Taking the natural log of both sides of the odds update:

$$
l_i \;\equiv\; \log(o_i) \;=\; \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

This converts the multiplicative update into an **additive** one, which is numerically stable even after thousands of updates.

**Step 4 — Combine the Terms**

The complete log-odds recursion becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_{i,t} \;=\; & \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \\
            & + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} \\
            & - \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} \quad \text{(prior)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compress to Standard Form**

Denoting the inverse sensor model log-odds as $I_{i,t}$ and the prior log-odds as $l_0$:

$$
l_{i,t} \;=\; l_{i,t-1} \;+\; I_{i,t} \;-\; l_0
$$

This is the *exact formula* students must reproduce in the KTU exam. Memorize this.

**Step 6 — Recover Probability (Inverse Logit)**

To display the map, convert log-odds back to probability:

$$
p(m_i) \;=\; \frac{\exp(l_i)}{1 + \exp(l_i)} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** A robot uses a 1-D occupancy grid with 5 cells, all initialized to $p = 0.5$ (log-odds $l = 0$). The prior is $l_0 = 0$. A single laser ray produces the following inverse sensor model values for cells 1 through 5:
> $$I = [\, -0.4,\; -0.4,\; +0.8,\; -0.2,\; 0.0 \,]$$
> (a) Compute the updated log-odds $l_{i,1}$ for each cell.
> (b) Convert each log-odds value back to a probability.
> (c) Classify each cell as *free*, *occupied*, or *unknown* using threshold $p \ge 0.6$ for occupied.

**Solution (a) — Log-odds update** (using $l_{i,1} = l_{i,0} + I_i - l_0 = 0 + I_i - 0 = I_i$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1 &= -0.4 \\
l_2 &= -0.4 \\
l_3 &= +0.8 \\
l_4 &= -0.2 \\
l_5 &= 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b) — Inverse logit** $p_i = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.4}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 0.6703} \;=\; 1 - 0.5987 \;=\; 0.4013 \\
p_2 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.4}} \;=\; 0.4013 \\
p_3 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{+0.8}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 2.2255} \;=\; 1 - 0.3100 \;=\; 0.6900 \\
p_4 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.2}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 0.8187} \;=\; 1 - 0.5498 \;=\; 0.4502 \\
p_5 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{0}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 1} \;=\; 1 - 0.5 \;=\; 0.5000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (c) — Classification** (threshold $p \ge 0.6$ ⇒ occupied; $p \le 0.4$ ⇒ free; else unknown):

| Cell | $l_i$ | $p_i$ | Class |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $-0.4$ | $0.4013$ | Unknown |
| 2 | $-0.4$ | $0.4013$ | Unknown |
| 3 | $+0.8$ | $0.6900$ | **Occupied** |
| 4 | $-0.2$ | $0.4502$ | Unknown |
| 5 | $0.0$ | $0.5000$ | Unknown |

**Engineering interpretation:** A single ray produces only one confident classification (Cell 3 — likely the obstacle hit). All other cells still need more measurements to cross the 0.6/0.4 thresholds. This is precisely why a robot must move and re-observe to build a *reliable* map.

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Grid Cells Required

If a rectangular environment has dimensions $L_x \times L_y$ meters and we choose a grid resolution $r$ (meters per cell), the number of cells is:

$$
N \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{r} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{r} \right\rceil
$$

For an $80 \text{ m} \times 60 \text{ m}$ outdoor area with $r = 0.20 \text{ m}$:

$$
N \;=\; 400 \times 300 \;=\; 120{,}000 \text{ cells}
$$

Memory required (storing one float per cell in Python-style 4-byte float): $120{,}000 \times 4 = 480 \text{ KB}$, easily affordable. With $r = 0.05$ m, the same area needs $N = 1600 \times 1200 = 1{,}920{,}000$ cells — about 7.5 MB. This trade-off is the classic *resolution vs. memory* compromise.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Occupancy Grid Mapping

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **signal flow** of the mapping subsystem, from raw sensor data to the rendered occupancy grid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sensor Raw Scan z_t"] --> B["Pose Estimation x_t via Odometry"]
    B --> C["Ray Tracing Module"]
    A --> C
    C --> D["Cell Indexing Along Each Ray"]
    D --> E["Inverse Sensor Model Lookup"]
    E --> F["Log Odds Update for Each Cell"]
    F --> G["Occupancy Grid Map m"]
    G --> H["Inverse Logit Conversion"]
    H --> I["Visualization on RViz"]
    G --> J["Path Planner Input"]
    J --> K["Navigation Stack Output"]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style G fill:#7f2f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#2f5f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2f5f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic State Diagram of the Bayesian Update

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["State: Initialize All Cells with Prior l0"] --> S2
    S2["State: Receive New Scan z_t and Pose x_t"] --> S3
    S3["State: Trace Ray from x_t Along Beam Direction"] --> S4
    S4{"State: Cell Free or Cell Hit"} --> S5
    S4 --> S6
    S5["Action: Add Negative Log Odds to Cell"] --> S7
    S6["Action: Add Positive Log Odds to Cell"] --> S7
    S7["State: Store Updated Log Odds l_i_t"] --> S8
    S8{"State: More Beams in Scan?"}
    S8 -- Yes --> S3
    S8 -- No --> S2
    style S1 fill:#4f4f7f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#7f4f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S7 fill:#2f7f4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Map Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Robot Needs a Map of the Environment"] --> Q1{"Is the Environment Small and Static?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> M1["Use Metric Occupancy Grid Map"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is High Level Routing the Main Goal?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M2["Use Topological Graph Map"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Need Both Local Precision and Global Routing?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> M3["Use Hybrid Map: Local Grids + Global Graph"]
    Q3 -- No --> M4["Re-evaluate Sensor Set and Mission Goals"]
    style M1 fill:#2f4f7f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style M2 fill:#7f2f4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style M3 fill:#4f7f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Inverse Sensor Model Ray Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Robot Pose x_t"] --> B1["Beam Angle theta_1"]
    R --> B2["Beam Angle theta_2"]
    R --> B3["Beam Angle theta_3"]
    B1 --> F1["Cell Free Region: 0 to z_t - delta over 2"]
    B1 --> H1["Cell Hit Region: z_t - delta over 2 to z_t + delta over 2"]
    B1 --> U1["Cell Unknown: Beyond z_t + delta over 2 up to z_max"]
    B2 --> F2["Cell Free Region"]
    B2 --> H2["Cell Hit Region"]
    B2 --> U2["Cell Unknown"]
    B3 --> F3["Cell Free Region"]
    B3 --> H3["Cell Hit Region"]
    B3 --> U3["Cell Unknown"]
    style R fill:#7f2f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H1 fill:#7f2f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H2 fill:#7f2f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H3 fill:#7f2f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#2f5f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F2 fill:#2f5f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F3 fill:#2f5f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an Occupancy Grid Map. Mention any two advantages of representing the environment as an occupancy grid.**

**Model Answer:**
An **occupancy grid map** is a discretized 2-D representation of the environment in which the world is divided into a finite number of square cells, each cell storing an estimate of the probability that it is occupied by an obstacle. The posterior over the map is factorized as the product of per-cell posteriors under the cell-independence assumption.

**Advantages:**
1. It is **easy to fuse measurements from multiple time steps** using the recursive Bayes update.
2. It is **directly usable by path planners** (A\*, D\*) without further geometric processing.
3. It handles **sensor noise and uncertainty** explicitly through probability values.
4. It can be **visualized and updated in real time** in frameworks like ROS.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the "Inverse Sensor Model" in occupancy grid mapping? State its role.**

**Model Answer:**
The **inverse sensor model** $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ is the conditional probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied *given* a single sensor measurement $z_t$ and the robot's current pose $x_t$. It essentially *inverts* the standard sensor model $p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$ to ask the question: "If the sensor returned this reading, how likely is this cell to be occupied?"

**Role:** It supplies the log-odds increment used in the additive log-odds update for each cell along the sensor ray. It is the **bridge** between raw measurements and grid updates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat mathematical expressions, derive the **Bayesian update rule** for a single cell in an occupancy grid. Show how the **log-odds representation** is obtained from this rule and explain why it is preferred over the direct probabilistic form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1-D grid of 4 cells initialized with prior probability $p(m_i) = 0.5$ for all $i$. A sonar sensor reports a range $z_t = 4.0$ m, and the cells lie at distances $d = [1.0,\, 2.0,\, 3.0,\, 4.0]$ m from the robot along the beam axis. The inverse sensor model returns the values $I = [-0.5,\, -0.5,\, -0.5,\, +1.2]$ for cells 1, 2, 3, 4 respectively. **Compute the updated log-odds and the updated probabilities for each cell. Classify the cells** using the standard threshold. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

We begin with Bayes' theorem for a single cell:

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

Define the **odds** of occupancy as:

$$
o_i \;\equiv\; \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

Dividing the Bayes update for "occupied" by that for "free" gives the odds update:

$$
o_{i,t} \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t)} \;\times\; o_{i,t-1}
$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$
l_{i,t} \;=\; \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t)} \;+\; l_{i,t-1}
$$

Using the inverse sensor model notation, this is compactly:

$$
l_{i,t} \;=\; l_{i,t-1} \;+\; I_{i,t} \;-\; l_0
$$

**Why log-odds is preferred:**

1. **Additive updates** — every new measurement *adds* a constant; no product overflow.
2. **Numerical stability** — log-odds values lie in $(-\infty, +\infty)$; no probability underflow even after 10,000+ updates.
3. **Symmetric representation** — the value 0 corresponds to a 50/50 belief, positive values to "more likely occupied," and negative to "more likely free."
4. **Efficient storage** — a single float (4 bytes) per cell, regardless of how many updates have been made.

#### Model Solution to (b)

Initial log-odds: $l_{i,0} = 0$ for all $i$ (since $p = 0.5$ gives $\log \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 0$). Prior $l_0 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Updated log-odds** $l_{i,1} = l_{i,0} + I_i - l_0 = I_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1 &= -0.5 \\
l_2 &= -0.5 \\
l_3 &= -0.5 \\
l_4 &= +1.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recover probabilities** $p_i = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 0.6065} \;=\; 1 - 0.6225 \;=\; 0.3775 \\
p_2 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}} \;=\; 0.3775 \\
p_3 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}} \;=\; 0.3775 \\
p_4 &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{+1.2}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 3.3201} \;=\; 1 - 0.2315 \;=\; 0.7685
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Classification** (using threshold $p \ge 0.65$ occupied, $p \le 0.35$ free):

| Cell | $l_i$ | $p_i$ | Class |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $-0.5$ | $0.3775$ | Unknown |
| 2 | $-0.5$ | $0.3775$ | Unknown |
| 3 | $-0.5$ | $0.3775$ | Unknown |
| 4 | $+1.2$ | $0.7685$ | **Occupied** |

**Engineering insight:** The sonar correctly identified the obstacle at 4.0 m, but the three free-space cells did not yet accumulate enough evidence to cross the "free" threshold. Multiple observations over time would push them toward $p \approx 0$.

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **metric (occupancy grid) maps** and **topological maps** with respect to representation, memory, suitability for path planning, and noise robustness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step algorithm** of occupancy grid mapping for a range-bearing sensor. Include a block diagram in textual form. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Criterion | Metric (Occupancy Grid) Map | Topological Map |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | 2-D array of probabilistic cells | Graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) |
| **Memory** | $O(N)$ where $N = W \times H$; can be very large (millions of cells) | $O(V + E)$ where $V$ = nodes, $E$ = edges; compact |
| **Resolution** | Fixed; tied to grid cell size $r$ | Does not store geometry; only connectivity |
| **Path planning** | Direct use of A\*, D\*, wavefront planners | Use of graph search (DFS, BFS, Dijkstra) |
| **Noise robustness** | Naturally handles noisy sensors via Bayes fusion | Requires reliable place recognition; sensitive to perceptual aliasing |
| **Scale suitability** | Best for small/medium indoor spaces | Best for large, structured environments (warehouses, buildings) |
| **Localization** | Suits fine-grained Monte Carlo localization | Suits global topological localization |
| **Human readability** | Visual map of free/occupied space | High-level symbolic map |

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Algorithm: Occupancy Grid Mapping (Range-Bearing Sensor)**

1. **Initialize** all grid cells $m_i$ to prior log-odds $l_0 = 0$ (i.e., $p = 0.5$).
2. **Acquire** new sensor scan $z_t = \{z_t^1, z_t^2, \dots, z_t^K\}$ and robot pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$.
3. **For each beam** $z_t^k$ in the scan:
   - (a) Compute the beam direction in the world frame: $\phi_k = \theta + \theta_{\text{offset},k}$.
   - (b) Trace the ray from $(x, y)$ along direction $\phi_k$ using **Bresenham's line algorithm** (or its analog for floating-point grids), producing a list of cells $\{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{M}\}$ lying on the ray.
   - (c) For each cell on the ray, evaluate the **inverse sensor model** $I(c_j, z_t^k, x_t)$:
     - Cells before the hit: use the *free* log-odds (e.g., $I = -0.85$).
     - Cell at the hit: use the *occupied* log-odds (e.g., $I = +0.85$).
     - Cells beyond the hit: no update (or revert to 0.5).
   - (d) Update the cell's log-odds: $l_{c_j} \leftarrow l_{c_j} + I - l_0$.
4. **After** all beams are processed, **optionally** clip the log-odds to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$ (e.g., $[-2, +2]$) to bound outdated cells.
5. **Render** the map by computing $p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$ for every cell.
6. **Repeat** from step 2 as the robot moves.

**Textual block diagram:**

```
[Pose x_t]  +  [Scan z_t] --> [Ray Tracer] --> [Cell List] --> [Inverse Sensor Model]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
                                                                  [Log-Odds Update]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
                                                                  [Grid Map l_i]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
                                                                  [Inverse Logit]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
                                                                       [Display]
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Mapping — Module 1):**
> 1. **Forgetting the cell-independence assumption.** If a student writes $p(m)$ as a joint distribution without factorizing it into $\prod p(m_i)$, the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks for missing the foundational assumption.
> 2. **Mixing up the sensor model with the inverse sensor model.** $p(z \mid m, x)$ is the forward model; $p(m \mid z, x)$ is the inverse model. This conflation costs the Bayesian-derivation sub-question completely.
> 3. **Sign errors in the log-odds update.** Free-space cells must receive a *negative* increment; occupied cells a *positive* increment. Reversing these signs inverts the entire map.
> 4. **Not clipping log-odds.** Real implementations clip $l_i \in [-2, +2]$ to prevent stale cells from saturating. Not mentioning this loses an applied/engineering mark.
> 5. **Confusing "topological" with "semantic" maps.** Topological maps store connectivity (graph), not object labels. Semantic maps add object-level annotations; this is a *Module 3 or 4* topic in KTU 2024.
> 6. **Forgetting the threshold for classification.** When asked to "classify the cells," students often just list probabilities without stating the threshold rule explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mapping** = estimating the posterior $p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ over the environment from sensor and odometry data.
- **Three map families:** metric (grid), topological (graph), hybrid (grid + graph).
- **Occupancy grid** factorizes the joint into per-cell posteriors under the cell-independence assumption.
- **Bayes update** per cell: $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) \propto p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})$.
- **Log-odds** $l_i = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$ convert multiplication into **addition** for numerical stability.
- **Log-odds update:** $l_{i,t} = l_{i,t-1} + I_{i,t} - l_0$, where $I_{i,t}$ is the inverse sensor model.
- **Inverse logit:** $p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$ to display the map.
- **Inverse sensor model** assigns a value to every cell along a ray: free cells negative, hit cell positive, beyond-hit cells zero.
- **Decision threshold:** typically $p \ge 0.65$ for occupied, $p \le 0.35$ for free; in-between is "unknown."
- **Grid resolution $r$** trades memory vs. precision: $r = 0.05$ m (precise) vs. $r = 0.30$ m (coarse).
- **Number of cells:** $N = \lceil L_x / r \rceil \times \lceil L_y / r \rceil$.
- **Clipping** log-odds to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$ (e.g., $[-2, +2]$) is standard practice in real ROS implementations.
- **Topological maps** scale better but require reliable place recognition; **metric maps** are precise but memory-heavy.
- **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) extends mapping to the case where the robot pose $x_t$ is itself unknown — a Module 2 / 3 topic in KTU 2024.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Localization and Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is a fundamental computational problem in mobile robotics wherein a robot is required to construct a global, consistent map of an unknown environment **while simultaneously estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that very map being built**. 

The problem is formally defined within the framework of **probabilistic robotics** as estimating the joint posterior probability distribution over the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$ given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_t\}$ denotes the sequence of robot poses up to time $t$
- $m = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n\}$ denotes the set of all landmark locations in the map
- $z_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of sensor observations
- $u_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of control commands (odometry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SLAM is classified as a "chicken-and-egg" or "correlational" problem because a good map is needed for localization, while an accurate pose estimate is required to build the map. Neither can be solved independently.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Explorer in a Dark Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine being blindfolded and placed in a completely dark, unfamiliar room. You are given a cane (sensor) and a pedometer (odometry). To navigate, you must *simultaneously* figure out:
> 1. **Where the furniture is** (build a map), and
> 2. **Where you currently stand** (localize yourself).
> 
> Every step you take using your pedometer accumulates small errors. Every time your cane touches a chair leg, you record its location, but you don't know exactly where *you* are. As you return to previously touched furniture, you recognize it ("loop closure") and realize, "Ah! I've been here before — my map was wrong, and so was my position estimate." You then adjust both simultaneously. This is SLAM.

## 1.3 Historical Context and Significance

SLAM was pioneered in the **1986 IEEE Robotics and Automation Conference** at San Francisco, where Cheeseman, Smith, and Durrant-Whyte formulated it as a state estimation problem. Over the past three decades, SLAM has evolved from a theoretical curiosity into the backbone of autonomous navigation, deployed in:

| Application Domain | SLAM Use-Case | Key Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Urban mapping & GPS-denied nav | LiDAR, Camera, Radar |
| Domestic Robotics (e.g., Roomba) | Floor plan reconstruction | RGB-D Camera, IR |
| Aerial Drones (UAV) | Indoor inspection | Stereo Camera, IMU |
| Underwater ROVs | Seabed cartography | Sonar, DVL |
| AR/VR (Apple ARKit, Google ARCore) | Visual-Inertial SLAM | Camera + IMU |

## 1.4 The Two Sub-Problems Decomposed

**Localization** $\rightarrow$ Given a map $m$, estimate pose $x_t$:
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

**Mapping** $\rightarrow$ Given a trajectory $x_{1:t}$, build map $m$:
$$p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SLAM solves them **jointly** because the two distributions are statistically coupled — uncertainty in the trajectory inflates map uncertainty, and vice versa, creating a complex correlated covariance structure that is the technical heart of the SLAM problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Formulation — Why Probability Theory?

Both robot motion and sensor measurements are **noisy stochastic processes**. Deterministic geometry fails because errors compound. SLAM therefore casts the problem as a **Bayesian recursive state estimation** problem. The state evolves via two fundamental models:

### 2.1.1 Motion Model (State Transition)

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the (nonlinear) kinematic model and $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ is **process noise** with covariance $R_t$.

### 2.1.2 Observation Model (Measurement)

$$z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$$

where $h(\cdot)$ is the sensor model and $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ is **measurement noise** with covariance $Q_t$.

## 2.2 The SLAM Bayesian Filter — Operational Logic

The full SLAM posterior is computed recursively in two steps:

**Step 1 — Prediction (Time Update):** Propagate state using the motion model.
$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 2 — Correction (Measurement Update):** Fuse current sensor reading via Bayes' rule.
$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\, p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 Scheme high-priority** for ESE (End Semester Examination) questions. Memorize the symbols and their units.

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimension / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Robot pose at time $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^3$ (2D: $[x, y, \theta]^T$) |
| $m_i$ | Location of $i$-th landmark | $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$ |
| $u_t$ | Control / odometry input | $\mathbb{R}^2$ (linear, angular vel) |
| $z_t$ | Sensor measurement at time $t$ | Varies (range, bearing, image) |
| $R_t$ | Process noise covariance | Positive semi-definite matrix |
| $Q_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | Positive definite matrix |
| $G_t$ | Jacobian of motion model $\partial f / \partial x$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| $H_t$ | Jacobian of observation model $\partial h / \partial x$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ |
| $K_t$ | Kalman Gain | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| $P_t$ | State covariance matrix | Symmetric positive definite |
| $\Sigma_{ij}$ | Cross-covariance between pose $i$ and map $j$ | Sub-block of $P$ |
| $\mu_t$ | State mean vector | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Gaussian distribution | — |

## 2.4 Key SLAM Equations (Exam-Critical Summary)

| Equation Name | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **EKF SLAM Prediction (Mean)** | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Propagate pose estimate |
| **EKF SLAM Prediction (Cov.)** | $\bar{P}_t = G_t P_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$ | Propagate uncertainty |
| **Innovation (Residual)** | $y_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ | Compute measurement error |
| **Innovation Covariance** | $S_t = H_t \bar{P}_t H_t^T + Q_t$ | Spread of residual |
| **Kalman Gain** | $K_t = \bar{P}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}$ | Optimal blending weight |
| **EKF Update (Mean)** | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t y_t$ | Corrected state |
| **EKF Update (Cov.)** | $P_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{P}_t$ | Corrected covariance |
| **Observation Range** | $z_t^i = \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y_t)^2} + v_r$ | Range-bearing sensor |
| **Observation Bearing** | $\theta_t^i = \text{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y_t,\; m_{i,x} - x_t) - \theta_t + v_\theta$ | Bearing model |
| **Jacobian of Observation** | $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial (x, y, m_{i,x}, m_{i,y})}$ | Linearization |

## 2.5 The Famous EKF SLAM Property: Monotonically Decreasing Landmark Uncertainty

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Dissanayake et al., 2001):** In EKF SLAM, the determinant of the submatrix $\Sigma_{mm}$ corresponding to landmark locations is monotonically non-increasing as more observations are made. In the limit, landmarks become **perfectly correlated** with the robot's pose — this is why the full SLAM covariance matrix is dense (unlike pure localization, where $P$ is sparse).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** LiDAR SLAM enables centimeter-level localization in GPS-denied tunnels.
- **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** SLAM-based maps allow dynamic re-routing.
- **Search & Rescue:** SLAM-equipped ground/aerial robots map disaster zones in real-time.
- **Planetary Exploration (NASA Mars Rovers):** Visual SLAM is critical for traversing unknown Martian terrain where communication delay prevents tele-operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Recursive SLAM Posterior

We begin with the full joint posterior and factor it using the **Markov assumption** (current state depends only on previous state and current control).

**Step 1: Apply Bayes' rule to the joint posterior.**
$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_{1:t}, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 2: Apply the measurement Markov assumption** (current observation depends only on current state and map).
$$p(z_t \mid x_{1:t}, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step 3: Apply the motion Markov assumption** to factor the prediction step.
$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 4: Combine to obtain the recursive Bayes filter for SLAM.**
$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$$

where $\eta$ is the normalization constant $1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$. This is the **fundamental SLAM recursion**.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) SLAM Update Equations

The Bayes recursion above is **intractable in closed form** for general nonlinear $f$ and $h$. EKF SLAM solves this by linearizing via first-order Taylor expansion and assuming Gaussian noise.

### Step A — Motion Model Linearization

Given the nonlinear motion model $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$, we linearize around the current estimate $(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$:

$$F_x = \left. \frac{\partial f(x_{t-1}, u_t)}{\partial x_{t-1}} \right|_{x_{t-1} = \mu_{t-1}}$$

The first-order approximation is:
$$x_t \approx f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + F_x (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t$$

### Step B — Predicted State Mean
Taking the expectation of both sides:
$$\bar{\mu}_t = E[x_t] = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

### Step C — Predicted State Covariance
Compute $P_t = E[(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)^T]$ by substituting the linearized expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= E[(F_x(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t)(F_x(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t)^T] \\
&= F_x \, E[(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})^T] \, F_x^T + E[w_t w_t^T] \\
\bar{P}_t &= F_x \, P_{t-1} \, F_x^T + R_t
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **EKF prediction equation for SLAM**.

### Step D — Observation Model Linearization

For landmark observation $z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$, we compute two Jacobians (w.r.t. robot pose and w.r.t. the observed landmark $m_i$):

$$H_x = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t} \right|_{(x_t, m_i) = (\bar{\mu}_t, m_i)} \quad , \quad H_m = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial m_i} \right|_{(x_t, m_i) = (\bar{\mu}_t, m_i)}$$

The linearized observation is:
$$z_t \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i) + H_x (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) + H_m (m_i - \hat{m}_i) + v_t$$

### Step E — Innovation Computation

The **innovation** (or measurement residual) measures the discrepancy between the actual sensor reading and what the model predicts:

$$y_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i)$$

### Step F — Innovation Covariance
The covariance of the innovation is obtained by propagating state uncertainty through the linearized observation model and adding sensor noise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_t &= E[y_t y_t^T] = H_x \, \bar{P}_t \, H_x^T + Q_t
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: in the full SLAM state, $H$ is a wide matrix acting on the full state $[x; m]$, so $S_t = H \bar{P}_t H^T + Q_t$.)

### Step G — Kalman Gain

The Kalman gain is derived by minimizing the **posterior covariance trace** $\text{tr}(P_t)$. Setting $\partial \text{tr}(P_t)/\partial K_t = 0$ yields:

$$K_t = \bar{P}_t H^T S_t^{-1}$$

### Step H — Updated State Mean and Covariance

The state is corrected by the Kalman-weighted innovation:
$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t y_t$$

The covariance update follows from the matrix identity for optimal fusion:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= (I - K_t H) \bar{P}_t (I - K_t H)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T \\
&= (I - K_t H) \bar{P}_t \quad \text{(simplified, equivalent form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The second (Joseph) form is numerically more stable and used in production SLAM stacks (e.g., Cartographer, ORB-SLAM).

## 3.3 Derivation: Observation Model Jacobians for a Range-Bearing Sensor

For a 2D landmark $m_i = [m_{i,x}, m_{i,y}]^T$ observed by a robot at pose $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$, the range-bearing model is:

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{i,x}-x)^2 + (m_{i,y}-y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{i,y}-y,\; m_{i,x}-x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} + v_t$$

Let $q = (m_{i,x}-x)^2 + (m_{i,y}-y)^2$. The Jacobian with respect to the robot pose is:

$$H_x = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{m_{i,x}-x}{\sqrt{q}} & -\frac{m_{i,y}-y}{\sqrt{q}} & 0 \\ \frac{m_{i,y}-y}{q} & -\frac{m_{i,x}-x}{q} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

And the Jacobian with respect to the landmark is:
$$H_m = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{m_{i,x}-x}{\sqrt{q}} & \frac{m_{i,y}-y}{\sqrt{q}} \\ -\frac{m_{i,y}-y}{q} & \frac{m_{i,x}-x}{q} \end{bmatrix}$$

These Jacobians are placed into the full SLAM Jacobian $H$ at the appropriate columns corresponding to the robot pose and the $i$-th landmark.

## 3.4 Computational Complexity of EKF SLAM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** The state dimension grows linearly with the number of landmarks $N$. The covariance update requires inverting $S_t$, which is $O(N^3)$ per landmark. **Total complexity is $O(N^2)$ per step** for sparse exploitation, but naive EKF SLAM is $O(N^3)$, making it infeasible beyond a few thousand landmarks.

| Algorithm | Time per Update | Memory | Max Practical Landmarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| EKF SLAM | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N^2)$ | $\sim 1{,}000$ |
| FastSLAM (Particle) | $O(M \log N)$ | $O(M N)$ | $\sim 10^5$ |
| Graph-SLAM (sparse) | $O(N^{1.5})$ | $O(N)$ | $\sim 10^6$ |
| ORB-SLAM3 (visual) | $O(\log N)$ | $O(N)$ | $\sim 10^5$ |

## 3.5 Loop Closure — The Heart of SLAM

A **loop closure** occurs when the robot re-observes a previously seen landmark, creating a measurement that ties two distant poses together. Mathematically, the cross-covariance between the current pose and the past pose becomes non-zero, and the Kalman update propagates information backward through the entire trajectory graph, **globally correcting** the map.

Without loop closure, EKF SLAM is essentially dead-reckoning with a growing error spiral.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level SLAM Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional data flow** of a generic EKF-SLAM system, mapping how sensor and control inputs interact with the prediction-update loop to maintain a consistent pose-and-map estimate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Pose x0 and Map m0] --> B[Control Input u_t from Odometry]
    A --> C[Sensor Reading z_t from LiDAR or Camera]

    B --> D[Prediction Stage: Motion Model f x u]
    C --> E[Data Association: Match z_t to existing landmarks]

    D --> F[Predicted State Mean mu_bar and Covariance P_bar]
    E --> G{Match Found in Map}

    G -- Yes --> H[Observation Model h x m and Jacobian H]
    G -- No --> I[Landmark Initialization: Add new landmark m_new to map]

    H --> J[Innovation y_t and Innovation Cov S_t]
    I --> F
    J --> K[Kalman Gain K_t = P_bar H^T S^-1]

    K --> L[State Update: mu_t = mu_bar + K_t y_t]
    K --> M[Covariance Update: P_t = I minus K_t H times P_bar]

    L --> N[Updated Robot Pose x_t]
    M --> O[Updated Map m_t with new landmark states]
    N --> P[Loop Closure Detection via Map Matching]
    O --> P
    P -- Loop Detected --> Q[Global Pose Graph Optimization and Map Correction]
    P -- No Loop --> R[Output: Pose Estimate and Occupancy Grid Map]

    Q --> R
    R --> S[Navigation Planner: Path Planning and Obstacle Avoidance]
    S --> B
```

## 4.2 SLAM State Vector and Covariance Structure

The following diagram illustrates the **structural anatomy** of the full SLAM state vector and its dense covariance matrix. The off-diagonal blocks $\Sigma_{xm}$ and $\Sigma_{mx}$ encode the strong correlation between robot pose and map — this is what makes SLAM a single coupled estimation problem rather than two independent ones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATE_VECTOR
        R[R: Robot Pose 3x1]
        M1[M1: Landmark 1 2x1]
        M2[M2: Landmark 2 2x1]
        Mn[Mn: Landmark n 2x1]
    end

    subgraph COVARIANCE_P
        SUB1[Sigma_rr 3x3 Pose Covariance]
        SUB2[Sigma_rm1 3x2 Pose to Landmark 1]
        SUB3[Sigma_rm2 3x2 Pose to Landmark 2]
        SUB4[Sigma_m1m1 2x2 Landmark 1 Self]
        SUB5[Sigma_m1m2 2x2 Cross Correlation]
        SUB6[Sigma_mnm2 2x2 Landmark n to Landmark 2]
    end

    R -.-> SUB1
    R -.-> SUB2
    R -.-> SUB3
    M1 -.-> SUB2
    M1 -.-> SUB4
    M1 -.-> SUB5
    M2 -.-> SUB3
    M2 -.-> SUB6
    Mn -.-> SUB4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Takeaway:** Notice that landmarks are correlated with *each other* (off-diagonal blocks like $\Sigma_{m_1 m_2}$). This is the unique signature of SLAM: observing a new landmark updates not just your pose, but the *position of every previously seen landmark*. This is why SLAM covariance matrices become dense.

## 4.3 SLAM Algorithm Variants — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SLAM[SLAM Algorithms]

    SLAM --> PF[Probabilistic Filters]
    SLAM --> GP[Graph Based Optimization]
    SLAM --> PFAM[Particle Filter Family]
    SLAM --> VS[Visual SLAM Variants]

    PF --> EKF[Extended Kalman Filter SLAM]
    PF --> UKF[Unscented Kalman Filter SLAM]
    PF --> IF[Information Filter SLAM]

    PFAM --> FAST[FastSLAM 1.0]
    PFAM --> FAST2[FastSLAM 2.0]

    GP --> TORO[Toro and Tree based Optimizer]
    GP --> G2O[g2o General Graph Optimization]
    GP --> CERES[Ceres Solver for Bundle Adjustment]

    VS --> MONO[Monocular ORB SLAM]
    VS --> STEREO[Stereo ORB SLAM]
    VS --> RGBD[RGBD SLAM with Kinect]
    VS --> LDS[LSD SLAM Large Scale Direct]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Why is it called a "chicken-and-egg" problem?**

**Model Answer:**
SLAM is the computational problem of concurrently building a map of an unknown environment and estimating the robot's pose within that map, using noisy sensor and odometry data. It is expressed probabilistically as estimating $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. It is called a "chicken-and-egg" problem because localization requires an accurate map, while mapping requires an accurate pose estimate — neither can be solved independently, and the uncertainties are mathematically coupled through the joint covariance matrix. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between EKF SLAM and FastSLAM in terms of computational complexity and representation of uncertainty.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | EKF SLAM | FastSLAM |
|---|---|---|
| Uncertainty Model | Single Gaussian over full state | Rao-Blackwellized: particles for paths, Gaussians per landmark |
| Complexity per step | $O(N^2)$ | $O(M \log N)$ |
| Scalability | Poor (dense covariance) | Excellent (up to $10^5$ landmarks) |
| Multi-modal handling | No (single hypothesis) | Yes (particle set) |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the EKF SLAM prediction equations for the joint robot–map state, starting from the Bayes filter recursion. Clearly state the Markov assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D differential-drive robot observing a point landmark with a range-bearing sensor, derive the observation model and its Jacobians with respect to both the robot pose and the landmark. Discuss why these Jacobians are essential for EKF SLAM. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Bayes Filter Recursion for SLAM:**
We begin with the joint posterior. Applying Bayes' rule:
$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_{1:t}, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Markov Assumptions:**
- *Measurement Markov:* $p(z_t \mid x_{1:t}, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$
- *Motion Markov:* $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$

**Step 3 — Recursive SLAM Posterior:**
$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{1:t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 4 — Linearize the Motion Model:**
With $G_t = \partial f / \partial x$ evaluated at $\mu_{t-1}$:
$$x_t \approx f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t$$

**Step 5 — Predicted Mean and Covariance:**
$$\bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$
$$\bar{P}_t = G_t P_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

The covariance $P_{t-1}$ here is the full $(3+2N) \times (3+2N)$ matrix encompassing both pose and all map landmarks.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Range-Bearing Observation Model:**
For robot at $[x, y, \theta]^T$ and landmark at $[m_x, m_y]^T$:

$$r = \sqrt{(m_x - x)^2 + (m_y - y)^2} + v_r$$
$$\phi = \text{atan2}(m_y - y, m_x - x) - \theta + v_\phi$$

**Step 2 — Jacobian with respect to Robot Pose $(x, y, \theta)$:**
Let $q = (m_x - x)^2 + (m_y - y)^2$. Then:

$$H_x = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{m_x - x}{\sqrt{q}} & -\frac{m_y - y}{\sqrt{q}} & 0 \\ \frac{m_y - y}{q} & -\frac{m_x - x}{q} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Jacobian with respect to Landmark $(m_x, m_y)$:**
$$H_m = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{m_x - x}{\sqrt{q}} & \frac{m_y - y}{\sqrt{q}} \\ -\frac{m_y - y}{q} & \frac{m_x - x}{q} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Why These Jacobians are Essential:**
EKF SLAM operates only on **linear** systems. The true observation model $h(x_t, m_i)$ is nonlinear in both pose and landmark. The Jacobians $H_x$ and $H_m$ provide a **first-order Taylor approximation** around the current estimate, allowing the Kalman update $K_t = \bar{P}_t H^T S_t^{-1}$ to be computed analytically. Without these Jacobians, the EKF cannot fuse the observation into the joint state. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the role of **data association** in SLAM. Discuss the **nearest neighbor** and **joint compatibility branch and bound (JCBB)** methods, and their impact on filter consistency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **loop closure** in SLAM? Describe the pose-graph optimization approach to correct accumulated drift when a loop is detected, and explain why it is essential for long-duration missions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Data Association — Definition:**
Data association is the process of determining **which landmark in the map corresponds to the current sensor measurement**. It is a critical sub-problem because an incorrect association injects false information into the EKF, causing the filter to diverge catastrophically.

**Nearest Neighbor (NN) Method:**
- Compute the Mahalanobis distance $d_j^2 = y_t^T S_t^{-1} y_t$ for each candidate landmark $j$.
- Pick the landmark with the smallest $d_j^2$ *iff* it passes a $\chi^2$ gating threshold.
- **Drawback:** Greedy and can fail in cluttered environments, leading to false positives.

**Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB):**
- Considers **all possible joint hypotheses** over a set of measurements simultaneously.
- Uses branch-and-bound search to find the hypothesis that is *jointly* consistent with the current state.
- **Advantage:** Far more robust in ambiguous scenes; reduces false associations.
- **Drawback:** Computationally expensive, exponential in worst case.

**Impact on Consistency:**
Wrong data association introduces a non-Gaussian bias that the EKF cannot model, causing the **estimated covariance to become over-optimistic** (the filter "thinks" it is more certain than it is). JCBB maintains consistency better than NN by rejecting ambiguous matches. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Loop Closure Definition:**
A loop closure occurs when the robot **re-observes a region previously mapped**, providing a measurement that constrains the current pose relative to an earlier pose.

**Pose-Graph Optimization Approach:**
1. Build a **graph** where nodes = robot poses $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T\}$ and edges = relative pose constraints from odometry or landmark matches.
2. Define an objective function summing squared Mahalanobis residuals over all edges:
$$x^* = \arg\min_x \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} \left( z_{ij} - \hat{z}_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \right)^T \Omega_{ij} \left( z_{ij} - \hat{z}_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \right)$$
3. When a loop is closed (edge between $x_i$ and $x_j$ with $\vert i - j \vert \gg 1$), the optimizer **redistributes error** across all intermediate poses, correcting accumulated odometric drift.
4. Solved iteratively using **Gauss-Newton** or **Levenberg-Marquardt** methods.

**Why Essential for Long Missions:**
Without loop closure, odometry drift grows **unbounded** (typically 1–10% of distance traveled). For a robot traversing 1 km, this could be 10–100 m of error. Loop closure provides the only mechanism to **globally bound** this error, making long-duration autonomy feasible. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Localization and SLAM posteriors:** Localization conditions on a *known* map $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, m)$, while SLAM jointly estimates both $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Writing the wrong conditional costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always linearize correctly:** EKF Jacobians $F_x$ and $H$ must be evaluated at the **current estimate**, not symbolic variables. Writing $\partial f / \partial x$ without the evaluation point is incomplete.
> 3. **Forgetting noise covariances $R_t$ and $Q_t$:** Omitting them in the prediction and update equations is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing landmark initialization with measurement update:** When a *new* landmark is first seen, you must initialize it in the state via the inverse observation model; the standard Kalman update only applies to *known* landmarks.
> 5. **Misstating complexity:** EKF SLAM is $O(N^2)$, not $O(N)$. Writing $O(N)$ loses a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **SLAM definition:** Joint estimation of robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and map $m$ from sensors $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$ — written as $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- **"Chicken-and-egg"** nature arises because map and pose are mutually dependent and correlated.
- **Two Markov assumptions** underpin the SLAM Bayes filter: measurement Markov and motion Markov.
- **EKF SLAM** linearizes $f(\cdot)$ and $h(\cdot)$ using Jacobians $F_x$, $H_x$, $H_m$ — assumes Gaussian noise.
- **Prediction equations:** $\bar{\mu}_t = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ and $\bar{P}_t = F_x P_{t-1} F_x^T + R_t$.
- **Update equations:** Innovation $y_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$, Kalman gain $K_t = \bar{P}_t H^T S_t^{-1}$, updated state $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t y_t$, updated covariance $P_t = (I - K_t H)\bar{P}_t$.
- **Range-bearing model:** $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ and $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$.
- **EKF SLAM complexity:** $O(N^2)$ per step, $O(N^2)$ memory — limits scalability to $\sim 10^3$ landmarks.
- **Data association:** NN is fast but fragile; JCBB is robust but expensive. Wrong association → filter divergence.
- **Loop closure:** Re-observing a known landmark constrains distant poses, bounding drift.
- **Pose-graph optimization:** Minimizes sum of Mahalanobis residuals over edges; solved via Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt.
- **FastSLAM** uses Rao-Blackwellized particles — $O(M \log N)$ — scales to $10^5$ landmarks.
- **Visual SLAM** (ORB-SLAM3, VINS-Mono) is the modern standard for camera-equipped robots.
- **Monotonic landmark uncertainty:** As observations accumulate, landmark positions become increasingly correlated with robot pose — proven by Dissanayake et al.
- **Sensor types used in SLAM:** Wheel odometry, IMU, LiDAR, monocular/stereo/RGB-D camera, GPS (outdoor).
- **Open-source SLAM stacks to know:** GMapping, Cartographer, ORB-SLAM3, RTAB-Map, LIO-SAM.
- **Common exam traps:** forgetting normalization constant $\eta$, swapping prediction/update order, using $H$ instead of $H_x$, claiming EKF SLAM is $O(N)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locomotion: Introduction to Stepper Motor and Servo Motor Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Locomotion: Introduction to Stepper Motor and Servo Motor Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Locomotion in Mobile Robotics — The Foundational Definition

**Locomotion** in the context of mobile robotics refers to the mechanism and actuation strategy that enables a robot to physically move through its environment. It is the actuator-level interface between the robot's *control intelligence* (software/path planner) and its *physical interaction* with the world (wheels, legs, tracks). In KTU 2024 Module 1, locomotion is studied as the *physical realization* layer — converting electrical energy and digital commands into controlled rotational or linear motion of the robot's drive joints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** Locomotion is treated as the **"mechatronics primer"** of mobile robotics — the building block upon which higher-level concepts (kinematics, perception, planning) are constructed. Mastery of actuator fundamentals (Stepper + Servo) is a prerequisite for ALL subsequent modules.

### 1.2 The Stepper Motor — Formal Definition

A **Stepper Motor** (also called a *step motor* or *stepping motor*) is a **brushless, synchronous electric motor** that converts a train of digital input pulses into discrete angular mechanical rotations (called *steps*). Each incoming pulse — typically a **square-wave pulse of 3 V to 24 V** — causes the rotor to advance by one *exact* angular increment called the **step angle** $\theta_s$.

Mathematically:
$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$

where $N_s$ is the number of steps per revolution (e.g., 200 steps/rev → $\theta_s = 1.8°$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike a conventional DC motor which rotates *continuously* in response to applied voltage, a stepper motor moves in **discrete quantized steps**. It is, therefore, a *digital actuator* — naturally suited for digital/open-loop command streams from a microcontroller.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Tick-Tock Clock of Robotics"

Imagine a **vintage analog clock's second hand** — it doesn't glide smoothly; it *ticks* one second at a time. Each tick is a precise, repeatable step. Now, suppose you could send a signal — say, by pressing a button — to advance the hand by *exactly one tick*.
