The button pulse is your digital command; the tick is the stepper motor's mechanical response. If you press the button 60 times, the hand completes a full minute (one revolution). 

If the clock hand was somehow *attached to a robot's wheel*, the robot's motion would be perfectly measurable: **1 pulse = 1 known angle of wheel rotation = 1 known distance travelled.** This is exactly why stepper motors dominated early mobile robotics — they give you **free odometry** without needing an encoder.

### 1.3 The Servo Motor — Formal Definition

A **Servo Motor** (commonly, in hobbyist/robotics contexts, a *RC Servo* or *positional servo*) is a **closed-loop rotary actuator** consisting of four integral components packaged in a single housing:

1. A small **DC motor** (the prime mover)
2. A **gearbox** (torque amplification + speed reduction)
3. A **potentiometer** (rotary position sensor — the feedback element)
4. A **control circuit board** (compares commanded vs. actual position)

The servo motor accepts a **Pulse-Width Modulated (PWM) command signal** at a standard frequency of **50 Hz** (period $T = 20$ ms), where the *pulse width* encodes the desired output shaft position. Typical pulse widths:

- **1.0 ms** → $0°$ (one extreme)
- **1.5 ms** → $90°$ (center/neutral)
- **2.0 ms** → $180°$ (other extreme)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A hobby "servo" is technically a *servo mechanism* (a closed-loop position-control system), not a "servo motor" in the industrial sense. Industrial servo motors (used in CNC, robotic arms) are larger, use brushless AC/DC designs, and accept analog or fieldbus commands. Both share the **closed-loop feedback principle**.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control of Rotation"

Think of a **car's power window**. You press the button ("go up" or "go down"). You *don't* directly turn the motor — the car's electronics figure out how long to spin, in which direction, and stop exactly when the window reaches the top or bottom (using current sensing or position sensors). 

Now imagine the button could command **any position** (not just fully up or fully down) — say, halfway. The car's electronics continuously check "where is the window now?" via a sensor, compare it to "where should it be?", and adjust the motor's spinning direction and duration accordingly until they match. That **continuous comparison → correction loop** is exactly what a servo motor does — except at millisecond speeds and sub-degree precision.

### 1.4 Why Stepper and Servo? — Relevance to Mobile Robotics

Both motors are ubiquitous in mobile robotics because they solve the same fundamental problem: **precise, controllable mechanical motion from electrical signals**.

| Robotic Subsystem | Typical Actuator | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel rotation (drive) | **DC Gear Motor + Encoder** (not stepper/servo) | High-speed, continuous rotation |
| Steering mechanism of car-like robot | **Servo Motor** | Limited-angle precise pivoting |
| Robotic arm joint | **Servo Motor** (hobby) or **Industrial Servo** (pro) | Torque + closed-loop precision |
| Pan-Tilt camera gimbal | **Servo Motors (×2)** | Bidirectional, locked-angle holding |
| CNC/3D-printer on mobile platform | **Stepper Motor** | Open-loop, accurate position holding |
| Leg-joint in small hexapod | **Micro Servo (SG90/MG90S)** | Compact, high torque, low cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
> - Standard servo PWM frequency: **$f_{PWM} = 50$ Hz** → period $T = 20$ ms
> - Standard stepper step resolutions: $1.8°$ (200 steps/rev), $0.9°$ (400 steps/rev), $7.5°$ (48 steps/rev)
> - Common supply voltages: Stepper drivers **12 V – 48 V**; Servo **4.8 V – 7.4 V**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stepper Motor — Internal Architecture & Theory of Operation

#### 2.1.1 Three Constructional Variants

| Type | Rotor | Stator | Step Angle Range | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron toothed rotor (no magnet) | Multi-toothed, energized coils | $5° - 15°$ | Low inertia, no detent torque, rare in robotics |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially-magnetized cylindrical magnet | Two/four salient-pole stator | $45° - 90°$ | Good detent torque, coarse steps, cheap |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | Toothed iron + axially-magnetized magnet (two cups) | Toothed stator | $0.9° - 1.8°$ | High resolution + high torque → **industry standard** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** Most exam questions are based on the **Permanent Magnet** and **Hybrid** types. The hybrid type combines the *detent torque* of PM with the *fine resolution* of VR.

#### 2.1.2 The Electromagnetic Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Stator energization:** A driver circuit energizes one (or two adjacent) stator phase coils based on a predefined *commutation sequence*.
2. **Magnetic field alignment:** The energized phase generates a magnetic flux that aligns the rotor's magnetic axis with the *minimum reluctance path* between stator pole teeth.
3. **Mechanical step:** The rotor physically rotates by exactly $\theta_s$ to reach this equilibrium.
4. **Pulse advance:** The driver de-energizes the current phase and energizes the *next* phase in sequence → rotor advances by another $\theta_s$.
5. **Direction control:** Reversing the sequence (advancing vs. retarding through the commutation table) reverses rotation.

#### 2.1.3 Commutation Sequences — The Four-Step Cycle

For a **2-phase, 4-step motor**, the standard *full-step* sequence is:

| Step | Phase A | Phase B | Phase C | Phase D |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | ON  | OFF | OFF | OFF |
| 2 | OFF | ON  | OFF | OFF |
| 3 | OFF | OFF | ON  | OFF |
| 4 | OFF | OFF | OFF | ON  |

This is the **wave-drive (one-phase-on)** mode. The **full-step (two-phase-on)** mode energizes two phases simultaneously, doubling the torque but consuming twice the power. The **half-step mode** alternates between these, yielding $400$ steps/rev for a motor rated $200$ steps/rev at full-step.

#### 2.1.4 Why Open-Loop is Possible (And Where It Fails)

Because the stepper's angular displacement is **deterministically tied** to the input pulse count, *no feedback sensor is required* for positioning. This is its chief advantage — simplicity. However, if the **load torque exceeds the motor's holding torque** *and* the step rate exceeds the motor's ability to respond, the rotor will *skip steps* — and the controller has no way of knowing. **Lost steps = positioning error** that accumulates over time. This is the stepper's fundamental Achilles' heel.

### 2.2 Servo Motor — Internal Architecture & Closed-Loop Theory

#### 2.2.1 The Four Subsystems (Detailed)

1. **DC Motor:** A brushed permanent-magnet DC motor, typically rated 3 V – 12 V. Provides the *raw rotational energy*. Direction is controlled via an H-bridge inside the servo PCB.
2. **Gear Train:** A series of plastic (in hobby servos) or metal (in premium servos) gears. Reduction ratios commonly range from **$\sim$30:1 to $\sim$400:1**. This *amplifies torque* and *reduces speed* — converting the DC motor's high-RPM/low-torque output into a low-RPM/high-torque shaft output suitable for robotic joints.
3. **Potentiometer:** A rotary resistive element mechanically coupled to the output shaft (NOT the motor shaft). Acts as the **position sensor**, outputting a voltage proportional to the shaft angle.
4. **Control Board:** Contains a comparator circuit and an internal reference generator (typically producing a voltage from 1.0 ms – 2.0 ms PWM internally). It performs the **error calculation**:
$$e(t) = V_{command}(t) - V_{feedback}(t)$$
The error signal drives the H-bridge — clockwise current if $e > 0$, counter-clockwise if $e < 0$, brake (no current) if $e \approx 0$.

#### 2.2.2 PWM-to-Angle Mapping (Linear Model)

The pulse width $\tau$ is linearly mapped to the commanded angle $\theta_{cmd}$:

$$\theta_{cmd} = \theta_{min} + \frac{(\tau - \tau_{min})}{(\tau_{max} - \tau_{min})} \cdot (\theta_{max} - \theta_{min})$$

For the standard hobby servo with $\tau_{min} = 1.0$ ms, $\tau_{max} = 2.0$ ms, $\theta_{min} = 0°$, $\theta_{max} = 180°$:

$$\theta_{cmd} = \frac{(\tau - 1.0 \text{ ms})}{1.0 \text{ ms}} \cdot 180°$$

#### 2.2.3 Pulse Repetition and Stability

The control signal must be refreshed every $T = 20$ ms ($f = 50$ Hz). If pulses stop entirely, the servo enters *free* state — the internal error becomes undefined, and the shaft returns to neutral (or floats). Hence, a continuously running PWM is required.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step angle | $\theta_s = 360° / N_s$ | degrees (°) | $N_s$ = steps per revolution |
| 2 | Rotor speed (stepper) | $\omega = f_{pulse} \cdot \theta_s$ | rad/s | $f_{pulse}$ = input pulse frequency |
| 3 | Rotor speed (stepper) | $n = f_{pulse} / N_s$ | rev/s | Linear in pulse frequency |
| 4 | Holding torque | $\tau_h$ | N·m | Max static load before slip |
| 5 | Detent torque | $\tau_d$ | N·m | Holding torque at zero excitation |
| 6 | Pull-out torque | $\tau_{po}$ | N·m | Max torque at a given speed |
| 7 | Pull-in torque | $\tau_{pi}$ | N·m | Max torque at which motor can start without losing steps |
| 8 | Slew speed | $\omega_{max} = f_{max} \cdot \theta_s$ | rad/s | Max no-load synchronous speed |
| 9 | Servo PWM period | $T = 20$ ms | ms | Fixed, $f = 50$ Hz |
| 10 | Servo pulse-width range | $1.0 \text{ ms} \le \tau \le 2.0 \text{ ms}$ | ms | Maps to $0°$ – $180°$ |
| 11 | Servo commanded angle | $\theta_{cmd} = 180° \cdot (\tau - 1.0) / 1.0$ | degrees | Standard hobby mapping |
| 12 | Servo angular resolution | $\Delta\theta = \theta_{max} / N_{ticks}$ | degrees | $N_{ticks}$ = timer ticks in 1 ms |
| 13 | Closed-loop error | $e(t) = \theta_{cmd}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)$ | degrees | Drives H-bridge direction |
| 14 | Microstep resolution | $\theta_{micro} = \theta_s / \mu$ | degrees | $\mu$ = microstep factor (2, 4, 8, 16, 32) |
| 15 | Torque from gear train | $\tau_{out} = \tau_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta$ | N·m | $G$ = gear ratio, $\eta$ = efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - *Stepper* dominates in **3D printers, CNC routers, camera sliders, plotter machines** where open-loop precision suffices and back-EMF-based positioning (rare in steppers) is not required.
> - *Servo* dominates in **robotic arms, humanoid robots, RC vehicles, animatronics** where torque, holding, and disturbance rejection are critical.
> - In a *mobile robot*, both are usually present: **DC gear motors** drive the wheels (for speed), while a **servo** steers (Ackermann) or **a stepper** drives a pan-tilt sensor head.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Step Angle vs. Resolution Trade-off

**Problem Setup:** A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has a rotor with $T_r = 50$ teeth and a stator with $T_s = 50$ teeth. The stator has $N_p = 2$ phase pairs (4 phase windings). Find the **full-step angle** and the **steps per revolution**.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the *tooth pitch* (mechanical angle subtended by one tooth and one slot):

$$
\theta_{tooth} = \frac{360°}{T_r}
$$

Substituting $T_r = 50$:

$$
\theta_{tooth} = \frac{360°}{50} = 7.2° \text{ per tooth}
$$

**Step 2:** In a 2-phase stepper, one full electrical cycle requires advancing by *one tooth pitch* of the rotor relative to the stator's excited pole. But for a hybrid motor with multiple pole pairs, the effective step is much smaller.

**Step 3:** General formula for hybrid stepper with $T_r$ rotor teeth:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{T_r \cdot N_p}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $T_r = 50$, $N_p = 2$:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{50 \cdot 2} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6° \text{ per step}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute steps per revolution:

$$
N_s = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Halving the tooth count from 50 → 25 (keeping $N_p = 2$) gives $\theta_s = 7.2°$ — coarser steps, but more torque per step. Designers balance resolution vs. torque based on application.

#### Worked Numerical Example 2: Pulse Frequency to Motor Speed

**Given:** A stepper with $\theta_s = 1.8°$ is driven by a microcontroller producing $f_{pulse} = 500$ pulses per second.

**Find:** Revolutions per second (rps) and revolutions per minute (rpm).

**Step 1:** Compute steps per revolution:

$$
N_s = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute revolutions per second:

$$
n = \frac{f_{pulse}}{N_s} = \frac{500 \text{ pulses/s}}{200 \text{ steps/rev}} = 2.5 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to RPM:

$$
n_{rpm} = n \cdot 60 = 2.5 \cdot 60 = 150 \text{ RPM}
$$

#### Worked Numerical Example 3: Servo PWM to Angle

**Given:** A standard hobby servo receives a PWM pulse of $\tau = 1.7$ ms at $f_{PWM} = 50$ Hz.

**Find:** Commanded shaft angle $\theta_{cmd}$.

**Step 1:** Verify pulse is within valid range. Since $1.0 \text{ ms} \le 1.7 \text{ ms} \le 2.0 \text{ ms}$ → **valid**.

**Step 2:** Apply linear mapping formula (derived in §2.2.2):

$$
\theta_{cmd} = 180° \cdot \frac{(\tau - 1.0 \text{ ms})}{1.0 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\tau = 1.7$ ms:

$$
\theta_{cmd} = 180° \cdot \frac{(1.7 - 1.0)}{1.0} = 180° \cdot 0.7 = 126°
$$

**Step 4:** Sanity check — $\tau = 1.5$ ms (center) → $\theta_{cmd} = 90°$ (center). ✓

#### Worked Numerical Example 4: Servo Resolution from Timer Ticks

**Given:** A microcontroller's PWM timer increments every $\Delta t = 1 \mu s$. The pulse width window (0° to 180°) spans $1.0$ ms.

**Find:** Achievable angular resolution.

**Step 1:** Number of timer ticks in $1.0$ ms:

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{1.0 \text{ ms}}{1 \mu s} = \frac{1000 \mu s}{1 \mu s} = 1000 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 2:** Resolution per tick:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{min}}{N_{ticks}} = \frac{180°}{1000} = 0.18° \text{ per tick}
$$

#### Worked Numerical Example 5: Microstepping Resolution

**Given:** A $1.8°$ stepper is driven in $\mu = 16$ microstep mode.

**Find:** Effective step angle $\theta_{micro}$ and steps per revolution $N_{micro}$.

**Step 1:** Apply microstep formula:

$$
\theta_{micro} = \frac{\theta_s}{\mu} = \frac{1.8°}{16} = 0.1125°
$$

**Step 2:** Effective steps per revolution:

$$
N_{micro} = N_s \cdot \mu = 200 \cdot 16 = 3200 \text{ microsteps/rev}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control in the context of stepper and servo motors used in mobile robotics.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Control type | **Open-loop** (no feedback sensor for shaft position) | **Closed-loop** (potentiometer feedback) |
| Position knowledge | Determined by *pulse count* only | Continuously measured and corrected |
| Failure mode on overload | **Skips steps silently** → accumulating error | **Stalls and resists** until error resolves |
| Torque at standstill | Only **holding torque** (residual magnetic alignment) | **Active holding** — motor actively brakes against disturbance |
| Complexity | Simpler electronics (driver only) | Internal PCB, requires continuous PWM |
| Cost | Lower (no internal sensor) | Slightly higher |

> (3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for identifying stepper as open-loop, 1 mark for servo as closed-loop with feedback, 1 mark for failure-mode distinction.)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]
**A standard hobby RC servo is given a PWM signal of 50 Hz with pulse width 1.5 ms. Calculate the period and the commanded shaft angle. What happens if the pulse train is suddenly stopped?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $f_{PWM} = 50$ Hz, $\tau = 1.5$ ms.

**Period:**
$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{50 \text{ Hz}} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Commanded angle** (using linear mapping):
$$
\theta_{cmd} = 180° \cdot \frac{(1.5 - 1.0)}{1.0} = 180° \cdot 0.5 = 90°
$$

The servo will hold its shaft at **90° (the neutral/center position)**.

**If pulse train stops:** The control board loses its reference signal. The internal error $e(t)$ becomes undefined. Depending on the servo's design, the shaft will either **return to neutral position under spring tension** (most common) or **freeze at last commanded position** until pulses resume. The servo *cannot* maintain position without refreshed PWM commands.

> (3 marks awarded for: 1 mark period calculation, 1 mark angle calculation, 1 mark explanation of stopped pulse behavior.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model — Module 1, 14-Mark Standard]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor** with neat diagrams. Derive the **step angle formula** for a 2-phase PM stepper with 12 stator poles and 50 rotor teeth. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A PM stepper motor consists of:

- **Stator:** A laminated steel core with **12 salient poles** (or salient teeth) arranged in two phases (Phase A: 6 poles; Phase B: 6 poles, offset by half a pole-pitch). Each pole carries an excitation coil. The poles project inward toward the rotor.
- **Rotor:** A cylindrical **axially-magnetized permanent magnet** (typically ferrite or rare-earth like NdFeB). The magnetization creates alternating North and South poles around its circumference. Common rotor pole counts: 12, 24, 50.
- **End bells and bearings:** Mechanical housing allowing free rotation.
- **Lead wires:** Usually 4 (for bipolar) or 5–6 (for unipolar) wires emerging from the housing.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

When Phase A is energized with DC current, the stator's Phase A poles become magnetized — alternating N-S-N-S-... around the stator circumference. The rotor's permanent magnets align such that the rotor's *North* poles face the stator's *South* poles (and vice versa) for minimum energy configuration. The rotor thus *snaps* to a unique angular position.

When Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized, the stator's magnetic pattern shifts by half a pole-pitch, forcing the rotor to rotate by the same angle. This mechanical increment is the **step angle**.

Pulses applied sequentially to Phase A → Phase B → Phase A (reverse) → Phase B (reverse) → ... produce continuous rotation. The direction is reversed by reversing the commutation sequence.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

For a 2-phase PM stepper:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{\text{(number of rotor poles)} \times \text{(number of stator phases)}}
$$

Substituting rotor teeth $= 50$, stator phases $= 2$:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{50 \times 2} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°
$$

Therefore $N_s = 100$ steps/revolution.

---

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **open-loop control architecture of a stepper motor** driving a robot's wheel. Discuss the **limitations of open-loop control** in mobile robotics and how microstepping alleviates (but does not eliminate) them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

```
[Controller] → [Pulse Generator] → [Stepper Driver IC] → [Stepper Motor] → [Wheel/Gearbox] → [Robot Motion]
       ↑                                                                                          
       └────────────── NO FEEDBACK PATH (Open Loop) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
```

The controller (microcontroller like Arduino/STM32) generates a stream of digital pulses. Each pulse is fed to the stepper driver IC (e.g., A4988, DRV8825, TMC2209). The driver IC commutates the stator phases of the stepper motor accordingly. The stepper's shaft — coupled to a wheel via a gearbox — rotates by $\theta_s$ per pulse. Since the relationship is deterministic (no feedback), the controller *assumes* each pulse resulted in one step.

**Microstepping (2 marks):**

Instead of fully energizing one phase at a time, the driver modulates the currents in the two phases using **sine-cosine weighted PWM**:

$$
I_A(t) = I_{max} \cdot \cos(\theta / \theta_s), \quad I_B(t) = I_{max} \cdot \sin(\theta / \theta_s)
$$

For $\mu = 16$ microstepping, the discrete sub-positions within one full step are generated, achieving smoother motion and $\theta_{micro} = \theta_s / 16$. Resonance and audible noise are dramatically reduced.

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **No feedback → silent slip:** If external load exceeds torque capacity, the rotor skips steps. The controller has no way of detecting this. **Positioning error accumulates** and can only be corrected by a homing routine at known physical limits.
2. **Resonance at low speeds:** Step pulses excite mechanical resonances in the rotor-load system, causing loss of torque and audible noise. Microstepping helps significantly by spreading excitation across multiple sub-frequencies.
3. **High heat dissipation:** Phase coils remain energized when stationary → continuous $\hat{I}^2R$ losses → heating. Efficiency at standstill is *zero*.
4. **Open-loop velocity is unreliable under variable load:** For a mobile robot climbing a slope or pushing through debris, load torque varies. The stepper's synchronous speed remains fixed (proportional to pulse rate), but it can lose sync — leading to permanent position error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):** Students frequently claim *"microstepping increases torque"* — this is **FALSE**. Microstepping *splits* each full step into $\mu$ smaller increments, but the **total torque per microstep is reduced** by roughly $\sin(\pi/\mu)$. The advantage is **smoothness and resolution, NOT torque**. Loses 1–2 marks if stated incorrectly.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Module 1, 14-Mark Standard]**

**(a)** Describe the **internal construction of a positional servo motor**. Draw and explain its **closed-loop control block diagram**. Derive the **PWM-to-angle mapping equation** for a servo whose pulse width range is $1.0$ ms to $2.0$ ms corresponding to $0°$ to $180°$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Internal Construction (2.5 marks):**

A hobby/positional servo motor integrates four subsystems into one housing:

1. **DC Motor:** Brushed permanent-magnet DC motor, typically 3–12 V, providing high-speed low-torque rotation.
2. **Gear Train:** A multi-stage spur gear reduction (plastic in cheap servos, metal/alloy in premium). Total ratio $G$ typically ranges from 30:1 to over 400:1. It converts the motor's high RPM to low RPM and amplifies torque by factor $G \cdot \eta$ ($\eta$ = gear train efficiency, ~0.6–0.85).
3. **Position Sensor:** A rotary potentiometer mechanically coupled (via the gear train) to the *output* shaft (not the motor shaft). The potentiometer's wiper voltage $V_{fb}$ is linearly proportional to the output shaft angle.
4. **Control Circuitry:** A small PCB containing:
   - A **pulse-width-to-voltage converter** (or comparator)
   - An **error amplifier** computing $e(t) = V_{cmd} - V_{fb}$
   - An **H-bridge motor driver** for bidirectional current control

**Closed-Loop Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
                +e(t)
[PWM Input] → [Reference V_cmd] → (+) → [Error Amp] → [H-Bridge] → [DC Motor] → [Gear Train] → [Output Shaft]
                                          (−) ↑                                                       │
                                              │                                                       │
                                              └──────────── [Potentiometer Feedback V_fb] ←────────────┘
```

The error amplifier's output polarity determines H-bridge direction:
- $e(t) > 0$ → motor drives CW
- $e(t) < 0$ → motor drives CCW
- $e(t) \approx 0$ → motor brakes (H-bridge enters low-side decay)

This continuous negative feedback forces the shaft to track the commanded position with high precision (typical $\pm 1°$ for hobby servos, $\pm 0.05°$ for industrial servos).

**PWM-to-Angle Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Given $\tau \in [1.0 \text{ ms}, 2.0 \text{ ms}]$ maps linearly to $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$. Using the **two-point linear interpolation** formula:

$$
\theta = \theta_{min} + (\tau - \tau_{min}) \cdot \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta_{min}}{\tau_{max} - \tau_{min}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\theta = 0° + (\tau - 1.0 \text{ ms}) \cdot \frac{180° - 0°}{2.0 \text{ ms} - 1.0 \text{ ms}}
$$

$$
\theta = (\tau - 1.0) \cdot 180° \quad [\tau \text{ in ms}]
$$

This is the standard hobby servo mapping formula.

---

**(b)** Compare the **performance of stepper and servo motors** for the following mobile robotics applications: (i) wheel drive of a differential-drive robot, (ii) steering of a car-like robot, (iii) joint actuation of a 6-DOF robotic arm. Justify your choice in each case with quantitative reasoning where applicable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Application (i): Differential-Drive Wheel Motor (3 marks):**

For the *drive wheels* of a differential-drive mobile robot, the actuator must:
- Rotate continuously (not just oscillate)
- Deliver high RPM (typical wheel speed: 100–300 RPM for indoor robots)
- Provide smooth torque over a wide speed range
- Tolerate overloads (bumps, debris) without damaging the drivetrain

**Best choice: Brushed/Brushless DC Gear Motor with encoder** — *not* stepper or servo in their stock form. Stepper motors lose torque rapidly above ~1000 RPM due to inductance; servo motors are designed for limited-angle positioning, not continuous rotation (though "continuous rotation servos" exist, they lose position-holding).

**Quantitative reasoning:** For a stepper rated $1.8°$ step at $1000$ RPM, pulse rate $= 1000 \times 200 / 60 = 3333$ Hz. At this rate, back-EMF and winding inductance dominate, reducing torque to near zero. So steppers are *not used* for wheel drive in practice.

**Application (ii): Car-Like Steering (Ackermann) (2 marks):**

Steering requires:
- Limited angular travel (typically $\pm 30°$ from center)
- High holding torque (the steering link must resist bumps while stationary)
- Precise commanded angle (for path following)
- Bidirectional motion

**Best choice: Servo Motor** — Its $180°$ range maps directly to steering geometry; closed-loop feedback rejects disturbances; high holding torque maintains straight-line driving. Stepper could work in principle, but open-loop slip would result in dangerous steering errors with no detection.

**Application (iii): 6-DOF Robotic Arm Joints (2 marks):**

Robotic arm joints require:
- Precise angular positioning ($\pm 0.5°$ or better)
- High torque density (joints 4–6 carry loads)
- Closed-loop stability (otherwise the arm collapses)

**Best choice: Servo Motor (hobby-grade: MG996R, DS3235; industrial: brushless servo with absolute encoder).** The closed-loop nature is essential — if a payload is added mid-motion, a servo *detects* the extra torque requirement and compensates; a stepper would lose steps silently. Modern humanoid robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics' early prototypes) and robotic arms like the MeArm use hobby servos.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):** Students often claim *"stepper motors are better for robotic arms because of open-loop precision."* This is **INCORRECT for loaded arms**. Open-loop precision is only valid when **load torque is known and constant**. In a robotic arm, joint torques vary *non-linearly* with arm pose (gravity moment changes). Closed-loop (servo) is mandatory. Loses 2 marks if stated otherwise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Locomotion: Stepper & Servo Motor Control**

**A. Stepper Motor Essentials**
- ☐ Definition: brushless, synchronous, *discrete-step* actuator driven by digital pulses
- ☐ Step angle formula: $\theta_s = 360° / N_s$ (most common $1.8°$, $0.9°$)
- ☐ Three types: VR, PM, Hybrid (Hybrid = dominant in robotics)
- ☐ Open-loop control — *no feedback*, position inferred from pulse count
- ☐ Failure mode: **silent slip / lost steps** → accumulating position error
- ☐ Commutation modes: wave-drive, full-step, half-step, microstep ($\mu = 2, 4, 8, 16, 32$)
- ☐ Microstepping uses sine-cosine weighted phase currents
- ☐ Standard driver ICs: **A4988, DRV8825, TMC2209**
- ☐ Typical supply: **12 V – 48 V DC**
- ☐ Speed relation: $n_{RPM} = 60 \cdot f_{pulse} / N_s$

**B. Servo Motor Essentials**
- ☐ Definition: integrated *closed-loop* position actuator (DC motor + gearbox + potentiometer + control PCB)
- ☐ Command interface: **PWM at 50 Hz** ($T = 20$ ms)
- ☐ Pulse width range: **1.0 ms – 2.0 ms** → angle $0° – 180°$
- ☐ Linear mapping formula: $\theta = 180° \cdot (\tau - 1.0)$ for $\tau$ in ms
- ☐ Error computation: $e(t) = V_{cmd}(t) - V_{fb}(t)$
- ☐ Holds position *actively* against external disturbance (residual torque)
- ☐ Typical supply: **4.8 V – 7.4 V DC**
- ☐ Resolution from timer ticks: $\Delta\theta = 180° / N_{ticks}$

**C. Comparative Insights (High-Yield for KTU)**
- ☐ Stepper = **open-loop digital precision**, cheap, but slips silently under overload
- ☐ Servo = **closed-loop analog/digital precision**, slightly costlier, never silently slips
- ☐ Stepper dominates: 3D printers, CNC, linear stages (low-speed, predictable load)
- ☐ Servo dominates: steering, arms, gimbals, animatronics (variable load, disturbance rejection)
- ☐ **Neither** is ideal for continuous-rotation wheel drive — use DC gear motor + encoder instead

**D. Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ☐ ❌ Claiming microstepping increases torque (it doesn't — total torque is unchanged, redistributed)
- ☐ ❌ Saying servo motors are "open-loop because no external encoder" (the potentiometer *is* the feedback)
- ☐ ❌ Confusing positional servo (limited angle) with continuous-rotation servo (modified, no position hold)
- ☐ ❌ Using $\theta_s = 360°/N_s$ for hybrid steppers without accounting for $N_p$ (number of phase pairs)
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting that PWM must be **continuously refreshed** at 50 Hz — stopping the signal = loss of servo control

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Mobile Robots - Differential Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheeled Mobile Robots - Differential Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Differential Drive** (also called a **Differential Steer** or **Skid-Steer**) wheeled mobile robot is a nonholonomic locomotion platform in which two independently actuated coaxial wheels, mounted on a common axle and driven by separate DC motors/encoders, provide both **translation** (linear velocity `v`) and **rotation** (angular velocity `ω`) of the robot. The vehicle is dynamically balanced and statically unstable, and its planar motion is described by a configuration vector **`q = [x, y, θ]ᵀ`** in the global Cartesian frame.

The defining property of a differential drive is the **nonholonomic constraint** that the wheel cannot move sideways — i.e., the lateral velocity component of the chassis at the contact point must always equal zero. Mathematically:

$$v_y \cos\theta - v_x \sin\theta = 0$$

This single constraint reduces the **3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of planar motion to only **2 controllable DOF** (`v` and `ω`), making the robot *under-actuated* in terms of global positioning, even though it has two driven wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** The single most examinable fact about a differential drive is that it has **2 DOF in configuration space** and **2 control inputs** (left & right wheel velocities), but is **subject to 1 nonholonomic constraint**, hence cannot move sideways, only forward/rotate.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are sitting on an office chair with wheels. The chair has **two big drive wheels** — one on your left, one on your right — and a small free-spinning **caster wheel** (or ball bearing) at the back to keep it balanced.

- If **both wheels spin forward at the same speed** → the chair rolls **straight ahead** like a car. 🛞
- If **both wheels spin forward at equal but opposite speeds** → the chair **spins in place** (turns on its own center) like a pirouette. 💫
- If the **right wheel spins faster than the left** → the chair **curves to the left**, because the left wheel becomes a pivot point. 🔄

The small caster wheel at the rear (or front) is **passive** — it does NOT drive, it only prevents the chair from falling over (static stability). The *Instantaneous Center of Rotation* (ICR) lies somewhere along the wheel axle, and its position depends purely on the ratio of the two wheel speeds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples students should recognize:**
> - **iRobot Roomba** (vacuum cleaning robot)
> - **TurtleBot** (research/educational platform)
> - **Mars Rover Sojourner / Spirit / Opportunity / Curiosity** (NASA JPL)
> - **BattleBots / Sumo Robots**
> - **Differential-drive AGVs** in warehouses (KIVA/Amazon Robotics in part)

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Highlighted)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel radius | **r** | **0.05 m to 0.15 m** |
| Axle length (wheelbase) | **L** (or 2ℓ) | **0.2 m to 0.5 m** |
| Half wheelbase | **ℓ = L/2** | **0.1 m to 0.25 m** |
| Max wheel angular velocity | **ω_max** | typically **2π rad/s** (≈ 60 rpm) |
| Wheel encoder resolution | **P** (pulses/rev) | **500 to 2000 counts/rev** |
| Body mass | **m** | 1 – 50 kg |
| Moment of inertia (about ICR) | **I** | kg·m² |
| Friction coefficient | **μ** | ≈ **0.5 – 0.8** (rubber on tile) |

---

## 1.4 Historical / KTU Context Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the term "Differential":** Borrowed from the **automotive differential gear**, which allows two coaxial wheels (on a car’s rear axle) to rotate at *different* speeds while still being driven by a single engine. In robotics, the principle is reversed — we use *independent* motors to deliberately create the speed difference, generating turning.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Robot Configuration and Reference Frames

A differential drive is modelled in **two reference frames**:

1. **Global (World) frame** `{X, Y}` — fixed inertial frame.
2. **Robot (Body) frame** `{xᵣ, yᵣ}` — attached to the chassis, with `xᵣ` pointing forward.

The **posture / pose** of the robot at any time `t` is the state vector:

$$\xi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ \theta(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

where `(x, y)` is the position of the **center of the axle (point P)** in the world frame, and `θ` is the **heading angle** measured from the world `X`-axis to the robot's `xᵣ`-axis (counter-clockwise positive).

## 2.2 The Three Kinematic Constraints (Wheel Constraints)

The two drive wheels roll **without slipping** in the direction of rolling and **cannot slide sideways**. The caster wheel imposes *no constraint* (it is passive). This yields **three kinematic constraints** on the planar motion:

### Constraint 1 — No lateral motion (the nonholonomic constraint)
The lateral velocity of point P in the world frame is identically zero.

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

### Constraint 2 & 3 — Pure rolling of left and right wheels
Let `vL` and `vR` be the linear velocities of the left and right contact points, and `ωL`, `ωR` the corresponding wheel angular speeds. Then:

$$v_L = r\,\omega_L, \quad v_R = r\,\omega_R$$

The linear and angular velocities of the chassis are then:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

## 2.3 Forward Kinematics (Wheel Velocities → Robot Velocities)

**Forward kinematics** is the mapping from **joint-space** (wheel velocities) to **task-space** (chassis velocities in the world frame):

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This `3 × 2` matrix is the **Jacobian** `J(θ)` of the differential drive. The fact that it is *not square* (3×2) mathematically confirms that the robot is **under-actuated** — three configuration variables are updated by only two control inputs.

## 2.4 Inverse Kinematics (Robot Velocities → Wheel Velocities)

The reverse problem — "given a desired `v` and `ω`, what wheel speeds are needed?" — has a clean closed-form solution:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & L/2 \\ 1 & -L/2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

Expanded:

$$\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v + \frac{L}{2}\omega\right), \quad \omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v - \frac{L}{2}\omega\right)$$

This is the equation used in **every line-following, waypoint, and SLAM motion controller** in mobile robotics.

## 2.5 Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

The **ICR** is the point about which the entire rigid body instantaneously rotates. For a differential drive, the ICR always lies **on the line joining the two wheel contact points** (i.e., on the axle).

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

Special cases:
- If `ωR = ωL = ω` → `R_ICR → ∞` (no rotation, straight-line translation).
- If `ωR = −ωL` → `R_ICR = 0` (rotation about the chassis center).
- If `ωL = 0` → `R_ICR = L/2` (pivot turn about the left wheel).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `q = [x, y, θ]ᵀ` | Robot pose | m, m, rad |
| 2 | `v = (r/2)(ωR + ωL)` | Forward velocity of chassis | m/s |
| 3 | `ω = (r/L)(ωR − ωL)` | Angular velocity of chassis | rad/s |
| 4 | `R_ICR = v/ω` | Radius of turning circle | m |
| 5 | `R_ICR = (L/2)(ωR+ωL)/(ωR−ωL)` | ICR from wheel speeds | m |
| 6 | `ωR = (1/r)(v + Lω/2)` | Inverse-kin: right wheel | rad/s |
| 7 | `ωL = (1/r)(v − Lω/2)` | Inverse-kin: left wheel | rad/s |
| 8 | `ẋsin θ − ẏcos θ = 0` | Nonholonomic constraint | — |
| 9 | `dd_actual = (ωR − ωL)·(r/2)·dt` | Distance differential per step | m |
| 10 | `Δθ = (r/L)(ωR − ωL)·dt` | Heading change per step | rad |
| 11 | `v_max = r·ω_max` | Max linear speed | m/s |
| 12 | `d_odometer = (ΔN_R + ΔN_L)·πr / P` | Encoder-based displacement | m |
| 13 | `ω_drive = (v + Lω/2) / r` | Right wheel command | rad/s |
| 14 | `ω_drive = (v − Lω/2) / r` | Left wheel command | rad/s |
| 15 | `e = √(Δx² + Δy²) < ε` | Goal-reached condition | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** Do NOT use `L` and `ℓ` interchangeably. The convention in KTU textbooks is `L = 2ℓ` (full wheelbase). Always state your assumption at the top of the answer sheet.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs** use differential drives to navigate narrow aisles using **encoder-based dead reckoning**.
- **Mars Rovers** use rocker-bogie suspensions but each corner wheel can be steered/differentially driven for hazard avoidance.
- **Disaster-response robots** (e.g., PackBot, TALON) use skid-steer tracks, which are kinematically equivalent to differential drives.
- **Robot soccer** (RoboCup Small Size League) uses omni-wheels, but the *learning* version (SSL-edu) starts with differential drives.
- **Service robots** (TOWARDS, Pepper, Temi) use differential drives for indoor navigation because of the zero-radius turn capability.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forward Kinematics from First Principles (Roll-Without-Slip)

**Starting assumptions** (must be stated in the exam):
1. The robot is **rigid**.
2. Both wheels roll **without slipping** (static friction provides the no-slip condition).
3. The wheelbase `L` is the distance between the two wheel contact points.
4. The reference point P is the **midpoint of the axle** (also the center of mass in the symmetric case).

### Step 1 — Define the contact-point velocities in the world frame

The left and right wheel contact points lie at:

$$P_L = (x - \tfrac{L}{2}\sin\theta,\; y + \tfrac{L}{2}\cos\theta), \quad P_R = (x + \tfrac{L}{2}\sin\theta,\; y - \tfrac{L}{2}\cos\theta)$$

The velocity of each point is the sum of the chassis translation and the rotation about P:

$$\vec{v}_{P_L} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} + \omega \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -\tfrac{L}{2}\sin\theta \\ \tfrac{L}{2}\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

The matrix on the right is the **2D skew-symmetric** operator for rotation by +90°. Multiplying it out:

$$\vec{v}_{P_L} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} - \tfrac{\omega L}{2}\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} - \tfrac{\omega L}{2}\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

By symmetry, for the right wheel:

$$\vec{v}_{P_R} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} + \tfrac{\omega L}{2}\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} + \tfrac{\omega L}{2}\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the roll-without-slip condition (component along the wheel's rolling direction)

The wheel rolls in the direction of the robot's `xᵣ`-axis. In world coordinates, the rolling direction is the unit vector `u = [cos θ, sin θ]ᵀ`. The sideways direction (no slip allowed) is `n = [−sin θ, cos θ]ᵀ`.

For the **right wheel**, the rolling-direction velocity equals `r·ωR`, and the lateral velocity is zero:

$$\vec{v}_{P_R} \cdot u = r\,\omega_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta + \tfrac{\omega L}{2} = r\,\omega_R$$

For the **left wheel**:

$$\vec{v}_{P_L} \cdot u = r\,\omega_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - \tfrac{\omega L}{2} = r\,\omega_L$$

### Step 3 — Solve simultaneously for `ẋ, ẏ, ω̇` (well, for `ω` since `ẋ, ẏ` are not independently controllable)

**Add** the two equations:

$$2(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta) + 0 = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

Therefore, the **forward linear velocity** of the chassis along `xᵣ` is:

$$\boxed{v = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)}$$

**Subtract** left from right:

$$0 + \omega L = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

Therefore, the **angular velocity** of the chassis is:

$$\boxed{\omega = \dot{\theta} = \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

These are the **fundamental kinematic equations** of the differential drive — *always* derive them in this order in the exam.

### Step 4 — Express `ẋ, ẏ` in the world frame

We know `v` (the forward speed in body frame) and `ω`. The body-frame velocity components are:

$$v_{x_r} = v, \quad v_{y_r} = 0$$

Rotating to the world frame via the rotation matrix `R(θ)`:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting the expressions for `v` and `ω`:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying the two matrices to obtain the **forward-kinematic Jacobian** `J(θ)`:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{L} & -\tfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix}}_{J(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observations from the Jacobian:**
> - The two columns are **linearly dependent** for the `x` and `y` rows (they are the same column scaled by `r/2`). This is the matrix signature of the **nonholonomic constraint**.
> - The third row is independent, encoding the differential action of the wheels.
> - The Jacobian rank is **2**, which equals the number of actuators — the robot is *exactly actuated for its constrained motion*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverse Kinematics (Given `v, ω` → Required `ωR, ωL`)

This derivation is the **algebraic inversion** of the forward map. We want to solve:

$$v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \quad \omega = \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

for `ωR` and `ωL` in terms of `v` and `ω`.

### Step 1 — Solve the linear system

From the first equation: `ωR + ωL = 2v/r`
From the second equation: `ωR − ωL = ωL/r = L·ω/r`

### Step 2 — Add and subtract

Adding: `2ωR = (2v + Lω)/r` → `ωR = v/r + (Lω)/(2r) = (1/r)(v + Lω/2)`
Subtracting: `2ωL = (2v − Lω)/r` → `ωL = v/r − (Lω)/(2r) = (1/r)(v − Lω/2)`

$$\boxed{\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v + \frac{L}{2}\omega\right), \quad \omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v - \frac{L}{2}\omega\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check for KTU board answer sheets:**
> - If the robot must turn **left** (positive `ω` in CCW convention), then `ωL` must *decrease* and `ωR` must *increase* — the right wheel "pulls" while the left wheel "drags". ✓
> - If `ω = 0` (straight motion), both wheels spin at the same speed `v/r`. ✓
> - If `v = 0` (pure rotation), the wheels spin in opposite directions with equal magnitude `Lω/(2r)`. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Posture Update (Discrete Euler Integration) for Dead Reckoning

In a **real digital controller** (Arduino, ROS, STM32), the kinematic equations are integrated **numerically** at each control tick `Δt`. The simplest scheme is **forward Euler**:

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$$

where the subscript `k` denotes the `k`-th time step and:

$$v_k = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_{R,k} + \omega_{L,k}), \quad \omega_k = \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_{R,k} - \omega_{L,k})$$

A **more accurate** integration (used in high-quality inertial systems) is the **exact arc-stretch update**:

$$\Delta\theta = \omega_k\Delta t$$

If `|Δθ| < ε` (a small threshold, e.g. `10⁻⁶` rad), use the linear approximation above. Otherwise, use the exact circular-arc update:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + R_{ICR,k}\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta_k\bigr]$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k - R_{ICR,k}\bigl[\cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \cos\theta_k\bigr]$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta, \quad \text{where } R_{ICR,k} = \frac{v_k}{\omega_k}$$

This avoids the **discretization drift** that causes a real differential drive to *not* return to its origin after a square path — a famous KTU viva question! 🎯

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Worked Numerical Example (Typical 7-Mark Question)

**Problem statement (typical KTU ESE):**
A differential-drive robot has wheel radius `r = 0.05 m` and wheelbase `L = 0.30 m`. The right wheel rotates at `ωR = 4 rad/s` (CCW) and the left wheel at `ωL = 1 rad/s` (CCW). Starting from pose `(x, y, θ) = (0, 0, 0)`, compute the new pose after `Δt = 1 s`.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute linear and angular velocities of the chassis.**

$$v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \tfrac{0.05}{2}(4 + 1) = 0.025 \times 5 = 0.125\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\omega = \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \tfrac{0.05}{0.30}(4 - 1) = 0.1\overline{6} \times 3 = 0.5\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ICR radius.**

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.125}{0.5} = 0.25\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the heading change.**

$$\Delta\theta = \omega \Delta t = 0.5 \times 1 = 0.5\ \text{rad} \approx 28.65°$$

**Step 4 — Update the pose using the exact arc formula.**

$$x_{new} = x + R_{ICR}\bigl[\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta\bigr] = 0 + 0.25\,[\sin(0.5) - 0] = 0.25 \times 0.4794 = 0.1199\ \text{m}$$

$$y_{new} = y - R_{ICR}\bigl[\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \cos\theta\bigr] = 0 - 0.25\,[\cos(0.5) - 1] = -0.25 \times (-0.1226) = 0.0306\ \text{m}$$

$$\theta_{new} = \theta + \Delta\theta = 0 + 0.5 = 0.5\ \text{rad}$$

**Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\xi_{new} = (0.1199\ \text{m},\ 0.0306\ \text{m},\ 0.5\ \text{rad})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The robot travelled `0.125 m` along an arc of radius `0.25 m` centred at a point `(−0.25, 0)` in the world frame, turning left by `28.65°` and ending slightly forward and slightly to the left of the start.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When Does the Robot Behave How?

| `ωR` | `ωL` | `v` | `ω` | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `+α` | `+α` | `+rα` | `0` | Straight-line motion forward |
| `0` | `0` | `0` | `0` | Stationary |
| `+α` | `−α` | `0` | `+2rα/L` | Pure CCW rotation in place |
| `−α` | `+α` | `0` | `−2rα/L` | Pure CW rotation in place |
| `+α` | `0` | `+rα/2` | `+rα/L` | Left turn (right wheel pulls, left pivots) |
| `0` | `+α` | `+rα/2` | `−rα/L` | Right turn (left wheel pulls, right pivots) |
| `+2α` | `+α` | `+3rα/2` | `+rα/L` | Forward + slight left curve |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Differential Drive Kinematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Robot Motion v_des and omega_des] --> B[Inverse Kinematics Block]
    B --> C[omega_R command in rad per sec]
    B --> D[omega_L command in rad per sec]
    C --> E[Right Wheel Motor + Encoder]
    D --> F[Left Wheel Motor + Encoder]
    E --> G[Right Wheel Contact Point]
    F --> H[Left Wheel Contact Point]
    G --> I[Chassis Rigid Body]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Forward Kinematics Block]
    J --> K[Measured v in m per s]
    J --> L[Measured omega in rad per s]
    K --> M[Odometry Pose Update x y theta]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Controller compares with desired v omega]
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Reference Frames and Constraint Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WorldFrame[Global Reference Frame X Y]
        WX[World X axis fixed]
        WY[World Y axis fixed]
    end
    subgraph RobotFrame[Robot Body Frame x_r y_r]
        RX[x_r axis along forward motion]
        RY[y_r axis along lateral direction]
    end
    subgraph Constraints[Three Kinematic Constraints]
        C1[No lateral slip at left wheel]
        C2[No lateral slip at right wheel]
        C3[Nonholonomic constraint at chassis center]
    end
    WorldFrame -- heading angle theta --> RobotFrame
    RobotFrame -- Roll-without-slip --> Constraints
    Constraints -- reduces DOF from 3 to 2 --> ControlInputs
    ControlInputs[v linear m per s]
    ControlInputs2[omega angular rad per s]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — ICR Geometry in the Robot Body Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Chassis center P at pose x y theta]
    WL[Left wheel contact point]
    WR[Right wheel contact point]
    IC[Instantaneous Center of Rotation ICR on axle]
    P0 --> WL
    P0 --> WR
    WL -- distance L over 2 --> IC
    WR -- distance L over 2 --> IC
    note1[If omega_R equals omega_L then ICR is at infinity]
    note2[If omega_R equals minus omega_L then ICR coincides with P]
    note3[If omega_L equals 0 then ICR is at left wheel]
    note4[If omega_R equals 0 then ICR is at right wheel]
    IC --> note1
    IC --> note2
    IC --> note3
    IC --> note4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Pose Update Algorithm (Dead Reckoning Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start initialization x equals 0 y equals 0 theta equals 0] --> S1[Read encoder counts n_L and n_R]
    S1 --> S2[Compute wheel angular speeds omega_L and omega_R]
    S2 --> S3[Compute chassis linear and angular velocities v and omega]
    S3 --> S4[Check if omega is approximately zero]
    S4 -- yes --> S5[Use straight line update x plus v cos theta dt, y plus v sin theta dt]
    S4 -- no --> S6[Use arc update with ICR radius v over omega]
    S5 --> S7[Update theta equals theta plus omega dt]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Loop back to read encoders]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Control Hierarchy (KTU-Standard Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[High level planner path and goal in world frame]
    Top --> Mid[Trajectory tracker produces v_des and omega_des]
    Mid --> Low[Low level motor controller produces PWM to left and right motors]
    Low --> Act[Physical differential drive robot]
    Act --> FB[Encoders feedback to low level]
    FB --> Low
    Mid --> IMU[Optional IMU feedback for heading correction]
    IMU --> Mid
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for students:** In Mermaid 1, the outer feedback loop `N → A` represents the *closed-loop control* — the measured pose is compared to the desired pose and the error drives the next command. In Mermaid 3, the four `noteX` nodes correspond exactly to the four special-case rows of the analytical matrix in Section 3.5.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "nonholonomic constraint" with respect to a differential-drive wheeled mobile robot. State the equation of the constraint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *nonholonomic constraint* is a kinematic restriction on a mechanical system that **cannot be expressed solely as a constraint on the configuration variables** (i.e., not integrable to a position-only equation) but is expressed as a constraint on the *velocities*. For a differential-drive robot, the wheel cannot move sideways, which means the lateral velocity of the chassis at the contact point must be zero at all times. The nonholonomic constraint equation (in the global frame) is:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Mark split (KTU pattern):** Definition `1 mark` · Equation `1 mark` · Justification / physical meaning `1 mark`.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for a differential drive? Where does it lie?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ICR** is the point in the plane about which the entire rigid chassis instantaneously rotates at a given instant (even when the robot's overall motion is a combination of translation and rotation). For a differential drive, because both wheels are coaxial and roll without slipping, the ICR **always lies on the line joining the two wheel-ground contact points** — i.e., on the wheel-axle. Its distance `R` from the chassis center is given by:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

When `ωR = ωL` the ICR is at infinity (pure translation); when `ωR = −ωL` it coincides with the chassis center (in-place rotation).

**Mark split:** ICR definition `1 mark` · Location `1 mark` · Limiting-case examples `1 mark`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **forward kinematic equations** of a differential-drive mobile robot, clearly stating the assumptions, defining all symbols, and writing the final Jacobian matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius `r = 0.04 m` and wheelbase `L = 0.25 m`. It must move with a desired linear velocity `v = 0.20 m/s` and angular velocity `ω = 0.4 rad/s`. Compute the required left and right wheel angular velocities. Also, determine the radius of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Assumptions** (state all 4 for full marks):
1. Both wheels roll **without slipping** (pure rolling contact).
2. The wheelbase `L` is constant (rigid chassis).
3. The reference point P is the **midpoint of the axle**.
4. The nonholonomic constraint holds: lateral velocity at P is zero.

**Derivation** (follow the 4-step procedure from Section 3.1):
- Express the contact-point velocities using rigid-body kinematics.
- Project them onto the rolling direction (`u`) and lateral direction (`n`).
- Apply the no-slip condition (lateral = 0, rolling = `r·ω`).
- Solve simultaneously to obtain:

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \quad \omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

- Express `ẋ, ẏ` in the world frame using the rotation matrix `R(θ)`.
- Final Jacobian:

$$J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \tfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \tfrac{r}{L} & -\tfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \dot{\xi} = J(\theta)\,[\omega_R,\ \omega_L]^T$$

**Mark split (7):** Assumptions `1m` · Velocity decomposition `2m` · Derivation of `v, ω` `2m` · Final Jacobian `1m` · Units / interpretation `1m`.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Write the inverse-kinematic equations** (from Section 3.2):

$$\omega_R = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v + \frac{L}{2}\omega\right), \quad \omega_L = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v - \frac{L}{2}\omega\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

Right wheel:

$$\omega_R = \frac{1}{0.04}\!\left(0.20 + \frac{0.25}{2}\times 0.4\right) = 25\,(0.20 + 0.05) = 25 \times 0.25 = 6.25\ \text{rad/s}$$

Left wheel:

$$\omega_L = \frac{1}{0.04}\!\left(0.20 - \frac{0.25}{2}\times 0.4\right) = 25\,(0.20 - 0.05) = 25 \times 0.15 = 3.75\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ICR radius.**

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.20}{0.4} = 0.50\ \text{m}$$

**Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\omega_R = 6.25\ \text{rad/s},\ \ \omega_L = 3.75\ \text{rad/s},\ \ R_{ICR} = 0.50\ \text{m}}$$

**Mark split (7):** Inverse-kin equation `2m` · Substitution `2m` · Numerical answer (both wheels) `2m` · ICR calculation `1m`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **posture update equations** for dead reckoning in a differential drive. Write the exact arc-stretch update and show that the linear-update form is recovered when `ω → 0`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive starts at `(x, y, θ) = (0, 0, π/4)`. For `Δt = 2 s`, the left wheel rotates at `ωL = 2 rad/s` and the right at `ωR = 5 rad/s`. The wheel radius is `r = 0.1 m` and the wheelbase is `L = 0.4 m`. Using the exact arc-stretch update, compute the new pose. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Explanation with diagram:**

At each control step `Δt`, the dead-reckoning algorithm reads encoder counts, computes the wheel angular displacements `ΔφL = ωL·Δt` and `ΔφR = ωR·Δt`, converts them to linear displacements `ΔsL = r·ΔφL`, `ΔsR = r·ΔφR`, and then uses the **arc-stretch geometry** to update the pose. The robot follows a circular arc of radius `R = Δs/(Δφ)` centred at the ICR.

**Exact arc-stretch update:**

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}\,\Delta t$$

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta\theta} = \frac{L(\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)}{2(\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)}$$

Pose update (for `Δθ ≠ 0`):

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + R_{ICR}\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \sin\theta_k\bigr]$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k - R_{ICR}\bigl[\cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta) - \cos\theta_k\bigr]$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$$

**Limiting case `ω → 0` (use L'Hôpital or Taylor series):**

Using `sin(a+b) − sin(a) ≈ b·cos(a)` and `cos(a+b) − cos(a) ≈ −b·sin(a)` for small `b = Δθ`, the arc-stretch update reduces to:

$$x_{k+1} \approx x_k + R_{ICR}\cdot\Delta\theta\cos\theta_k = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$$

$$y_{k+1} \approx y_k + R_{ICR}\cdot\Delta\theta\sin\theta_k = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$$

which is the **linear (forward-Euler) update** valid for nearly-straight motion.

**Mark split (7):** Diagram + explanation `2m` · Exact arc equations `2m` · Limiting-case derivation `2m` · Conclusion `1m`.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute chassis velocities.**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.1}{2}(5 + 2) = 0.35\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.1}{0.4}(5 - 2) = 0.75\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute heading change and ICR radius.**

$$\Delta\theta = \omega \Delta t = 0.75 \times 2 = 1.5\ \text{rad}$$

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.35}{0.75} = 0.4\overline{6}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the exact arc-stretch update.**

With `(x, y, θ) = (0, 0, π/4) ≈ (0, 0, 0.7854 rad)` and final heading `θ + Δθ = 0.7854 + 1.5 = 2.2854 rad`:

$$x_{new} = 0 + 0.4\overline{6}\,[\sin(2.2854) - \sin(0.7854)] = 0.4\overline{6} \times (0.7602 - 0.7071) = 0.4\overline{6} \times 0.0531 \approx 0.0249\ \text{m}$$

$$y_{new} = 0 - 0.4\overline{6}\,[\cos(2.2854) - \cos(0.7854)] = -0.4\overline{6} \times (-0.6497 - 0.7071) = 0.4\overline{6} \times 1.3568 \approx 0.6332\ \text{m}$$

$$\theta_{new} = \pi/4 + 1.5 \approx 2.2854\ \text{rad}\ (\approx 130.95°)$$

**Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\xi_{new} = (0.0249\ \text{m},\ 0.6332\ \text{m},\ 2.2854\ \text{rad})}$$

**Mark split (7):** Velocity computation `2m` · `Δθ` and `R_ICR` `2m` · Arc-update application `2m` · Final numerical answer `1m`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** `ωR − ωL` gives *positive* `ω` for a *left* turn (CCW). If a student writes `ωL − ωR`, the sign is flipped and the entire trajectory is mirrored. Always state your sign convention explicitly.
> 2. **Degrees vs radians trap:** KTU 2024 expects radian answers for `θ` and `ω`. Mixing degrees will lose `1–2 marks` per occurrence.
> 3. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** The "rigid body, no slip, symmetric" assumptions are worth `1 mark` cumulatively. Examiners actively deduct for skipping them.
> 4. **Confusing `L` and `2ℓ`:** Use `L` as the full wheelbase throughout. If you switch notation mid-answer, marks are lost.
> 5. **Mixing up the Jacobian dimension:** A common slip is to write a `2×2` Jacobian. The correct forward-kinematic Jacobian is `3×2`. Examiners WILL check the dimensions.
> 6. **Linear-update when arc-update is asked:** When a problem specifies "exact update", the linear Euler form is *not* acceptable — expect to lose `2–3 marks`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A differential drive uses **two coaxial driven wheels** plus **one passive caster** for balance.
- [x] **Configuration (pose):** `q = [x, y, θ]ᵀ` — position `(x, y)` of axle midpoint + heading `θ`.
- [x] **Nonholonomic constraint:** `ẋ sin θ − ẏ cos θ = 0` (no sideways motion).
- [x] **Number of DOF (configuration space):** 3. **Number of actuators:** 2. **Effective planar DOF:** 2.
- [x] **Forward kinematics:** `v = (r/2)(ωR + ωL)`, `ω = (r/L)(ωR − ωL)`.
- [x] **Inverse kinematics:** `ωR = (1/r)(v + Lω/2)`, `ωL = (1/r)(v − Lω/2)`.
- [x] **Jacobian** `J(θ)` is `3 × 2`; its first two rows are linearly *dependent* — signature of nonholonomy.
- [x] **ICR** lies on the wheel-axle at distance `R_ICR = v/ω = (L/2)(ωR+ωL)/(ωR−ωL)`.
- [x] **Special cases:** `ωR = ωL` → straight line; `ωR = −ωL` → in-place rotation; `ωL = 0` → pivot about left wheel.
- [x] **Dead-reckoning update** can be done via **forward Euler** (linear) or **arc-stretch** (exact).
- [x] **Drift problem:** Encoder-only odometry accumulates error; a real robot cannot perfectly return to its start after a closed path.
- [x] **Real robots** that use it: Roomba, TurtleBot, Mars rovers (corner wheels), PackBot.
- [x] **Sign convention:** Right wheel faster than left → robot turns **left** (positive `ω` in CCW).
- [x] **Limiting wheel speed:** `|ωR|, |ωL| ≤ ω_max` from motor specs; commanded velocities must be saturated.
- [x] **Units:** `r, L` in metres; `ω` in rad/s; `v` in m/s; `θ` in rad.
- [x] **KTU 2024 weightage hint:** This topic is consistently worth `14–21 marks` in the ESE Module-1 section.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Synchronous Drive (Synchro Drive)** is a holonomic wheeled locomotion mechanism in which **all wheels are mechanically coupled** so that they (i) rotate at the **same angular velocity** about their own axles (synchronous drive) and (ii) steer to the **same orientation angle** about their vertical pivot axes (synchronous steering). Because of this mechanical coupling, the robot possesses only **two effective degrees of freedom in translation** (x, y) and can additionally control heading (θ) if a third body-rotation actuator is present.

The configuration was popularized by the **Nomad series of field robots** developed at Carnegie Mellon University and remains a benchmark mechanism in KTU Mobile Robotics coursework for studying *kinematically clean* omnidirectional platforms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a waiter carrying a round tray in a restaurant. No matter how he rotates his wrist, the cups on the tray all face the same direction and roll together without slipping against each other. A synchronous drive robot behaves exactly like this tray:

- **One motor = the waiter's wrist rotation** → makes all wheels spin together.
- **Second motor = the waiter's wrist tilt** → makes all wheels point in the same direction.
- The **tray (chassis)** stays put in orientation while the wheels underneath glide freely.

The result is that the **robot can glide sideways, diagonally, or rotate in place** with no skidding — something a car-like (Ackermann) or differential drive robot fundamentally cannot do.

> [!NOTE] **Why "Synchronous"?**
> The term *synchronous* comes from the Greek *syn* (together) + *chronos* (time). All wheels act **in time** — same speed, same direction, always.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are typically reported for synchronous drive platforms in KTU laboratory worksheets:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheel radius** | $r$ | **0.05 m to 0.15 m** |
| Wheel base radius (center to wheel) | $R$ | 0.20 m to 0.50 m |
| Number of wheels | $n$ | **3 (most common)** or 4 |
| Maximum translational speed | $v_{\max}$ | **0.5 m/s to 2.0 m/s** |
| Synchronization tolerance | $\Delta\omega$ | < 1 % between wheels |
| Degrees of freedom (motion) | DOF | **2 (translational) + 1 (heading)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The synchronous drive exploits **mechanical synchronization** rather than electronic feedback. Each step below describes a layer of the mechanism:

1. **Drive Synchronization Layer**
   - A single drive motor drives a central horizontal shaft.
   - Through timing belts, chains, or gears, this shaft is coupled to the axle of every wheel.
   - Therefore: $\omega_1 = \omega_2 = \dots = \omega_n = \omega_w$ for all $n$ wheels.
2. **Steer Synchronization Layer**
   - A second motor rotates a vertical "turret" shaft concentric with the drive shaft.
   - Each wheel is mounted on a vertical king-pin attached to this turret.
   - Therefore: $\phi_1 = \phi_2 = \dots = \phi_n = \phi$ for all wheels.
3. **Chassis Decoupling**
   - The chassis is mechanically isolated from the steer turret (it sits above or below).
   - The chassis does **not** rotate when the wheels steer.
4. **Translation Without Chassis Rotation**
   - By symmetry, the tangential wheel forces/torques cancel around the chassis center.
   - Net moment about vertical axis = 0 ⟹ **no chassis rotation** ($\dot{\theta} = 0$).
5. **Body-Rotation Override (Optional Third Motor)**
   - A third actuator (often an auxiliary motor or a clutched ring gear) rotates the chassis independently.
   - This converts the 2-DOF translational holonomic platform into a **3-DOF holonomic platform**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside tables to prevent markdown breaks. All formulas use **SI units**.

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = r \cdot \omega_w$ | Linear speed of every wheel = translational speed of robot | No wheel slip |
| 2 | $\dot{x} = v \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | World-frame X velocity | $\phi$ measured from chassis X-axis |
| 3 | $\dot{y} = v \cdot \sin(\phi)$ | World-frame Y velocity | — |
| 4 | $\dot{\theta} = \omega_b$ | Chassis angular velocity (3-motor version) | $\omega_b = 0$ in 2-motor version |
| 5 | $v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}}$ | Inverse kinematics — drive speed | — |
| 6 | $\phi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\dot{y}}{\dot{x}}\right)$ | Inverse kinematics — steer angle | Use `atan2` for full quadrant |
| 7 | $\text{ICR} = \infty$ | Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (in 2-motor version) | Pure translation |
| 8 | $\rho = \dfrac{v}{\omega_b}$ | Turning radius (in 3-motor version) | $\omega_b \neq 0$ |
| 9 | $a_c = \omega_b^{2} \cdot \rho$ | Centripetal acceleration (3-motor version) | Chassis rotation present |

## 2.3 Why and How the Synchronous Drive Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Utility (KTU Industrial Context)**
> Synchronous drives are used in (i) **hospital delivery robots** (e.g., HelpMate), (ii) **planetary exploration rovers** (CMU Nomad), (iii) **warehouse AGVs** that must dock sideways into racks, and (iv) **research testbeds** where *clean kinematics* (no slip, no skid) is required to validate SLAM and odometry algorithms.

The engineering appeal is **deterministic odometry**: because no wheel ever slips sideways, the encoder reading directly equals the chassis motion — making dead-reckoning extremely accurate compared to differential drive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Forward Kinematics of a 3-Wheel Synchronous Drive

**Given:** Three wheels of radius $r$ arranged symmetrically at $120^{\circ}$ on a circle of radius $R$ around the chassis centre. All wheels share drive speed $\omega_w$ and steer angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Velocity of wheel $i$ in its own direction.**
Each wheel contributes a tangential velocity along the wheel-rolling direction (which is the steer angle $\phi$):

$$
v_{w,i} = r \cdot \omega_w, \quad i = 1,2,3
$$

*Logic:* All three wheels spin at the same angular rate by mechanical coupling.

**Step 2 — Express each wheel's velocity in world frame.**
Wheel $i$ is positioned at angle $\alpha_i = \dfrac{2\pi(i-1)}{3}$ from the chassis X-axis. Its instantaneous rolling direction is $\phi$ (world frame). Therefore its velocity vector is:

$$
\vec{v}_{w,i} = r \cdot \omega_w \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\phi) \\ \sin(\phi) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum contributions to find chassis linear velocity.**
Because all three wheel velocity vectors are **identical** (same magnitude, same direction), the vector sum simply multiplies by 3 — but to obtain the chassis velocity we normalize by the kinematic constraint. Using rigid-body velocity composition:

$$
\vec{v}_c = \vec{\omega}_c \times \vec{r}_c + \vec{v}_{c/\text{frame}}
$$

For a non-rotating chassis ($\vec{\omega}_c = 0$), the chassis velocity equals the common wheel velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= r \cdot \omega_w \cdot \cos(\phi) \\
\dot{y} &= r \cdot \omega_w \cdot \sin(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Prove the chassis does not rotate.**
The torque (or moment) about the chassis centre contributed by wheel $i$ is:

$$
\vec{M}_i = \vec{r}_i \times \vec{F}_{f,i}
$$

The friction force at wheel $i$ acts in direction $\phi$ (rolling without slipping), so $\vec{F}_{f,i} = F_f \cdot [\cos\phi,\ \sin\phi]^{T}$. The moment about the chassis centre is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_i &= R \cdot F_f \cdot \sin(\phi - \alpha_i) \\
&= R \cdot F_f \cdot \bigl[\sin\phi \cos\alpha_i - \cos\phi \sin\alpha_i\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing over the three wheels ($i=1,2,3$ with $\alpha_i = 0^{\circ}, 120^{\circ}, 240^{\circ}$):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3} \cos\alpha_i = 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{3} \sin\alpha_i = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3} M_i = R \cdot F_f \bigl[\sin\phi (0) - \cos\phi (0)\bigr] = 0
$$

*Conclusion:* **Net moment = 0**, hence $\dot{\theta} = 0$. The chassis translates without rotating.

## 3.2 Derivation — Inverse Kinematics

**Given:** Desired chassis velocity $(\dot{x}_d, \dot{y}_d)$. Solve for required drive speed $\omega_w$ and steer angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Compute drive speed (magnitude).**

$$
v = \sqrt{\dot{x}_d^{2} + \dot{y}_d^{2}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_w = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\sqrt{\dot{x}_d^{2} + \dot{y}_d^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute steer angle (direction).**

$$
\phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\dot{y}_d}{\dot{x}_d}\right) \quad \text{(full-quadrant via } \operatorname{atan2}\text{)}
$$

*Logic:* Dividing $\dot{y}$ by $\dot{x}$ and applying $\arctan$ recovers the heading angle of the velocity vector, which by construction equals the steer angle of every wheel.

## 3.3 Derivation — Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)

The ICR is located at the intersection of perpendiculars to all wheel-velocity vectors. For the synchronous drive:

- Every wheel has velocity vector $v \cdot [\cos\phi,\ \sin\phi]^{T}$.
- Perpendicular to this is the line $\phi + 90^{\circ}$.
- All three perpendiculars are **parallel** (they never intersect in the finite plane).

Therefore:

$$
\text{ICR} = \infty \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Chassis angular velocity} = \frac{v}{\text{ICR}} = 0
$$

This geometrically proves the chassis does not rotate — a **classic KTU board question** asked in the form *"Show that the ICR of a synchronous drive lies at infinity."*

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Mechanism | Wheels | DOF | ICR | Can Move Sideways? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous Drive** | 3 or 4 | 2 (+1 optional) | $\infty$ | **Yes** |
| Differential Drive | 2 (active) + caster | 2 | Variable | No |
| Ackermann (Car-like) | 4 | 2 | Variable | No |
| Omni-wheel Drive | 3 or 4 | 2 or 3 | $\infty$ (3-omni) | Yes |
| Mecanum | 4 | 3 | $\infty$ | **Yes + rotate** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Synchronization Mechanism

The physical layout of a 3-wheel synchronous drive is complex (concentric shafts, king-pins, timing belts). The **block-level functional architecture** below captures the *interactions* between the two prime movers and the three wheels, which is the level KTU examiners test.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    drvMtr["DRIVE MOTOR - Provides common rotational speed"]
    strMtr["STEER MOTOR - Provides common steer angle"]
    drvShaft["DRIVE SHAFT - Horizontal coupling rod"]
    strShaft["STEER TURRET - Vertical concentric shaft"]
    whl1["WHEEL 1 - 120 deg position"]
    whl2["WHEEL 2 - 240 deg position"]
    whl3["WHEEL 3 - 0 deg position"]
    chassis["CHASSIS - Carries payload, no rotation"]

    drvMtr --> drvShaft
    drvShaft --> whl1
    drvShaft --> whl2
    drvShaft --> whl3

    strMtr --> strShaft
    strShaft --> whl1
    strShaft --> whl2
    strShaft --> whl3

    whl1 -. "rolling contact" .-> ground["GROUND PLANE"]
    whl2 -. "rolling contact" .-> ground
    whl3 -. "rolling contact" .-> ground

    chassis --- drvMtr
    chassis --- strMtr

    classDef motor fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef shaft fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wheel fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef frame fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class drvMtr,strMtr motor
    class drvShaft,strShaft shaft
    class whl1,whl2,whl3 wheel
    class chassis,ground frame
```

## 4.2 Wheel Layout — Top View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    robCtr((ROBOT CENTRE))
    whlA["WHEEL A"]
    whlB["WHEEL B"]
    whlC["WHEEL C"]

    robCtr --- whlA
    robCtr --- whlB
    robCtr --- whlC

    whlA --- whlB
    whlB --- whlC
    whlC --- whlA

    note1["Equilateral triangle - 120 deg spacing - wheel base radius R"]

    note1 -.- whlA

    classDef ctr fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef whl fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#fff9e6,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class robCtr ctr
    class whlA,whlB,whlC whl
    class note1 note
```

## 4.3 Control Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    setpoint["DESIRED VELOCITY - v_des, phi_des"]
    ctrlr["MOTION CONTROLLER - PI or PID"]
    driveCmd["DRIVE COMMAND - omega_w"]
    steerCmd["STEER COMMAND - phi"]
    bodyCmd["BODY COMMAND - omega_b optional"]

    setpoint --> ctrlr
    ctrlr --> driveCmd
    ctrlr --> steerCmd
    ctrlr --> bodyCmd

    driveCmd --> drvMtr2["DRIVE MOTOR"]
    steerCmd --> strMtr2["STEER MOTOR"]
    bodyCmd --> bodyMtr["BODY ROTATION MOTOR"]

    drvMtr2 --> synPlant["SYNCHRONOUS DRIVE PLANT"]
    strMtr2 --> synPlant
    bodyMtr --> synPlant

    synPlant --> encoder["ENCODER FEEDBACK"]
    encoder --> ctrlr

    synPlant --> robotMotion["CHASSIS TRANSLATION - x, y and theta"]

    classDef ctrl fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#1d6f1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cmd fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plnt fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#f5e1f5,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class setpoint,ctrlr,robotMotion ctrl
    class driveCmd,steerCmd,bodyCmd cmd
    class drvMtr2,strMtr2,bodyMtr,synPlant plnt
    class encoder fb
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

**Q: Define synchronous drive. Why is it called "holonomic" in the 2-motor configuration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **synchronous drive** is a wheeled locomotion mechanism in which all wheels are mechanically coupled so that they rotate at the **same angular velocity** and steer to the **same orientation angle** simultaneously.

It is called **holonomic** because the number of controllable degrees of freedom (2 — namely $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$) equals the dimension of the configuration space available for translation, allowing the robot to move in **any direction without re-orienting** its wheels first. The ICR lies at **infinity**, confirming pure translation.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for synchronous rotation+steering, 1 mark for holonomic justification with ICR.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: List any **three** advantages and **two** disadvantages of synchronous drive robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **No wheel slip** during translation → highly accurate odometry.
2. **Omnidirectional** motion (sideways, diagonal) without reconfiguration.
3. **Symmetric structure** → balanced dynamic loading.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Complex mechanical design** — concentric shafts and synchronization belts.
2. **Vibration and backlash** from timing belts → reduced accuracy at high speeds.

*(Mark split: 1 mark per correct point; any sensible engineering tradeoff accepted.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of a 3-wheel synchronous drive robot with a neat block diagram. Derive the kinematic equations relating wheel angular velocity, steer angle, and chassis linear velocity.**

**Model Solution:**
