**Step 1 — Mechanism (3 marks).**
A 3-wheel synchronous drive has three wheels mounted at 120° apart. A **drive motor** drives a common shaft coupled to all wheels via belts, ensuring equal angular velocity $\omega_w$. A **steer motor** rotates a vertical turret on which all wheels are mounted, ensuring equal steer angle $\phi$. The chassis sits on a separate bearing and does not rotate with the turret. [Block diagram as in Section 4.1 above.]

**Step 2 — Derivation (4 marks).**
Each wheel's velocity vector in world frame is:

$$
\vec{v}_{w,i} = r\omega_w[\cos\phi,\ \sin\phi]^{T}
$$

By symmetry of the 3 wheels on a circle, summing the friction moments about the chassis centre gives zero (as derived in Section 3.1), so $\dot{\theta} = 0$. The chassis velocity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= r \cdot \omega_w \cdot \cos(\phi) \\
\dot{y} &= r \cdot \omega_w \cdot \sin(\phi) \\
\dot{\theta} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**A 3-wheel synchronous drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m and is commanded to translate with velocity $(v_x, v_y) = (0.5,\ 0.3)$ m/s. Compute the required drive angular velocity $\omega_w$ and steer angle $\phi$. Also state the resulting chassis angular velocity.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Drive speed magnitude (2 marks).**

$$
v = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.3)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.34} \approx 0.5831\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Wheel angular velocity (2 marks).**

$$
\omega_w = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{0.5831}{0.1} \approx 5.831\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Converting to rpm: $\dfrac{5.831 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 55.67$ rpm.

**Step 3 — Steer angle (2 marks).**

$$
\phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.3}{0.5}\right) = \arctan(0.6) \approx 30.96^{\circ} \approx 0.5404\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Chassis angular velocity (1 mark).**

$$
\dot{\theta} = 0 \quad \text{(no body rotation motor specified)}
$$

---

### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Alternative]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**With suitable mathematical reasoning, prove that the instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR) of a 2-motor synchronous drive robot lies at infinity.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of ICR (1 mark).**
The ICR is the point in the plane (or at infinity) about which the rigid body instantaneously rotates.

**Step 2 — Geometric construction (3 marks).**
For each wheel, the perpendicular to its velocity vector passes through the ICR. In a synchronous drive, every wheel has the same velocity direction (angle $\phi$). Therefore the perpendiculars to all wheel velocities are **parallel lines** with direction $(\phi + 90^{\circ})$.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (3 marks).**
Parallel lines in the Euclidean plane **never intersect** in the finite plane. Therefore the ICR lies at infinity. The angular velocity of the chassis is:

$$
\omega_c = \frac{v}{\text{ICR distance}} = \frac{v}{\infty} = 0
$$

Hence the chassis undergoes **pure translation** — the defining property of a synchronous drive.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**Compare synchronous drive with differential drive in terms of (i) degrees of freedom, (ii) ability to move sideways, (iii) odometry accuracy, and (iv) mechanical complexity. Tabulate your answer.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Synchronous Drive | Differential Drive |
|---|---|---|
| (i) DOF | **2 translational + 1 heading (3-motor)** | 2 translational only |
| (ii) Sideways motion | **Yes (holonomic)** | **No (non-holonomic)** |
| (iii) Odometry accuracy | **High** (no slip) | Lower (slip during turns) |
| (iv) Mechanical complexity | **High** (concentric shafts, belts) | Low (2 motors + casters) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Payload (typical) | 10–50 kg | 5–500 kg |

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks per correctly compared parameter plus 1 mark for overall clarity.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Synchronous Drive Questions**
> 1. **Confusing steer angle $\phi$ with chassis heading $\theta$** — they are *independent*. In 2-motor versions, $\theta$ is constant; in 3-motor versions, $\theta$ is an extra state.
> 2. **Forgetting to use `atan2` instead of `arctan`** — a single arctan gives only the principal value $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ and **loses quadrant information**, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the units in the final answer** — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing SI units in numerical answers.
> 4. **Writing $|\dot{x}|$ using the pipe character** — KTU accepts $\vert \dot{x} \vert$ (LaTeX) but markdown parsing of literal `|` may break tables; always use `\vert`.
> 5. **Stating "ICR = 0"** — the correct statement is **ICR = $\infty$**, indicating pure translation. Saying "ICR = 0" implies the chassis rotates infinitely fast — a fatal conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Synchronous drive** = wheels share drive + steer via mechanical coupling.
- [x] Typically uses **3 wheels at 120°** or **4 wheels at 90°**.
- [x] Two motors minimum: **drive motor** + **steer motor**.
- [x] Optional third motor enables **independent chassis rotation**.
- [x] Forward kinematics: $\dot{x} = r\omega_w\cos\phi$, $\dot{y} = r\omega_w\sin\phi$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega_b$.
- [x] Inverse kinematics: $\omega_w = \frac{1}{r}\sqrt{\dot{x}^{2}+\dot{y}^{2}}$, $\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(\dot{y},\dot{x})$.
- [x] **ICR at infinity** ⟹ pure translation ⟹ holonomic behaviour.
- [x] **Advantages**: omnidirectional, no slip, accurate odometry.
- [x] **Disadvantages**: vibration, mechanical complexity, backlash.
- [x] **Real-world examples**: Nomad (CMU), HelpMate (hospital robot), Uranus research platform.
- [x] Always mention **mechanical synchronization via belts/chains/gears** when describing the mechanism.
- [x] Always state the **boundary condition** that the no-slip assumption holds (otherwise the kinematic model fails).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steered Wheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steered Wheels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **steered wheel** (also called a *steerable wheel* or *oriented wheel*) is a standard fixed-type wheel equipped with a vertical pivot axis (steering axis) that allows the wheel's rolling direction to be actively rotated relative to the robot chassis. Unlike a *castor wheel* (passively steered) or a *Swedish wheel* (omnidirectional), a steered wheel introduces a **controllable orientation degree of freedom** that is critical for executing non-holonomic maneuvers such as parallel parking, sharp U-turns, and Ackermann-style cornering in car-like mobile robots (CLMRs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Steered wheels are foundational to the kinematic modelling of wheeled mobile robots (WMRs). Every WMR configuration (differential drive, tricycle, Ackermann, 4-Wheel Car) is composed of a strategic combination of fixed and steered wheels. Mastery of the ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) is the gateway to deriving forward/inverse kinematics.

### 1.2 Physical Components of a Steered Wheel
A typical steered wheel assembly consists of:
- The **wheel** (rolling element) of radius $r$
- The **steering axis** (vertical pivot) passing through the wheel center
- The **rolling axis** (horizontal, perpendicular to the steering axis)
- The **suspension linkage** connecting the wheel to the chassis

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Shopping Cart vs. The Office Chair**
> Imagine pushing a **shopping cart** in a supermarket aisle. The two rear wheels are fixed (cannot rotate about a vertical axis), while the two front wheels are *steered* — you turn the handle, and the wheels pivot to follow a curved path. The wheels *roll without slipping* in the direction they are currently pointing.
> 
> Now compare this to an **office chair with castors**. The castors spin freely — the chair can slide sideways. A steered wheel **cannot** slide sideways. This property is the essence of the **non-holonomic rolling constraint**, which is mathematically the soul of mobile robotics kinematics.

### 1.4 Classification of Steered Wheels
| Class | Sub-Type | Description | Application |
|-------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Conventional Steered** | Ackermann Inner/Outer | Coupled via tie-rod for pure rolling | Cars, AGVs |
| **Independent Steered** | 4-Wheel Independent Steering (4WIS) | Each wheel steered independently | Heavy machinery, research robots |
| **Synchronously Steered** | Crab / Omni-Directional Mode | All wheels steered to same angle | Warehouse robots |
| **Actively Steered (Trailed)** | Trailer Steered Axle | Steered wheel of a passive trailer | Truck-trailer systems |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Classify wheels in mobile robotics"*, always begin with the four-fold classification: *Fixed, Steered, Castor, and Swedish/Spherical*. Steered wheels are **active**, while castor wheels are **passive** — examiners love this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Rolling Without Slipping Constraint
For a steered wheel, two simultaneous constraints must be satisfied at all times:

**Constraint 1 — Rolling Constraint (motion along the wheel plane):**
The velocity of the wheel center along the rolling direction must equal $\omega \cdot r$ (where $\omega$ is the wheel's spin rate).

**Constraint 2 — Lateral (Perpendicular) Constraint (motion along the steering axis):**
The velocity of the wheel center perpendicular to the wheel plane (i.e., along the steering axis) must be **zero** because the wheel cannot slide sideways.

### 2.2 The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)
When a robot with steered wheels moves, every wheel must rotate about a **single common point** at that instant — this point is the **ICR**, often denoted $P_{ICR}$ or simply $P$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem (Canny's Lemma for WMRs):** During any motion of a wheeled mobile robot, the projections of the wheel axes (both rolling axes and steering axes, when extended) must **concur** at a single point — the ICR. This is the geometric heart of all mobile robot kinematics.

### 2.3 Geometric Derivation of the Steering Angle
Consider a robot with two steered wheels on a common rear axle, separated by wheelbase length $L$ (distance between front and rear axles) and track width $W$ (distance between left and right wheels).

For the wheel to roll without slipping while rotating about the ICR:
- The **inner wheel** must turn at a *larger* steering angle than the **outer wheel**
- This is precisely the **Ackermann Condition**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables | Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Ideal Ackermann steering angle for vehicle center | $\alpha$ = steer angle, $L$ = wheelbase, $R$ = turning radius | Pure rolling, no slip |
| 2 | $\cot(\alpha_i) - \cot(\alpha_o) = \dfrac{W}{L}$ | Ackermann geometric condition | $\alpha_i$ = inner wheel angle, $\alpha_o$ = outer wheel angle, $W$ = track width | Both steered wheels share same ICR |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | Turning radius from steering angle | All as above | $\alpha \neq 90^\circ$ |
| 4 | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | Rolling constraint | $v$ = translational velocity, $\omega$ = wheel angular vel., $r$ = wheel radius | No slip |
| 5 | $\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0$ | Non-holonomic constraint (1 wheel) | $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$ = wheel center velocity, $\phi$ = steer angle | Wheel aligned with heading $\phi$ |
| 6 | $P_{ICR} = (x - \dot{y}/\dot{\theta}, \, y + \dot{x}/\dot{\theta})$ | Position of ICR from robot pose | $(x, y, \theta)$ = pose, $\dot{\theta}$ = angular velocity | $\dot{\theta} \neq 0$ |
| 7 | $v_i / v_o = R_i / R_o$ | Velocity ratio of inner/outer wheels | $v$ = velocity, $R$ = radius from ICR | Pure rolling |
| 8 | $\beta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{L_b \tan\alpha}{L_b - L_f \tan\alpha}\right)$ | Bicycle model slip angle | $L_f, L_b$ = front/rear wheelbase from CG | 4-wheel vehicle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Ackermann condition eliminates tire scrub during cornering. In real cars, this is achieved by a *steering trapezium* (mechanical tie-rod linkage). In AGVs and research robots, software-based *Ackermann steering* achieves the same goal via independent wheel control — a frequent KTU long-answer topic.

### 2.5 The Bicycle Model (Simplified Ackermann)
For kinematic analysis, a 4-wheeled car is often reduced to a **2-wheel bicycle model**:
- One virtual front wheel (with steering angle $\delta$)
- One virtual rear wheel (fixed)

The state equations are:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$$

$$\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$$

$$\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v}{L} \tan(\delta)$$

This is the foundational model for **autonomous car path planning** (e.g., Stanford's Junior, Google's Waymo ancestors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ackermann Steering Condition

**Setup:** Two steered wheels at the rear of a vehicle, separated by track width $W$. A single steered front wheel at the center. All wheels must roll without slipping about the same ICR.

**Step 1:** Let the ICR be at perpendicular distance $R$ from the centerline of the vehicle (i.e., the ICR is laterally offset by $R$ from the rear axle midpoint, and longitudinally by $L$ from the rear axle).

The inner wheel's distance to the ICR is:

$$R_i = R - \dfrac{W}{2}$$

The outer wheel's distance to the ICR is:

$$R_o = R + \dfrac{W}{2}$$

**Step 2:** By the no-slip rolling constraint, both rear wheels (treated as having zero steer angle) and the front wheels must rotate about the ICR. The center of curvature for each wheel's circular path is at the ICR.

**Step 3:** The steering angle of the inner wheel is the angle between the wheel's forward direction and the vehicle's longitudinal axis. From the geometry of right triangles:

$$\tan(\alpha_i) = \dfrac{L}{R_i} = \dfrac{L}{R - W/2}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly, the steering angle of the outer wheel is:

$$\tan(\alpha_o) = \dfrac{L}{R_o} = \dfrac{L}{R + W/2}$$

**Step 5:** We want to find a relationship between $\alpha_i$ and $\alpha_o$ that depends only on $L$ and $W$ (the vehicle's intrinsic geometry), not on $R$. Take the cotangent (reciprocal of tangent) of both expressions:

$$\cot(\alpha_i) = \dfrac{R - W/2}{L}$$

$$\cot(\alpha_o) = \dfrac{R + W/2}{L}$$

**Step 6:** Subtract the two equations:

$$\cot(\alpha_i) - \cot(\alpha_o) = \dfrac{(R - W/2) - (R + W/2)}{L} = \dfrac{-W}{L}$$

**Step 7:** Taking the absolute value (since inner angle > outer angle, but we want a positive relation), we get the canonical Ackermann condition:

$$\cot(\alpha_o) - \cot(\alpha_i) = \dfrac{W}{L}$$

This elegant result says: **The difference in cotangents of the outer and inner steering angles must equal the ratio of track width to wheelbase — a constant of the vehicle.**

### 3.2 Derivation of the ICR Coordinates from a Robot's Velocity Field

**Given:** A wheeled mobile robot at pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in the global frame, with translational velocity $v$ along its heading and angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ (yaw rate).

**Goal:** Locate the ICR in the global frame.

**Step 1:** The velocity of the robot's reference point is decomposed as:

$$v_x = \dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$$

$$v_y = \dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$$

**Step 2:** For pure rotation about the ICR, the velocity of the reference point is perpendicular to the line from the reference point to the ICR. Therefore, the ICR lies on the line that is perpendicular to the velocity vector and passes through the reference point.

**Step 3:** The line perpendicular to $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$ passing through $(x, y)$ has the equation:

$$(\dot{x})(X - x) + (\dot{y})(Y - y) = 0$$

where $(X, Y)$ is a generic point on the perpendicular line.

**Step 4:** Additionally, the ICR is at distance $R = v / \dot{\theta}$ from the reference point. The ICR lies on a circle of radius $R$ around the reference point:

$$(X - x)^2 + (Y - y)^2 = R^2 = \left(\dfrac{v}{\dot{\theta}}\right)^2$$

**Step 5:** Solving these two equations simultaneously (and using $v^2 = \dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2$), we obtain the closed-form ICR coordinates:

$$\boxed{X_{ICR} = x - \dfrac{\dot{y}}{\dot{\theta}}}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{ICR} = y + \dfrac{\dot{x}}{\dot{\theta}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Sign Convention:** When $\dot{\theta} > 0$ (counter-clockwise rotation) and the robot moves forward ($\dot{x} > 0$), the ICR should lie to the *left* of the robot (positive $Y$ offset relative to heading). The positive sign on the $\dot{x}/\dot{\theta}$ term in $Y_{ICR}$ confirms this — the ICR is on the left side for CCW rotation, which is geometrically correct for standard mathematical convention.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Bicycle Model State Equations

**Given:** A simplified two-wheel (bicycle) model with rear wheel fixed at origin and front wheel at distance $L$ ahead, steered at angle $\delta$.

**Step 1:** The rear wheel has no lateral velocity (non-holonomic constraint):

$$\dot{x}_r \sin(\theta) - \dot{y}_r \cos(\theta) = 0$$

This gives $\dot{x}_r = v \cos(\theta)$ and $\dot{y}_r = v \sin(\theta)$ where $v$ is the rear wheel's forward speed.

**Step 2:** The front wheel also cannot slide sideways — its velocity component along its *steering axis* must be zero:

$$\dot{x}_f \sin(\theta + \delta) - \dot{y}_f \cos(\theta + \delta) = 0$$

**Step 3:** The front wheel position is $(x_f, y_f) = (x_r + L\cos\theta, y_r + L\sin\theta)$.

**Step 4:** Differentiate and substitute into the front wheel constraint. After algebraic simplification:

$$\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v \tan(\delta)}{L}$$

This is the famous **kinematic bicycle model**, which is the basis for almost all autonomous vehicle control algorithms in industry and research.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Steered Wheel Geometries

| Geometry Type | DOF (Locomotion) | DOF (Steering) | Holonomic? | Typical Use |
|---------------|------------------|----------------|------------|-------------|
| **Differential Drive (2 fixed + castors)** | 2 | 0 | No | Indoor robots, vacuum cleaners |
| **Tricycle (1 fixed + 2 steered)** | 2 | 1 | No | Wheelchairs, small AGVs |
| **Ackermann Car (2 fixed + 2 steered)** | 1 | 1 | No | Automobiles, outdoor AGVs |
| **Synchronous 4WIS (4 steered + 0 fixed)** | 2 | 2 | No | Crab-walking construction vehicles |
| **4WID + 4WIS (4 driven + 4 steered)** | 3 | 2 | No | Heavy mining trucks |
| **Mecanum / Swedish** | 2 | 0 | **Yes** | Omnidirectional holonomic platforms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pearl:** "Holonomic" means the controllable DOF equals the total DOF in the planar workspace (3 for a ground robot: $x, y, \theta$). Ackermann and tricycle configurations are *non-holonomic* because they have only 2 controllable DOF but operate in a 3-DOF space.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Ackermann Steering Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driver Input or Autonomous Controller] --> B[Steering Angle Command delta]
    A --> C[Throttle Command v]
    B --> D{Steering Mechanism}
    D --> E[Left Front Wheel Actuator]
    D --> F[Right Front Wheel Actuator]
    E --> G[Inner Wheel Steer Angle alpha_i]
    F --> H[Outer Wheel Steer Angle alpha_o]
    C --> I[Drive Motor]
    I --> J[Left Rear Wheel omega_L]
    I --> K[Right Rear Wheel omega_R]
    G --> L[Instantaneous Center of Rotation ICR]
    H --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Vehicle Follows Curved Path of Radius R]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: ICR Computation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Read Robot Pose x, y, theta] --> S2[Read Velocities v and theta_dot]
    S2 --> S3{Is theta_dot approx 0?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Pure Translation ICR at Infinity]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Compute X_ICR = x - v_y / theta_dot]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Y_ICR = y + v_x / theta_dot]
    S6 --> S7[Verify Ackermann Condition]
    S7 --> S8{All Wheel Axes Concur at ICR?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Valid Motion - No Slip]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Slip Detected - Apply Correction]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Steered Wheel Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SensorLayer [Sensor Layer]
        S1[Wheel Encoder]
        S2[Steering Potentiometer]
        S3[IMU Yaw Rate]
    end
    subgraph ControlLayer [Control Layer]
        C1[Low Level Wheel Controller]
        C2[Steering Angle Controller PID]
        C3[Kinematic Model]
    end
    subgraph ActuatorLayer [Actuator Layer]
        A1[Drive Motor]
        A2[Steering Servo Motor]
    end
    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C2
    S3 --> C3
    C1 --> A1
    C2 --> A2
    C3 --> C2
    C3 --> C1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Conceptual Map: Wheel Types in Mobile Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[Wheeled Mobile Robot Wheels] --> F[Fixed Standard Wheel]
    W --> ST[Steered Standard Wheel]
    W --> C[Castor Wheel]
    W --> SW[Swedish or Spherical Wheel]
    F --> F1[Non steerable, non holonomic]
    ST --> ST1[Steerable, non holonomic]
    ST --> ST2[Ackermann Geometry]
    ST --> ST3[4 Wheel Independent Steering]
    C --> C1[Passive steering, non holonomic]
    SW --> SW1[Holonomic, omnidirectional]
    SW --> SW2[Mecanum and Omni wheels]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked to "draw the Ackermann geometry", always include: (1) the vehicle outline, (2) the inner and outer steered wheels with their angle arcs, (3) the ICR as a clearly marked dot, (4) the perpendicular dropped from the rear axle midpoint to the ICR, and (5) the labels $L$, $W$, $R$, $\alpha_i$, $\alpha_o$. Examiners allocate marks for *all* of these.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a steered wheel. How does it differ from a castor wheel in terms of control and constraint?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A steered wheel is a standard wheel mounted on a vertical pivot (steering axis) that can be actively rotated to a desired orientation. The **key distinction from a castor wheel** lies in *active vs. passive* control:
- A **steered wheel** has an actuated steering joint — its orientation $\phi$ is a *controllable input*.
- A **castor wheel** has an *unactuated* steering joint — its orientation is determined by the kinematics of the robot's motion (passive).
Both impose the non-holonomic rolling constraint ($\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0$), but only the steered wheel offers an extra DOF of control.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series 1]**
*What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)? Why is it significant in the kinematic analysis of steered wheels?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ICR is the unique point in the plane about which a rigid body (or wheeled mobile robot) is instantaneously rotating, even if the overall motion appears curvilinear. For a steered wheel system, **all wheel axes (rolling and steering) must concur at the ICR** for pure rolling without slip. Its significance includes:
1. It converts complex 2D motion into a simple 1D rotation about a fixed point.
2. It provides the geometric basis for the **Ackermann condition** $\cot(\alpha_o) - \cot(\alpha_i) = W/L$.
3. It allows direct computation of the **turning radius** $R = L/\tan(\alpha)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common pitfalls that cost students 3-5 marks in steered wheel problems:
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in $\cot(\alpha_o) - \cot(\alpha_i) = W/L$. The *outer* wheel's cotangent is subtracted from the *inner* wheel's cotangent — get this backwards and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up wheelbase $L$ with track width $W$.** Wheelbase is front-to-rear; track width is left-to-right. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Omitting the non-holonomic constraint equation** in kinematics derivations. Always state $\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0$ explicitly.
> 4. **Not drawing the ICR geometry diagram** — this alone costs 2 marks in long answers.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Tricycle / Ackermann Kinematics** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the geometric relationship between the inner and outer steering angles of a car-like mobile robot using the Ackermann steering condition. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a car-like robot with wheelbase $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$ and track width $W = 1.5 \text{ m}$, calculate the inner and outer steering angles when the vehicle takes a turn of radius $R = 8 \text{ m}$. Also compute the position of the ICR relative to the rear axle midpoint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.* The final result is:

$$\cot(\alpha_o) - \cot(\alpha_i) = \dfrac{W}{L}$$

Assumptions:
- Both steered wheels share a common ICR (pure rolling, no slip).
- The vehicle is rigid.
- The steering mechanism is ideal (no compliance or play).
- Small steering angles, rigid body motion in 2D plane.

**(b)** *Step 1: Identify vehicle parameters.*
$L = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $W = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $R = 8 \text{ m}$.

*Step 2: Compute the ideal steering angle (centerline) of the vehicle.*
Using $\tan(\alpha) = L/R$:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{2.5}{8} = 0.3125$$

$$\alpha = \arctan(0.3125) \approx 17.35^\circ$$

*Step 3: Compute inner and outer wheel distances to ICR.*

$$R_i = R - \dfrac{W}{2} = 8 - 0.75 = 7.25 \text{ m}$$

$$R_o = R + \dfrac{W}{2} = 8 + 0.75 = 8.75 \text{ m}$$

*Step 4: Compute inner and outer steering angles.*

$$\tan(\alpha_i) = \dfrac{L}{R_i} = \dfrac{2.5}{7.25} = 0.3448 \Rightarrow \alpha_i \approx 19.02^\circ$$

$$\tan(\alpha_o) = \dfrac{L}{R_o} = \dfrac{2.5}{8.75} = 0.2857 \Rightarrow \alpha_o \approx 15.95^\circ$$

*Step 5: Verify the Ackermann condition.*

$$\cot(\alpha_o) - \cot(\alpha_i) = \dfrac{1}{\tan(15.95^\circ)} - \dfrac{1}{\tan(19.02^\circ)}$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{0.2857} - \dfrac{1}{0.3448} = 3.5 - 2.9 = 0.6$$

And indeed, $W/L = 1.5/2.5 = 0.6$. ✓ Ackermann condition is satisfied.

*Step 6: Locate the ICR relative to the rear axle midpoint.*
The ICR is at:
- **Lateral offset** (perpendicular to vehicle centerline): $R = 8 \text{ m}$ to the side of the vehicle's center of curvature.
- **Longitudinal offset** (along centerline): $0 \text{ m}$ from the rear axle midpoint (the ICR lies on the line extending the rear axle).

In the vehicle's local frame (with rear axle midpoint as origin, forward as +X, left as +Y), the ICR is at $(0, R) = (0, 8) \text{ m}$ for a left turn.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): ICR-Based Kinematic Modelling** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, derive the expressions for the location of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) of a differential-drive mobile robot that has been retrofitted with a single steered front wheel (i.e., a tricycle configuration). State the non-holonomic constraints imposed by each wheel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tricycle robot has a steered front wheel at distance $L = 1.2 \text{ m}$ from the fixed rear axle. The front wheel is steered at $\delta = 25^\circ$ and the robot is moving forward at $v = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$. Determine:
  (i) The turning radius $R$ of the robot.
  (ii) The angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ of the chassis.
  (iii) The global coordinates of the ICR, given that the robot's current pose is $(x, y, \theta) = (2 \text{ m}, 3 \text{ m}, 30^\circ)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the general derivation.*
For a tricycle (1 fixed rear, 1 steered front):
- **Rear wheel (fixed):** Non-holonomic constraint: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$
- **Front wheel (steered at $\delta$):** Non-holonomic constraint: $\dot{x}_f \sin(\theta + \delta) - \dot{y}_f \cos(\theta + \delta) = 0$

ICR is the intersection of the rear wheel's axle line (extended laterally) and the line perpendicular to the front wheel's rolling direction (extended through the front wheel center).

$$X_{ICR} = x - \dfrac{\dot{y}}{\dot{\theta}}, \quad Y_{ICR} = y + \dfrac{\dot{x}}{\dot{\theta}}$$

**(b)** *Step 1: Compute the turning radius.*

$$R = \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta)} = \dfrac{1.2}{\tan(25^\circ)} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.4663} \approx 2.574 \text{ m}$$

*Step 2: Compute the angular velocity of the chassis.*

$$\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v}{R} = \dfrac{0.8}{2.574} \approx 0.3108 \text{ rad/s}$$

*Step 3: Compute the velocity components in the global frame.*

$$\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta) = 0.8 \times \cos(30^\circ) = 0.8 \times 0.8660 = 0.6928 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta) = 0.8 \times \sin(30^\circ) = 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 4: Compute the ICR coordinates.*

$$X_{ICR} = x - \dfrac{\dot{y}}{\dot{\theta}} = 2 - \dfrac{0.4}{0.3108} = 2 - 1.287 = 0.713 \text{ m}$$

$$Y_{ICR} = y + \dfrac{\dot{x}}{\dot{\theta}} = 3 + \dfrac{0.6928}{0.3108} = 3 + 2.229 = 5.229 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** ICR is located at $(0.713 \text{ m}, \, 5.229 \text{ m})$ in the global frame.

**Verification (Step 5 — sanity check):** Distance from robot to ICR should equal $R$:

$$\sqrt{(0.713 - 2)^2 + (5.229 - 3)^2} = \sqrt{1.655 + 4.968} = \sqrt{6.623} \approx 2.574 \text{ m} = R \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Steered Wheels:**

- **Definition:** A steered wheel is an *actively* orientable wheel pivoting about a vertical steering axis. It is *not* a castor (passive) or a Swedish wheel (omnidirectional).

- **Two Constraints Always Apply:** (1) Rolling along wheel plane $v = \omega r$, and (2) Zero lateral velocity (non-holonomic): $\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0$.

- **ICR Theorem (Canny's Lemma):** All wheel axes (rolling and steering), when extended, must *concur* at the ICR for slip-free motion.

- **Ackermann Condition (the gold formula):** $\cot(\alpha_o) - \cot(\alpha_i) = W/L$
  - Inner wheel turns *more* sharply than outer wheel.
  - Inner angle $>$ outer angle (this is the reverse of what intuition suggests — examiners love this trick).

- **Turning Radius:** $R = L / \tan(\alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the steering angle of the (virtual) centerline front wheel.

- **Bicycle Model (most-used simplification):** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\delta$.

- **ICR Coordinates:** $X_{ICR} = x - \dot{y}/\dot{\theta}$, $Y_{ICR} = y + \dot{x}/\dot{\theta}$ (valid when $\dot{\theta} \neq 0$).

- **Common Traps to Avoid:**
  - $L$ = wheelbase (front-to-rear), $W$ = track width (left-to-right). Never swap them.
  - The bicycle model has *one* virtual front wheel — not two.
  - Ackermann geometry applies to *non-holonomic* ground vehicles; it does **not** apply to Mecanum or holonomic platforms.

- **Real-World Linkage:** Modern autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla Autopilot, Mobileye) use the bicycle model with added dynamic extensions (slip, tire deformation, lateral load transfer) on top of the kinematic foundation you learned here.

- **Quick Memory Aid (Mnemonic):** **"Inner Is Larger"** — the inner wheel's steering angle is *larger* than the outer wheel's. **"OC-IC = W/L"** — Outer Cotangent minus Inner Cotangent equals Width over Length.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tricycle Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Tricycle Drive # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tricycle Drive** is a three-wheeled mobile robot locomotion architecture in which a single **steerable** wheel (front) is combined with **two non-steerable drive wheels** (rear) — or vice versa — such that the steering action is decoupled from the traction action. The robot's planar motion is controlled by two independent control variables: the **linear velocity `v`** of the drive wheel(s) and the **steering angle `α`** of the steerable wheel. The tricycle drive belongs to the class of **non-holonomic** wheeled mobile robots. Its kinematics is canonically approximated by the **bicycle model**, which collapses the two rear drive wheels into a single equivalent rear axle and represents the front steering mechanism as a single virtual wheel. This is the same kinematic abstraction used for car-like robots and Ackermann-steered vehicles. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEBRT522 — Module 1):** Tricycle Drive is introduced to establish the link between wheel configuration, instantaneous geometry, and the differential kinematic constraints that govern all car-like autonomous vehicles. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of an **auto-rickshaw** in Kerala. The driver controls: 1. The **throttle** (how fast the rear wheels spin) → this is the linear velocity `v`. 2. The **handlebar** (the angle the front wheel is turned relative to the chassis) → this is the steering angle `α`. When the handlebar is straight (`α = 0°`), the rickshaw goes straight. When the handlebar is rotated, the front wheel pushes the entire chassis into a curve. The rickshaw does **not** have any wheel that can move sideways — it must always "swing" its nose to change direction. This side-effect impossibility of lateral motion is what makes the tricycle drive **non-holonomic**. > [!TIP] > If you lock the steering and spin the wheel, you trace a circle. The radius of that circle depends on how much the handlebar is rotated — that geometric relationship is the **heart** of tricycle kinematics. ## 1.3 Physical Configurations of Tricycle Drive A tricycle drive can be realized in two principal configurations: | Configuration | Front Wheels | Rear Wheel(s) | Common Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Type 1 — Front Steer / Rear Drive** | 1 steerable (virtual) + 2 drive | Non-steerable, differential-locked pair | Car-like robots, autonomous AGVs | | **Type 2 — Front Steer (Ackermann pair) / Rear Drive** | 2 steerable (Ackermann geometry) | 1 central drive | Passenger cars, large research robots | For both configurations, the **bicycle model approximation** is used to derive the kinematic equations, because the two rear (or two front) wheels share the same axle and rotational speed. ## 1.4 Key Physical Parameters The following standard metrics and geometric quantities recur throughout tricycle drive analysis: - **Wheelbase `L`** — distance from the rear-axle midpoint to the front steerable wheel contact point (measured along the chassis). - **Steering angle `α`** — the angle between the front-wheel heading and the chassis longitudinal axis. Bounded as $\vert\alpha\vert < \pi/2$. - **Drive radius `r_d`** — radius of the drive wheel(s); determines linear velocity from wheel rotation. - **Heading angle `θ`** — orientation of the robot frame with respect to the global X-axis. - **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** — the unique point in the ground plane about which the rigid robot body instantaneously rotates. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Treating the two rear wheels as independently steerable. In a tricycle drive, the rear pair is **rigidly aligned** with the chassis — they translate and rotate as a unit, they do **not** pivot. --- ## 1.5 Reference Frames Used Two coordinate frames are used to describe the tricycle drive: - **Global (World) frame** $\{X_W, Y_W\}$: fixed to the ground; coordinates are $(x, y, \theta)$. - **Robot (Local) frame** $\{x_R, y_R\}$: attached to the chassis; the origin is conventionally placed at the midpoint of the rear axle, with $x_R$ pointing forward. The **pose** of the robot at any instant is the triplet $(x, y, \theta)$, where $\theta$ is the angle from the global $X$-axis to the local $x_R$-axis. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle (Bulleted Logic Decomposition) The tricycle drive operates on a **two-input, fully-actuated** control scheme at the velocity level. The following bullet-list captures the operational logic in the order in which the student must internalize it: - **Step 1 — Identify the Drive Source:** The drive wheels (rear, in Type 1) rotate at a controlled angular speed $\omega_d$. This produces a forward linear velocity $v = r_d \cdot \omega_d$ at the rear axle contact point. - **Step 2 — Identify the Steering Source:** The single front wheel is rotated about its vertical kingpin by an actuator, producing the steering angle $\alpha$ relative to the chassis $x_R$-axis. - **Step 3 — Locate the ICR:** Because the robot body is rigid and wheels do not slip, the *only* motion consistent with both wheel constraints is a pure rotation about a point in the ground plane. This point is the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. - **Step 4 — Compute Turning Radius:** The ICR is located on the line through the rear axle, perpendicular to the chassis. Its perpendicular distance from the rear axle is the **turning radius** $R$. The perpendicular distance from the front wheel to the ICR is the **wheelbase** $L$ rotated through the steering geometry. - **Step 5 — Compute Angular Velocity:** The angular velocity of the chassis is $\omega = v / R$. This $\omega$ equals $\dot{\theta}$, the rate of change of the heading angle. - **Step 6 — Propagate the Pose:** The change in global pose is computed by projecting $v$ onto the global axes using the current heading $\theta$. ## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Kinematic Constraint > **Why a single steering input fully specifies the turn?** > A rigid body undergoing planar motion has only three degrees of freedom $(x, y, \theta)$. A tricycle has **two** independently driven/steered inputs ($v$ and $\alpha$). The non-holonomic rolling constraint at the rear wheels (no lateral slip) removes **one** DOF, reducing the controllable DOFs to **two** — exactly the number of inputs. Hence, the system is *perfectly actuated* in the velocity sense. > **How is the ICR located geometrically?** > The velocity vector of a wheel must be perpendicular to the line joining the wheel contact point to the ICR. Therefore: > - For the rear wheel (velocity along $x_R$): the ICR lies on the $y_R$-axis of the robot frame. > - For the front wheel (velocity at angle $\alpha$ to $x_R$): the ICR lies on a line through the front wheel contact point, perpendicular to the front-wheel velocity direction. > - The intersection of these two lines uniquely determines the ICR. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **master formula sheet** for the tricycle drive topic. Memorize it before attempting any numerical. | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linear velocity of robot | $v$ | $v = r_d \cdot \omega_d$ | m/s | $\omega_d$ is drive wheel angular speed | | 2 | Turning radius (rear axle to ICR) | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\alpha}$ | m | Defined for $\vert\alpha\vert < \pi/2$ and $\alpha \ne 0$ | | 3 | Limit turning radius ($\alpha \to 0$) | $R_{\max}$ | $R \to \infty$ | m | Straight-line motion | | 4 | Limit turning radius ($\alpha \to \pm\pi/2$) | $R_{\min}$ | $R \to 0$ | m | Pivot-in-place (theoretical) | | 5 | Angular velocity of chassis | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{R} = \dfrac{v\tan\alpha}{L}$ | rad/s | Equivalent to $\dot{\theta}$ | | 6 | Forward kinematics — X | $\dot{x}$ | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | m/s | Global frame | | 7 | Forward kinematics — Y | $\dot{y}$ | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | m/s | Global frame | | 8 | Forward kinematics — heading | $\dot{\theta}$ | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v\tan\alpha}{L}$ | rad/s | Equivalent to $\omega$ | | 9 | Non-holonomic constraint | — | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | — | Pfaffian form; no lateral slip | | 10 | Inverse kinematics — velocity | $v$ | $v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}}$ | m/s | Magnitude of desired planar velocity | | 11 | Inverse kinematics — steering | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L\dot{\theta}}{\sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}}}\right)$ | rad | Use $\text{atan2}$ for quadrant safety | | 12 | ICR position (robot frame) | $(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR})$ | $(0, R) = \left(0, \dfrac{L}{\tan\alpha}\right)$ | m | Origin at rear-axle midpoint | | 13 | Curvature of path | $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\tan\alpha}{L}$ | 1/m | Inverse of turning radius | | 14 | Discrete pose update (Euler) | $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}, \theta_{k+1})$ | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$
$y_{k+1} = y_k + v\sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$
$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \dfrac{v\tan\alpha}{L}\cdot\Delta t$ | m, rad | First-order Euler integration | > **Note on absolute value notation in tables:** Throughout the KTU 2024 notes, magnitudes are written as $\vert x \vert$ (using the `\vert` LaTeX command) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The tricycle drive kinematic model is the **workhorse abstraction** for a vast engineering class: - **Autonomous Passenger Vehicles:** Every modern self-driving car (Waymo, Tesla, Mobileye) uses the bicycle/tricycle model as its **baseline local planner**, with higher-order corrections for slip and Ackermann mismatch. - **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Most Kiva-style and Locus-style robots in Amazon fulfillment centers use tricycle or car-like kinematics. - **Agricultural Robotics:** Tractor autonomy kits (John Deere AutoTrac, Trimble Ag) are pure tricycle/bicycle models with extended trailers. - **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance) use a rocker-bogie suspension, but the **path planner** operates on a tricycle-style model at the high level. - **Underwater and Surface Marine Autonomy:** The horizontal-plane motion of an AUV is described by the same equations, with `L` replaced by the rudder-to-thruster separation. > [!TIP] > If you understand the tricycle model, you can read **80% of the mobile robotics literature** on path planning, trajectory tracking, and control of car-like robots. Master it. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Every algebraic transition below is **explicitly written**. No steps are skipped, no intermediate results are abbreviated. The student should be able to follow this derivation line-by-line at examination speed. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Location of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) **Given:** - Tricycle drive in the **bicycle model** configuration. - Origin of the robot frame is at the midpoint of the rear drive axle. - Rear wheel contact is at $(0, 0)$ in the robot frame; front steerable wheel contact is at $(L, 0)$. - Front wheel is steered by an angle $\alpha$ from the chassis $x_R$-axis. **Step 1 — Velocity direction at the rear wheel.** The rear wheel is non-steerable, so its rolling direction is along the robot's longitudinal axis. Its unit velocity vector is: $$\hat{v}_{rear} = (\cos 0, \sin 0) = (1, 0)$$ **Step 2 — Velocity direction at the front wheel.** The front wheel is steered by angle $\alpha$, so its unit velocity vector is: $$\hat{v}_{front} = (\cos \alpha, \sin \alpha)$$ **Step 3 — Perpendicular to the rear-wheel velocity at the rear wheel.** A line perpendicular to $(1, 0)$ passing through $(0, 0)$ is the $y_R$-axis of the robot frame. Its equation is: $$x_{ICR} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Perpendicular to the front-wheel velocity at the front wheel.** A line perpendicular to $(\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)$ has direction vector $(-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$. It passes through $(L, 0)$, so its parametric form is: $$(x(\tau), y(\tau)) = (L - \tau \sin\alpha, \; \tau \cos\alpha)$$ **Step 5 — Intersect the two perpendiculars.** Setting $x(\tau) = 0$ from Step 3: $$L - \tau \sin\alpha = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \tau = \frac{L}{\sin\alpha}$$ Substituting $\tau$ back into $y(\tau)$: $$y_{ICR} = \frac{L}{\sin\alpha} \cdot \cos\alpha = \frac{L \cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{L}{\tan\alpha}$$ **Step 6 — State the result.** The ICR in the robot frame is therefore: $$\boxed{(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR}) = \left(0, \; \frac{L}{\tan\alpha}\right)}$$ > **Geometric interpretation:** The ICR lies on the lateral axis through the rear axle, at a perpendicular distance $R = L/\tan\alpha$ from the chassis. This perpendicular distance is the **turning radius**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Kinematic Equations **Given:** ICR location $(0, L/\tan\alpha)$ and rear wheel speed $v$. **Step 1 — Angular velocity of the chassis.** Since the entire robot is rigid and rotates about the ICR, the angular velocity $\omega$ is given by: $$\omega = \frac{\text{tangential speed at rear wheel}}{\text{distance from ICR to rear wheel}} = \frac{v}{L/\tan\alpha} = \frac{v \tan\alpha}{L}$$ Because the chassis heading $\theta$ rotates at this rate: $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\alpha}{L}$$ **Step 2 — Linear velocity in the global frame.** The chassis moves with linear speed $v$ along its own $x_R$-axis. The unit vector of the chassis $x_R$-axis in the global frame is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Therefore, the velocity of the rear-axle midpoint in the global frame is: $$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — State the complete forward kinematic model.** The forward kinematic equations of the tricycle drive are: $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \frac{v\tan\alpha}{L} \end{aligned}}$$ **Step 4 — Verification by dimensional check.** - $\dot{x}$ has units $[\text{m/s}]$ ✓ - $\dot{\theta}$ has units $[\text{m/s} \cdot 1/\text{m}] = [\text{rad/s}]$ ✓ **Step 5 — Verification by boundary conditions.** - When $\alpha = 0$: $\dot{\theta} = 0$, the robot moves in a straight line ✓ - When $\alpha \to \pi/2$: $\dot{\theta} \to \infty$ (theoretical pivot) ✓ - When $v = 0$: all derivatives are zero, the robot is stationary ✓ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Kinematics **Given:** A desired chassis twist $(\dot{x}_d, \dot{y}_d, \dot{\theta}_d)$. **Step 1 — Recover the linear speed $v$.** The desired linear speed is the magnitude of the planar velocity vector: $$v = \sqrt{\dot{x}_d^{2} + \dot{y}_d^{2}}$$ **Step 2 — Recover the steering angle $\alpha$.** From the third forward kinematic equation: $$\dot{\theta}_d = \frac{v \tan\alpha}{L} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \tan\alpha = \frac{L\dot{\theta}_d}{v}$$ Taking the inverse tangent: $$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}_d}{v}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}_d}{\sqrt{\dot{x}_d^{2} + \dot{y}_d^{2}}}\right)$$ **Step 3 — State the inverse kinematic result.** $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{\dot{x}_d^{2} + \dot{y}_d^{2}} \\ \alpha &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}_d}{\sqrt{\dot{x}_d^{2} + \dot{y}_d^{2}}}\right) \end{aligned}}$$ > [!WARNING] > In practice, the `arctan` function must be replaced by `atan2` to correctly handle all four quadrants of $(\dot{x}_d, \dot{y}_d)$. Students lose marks when they use plain `arctan` for the heading recovery. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Worked Numerical Example **Problem Statement (typical KTU 2-mark or 7-mark sub-part):** A tricycle-drive mobile robot has a wheelbase of $L = 1.0$ m. It is driven at a constant linear velocity of $v = 0.5$ m/s with a steering angle of $\alpha = 30°$. At the instant the robot is at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (2, 3, 45°)$, compute: - (a) the angular velocity of the chassis, - (b) the turning radius, - (c) the global-frame velocity components $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$. **Solution:** **(a) Angular velocity.** $$\omega = \dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\alpha}{L} = \frac{0.5 \cdot \tan(30°)}{1.0}$$ $$\tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$$ $$\omega = 0.5 \times 0.5774 = 0.2887 \text{ rad/s}$$ **(b) Turning radius.** $$R = \frac{L}{\tan\alpha} = \frac{1.0}{0.5774} = 1.7321 \text{ m}$$ Cross-check: $R = v/\omega = 0.5 / 0.2887 = 1.7321$ m ✓ **(c) Global velocity components.** $\theta = 45° = \pi/4$ rad, $\cos(45°) = \sin(45°) = \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.7071$ $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \text{ m/s}$$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Quantity | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Angular velocity $\omega$ | 0.2887 | rad/s | | Turning radius $R$ | 1.7321 | m | | $\dot{x}$ | 0.3536 | m/s | | $\dot{y}$ | 0.3536 | m/s | | $\dot{\theta}$ | 0.2887 | rad/s | --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Non-Holonomic Constraint Derivation The tricycle drive's wheels cannot slip sideways. For the rear wheel moving at the chassis origin, the velocity must be aligned with $x_R$: $$\frac{\dot{y}}{\dot{x}} = \tan\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ This is the famous **Pfaffian constraint** of car-like robots. It is non-integrable (cannot be reduced to a constraint on $(x, y, \theta)$ alone), which is precisely why it is called **non-holonomic**. > This is the same equation that appears in the Brockett condition for non-holonomic systems and is the reason a tricycle robot cannot be stabilized by a *smooth, time-invariant* state feedback — yet another reason this is examinable content. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following block-level functional architecture diagrams (Mermaid) describe the **kinematic information flow** of the tricycle drive system. Node IDs are alphanumeric and contain no reserved keywords. ## 4.1 Forward Kinematics Block Diagram (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["Input: Linear Velocity v (m/s)"] node2["Input: Steering Angle alpha (rad)"] node3["Input: Current Pose x, y, theta"] node4["Compute Curvature kappa = tan(alpha) / L"] node5["Compute Angular Velocity omega = v * kappa"] node6["Compute dtheta = omega"] node7["Compute dx = v * cos(theta)"] node8["Compute dy = v * sin(theta)"] node9["Integrate: x_new = x + dx * dt"] node10["Integrate: y_new = y + dy * dt"] node11["Integrate: theta_new = theta + dtheta * dt"] node12["Output: Updated Pose x_new, y_new, theta_new"] node1 --> node7 node1 --> node8 node1 --> node5 node2 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node3 --> node7 node3 --> node8 node3 --> node9 node3 --> node10 node3 --> node11 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node11 node7 --> node9 node8 --> node10 node9 --> node12 node10 --> node12 node11 --> node12 ``` ## 4.2 Tricycle Drive Wheel Layout (Top-View Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph robotFrame["ROBOT FRAME (Chassis)"] nodeA["Rear Axle (Non-Steerable Drive Pair)"] nodeB["Front Steerable Wheel (Kingpin Pivoted)"] nodeC["Wheelbase L (Geometric Constraint)"] nodeD["Center of Mass / ICR Reference Origin"] end subgraph ctrlInputs["CONTROL INPUTS"] nodeE["Drive Command: v (linear velocity)"] nodeF["Steer Command: alpha (steering angle)"] end subgraph wheelConstraints["WHEEL ROLLING CONSTRAINTS"] nodeG["Rear Wheel: velocity along x_R axis"] nodeH["Front Wheel: velocity at angle alpha to x_R axis"] nodeI["No Lateral Slip: dy_global / dx_global = tan(theta)"] end subgraph outputs["MOTION OUTPUTS"] nodeJ["Pose Update: x, y, theta"] nodeK["Instantaneous Center of Rotation: (0, L / tan(alpha))"] nodeL["Angular Velocity: omega = v * tan(alpha) / L"] end nodeE --> nodeA nodeF --> nodeB nodeA --- nodeC nodeB --- nodeC nodeD --- nodeA nodeA --> nodeG nodeB --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeK nodeG --> nodeL nodeH --> nodeL ``` ## 4.3 Inverse Kinematics Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR inpA["Desired Twist: xdot_d, ydot_d, thetadot_d"] inpB["Robot Parameter: Wheelbase L"] procA["Compute v = sqrt(xdot_d^2 + ydot_d^2)"] procB["Compute alpha = atan2(L * thetadot_d, sqrt(xdot_d^2 + ydot_d^2))"] outA["Command: Drive Velocity v"] outB["Command: Steering Angle alpha"] inpA --> procA inpA --> procB inpB --> procB procA --> outA procB --> outB ``` > **Reading the diagrams:** The forward-kinematics diagram (4.1) shows that a single cycle of pose update requires all three inputs and produces one updated pose. The wheel-layout diagram (4.2) emphasizes that the wheelbase $L$ is the **bridge** between the rear drive axle and the front steerable wheel. The inverse kinematics (4.3) shows that given the desired chassis twist, we must recover both $v$ and $\alpha$ as the two actuator commands. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define "Instantaneous Center of Rotation" (ICR) for a tricycle-drive mobile robot. How is it geometrically located for the bicycle model?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the unique point in the ground plane about which a rigid mobile robot instantaneously rotates during a curved motion. For the tricycle drive (bicycle model), the ICR is geometrically located at the **intersection of the two perpendiculars** drawn to the wheel velocity vectors at their respective contact points: - The perpendicular to the rear wheel's velocity vector (which lies along $x_R$) passes through the rear-axle midpoint and is aligned with $y_R$. - The perpendicular to the front wheel's velocity vector (which is at angle $\alpha$ to $x_R$) passes through the front wheel contact point. The intersection gives the ICR at robot-frame coordinates $\left(0, L/\tan\alpha\right)$, located at a distance equal to the **turning radius** $R = L/\tan\alpha$ from the rear axle. (3 marks) --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Why is the tricycle drive classified as a non-holonomic system? Write the Pfaffian constraint equation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A tricycle drive is **non-holonomic** because its wheels cannot slip sideways — the robot can only move in the direction of its wheel orientation. This imposes a velocity-level constraint that **cannot be integrated** into a position-level constraint on $(x, y, \theta)$ alone. The Pfaffian form of this non-holonomic constraint is: $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ The constraint enforces that the chassis's lateral velocity (in the robot frame) is identically zero. Despite this constraint, the robot can still reach **any** $(x, y, \theta)$ configuration in the plane — a hallmark of non-holonomic systems. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Module internal choice provides two question alternatives, each typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice]` **(a)** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a tricycle-drive mobile robot. Clearly define all variables and state the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tricycle-drive robot has a wheelbase $L = 0.8$ m. It moves with a linear velocity $v = 0.4$ m/s and a steering angle $\alpha = 20°$. Compute the turning radius, the angular velocity, and the ICR position in the robot frame. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** - The robot moves on a flat, horizontal plane (2D motion). - Wheels roll without slipping. - The bicycle model is valid: the two rear drive wheels are replaced by a single equivalent rear axle, and the front steering wheel is treated as a single virtual wheel. - The origin of the robot frame is at the midpoint of the rear axle. **Derivation:** Let $(x, y)$ be the global position of the rear axle and $\theta$ be the heading angle. Let $v$ be the linear velocity and $\alpha$ be the steering angle. The wheelbase is $L$. The chassis moves with speed $v$ along its $x_R$-axis. The unit vector of $x_R$ in the global frame is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Therefore: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta \quad\quad (1)$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta \quad\quad (2)$$ The instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) is located at $\left(0, L/\tan\alpha\right)$ in the robot frame (derived in Section 3.1). The angular velocity of the chassis equals the tangential speed at the rear axle divided by the turning radius: $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L/\tan\alpha} = \frac{v\tan\alpha}{L} \quad\quad (3)$$ Equations (1), (2), (3) constitute the **forward kinematic model** of the tricycle drive: $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \frac{v\tan\alpha}{L} \end{aligned}}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):** **Given:** $L = 0.8$ m, $v = 0.4$ m/s, $\alpha = 20°$. **Step 1 — Compute $\tan\alpha$:** $$\tan(20°) \approx 0.3640$$ **Step 2 — Turning radius:** $$R = \frac{L}{\tan\alpha} = \frac{0.8}{0.3640} = 2.1978 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Angular velocity:** $$\omega = \dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\alpha}{L} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.3640}{0.8} = 0.1820 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — ICR position (robot frame):** $$(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR}) = (0, R) = (0, 2.1978) \text{ m}$$ **Final Answer (boxed values):** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Turning radius $R$ | $2.20$ m | | Angular velocity $\omega$ | $0.182$ rad/s | | ICR position | $(0, 2.20)$ m in robot frame | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 Internal Choice]` **(a)** Explain the concept of inverse kinematics for a tricycle-drive robot. Starting from the desired chassis twist $(\dot{x}_d, \dot{y}_d, \dot{\theta}_d)$, derive the expressions for the required linear velocity $v$ and steering angle $\alpha$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast the tricycle drive with the differential drive mobile robot. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each, and indicate which is more suitable for tight indoor environments. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Inverse Kinematics Derivation (7 marks):** **Concept:** Inverse kinematics solves the *actuator-level command* problem: given a desired chassis-level velocity (the *twist*), find the values of $v$ and $\alpha$ that the drive and steering actuators must produce. **Derivation:** The forward kinematic model gives: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta \quad\quad (1)$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta \quad\quad (2)$$ $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\alpha}{L} \quad\quad (3)$$ **Step 1 — Recover $v$.** Square (1) and (2) and add: $$\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2} = v^{2}\cos^{2}\theta + v^{2}\sin^{2}\theta = v^{2}(\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta) = v^{2}$$ Therefore: $$v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}} \quad\quad (4)$$ **Step 2 — Recover $\alpha$.** From (3): $$\tan\alpha = \frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v} = \frac{L\dot{\theta}}{\sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}}}$$ Therefore: $$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}}{\sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}}}\right) \quad\quad (5)$$ Equations (4) and (5) constitute the **inverse kinematic model** of the tricycle drive. **Part (b) — Comparison (7 marks):** | Aspect | Tricycle Drive | Differential Drive | |---|---|---| | **Number of drive actuators** | 1 (or 2 Ackermann-linked) | 2 (independently driven wheels) | | **Steering mechanism** | Dedicated steerable wheel | Differential wheel-speed control | | **Kinematic DOFs vs actuators** | 2 inputs, 2 DOFs (after non-holonomic constraint) — perfectly actuated | 2 inputs, 2 DOFs — perfectly actuated | | **Turning radius** | Bounded away from zero: $R_{min} = L/\tan(\alpha_{max})$ | Can pivot in place: $R = 0$ achievable | | **Power efficiency** | Higher (less wheel scrub) | Lower (wheels scrub during turns) | | **Mechanical complexity** | Higher (steering linkage needed) | Lower (no steering mechanism) | | **Load capacity** | Higher (4 contact points in 4-wheel variant) | Lower (typically 2 driven wheels) | **Advantage / Disadvantage Summary:** - **Tricycle Advantage:** Higher mechanical efficiency on roads; natural extension to car-like vehicles; better odometry accuracy in straight-line motion. - **Tricycle Disadvantage:** Cannot turn in place; minimum turning radius is non-zero; mechanically complex. - **Differential Advantage:** Can rotate about its own axis (zero-radius turn); mechanically simple; highly maneuverable in tight spaces. - **Differential Disadvantage:** Wheels scrub during turns; lower odometry accuracy; inefficient on long straight paths. **Best Choice for Tight Indoor Environments:** The **differential drive** is generally more suitable for tight indoor environments (warehouses, hospital corridors) because it can achieve zero-radius turning, allowing the robot to navigate narrow aisles and rotate in confined spaces — a capability the tricycle drive fundamentally lacks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Tricycle Drive Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to square-and-add when recovering $v$:** Students often write $v = \dot{x}$ or $v = \dot{y}$ alone, missing the magnitude computation. (Loss: up to 2 marks) > 2. **Sign error in the steering-angle formula:** The correct form is $\tan\alpha = L\dot{\theta}/v$, not $v\tan\alpha / L$ inverted incorrectly. (Loss: 1–2 marks) > 3. **Treating the bicycle model as identical to the full four-wheel tricycle:** The bicycle model **neglects** the lateral wheel separation; students often lose marks for failing to state this assumption explicitly. > 4. **Using $\arctan$ instead of $\text{atan2}$:** A subtle bug that produces wrong quadrant results; examiners will deduct 1 mark if your derivation acknowledges the quadrant issue. > 5. **Failing to write the non-holonomic constraint:** Even when not explicitly asked, writing the Pfaffian constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ shows mastery and fetches 1 extra mark in many valuation schemes. > 6. **Mixing up $L$ (wheelbase) with $d$ (track width):** In the bicycle model, $L$ is the longitudinal wheelbase, **not** the lateral wheel separation. Mislabeling costs 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this the night before the exam:** - **Definition:** Tricycle drive = three-wheeled locomotion with decoupled steering and traction, modeled as a bicycle. - **Two control inputs:** Linear velocity $v$ and steering angle $\alpha$. - **Three outputs (chassis twist):** $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$. - **Forward Kinematics Equations (memorize verbatim):** - $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ - $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ - $\dot{\theta} = (v\tan\alpha)/L$ - **Turning radius formula:** $R = L/\tan\alpha$ — bound: $R \to \infty$ as $\alpha \to 0$, and $R \to 0$ as $\alpha \to \pi/2$. - **Angular velocity:** $\omega = v/R = (v\tan\alpha)/L$. - **ICR in robot frame:** $(0, L/\tan\alpha)$ — i.e., $(0, R)$. - **Inverse Kinematics:** - $v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}}$ - $\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L\dot{\theta}}{\sqrt{\dot{x}^{2} + \dot{y}^{2}}}\right)$ (use `atan2` in code). - **Non-holonomic Pfaffian constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. - **Discretization (Euler integration):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$, and similarly for $y$ and $\theta$. - **Bicycle model assumption:** Replaces paired rear/front wheels with a single virtual wheel — must be stated explicitly. - **Comparison with differential drive:** Tricycle = higher efficiency, bounded turning radius; Differential = zero-radius turn possible, mechanically simpler. - **Real-world applications:** Self-driving cars, AGVs, agricultural tractors, planetary rovers (high-level planner), marine AUVs. - **The single most exam-likely derivation:** Forward kinematics from the ICR definition. - **The single most exam-likely numerical:** Given $L$, $v$, $\alpha$, compute $R$, $\omega$, ICR position. - **Common pitfall:** Mixing $L$ (wheelbase) with $d$ (track width). Remember: $L$ is the **longitudinal** distance from rear axle to front wheel; $d$ is the **lateral** distance between paired wheels.

topics.title: Car Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Car Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Car-Drive Kinematics (Ackermann Steering Mobile Robot):**
> A *Car-Like Mobile Robot (CLMR)* is a wheeled ground robot whose motion mimics that of a standard automobile. It possesses a **fixed rear axle** and a **steerable front axle**, with the geometric constraint that all steered wheels rotate about a **single common Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. This constraint is called the **Ackermann Steering Geometry**, and it gives rise to a **non-holonomic** system that cannot achieve arbitrary instantaneous velocities but can reach any desired pose via path planning.

The mobile robot's configuration (pose) in the 2D plane is uniquely described by the state vector:

$$
q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $x, y$ = global Cartesian coordinates of the **rear axle midpoint** (the reference point).
- $\theta$ = heading angle of the vehicle (orientation of the longitudinal axis with respect to the global $X$-axis).
- The system has **3 degrees of freedom (DOF)** in configuration space, but only **2 controllable inputs** ($v$ and $\phi$), making it a classic under-actuated, non-holonomic system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: Driving a Maruti 800 in a parking lot.**
> When you turn the steering wheel, the **front wheels pivot** about a single point located on an extended line drawn from the rear axle — this point is the ICR. The entire car *rotates around that point* during an infinitesimal moment. Notice two things you (the driver) **cannot do**:
> 1. You **cannot slide sideways** — your car is constrained to roll forward/backward. This is the non-holonomic constraint.
> 2. You **cannot change orientation without moving** (no in-place spin like a tank or a differential drive robot).
>
> The "Car Drive" model mathematically captures exactly this everyday experience of a real automobile.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Parameters

The car-drive model is governed by the following standard physical parameters (often given in datasheets):

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value (Passenger Car) | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $L$ | **Wheelbase** (distance between front and rear axles) | **2.5 m** | meters (m) |
| $w$ | Track width (lateral distance between wheels) | **1.5 m** | meters (m) |
| $\phi$ | Steering angle of the front wheel (virtual) | $\pm 30°$ (max) | radians (rad) |
| $v$ | Linear velocity of the rear axle midpoint | 0 to 30 | m/s |
| $R$ | Turning radius (ICR distance) | variable | meters (m) |
| $R_{min}$ | Minimum turning radius | **5.0 m** | meters (m) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 of PEBRT522 explicitly covers the kinematic modeling of *Car Drive* as a canonical example of a *non-holonomic* wheeled mobile robot. Mastery of this section is essential to answer Part-B (14 mark) questions on mobile robot kinematics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bicycle Model — Analytical Reduction

To make the math tractable, the four-wheeled automobile is reduced to a **two-wheeled bicycle model**:

- **Rear wheel**: Fixed at the midpoint of the rear axle, oriented along the vehicle's heading $\theta$.
- **Front wheel**: A *virtual* wheel at the midpoint of the front axle, allowed to steer by angle $\phi$ relative to the longitudinal axis.
- The wheelbase $L$ is the distance between these two virtual contact points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the bicycle model?** It preserves the exact same kinematic constraints as the four-wheeled car (one ICR, one steering angle) while reducing complexity from 4 steerable wheels to 1. KTU examiners almost exclusively use the bicycle model in numerical problems.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Define the configuration state.**
The robot's pose at time $t$ is given by $q(t) = (x(t), y(t), \theta(t))$.

**Step 2 — Define the control inputs.**
The two independent control inputs are:
- $v(t)$ → linear velocity of the rear wheel (m/s)
- $\phi(t)$ → steering angle of the front wheel (rad)

**Step 3 — Impose the non-holonomic rolling constraint.**
The front wheel velocity vector is **perpendicular to the wheel itself** (pure rolling, no slipping). This is what generates the geometric relationship between the rear wheel, the front wheel, and the ICR.

**Step 4 — Express the ICR location.**
All three points (rear wheel, front wheel, ICR) form a right-angled triangle. The ICR lies on a line perpendicular to the front wheel, passing through the front contact point, and on a line perpendicular to the rear wheel (i.e., the lateral axis) passing through the rear contact point.

**Step 5 — Derive the angular velocity.**
The instantaneous angular velocity of the vehicle is:

$$
\omega = \dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\phi}{L}
$$

**Step 6 — Write the forward kinematics.**
Project the rear wheel velocity $v$ along the global $X$ and $Y$ axes using the heading $\theta$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation you need to answer any KTU question on Car Drive.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **State vector** | $q = (x, y, \theta)^T$ | Robot pose in 2D plane |
| 2 | **Forward Kinematics (FK)** | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | $X$-velocity |
| 3 | Forward Kinematics (FK) | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | $Y$-velocity |
| 4 | Forward Kinematics (FK) | $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$ | Heading rate |
| 5 | **Turning Radius** | $R = L / \tan\phi$ | Distance to ICR |
| 6 | **Ackermann Inner Wheel** | $\cot\delta_i = \cot\phi - (w / 2L)$ | Inner steering angle |
| 7 | **Ackermann Outer Wheel** | $\cot\delta_o = \cot\phi + (w / 2L)$ | Outer steering angle |
| 8 | **Ackermann Difference** | $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = w/L$ | Geometric identity |
| 9 | **Non-holonomic constraint** | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | No lateral slip |
| 10 | **Inverse Kinematics** | $\phi = \arctan(L / R)$ | Required steering angle |
| 11 | Inverse Kinematics | $v = R \cdot \dot{\theta}$ | Required linear velocity |
| 12 | **Steering limit** | $\vert\phi\vert \leq \phi_{max}$ | Physical actuator limit |
| 13 | **Min. turning radius** | $R_{min} = L / \tan(\phi_{max})$ | Tightest possible turn |
| 14 | **Path curvature** | $\kappa = 1/R = \tan\phi / L$ | Inverse of turning radius |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly write $\dot{\theta} = v \cdot \tan\phi$ **without the $L$ in the denominator**. The wheelbase $L$ is mandatory — this single omission will cost you **2 of 14 marks**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Self-Driving Cars (e.g., Waymo, Tesla Autopilot)**: Path planners use the bicycle model FK to predict vehicle trajectories 2–5 seconds into the future.
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses**: Car-drive kinematics enable smooth, energy-efficient turns along curved factory floors.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA's Curiosity / Perseverance)**: Despite rugged terrain, the rocker-bogie suspension is kinematically simplified to a car-drive model for navigation.
- **Underwater ROVs and AMRs**: When fitted with vectored thrusters, the same non-holonomic analysis applies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Kinematic Model (Bicycle Model)

**Given:**
- Rear wheel position $(x, y)$ with heading $\theta$.
- Front wheel position $(x_f, y_f)$ with steering angle $\phi$.
- Wheelbase $L$ (distance between rear and front contact points along the body axis).

**Goal:** Derive the differential equations $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$ in terms of $v, \phi, L, \theta$.

---

**Step 1 — Position of the front wheel.**

The front wheel is offset from the rear wheel by the wheelbase $L$ along the longitudinal axis of the vehicle. Therefore:

$$
x_f = x + L \cos\theta
$$

$$
y_f = y + L \sin\theta
$$

*Reason:* The vehicle's local $X$-axis (heading) is rotated by $\theta$ relative to the global frame, so we project $L$ along $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$.

---

**Step 2 — Velocity of the rear wheel.**

The rear wheel rolls in the direction of the heading. Its velocity vector has magnitude $v$ and direction $\theta$:

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta \quad \quad (1)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v \sin\theta \quad \quad (2)
$$

---

**Step 3 — Velocity of the front wheel (rolling constraint).**

The front wheel rolls without slipping, meaning its velocity vector is **perpendicular to the wheel's plane**. The wheel's plane makes an angle $(\theta + \phi)$ with the global $X$-axis. Therefore the front wheel's velocity components are:

$$
\dot{x}_f = v_f \cos(\theta + \phi)
$$

$$
\dot{y}_f = v_f \sin(\theta + \phi)
$$

where $v_f$ is the (unknown) speed of the front wheel's contact point.

---

**Step 4 — Differentiate the position equations.**

Differentiate the position of the front wheel from Step 1 with respect to time:

$$
\dot{x}_f = \dot{x} - L \sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}
$$

$$
\dot{y}_f = \dot{y} + L \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}
$$

*Reason:* Application of the chain rule, since both $x$ and $\theta$ are functions of time.

---

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $\dot{x}_f$ and $\dot{y}_f$.**

$$
v \cos\theta - L \sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} = v_f \cos(\theta + \phi) \quad \quad (3)
$$

$$
v \sin\theta + L \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} = v_f \sin(\theta + \phi) \quad \quad (4)
$$

---

**Step 6 — Eliminate $v_f$ using trigonometric identities.**

Multiply equation (3) by $\sin(\theta + \phi)$ and equation (4) by $\cos(\theta + \phi)$, then subtract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bigl(v \cos\theta - L \sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}\bigr) \sin(\theta + \phi) &- \bigl(v \sin\theta + L \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}\bigr) \cos(\theta + \phi) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reason:* This eliminates $v_f$ from the system. After expanding using the identities $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$ and $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$, the $v$ terms cancel by the Pythagorean identity, leaving:

$$
-L\dot{\theta}\bigl[\sin\theta \cos(\theta+\phi) + \cos\theta \sin(\theta+\phi)\bigr] = 0
$$

Apply $\sin(A+B)$ identity to the bracket:

$$
-L\dot{\theta} \sin(2\theta + \phi) = 0
$$

Since $\sin(2\theta + \phi) \neq 0$ in general motion, we have $\dot{\theta} = 0$ trivially — *this is a dead end, so we use a different elimination method.*

---

**Step 6 (Corrected) — Use the lateral (non-holonomic) constraint directly.**

Instead, observe that the front wheel's velocity has **no component along the wheel itself**. The wheel's direction unit vector is $(\cos(\theta+\phi), \sin(\theta+\phi))$. The component of the front wheel's velocity *along* this direction must equal $v_f$. The component *perpendicular* to the wheel must be zero. This perpendicular condition is:

$$
-\sin(\theta+\phi) \cdot \dot{x}_f + \cos(\theta+\phi) \cdot \dot{y}_f = 0
$$

Substituting the differentiated positions:

$$
-\sin(\theta+\phi)(v\cos\theta - L\sin\theta\dot{\theta}) + \cos(\theta+\phi)(v\sin\theta + L\cos\theta\dot{\theta}) = 0
$$

Expanding and grouping the $\dot{\theta}$ terms:

$$
L\dot{\theta}\bigl[\sin\theta\sin(\theta+\phi) + \cos\theta\cos(\theta+\phi)\bigr] = v\bigl[\cos\theta\sin(\theta+\phi) - \sin\theta\cos(\theta+\phi)\bigr]
$$

Apply the identities:
- $\cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B)$, so the LHS bracket becomes $\cos\phi$.
- $\sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B = \sin(B-A)$, so the RHS bracket becomes $\sin\phi$.

Therefore:

$$
L \dot{\theta} \cos\phi = v \sin\phi
$$

---

**Step 7 — Solve for $\dot{\theta}$.**

$$
\boxed{\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\phi}{L}}
$$

---

**Step 8 — Final forward kinematics.**

The complete forward kinematic model of the car-drive mobile robot is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{v \tan\phi}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
q\,'(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ v\tan\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Turning Radius

From Step 7, the angular velocity is $\omega = \dot{\theta} = v \tan\phi / L$. For a vehicle moving in a circle of radius $R$, the relationship between linear and angular velocity is $v = \omega R$. Therefore:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{v}{v \tan\phi / L}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** When $\phi = 0$ (no steering), $R \to \infty$ (straight line motion). When $\phi = \pm\phi_{max}$, $R$ is at its minimum (tightest turn).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Ackermann Steering Geometry (4-Wheel Model)

For a true 4-wheeled car, the inner and outer front wheels must steer at **different angles** so that all wheel axes intersect at a single ICR.

**Setup:**
- Wheelbase $L$, track width $w$.
- Virtual (bicycle) steering angle $\phi$ is the *average* of the two actual front wheel angles.
- Turning radius $R$ is measured to the *center* of the rear axle.

**Inner wheel radius:** $R_i = R - w/2$
**Outer wheel radius:** $R_o = R + w/2$

Using the bicycle-model relation $R = L / \tan\phi$ for each wheel independently:

$$
\tan\delta_i = \frac{L}{R - w/2} \quad , \quad \tan\delta_o = \frac{L}{R + w/2}
$$

Inverting these in terms of $\cot$:

$$
\cot\delta_i = \frac{R - w/2}{L} = \frac{R}{L} - \frac{w}{2L} = \cot\phi - \frac{w}{2L}
$$

$$
\cot\delta_o = \frac{R + w/2}{L} = \frac{R}{L} + \frac{w}{2L} = \cot\phi + \frac{w}{2L}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\boxed{\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = \frac{w}{L}}
$$

This elegant identity is the **Ackermann condition**: it must be satisfied for true rolling without tire scrub.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forward velocity prediction | $v, \phi, L, \theta$ | $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$ | FK equations |
| Trajectory simulation | $v(t), \phi(t), q(0)$ | $x(t), y(t), \theta(t)$ | Integrate FK using Euler/RK4 |
| Turning radius | $L, \phi$ | $R$ | $R = L/\tan\phi$ |
| Required steering | $L, R_{desired}$ | $\phi$ | $\phi = \arctan(L/R)$ |
| Ackermann angles | $L, w, R$ | $\delta_i, \delta_o$ | $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = w/L$ |
| Path curvature | $L, \phi$ | $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \tan\phi / L$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Car-Drive Kinematic Schematic (Bicycle Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Global Frame X Y"] --> B["Vehicle Pose State x y theta"]
    B --> C["Rear Axle Reference Point"]
    C --> D["Wheelbase L"]
    D --> E["Front Axle Contact Point"]
    E --> F["Steering Angle phi"]
    F --> G["Front Wheel Direction theta + phi"]
    G --> H["Instantaneous Center of Rotation ICR"]
    C --> H
    H --> I["Turning Radius R = L over tan phi"]
    I --> J["Angular Velocity omega = v tan phi over L"]
    J --> K["Linear Velocity v along heading theta"]
    K --> L["State Update xdot ydot thetadot"]
    L --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Ackermann Steering Geometry (4-Wheel View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Rear Left Wheel"] --> B["Rear Right Wheel"]
    B --> C["Front Right Outer Wheel delta_o"]
    C --> D["Front Left Inner Wheel delta_i"]
    D --> A
```
