```mermaid
    C --> E["ICR Instantaneous Center"]
    D --> E
    A --> E
    B --> E
    F["Track Width w"] --> A
    F --> B
    F --> C
    F --> D
    G["Wheelbase L"] --> A
    G --> C
    H["Condition cot delta_i minus cot delta_o equals w over L"] --> E
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Control Flow Architecture for Car-Drive Robot

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Reference Path xref yref"] --> B["Trajectory Planner"]
    B --> C["Inverse Kinematics Solver"]
    C --> D["Steering Command phi"]
    C --> E["Velocity Command v"]
    D --> F["Steering Actuator"]
    E --> G["Drive Motor"]
    F --> H["Bicycle Model FK"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Updated Pose x y theta"]
    I --> J["Feedback to Planner"]
    J --> B
    H --> K["Non Holonomic Constraint Check"]
    K --> L["Safety Layer"]
    L --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagram above shows the **closed-loop control architecture** for an autonomous car-drive robot. The planner generates a reference path; inverse kinematics converts it to actuator commands; the bicycle-model forward kinematics predicts the new pose; the non-holonomic constraint is continuously verified to prevent infeasible maneuvers (e.g., lateral motion requests).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. What is a non-holonomic constraint? Show that the car-drive mobile robot is non-holonomic.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A non-holonomic constraint is a velocity-level constraint that **cannot be integrated** to a position-level (configuration) constraint. It restricts the *instantaneous* allowable motions of the robot but does not restrict the *reachable* configuration space.

For the car-drive robot, the rear wheel cannot move sideways. The constraint is:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

**Proof of non-holonomic nature:** The constraint is linear in velocities and involves the configuration variable $\theta$. It cannot be written as $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$ (a position-level constraint), because integration would require $\theta$ to be a function of $x$ and $y$ only, which is not generally true for arbitrary paths. Hence the system is non-holonomic. The robot has 3 DOF but only 2 controllable inputs, confirming its non-holonomic nature.

---

**Q2. Define the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for a car-like mobile robot. How is it related to the steering angle?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the point about which the entire vehicle rotates during an infinitesimal time interval. For the bicycle model, the ICR lies on the line perpendicular to the rear wheel (i.e., on the lateral axis) and on the line perpendicular to the front wheel. The distance from the rear wheel midpoint to the ICR is the **turning radius**:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}
$$

As $\phi \to 0$, $R \to \infty$ (straight motion). As $\phi$ increases, the ICR moves closer to the vehicle, producing tighter turns.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a car-drive mobile robot (bicycle model) with wheelbase $L$, steering angle $\phi$, and linear velocity $v$. Clearly state all assumptions. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A car-drive robot has $L = 2.0$ m and maximum steering angle $\phi_{max} = 30°$. The robot is currently at pose $(0, 0, 0)$ moving with $v = 1.5$ m/s and $\phi = 20°$.
- (i) Compute the turning radius $R$ and angular velocity $\omega$.
- (ii) Find the pose $(x, y, \theta)$ after $\Delta t = 2$ seconds using Euler integration.
- (iii) What is the minimum turning radius of this robot? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1–8** above. The complete derivation yields:

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta \quad , \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta \quad , \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\phi}{L}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Pure rolling, no slipping at any wheel.
2. Bicycle model simplification (4 wheels → 2 virtual wheels).
3. Rigid body, flat ground (no suspension dynamics).
4. Steering angle $\phi$ is the same for both front wheels (averaged via Ackermann).
5. The reference point is the rear axle midpoint.

---

**(b) (i) Turning radius and angular velocity:**

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi} = \frac{2.0}{\tan 20°} = \frac{2.0}{0.36397} = 5.495 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v \tan\phi}{L} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.36397}{2.0} = 0.2730 \text{ rad/s}
$$

---

**(b) (ii) Euler integration with $\Delta t = 2$ s:**

Current state: $x_0 = 0, y_0 = 0, \theta_0 = 0$.

**Iteration 1** (from $t = 0$ to $t = 2$ s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= x_0 + \dot{x} \cdot \Delta t = 0 + (1.5 \cos 0)(2) = 0 + 3.0 = 3.0 \text{ m} \\
y_1 &= y_0 + \dot{y} \cdot \Delta t = 0 + (1.5 \sin 0)(2) = 0 + 0 = 0 \text{ m} \\
\theta_1 &= \theta_0 + \dot{\theta} \cdot \Delta t = 0 + (0.2730)(2) = 0.5460 \text{ rad} \approx 31.28°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pose at $t = 2$ s:** $(3.0 \text{ m}, \ 0 \text{ m}, \ 0.5460 \text{ rad})$.

*Note:* For higher accuracy, KTU may require **two sub-steps** of $\Delta t = 1$ s. With the above parameters, the answer would change slightly (you would update $\theta$ at the half-step). The single-step answer is acceptable unless the question specifies otherwise.

---

**(b) (iii) Minimum turning radius:**

At $\phi_{max} = 30°$:

$$
R_{min} = \frac{L}{\tan\phi_{max}} = \frac{2.0}{\tan 30°} = \frac{2.0}{0.57735} = 3.464 \text{ m}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Ackermann steering geometry for a four-wheeled car-like mobile robot. Derive the condition $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = w/L$. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** For a car-drive robot, $L = 2.5$ m, $w = 1.5$ m, and the desired turning radius (measured to the center of the rear axle) is $R = 10$ m.
- (i) Compute the virtual (bicycle) steering angle $\phi$.
- (ii) Compute the inner and outer Ackermann steering angles $\delta_i$ and $\delta_o$.
- (iii) Verify the Ackermann condition. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 3.3** above. The Ackermann condition ensures all four wheels roll without tire scrub. The condition is:

$$
\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = \frac{w}{L}
$$

**Neat diagram:** (See **Section 4.2** Mermaid block — a clear schematic with all four wheels, ICR, $L$, and $w$ labeled.)

---

**(b) (i) Virtual steering angle:**

$$
\phi = \arctan\left(\frac{L}{R}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{2.5}{10}\right) = \arctan(0.25) = 0.24498 \text{ rad} = 14.04°
$$

---

**(b) (ii) Inner and outer steering angles:**

$$
\delta_i = \arctan\left(\frac{L}{R - w/2}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{2.5}{10 - 0.75}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{2.5}{9.25}\right) = \arctan(0.27027)
$$

$$
\delta_i = 0.26482 \text{ rad} = 15.18°
$$

$$
\delta_o = \arctan\left(\frac{L}{R + w/2}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{2.5}{10 + 0.75}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{2.5}{10.75}\right) = \arctan(0.23256)
$$

$$
\delta_o = 0.22848 \text{ rad} = 13.09°
$$

---

**(b) (iii) Verification:**

$$
\cot\delta_i = \frac{1}{\tan(0.26482)} = \frac{1}{0.27027} = 3.7000
$$

$$
\cot\delta_o = \frac{1}{\tan(0.22848)} = \frac{1}{0.23256} = 4.3000
$$

$$
\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = 3.7000 - 4.3000 = -0.6000
$$

Wait — the sign convention. The correct statement is $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w/L$ when the inner wheel is on the left. Let me restate:

$$
\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = \frac{R_i - R_o}{L} = \frac{(R - w/2) - (R + w/2)}{L} = -\frac{w}{L}
$$

So the magnitudes satisfy $\vert \cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o \vert = w/L = 1.5 / 2.5 = 0.6$. ✓

The condition is satisfied.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Ackermann condition:** Many students write $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = w/L$ **without checking the sign**. The correct signed version is $-\cot\delta_i + \cot\delta_o = w/L$ (outer cot minus inner cot). The **magnitude** identity $\vert\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o\vert = w/L$ is what KTU expects. — *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting the bicycle model assumption:** If a question asks to derive FK for "a car-drive mobile robot", you **must state the bicycle model reduction** as the first step. — *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Euler integration with constant $v, \phi$:** Use $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ with the **current** $\theta$, not the final $\theta$. Many students erroneously use the final $\theta$ to compute the initial velocity. — *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 4. **Units in $\arctan$:** Always confirm the argument of $\arctan$ is dimensionless. $L/R$ is dimensionless (m/m), so the result is in radians. Convert to degrees **only at the end** of the problem. — *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Car Drive, Module 1**

- ☐ The car-drive robot (Ackermann steering) is a **non-holonomic** system with **3 configuration DOF** and **2 control inputs** ($v, \phi$).
- ☐ The **bicycle model** reduces the 4-wheeled car to a 2-wheeled equivalent for kinematic analysis — this is the standard KTU assumption.
- ☐ The **state vector** is $q = (x, y, \theta)^T$, where the reference point is the rear axle midpoint.
- ☐ **Forward Kinematics (FK) — memorize exactly:**
  - $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$
  - $\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$
  - $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v \tan\phi}{L}$
- ☐ The **non-holonomic constraint** equation is $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — represents "no sideways motion".
- ☐ **Turning radius:** $R = L / \tan\phi$. As $\phi \to 0$, $R \to \infty$ (straight); as $\phi \to \pm\phi_{max}$, $R \to R_{min}$.
- ☐ **Minimum turning radius:** $R_{min} = L / \tan(\phi_{max})$.
- ☐ **Ackermann geometry** (4-wheel): $\cot\delta_i$ and $\cot\delta_o$ differ such that the magnitude $\vert\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o\vert = w / L$.
- ☐ **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** $\phi = \arctan(L/R)$ and $v = R \cdot \dot{\theta}$.
- ☐ **Path curvature:** $\kappa = 1/R = \tan\phi / L$ — useful in trajectory planning.
- ☐ **Actuator limits** $|\phi| \leq \phi_{max}$ and $|v| \leq v_{max}$ define the feasible control set.
- ☐ **Integration schemes:** For simulation, use Euler or RK4 with step size $\Delta t \leq 0.05$ s for accuracy.
- ☐ **Common KTU trick:** A question may ask "show that the car-drive robot is non-holonomic but can still reach any pose" — answer involves showing that although instantaneous lateral motion is forbidden, the reachable workspace is still $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ via path planning.
- ☐ **Distinguish from other drives:** Car-drive is **not** omnidirectional; it **cannot** translate sideways or rotate in place — unlike differential drive, mecanum, or synchronous (Swedish) wheels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limbed Locomotion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Limbed Locomotion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Limbed Locomotion

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Limbed Locomotion** is a class of mobile robot propulsion in which discrete, articulated mechanical structures called **legs** (or limbs) are used to propel the robot body across a substrate through a sequence of **stance phases** (foot in ground contact) and **swing phases** (foot in air transfer). Unlike wheeled or tracked systems, limbed locomotion is *discontinuous* — the system propels itself by applying discrete reaction forces at a finite set of ground contact points, one foot at a time or in coordinated groups.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEBRT522)**, Limbed Locomotion is introduced as the foundational topic of Module 1 to establish the contrast between **terrain-adaptive** (legged) and **terrain-restricted** (wheeled) mobility. The taxonomy of legged robots is formally classified by the number of legs (degree of locomotion redundancy), the gait cycle (sequence and timing of leg transfers), and the stability criterion (static vs. dynamic equilibrium).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The student is expected to articulate the **advantages of legged systems over wheeled systems on uneven terrain**, derive the **stability margin** using the *support polygon*, and analyze popular gaits like the **wave gait**, **crawl gait**, **trot**, **pace**, **bound**, and **gallop**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are walking across a rocky beach. A car (wheeled robot) would get stuck or tipped over because its wheels need continuous, even ground contact. A tracked tank (tracked robot) does better but still struggles on boulders. **Your two legs**, however, are a masterpiece of engineering: at every instant, you choose a handful of stable rocks to step on, lift the rest of your body, and *throw it forward* through the air. That "throwing" of the body between stable support points is exactly what a legged robot does.

A **leg** is essentially a *discrete, repositionable prop*. The robot body is a payload that gets carried through space by re-planting these props in a sequenced, periodic manner. The pattern in which the legs are lifted and placed is called the **gait**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why legs?** The single biggest motivation is **terrain adaptability**. A robot with $N$ legs can negotiate steps, gaps, rubble, stairs, and unstructured natural ground that would defeat any wheeled platform. The trade-off is *mechanical complexity, energy cost, and control difficulty*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

The following standard metrics govern limbed locomotion and appear frequently in KTU numericals and derivations:

- **Number of Legs ($N$):** $1$ (hoppers, e.g., Boston Dynamics Handle), $2$ (humanoids, e.g., ASIMO, Atlas), $4$ (quadrupeds, e.g., Spot, ANYmal), $6$ (hexapods, e.g., ATHLETE, Weaver), $8$ (octopods, e.g., Lauron series).
- **Stride Length ($L_s$):** The distance the body translates during one complete cycle of a leg. Measured in **meters (m)**.
- **Step Length ($L_{step}$):** The distance a single foot moves during its swing phase relative to the body.
- **Cycle Time ($T$):** Time for one complete gait period.
- **Duty Factor ($\beta$):** Fraction of the cycle time a leg spends in stance. $\beta = T_{stance} / T$.
- **Center of Mass (CoM):** Point at which the body's mass is effectively concentrated. The CoM must be supported for stability.
- **Support Polygon ($\mathcal{S}$):** The convex hull of all ground-contact points (or feet). Stability requires the projection of the CoM onto the ground to lie inside $\mathcal{S}$.
- **Stability Margin ($S_m$):** Shortest distance from the CoM's vertical projection to any edge of the support polygon. Measured in **meters (m)**.
- **Zero Moment Point (ZMP):** Point on the ground where the net moment of the inertial and gravitational forces equals zero. Must lie inside $\mathcal{S}$ for stable locomotion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Klein & Krotkov, 1989):** *If a leg is in swing phase, it cannot contribute to support. Only stance-phase legs matter for stability.* This single rule drives the entire theory of legged gait stability.

### 1.4 Static vs. Dynamic Stability

A legged robot is **statically stable** if it can pause at *any instant* of its gait and remain in equilibrium without tipping. This is only possible if at every instant:

1. At least **three** non-collinear legs are in stance (for ground support), AND
2. The CoM projection lies inside the support polygon $\mathcal{S}$ of the stance feet.

If the robot must keep moving to avoid falling, it is **dynamically stable** — typical of bipeds and fast-running quadrupeds. Dynamic stability relies on the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** criterion and the angular momentum of the body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statically Stable Walking Theorem (Klein & Krotkov):** A walking machine with $N$ legs can exhibit statically stable walking only if the maximum number of legs that can be lifted at any instant is $N - 3$. So a 4-legged robot can lift only 1 leg at a time, a 6-legged robot can lift up to 3 legs, and so on. This theorem sets the upper bound on the **leg-lifting freedom** of any statically stable gait.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Legged Locomotion System

A legged robot is mechanically decomposed into three hierarchical subsystems:

1. **Body (Payload / Trunk):** Carries sensors, batteries, and compute. The CoM is assumed to lie on the body.
2. **Legs (Limbs):** Articulated kinematic chains — usually 2 or 3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) per leg. The foot is the *end-effector* that interacts with the terrain.
3. **Actuators:** Electric (DC, BLDC, servo), hydraulic, or pneumatic motors that drive the joints.

The locomotion problem reduces to a *periodic trajectory planning* problem: at every instant, the controller must decide **which legs are in stance, which are in swing, and what joint trajectories to execute** such that the body follows a desired path.

## 2.2 Phases of a Single Leg Cycle

Each leg independently progresses through a **periodic cycle** of two phases:

| Phase | Foot State | Function | Force on Foot |
|-------|------------|----------|----------------|
| **Stance Phase** | On ground | Supports body weight, propels body | Non-zero reaction |
| **Swing Phase** | In air | Repositions to next foothold | Zero reaction |

The **duty factor $\beta$** is the fraction of the total cycle time spent in stance:

$$\beta = \frac{T_{stance}}{T_{stance} + T_{swing}} = \frac{T_{stance}}{T}$$

**Special cases:**
- $\beta = 0.5$: Equal stance and swing — typical of fast dynamic gaits (trot, pace, bound).
- $\beta > 0.5$: Always at least $N-1$ legs in stance — *crawling* gaits.
- $\beta = 1.0$: All legs permanently in stance — *static standing*.

## 2.3 Stability Criteria — Derivation Setup

### 2.3.1 Static Stability

For a robot of total mass $M$ and CoM at height $h$ above the ground, the support polygon $\mathcal{S}$ is the convex hull formed by connecting all stance feet. Static equilibrium requires:

1. The vertical projection of the CoM onto the ground, $\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{\perp}$, must lie strictly inside $\mathcal{S}$.
2. The shortest distance from $\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{\perp}$ to any edge of $\mathcal{S}$ must be positive.

The **stability margin** is:

$$S_m = \min_{e \in \partial \mathcal{S}} \, d(\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{\perp}, e)$$

where $\partial \mathcal{S}$ is the boundary of $\mathcal{S}$ and $d$ is the perpendicular Euclidean distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three legs are minimal for static stability on a plane:** Two contact points define a *line* of support. The CoM can be anywhere on that line, but a small lateral perturbation perpendicular to the line creates an unbalanced moment. Three non-collinear points define a *triangle*, providing rotational stability in the plane.

### 2.3.2 Dynamic Stability — Zero Moment Point (ZMP)

When the body accelerates, the inertial forces $\mathbf{F}_{inertia} = -M \mathbf{a}_{CoM}$ contribute to the moment balance. The **Zero Moment Point** is defined as the unique point on the ground where the *net moment due to gravity and inertia* is zero.

For a robot with CoM at $(x, y, h)$ and CoM acceleration $(\ddot{x}, \ddot{y}, \ddot{z})$, the ZMP coordinates are:

$$x_{ZMP} = x - \frac{h(\ddot{x} \cdot M + \dot{L}_y)}{M g + \ddot{z} \cdot M}$$

$$y_{ZMP} = y - \frac{h(\ddot{y} \cdot M + \dot{L}_x)}{M g + \ddot{z} \cdot M}$$

where $L_x, L_y$ are components of angular momentum about the CoM. For the simplified planar case with negligible angular momentum rate:

$$x_{ZMP} \approx x - \frac{h \, \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}, \quad y_{ZMP} \approx y - \frac{h \, \ddot{y}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$

**ZMP Stability Criterion (Vukobratović, 1969):** A legged robot is dynamically stable if and only if the ZMP lies strictly inside the support polygon $\mathcal{S}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas are the *most-asked* in KTU ESE Module 1.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Duty Factor | $\beta = T_{stance}/T$ | $0 < \beta \le 1$ |
| 2 | Number of Stance Legs | $N_s = \lceil N \beta \rceil$ | For periodic gait |
| 3 | Max. Lifted Legs (Static) | $N - 3$ | Klein & Krotkov theorem |
| 4 | Stability Margin | $S_m = \min_{e \in \partial \mathcal{S}} d(\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{\perp}, e)$ | In meters |
| 5 | ZMP (planar) | $x_{ZMP} = x - h\ddot{x}/(g+\ddot{z})$ | Vertical CoM at $h$ |
| 6 | Wave Gait Period | $T = 2(N-1)\Delta t$ | Sequential leg lift |
| 7 | Energy of Lift per Step | $E_{lift} = M g h_{step}$ | Per leg lift |
| 8 | Specific Resistance | $\epsilon = P/(M g v)$ | Figure of merit |
| 9 | Stride Frequency | $f = v/L_s$ | $v$ = body velocity |
| 10 | Crab Walking Offset | $x_{offset} = (L_{step}/2)(N-1)$ | Hexapod wave gait |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** Whenever you write absolute value $\vert x \vert$ in a KTU exam answer, use the LaTeX `\vert` command — never the bare `|` character — to avoid breaking the markdown table parser in digital submissions.

## 2.5 Why Legged Locomotion? — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Legged? | Example Robot |
|-------------|------------|----------------|
| **Disaster Response** | Climb rubble, broken stairs, debris | Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYmal |
| **Planetary Exploration** | Negotiate rocks, craters, slopes | NASA's ATHLETE (6-legged) |
| **Search & Rescue** | Enter collapsed buildings, climb ladders | Quadrupeds with arms |
| **Humanoid Assistance** | Use human tools, navigate stairs | ASIMO, Atlas, Optimus |
| **Agriculture** | Climb orchard terraces, soft soil | AgBot quadrupeds |
| **Military Recon** | Cross trenches, rough terrain | Boston Dynamics Legged Squad Support System (LS3) |

**Disadvantages:** High mechanical complexity, low energy efficiency compared to wheels (energy cost of *lifting* the body), sophisticated control required, slower top speeds.

## 2.6 Comparison: Wheels vs. Legs

| Metric | Wheeled | Legged |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Energy efficiency on flat ground | **High** | Low |
| Step/stair climbing | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | High |
| Control complexity | Low | **Very high** |
| Speed | **High** | Moderate |
| Terrain adaptability | Restricted | **Universal** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Insight (Gabrielli & von Krumann, 1950):** For continuous motion, the **Specific Resistance** $\epsilon$ — power per unit weight per unit velocity — is a universal figure of merit. A perfectly efficient system has $\epsilon \to 0$. Typical values: $\epsilon \approx 0.05$ for a bicycle, $\epsilon \approx 0.2$ for walking humans, $\epsilon \approx 0.5$–$1.0$ for a hexapod robot.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Klein & Krotkov Static Stability Theorem

**Statement:** A walking machine with $N$ legs can exhibit statically stable walking if and only if it lifts at most $N-3$ legs at any instant.

**Derivation:**

Consider a legged robot with $N$ legs on a flat horizontal ground plane. For static stability, we require:
- The vertical projection of the CoM onto the ground must lie within the support polygon $\mathcal{S}$.

**Step 1 — Minimum stance legs for a planar support polygon:**

A polygon in 2D is *non-degenerate* (i.e., it encloses an area and resists rotation) if and only if it has at least **3 non-collinear vertices**. Therefore, a minimum of 3 legs must be in stance to form a non-degenerate support polygon.

**Step 2 — Maximum legs that can be lifted:**

If $k$ legs are lifted (in swing phase), the remaining $N - k$ legs form the support polygon. For this polygon to remain non-degenerate:

$$N - k \ge 3$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $k$:**

$$k \le N - 3$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{k_{max} = N - 3}$$

This is the **Klein & Krotkov theorem**. Application examples:
- $N = 4$ (quadruped): $k_{max} = 1$. Lift one leg at a time → *crawl gaits*.
- $N = 6$ (hexapod): $k_{max} = 3$. Lift up to 3 legs in tripod gait.
- $N = 2$ (biped): $k_{max} = -1$. Impossible. Therefore, bipeds are *never statically stable*; they require *dynamic stability*.
- $N = 1$ (hopper): $k_{max} = -2$. Always dynamically stable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Duty Factor and Average Number of Stance Legs

**Derivation:**

Let $N$ be the total number of legs. In a periodic gait of period $T$, each leg spends a fraction $\beta$ of $T$ in stance and $(1-\beta)$ in swing. Assuming legs are statistically independent (a "regular" or "periodic" gait), the **expected number of legs in stance at any instant** is:

**Step 1 — Stance probability per leg:**

Each leg has probability $\beta$ of being in stance at a random instant. So the expected count of stance legs is:

$$\mathbb{E}[N_s] = N \cdot \beta$$

**Step 2 — For static stability on the average:**

The average count of stance legs must satisfy $\mathbb{E}[N_s] \ge 3$:

$$N \beta \ge 3 \implies \beta \ge \frac{3}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Tabulating the minimum duty factor for static stability:**

| $N$ (legs) | $\beta_{min}$ | $\beta_{min}$ (decimal) | Interpretation |
|------------|---------------|-------------------------|----------------|
| 3 | $1.000$ | $1.00$ | Tripod must always be planted |
| 4 | $0.750$ | $0.75$ | Three legs down 75% of the time |
| 5 | $0.600$ | $0.60$ | Three legs down 60% of the time |
| 6 | $0.500$ | $0.50$ | Three legs down 50% — *tripod gait* |
| 8 | $0.375$ | $0.375$ | Three legs down 37.5% of the time |
| $N \to \infty$ | $0.000$ | $\to 0$ | Asymptotically wheeled behavior |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\beta_{min} = \frac{3}{N}}$$

This means: as the number of legs increases, the legged robot approaches a continuously-supported "wheel-like" system. This is one of the central reasons hexapods and octopods are popular in research — they can be statically stable with very fast, dynamic-feeling gaits.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ZMP Formula for a Simplified Biped

**Setup:** A planar biped with CoM at height $h$ above flat ground. Let $x$ denote the horizontal position of the CoM, $\ddot{x}$ its horizontal acceleration, and $g$ gravitational acceleration.

**Step 1 — Define the forces and moments acting at the foot:**

The ground exerts a normal force $N$ and a horizontal friction force $F$ on the stance foot. The net force on the CoM equals $M \ddot{x}$ horizontally and $M g$ vertically (assuming $\ddot{z} = 0$).

**Step 2 — Sum the moments about the candidate ZMP location $(x_{ZMP}, 0)$:**

Taking counterclockwise as positive, the moment due to gravity about $(x_{ZMP}, 0)$ is:

$$M_{gravity} = M g (x - x_{ZMP})$$

The moment due to inertia about the CoM, transferred to $(x_{ZMP}, 0)$, is:

$$M_{inertia} = -M \ddot{x} \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Set the net moment to zero (definition of ZMP):**

$$M g (x - x_{ZMP}) - M \ddot{x} \cdot h = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x_{ZMP}$:**

$$x - x_{ZMP} = \frac{h \ddot{x}}{g}$$

$$\boxed{x_{ZMP} = x - \frac{h \ddot{x}}{g}}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

- If $\ddot{x} = 0$ (no acceleration), the ZMP coincides with the CoM's vertical projection: $x_{ZMP} = x$.
- If $\ddot{x} > 0$ (accelerating forward), the ZMP shifts **backward** by $h\ddot{x}/g$ relative to the CoM. The robot must "lean forward" to balance.
- If $x_{ZMP}$ leaves the support polygon, the robot tips over.

**Step 6 — Energy of one step (additional related derivation):**

Each step lifts the CoM by an effective height $h_{step}$. The energy expended per step per leg is:

$$E_{step} = M g h_{step}$$

For a hexapod walking at frequency $f$ steps/s, the average power required just to *lift* the body is:

$$P_{lift} = N f \cdot M g h_{step} \cdot \beta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hexapods beat bipeds energetically in theory:** A hexapod can keep 3 or more legs on the ground at all times, so the CoM does not have to be "thrown" between single support phases. This dramatically reduces the energy dissipated in *landing impacts*, even though the absolute mechanical work is similar.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Legged Locomotion System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HMI[High Level Mission Planner] --> GPM[Gait Pattern Module]
    GPM --> FRP[Footstep Planner]
    FRP --> TM[Trajectory Module]
    TM --> IK[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    IK --> JTC[Joint Trajectory Controller]
    JTM[Joint Torque Module] --> JTC
    SENS[IMU and Force Torque Sensors] --> SE[State Estimator]
    SE --> ZMP[ZMP Calculator]
    ZMP --> STM[Stability Margin Monitor]
    STM --> GPM
    JTC --> ACT[Leg Actuators]
    ACT --> LEG[Physical Legs]
    LEG --> GRD[Ground Contact]
    GRD --> SENS
    ACT --> FB[Force Feedback]
    FB --> JTM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The mission planner chooses a gait (e.g., wave, trot). The footstep planner picks the next contact points on the terrain. The trajectory module generates a body path. Inverse kinematics maps foot positions to joint angles. The joint controller drives the actuators. Sensors close the loop through a state estimator, which feeds the ZMP and stability margin back into the gait pattern module. This is the *canonical control loop* of a statically or dynamically stable legged robot.

## 4.2 Gait State Machine for a Hexapod

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TripodA
    TripodA --> TripodB : Lift Right Front Left Mid Right Rear
    TripodB --> TripodA : Lift Left Front Right Mid Left Rear
    TripodA --> Wave : Switch to Wave Gait
    TripodB --> Wave : Switch to Wave Gait
    Wave --> TripodA : Switch to Tripod
    TripodA --> Stand : All Legs Down
    TripodB --> Stand : All Legs Down
    Stand --> TripodA : Resume Locomotion
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tripod Gait:** Two sets of three legs (tripods) alternate. Each set is in stance while the other is in swing. Duty factor $\beta = 0.5$. This is the **fastest statically stable hexapod gait**.

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing a Gait

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Number of Legs N] --> B{N equals 2}
    B -- Yes --> C[Dynamic Walking ZMP Control]
    B -- No --> D{N equals 4}
    D -- Yes --> E{Terrain}
    E -- Flat and Fast --> F[Trot or Pace Dynamic]
    E -- Rough and Slow --> G[Crawl Static]
    D -- No --> H{N equals 6 or more}
    H -- Yes --> I{Terrain}
    I -- Flat and Fast --> J[Tripod Static]
    I -- Rough --> K[Wave Gait Static]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Limbed Locomotion. List any two advantages of legged robots over wheeled robots. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Limbed Locomotion is a mode of mobile robot propulsion in which the robot body is moved by a sequence of *discrete contacts* between articulated legs (limbs) and the ground. Each leg alternates between a *stance phase* (foot in ground contact, supporting and propelling the body) and a *swing phase* (foot in air, repositioning to the next foothold).

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Superior terrain adaptability:** Can climb stairs, traverse rubble, and negotiate gaps that defeat wheels.
2. **Active suspension:** The body can be kept level on uneven ground by coordinated leg motion.
3. **Discrete foothold selection:** The robot can pick stable contact points, reducing the impact of bad ground patches.

---

### Question 2: Define Duty Factor. State the Klein and Krotkov theorem for static walking. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Duty Factor (1.5 marks):** The duty factor $\beta$ of a leg in a periodic gait is the fraction of the gait cycle period $T$ during which the leg is in the stance phase:

$$\beta = \frac{T_{stance}}{T}$$

**Klein and Krotkov Theorem (1.5 marks):** A walking machine with $N$ legs can exhibit statically stable walking if and only if it lifts at most $N - 3$ legs at any instant. Equivalently, at least 3 legs must remain in stance to maintain a non-degenerate support polygon.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[Internal Choice Option A]`

### Question A: `(a)` Derive the condition for static stability of a legged robot. State the Klein and Krotkov theorem with proof. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the support polygon $\mathcal{S}$ as the convex hull of all stance feet projected onto the ground. Define the CoM vertical projection $\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{\perp}$. Static stability requires $\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{\perp} \in \mathcal{S}$ (i.e., the projection lies strictly inside the polygon, not on the boundary).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** A planar support polygon is non-degenerate (encloses area, resists rotation) only if it has at least 3 non-collinear vertices. Hence, at least 3 legs must be in stance:

$$N - k \ge 3 \implies k \le N - 3$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Therefore, the maximum number of legs that can be lifted at any instant is $k_{max} = N - 3$. This is the **Klein and Krotkov theorem**.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Examples:
- $N=4$: $k_{max} = 1$ → crawl gait (lift one leg at a time).
- $N=6$: $k_{max} = 3$ → tripod gait (lift three legs at a time).
- $N=2$: $k_{max} = -1$ → impossible → bipeds are not statically stable; they require *dynamic* stability (ZMP).

---

### Question A: `(b)` Explain the wave gait of a six-legged robot with a neat state transition diagram. Compute its period. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** In a **wave gait**, legs are lifted in a *sequential* manner starting from the rear and propagating to the front (or vice versa) on each side. The legs of a hexapod are conventionally numbered 1 (front right), 2 (mid right), 3 (rear right), 4 (rear left), 5 (mid left), 6 (front left). A typical wave-gait sequence: 6 → 5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The wave gait maintains the *maximum* stability margin at all times because at any instant, the support polygon is as large as possible (5 legs in stance out of 6). The body sways laterally to shift the CoM over the tripod of stance feet, then a single leg is lifted.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Period of the wave gait:** Let $\Delta t$ be the time interval between successive leg lifts. A complete wave-gait cycle has $2(N-1) = 2(6-1) = 10$ leg lift events. Therefore:

$$T_{wave} = 2(N-1)\Delta t = 10 \Delta t$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Speed: each leg lift advances the body by approximately one-half step. The duty factor for a wave gait is $\beta = (N-1)/N = 5/6 \approx 0.833$. This is the *slowest* but *most stable* hexapod gait. It is the preferred gait on *rough, uneven, or unknown* terrain.

**State Transition (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state WG1[Leg6 Swing] >> Stance5
    Stance5 --> WG1
    state WG2[Leg5 Swing] >> Stance5
    Stance5 --> WG2
    state WG3[Leg4 Swing]
    WG2 --> WG3
    state WG4[Leg3 Swing]
    WG3 --> WG4
    state WG5[Leg2 Swing]
    WG4 --> WG5
    state WG6[Leg1 Swing]
    WG5 --> WG6
    state WG7[Leg1 Swing] >> Return Wave
    WG6 --> WG7
    WG7 --> Stance5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming note for stateDiagram-v2:** Mermaid does not allow reserved words like `end` as node IDs. The block above uses prefixed identifiers like `WG1`, `WG2`, ... to remain compliant with the alpha-prefix rule.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[Internal Choice Option B]`

### Question B: `(a)` Define Zero Moment Point (ZMP). Derive the ZMP coordinate for a planar biped. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** **Definition:** The Zero Moment Point is the unique point on the ground where the net moment of the inertial forces and the gravitational forces acting on the robot equals zero. **Stability Criterion (Vukobratović, 1969):** A legged robot is dynamically stable if and only if the ZMP lies strictly inside the support polygon formed by the stance feet.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Setup:** Consider a planar biped with CoM at $(x, h)$. The CoM has horizontal acceleration $\ddot{x}$ and vertical acceleration $\ddot{z}$. Let the stance foot apply a normal force $N$ and a friction force $F$ at the candidate ZMP location $(x_{ZMP}, 0)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Newton-Euler equations** (forces):
- Horizontal: $F = M \ddot{x}$
- Vertical: $N - M g = M \ddot{z}$, so $N = M(g + \ddot{z})$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Sum of moments about $(x_{ZMP}, 0)$ = 0 (definition of ZMP):**

$$M g (x - x_{ZMP}) - M \ddot{x} \cdot h = 0$$

Solving for $x_{ZMP}$:

$$\boxed{x_{ZMP} = x - \frac{h \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}}$$

**Step 5 (optional, in KTU answer — bonus):** For most walking gaits $\ddot{z} \approx 0$, simplifying to $x_{ZMP} \approx x - (h \ddot{x})/g$.

---

### Question B: `(b)` Compare statically stable and dynamically stable walking. Give one example robot for each category and state the condition for stability in each case. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2.5 marks):** **Static Stability:** A robot is statically stable if it can pause at *any instant* of its gait and remain in equilibrium without falling. This requires:
- At least 3 non-collinear legs in stance at every instant.
- The CoM vertical projection $\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{\perp}$ must lie inside the support polygon $\mathcal{S}$ (Stability margin $S_m > 0$).
- *No requirement* on body momentum.
- **Example:** Boston Dynamics **Spot** walking in *creep mode*, or any statically-walking hexapod using a wave gait.

**Step 2 (2.5 marks):** **Dynamic Stability:** A robot is dynamically stable if it is in equilibrium only *on average* or *while moving*, but would fall if it were paused. This requires:
- Active control of body momentum.
- The ZMP must lie inside the support polygon (Vukobratović criterion).
- Or equivalently, the angular momentum about the CoM must be regulated to prevent tipping.
- **Example:** Boston Dynamics **Atlas** (biped humanoid) performing a backflip, Honda **ASIMO** climbing stairs.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Minimum legs | 3 (Klein and Krotkov) | 1 (hopper), 2 (biped) |
| Pause behavior | Holds position | Falls over |
| Control complexity | Low (geometric) | High (dynamic, ZMP, MPC) |
| Energy | Higher (slow gait) | Lower (efficient momentum transfer) |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Terrain | Static-stable only | Can be used for running |

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Bipeds are *inherently* dynamically stable because the Klein and Krotkov theorem gives $k_{max} = -1$, meaning statically stable bipedal walking is *mathematically impossible*. This is why every humanoid robot uses ZMP-based dynamic control.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in Module 1 (Limbed Locomotion) — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Stating "two legs provide static stability":** WRONG. A biped is NEVER statically stable. The minimum number of legs for static stability is **3**, and you must quote the Klein and Krotkov theorem: at least 3 stance legs in a non-collinear configuration.
>
> 2. **Confusing duty factor with stride length:** Duty factor is a *time ratio* (dimensionless, $0 < \beta \le 1$). Stride length is a *distance* (meters). Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit confusion.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the convex hull in the support polygon definition:** A "support polygon" is the **convex hull** of the foot contact points, not the polygon formed by connecting them in arbitrary order. Without convex hull, the support region is not necessarily convex, and the stability criterion fails.
>
> 4. **In ZMP derivations, forgetting the moment of the inertia force:** Many students write only the gravitational moment and forget the $-M \ddot{x} h$ inertial term. The ZMP is *defined* as the point where the **sum** of moments is zero.
>
> 5. **Saying "tripod gait lifts 3 legs at a time" without stating which 3:** You must specify that the **alternating tripods** are *front-right + mid-left + rear-right* and *front-left + mid-right + rear-left*. The examiner will not give full credit otherwise.
>
> 6. **Writing the Klein and Krotkov theorem as "N/2 legs must remain in stance":** That is the *tripod gait* heuristic, not the theorem. The exact theorem is **at least 3 stance legs at every instant**, or equivalently, $k_{max} = N - 3$ lifted legs.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Limbed Locomotion = body propelled by sequential, discrete ground contacts through articulated legs.
- ☐ **Two phases of a leg cycle:** Stance (foot down) and Swing (foot in air). Duty factor $\beta = T_{stance}/T$.
- ☐ **Klein and Krotkov theorem:** At any instant, **at least 3 legs** must be in stance for static stability. Max lift = $N - 3$.
- ☐ **Bipeds are *never* statically stable** — they require dynamic (ZMP) control.
- ☐ **Support polygon $\mathcal{S}$** = convex hull of stance feet. CoM vertical projection must lie inside $\mathcal{S}$.
- ☐ **Stability margin** = shortest distance from CoM projection to any edge of $\mathcal{S}$.
- ☐ **ZMP definition** = ground point where net moment of gravity + inertia = 0.
- ☐ **ZMP formula (planar):** $x_{ZMP} = x - h\ddot{x}/(g + \ddot{z})$.
- ☐ **ZMP stability criterion (Vukobratović):** $x_{ZMP}$ must lie inside $\mathcal{S}$.
- ☐ **Wave gait (hexapod):** Sequential leg lift, slowest, most stable, $\beta = 5/6$.
- ☐ **Tripod gait (hexapod):** Two alternating tripods of 3 legs, fastest statically stable, $\beta = 0.5$.
- ☐ **Crawl gait (quadruped):** Lift one leg at a time, $\beta = 0.75$, statically stable.
- ☐ **Trot (quadruped):** Diagonal pairs, $\beta = 0.5$, dynamic.
- ☐ **Pace (quadruped):** Lateral pairs, $\beta = 0.5$, dynamic.
- ☐ **Bound and gallop (quadruped):** Dynamic, fast, used by cheetah robots.
- ☐ **Energy of step:** $E_{step} = M g h_{step}$. Specific resistance $\epsilon = P/(M g v)$ is the universal figure of merit.
- ☐ **Hexapod leg-lifting freedom:** $k_{max} = 6 - 3 = 3$ → tripod gait uses this maximum.
- ☐ **Numbering convention:** For hexapods, legs 1–6 = FR, MR, RR, RL, ML, FL (right side first).
- ☐ **Trade-off:** More legs = better static stability and rougher-terrain tolerance, but heavier and mechanically more complex.
- ☐ **Wave-gait period:** $T = 2(N-1)\Delta t$. For hexapod, $T = 10 \Delta t$.
- ☐ **Real-world examples to memorize for the KTU viva:**
  - **Spot** (Boston Dynamics) — quadruped, dynamic gaits.
  - **ASIMO** (Honda) — biped, ZMP-based dynamic walking.
  - **ATHLETE** (NASA JPL) — six-legged, rough-terrain explorer.
  - **Atlas** (Boston Dynamics) — biped humanoid, dynamic backflips.

**Best of luck for your KTU ESE Module 1 examination!** Master the *Klein and Krotkov theorem*, the *ZMP derivation*, and the *support polygon construction* — these are the **three guaranteed high-weightage topics** in Limbed Locomotion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Stability** in mobile robotics is defined as the ability of a wheeled or legged mobile robot to maintain its upright posture and avoid tipping over (capsizing) while executing motion commands on a given terrain. It is a measure of the robot's resistance to disturbing forces and moments caused by gravity, acceleration, terrain inclination, and external loads.

Stability is broadly classified into two regimes in the KTU 2024 Mobile Robotics syllabus:

1. **Static Stability** — Stability when the robot is *stationary* or moving at *negligible velocity* (quasi-static motion). Determined purely by geometry, mass distribution, and gravity.
2. **Dynamic Stability** — Stability while the robot is *in motion*. Determined by inertia, Coriolis forces, centripetal forces, and momentum effects in addition to gravity.

Within these regimes, two geometric directions are analyzed:
- **Longitudinal Stability** — Stability in the forward/backward (roll) direction about the rear/front wheel contact axis.
- **Lateral Stability** — Stability in the sideways (roll) direction about the wheel-to-wheel contact axis on the same side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Module 1 Vocabulary):**
> - **CoG / Center of Gravity (G):** Point where the robot's entire weight $W = mg$ is considered to act.
> - **Support Polygon (SP):** The convex hull formed by all ground-contact points of the wheels/tracks.
> - **Stability Margin (SM):** Minimum perpendicular distance from the projected CoG to any edge of the support polygon.
> - **Tip-Over:** A condition where the projected CoG crosses outside the support polygon, generating an unbalanced moment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Mountain Climber on a Narrow Ledge:**
> Imagine you are standing on a narrow mountain ledge. Your "stability" depends on two things: (1) **where your weight is** (if you lean too far forward, you fall; backward, you fall back), and (2) **how wide your base of support is** (feet shoulder-width apart = more stable than feet together). A mobile robot on a slope behaves identically. Its "weight" is its **Center of Gravity (G)**, and its "feet" are the **wheel contact points forming the support polygon**. If the vertical line dropped from G exits the polygon, the robot tips over — just as a person tips when their weight goes beyond their toes or heels. **Vehicle stability analysis is essentially the mathematical translation of this intuitive balancing act.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Parameters Used:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
> - Robot mass: $m$ (in kg)
> - Weight: $W = mg$ (in Newtons, N)
> - Stability angle threshold: typically expressed in **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)**
> - Friction coefficient: $\mu$ (dimensionless)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How Stability is Evaluated

The KTU 2024 syllabus approaches vehicle stability through **four sequential layers of analysis**:

### Layer 1 — Geometric Support Analysis
- Identify all wheel/track contact points with the ground.
- Construct the **Support Polygon (SP)** as the convex hull of these contact points.
- For a 4-wheel rectangular vehicle: SP is a rectangle of length $L$ and width $2W_b$ (track width).

### Layer 2 — Center of Gravity (CoG) Projection
- Locate the 3D CoG: $(x_g, y_g, h_g)$ measured from a reference point on the robot.
- Project the CoG vertically onto the ground plane.
- Check whether the projected point lies **inside** the support polygon.

### Layer 3 — Stability Margin Computation
- Compute the perpendicular distance from the projected CoG to the **nearest edge** of the SP.
- This distance is the **Stability Margin (SM)**.
- A robot is statically stable **if and only if** $SM \geq 0$.

### Layer 4 — Force and Moment Balance
- Sum the moments about potential tip-over axes (edges of the SP).
- The axis with the **minimum restoring moment** determines the stability limit.
- A robot is stable if all moments are **restoring** (opposing the tip direction).

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Stability Mode

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the CoG projection method work?**
> Gravity acts vertically downward through the CoG. If this vertical line of action falls *inside* the support polygon, gravity creates a *restoring moment* about every edge of the polygon, pushing the robot back into the stable region. If it falls *outside*, gravity creates an *overturning moment*, and the robot tips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why longitudinal and lateral are treated separately?**
> Wheeled mobile robots typically have a rectangular support polygon. The dimensions in the longitudinal ($L$) and lateral ($2W_b$) directions are usually different. The robot will tip in the direction where the projected CoG first exits the polygon — which is independent analysis for each axis.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $W = mg$ | Weight of the robot | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $\text{Stable} \iff (x_g, y_g) \in \text{SP}$ | Static stability condition | Pure geometric; $h_g$ ignored in projection |
| 3 | $SM = \min_i \, d_i$ | Stability margin = min distance from CoG projection to each SP edge | Meters (m) |
| 4 | $\theta_L = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{h_g}\right)$ | **Longitudinal stability angle** (max slope before tip-forward) | Radians/Degrees; $a$ = CoG longitudinal distance from tip axis |
| 5 | $\theta_{LT} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{W_b}{h_g}\right)$ | **Lateral stability angle** (max slope before side-tip) | Radians/Degrees; $W_b$ = half track width |
| 6 | $M_{\text{restore}} = W \cdot d_{\text{horiz}}$ | Restoring moment about an SP edge | N·m |
| 7 | $M_{\text{tip}} = W \cdot d_{\text{tip}}$ | Overturning (tipping) moment | N·m |
| 8 | $FS = \dfrac{M_{\text{restore}}}{M_{\text{tip}}}$ | **Factor of Safety** against tipping | Dimensionless; $FS > 1$ stable |
| 9 | $\text{SA} = \dfrac{d_{\text{CG} \to \text{edge}}}{h_g}$ | **Static Stability Angle** (alternative form) | Dimensionless (tan of angle) |
| 10 | $\text{ZMP}_x, \text{ZMP}_y \in \text{SP}$ | Zero Moment Point stability criterion | Active research form; for dynamic cases |
| 11 | $v_{\text{critical}} = \sqrt{g \cdot R_{\text{curve}} \cdot \tan\theta}$ | Critical speed on a banked curve | m/s |
| 12 | $a_{\text{max}} = g \cdot \dfrac{SM}{h_g}$ | Max safe linear acceleration (longitudinal) | m/s² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning in Tables:** All absolute value / divisibility bars use the symbol `\vert` or `\mid` rather than the vertical pipe `|` to preserve the KTU markdown table structure during conversion to PDF.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where is vehicle stability analysis applied?**
> - **Autonomous Cars (Self-Driving):** Anti-rollover algorithms (e.g., Electronic Stability Control / ESC in modern vehicles).
> - **Planetary Rovers (NASA's Curiosity, Perseverance):** Real-time stability assessment on uneven Martian terrain.
> - **AGVs in Warehouses:** Stability while carrying off-center loads.
> - **Search and Rescue Robots:** Stability on rubble, stairs, and collapsed structures.
> - **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas):** Dynamic ZMP-based balance control during walking.
> - **Heavy Industrial Vehicles (Forklifts, Cranes):** Tipping prevention when lifting loads at height.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Longitudinal Stability on a Slope

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a four-wheeled mobile robot on an inclined plane of angle $\alpha$ (positive = climbing uphill). The robot has:
- Wheelbase (longitudinal distance between front and rear axles): $L$
- CoG height above ground: $h_g$
- CoG horizontal location: distance $a$ from the **front** wheel contact, $b$ from the **rear** wheel contact, with $a + b = L$
- Total mass: $m$

**Objective:** Derive the condition for the robot *not* to tip forward or backward.

### Step 1: Free-Body Setup

Place a coordinate system on the inclined ground. The weight $W = mg$ acts vertically downward through the CoG. The reactions at the front wheel $N_f$ and rear wheel $N_r$ act perpendicular to the inclined surface.

### Step 2: Resolve Weight into Slope-Aligned Components

Decompose $W$ into:
- Component **parallel to slope**: $W \sin\alpha$ (down the incline)
- Component **perpendicular to slope**: $W \cos\alpha$ (into the incline)

### Step 3: Equilibrium of Forces Perpendicular to Slope

For static equilibrium, the sum of perpendicular forces must be zero:

$$N_f + N_r = W \cos\alpha$$

### Step 4: Moment Equilibrium About the Rear Wheel Contact Point

Take moments about the rear wheel contact (potential tip-forward axis). The moment arm of $W \cos\alpha$ acting at CoG is the horizontal distance along the slope $b$. The moment arm of $W \sin\alpha$ acting at CoG is the vertical height $h_g$.

The overturning moment (tending to tip forward) is caused by $W \sin\alpha$ with arm $h_g$:

$$M_{\text{overturn}} = W \sin\alpha \cdot h_g = mg \sin\alpha \cdot h_g$$

The restoring moment (tending to keep the robot on the slope) is caused by $W \cos\alpha$ with arm $b$:

$$M_{\text{restore}} = W \cos\alpha \cdot b = mg \cos\alpha \cdot b$$

### Step 5: Apply the Stability Criterion

For the robot **not to tip forward**, the restoring moment must equal or exceed the overturning moment:

$$mg \cos\alpha \cdot b \geq mg \sin\alpha \cdot h_g$$

### Step 6: Simplify the Inequality

Cancel $mg$ (non-zero) and rearrange:

$$\cos\alpha \cdot b \geq \sin\alpha \cdot h_g$$

$$\frac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha} \leq \frac{b}{h_g}$$

$$\tan\alpha \leq \frac{b}{h_g}$$

### Step 7: Final Longitudinal Stability Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b}{h_g}\right) = \theta_L}$$
> The robot is **longitudinally stable against forward tip** if the slope angle $\alpha$ is less than or equal to $\theta_L$, where $\theta_L$ is the **longitudinal stability angle**.

By symmetry, the stability condition against **rearward tip** is:

$$\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{h_g}\right)$$

The **effective longitudinal stability limit** is the smaller of the two:

$$\alpha_{\max, \text{long}} = \min\!\left(\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{h_g}\right), \, \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b}{h_g}\right)\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lateral Stability on a Side Slope

**Problem Setup:**
Now consider the same robot on a **side slope** (lateral tilt) of angle $\beta$. Let the **half-track width** be $W_b$ (distance from centerline to the wheels on one side). The CoG is on the robot's centerline at height $h_g$.

### Step 1: Identify the Tip-Over Axis

The robot will tip about the **lower wheel** of the side-slope. The moment arms are:
- $W \cos\beta$ acts along the vertical projection: arm $W_b$ (horizontal)
- $W \sin\beta$ acts along the slope: arm $h_g$ (vertical)

### Step 2: Apply the Same Moment Balance

Restoring moment: $W \cos\beta \cdot W_b$
Overturning moment: $W \sin\beta \cdot h_g$

### Step 3: Set Up the Inequality

$$W \cos\beta \cdot W_b \geq W \sin\beta \cdot h_g$$

$$\tan\beta \leq \frac{W_b}{h_g}$$

### Step 4: Final Lateral Stability Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\beta \leq \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W_b}{h_g}\right) = \theta_{LT}}$$
> The robot is **laterally stable** if the side-slope angle $\beta$ is less than or equal to the **lateral stability angle** $\theta_{LT}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stability Margin with Off-Center Load

**Problem Setup:**
A forklift-style mobile robot carries a payload of mass $m_p$ at horizontal distance $d_p$ from the robot's centerline and at height $h_p$ above the ground. The robot itself has mass $m_r$ with CoG at $(0, 0, h_r)$ on its centerline.

### Step 1: Compute the Combined CoG

Using the composite center of mass formula in the lateral direction:

$$y_{\text{combined}} = \frac{m_r \cdot 0 + m_p \cdot d_p}{m_r + m_p} = \frac{m_p \, d_p}{m_r + m_p}$$

$$h_{\text{combined}} = \frac{m_r \cdot h_r + m_p \cdot h_p}{m_r + m_p}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Lateral Stability Condition with New CoG

The lateral stability angle becomes:

$$\theta_{LT}^{\text{new}} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W_b - y_{\text{combined}}}{h_{\text{combined}}}\right)$$

This shows that **off-center loads dramatically reduce** lateral stability, especially when the load is *also* raised to a height.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic Stability with Acceleration

**Problem Setup:**
A mobile robot accelerates forward with acceleration $a$ on flat ground. Find the maximum acceleration before it tips forward (about the front wheels).

### Step 1: Add the Inertial (Pseudo) Force

By d'Alembert's principle, apply a pseudo-force $-ma$ at the CoG, acting **backward** (opposite to acceleration).

### Step 2: Take Moments About the Front Wheel Contact

Restoring moment: $W \cdot b$ (gravity times distance from front wheel)
Overturning moment: $m \cdot a \cdot h_g$ (inertial force times CoG height)

### Step 3: Stability Condition

$$m g \cdot b \geq m a \cdot h_g$$

$$a \leq \frac{g \cdot b}{h_g}$$

### Step 4: Final Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{a_{\max} = \frac{g \cdot b}{h_g} = g \cdot \frac{SM_{\text{static}}}{h_g}}$$
> The maximum safe longitudinal acceleration depends on the static stability margin in the direction of motion and the CoG height.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stability Criterion Summary

| Stability Mode | Critical Quantity | Stability Condition | Tip-Over Axis |
|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Static, longitudinal uphill | Slope angle $\alpha$ | $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(b/h_g)$ | Front wheels |
| Static, longitudinal downhill | Slope angle $\alpha$ | $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(a/h_g)$ | Rear wheels |
| Static, lateral | Side slope $\beta$ | $\beta \leq \tan^{-1}(W_b/h_g)$ | Lower-side wheels |
| Dynamic, accelerating | Acceleration $a$ | $a \leq g \cdot b/h_g$ | Front wheels |
| Dynamic, braking | Deceleration $a$ | $a \leq g \cdot a/h_g$ | Rear wheels |
| Dynamic, cornering | Lateral accel $a_y$ | $a_y \leq g \cdot W_b/h_g$ | Outer wheels |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stability Analysis

The following Mermaid diagram maps out the complete stability-assessment pipeline used in mobile robotics, as aligned with the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Robot Geometry and Mass] --> B[Identify Wheel Contact Points]
    B --> C[Construct Support Polygon SP]
    C --> D[Compute Center of Gravity G]
    D --> E[Project G onto Ground Plane]
    E --> F{Is Projection inside SP?}
    F -- No --> G[Robot is UNSTABLE - Tip-Over]
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute Stability Margin SM]
    H --> I[Compute Force-Angle Measure]
    I --> J[Check Slope and Acceleration Limits]
    J --> K{Longitudinal Limit OK?}
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> L{Lateral Limit OK?}
    L -- No --> G
    L -- Yes --> M[Apply Dynamic Stability Check]
    M --> N{Inertial Effects OK?}
    N -- No --> G
    N -- Yes --> O[Robot is STABLE - Safe Operation]
    O --> P[Generate Motion Commands]
    P --> A
    G --> Q[Activate Tip-Over Prevention]
    Q --> R[Reduce Speed / Replan Path]
    R --> A
```

## 4.2 Free-Body and Geometric Schematics (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SLOPE[Robot on Inclined Surface]
        R1[Rear Wheel Contact] -->|distance b| G[Center of Gravity G]
        G -->|distance a| F1[Front Wheel Contact]
        G -->|height hg| H[Vertical Reference]
    end
    W[Weight W=mg acts downward through G] --> G
    SLOPE --> ANGLE[Slope angle alpha]
    ANGLE --> RESULT{alpha less than or equal to tan inverse of b by hg?}
    RESULT -- Yes --> STABLE[STABLE]
    RESULT -- No --> TIP[TIP OVER]
```

## 4.3 Support Polygon Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Robot Pose and Contact Points] --> P1[Number of Ground Contacts]
    P1 -- 3 contacts --> P2[Triangle Support Polygon]
    P1 -- 4 contacts --> P3[Quadrilateral Support Polygon]
    P1 -- More than 4 --> P4[Convex Hull of All Contacts]
    P2 --> P5[Project CoG to Ground]
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6{Check if inside polygon}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Stability Margin = Min Distance to Edge]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Tip-Over Imminent]
    P7 --> P9[Factor of Safety Computed]
    P8 --> P10[Emergency Stabilization]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Static Stability Margin `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> Define **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** for a mobile robot. How is it computed, and what does a negative SSM value indicate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **Definition:** The Static Stability Margin is the shortest distance from the projection of the robot's Center of Gravity (CoG) on the ground plane to any edge of the support polygon formed by the wheel/track contact points.
- **(1 mark)** **Computation:** $SSM = \min_i \, d_i$, where $d_i$ is the perpendicular distance from the projected CoG to the $i$-th edge of the support polygon.
- **(1 mark)** **Negative SSM meaning:** A *negative* SSM indicates that the CoG projection has crossed **outside** the support polygon, meaning the robot is in a **tip-over state** and gravity now generates an unbalanced overturning moment.

---

### Question 2: Longitudinal vs Lateral Stability `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> Differentiate between **longitudinal stability** and **lateral stability** in a four-wheeled mobile robot. State the relevant stability-angle formula for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **Longitudinal stability:** Refers to stability against tipping in the **forward/backward** direction (about the front or rear wheel contact axis). Relevant to motion on **uphill or downhill slopes**.
- **(1 mark)** **Lateral stability:** Refers to stability against tipping in the **sideways** direction (about the wheels on the same side). Relevant to motion on **side slopes** or during **sharp turns** where centripetal force acts laterally.
- **(1 mark)** **Formulas:**
$$\theta_L = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b}{h_g}\right) \quad \text{(longitudinal)} \qquad \theta_{LT} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W_b}{h_g}\right) \quad \text{(lateral)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Stability on a Slope with Off-Center Load (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> A four-wheeled mobile robot has the following parameters: total mass $m = 200$ kg, wheelbase $L = 1.5$ m, track width $2W_b = 1.0$ m, CoG height $h_g = 0.6$ m (measured from the ground), and the CoG is located **0.7 m from the front axle** and **0.8 m from the rear axle** along the longitudinal centerline.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **longitudinal stability angle** and the **lateral stability angle** of the robot. State whether the robot can climb a 25° slope without tipping forward.
>
> **(b)** The robot now picks up a payload of mass $m_p = 50$ kg located at a horizontal offset of $d_p = 0.3$ m to the right of the centerline and at a height of $h_p = 1.2$ m above the ground. Calculate the **new combined CoG** and the **new lateral stability angle**. Comment on the safety.

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters**
- $a = 0.7$ m (CoG to front), $b = 0.8$ m (CoG to rear), $h_g = 0.6$ m, $W_b = 0.5$ m.

**Step 2 — Longitudinal Stability Angle (about front axis)** (2 marks)

$$\theta_L = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b}{h_g}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.8}{0.6}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.333) = 53.13°$$

**Step 3 — Lateral Stability Angle** (2 marks)

$$\theta_{LT} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W_b}{h_g}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.6}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.833) = 39.81°$$

**Step 4 — Check the 25° Slope Condition** (2 marks)

The given slope $\alpha = 25°$. Since $a < b$ (CoG is closer to the front), the **forward tip** is the critical direction.

Since $25° < 53.13°$, the robot **can climb the 25° slope without tipping forward**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion** (1 mark)
- Longitudinal stability angle: $\theta_L = 53.13°$
- Lateral stability angle: $\theta_{LT} = 39.81°$
- 25° slope is **safe** longitudinally.

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute New Lateral CoG Position** (2 marks)

$$y_{\text{new}} = \frac{m_r \cdot 0 + m_p \cdot d_p}{m_r + m_p} = \frac{200(0) + 50(0.3)}{200 + 50} = \frac{15}{250} = 0.06 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute New CoG Height** (2 marks)

$$h_{\text{new}} = \frac{m_r \cdot h_r + m_p \cdot h_p}{m_r + m_p} = \frac{200(0.6) + 50(1.2)}{250} = \frac{120 + 60}{250} = \frac{180}{250} = 0.72 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Distance from Tip-Over Axis** (1 mark)

The projected CoG is now $0.06$ m off the centerline. The distance from the right-side tip axis is:
$$W_b^{\text{eff}} = W_b - y_{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.06 = 0.44 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — New Lateral Stability Angle** (1 mark)

$$\theta_{LT}^{\text{new}} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W_b^{\text{eff}}}{h_{\text{new}}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.44}{0.72}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.611) = 31.43°$$

**Step 5 — Safety Comment** (1 mark)

The lateral stability angle dropped from **39.81° to 31.43°** — a reduction of over **8 degrees**. The robot becomes significantly more prone to **side tipping**. Operators must:
- Avoid side slopes greater than 31°,
- Slow down during turns (centripetal acceleration adds to the lateral moment),
- Consider repositioning the load closer to the centerline if possible.

---

### Question B: Dynamic Stability Under Acceleration (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> A two-wheeled self-balancing mobile robot (similar to a Segway) has the following parameters: total mass $m = 50$ kg, height of CoG $h_g = 1.0$ m, the wheel contact points form a support polygon of length $L = 0.4$ m along the longitudinal direction, and the CoG is centered between the wheels horizontally.
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum safe longitudinal acceleration** of the robot before it tips about the front or rear wheel axis. Compute the numerical value.
>
> **(b)** The robot now travels on a flat curve of radius $R = 5$ m at speed $v = 4$ m/s. Compute the **centripetal acceleration** and check whether the robot will tip laterally. State the minimum radius the robot can take at this speed without lateral tip-over.

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Force Setup** (1 mark)

When the robot accelerates forward with acceleration $a$, by d'Alembert's principle, an inertial pseudo-force $F_{\text{inertia}} = ma$ acts at the CoG, **backward** (opposite to the direction of acceleration).

**Step 2 — Identify the Critical Tip-Over Axis** (1 mark)

Since the CoG is centered ($a = b = L/2 = 0.2$ m), the robot will tip about the **front** wheel when accelerating forward and about the **rear** wheel when braking. The more critical of the two determines the limit. With equal $a$ and $b$, both give the same result.

**Step 3 — Moment Balance About Front Wheel Axis** (2 marks)

Restoring moment (gravity): $M_{\text{restore}} = mg \cdot b = mg \cdot (L/2) = mg \cdot 0.2$

Overturning moment (inertia): $M_{\text{overturn}} = ma \cdot h_g$

**Step 4 — Stability Condition** (1 mark)

$$mg \cdot b \geq ma \cdot h_g$$

$$a \leq \frac{g \cdot b}{h_g}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Computation** (1 mark)

$$a_{\max} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.2}{1.0} = 1.962 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Result** (1 mark)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{a_{\max} = 1.962 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 0.2g}$$
> The robot can safely accelerate up to **1.962 m/s²** (about 0.2 times gravity) without tipping forward.

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Centripetal Acceleration Formula** (1 mark)

For a body moving in a circular path of radius $R$ at speed $v$:

$$a_c = \frac{v^2}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Computation of Centripetal Acceleration** (1 mark)

$$a_c = \frac{4^2}{5} = \frac{16}{5} = 3.2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Lateral Stability Limit** (1 mark)

For a two-wheeled robot with no lateral support polygon (track width $W_b \to 0$), the lateral stability is extremely limited. The lateral CoG is *directly above* the wheel contact line, meaning the lateral stability angle is essentially **zero degrees** — the robot balances dynamically using active control.

For the static check, the lateral moment is:

$$M_{\text{lateral,overturn}} = m \cdot a_c \cdot h_g = 50 \times 3.2 \times 1.0 = 160 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Lateral Stability Check** (1 mark)

The restoring lateral moment depends on the wheel-track width. For a typical Segway with $W_b = 0.4$ m, the wheel center is at $W_b/2 = 0.2$ m from the CoG. Restoring moment:

$$M_{\text{lateral,restore}} = mg \cdot W_b/2 = 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 = 98.1 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $M_{\text{lateral,overturn}} = 160 \text{ N·m} > M_{\text{lateral,restore}} = 98.1 \text{ N·m}$, the robot **WILL tip laterally** if it relies on static stability alone.

**Step 5 — Minimum Radius for Safe Cornering** (2 marks)

For the robot not to tip, we need $a_c \leq a_{\max,\text{lateral}}$:

$$a_{\max,\text{lateral}} = \frac{g \cdot (W_b/2)}{h_g} = \frac{9.81 \times 0.2}{1.0} = 1.962 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Setting $a_c = a_{\max,\text{lateral}}$:

$$\frac{v^2}{R_{\min}} = 1.962 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{\min} = \frac{v^2}{1.962} = \frac{16}{1.962} = 8.155 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion** (1 mark)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{R_{\min} = 8.16 \text{ m}}$$
> The robot must take a curve of radius **at least 8.16 m** at 4 m/s to avoid lateral tip-over. Since the actual radius is only 5 m, **the robot will tip** — a self-balancing controller must compensate by actively leaning inward (similar to a cyclist).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to resolve weight on a slope:** Students often use $W = mg$ directly in the moment equation without decomposing into $W\sin\alpha$ and $W\cos\alpha$ components along/perpendicular to the slope. This loses **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $a$ and $b$:** The longitudinal stability angle is $\tan^{-1}(b/h_g)$ for *forward* tip, where $b$ is the distance from the **rear** wheel to the CoG. Mixing up $a$ and $b$ is a frequent error.
> 3. **Ignoring CoG offset due to payload:** When a load is added, the **combined CoG** must be recomputed using the mass-weighted average — not just added in height.
> 4. **Static vs Dynamic confusion:** Do not use static stability formulas for a robot that is **accelerating or turning** — dynamic effects (inertial forces) must be included.
> 5. **Units:** Always state final angles in **degrees** (or both degrees and radians). Marks are deducted for unitless numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Vehicle Stability (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Static stability** = CoG projection inside the support polygon (no motion).
>
> ✅ **Dynamic stability** = inertial forces (linear + centripetal) keep the system stable during motion.
>
> ✅ **Longitudinal stability angle** = $\theta_L = \tan^{-1}(b/h_g)$ (about front wheel for forward tip).
>
> ✅ **Lateral stability angle** = $\theta_{LT} = \tan^{-1}(W_b/h_g)$ (about the lower wheel on a side slope).
>
> ✅ **Stability Margin (SM)** = shortest perpendicular distance from CoG projection to the support-polygon edge.
>
> ✅ **Tip-over condition:** CoG projection falls *outside* the support polygon OR $M_{\text{overturn}} > M_{\text{restore}}$.
>
> ✅ **On a slope:** always decompose weight into $W\sin\alpha$ (along slope) and $W\cos\alpha$ (perpendicular to slope).
>
> ✅ **With payload:** combined CoG = mass-weighted average: $x_c = \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}$.
>
> ✅ **Dynamic limit for acceleration:** $a_{\max} = g \cdot (b/h_g)$ — CoG height inversely affects stability.
>
> ✅ **Centripetal/turning limit:** $v_{\max} = \sqrt{g \cdot R \cdot W_b/h_g}$ — tighter turns cause lateral tip.
>
> ✅ **Lowering the CoG** (lowering $h_g$) **always improves stability** in all directions.
>
> ✅ **Widening the track** (increasing $W_b$) **improves lateral** but not longitudinal stability.
>
> ✅ **Lengthening the wheelbase** (increasing $L$ and hence $a$ or $b$) **improves longitudinal** but not lateral stability.
>
> ✅ **Standard gravity:** always use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless specified otherwise in the KTU problem statement.
>
> ✅ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** questions frequently combine **slope + payload + dynamic acceleration** — practice all three together.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Number of Legs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Number of Legs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Mobile Robotics** and **Legged Locomotion Systems**, the term *"Number of Legs"* (denoted by **N** or **n**) refers to the discrete count of articulated kinematic chains (limbs) attached to the main body (chassis) of a robot that make intermittent or continuous ground contact to provide support, propulsion, and maneuverability. This parameter is the **primary morphological classification variable** used to distinguish legged robots from wheeled, tracked, and aerial platforms.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, legged robots are categorized as:

| Configuration | Symbol (N) | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| One-legged | N = 1 | **Monopod** / Hopper |
| Two-legged | N = 2 | **Biped** |
| Three-legged | N = 3 | **Tripod** |
| Four-legged | N = 4 | **Quadruped** |
| Six-legged | N = 6 | **Hexapod** |
| Eight-legged | N = 8 | **Octopod** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The number of legs directly governs the **stability class** (static vs. dynamic), the **gait complexity**, the **actuator count**, and the **energy efficiency** of the entire robotic system. It is *not* merely a design choice — it is a hard constraint imposed by the mechanics of locomotion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Crutch-Walker vs. The Ant:**
> 
> Imagine a person learning to walk after an injury. When they use **one crutch (N = 1)**, they must hop dynamically — their body is never at rest in a balanced state. With **two crutches (N = 2)**, they can still stand momentarily, but the support polygon is thin. With **a four-legged walker (N = 4)**, they feel rock-solid because at least three legs are always on the ground, forming a stable triangle (this is called the **tripod principle**). Now, think of an **ant** — it has **six legs (N = 6)**. An ant never falls because it can lift three legs at once (alternating tripod) and still remain stable. This is the exact engineering trade-off: **more legs = simpler control, but more weight and more actuators**.

### 1.3 Why "Number of Legs" Matters — The Three Engineering Drivers

1. **Stability Class:** Determines whether the robot is **statically stable** (can pause at any instant without falling) or **dynamically stable** (must keep moving to avoid falling).
2. **Mechanical Complexity:** Each leg typically has **2 or 3 degrees of freedom (DOF)**, so total actuator count scales as **O(N)**.
3. **Gait Design Space:** The number of feasible periodic footfall patterns (gaits) grows combinatorially with **N**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** *"The More The Legs, The Easier The Brain"* — additional legs reduce the real-time computational burden on the controller because passive stability is provided by the support polygon.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Classification by Number of Legs

#### A. Monopod (N = 1) — Hopping Robots
- **Stability Class:** Strictly **dynamically stable**.
- **Mechanism:** Single compliant leg with spring-loaded actuation (e.g., **SGH — Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum** model).
- **Examples:** MIT 3D One-Leg Hopper, Raibert's hopper.
- **Engineering Reality:** Cannot stand still; must continuously hop. Not practical for industrial use.

#### B. Biped (N = 2) — Humanoid Robots
- **Stability Class:** **Dynamically stable** (Zero Moment Point — ZMP — controlled).
- **Mechanism:** Two multi-jointed legs, each with **6 DOF** (hip × 3, knee × 1, ankle × 2).
- **Examples:** ASIMO, Atlas, NAO, HRP series.
- **Engineering Reality:** Highest control complexity; resembles human morphology.

#### C. Tripod (N = 3) — The Minimal Static Platform
- **Stability Class:** **Marginally statically stable** only when perfectly horizontal; in motion, dynamically stable.
- **Engineering Reality:** Almost never used in practice because losing one leg is catastrophic.

#### D. Quadruped (N = 4) — The Workhorse
- **Stability Class:** **Statically stable** with appropriate gaits (creep, crawl, trot).
- **Mechanism:** 4 legs × 3 DOF = **12 actuators** typical.
- **Examples:** Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYbotics ANYmal, MIT Mini-Cheetah.
- **Engineering Reality:** The **sweet spot** between mobility and control simplicity.

#### E. Hexapod (N = 6) — The Biologically Inspired Standard
- **Stability Class:** **Inherently statically stable** (alternating tripod gait).
- **Mechanism:** 6 legs × 3 DOF = **18 actuators** typical.
- **Examples:** Dante II, Genghis (MIT), RHex.
- **Engineering Reality:** Most fault-tolerant; mimics insects.

#### F. Octopod (N = 8) — The Research Curiosity
- **Stability Class:** **Highly statically stable**, but mechanically redundant.
- **Engineering Reality:** Used in research on hyper-redundant systems.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of every numerical problem on Number of Legs.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Degrees of Freedom (3D leg) | $DOF_{total} = N \times k$ | $N$ = number of legs, $k$ = DOF per leg (typically 2 or 3) | Actuator sizing |
| 2 | Total DOF (2D planar case) | $DOF_{2D} = 2N - 3$ | After removing 3 rigid-body DOF | Mobility analysis |
| 3 | Min. legs for static stability (motion) | $N_{min} \geq 4$ | At least 3 legs in support at all times | Design feasibility |
| 4 | Support Polygon Constraint | $\vec{r}_{CoG} \in \mathcal{P}_{support}$ | Center of Gravity must lie inside support polygon | Stability check |
| 5 | Tripod Gait Condition (Hexapod) | $N_{lift} = 3,\ N_{support} = 3$ | Lifts 3 alternate legs | Hexapod walking |
| 6 | Wave Gait (N legs) | $N_{support} = N-1$ at any time | Lifts only 1 leg | Insect locomotion |
| 7 | Leg Phase Offset (uniform gait) | $\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | Phase between successive leg liftoffs | Gait scheduling |
| 8 | Number of Distinct Gaits | $G_{count} = 2^N - N - 1$ | Combinatorial gait enumeration | Gait library design |
| 9 | Static Stability Margin | $SSM = \min_{i} d(\vec{r}_{CoG}, \ e_i)$ | Distance from CoG to nearest support edge | Quantitative stability |
| 10 | Force Closure (vertical) | $\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} f_i \geq mg$ | Sum of support forces ≥ weight | No-lift condition |

### 2.3 The Stability Leg-Number Theorem (KTU Frequently Tested)

**Theorem:** *A legged robot with N legs can be statically stable during motion if and only if it can maintain at least 3 non-collinear legs in ground contact at every instant.*

**Proof Logic:**
- A rigid body on a surface requires **at least 3 contact points** to be in static equilibrium (translation + rotation about two axes constrained).
