The same principles are abstracted into prosthetic limbs and exoskeletons in medical robotics.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standardized Terms

- **Duty Factor (β):** The fraction of the total cycle time that a given leg is in the *support phase* (in contact with the ground). A value of **0 ≤ β ≤ 1**.
- **Stride Length (λ):** The distance the body travels during one complete gait cycle. Unit: **meters (m)**.
- **Step Length:** The distance the body moves during the transfer (swing) of one leg.
- **Stability Margin (S):** The shortest distance from the projected Center of Gravity (CoG) to the boundaries of the **support polygon**. Unit: **meters (m)**.
- **Center of Mass (CoM) / Center of Gravity (CoG):** The point where the robot's mass is effectively concentrated for gravitational analysis.
- **Zero Moment Point (ZMP):** The point on the ground where the net moment of the inertial and gravitational forces equals zero. Coincides with CoG projection in static conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

A complete study of Gait and Body Control can be decomposed into the following logical layers:

**A. Fundamental Gait Parameters (Per Leg)**
1. Each leg alternates between two phases: **Stance (Support) Phase** and **Swing (Transfer) Phase**.
2. The **cycle time `T`** is the time for one leg to complete one full stance + swing sequence.
3. **Stance time `Ts`** = β · T, **Swing time `Tr`** = (1 − β) · T.
4. The **speed `V`** of a legged robot is given by `V = λ / T`, where λ is the stride length.

**B. Stability Frameworks**
1. **Static Stability:** The robot is stable at *every instant*, even when motion freezes. Requires the CoG projection to lie strictly *inside* the support polygon. Used in slow, multi-legged robots (e.g., hexapods).
2. **Dynamic Stability:** The robot may be unstable when frozen but stable *on average* over time. Uses momentum, ZMP control. Used in bipedal/humanoid robots.
3. **Quasi-Static Walking:** A hybrid where motion is slow enough to approximate static stability but uses dynamic corrections to speed up transitions.

**C. Classification of Gaits**
1. **Periodic Gaits:** The leg sequence repeats identically each cycle. Includes *wave gaits* and *ripple gaits*.
2. **Free Gaits:** Adapts to irregular terrain without a fixed cycle (used in real-world, unstructured environments).
3. **Symmetric Gaits:** Left and right legs of the same pair move in mirror symmetry. Includes *walk, trot, pace, bound*.
4. **Asymmetric Gaits:** No such symmetry (used in turning maneuvers).

**D. Leg Coordination Strategies**
1. **Wave Gait (Slowest, Most Stable):** Legs lift in a wave from rear to front, one at a time. Only one leg in swing at any moment. Used in 6-legged or 8-legged machines.
2. **Ripple Gait:** Multiple waves propagate, with overlapping swing phases. Faster than wave gait.
3. **Tripod Gait:** Three legs swing together (front-left, mid-right, hind-left, etc.) forming two alternating tripods. Common in hexapods; resembles insect locomotion.
4. **Crawl Gait:** Always maintains at least three legs in support (tripod of static stability). Used in uneven terrain.

**E. Body Control Mechanisms**
1. **Active Body Posture Control:** Uses IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) feedback to keep the torso level.
2. **Reaction Wheel / Flywheel Control:** Stores angular momentum to counteract tipping.
3. **Counter-Weight Shifting:** The CoG is deliberately moved to remain over the support polygon.
4. **Whole-Body Control (WBC):** A modern optimization-based approach where joint torques are computed to satisfy multiple objectives (balance, tracking, contact forces) simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Questions frequently ask students to *calculate* the duty factor given cycle time and swing time, or to *identify* which gait is most stable for a given number of legs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T = T_s + T_r$ | Total cycle time = Stance time + Swing time | s |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{T_s}{T}$ | Duty factor definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_r = (1 - \beta) \cdot T$ | Swing time as a function of duty factor | s |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{\lambda}{T}$ | Locomotion speed of legged robot | m/s |
| 5 | $\lambda = V \cdot T$ | Stride length as a function of speed and cycle | m |
| 6 | $S = \min_i \, d_i$ | Stability margin = shortest distance from CoG projection to support polygon edge | m |
| 7 | $\beta \geq 0.5$ (for $n=4$ legs) | Necessary condition for static stability in quadruped walk | dimensionless |
| 8 | $N_{support} \geq 3$ (non-collinear) | Minimum support legs for static stability on flat ground | count |
| 9 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Gait frequency (steps per second) | Hz |
| 10 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{L}{2 \sin(\pi/n)}$ | Minimum turning radius for `n` legs equally spaced | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are deployed in the gait planners of quadruped robots like **Boston Dynamics Spot** and **MIT Mini Cheetah**. The ZMP-based body control equations underpin the balance controllers of humanoid robots like **Honda ASIMO** and **Toyota T-HR3**.

### 2.3 Body Control — The "Why" and "How"

The **"why"** of body control is simple: *prevent the robot from falling*. The **"how"** is layered:

- **Sensory Layer:** IMUs (accelerometer + gyroscope), force/torque sensors in feet, joint encoders provide real-time state feedback.
- **Decision Layer:** A controller (PID, LQR, MPC, or learning-based) computes *desired* joint trajectories and CoG adjustments.
- **Actuation Layer:** Motors at each joint execute the commands at high bandwidth (typically 1 kHz control loops).
- **Stability Criterion:** The classic **ZMP criterion** — the ZMP must remain inside the *support polygon* (the convex hull of all ground-contact feet) for stability.

For dynamically stable robots, the dynamic equations are governed by:

$$\sum F_x = m \ddot{x}_{CoM}, \quad \sum F_y = m \ddot{y}_{CoM}, \quad \sum F_z = m(\ddot{z}_{CoM} + g)$$

The moments about the ZMP must vanish, yielding the classical ZMP equations used in humanoid balance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stride Length from Duty Factor and Body Geometry

**Problem Context (KTU-style):** A quadruped robot has legs of length $L = 0.5 \, \text{m}$. During a symmetric walk, the body moves forward with the hip joint tracing a horizontal path. Each leg's foot lifts to a height $h = 0.1 \, \text{m}$ above ground during swing. The cycle time is $T = 2.0 \, \text{s}$, and the duty factor is $\beta = 0.75$. Derive the stride length $\lambda$ and the locomotion speed $V$.

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship between cycle time and phase durations.**

We know that $T = T_s + T_r$, where $T_s$ is the stance time and $T_r$ is the swing time.

From the duty factor definition:
$$\beta = \frac{T_s}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_s = \beta \cdot T$$

Substituting $\beta = 0.75$ and $T = 2.0 \, \text{s}$:
$$T_s = 0.75 \times 2.0 \, \text{s} = 1.5 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the swing time.**

$$T_r = T - T_s = 2.0 \, \text{s} - 1.5 \, \text{s} = 0.5 \, \text{s}$$

Equivalently, $T_r = (1 - \beta) \cdot T = (1 - 0.75) \cdot 2.0 = 0.5 \, \text{s}$. ✓

**Step 3 — Geometric derivation of stride length.**

During the **swing phase**, the foot traces an arc (assuming the leg pivots about the hip). The horizontal displacement of the foot during swing equals the stride length $\lambda$ (for a symmetric periodic gait with a stationary body reference):

$$\lambda = 2 \sqrt{L^2 - (L - h)^2}$$

Let us simplify the right-hand side.

$$L^2 - (L - h)^2 = L^2 - (L^2 - 2Lh + h^2) = 2Lh - h^2$$

Substituting $L = 0.5 \, \text{m}$ and $h = 0.1 \, \text{m}$:

$$2Lh - h^2 = 2(0.5)(0.1) - (0.1)^2 = 0.1 - 0.01 = 0.09$$

Taking the square root:

$$\sqrt{0.09} = 0.3 \, \text{m}$$

Therefore:
$$\lambda = 2 \times 0.3 = 0.6 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the locomotion speed.**

$$V = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \frac{0.6 \, \text{m}}{2.0 \, \text{s}} = 0.3 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\lambda = 0.6 \, \text{m}, \quad V = 0.3 \, \text{m/s}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Number of Legs for Static Stability

**Problem Context (KTU-style):** A legged robot must remain statically stable on flat ground at all times. Derive the minimum number of legs `n` required, and show why `n = 3` is the boundary case.

**Step 1 — Define the static stability condition.**

A robot is *statically stable* if the vertical projection of its Center of Gravity (CoG) lies strictly inside the **support polygon** formed by its grounded feet.

**Step 2 — Support polygon dimensionality.**

For `n` feet on flat ground:
- If `n = 1`: The support polygon is a single point. Any perturbation causes the CoG to leave the polygon ⇒ **unstable**.
- If `n = 2`: The support polygon is a line segment. The CoG must lie exactly on this line ⇒ **marginally stable** (zero-area polygon). Any lateral perturbation causes fall.
- If `n = 3`: The support polygon is a triangle (a 2D area). The CoG can lie anywhere inside this triangle ⇒ **statically stable** (assuming non-collinear foot placement).

**Step 3 — Generalize to `n` legs.**

For static stability on flat ground, the support polygon must have a non-zero 2D area. This requires:
$$n \geq 3, \quad \text{with feet not collinear.}$$

**Step 4 — For 4 or more legs during locomotion.**

During walking, at least one leg is in swing. So at any instant, the support polygon is formed by `n − 1` feet. For static stability:
$$n - 1 \geq 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \geq 4$$

Hence, a **quadruped** can be statically stable during walking if 3 legs are always grounded.

**Step 5 — Extend to hexapods.**

For `n = 6` legs, a **tripod gait** keeps 3 legs on the ground (forming a stable triangle) at all times. This is the famous **alternating tripod** seen in insects.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{n_{\min} = 3 \text{ (stationary)}, \quad n_{\min} = 4 \text{ (during walking)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Gait Selection Decision Table

| Robot Type | # Legs | Best Gait | Static Stability? | Speed Class | KTU Exam Frequency |
|------------|--------|-----------|--------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| Biped (Humanoid) | 2 | Dynamic walk / run | No (uses ZMP) | Medium-High | **Very High** |
| Quadruped (Spot) | 4 | Crawl / Trot / Pace | Yes (crawl only) | High | **High** |
| Hexapod (Insect) | 6 | Tripod / Wave / Ripple | Yes | Medium | **Very High** |
| Octopod | 8 | Wave / Ripple | Yes | Slow-Medium | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Gait + Body Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[High-Level Mission Planner] --> B[Gait Pattern Generator]
    B --> C[Foot Trajectory Planner]
    C --> D[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    D --> E[Joint Trajectory Buffer]
    E --> F[Low-Level Motor Controllers]
    F --> G[Robot Joint Actuators]
    G --> H[Physical Robot Body]
    H --> I[IMU Sensor]
    H --> J[Force Torque Sensors in Feet]
    I --> K[State Estimator]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Body Balance Controller]
    L --> M[ZMP CoG Stabilizer]
    M --> N[Gait Adaptation Module]
    N --> B
    M --> C
    K --> A
```

**Interpretation of the architecture:**

- The **High-Level Mission Planner** sets goals (e.g., "walk 5 m forward").
- The **Gait Pattern Generator** selects the gait (wave, tripod, crawl, etc.) and outputs the leg-phase sequence.
- The **Foot Trajectory Planner** computes the swing-phase foot path (e.g., a raised arc).
- **Inverse Kinematics** converts Cartesian foot targets into joint angles.
- The **Motor Controllers** close the inner loop at high frequency (~1 kHz).
- **Sensors** (IMU + force/torque) feed back to a **State Estimator**.
- The **Body Balance Controller** computes ZMP and CoG corrections.
- The **Gait Adaptation Module** modifies the gait in real time if stability margins are violated.

---

### 4.2 State Diagram: Periodic Gait Cycle for a Single Leg

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stance
    Stance --> PreSwing : Lift off
    PreSwing --> Swing
    Swing --> Touchdown : Foot contact
    Touchdown --> Stance
    Stance --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every leg alternates between four conceptual states — *Stance* (foot on ground, supporting body), *Pre-Swing* (decision phase), *Swing* (foot in air, moving forward), and *Touchdown* (foot re-contacts the ground).

---

### 4.3 Support Polygon Schematic (Tripod Gait — Hexapod)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Support_Triangle_A[Support Triangle A]
        FL1[Front Left]
        MR1[Middle Right]
        HL1[Hind Left]
    end
    subgraph Support_Triangle_B[Support Triangle B]
        FR1[Front Right]
        ML1[Middle Left]
        HR1[Hind Right]
    end
    CoG1[Projected CoG] -. Inside Triangle A .-> Support_Triangle_A
    CoG2[Projected CoG] -. Inside Triangle B .-> Support_Triangle_B
```

**Interpretation:** In a hexapod's tripod gait, two alternating tripods (A and B) provide the support polygon. The CoG projection always remains inside the active triangle, ensuring static stability throughout the cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "duty factor" of a leg in a legged robot. State its range and significance in gait analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **duty factor (β)** of a leg is defined as the ratio of the time the leg spends in the **stance (support) phase** to the **total cycle time** $T$:

$$\beta = \frac{T_s}{T}$$

where $T_s$ is the stance time. The range of β is **0 ≤ β ≤ 1**. A higher duty factor means the leg spends more time supporting the body, which increases static stability but reduces locomotion speed. For statically stable walking, β must be greater than 0.5 in a quadruped.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for range/formula, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between static stability and dynamic stability in legged robots with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Robot is stable even if motion is frozen at any instant | Robot is unstable when frozen but stable during motion |
| CoG Condition | CoG projection always inside support polygon | CoG projection may exit polygon, but ZMP stays inside |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Control | Simple (geometric) | Complex (ZMP, momentum-based) |
| Example | Hexapod with wave/tripod gait | Humanoid robot (ASIMO) running |

*(Award: 1.5 marks for definitions, 1.5 marks for the example table.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **wave gait** and **tripod gait** for a six-legged robot. Compare their stability and speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quadruped robot walks with a **crawl gait**. The cycle time is $T = 1.6 \, \text{s}$, and the duty factor of each leg is $\beta = 0.75$. The leg length is $L = 0.4 \, \text{m}$, and the foot lift height during swing is $h = 0.08 \, \text{m}$. Calculate (i) the swing time, (ii) the stride length, and (iii) the locomotion speed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A:**

**(a) Wave Gait and Tripod Gait (7 marks):**

**Wave Gait:**
- The legs are numbered 1 to 6 from back to front.
- Legs lift in a sequential wave: leg 1, then leg 2, ..., then leg 6.
- At any instant, **only one leg is in swing**, and the remaining **five legs** form the support polygon.
- This is the **slowest but most stable** gait.
- Phase lag between successive legs: $\phi = 1/n = 1/6$ of the cycle.

**Tripod Gait:**
- The six legs are grouped into two sets of three:
  - **Tripod A:** Front-Left (FL), Middle-Right (MR), Hind-Left (HL)
  - **Tripod B:** Front-Right (FR), Middle-Left (ML), Hind-Right (HR)
- The two tripods alternate — when one is in swing, the other supports the body.
- At any instant, **three legs** form a stable triangular support polygon.
- This gait is **faster** than the wave gait (50% duty factor per leg) and is the **natural gait of insects**.
- Phase lag between legs in the same tripod: $\phi = 0.5$ (half-cycle).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | Wave Gait | Tripod Gait |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Legs in swing at once | 1 | 3 |
| Legs in support | 5 | 3 |
| Stability | Very High | Moderate-High |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Energy efficiency | Low (long cycle) | High (short cycle) |
| Terrain adaptability | Excellent | Good |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T = 1.6 \, \text{s}$, $\beta = 0.75$, $L = 0.4 \, \text{m}$, $h = 0.08 \, \text{m}$.

**(i) Swing Time $T_r$ (2 marks):**

$$T_s = \beta \cdot T = 0.75 \times 1.6 = 1.2 \, \text{s}$$

$$T_r = T - T_s = 1.6 - 1.2 = 0.4 \, \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T_r = 0.4 \, \text{s}}$$

**(ii) Stride Length $\lambda$ (3 marks):**

Using the geometric relation derived earlier:

$$\lambda = 2 \sqrt{L^2 - (L - h)^2}$$

Compute the inner expression:
$$L^2 = (0.4)^2 = 0.16$$
$$(L - h)^2 = (0.4 - 0.08)^2 = (0.32)^2 = 0.1024$$
$$L^2 - (L - h)^2 = 0.16 - 0.1024 = 0.0576$$
$$\sqrt{0.0576} = 0.24 \, \text{m}$$
$$\lambda = 2 \times 0.24 = 0.48 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 0.48 \, \text{m}}$$

**(iii) Locomotion Speed $V$ (2 marks):**

$$V = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \frac{0.48}{1.6} = 0.3 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V = 0.3 \, \text{m/s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** in the context of bipedal robot balance. Derive the relationship between the ZMP coordinates $(p_x, p_y)$ and the Center of Mass coordinates $(x, y, z)$ under a simplified inverted-pendulum model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **crawl gait** for a quadruped robot. A quadruped walks with the crawl gait. The body length is 1.0 m and the leg spacing is 0.5 m. If the robot must maintain static stability with at least 3 legs on the ground at all times, verify the gait's feasibility and calculate the minimum turning radius. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B:**

**(a) ZMP Concept and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the point on the ground where the **net moment of the inertial forces and gravity** about that point is **zero**. For a legged robot to be dynamically stable, the ZMP must lie within the support polygon (convex hull of contact feet). If the ZMP leaves the polygon, the robot begins to rotate (tip over).

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Consider the robot's total mass $m$ concentrated at its CoM at position $(x, y, z)$. The linear momentum is $P = m \dot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}$ and angular momentum about the CoM is $L$.

The moments about a point $(p_x, p_y, 0)$ on the ground are:

$$\tau_x = mg(x - p_x) - m \ddot{z}(p_y - 0) - \dot{P}_y z + \dot{L}_y$$

For ZMP, $\tau_x = 0$. Ignoring the angular momentum rate (linear inverted pendulum model):

$$p_x = x - \frac{z \ddot{x}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$

Similarly:

$$p_y = y - \frac{z \ddot{y}}{g + \ddot{z}}$$

These are the **ZMP equations**. For a constant CoM height $z = h$ (linear inverted pendulum mode, LIPM), they reduce to:

$$p_x = x - \frac{h \ddot{x}}{g}, \quad p_y = y - \frac{h \ddot{y}}{g}$$

Stability condition: $(p_x, p_y)$ must lie inside the support polygon.

---

**(b) Crawl Gait Description and Verification (7 marks):**

**Crawl Gait Description (3 marks):**
The **crawl gait** is a **sequentially alternating gait** where only one leg is in swing at a time. The legs lift in a specific order (e.g., FL → FR → HL → HR or similar cyclic pattern) so that **three legs always remain on the ground**, forming a stable triangle. It is the **safest and most statically stable** gait for a quadruped, ideal for uneven or rocky terrain.

**Verification of Static Stability (2 marks):**
For a quadruped, static stability during walking requires $n - 1 = 3$ support legs. Since the crawl gait always keeps 3 legs grounded (non-collinear), the projected CoG remains inside the triangular support polygon at all times. Therefore, the gait is **feasible for static stability** ✓.

**Minimum Turning Radius (2 marks):**
For a quadruped with body length $L_b = 1.0 \, \text{m}$ and leg spacing $L_s = 0.5 \, \text{m}$:

$$R_{min} = \frac{L_s}{2 \sin(\pi/n)} = \frac{0.5}{2 \sin(45°)} = \frac{0.5}{2 \times 0.7071} = \frac{0.5}{1.4142} \approx 0.354 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{R_{min} \approx 0.354 \, \text{m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in $V = \lambda / T$ — always write m/s explicitly. (Lose 0.5 mark)
> 2. **Confusing stride length with step length** — stride is *per cycle*, step is *per leg swing*. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Misapplying the duty factor formula** — $\beta$ is per leg, not per robot. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **In ZMP questions**, students often forget the gravitational acceleration $g$ in the denominator. (Lose 1.5 marks)
> 5. **Sketches in gait questions** must be drawn — a description without a diagram loses at least 1 mark.
> 6. **For crawl vs tripod gaits**, do not interchange them — crawl = 1 leg swing, tripod = 3 legs swing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Gait** = cyclic, coordinated limb movement pattern for legged locomotion.
- ✅ **Body Control** = real-time regulation of CoG/ZMP for stability.
- ✅ **Duty factor** $\beta = T_s / T$; range $[0, 1]$; **higher β = more stable but slower**.
- ✅ **Stride length** $\lambda = 2\sqrt{L^2 - (L-h)^2}$ for a leg lifting to height $h$.
- ✅ **Locomotion speed** $V = \lambda / T$.
- ✅ **Static stability** requires CoG projection *inside* the support polygon at all times.
- ✅ **Dynamic stability** uses ZMP and momentum — required for bipedal walking.
- ✅ **Minimum legs for static walking** = **4** (3 must always be grounded).
- ✅ **Wave gait** = 1 leg swing, **Tripod gait** = 3 legs swing, **Crawl gait** = 1 leg swing (sequentially).
- ✅ **Hexapod with tripod gait** is the most common statically stable configuration.
- ✅ **ZMP** must remain *inside* the support polygon; if it exits, the robot tips over.
- ✅ **Whole-Body Control (WBC)** is the modern optimization-based approach to body control.
- ✅ **Sensors for body control:** IMU (accel + gyro), force/torque sensors in feet, joint encoders.
- ✅ **Biped = dynamic**, **Quadruped = crawl is static**, **Hexapod = tripod is static** — remember this hierarchy!
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** Always include a **neat sketch** when describing a gait. Always **state units** in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Gaits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Gaits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **dynamic gait** in mobile robotics is a periodic, legged locomotion pattern in which the robot exploits **momentum exchange**, **inertial effects**, and **gravity** to move forward, rather than relying on quasi-static stability at every instant of the motion cycle. The robot may be statically unstable at certain phases of the gait (i.e., the projection of the Center of Mass, **CoM**, lies outside the support polygon), but the **cyclic motion remains dynamically stable** as a whole due to conserved mechanical energy and the controller's ability to reject disturbances within a bounded region of attraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PEBRT522 Module 1):**
> Dynamic Gaits belong to the broader umbrella of **Periodic Gaits**, studied alongside **Aperiodic Gaits** and **Non-Periodic Gaits**. The focus lies on understanding the relationship between gait symmetry, energy consumption, and stability in legged and hybrid mobile platforms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing. You do not hold the child perfectly still at every instant; instead, you give a small push at the right moment, and the swing continues due to its built-up momentum. The swing is **dynamically stable** even though at the top of each arc, it momentarily slows to nearly zero speed (a state that is statically "unstable" because gravity is pulling it back).

A **dynamic gait** works exactly the same way:
- The robot "falls forward" and "catches itself" with the next leg contact.
- Energy is stored and released in springs (mechanical or virtual) much like the swing.
- The gait is a **limit cycle**: a closed loop in state-space (position, velocity) that the system follows naturally.
- Small disturbances only nudge the state slightly off the cycle, and the system's natural dynamics pull it back.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction**
> - **Static Gait**: Stable at *every* instant → slow, energy-inefficient.
> - **Dynamic Gait**: Stable *over one cycle* → fast, efficient, natural-looking.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following quantities appear universally in dynamic gait analysis:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Body length scale | $L$ | meters (m) |
| Froude number (dimensionless speed) | $Fr$ | dimensionless |
| Strouhal number | $St$ | dimensionless |
| Specific resistance | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless |
| Duty factor | $\beta$ | $0 < \beta \leq 0.5$ (dynamic) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### Step 1: From Static to Dynamic — The Motivating Problem
A statically stable walker must keep its CoM over the support polygon at *all times*. This forces:
- A minimum of **three legs on the ground** at any moment (for quadrupeds).
- Slow, "stop-and-go" foot placement.
- High energy cost per unit distance.

Dynamic gaits break this rigidity. By allowing the CoM to leave the support polygon momentarily and using inertia to carry the body forward, robots can achieve **higher speeds** and **lower energy expenditure**.

### Step 2: Periodic Forcing and Limit Cycles
A dynamic gait is a **limit cycle** in the system's state space. Mathematically, if $x(t)$ is the state vector (positions, velocities, leg contact modes), then a periodic gait satisfies:

$$x(t + T) = x(t)$$

where $T$ is the gait period. A limit cycle is **stable** if perturbations decay back to the cycle, which is the defining property of a useful dynamic gait.

### Step 3: Energy Storage in Elastic Elements
Animals and efficient robots exploit **compliant elements** (tendons, springs, series elastic actuators). The canonical model is the **Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum (SLIP)**:

- **Stance phase**: Leg acts as a compressed spring, storing energy.
- **Flight phase**: Leg leaves ground; body travels as a projectile.
- **Touchdown**: Energy is re-injected into the next stance.

### Step 4: Gait Classification by Foot-Fall Pattern
The four canonical dynamic quadruped gaits are:

| Gait | Foot-Fall Pattern | Symmetry | Typical Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trot** | Diagonal pairs (LF+RB, RF+LB) | Symmetric | Medium-High |
| **Pace** | Lateral pairs (LF+LB, RF+RB) | Symmetric | High (camelids) |
| **Bound** | Front pair then rear pair | Symmetric | High |
| **Gallop** | Asymmetric transverse/rotary | Asymmetric | Highest |

> [!NOTE]
> At the boundary between walking (static) and running (dynamic) lies the **Froude number threshold** $Fr \approx 0.5$, beyond which an animal naturally transitions into a dynamic gait.

### Step 5: Stability Analysis Tools
Three mathematical tools dominate dynamic gait analysis:
1. **Poincaré Map** — Samples the state once per gait cycle; stability = eigenvalues inside the unit circle.
2. **Zero-Moment Point (ZMP)** — For dynamic gaits, the ZMP must remain inside the support polygon; otherwise, the gait is unstable.
3. **Lyapunov-like energy methods** — Total mechanical energy bounded above and below.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Fr = \dfrac{v^{2}}{gL}$ | Froude number; transitions from walk to run at $Fr \approx 0.5$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{t_{stance}}{T}$ | Duty factor; $\beta < 0.5 \Rightarrow$ dynamic gait | dimensionless |
| 3 | $St = \dfrac{f A}{v}$ | Strouhal number; $A$ = stride amplitude, $f$ = frequency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{P}{mgv}$ | Specific resistance; energy cost per unit weight per distance | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sum \tau_{ext} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(I\omega)$ | Rotational dynamics; angular momentum conservation in flight | N·m·s |
| 6 | $E_{mech} = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2} + mgh + \tfrac{1}{2}k x^{2}$ | Total mechanical energy (kinetic + gravitational + elastic) | J |
| 7 | $T_{stride} = \dfrac{1}{f_{stride}}$ | Stride period | s |
| 8 | $\dot{x}_{CoM} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i} F_{i} \cdot \Delta t$ | CoM impulse from ground reaction forces | m/s |
| 9 | $\vert \vec{r}_{ZMP} - \vec{r}_{CoM} \vert \leq \dfrac{d_{support}}{2}$ | ZMP bounded within support polygon | m |
| 10 | $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1,\ \forall i$ | Eigenvalues of Poincaré return map (stability criterion) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Dynamic gaits underpin the design of quadruped robots such as Boston Dynamics' Spot, ANYbotics' ANYmal, MIT Mini-Cheetah, and humanoid platforms like Atlas. Mastering these equations is essential for **gait synthesis**, **energy-optimal control**, and **rough-terrain traversal** in real-world deployments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Froude Number from Dimensional Analysis

The Froude number compares **inertial forces** to **gravitational forces** — the two dominant forces in legged locomotion. We derive it systematically.

**Step 1:** List the relevant physical quantities for a legged system moving at speed $v$:
- Speed: $v$ (m/s)
- Leg length (characteristic length scale): $L$ (m)
- Gravitational acceleration: $g$ (m/s²)
- Body mass: $m$ (kg)

**Step 2:** Form the dimensionless group using the Buckingham Pi theorem. Inertial acceleration scales as $v^{2}/L$, and gravitational acceleration as $g$.

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{v^{2}/L}{g} = \frac{v^{2}}{gL}$$

**Step 3:** Identify this ratio as the **Froude number**:

$$\boxed{Fr = \frac{v^{2}}{gL}}$$

**Step 4 (Interpretation):**
- $Fr \ll 1$: gravity dominates → quasi-static walking regime.
- $Fr \approx 0.5$: walk-run transition (empirically observed in humans and horses).
- $Fr \gg 1$: inertia dominates → fully dynamic, near-flight regime.

> **Conversion Logic:** A horse with $L = 1.5$ m moving at $v = 3$ m/s gives $Fr = 9/(9.81 \times 1.5) \approx 0.61$, comfortably above the walk-run transition — consistent with a walking-trot gait.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability of a Dynamic Gait via the Poincaré Map

For a periodic dynamic gait, define the **Poincaré section** $\Sigma$ as the hyperplane crossed once per cycle (e.g., the instant just after hind-leg touchdown). Let the state at cycle $k$ be $\mathbf{x}_k$.

**Step 1:** Express the gait dynamics as a discrete map:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

**Step 2:** Linearize around the fixed point $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ (the nominal periodic gait):

$$\delta \mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{P}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\mathbf{x}^{*}} \delta \mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{J}\,\delta\mathbf{x}_k$$

**Step 3:** The Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ is the **Monodromy matrix**. The eigenvalues $\{\lambda_i\}$ are the **characteristic multipliers**.

**Step 4 (Stability Criterion):** The gait is locally asymptotically stable if and only if:

$$\boxed{\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1,\quad \forall\, i = 1, 2, \ldots, n}$$

**Step 5:** If any $\vert \lambda_i \vert > 1$, perturbations grow and the robot falls. If $\vert \lambda_i \vert = 1$, the gait is marginally stable (limit cycle boundary).

> **Conversion Logic:** This is why a robot such as ANYmal can trot over a small rock without falling — the eigenvalues of its Poincaré map all lie within a radius of approximately $0.7$–$0.9$, providing a stable basin of attraction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Specific Resistance (Energy Efficiency Metric)

**Step 1:** The mechanical power delivered by the actuators is $P$ (W). For steady locomotion at speed $v$ against gravity $mg$:

$$P = \varepsilon \cdot mgv$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the dimensionless **specific resistance**:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{P}{mgv}}$$

**Step 3:** Reference values for context:

| Locomotion Mode | Typical $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|
| Crawling robot | $10$ – $50$ |
| Walking human | $0.2$ |
| Dynamic walking robot (passive) | $0.05$ – $0.2$ |
| Running cheetah | $\approx 0.4$ |
| Wheeled robot (ideal) | $0.01$ |

> **Conversion Logic:** A passive-dynamic walker descends a slope at $v = 0.5$ m/s with no actuator power; then $\varepsilon \to 0$ asymptotically — the gold standard for energy-efficient dynamic gaits.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Gaits

| Gait | Duty Factor $\beta$ | ZMP within Polygon? | Energy Storage | Typical Fr |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static walk | $\beta > 0.5$ | Always | Minimal | $< 0.5$ |
| **Trot** | $\beta \approx 0.4$ | Diagonal-pair alternation | Spring + inertia | $0.5$ – $2$ |
| **Pace** | $\beta \approx 0.35$ | Lateral-pair alternation | High | $1$ – $3$ |
| **Bound** | $\beta \approx 0.3$ | Front/rear alternation | Very high | $2$ – $5$ |
| **Gallop** | $\beta \approx 0.25$ | Asymmetric | Maximum | $> 5$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Dynamic Gait State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stance Phase: Leg Contact] --> B{CoM Within Support Polygon?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Stable Subphase]
    B -- No --> D[Flight Phase: Aerial]
    C --> E[Energy Stored in Spring]
    E --> F[Push-Off Impulse]
    F --> A
    D --> G[Projectile Trajectory]
    G --> H{Touchdown Event}
    H --> A
    H --> I{Poincare Map Sample}
    I --> J[Compute Monodromy Matrix]
    J --> K{All Eigenvalues within Unit Circle?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Gait Stable]
    K -- No --> M[Gait Unstable - Fall]
    L --> A
    M --> N[Recovery Controller Activates]
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Gait Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Speed Command v] --> Q[Compute Froude Number]
    Q --> R{Fr less than 0.5}
    R -- Yes --> S[Static Walk Gait]
    R -- No --> T{Fr between 0.5 and 1.5}
    T -- Yes --> U[Trot Gait]
    T -- No --> V{Fr between 1.5 and 3}
    V -- Yes --> W[Bound or Pace Gait]
    V -- No --> X[Gallop Gait]
    S --> Y[Energy Inefficient]
    U --> Z[Energy Optimal]
    W --> Z
    X --> AA[High Energy but Fast]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dynamic Gait Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        S1[Desired Velocity Vector]
        S2[Terrain Slope Estimator]
        S3[IMU Sensor Stream]
        S4[Leg Position Encoders]
    end
    subgraph GaitPlanner[Gait Planner]
        GP1[Froude Number Calculator]
        GP2[Gait Selector FSM]
        GP3[Footfall Pattern Generator]
    end
    subgraph ControllerCore[Controller Core]
        CC1[SLIP Model Reference]
        CC2[Virtual Model Controller]
        CC3[MPC or Whole-Body Controller]
    end
    subgraph ActuationLayer[Actuation Layer]
        AL1[Leg Joint Torque Commands]
        AL2[Series Elastic Actuators]
        AL3[Ground Reaction Force Sensors]
    end
    subgraph FeedbackLoop[Feedback Loop]
        FB1[State Estimator]
        FB2[Poincare Stability Monitor]
        FB3[Disturbance Rejection]
    end
    S1 --> GP1
    S2 --> GP1
    GP1 --> GP2
    S3 --> FB1
    S4 --> FB1
    FB1 --> CC2
    GP2 --> GP3
    GP3 --> CC1
    CC1 --> CC3
    CC2 --> CC3
    CC3 --> AL1
    AL1 --> AL2
    AL2 --> AL3
    AL3 --> FB2
    FB2 --> CC3
    AL3 --> FB3
    FB3 --> GP2
```

## 4.4 Schematic: SLIP Model Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Kinetic Energy: 0.5 m v^2] --> B[Spring Compression Phase]
    B --> C[Elastic PE: 0.5 k x^2]
    C --> D[Push-Off Phase]
    D --> E[Gravitational PE: mgh]
    E --> F[Flight Phase Ballistic]
    F --> A
    G[Energy Loss: damping eta] -.->|subtracts from cycle| B
```

> [!NOTE]
> In an **ideal SLIP model**, the cycle $A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F \to A$ is **conservative**, meaning total mechanical energy is preserved. In real robots, damping $\eta$ bleeds energy, which the controller must replenish via actuators.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define dynamic gait in mobile robotics. How does it differ from a static gait?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **dynamic gait** is a periodic locomotion pattern in which the robot exploits momentum, gravity, and stored elastic energy to move forward, and may be statically unstable for parts of the cycle. The gait as a whole remains dynamically stable if perturbations decay over successive cycles.
A **static gait** maintains the projection of the CoM inside the support polygon at every instant, requiring no momentum-based balancing. Dynamic gaits are faster and more energy-efficient, while static gaits are inherently more robust on rough terrain at low speeds.

### Question 2
**Q: What is the role of the Froude number in gait classification?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Froude number** $Fr = v^{2}/(gL)$ is a dimensionless quantity that compares inertial to gravitational forces during legged locomotion. It predicts gait transitions: at $Fr \approx 0.5$ animals and robots transition from a walk to a trot or run; higher values correspond to pace, bound, and gallop. It enables gait selection independent of body size, making it a universal scaling parameter in mobile robotics.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Derive the Froude number from dimensional analysis and explain its role in selecting between static walking and dynamic gaits. Show with numerical examples. **[(7 Marks)]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i)** List the relevant variables: $v$ [m/s], $L$ [m], $g$ [m/s²], $m$ [kg].
**(ii)** Form the dimensionless group by dividing inertial acceleration $v^{2}/L$ by gravitational acceleration $g$:

$$Fr = \frac{v^{2}}{gL}$$

**(iii)** Numerical example — quadruped robot with $L = 0.5$ m:
- Walking at $v = 0.5$ m/s: $Fr = 0.25/(9.81 \times 0.5) \approx 0.05$ → static walk.
- Trotting at $v = 1.5$ m/s: $Fr = 2.25/(9.81 \times 0.5) \approx 0.46$ → near transition.
- Galloping at $v = 3.0$ m/s: $Fr = 9/(9.81 \times 0.5) \approx 1.83$ → dynamic gallop.

**(iv)** Interpretation: At $Fr < 0.5$, gravity dominates and a static gait suffices. At $Fr > 0.5$, inertia dominates and a dynamic gait (trot/bound/gallop) is preferred for energy efficiency and stability.

---

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the four canonical dynamic gaits of a quadruped robot. Discuss how foot-fall pattern influences energy storage and stability. **[(7 Marks)]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Trot:** Diagonal leg pairs (LF+RB, RF+LB) move in synchrony. Two feet are always in stance. Suitable for medium-high speeds; low lateral oscillation; high energy efficiency.

**Pace:** Lateral leg pairs (LF+LB, RF+RB) move together. Common in camels. Reduces rolling but induces yaw moments.

**Bound:** Front pair alternates with rear pair. Front-rear coupling enables large spring storage; favoured in fast, sprawled-posture robots (e.g., MIT Mini-Cheetah).

**Gallop:** Asymmetric and fastest. Includes a gathered and a suspended phase; aerial intervals maximize spring recoil. Used in high-speed robotic cheetah platforms.

**Influence on Stability and Energy:**
- Symmetric gaits (trot, pace) ⇒ simpler control, predictable Poincaré maps.
- Asymmetric gaits (gallop) ⇒ larger energy storage but more complex stability analysis.
- Foot-fall pattern determines **duty factor** $\beta$; lower $\beta$ ⇒ more aerial phase ⇒ higher dynamic component.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Describe the Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum (SLIP) model as a unifying framework for dynamic running gaits. Derive the energy expressions for stance and flight phases. **[(7 Marks)]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **SLIP model** represents the body as a point mass $m$ on a massless, compliant leg modelled as a linear spring of stiffness $k$ and rest length $L_0$.

**Stance phase energy:**
The spring compression $x = L_0 - L$ stores elastic potential energy:

$$E_{stance} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} + \frac{1}{2} k (L_0 - L)^{2} + m g h$$

**Flight phase energy:**
No ground contact; body is a projectile:

$$E_{flight} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} + m g h$$

The SLIP model unifies running in animals and robots because:
- Stance phase converts kinetic energy to elastic PE and back (like a bouncing ball).
- Flight phase conserves mechanical energy.
- Proper tuning of $k$, $L_0$, and touchdown angle yields stable, energy-efficient gaits — verified on robots like the MIT Leg Lab monopod and RHex.

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of a limit cycle in dynamic gait stability. Describe how the Poincaré map is used to analyze periodic gait stability, and state the stability criterion in terms of eigenvalues. **[(7 Marks)]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Limit Cycle:** A closed, isolated trajectory in state space $\mathbf{x} = (q, \dot{q})$ that the system follows periodically. A **stable** limit cycle attracts nearby trajectories; an **unstable** one repels them.

**Poincaré Map Construction:**
1. Choose a transversal section $\Sigma$ (e.g., the moment after a leg touchdown).
2. Record state $\mathbf{x}_k$ at each $k$-th crossing of $\Sigma$.
3. The map $\mathbf{P} : \Sigma \to \Sigma$ advances one full gait cycle:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

4. A periodic gait corresponds to a fixed point $\mathbf{x}^{*} = \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}^{*})$.

**Linearization and Stability:**
Compute the Jacobian (Monodromy matrix):

$$\mathbf{J} = \left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{P}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\mathbf{x}^{*}}$$

with eigenvalues $\{\lambda_i\}$. The gait is **asymptotically stable** iff:

$$\boxed{\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1,\quad \forall i}$$

A disturbance $\delta \mathbf{x}_k$ evolves as $\delta \mathbf{x}_{k+n} = \mathbf{J}^{n}\delta \mathbf{x}_k$; since $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$, perturbations decay geometrically.

**Practical Use:** Implemented on BigDog, ANYmal, and Mini-Cheetah controllers to verify gait robustness before deployment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** The Froude number is dimensionless — writing $Fr = v^{2}/(gL)$ without noting units will cost a mark.
> 2. **Confusing duty factor:** $\beta < 0.5$ defines a *dynamic* gait; do not state "dynamic gaits have $\beta > 0.5$".
> 3. **Poincaré map stability:** Many students write $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 0$ (wrong sign); the correct inequality is $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$.
> 4. **SLIP derivation:** Do not skip the flight-phase derivation; examiners explicitly check both phases.
> 5. **ZMP vs CoM:** In dynamic gaits, ZMP may leave the support polygon *briefly*; the gait is still stable if the limit cycle is attracting. Do not write "ZMP must always stay inside".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dynamic gait** = periodic, momentum-based, possibly statically unstable but cyclically stable locomotion.
- **Froude number** $Fr = v^{2}/(gL)$: walk-run transition near $Fr \approx 0.5$; used for size-independent gait scaling.
- **Duty factor** $\beta = t_{stance}/T$: dynamic gaits have $\beta < 0.5$.
- **Four canonical dynamic quadruped gaits:** Trot, Pace, Bound, Gallop — each with distinct foot-fall pattern, symmetry, and speed range.
- **SLIP model** unifies running dynamics via spring energy storage; stance stores elastic PE, flight conserves total mechanical energy.
- **Limit cycle**: the closed periodic trajectory in state space that defines a stable gait.
- **Poincaré map**: discrete-time tool to test stability; criterion $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for all eigenvalues of the Monodromy matrix.
- **Specific resistance** $\varepsilon = P/(mgv)$ quantifies energy efficiency; lower is better; passive walkers achieve near-zero.
- **ZMP** is a useful diagnostic but not a strict requirement during transient phases of dynamic gaits.
- **Symmetry** (trot, pace) simplifies control; **asymmetry** (gallop) enables higher speed and energy storage.
- **Engineering relevance:** Spot, ANYmal, Mini-Cheetah, Atlas all rely on dynamic gait theory for their controllers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Off-Board Communication: Tethered
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Off-Board Communication: Tethered # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tethered Off-Board Communication** in mobile robotics refers to a wired communication and/or power transmission architecture in which a **physical cable (tether)** establishes a continuous, hard-wired link between the **mobile robotic platform** (on-board subsystem) and a **stationary off-board base station** (ground control unit, host computer, or power supply). In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this is classified under **Module 1: Robot Subsystems and Components**, where communication is divided into two broad families: - **On-Board Communication** — internal bus-level links (e.g., CAN, I²C, SPI, UART) between robot modules. - **Off-Board Communication** — links crossing the robot's physical boundary, which are further classified as: 1. **Tethered (Wired)** — physical cable coupling. 2. **Wireless** — RF, optical, acoustic coupling. > [!NOTE] > The term **tether** is derived from Old English *teþor*, originally meaning a rope or chain by which an animal is held. In robotics, it has the same connotation: a **physical leash** that maintains a permanent association between the robot and its host. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation Imagine you have a **remote-controlled toy car** that not only receives commands through a wire but also **draws its power from the controller's battery** through that same wire. That wire is the **tether**. A more relatable analogy is an **underwater diver**: - The diver (robot) explores freely within the length of the air hose. - The surface ship (base station) supplies air (power) and receives voice/sensor data (communication) through the hose. - Cut the hose, and the diver is on their own — the robot becomes autonomous or fails. In the same way, a **tethered mobile robot** enjoys: - **Unlimited runtime** (power delivered continuously). - **High-bandwidth, low-latency, noise-free** communication. - But is **range-restricted** by the cable length and must **manage cable drag**. ## 1.3 Why Tethered Communication? — The Engineering Rationale > [!NOTE] > **Standard Performance Metrics** for tethered links include: > - **Bandwidth:** typically **10 Mbps to 10 Gbps** (e.g., Ethernet over Cat6a). > - **Latency:** typically **< 1 ms** for short tethers. > - **Power Delivery:** **up to several kW** depending on conductor gauge. > - **Maximum Practical Length:** **~100 m for copper Ethernet**, **several km for fiber-optic tethers**. The fundamental reason to choose a tether is **determinism**. Wireless links suffer from **interference, multipath fading, packet loss, and security vulnerabilities**. A wired link is essentially deterministic — the signal either arrives intact or the cable is broken. ## 1.4 Classification of Tethers Tethers can be classified by **function** and by **physical medium**: ### A. By Function | Function | Description | Typical Use | |----------|-------------|-------------| | **Power Tether** | Carries only electrical power (DC or AC) | Industrial AGVs, charging while operating | | **Communication Tether** | Carries only data signals (e.g., Ethernet, USB, fiber) | Surveillance robots | | **Hybrid Tether** | Carries both power and data | ROVs, bomb disposal robots | | **Opto-Electric Tether** | Power over copper + data over fiber | Long-range or EMI-critical missions | ### B. By Physical Medium | Medium | Material | Max Bandwidth | EMI Immunity | Cost | |--------|----------|---------------|--------------|------| | **Twisted Pair Copper** | Cat5e, Cat6a | 10 Gbps | Low | Low | | **Coaxial Cable** | RG-58, RG-174 | Few Gbps | Moderate | Moderate | | **Fiber Optic** | Glass / Plastic | 100+ Gbps | Very High | High | | **Hybrid Composite** | Copper + Fiber | Multi-Tbps | High | High | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Tethered Robotic System A tethered robotic system has **three core architectural layers**: 1. **On-Board (Robot-Side) Subsystem** - Actuators, sensors, embedded controllers. - Tether interface board (slip ring, cable gland, strain relief). 2. **The Tether (Physical Medium)** - Conductors / optical fibers. - Mechanical reinforcement (Kevlar, steel braid). - Jacket (PUR, PVC, TPE) for environmental protection. 3. **Off-Board (Base-Side) Subsystem** - Power supply unit (PSU) or battery. - Host computer / HMI (Human-Machine Interface). - Cable management system (winch, spool, cable reel). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** In Part B questions, students are expected to draw a **clear functional block diagram** showing these three layers and label the direction of **power flow** and **data flow** distinctly. Examiners reward **separate arrow styles** (e.g., solid red for power, dashed blue for data). ## 2.2 Power Transmission in Tethers ### 2.2.1 Voltage Drop in Copper Tether The most critical design constraint in a power tether is the **resistive voltage drop** over the length of the cable. For a DC system: $$\Delta V = 2 \cdot I \cdot R_c \cdot L$$ where: - $\Delta V$ = voltage drop (in Volts), - $I$ = current drawn by the robot (in Amperes), - $R_c$ = **DC resistance per unit length** of one conductor (in Ω/m), - $L$ = length of the tether (in meters). The **factor of 2** appears because current must flow out *and* return through the tether (closed loop). ### 2.2.2 Power Loss in Tether The resistive loss (heat dissipated) is: $$P_{loss} = I^{2} \cdot 2 R_c L = \frac{(\Delta V)^{2}}{2 R_c L}$$ ### 2.2.3 Maximum Tether Length for Power Budget For a given **acceptable voltage drop** $\Delta V_{max}$ and **robot current demand** $I$: $$L_{max} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{2 \cdot I \cdot R_c}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** This formula is the first thing an ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle) designer calculates. Subsea ROVs often operate at 3000 V at the surface, stepping down to lower voltages at the robot — *because high voltage reduces current, and current reduction reduces I²R losses quadratically.* ## 2.3 Data Transmission in Tethers ### 2.3.1 Signal Attenuation For a copper conductor, signal loss per unit length (in dB/m) is: $$\alpha_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) \quad \text{per meter}$$ For differential signaling (e.g., RS-485, Ethernet), the **maximum cable length** is governed by the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** at the receiver: $$SNR_{dB} = P_{tx} - \alpha_{dB} \cdot L - N_{floor}$$ where: - $P_{tx}$ = transmit power (dBm), - $\alpha_{dB}$ = attenuation coefficient (dB/m), - $L$ = cable length (m), - $N_{floor}$ = noise floor of receiver (dBm). ### 2.3.2 Fiber Optic Tethers Fiber optics eliminate **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** entirely. The **attenuation** in single-mode fiber is governed by: $$P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$$ For standard **single-mode fiber (SMF-28)**, $\alpha \approx \mathbf{0.2\ dB/km}$ at 1550 nm wavelength — meaning **virtually no loss** for tether lengths under 10 km. ## 2.4 Cable Management — The Hidden Engineering Challenge > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Cable management is *the* most asked sub-topic in Part B. Students who skip it lose 3–4 marks per question. ### 2.4.1 Tether Drag Force When a robot pulls a tether, the **slack tether** experiences **friction** with the ground. The total drag force is: $$F_{drag} = \mu \cdot w \cdot L_{ground}$$ where: - $\mu$ = **coefficient of friction** between tether jacket and ground (typically **0.2 to 0.6**), - $w$ = **weight per unit length** of the tether (N/m), - $L_{ground}$ = length of tether in contact with the ground (m). ### 2.4.2 Tether Management Strategies | Strategy | Principle | Application | |----------|-----------|-------------| | **Slack Tether** | Excess cable laid behind robot | Indoor mobile robots | | **Spool / Winch** | Active cable reel maintaining constant tension | ROVs, aerial drones | | **Top-Roller / Boom** | Cable suspended above operating area | Crane-mounted robots | | **Tether-Free Zones** | Robot disconnects tether at docking station | Hybrid autonomous-tethered | | **Buoyancy Compensation** (Subsea) | Tether is neutrally buoyant in water | Deep-sea ROVs | ## 2.5 Slip Rings — The Rotational Coupler When the robot or base station **rotates continuously**, a **slip ring** (also called a **rotary electrical joint**) is required to pass signals across a rotating interface. Slip rings have: - **Number of circuits** (electrical channels), - **Voltage rating** (e.g., 240 V AC, 48 V DC), - **Current rating** (e.g., 2 A per channel), - **Bandwidth** (for data slip rings: up to **10 Gbps Ethernet**), - **Rotational speed** (RPM). > [!NOTE] > **For fiber-optic tethers, Fiber Optic Rotary Joints (FORJs)** are used. These are passive, contactless devices using **collimated lenses** to pass light across a rotating gap with insertion loss typically **< 1 dB**. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Variables | Application | |---|---------|-----------|-------------| | 1 | $\Delta V = 2 I R_c L$ | Voltage drop over copper tether | Power budget calculation | | 2 | $L_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{max}}{2 I R_c}$ | Max tether length for power budget | ROV design | | 3 | $P_{loss} = I^{2} \cdot 2 R_c L$ | Resistive heat loss | Energy efficiency | | 4 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | Optical signal attenuation | Fiber-optic tether | | 5 | $F_{drag} = \mu w L_{ground}$ | Tether ground friction | Cable management | | 6 | $SNR_{dB} = P_{tx} - \alpha L - N_{floor}$ | Link budget | Communication tether | | 7 | $B_{max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot SNR}{\log_2 M}$ | Channel capacity (Shannon-like) | Maximum data rate | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** In markdown table cells, students (and answer scripts) often break the **vertical pipe** `\|` in absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$. This destroys table rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Tether Length from Power Budget ### Problem Setup A tethered mobile robot draws **5 A** at **24 V DC** through a copper tether of **resistance 0.05 Ω per conductor per km**. The base station can supply a maximum of **27 V**. The on-board regulator requires at least **22 V** to function. What is the **maximum tether length**? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the allowable voltage drop.** The voltage at the base station is 27 V. The minimum required at the robot is 22 V. The allowable drop is: $$\Delta V_{max} = V_{supply} - V_{min} = 27 - 22 = 5\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Express resistance per meter.** The given resistance is 0.05 Ω per conductor **per kilometer**. Converting to per meter: $$R_c = \frac{0.05\ \Omega}{1000\ \text{m}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \Omega/\text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the voltage-drop formula.** Using the equation from Section 2.2.1: $$\Delta V = 2 \cdot I \cdot R_c \cdot L$$ **Step 4 — Solve for L.** Rearranging: $$L = \frac{\Delta V}{2 \cdot I \cdot R_c}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.** $$L = \frac{5}{2 \times 5 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$L = \frac{5}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$L = \frac{5}{0.0005}$$ $$L = 10{,}000\ \text{m} = \mathbf{10\ km}$$ ### Conclusion The robot can operate up to **10 km** from the base station, assuming a uniform conductor and no derating. In practice, derating factors for temperature, voltage safety margin, and connector losses reduce this to **~7–8 km**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Loss in the Tether ### Problem Setup Using the same scenario: $I = 5$ A, $R_c = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ Ω/m, $L = 10{,}000$ m. Find the **power dissipated as heat** in the tether and the **efficiency** of power transmission. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Total tether loop resistance.** The current flows out and back, so the effective resistance is: $$R_{loop} = 2 \cdot R_c \cdot L = 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 10{,}000$$ $$R_{loop} = 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-1} = 1.0\ \Omega$$ **Step 2 — Compute I²R loss.** $$P_{loss} = I^{2} \cdot R_{loop} = (5)^{2} \times 1.0 = 25\ \text{W}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the input power at the base station.** The current is delivered at 27 V (supply voltage): $$P_{in} = V_{supply} \times I = 27 \times 5 = 135\ \text{W}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the received power at the robot.** $$P_{robot} = V_{robot} \times I = 22 \times 5 = 110\ \text{W}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the efficiency.** $$\eta = \frac{P_{robot}}{P_{in}} = \frac{110}{135} = 0.8148$$ $$\eta = \mathbf{81.48\%}$$ ### Engineering Insight Even in a well-designed tether, **~18% of input power is wasted as heat**. This is why long-distance subsea ROVs use **high-voltage transmission (3 kV–10 kV)** — to drop the current, and hence cut I²R losses quadratically. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fiber-Optic Link Budget ### Problem Setup A subsea tethered robot uses a **single-mode fiber** with attenuation $\alpha = 0.25$ dB/km at 1550 nm. The transmitter launches $P_{tx} = +3$ dBm. The receiver sensitivity is $N_{floor} = -27$ dBm. Find the **maximum tether length** for a 3 dB SNR margin. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the total allowable attenuation.** Total loss budget = Transmit power − Receiver sensitivity − SNR margin: $$A_{max} = P_{tx} - N_{floor} - SNR_{margin}$$ $$A_{max} = 3 - (-27) - 3 = 27\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for maximum length.** $$L_{max} = \frac{A_{max}}{\alpha} = \frac{27\ \text{dB}}{0.25\ \text{dB/km}}$$ $$L_{max} = \mathbf{108\ km}$$ ### Conclusion A single-mode fiber-optic tether can theoretically reach **108 km** with a 3 dB margin. In practice, connector losses (0.3 dB each × N connectors), splice losses, and aging derate this to **~80 km**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tether Selection Decision Tree | Constraint | Choose Copper | Choose Fiber | Choose Hybrid | |------------|---------------|--------------|----------------| | Distance < 100 m | ✓ (cheap, simple) | ✗ (overkill) | ✗ | | EMI environment | ✗ (susceptible) | ✓ (immune) | ✓ | | Need power transfer | ✓ (native) | ✗ (fiber carries no power) | ✓ | | Need high bandwidth > 1 Gbps | ✗ (limited) | ✓ (10+ Gbps easy) | ✓ | | Subsea / long range | ✗ (loss) | ✓ (low loss) | ✓ | | Cost-sensitive | ✓ (cheap) | ✗ (expensive) | ✗ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Tethered Robotic System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Base Station PSU 24V DC] -->|Power via Copper| nodeB[Tether Interface Unit Slip Ring] nodeC[Host Computer HMI] -->|Ethernet TX RX| nodeB nodeB -->|Hybrid Composite Cable| nodeD[Onboard Power Conditioner 24V to 5V 3V3] nodeB -->|Ethernet to MCU| nodeE[Main Controller STM32] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE -->|PWM GPIO| nodeF[Motor Driver L298N] nodeF --> nodeG[DC Motors with Encoders] nodeE -->|I2C SPI UART| nodeH[Sensor Array IMU Lidar Camera] nodeG -.->|Tactile Feedback| nodeE nodeH -.->|Telemetry Data| nodeE nodeE -.->|Return Path| nodeB ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Power and Data Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant BS as Base Station participant TIU as Tether Interface Unit participant RBT as Mobile Robot participant SEN as Onboard Sensors BS->>TIU: Regulated DC power 24V BS->>TIU: Command packet High level TIU->>RBT: Power over copper conductors TIU->>RBT: Serialized data over differential pair RBT->>SEN: Trigger sensor read I2C SEN-->>RBT: Sensor data IMU Lidar RBT->>TIU: Telemetry stream encoded TIU->>BS: Decode and display on HMI Note over BS,RBT: All communication is deterministic
and synchronous via tether ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Hybrid Composite Tether ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Outer Jacket PUR abrasion resistant] --> nodeB[Kevlar Braid tensile member] nodeB --> nodeC[Inner Jacket TPE dielectric] nodeC --> nodeD1[Power Conductor 14 AWG copper x 2] nodeC --> nodeD2[Signal Pair Cat6a twisted x 4] nodeC --> nodeD3[Single Mode Fiber G652D x 2] nodeC --> nodeD4[Drain Wire bare copper] nodeD4 --> nodeE[Total OD approx 12 mm] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flow — Cable Management Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[Robot Mission Start] --> nodeQ1{Operating Environment} nodeQ1 -->|Indoor Flat| nodeP1[Slack Tether with Cable Tray] nodeQ1 -->|Outdoor Uneven| nodeP2[Active Winch with Tension Sensor] nodeQ1 -->|Subsea Underwater| nodeP3[Buoyancy Modulated Tether with FORJ] nodeQ1 -->|Aerial UAV| nodeP4[Top Suspended Tether on Boom] nodeP1 --> nodeEnd[Cable Management Active] nodeP2 --> nodeEnd nodeP3 --> nodeEnd nodeP4 --> nodeEnd ``` ## 4.5 Subsystem Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Offboard["OFFBOARD SUBSYSTEM"] sA[Host Computer] sB[Power Supply Unit] end subgraph TetherLayer["TETHER MEDIUM"] sC[Hybrid Cable] end subgraph Onboard["ONBOARD SUBSYSTEM"] sD[Motor Drivers] sE[Sensors] sF[Controller MCU] end sA <-->|Ethernet| sC sB -->|DC Power| sC sC <-->|Power and Data| sF sF --> sD sF <--> sE ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define tethered communication in mobile robotics. Mention any two of its advantages over wireless communication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Tethered communication is a method of establishing a wired physical link between a mobile robot and a base station using a cable (tether), which carries power and/or data signals. - **(1 mark)** *Advantage 1:* It provides a **deterministic, noise-immune, high-bandwidth** communication channel with latency typically < 1 ms. - **(1 mark)** *Advantage 2:* It enables **continuous power delivery**, removing the runtime limitation imposed by on-board batteries. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **List any three cable management strategies used in tethered mobile robots.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **(1 mark)** **Slack tether management** — excess cable is laid behind the robot on the ground. 2. **(1 mark)** **Active winch/spool system** — a motor-driven reel maintains constant tension on the tether. 3. **(1 mark)** **Top-mounted boom or overhead roller** — the cable is suspended above the operating area to eliminate ground friction. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **tethered mobile robotic system**. Label the **off-board subsystem, tether, and on-board subsystem**, and indicate the direction of **power and data flow**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A tethered mobile robot operates from a **48 V DC** supply. The robot draws a steady current of **10 A**. The copper tether has a resistance of **0.02 Ω per conductor per km**. The on-board electronics require a **minimum of 42 V** to function correctly. **(i)** Determine the **maximum allowable tether length**. **(ii)** Compute the **power loss** in the tether at this maximum length. **(iii)** Comment on the **efficiency** of power transfer. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A ### (a) Architecture Explanation (7 marks) **Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Base Station 48V PSU] -->|Power| nodeB[Tether Interface Unit Slip Ring and Connectors] nodeC[Host Computer HMI] -->|Command Data| nodeB nodeB -->|Hybrid Cable| nodeD[Onboard Power Conditioner] nodeB -->|Ethernet UART| nodeE[Robot Main Controller] nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF[Motor Drivers and Actuators] nodeE --> nodeG[Sensors IMU Lidar] nodeF -.->|Encoder Feedback| nodeE nodeG -.->|Sensor Data| nodeE nodeE -.->|Telemetry to Base| nodeB ``` **Explanation (4 marks):** - **(1 mark)** The **off-board subsystem** consists of the host computer (HMI) and the power supply unit, which together generate the control commands and electrical power. - **(1 mark)** The **tether** is a hybrid composite cable containing power conductors (copper) and data pairs (twisted pair / fiber), mechanically reinforced with Kevlar braid and protected by a PUR jacket. - **(1 mark)** The **on-board subsystem** includes the tether interface, power conditioner, robot controller, motor drivers, and sensors. The power conditioner steps down 48 V to logic levels (e.g., 5 V, 3.3 V). - **(1 mark)** Communication is **bidirectional**: commands flow from base to robot, telemetry flows from robot to base. Power flow is **unidirectional** (base to robot). ### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $V_{supply} = 48$ V, $I = 10$ A, $R_c = 0.02$ Ω/km, $V_{min} = 42$ V. **(i) Maximum tether length (3 marks):** **Step 1 — Allowable voltage drop:** $$\Delta V_{max} = 48 - 42 = 6\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Convert resistance to per-meter:** $$R_c = \frac{0.02\ \Omega}{1000\ \text{m}} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \Omega/\text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Apply formula:** $$L_{max} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{2 \cdot I \cdot R_c} = \frac{6}{2 \times 10 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$L_{max} = \frac{6}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = \mathbf{15{,}000\ \text{m} = 15\ km}$$ **(ii) Power loss (2 marks):** $$R_{loop} = 2 \cdot R_c \cdot L = 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 15{,}000 = 0.6\ \Omega$$ $$P_{loss} = I^{2} \cdot R_{loop} = (10)^{2} \times 0.6 = \mathbf{60\ W}$$ **(iii) Efficiency comment (2 marks):** Input power at base: $P_{in} = 48 \times 10 = 480$ W. Received power at robot: $P_{robot} = 42 \times 10 = 420$ W. $$\eta = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 = \mathbf{87.5\%}$$ **Comment:** The system achieves **87.5% efficiency**, which is reasonable for a 15 km copper tether. For longer distances, the cable cross-section must be increased or the supply voltage raised to reduce current and hence I²R losses. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Alternative]** **(a)** Compare **tethered and wireless** off-board communication in mobile robots across **bandwidth, latency, range, EMI immunity, and power delivery**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a **slip ring**? Explain its role in a tethered robotic system with a **rotating base** (e.g., a pan-tilt surveillance robot). Differentiate between an **electrical slip ring** and a **fiber-optic rotary joint (FORJ)**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B ### (a) Tethered vs Wireless Comparison (7 marks) | Parameter | Tethered (Wired) | Wireless (RF/Optical) | Marks | |-----------|------------------|----------------------|-------| | **Bandwidth** | Very high (10 Mbps – 100 Gbps) | Limited (shared spectrum) | 1 | | **Latency** | Low and deterministic (< 1 ms) | Variable (10–100 ms) | 1 | | **Range** | Limited by cable length (≤ 10 km copper) | Practically unbounded with repeaters | 1 | | **EMI Immunity** | Low for copper, high for fiber | Moderate (susceptible to jamming) | 1 | | **Power Delivery** | Native (power over tether) | Not possible (data only) | 1 | | **Security** | High (physical access needed) | Low (interceptable) | 1 | | **Mobility** | Constrained by cable management | Full freedom | 1 | ### (b) Slip Ring Explanation (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** A **slip ring** is a rotary electromechanical device that allows the transmission of **power and/or electrical signals** from a stationary structure to a continuously rotating structure. It consists of a stationary set of **stator brushes** that ride on a rotating set of **conductive rings**. **Role in tethered robots (3 marks):** - In a pan-tilt surveillance robot, the **pan axis** rotates continuously (360°) to scan the environment. - The tether enters through the base and must pass through the rotating joint to reach the on-board electronics. - A **slip ring** maintains electrical continuity across the rotating interface **without twisting the cable**. - Without a slip ring, the tether would coil and eventually sever, halting the robot. **Electrical Slip Ring vs FORJ (2 marks):** | Feature | Electrical Slip Ring | Fiber-Optic Rotary Joint (FORJ) | |---------|----------------------|----------------------------------| | **Signal Type** | Electrical (copper) | Optical (light through fiber) | | **Mechanism** | Brushes on metal rings | Collimated lens across a gap | | **Contact** | Physical contact (wears out) | Non-contact (no wear) | | **Bandwidth** | Limited by copper | Very high (up to 100 Gbps) | | **EMI** | Susceptible | Immune | | **Lifespan** | Limited by brush wear | Very long (no contact) | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tethered Communication Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the voltage drop formula $\Delta V = 2 I R_c L$. This single error costs **2 marks** in a Part B numerical. Always remember current flows *out* and *back* through the tether. > 2. **Drawing the block diagram without arrow labels.** Examiners require distinct, labeled arrows for power and data. Use **solid red** for power and **dashed blue** for data. > 3. **Confusing bandwidth with range.** Bandwidth is in bits/second; range is in meters. Many students write "high range" instead of "high data rate" for fiber optics. > 4. **Skipping the unit conversions.** $R_c$ is often given in Ω/km but the formula needs Ω/m. Forgetting to divide by 1000 is a 1-mark error that cascades. > 5. **Omitting cable management discussion.** Even in pure numerical questions, a one-sentence note on **tether drag** or **slip rings** often earns a bonus mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Tethered communication** = wired link between robot and base carrying **power, data, or both**. - **Three architectural layers:** off-board subsystem, tether, on-board subsystem. - **Voltage drop formula:** $\Delta V = 2 I R_c L$ — always include the **factor of 2** for the return path. - **Maximum tether length for power:** $L_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{max}}{2 I R_c}$. - **Power loss** in a copper tether scales as $I^{2} R_{loop}$ — high current is the enemy. - **Fiber-optic tethers** are immune to EMI and have attenuation as low as **0.2 dB/km**. - **Slip rings** (or FORJs for fiber) are mandatory for continuously rotating tethered bases. - **Cable management strategies:** slack tether, active winch, overhead boom, buoyancy-modulated (subsea). - **Tether drag force:** $F_{drag} = \mu w L_{ground}$ — important for traction-limited mobile robots. - **Hybrid tethers** combine copper (for power) and fiber (for data) in a single reinforced jacket. - **Typical applications:** ROVs (subsea), bomb-disposal EOD robots, planetary rovers (early prototypes), tethered UAVs, medical endoscopic capsules, and industrial AGVs. - **Tethered vs wireless:** Tethered wins on **determinism, bandwidth, security, and power delivery**; wireless wins on **range and mobility**. - **High-voltage DC transmission** is the industry trick to push tether lengths beyond 10 km. - **Remember the unit:** $R_c$ in formulas must be in **Ω/m** unless the length is in **km**.

topics.title: Untethered
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Untethered

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Untethered Robot** (also termed an *Autonomous Mobile Robot* or *Wireless Mobile Platform*) is a class of mobile robotic systems that operate **without any physical umbilical connection** to a fixed infrastructure. This independence is achieved across three critical dimensions: **(i) Power Autonomy** (onboard energy storage such as batteries, fuel cells, or solar arrays), **(ii) Computational Autonomy** (onboard processors executing real-time control laws), and **(iii) Communication Autonomy** (wireless data links replacing tethered signal cables).

In sharp contrast, a *tethered robot* relies on an **umbilical cord** that simultaneously supplies electrical power, control signals, and often data telemetry from a host station. Untethered systems must therefore solve the **energy management problem**, the **localization problem**, and the **fault-tolerance problem** using only onboard resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — KTU PEBRT522):**
> The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on the **trade-off analysis** between onboard payload mass, mission endurance, degrees of freedom, and computational capability — collectively known as the *Untethered Design Trilemma*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Remote-Control Car vs. The Smartphone":**
> Imagine an old **remote-controlled toy car** (tethered metaphorically) — it is useless without the handheld controller continuously transmitting signals and a wire feeding power in some industrial variants. Now picture a **robotic vacuum cleaner** (e.g., a Roomba) — it has its own battery, its own brain (microcontroller), its own sensors, and it can even recharge itself. **The Roomba is untethered.** It is free to roam, decide, and survive independently within its operational envelope.

Another vivid analogy is comparing a **docked laptop** to a **smartwatch**: the laptop can be plugged in, the smartwatch must *carefully ration* its milliwatts across sensing, computing, and actuation. This is the essence of untethered robotics — **every joule, every byte, and every gram matters**.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Untethered Operation

| Pillar | Tethered Robot | Untethered Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Power** | Continuous supply via cable | Onboard battery / fuel cell / solar |
| **Computation** | Often offloaded to host PC | Onboard MCU / SoC / embedded GPU |
| **Communication** | Wired (CAN, USB, Ethernet) | Wireless (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, ZigBee) |
| **Mobility Range** | Limited by cable length | Practically unlimited (terrain-dependent) |
| **Failure Mode** | Cable break = hard stop | Graceful degradation + return-to-base |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics for Untethered Systems:**
> - **Specific Energy of Li-ion cell:** **~150–250 Wh/kg**
> - **Power density of onboard computer (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano):** **~7–15 W** under typical SLAM workload
> - **Wireless latency over Wi-Fi 6:** **~1–3 ms** (indoor), **~20–50 ms** (outdoor long-range)
> - **Mission endurance benchmark:** **30 minutes to 8 hours** for most field-grade UGVs
> - **Self-discharge rate of Li-Po:** **~2–5 % per month**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Untethered Robot

An untethered mobile robot is fundamentally a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** integrating mechanical, electrical, electronic, and computational subsystems. Its operational loop can be decomposed into the following structured stages:

### Stage 1 — Energy Acquisition & Regulation
- The onboard energy source (battery pack, fuel cell, or photovoltaic array) delivers a nominal voltage $V_{bat}$.
- A **DC-DC buck/boost converter** regulates this to the multiple voltage rails required by the system: typically **5 V** (sensors), **3.3 V** (logic), **12 V** (motors), and **19 V** (compute board).
- A **battery management system (BMS)** monitors State-of-Charge (SoC) and State-of-Health (SoH).

### Stage 2 — Sensing & Perception
- **Proprioceptive sensors** (wheel encoders, IMU, current sensors) report internal state.
- **Exteroceptive sensors** (LIDAR, depth cameras, ultrasonic arrays, GPS) report external state.
- Data is fused via onboard **EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** or **particle filter**.

### Stage 3 — Onboard Computation
- The **central processing unit** runs the **sense-plan-act** loop at a fixed control frequency $f_c$ (typically **20–100 Hz** for ground robots, **>200 Hz** for drones).
- Algorithms include SLAM, path planning (A*, D*), obstacle avoidance (VFH, DWA), and behavior arbitration.

### Stage 4 — Wireless Communication
- Bidirectional telemetry with operator: **telemetry uplink (status), command downlink (control)**.
- For fully autonomous missions, the wireless link may be optional (used only for monitoring).

### Stage 5 — Actuation
- DC motors / servo motors / stepper motors convert electrical energy to mechanical work.
- Motor drivers (H-bridge, ESC) modulate power via **PWM signals** from the controller.

## 2.2 The Energy Budget Equation (High-Yield KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation — Mission Endurance Estimation:**
> The operational lifetime $T_{mission}$ of an untethered robot is determined by the **Energy Budget Equation**:
>
> $$T_{mission} = \frac{E_{store}}{\bar{P}_{total}} = \frac{E_{store}}{P_{compute} + P_{sense} + P_{actuate} + P_{comm}}$$

Where:
- $E_{store}$ = total usable energy stored in the battery (Joules)
- $\bar{P}_{total}$ = average total power draw (Watts)
- The denominator is the **sum of all subsystem power consumptions**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Board Examination:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{store} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat} \cdot 3600$ | Stored energy from battery capacity | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $T_{mission} = \dfrac{E_{store}}{\bar{P}_{total}}$ | Mission endurance | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $P_{motor} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power of motor | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $\eta_{total} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{driver} \cdot \eta_{gear}$ | Total drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{required} = \dfrac{F_{drag} \cdot v}{\eta_{total}}$ | Power needed to overcome drag/friction | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $\Delta t_{latency} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | Wireless one-way latency (line-of-sight) | seconds (s) |
| 7 | $PL_{dB}(d) = PL_0 + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | Log-distance path loss model | dB |
