| 8 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \dfrac{1}{C_{bat}}\int_0^t I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | State of Charge (Coulomb counting) | % |
| 9 | $R_{max} = \dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{rx}}{(4\pi d/\lambda)^2 \cdot P_{rx,min}}$ | Friis transmission equation (max range) | meters (m) |
| 10 | $f_{c,min} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{\Delta s_{max}}$ | Minimum control loop frequency | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> In Formula 7, the parameter $n$ is the **path loss exponent** ($n = 2$ for free space, $n = 3$–$4$ for indoor with obstacles). In Formula 9, $\lambda = c/f$ is the carrier wavelength.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The untethered paradigm is foundational to:
- **Search and Rescue (SAR)** operations in collapsed structures where cables would snag.
- **Planetary Exploration** (NASA's Perseverance, ISRO's Chandrayaan rover) — no cables possible on alien terrain.
- **Precision Agriculture** — autonomous tractors and drones for crop monitoring.
- **Warehouse Automation** — Amazon Kiva robots transport shelves fully autonomously.
- **Defence & Surveillance** — reconnaissance UGVs and UAVs.

The *Why* of untethered design is therefore not merely convenience — it is a **mission-enabling capability** in scenarios where physical access is impossible, hazardous, or economically prohibitive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mission Endurance from Battery Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A differential-drive ground robot uses a **24 V, 10 Ah Li-ion battery pack**. Subsystem power measurements yield:
> - Onboard computer: $P_{compute} = 15$ W
> - Sensor suite (LIDAR + IMU + 2 cameras): $P_{sense} = 12$ W
> - Two drive motors (under typical load): $P_{actuate} = 40$ W
> - Wireless link (active): $P_{comm} = 3$ W
>
> **Find:** The mission endurance in minutes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the total stored energy $E_{store}$.**

Using Formula 1 from the cheat sheet:

$$E_{store} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat} \cdot 3600$$

Substituting $V_{bat} = 24$ V and $C_{bat} = 10$ Ah:

$$E_{store} = 24 \cdot 10 \cdot 3600$$

$$E_{store} = 864{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the average power draw across all subsystems.**

$$\bar{P}_{total} = P_{compute} + P_{sense} + P_{actuate} + P_{comm}$$

$$\bar{P}_{total} = 15 + 12 + 40 + 3$$

$$\bar{P}_{total} = 70 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Energy Budget Equation (Formula 2).**

$$T_{mission} = \frac{E_{store}}{\bar{P}_{total}}$$

$$T_{mission} = \frac{864{,}000}{70}$$

$$T_{mission} = 12{,}342.86 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to minutes for a board-friendly answer.**

$$T_{mission} = \frac{12{,}342.86}{60} \approx 205.7 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\boxed{T_{mission} \approx 3.43 \text{ hours}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Wireless Communication Range (Friis Equation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A ground robot transmits telemetry at **2.4 GHz** with a transmit power of $P_{tx} = 100$ mW. Both antennas have unity gain ($G_{tx} = G_{rx} = 1$). The receiver sensitivity is $P_{rx,min} = -90$ dBm. Determine the theoretical maximum line-of-sight range.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to linear SI units.**

$$P_{tx} = 100 \text{ mW} = 0.1 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{rx,min} = -90 \text{ dBm} = 10^{(-90-30)/10} = 10^{-12} \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the carrier wavelength.**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9}$$

$$\lambda = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the Friis equation (Formula 9) to solve for $R_{max}$.**

$$R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{rx}}{P_{rx,min}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$R_{max} = \frac{0.125}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{0.1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1}{10^{-12}}}$$

$$R_{max} = \frac{0.125}{12.566}\sqrt{10^{11}}$$

$$R_{max} = 0.00995 \cdot 316{,}227.77$$

$$R_{max} \approx 3145 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{R_{max} \approx 3.15 \text{ km (free-space, ideal conditions)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** In real environments, the path loss exponent $n$ rises above 2 (e.g., $n = 3.5$ for urban/foliage). The effective range can drop by an order of magnitude. Always mention the *free-space assumption* in your answer.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Control Loop Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A mobile robot moves at maximum translational velocity $v_{max} = 1.5$ m/s. Its safety specification requires that it must react before moving more than $\Delta s_{max} = 5$ cm between control updates. Find the minimum control loop frequency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Formula 10 directly.**

$$f_{c,min} = \frac{v_{max}}{\Delta s_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$f_{c,min} = \frac{1.5}{0.05}$$

$$f_{c,min} = 30 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{c,min} = 30 \text{ Hz}}$$

This means the controller must execute the **sense-plan-act** loop at least **30 times per second** to guarantee safety.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tethered vs. Untethered Robots

| Design Parameter | Tethered Robot | Untethered Robot | Trade-off Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Endurance** | Unlimited (grid power) | Bounded (battery capacity) | Mission time is the **first constraint** |
| **Compute Power** | High (offboard GPU) | Limited (thermal/power budget) | Algorithm choice must be **embedded-friendly** |
| **Payload Mass** | Higher (cable adds mass above) | Limited (mass ↑ → energy ↑) | Tighter **mass-power coupling** |
| **Mobility** | Constrained (cable snag risk) | Free (terrain-limited only) | Untethered enables **cluttered environments** |
| **Communication Latency** | Negligible (wired) | 1–50 ms wireless | Real-time control must be **onboard** |
| **Fault Recovery** | Often requires external reset | Must include **return-to-base** logic | Higher **autonomy requirement** |
| **Cost** | Lower (no expensive batteries) | Higher (Li-ion + BMS + comms) | Budget tradeoff in mass-market robotics |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Untethered Mobile Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR["POWER SUBSYSTEM"]
        batNode["Li-ion Battery Pack 24V 10Ah"]
        bmsNode["Battery Management System BMS"]
        dcdcNode["DC-DC Converters 5V 3.3V 12V 19V"]
    end

    subgraph SNS["SENSING SUBSYSTEM"]
        lidarNode["2D LIDAR"]
        imuNode["IMU 6-axis"]
        camNode["Stereo Camera"]
        encNode["Wheel Encoders"]
        gpsNode["GPS Module"]
    end

    subgraph CPU["COMPUTATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        mcuNode["Main SoC Embedded Computer"]
        eknode["EKF SLAM Estimator"]
        plannode["Path Planner A-star D-star"]
        ctrlNode["Motor Controller PWM"]
    end

    subgraph COM["COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        wifinode["Wi-Fi 6 Module"]
        blenode["BLE Telemetry"]
        gsmnode["4G LTE Optional"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        lmotNode["Left Drive Motor"]
        rmotNode["Right Drive Motor"]
        steerNode["Steering Servo Optional"]
    end

    batNode --> bmsNode --> dcdcNode
    dcdcNode --> mcuNode
    dcdcNode --> lidarNode
    dcdcNode --> imuNode
    dcdcNode --> camNode
    dcdcNode --> wifinode

    lidarNode --> mcuNode
    imuNode --> mcuNode
    camNode --> mcuNode
    encNode --> mcuNode
    gpsNode --> mcuNode

    mcuNode --> eknode --> plannode --> ctrlNode
    ctrlNode --> lmotNode
    ctrlNode --> rmotNode
    ctrlNode --> steerNode

    mcuNode --> wifinode
    mcuNode --> blenode
    mcuNode --> gsmnode
```

## 4.2 Sense–Plan–Act Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Mission Start]) --> sense["SENSE: Read LIDAR IMU Encoders GPS"]
    sense --> fuse["FUSE: EKF Localisation"]
    fuse --> mapnode["MAP: Occupancy Grid Update"]
    mapnode --> plan["PLAN: A-star Path Computation"]
    plan --> act["ACT: Generate Motor PWM Commands"]
    act --> check{"Battery SoC > 20 percent?"}
    check -- Yes --> sense
    check -- No --> rtb["Return to Base RTB Routine"]
    rtb --> dock([End Mission at Charging Dock])
```

## 4.3 Power Flow Diagram (Energy Budget Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    source["Energy Source 864000 J"] --> conv["DC-DC Loss ~8 percent"]
    conv --> branch1["Compute 15 W"]
    conv --> branch2["Sensors 12 W"]
    conv --> branch3["Motors 40 W"]
    conv --> branch4["Comms 3 W"]
    branch3 --> mech["Mechanical Work"]
    branch1 --> heat1["Heat Dissipation"]
    branch2 --> heat2["Heat Dissipation"]
    branch4 --> antenna["RF Radiation"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an untethered mobile robot. List any four challenges unique to untethered operation.**

**Model Answer:**
An untethered mobile robot is a robotic system that operates without any physical cable connection for power, control, or data transfer. It must carry its own energy source, onboard computer, and wireless communication module.

**Four unique challenges:**
1. **Limited energy budget** — mission time bounded by battery capacity.
2. **Onboard computation constraints** — algorithm must fit within thermal/power envelope.
3. **Wireless communication reliability** — latency, packet loss, range limits.
4. **Autonomous fault recovery** — no human can intervene mid-mission.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between tethered and untethered robots in terms of power supply and mobility.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tethered Robot | Untethered Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Supply** | External via cable | Onboard battery/fuel cell |
| **Mobility** | Restricted by cable length & snag risk | Unrestricted (terrain-limited) |
| **Endurance** | Practically infinite | Finite (battery-limited) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (energy storage cost) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In energy-budget problems, students frequently **forget to convert Ampere-hours to Joules** (missing the $\times 3600$ factor) or **mix units** (using mAh with V directly to get J). Always show unit conversions explicitly. Also, do not omit the **path loss exponent** discussion in wireless range problems — the examiner awards marks for environmental realism.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the major subsystems of an untethered mobile robot and explain the role of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An untethered differential-drive robot is powered by a **24 V, 20 Ah** Li-ion battery. The average power consumption of the compute unit is **18 W**, the sensor suite draws **10 W**, the two drive motors consume **55 W** combined, and the wireless link consumes **4 W**.
   (i) Compute the total stored energy in the battery pack.
   (ii) Estimate the mission endurance in hours.
   (iii) Suggest two design modifications to extend the mission time by at least 30%. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Subsystem Description (7 Marks):**

The major subsystems are:
1. **Power Subsystem** (battery, BMS, DC-DC converters) — provides regulated voltage rails.
2. **Sensing Subsystem** (LIDAR, IMU, cameras, encoders, GPS) — provides internal and external state.
3. **Computation Subsystem** (embedded SoC running SLAM, planning, control) — implements the sense-plan-act loop.
4. **Communication Subsystem** (Wi-Fi, BLE, 4G) — provides operator telemetry and optional command downlink.
5. **Actuation Subsystem** (motors, motor drivers, wheels) — converts electrical energy to motion.

*(Full credit is awarded for the block diagram and clear role descriptions.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Total stored energy:**

$$E_{store} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{bat} \cdot 3600 = 24 \cdot 20 \cdot 3600 = 1{,}728{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Total average power:**

$$\bar{P}_{total} = 18 + 10 + 55 + 4 = 87 \text{ W}$$

**Mission endurance:**

$$T_{mission} = \frac{1{,}728{,}000}{87} \approx 19{,}862 \text{ s} \approx 5.52 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Two design modifications to extend endurance by ≥ 30%:**

1. **Replace the compute unit with a low-power variant** (e.g., switch from 18 W to 8 W by using a Jetson Orin Nano in 15 W mode) — saves 10 W, reducing total to 77 W → endurance jumps to 6.23 hours (**+13%**).
2. **Add a photovoltaic top deck** to trickle-charge during outdoor missions, supplementing the battery — provides 5–10 W continuous solar input, which can extend effective endurance by **20–40%** depending on insolation.

Combined, these modifications can easily achieve a **30%+ endurance gain**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Energy Budget Equation** for an untethered robot. Why is this equation central to the design philosophy of mobile robotics? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A ground robot transmits at **5.8 GHz** with a transmit power of **200 mW**, transmit antenna gain of **2 dBi**, receiver antenna gain of **2 dBi**, and a receiver sensitivity of **−92 dBm**. Assuming free-space propagation, calculate the theoretical maximum line-of-sight communication range. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Energy Budget Equation (7 Marks):**

The energy budget equation is:

$$T_{mission} = \frac{E_{store}}{P_{compute} + P_{sense} + P_{actuate} + P_{comm}}$$

**Why it is central to design philosophy:**
- It **couples** the electrical (battery), mechanical (motor load), computational (algorithm complexity), and communication subsystems into a single design constraint.
- It forces the engineer to make **informed trade-offs**: e.g., choosing a lighter LIDAR (lower $P_{sense}$) at the cost of a smaller field-of-view, or a lower-power MCU (lower $P_{compute}$) at the cost of slower SLAM updates.
- It defines the **mission feasibility boundary** — a robot whose $T_{mission} < T_{required}$ simply cannot complete the task. Hence every design decision is ultimately evaluated against this equation.

**Part (b) — Friis Range Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert gains to linear scale.**

$$G_{tx} = 2 \text{ dBi} = 10^{0.2} = 1.585$$

$$G_{rx} = 2 \text{ dBi} = 1.585$$

**Step 2 — Convert power and sensitivity to linear scale.**

$$P_{tx} = 200 \text{ mW} = 0.2 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{rx,min} = -92 \text{ dBm} = 10^{(-92-30)/10} = 10^{-12.2} = 6.31 \times 10^{-13} \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute wavelength.**

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.8 \times 10^9} = 0.0517 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange and substitute into Friis equation.**

$$R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{rx}}{P_{rx,min}}}$$

$$R_{max} = \frac{0.0517}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{0.2 \cdot 1.585 \cdot 1.585}{6.31 \times 10^{-13}}}$$

$$R_{max} = 0.00412 \cdot \sqrt{7.98 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$R_{max} = 0.00412 \cdot 893{,}228$$

$$\boxed{R_{max} \approx 3{,}678 \text{ m} \approx 3.68 \text{ km}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** This is the free-space theoretical maximum. In a cluttered outdoor or indoor environment with path loss exponent $n \approx 3.5$, the practical range will reduce to roughly **500 m – 1 km**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Untethered Robots**

- ✅ An **untethered robot** is one without any physical cable for power, control, or data — it must be **power-autonomous, compute-autonomous, and comm-autonomous**.
- ✅ The **three pillars** are: onboard energy, onboard processing, wireless communication.
- ✅ The **Energy Budget Equation** $T_{mission} = E_{store}/\bar{P}_{total}$ is the **single most important formula** for KTU 2024 board questions.
- ✅ Always remember the conversion $E_{store} = V \cdot C \cdot 3600$ to obtain Joules from V and Ah.
- ✅ The **Friis transmission equation** governs wireless range; path loss exponent $n$ determines real-world degradation.
- ✅ **Minimum control loop frequency** is determined by $f_{c,min} = v_{max}/\Delta s_{max}$ — a critical real-time constraint.
- ✅ The **Untethered Design Trilemma**: *endurance vs. payload vs. computational capability* — improving one degrades the others.
- ✅ Common pitfalls: unit-mixing errors, forgetting $\times 3600$ conversion, ignoring path loss exponent.
- ✅ Standard onboard voltages: 5 V (sensors), 3.3 V (logic), 12 V (motors), 19–24 V (compute boards).
- ✅ State-of-Charge (SoC) tracking via **Coulomb counting** is essential for return-to-base autonomy.
- ✅ Wireless protocols used: Wi-Fi 6 (high bandwidth, short range), BLE (low power, telemetry), 4G/5G (long range), LoRa (ultra-long range, low data rate).
- ✅ Real-world examples: Mars rovers, drone swarms, robotic vacuum cleaners, autonomous warehouse robots, agricultural UGVs.
- ✅ Energy density of modern Li-ion cells: **150–250 Wh/kg** — the limiting factor for mission duration.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radio Modems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Radio Modems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Radio Modem** (Radio Modulator-Demodulator) is a wireless transceiver device that converts digital data streams into radio frequency (RF) signals for over-the-air transmission, and conversely, demodulates received RF signals back into digital data. In mobile robotics, radio modems serve as the **wireless communication backbone** that enables teleoperation, multi-robot coordination, telemetry exchange, and remote sensor data acquisition without physical cabling. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Radio modems belong to the broader category of *wireless communication subsystems* in mobile robots. They are explicitly categorized under **unlicensed ISM band devices** (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) such as **433 MHz**, **868 MHz**, **915 MHz**, and **2.4 GHz**, which are the predominant operating frequencies for low-cost mobile platforms. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Tether" Imagine a robotic dog that you wish to control from **500 meters** away using a joystick. A physical wire would be impractical — the dog would get tangled. A **Radio Modem** is essentially the engineering solution to this problem: - The **transmitting modem** sits on your joystick controller. It takes the digital command (e.g., *"move forward 2 meters"*) and **superimposes** it onto a high-frequency carrier wave (like a person shouting across a noisy room by using a megaphone at a specific pitch). - The **receiving modem** sits on the robot. It **filters out** the carrier wave and extracts the original digital command. - The system behaves like an **"invisible wire"** (a wireless serial cable) where both ends speak the same digital protocol (UART, RS-232, RS-485). ### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Range | Units | Significance | |-----------|---------------|-------|--------------| | Operating Frequency | 433 / 868 / 915 / 2400 | MHz | Regulatory and propagation behavior | | Transmit Power | 10 to 1000 (mW); 10 to 30 (dBm) | mW / dBm | Governs legal range and battery drain | | Receiver Sensitivity | -110 to -80 | dBm | Minimum detectable signal strength | | Data Rate (Air) | 1.2 to 250 | kbps | Throughput limit | | Range (Line-of-Sight) | 100 m to 50+ km | m / km | Application envelope | | Interface | UART / RS-232 / RS-485 / SPI | — | Host integration | ### 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU 2024 > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Modulation**: The process of varying one or more properties of a periodic waveform (the *carrier signal*) with a modulating signal that contains information to be transmitted. > - **Transceiver**: A combined transmitter and receiver in a single package. > - **Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex**: Half-duplex allows transmission in one direction at a time; full-duplex allows simultaneous bidirectional communication. > - **ISM Band**: Industrial, Scientific, and Medical radio bands reserved internationally for low-power unlicensed use. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Radio Modem A radio modem internally consists of two parallel chains sharing a single antenna through a **duplexer** (for full-duplex) or **T/R switch** (for half-duplex). **A. Transmit (TX) Chain — Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. **Host Interface Reception**: Digital data arrives via UART / RS-232 / SPI from the robot's microcontroller. 2. **Baseband Processing**: Data is encoded, error-corrected (FEC — Forward Error Correction), and framed (preamble, sync word, payload, CRC). 3. **Modulation**: The baseband digital signal modulates an RF carrier. Common schemes: - **FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying) — robust, low data rate. - **GFSK** (Gaussian FSK) — used in Bluetooth Low Energy. - **LoRa CSS** (Chirp Spread Spectrum) — long range, low power. - **OOK / ASK** (On-Off Keying) — simplest, used in 433 MHz toys. 4. **Power Amplification (PA)**: The modulated RF signal is amplified to the desired transmit power (e.g., **+20 dBm**). 5. **Antenna Radiation**: Signal radiates through the antenna at the carrier frequency $f_c$. **B. Receive (RX) Chain — Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. **Antenna Capture**: Weak RF signal (typically **-90 to -110 dBm**) is induced in the antenna. 2. **Low-Noise Amplification (LNA)**: Signal is amplified with minimal added noise. 3. **Demodulation**: The modulated carrier is mixed down to baseband, extracting the original digital bitstream. 4. **Synchronization & Decoding**: Bit clock is recovered, sync word detected, FEC decoded. 5. **Host Delivery**: Clean digital data exits via the host interface. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas use SI units unless noted. Distance $d$ in meters, frequency $f$ in Hz, power in W or dBm.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units | |---|--------------|----------|-----------|-------| | 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | m | | 2 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | dB | | 3 | Friis Transmission Equation | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $P$ in W | linear | | 4 | Received Power in dB | $P_r[\text{dBm}] = P_t[\text{dBm}] + G_t[\text{dBi}] + G_r[\text{dBi}] - FSPL[\text{dB}]$ | — | dBm | | 5 | Link Margin | $LM = P_r - S_{\min}$ | $S_{\min}$ = sensitivity | dB | | 6 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = SNR | bits/s | | 7 | Path Loss Exponent Model | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $n$ = 2 (free space), 3–4 (urban) | dB | | 8 | dBm to Watts | $P[\text{W}] = 10^{\,P[\text{dBm}]/10} \times 10^{-3}$ | — | W | | 9 | Isotropic Radiated Power | $EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_c$ | $L_c$ = cable loss | dBm | ### 2.3 Engineering Utility — The "Why" - **Teleoperated Search & Rescue Robots**: Radio modems transmit live video and sensor telemetry through rubble where Wi-Fi fails. - **Swarm Robotics**: Hundreds of small robots coordinate via low-power radio (e.g., **Zigbee / XBee at 2.4 GHz**). - **Agricultural Autonomous Vehicles**: Long-range modems (LoRa at **868/915 MHz**) maintain control over multi-kilometer farm fields. - **Underwater ROVs**: Special *acoustic modems* substitute radio (since RF attenuates in water), but the architectural principle remains identical. > [!NOTE] > The **Friis Equation** is the single most-tested formula in KTU wireless assessments. Master it before any other. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) Equation We start from the **Friis Transmission Equation** in linear scale: $$ P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 1 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$ into the squared term:** $$ P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\frac{c}{4\pi f d}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Express the propagation loss factor in decibels by taking $10\log_{10}$:** $$ \text{Path Loss (dB)} = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{c}{4\pi f d}\right)^{2}\right] $$ **Step 3 — Distribute the logarithm:** $$ PL = -10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{4\pi f d}\right) = -20\log_{10}(c) + 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(d) $$ **Step 4 — Insert the physical constants and evaluate the constant terms numerically:** - $20\log_{10}(c) = 20\log_{10}(3 \times 10^{8}) = 169.54$ dB (when $f$ in Hz, $d$ in m) - $20\log_{10}(4\pi) = 20 \times \log_{10}(12.566) = 21.98$ dB **Step 5 — Combine the constants:** $$ PL = (169.54 + 21.98) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(d) $$ $$ PL = 191.52 + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(d) $$ **Step 6 — Convert units to the *engineer's* form ($f$ in MHz, $d$ in km):** - $20\log_{10}(f[\text{MHz}]) = 20\log_{10}(f[\text{Hz}]/10^{6}) = 20\log_{10}(f[\text{Hz}]) - 120$ - $20\log_{10}(d[\text{km}]) = 20\log_{10}(d[\text{m}]/10^{3}) = 20\log_{10}(d[\text{m}]) - 60$ Substituting back: $$ PL = 191.52 + \big[20\log_{10}(f[\text{Hz}]) - 120\big] + \big[20\log_{10}(d[\text{m}]) - 60\big] $$ $$ PL = 11.52 + 20\log_{10}(f[\text{Hz}]) + 20\log_{10}(d[\text{m}]) $$ Applying the same conversion in reverse: $$ \boxed{FSPL\,[\text{dB}] = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d[\text{km}]) + 20\log_{10}(f[\text{MHz}])} $$ This is the **canonical FSPL equation** expected in KTU numerical problems. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Link Budget Calculation **Problem:** A mobile robot uses a radio modem operating at **2.4 GHz** with transmit power **20 dBm**, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and receiver sensitivity $-100$ dBm. Find the maximum operating range assuming free-space propagation and a minimum link margin of 10 dB. **Step 1 — Compute link margin requirement:** $$ LM_{\text{req}} = S_{\min} + 10 = -100 + 10 = -90 \text{ dBm (required } P_r\text{)} $$ Wait — corrected: link margin is $LM = P_r - S_{\min}$. Required $P_r = S_{\min} + LM = -100 + 10 = -90$ dBm. **Step 2 — Solve for maximum allowed FSPL:** $$ FSPL_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_r $$ $$ FSPL_{\max} = 20 + 2 + 2 - (-90) = 114 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3 — Invert the FSPL formula for distance:** $$ FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(d) = FSPL - 32.44 - 20\log_{10}(f) $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $f = 2400$ MHz:** $$ 20\log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.380 = 67.60 $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(d) = 114 - 32.44 - 67.60 = 13.96 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \log_{10}(d) = 0.698 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d = 10^{0.698} \approx 4.99 \text{ km} $$ $$ \boxed{d_{\max} \approx 5 \text{ km}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Shannon Channel Capacity Shannon's theorem states the theoretical maximum error-free data rate over a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$: $$ C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/second]} $$ This sets the **upper bound** on the throughput of any radio modem regardless of modulation scheme. **Example:** If a 2.4 GHz modem uses a 20 MHz channel with SNR = 30 dB (i.e., $S/N = 1000$): $$ C = 20 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}(1001) = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 9.97 \approx 199 \text{ Mbps} $$ > [!NOTE] > Real modems never reach this limit because Shannon's bound is theoretical; practical systems achieve 50–80% of $C$ through OFDM and adaptive coding. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Radio Modem The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete internal signal path of a half-duplex radio modem (the architecture used in modules like **XBee, HC-12, and LoRa Ra-02**). ```mermaid flowchart LR host1["HOST MCU"] --> iface1["UART / RS-232 / SPI INTERFACE"] iface1 --> bbproc["BASEBAND PROCESSOR
Framing, FEC, CRC"] bbproc --> mod1["MODULATOR
FSK or LoRa CSS"] mod1 --> pa["POWER AMPLIFIER
PA"] pa --> trsw["T/R SWITCH"] trsw --> ant["ANTENNA
433 / 868 / 2.4 GHz"] ant --> trsw2["T/R SWITCH"] trsw2 --> lna["LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER
LNA"] lna --> dem["DEMODULATOR
FSK or LoRa CSS"] dem --> bbdec["BASEBAND DECODER
Sync, FEC, CRC"] bbdec --> iface2["UART / RS-232 / SPI INTERFACE"] iface2 --> host2["HOST MCU"] style iface1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style bbproc fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style mod1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style pa fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style lna fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style dem fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style bbdec fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style iface2 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **yellow blocks** represent the host-facing digital interfaces. - The **blue blocks** perform digital signal processing (framing, error correction). - The **green blocks** are the modulation/demodulation stages operating at baseband. - The **red blocks** are the analog RF front-end (PA on transmit, LNA on receive). - The **T/R switch** is a single-pole double-throw (SPDT) RF switch that toggles the antenna between TX and RX paths in half-duplex. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — Communication Between Two Robots ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ctrl["REMOTE CONTROL STATION"] ctrlMCU["CONTROLLER MCU
Joystick + Display"] ctrlMod["RADIO MODEM TX
Modulates Commands"] ctrlAnt["ANTENNA
Yagi / Whip"] end subgraph robot["MOBILE ROBOT"] robAnt["ANTENNA
Whip / Patch"] robMod["RADIO MODEM RX
Demodulates Commands"] robMCU["ROBOT MCU
Motor Drivers"] end ctrlMCU --> ctrlMod --> ctrlAnt ctrlAnt -. "Wireless RF Link
2.4 GHz / 868 MHz" .-> robAnt robAnt --> robMod --> robMCU robMCU -. "Telemetry Feedback" .-> robMod robMod -. "Reverse RF Link" .-> ctrlMod style ctrlMod fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style robMod fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` This bidirectional link demonstrates how two modems (one at each end) form a **transparent serial cable replacement**, with both forward (command) and reverse (telemetry) traffic sharing the same RF channel via time-division duplexing. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a radio modem. List any two advantages and one limitation of using radio modems in mobile robotic applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A radio modem is a wireless transceiver that **modulates** digital data onto an RF carrier for transmission and **demodulates** received RF signals back into digital data, providing a wireless replacement for serial communication cables. **Advantages:** 1. Eliminates tethering — enables untethered teleoperation and free-roaming navigation. 2. Supports bidirectional telemetry between the robot and remote station over ranges from a few meters to several kilometers. **Limitation:** 1. Susceptible to interference, multipath fading, and regulatory power restrictions (e.g., FCC/ETSI limits on ISM bands) which cap range and data rate. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two advantages, 1 mark for limitation)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between half-duplex and full-duplex communication in radio modem systems. Give one example modem type for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex | |---------|-------------|-------------| | Direction | One direction at a time | Both directions simultaneously | | Hardware | Single antenna + T/R switch | Dual antenna or duplexer | | Example | **HC-12 (433 MHz)** | **XBee Pro S2C (Zigbee)** | *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for hardware difference, 1 mark for examples)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]** **Q. (a)** Explain the internal block diagram of a typical radio modem, describing the function of each block in the transmit and receive chains. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A radio modem operates at **915 MHz** with a transmit power of **+25 dBm**, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 3$ dBi, and a minimum receiver sensitivity of **−105 dBm**. Assuming free-space propagation and a required link margin of **15 dB**, compute the maximum line-of-sight range. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution **(a) Block Diagram Explanation [7 marks]** A radio modem consists of two parallel signal-processing chains — **Transmit (TX)** and **Receive (RX)** — sharing a common antenna through a T/R switch (half-duplex) or duplexer (full-duplex). **Transmit Chain (3.5 marks):** 1. **Host Interface (UART/RS-232/SPI)** — receives digital data from the robot's microcontroller at standard baud rates (e.g., 9600, 115200 bps). 2. **Baseband Processor** — frames the data into packets containing *preamble*, *sync word*, *payload*, and *CRC* checksum; applies Forward Error Correction (FEC). 3. **Modulator** — modulates the carrier frequency (915 MHz) using **FSK, GFSK, or LoRa CSS** based on the chosen modem chipset. 4. **Power Amplifier (PA)** — boosts the modulated RF signal to the configured transmit power (e.g., +25 dBm ≈ 316 mW). 5. **T/R Switch** — connects the PA output to the antenna during TX. 6. **Antenna** — radiates the RF energy into free space. **Receive Chain (3.5 marks):** 1. **Antenna** — captures extremely weak RF signals (typically −100 to −110 dBm). 2. **T/R Switch** — routes incoming RF to the LNA during RX. 3. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** — amplifies the signal by ~15–20 dB while adding minimal noise (NF ≈ 2–4 dB). 4. **Demodulator** — extracts the original baseband bitstream by mixing the incoming RF down to baseband. 5. **Baseband Decoder** — performs sync-word detection, FEC decoding, and CRC verification. 6. **Host Interface** — delivers clean digital data back to the microcontroller. --- **(b) Numerical Problem [7 marks]** **Given:** - Frequency $f = 915$ MHz - Transmit power $P_t = +25$ dBm - Antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 3$ dBi - Receiver sensitivity $S_{\min} = -105$ dBm - Required link margin $LM = 15$ dB **Step 1: Compute the minimum required received power.** $$ P_r^{\text{req}} = S_{\min} + LM = -105 + 15 = -90 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 2: Compute maximum allowable path loss.** $$ FSPL_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_r^{\text{req}} $$ $$ FSPL_{\max} = 25 + 3 + 3 - (-90) = 121 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3: Apply the FSPL equation and solve for $d$.** $$ FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d[\text{km}]) + 20\log_{10}(f[\text{MHz}]) $$ $$ 121 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(915) $$ **Step 4: Evaluate $20\log_{10}(915)$.** $$ \log_{10}(915) = 2.961 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 20\log_{10}(915) = 59.22 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 5: Solve for distance.** $$ 20\log_{10}(d) = 121 - 32.44 - 59.22 = 29.34 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \log_{10}(d) = 1.467 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d = 10^{1.467} \approx 29.31 \text{ km} $$ $$ \boxed{d_{\max} \approx 29.3 \text{ km}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks):** Students often forget to convert $f$ to MHz and $d$ to km when using the constant **32.44**. Always show unit conversions explicitly. > 2. **Sign error on $P_r$ (–2 marks):** Subtracting a negative dBm value — write it as $25 + 3 + 3 + 90 = 121$ to avoid confusion. > 3. **Forgetting link margin (–1 mark):** The link margin of 15 dB is *in addition* to sensitivity. Total $P_r$ threshold = $-105 + 15 = -90$ dBm. > 4. **Skipping unit declaration (–1 mark):** Always state "d is in km" before substituting. --- ### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks]** **Q. (a)** Compare the major modulation schemes used in radio modems — **OOK, FSK, GFSK, and LoRa CSS** — in terms of data rate, range, power consumption, and typical applications. **[7 marks]** **(b)** With reference to **Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem**, derive the maximum theoretical data rate achievable by a radio modem operating in a 250 kHz bandwidth channel with an SNR of **20 dB**. Comment on why real-world modem throughput falls below this theoretical value. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison of Modulation Schemes [7 marks]** | Modulation | Data Rate | Range | Power Consumption | Typical Application | |------------|-----------|-------|-------------------|---------------------| | **OOK** (On-Off Keying) | 1–10 kbps | Short (10–100 m) | Very low | Simple 433 MHz remote controls, tire-pressure sensors | | **FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying) | 10–50 kbps | Medium (100 m–2 km) | Low | HC-12 modules, industrial telemetry | | **GFSK** (Gaussian FSK) | 50–250 kbps | Medium (100 m–1 km) | Low | Bluetooth LE, Nordic nRF24L01 | | **LoRa CSS** (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | 0.3–50 kbps | Long (2–15 km) | Very low | LoRaWAN IoT, agricultural robots | **Key Conceptual Points (any 4 for full 7 marks):** 1. **OOK** is the simplest — the carrier is either ON or OFF — making it the cheapest but most noise-sensitive. 2. **FSK** uses two distinct frequencies (mark/space) for binary 1 and 0, offering better noise immunity than OOK. 3. **GFSK** applies a Gaussian filter to the FSK signal, reducing spectral sidelobes and improving bandwidth efficiency — the basis of Bluetooth. 4. **LoRa CSS** uses chirps whose frequency linearly increases or decreases across the entire channel bandwidth; this *spreads* the signal energy, allowing reception at SNR values **below −20 dB** — hence its extreme range. --- **(b) Shannon Capacity Derivation [7 marks]** **Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem** states that for a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$, the maximum theoretical error-free data rate $C$ is: $$ C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/second]} $$ **Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.** Given SNR = 20 dB: $$ \frac{S}{N} = 10^{20/10} = 10^{2} = 100 $$ **Step 2: Substitute into Shannon's equation with $B = 250$ kHz = $2.5 \times 10^{5}$ Hz.** $$ C = (2.5 \times 10^{5}) \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 100) $$ **Step 3: Evaluate the logarithm.** $$ \log_{2}(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658 \text{ bits/symbol} $$ **Step 4: Multiply by bandwidth.** $$ C = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \times 6.658 \approx 1.665 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s} $$ $$ \boxed{C \approx 1.665 \text{ Mbps}} $$ **Commentary on real-world throughput (3 marks):** 1. **Modulation limit:** Shannon's bound assumes *ideal* Gaussian signaling; practical modems use M-ary FSK/QAM with finite constellations, achieving 50–80% of $C$. 2. **Channel impairments:** Multipath fading, interference, and non-AWGN noise reduce usable SNR, forcing lower-order modulations. 3. **Protocol overhead:** Preamble, sync word, CRC, FEC parity bits, and retransmission (ARQ) consume channel capacity. 4. **Regulatory duty cycle limits:** In ISM bands (e.g., 868 MHz in EU), regulations enforce ≤ 1% duty cycle, throttling average throughput. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. Forgetting the "+1" inside the log — only $(1 + S/N)$, not $S/N$ alone (loss of 2 marks). > 2. Using bandwidth in kHz directly without converting to Hz before multiplication (loss of 1 mark). > 3. Mixing up $B$ (Hz) and $S/N$ — make a clear table of given values (loss of 1 mark). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Radio Modems** - **Definition:** Radio modem = RF transceiver that converts digital data ↔ radio signals. - **Operating Frequencies:** 433 / 868 / 915 / 2400 MHz — all in **ISM unlicensed** bands. - **Three Core Functional Blocks:** (1) Baseband processing (framing, FEC, CRC), (2) Modulator/Demodulator, (3) RF Front-End (PA + LNA + Antenna). - **Key Formula #1:** $\lambda = c/f$ — wavelength links physical antenna size to frequency. - **Key Formula #2 (THE KTU Favorite):** $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d[\text{km}]) + 20\log_{10}(f[\text{MHz}])$. - **Key Formula #3:** Friis equation — $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$ (all in dB). - **Key Formula #4:** Link Margin $LM = P_r - S_{\min}$ must be ≥ 10 dB for reliable link. - **Key Formula #5:** Shannon Capacity $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ — the *theoretical ceiling*. - **Modulation Hierarchy (range vs rate):** OOK < FSK < GFSK < LoRa CSS (longest range, lowest rate). - **Half-Duplex:** One direction at a time, single antenna with T/R switch (HC-12). - **Full-Duplex:** Both directions simultaneously, requires duplexer or two antennas (XBee Pro). - **Application Areas:** Teleoperation, swarm coordination, agricultural robots, search & rescue, IoT telemetry. - **Pitfall to Avoid:** Always state units (km vs m, MHz vs Hz) explicitly — silent unit errors account for **~30% of lost marks** in KTU wireless problems.

topics.title: Basic Concepts - Sensors: Bumpers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts - Sensors: Bumpers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mobile Robotics**, a **Bumper** is defined as a *contact-type (tactile) proprioceptive/exteroceptive sensor* that provides a **binary (Boolean) signal** indicating whether the robot's chassis has made physical contact with an external object in its immediate environment. Bumpers belong to the most fundamental layer of the robot's perception hierarchy and serve as the **last line of defense** in obstacle avoidance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** Bumpers are classified under *"Contact Sensors"* or *"Binary Tactile Sensors"* in the KTU PEBRT522 Module 2 syllabus. They convert mechanical displacement into an electrical switching action.

**Standard Operating Metric:** The response is typically reported as a logical `HIGH` (contact detected) or `LOW` (no contact) — a **1-bit digital measurement** with units of **Boolean (True/False)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **blindfolded person walking with two canes**. The canes touch obstacles in front, and the person instantly knows, *"Yes, I hit something."* A bumper sensor does exactly the same thing for a mobile robot:

- The **bumper plate** = the cane's tip
- The **micro-switch** = the nerves in the person's hand
- The **controller** = the brain reacting to stop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Bumpers do *not* measure distance, do *not* measure speed, and do *not* see the obstacle beforehand. They *only* confirm that a collision has **already occurred**. They are the robot's equivalent of a **fingertip touch sensor**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in bumper sensor design:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Contact Force Threshold | $F_{th}$ | **0.1 N to 2 N** (light bumpers) |
| Switch Bounce Time | $t_b$ | **1 ms to 10 ms** |
| Response Latency | $\tau$ | **< 5 ms** |
| Output Voltage (Logic HIGH) | $V_{OH}$ | **+3.3 V or +5 V** (TTL/CMOS) |
| Output Voltage (Logic LOW) | $V_{OL}$ | **0 V (GND)** |
| Mechanical Travel | $x$ | **0.5 mm to 3 mm** |

---

## 1.4 Classification in the Robot Sensor Family

Bumpers occupy a unique position in the robot's sensor taxonomy:

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensor Hierarchy Place:** Bumpers are at the **lowest level** of robot sensing — they are triggered *only after* other sensors (ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR) ideally should have prevented the collision. They are mandatory **safety-grade sensors** in industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) and service robots.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

The operational sequence of a mechanical bumper sensor follows a strict **five-stage causal chain**:

1. **Mechanical Displacement** — When the robot's body collides with an obstacle, the bumper plate (mounted on a compliant suspension) is pushed inward by a force $F_c$.
2. **Force Transmission** — The displacement $x$ is transferred to a lever or plunger connected to an electromechanical switch (typically a **micro-switch / SPDT reed switch**).
3. **Electrical Switching** — Once the displacement exceeds the mechanical travel threshold $x_{th}$, the switch contacts close (or open), producing an abrupt change in electrical state.
4. **Signal Conditioning** — The raw signal is passed through a **debouncing filter** (RC low-pass or software delay) to eliminate multiple spurious transitions.
5. **Controller Interpretation** — The microcontroller's GPIO pin reads the conditioned signal as a digital `0` or `1` and triggers an **emergency stop**, *reverse maneuver*, or *turn-away* behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bumpers Cannot Replace Range Sensors:** Because bumpers only respond *after* contact, they offer **zero predictive capability**. KTU exam answers must explicitly contrast this with ultrasonic/IR sensors that provide range $R$ *before* collision.

---

## 2.2 Types of Bumper Sensors (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Working Principle | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Micro-switch)** | Physical contact closes metal contacts | Cheap, robust, simple | Mechanical wear, bounce |
| **Pneumatic** | Air-pressure change in a sealed bladder | Sensitive, no arcing | Requires pump, slow response |
| **Capacitive** | Change in capacitance due to proximity | No mechanical wear | False triggers near humans |
| **Optical / Infrared** | Light beam interruption | No mechanical contact | Affected by dust, ambient light |
| **Whisker / Hall-Effect** | Magnet attached to a flexible whisker | Smooth analog response | Calibration needed |
| **Conductive Polymer / Quantum Tunneling** | Resistance change under pressure | High sensitivity | Expensive, niche |

> [!NOTE]
> The **most commonly tested** type in KTU exams is the **Mechanical Micro-switch Bumper**, followed by **Pneumatic** and **Optical** bumpers.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_c = m \cdot a$ | Contact force from robot mass × deceleration | N (Newton) | Impact force estimation |
| $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy absorbed on collision | J (Joule) | Bumper energy rating |
| $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/RC})$ | RC debounce capacitor charging | Volts | Debounce circuit design |
| $t_b \approx 3 RC$ | Debounce time constant (99% settled) | seconds | Filter design |
| $f_{sample} > 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz | Avoiding aliasing of bounce |
| $\tau = R C$ | Time constant of RC filter | seconds | Bumper signal smoothing |
| $x(t) = x_0 \, e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \cos(\omega_d t)$ | Damped oscillation of bumper plate | metres | Bounce analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The vertical bar symbol `|` is **banned** in markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bumpers are deployed in:

- **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in warehouses — mandatory by **ISO 3691-4** safety standard.
- **Domestic vacuum robots** (e.g., Roomba legacy models) — front half-ring bumper.
- **Autonomous wheelchairs** — safety stop on contact with a person.
- **Mars rovers (NASA)** — heritage design of low-cost contact triggers.
- **Industrial robotic arms with mobile bases** — anti-crash safety layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a layered robot architecture, bumpers sit at **Level 0** (Safety Layer) below planning, perception, and control. Failure of the bumper layer constitutes a **safety-critical fault**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Contact Force Threshold of a Bumper Plate

**Problem Setup:** A mobile robot of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ collides with a rigid wall. The bumper plate has a spring constant $k$ and compresses by displacement $x$ before the micro-switch triggers. Derive the relationship between velocity, mass, and trigger displacement.

### Step 1 — Write the Energy Balance

The kinetic energy of the robot is fully absorbed by the spring action of the bumper plate.

$$
E_k = U_{spring}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Standard Expressions

The translational kinetic energy of the robot is given by:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

The elastic potential energy stored in the bumper spring at compression $x$ is:

$$
U_{spring} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2
$$

### Step 3 — Equate and Solve for $x$

$$
\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} k x^2
$$

Cancel the common factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ from both sides of the equation.

$$
m v^2 = k x^2
$$

Divide both sides by $k$ to isolate $x^2$.

$$
x^2 = \frac{m v^2}{k}
$$

Take the positive square root (since displacement is a magnitude).

$$
x = v \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}
$$

### Step 4 — Trigger Condition

The micro-switch fires when $x \geq x_{th}$. Substituting the trigger condition:

$$
v \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \geq x_{th}
$$

Solving for the **minimum velocity** $v_{min}$ that triggers the bumper:

$$
v_{min} = x_{th} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}
$$

### Interpretation

A **stiffer** bumper (large $k$) requires a **higher** velocity to trigger — meaning it is *less sensitive*. A **lighter** robot (small $m$) triggers the bumper at *lower* velocities. This is the **fundamental design trade-off** in bumper engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RC Debounce Time Constant

**Problem Setup:** A mechanical micro-switch has a worst-case bounce duration of $t_b = 5$ ms. Design an RC low-pass filter that suppresses this bounce by treating the bounce as a high-frequency oscillation to be attenuated.

### Step 1 — Define the Cutoff Frequency

The filter must attenuate the bounce signal. The bounce appears as a high-frequency oscillation. We choose the cutoff frequency $f_c$ such that:

$$
f_c \ll \frac{1}{t_b}
$$

Pick $f_c = \frac{1}{10 t_b}$. Substituting $t_b = 5$ ms:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{10 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 2 — Express Cutoff in Terms of RC

For a first-order RC low-pass filter, the cutoff frequency is:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}
$$

### Step 3 — Choose R and Compute C

Choose $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (a standard pull-up value). Rearrange to solve for $C$:

$$
C = \frac{1}{2 \pi R f_c}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
C = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10 \times 10^{3} \times 20}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

$$
C = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{4} \times 20}
$$

$$
C = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
C \approx 7.96 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F}
$$

$$
C \approx 0.796 \, \mu\text{F} \approx 1 \, \mu\text{F (standard value)}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify the Time Constant

$$
\tau = R C = 10^4 \times 10^{-6} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

Since $10 \text{ ms} > t_b = 5 \text{ ms}$, the bounce is fully absorbed by the filter. **Design validated.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the assumption *bounce treated as step transient* and the design rule $\tau \geq 3 t_b$ for **95% settling** when answering debounce questions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bumper Sensor Performance Comparison

| Performance Parameter | Mechanical | Pneumatic | Optical | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Response Time | **< 1 ms** | 5 – 20 ms | < 1 ms | < 1 ms |
| Sensitivity | Low (needs force) | High | High | Very High |
| False-Trigger Rate | Very Low | Low | Medium | High |
| Durability (Cycles) | $10^6$ | $10^5$ | $> 10^7$ | $> 10^7$ |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | Medium | High |
| Operating Range | **0 mm (contact only)** | 0 mm | 0 – 5 mm | 0 – 10 mm |
| Maintenance | Periodic | Air-supply | Lens cleaning | None |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | High | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bumper Sensor System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow from mechanical contact to robot motor response.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Obstacle in Path] --> B[Bumper Plate Mounted on Robot Chassis]
    B -->|Mechanical Force Fc| C[Compliant Suspension with Spring Constant k]
    C -->|Displacement x crosses xth| D[Micro-Switch SPDT Contacts]
    D -->|Raw Bouncing Signal| E[RC Low-Pass Debounce Filter]
    E -->|Cleaned Digital Signal| F[Microcontroller GPIO Input Pin]
    F -->|Interrupt Service Routine| G[Robot Main Controller CPU]
    G -->|Stop or Reverse Command| H[Motor Driver Circuit H-Bridge]
    H -->|PWM Signal| I[Left and Right Wheel DC Motors]
    I -->|Robot Decelerates and Turns| J[Collision Avoided]

    style A fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style D fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style E fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style F fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#009900,color:#000
    style G fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#009900,color:#000
    style H fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    style I fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    style J fill:#d0d0d0,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Detailed Mechanical Construction Cross-Section (Block Form)

The following block schematic depicts the *internal layered structure* of a typical mechanical bumper assembly.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Outer Rubber Skin for Impact Absorption]
    L2[Layer 2 Rigid Plastic Bumper Plate]
    L3[Layer 3 Micro-Switch Bank Mounted on Inner Chassis]
    L4[Layer 4 PCB with Pull-Up Resistors and RC Filter]
    L5[Layer 5 Microcontroller Interface Connector]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5

    style L1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style L5 fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Robot Response on Bumper Trigger

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Robot Moving Forward] --> S1{Bumper Signal HIGH}
    S1 -->|No| S0
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Read Direction of Impact Left Center or Right]
    S2 --> S3{Which Bumper Activated}
    S3 -->|Left| S4[Reverse then Turn Right]
    S3 -->|Center| S5[Reverse then Turn Right or Left Random]
    S3 -->|Right| S6[Reverse then Turn Left]
    S4 --> S7[Resume Forward Motion]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S0

    style S0 fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#997a00,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#997a00,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#ffaa80,stroke:#993300,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style S6 fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style S7 fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** All Mermaid node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `L1`, `S0`) — reserved words like `end` and `subgraph` are never used as standalone node names. All labels are plain uppercase text without bold or italic markdown to ensure clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a bumper sensor. List any two applications of bumper sensors in mobile robots.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A bumper sensor is a **contact-type tactile sensor** mounted on the chassis of a mobile robot that produces a **binary electrical signal** when physical contact with an external object occurs. It is essentially a mechanical switch that closes (or opens) a circuit upon impact. (1½ marks)

**Two Applications:** (1½ marks — ¾ each)
1. **Obstacle Detection in AGVs** — used as a safety stop mechanism in warehouse automated guided vehicles to prevent collisions with racks, pallets, or workers.
2. **Wall-Following in Domestic Robots** — vacuum cleaning robots use a half-ring bumper to detect walls and furniture edges for navigation and mapping.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Mention any three limitations of bumper sensors in mobile robot applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Reactive (Non-Predictive) Nature** — Bumpers only detect collisions *after* they occur, providing no early warning or pre-collision data. (1 mark)
2. **No Distance or Velocity Information** — A bumper gives only a binary `0/1` signal; it cannot measure the distance to the obstacle, its size, or the approach speed. (1 mark)
3. **Mechanical Wear and Bounce** — Physical switches suffer contact bounce, wear-and-tear over millions of cycles, and require periodic maintenance. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-end exam questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice between two full questions. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must attempt *one full question*.

---

### Choice A — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q(A)(a) Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a mechanical micro-switch based bumper sensor used in mobile robots. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A mechanical bumper sensor consists of the following components:
- **Bumper Plate** — a rigid plastic plate that forms the outermost skin of the robot.
- **Compliant Suspension** — a spring or rubber mount that allows the plate to move inward on impact.
- **Micro-switch (SPDT type)** — a snap-action electromechanical switch triggered by displacement of the plate.
- **Pull-up Resistor** — connected between the output line and $V_{cc}$ to define the logic HIGH state.
- **Output Wire** — connected to a GPIO pin of the microcontroller.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. When the robot moves and contacts an obstacle, the bumper plate receives a force $F_c$.
2. The plate moves inward by displacement $x$, compressing the suspension spring.
3. When $x \geq x_{th}$, the micro-switch contacts **snap closed** (or open, depending on NO/NC configuration).
4. The output voltage transitions from $V_{cc}$ to $0$ V (or vice versa), producing a clean digital edge.
5. The microcontroller detects the change and triggers an **interrupt service routine** that commands the robot to stop and reverse.

**Neat Diagram (1 mark):**
The block architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes (Robot Chassis → Bumper Plate → Micro-switch → Debounce Filter → Microcontroller → Motor Driver → Wheels) should be redrawn neatly in the answer script.

---

**Q(A)(b) Discuss the different types of bumper sensors used in mobile robots, comparing their relative advantages and disadvantages. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major types of bumper sensors are:

| Type | Working Principle | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Physical contact closes micro-switch contacts | Simple, cheap, robust, reliable | Wear, bounce, contact-only |
| **Pneumatic** | Air bladder pressure change | High sensitivity, no electrical arcing | Requires pump, slower |
| **Optical** | IR beam interrupted by obstacle | No mechanical contact, fast | Affected by dust/light |
| **Capacitive** | Capacitance change near object | Detects soft contact, no wear | False triggers |
| **Hall-Effect Whisker** | Magnet on flexible whisker | Analog response, smooth | Needs calibration |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Mechanical bumpers are preferred in industrial AGVs for their ruggedness, while capacitive and optical bumpers are used in domestic service robots where silent operation and longer life are required.

---

### Choice B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q(B)(a) Explain the phenomenon of switch bouncing in micro-switch-based bumpers. With a circuit diagram, describe an RC debounce filter and derive the relationship between R, C, and bounce time. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Switch Bouncing (2 marks):**
When mechanical contacts of a micro-switch close, they physically bounce several times before settling. This produces a series of rapid open/close transitions (typically 1–10 ms) at the output, which a microcontroller may interpret as *multiple* impacts rather than one.

**RC Debounce Filter (3 marks):**
A first-order RC low-pass filter connected to the switch output smooths the bouncing transitions.
- The resistor $R$ is the pull-up (or pull-down) component.
- The capacitor $C$ charges/discharges through $R$, slowing the edge transitions.
- The cutoff frequency $f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ is chosen to be far below the bounce frequency.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
For the RC filter to suppress bounce of duration $t_b$, the time constant $\tau = RC$ must satisfy:

$$
\tau \geq 3 t_b
$$

This is derived from the capacitor charging equation $V(t) = V_{cc}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ by requiring $V(t)$ to reach 95% of its final value (i.e., $e^{-3} \approx 0.05$). For a 5 ms bounce:

$$
RC \geq 15 \text{ ms}
$$

Choosing $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ gives $C \geq 1.5 \text{ \mu F}$ (use standard $2.2 \text{ \mu F}$).

---

**Q(B)(b) A mobile robot of mass 50 kg moving at 1.5 m/s collides with a wall. The bumper has a spring constant of 2000 N/m. Calculate (i) the compression of the bumper spring, and (ii) the minimum velocity at which the bumper will trigger if the micro-switch threshold is 0.05 m. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 50$ kg
- Velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Spring constant $k = 2000$ N/m
- Threshold displacement $x_{th} = 0.05$ m

**Part (i) — Compression of bumper spring (3 marks):**

Using the energy balance equation derived in Section 3.1:

$$
x = v \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
x = 1.5 \times \sqrt{\frac{50}{2000}}
$$

$$
x = 1.5 \times \sqrt{0.025}
$$

$$
x = 1.5 \times 0.1581
$$

$$
x \approx 0.237 \text{ m}
$$

**Part (ii) — Minimum trigger velocity (4 marks):**

Using the trigger condition from Section 3.1:

$$
v_{min} = x_{th} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}
$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$
v_{min} = 0.05 \times \sqrt{\frac{2000}{50}}
$$

$$
v_{min} = 0.05 \times \sqrt{40}
$$

$$
v_{min} = 0.05 \times 6.3246
$$

$$
v_{min} \approx 0.316 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the actual velocity (1.5 m/s) ≫ $v_{min}$ (0.316 m/s), the bumper **will definitely trigger** on this impact.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating Bumpers with Range Sensors** — Students frequently describe bumpers as *"sensors that detect obstacles at a distance."* This is **factually wrong** and attracts 0 marks. Always state: *"Bumpers detect contact, not distance."*
> 
> 2. **Skipping the Debounce Explanation** — KTU examiners specifically test whether students know that micro-switches *bounce*. If your answer describes a clean digital transition without mentioning bounce and filtering, expect a **2-mark deduction**.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting Units** — In numerical problems, writing $x = 0.237$ without the unit (metres) is a **½ to 1 mark** deduction. **Always include units** in final answers.
> 
> 4. **No Diagram = Lost Mark** — For 7-mark questions, a missing or poorly labelled diagram costs **at least 1 mark**. Always include a block diagram even if not explicitly asked.
> 
> 5. **Mixing Up NO/NC Logic** — Normally-Open (NO) closes on impact; Normally-Closed (NC) opens on impact. Confusing the two leads to **sign-inverted logic** and full mark loss on the working section.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Definition:** Bumper = binary contact sensor producing a `HIGH/LOW` digital signal upon physical impact.
- **Classification:** Contact-type, tactile, exteroceptive sensor — part of the robot's **safety layer**.
- **Most Common Type:** Mechanical micro-switch (SPDT) with spring-loaded bumper plate.
- **Key Formula:** $x = v\sqrt{m/k}$ — relates collision velocity, robot mass, and bumper stiffness.
- **Trigger Condition:** $x \geq x_{th}$ where $x_{th}$ is the micro-switch mechanical travel (0.5–3 mm typical).
- **Switch Bounce:** 1–10 ms spurious transitions; suppressed by RC low-pass filter with $\tau \geq 3 t_b$.
- **Debounce Cutoff:** $f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$, must be much less than the bounce frequency.
- **Energy Equation:** $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$ — for elastic collision analysis.
- **Signal Type:** Digital, 1-bit, Boolean — *no* distance, velocity, or size information.
- **Response:** Microseconds-level latency; **faster** than ultrasonic/IR for direct collision confirmation.
- **Limitations:** Non-predictive, mechanical wear, no range data, susceptible to switch bounce.
- **Applications:** AGVs, vacuum robots, autonomous wheelchairs, Mars rovers, mobile manipulators.
- **Safety Standard:** Bumpers are mandatory in **ISO 3691-4** compliant industrial mobile robots.
- **Placement in Architecture:** **Layer 0 (Safety)** — below perception, planning, and motion control.
- **Comparison Point:** Always contrast with **ultrasonic/IR/LiDAR** in exam answers to show conceptual clarity.
- **Exam Rule:** Include **units, diagrams, and explicit assumptions** in every numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accelerometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accelerometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
An **accelerometer** is a *single-axis* or *multi-axis* electromechanical sensor that measures **proper acceleration** — the rate of change of velocity of a body relative to a free-fall inertial frame. In the context of mobile robotics, accelerometers are the primary sensors used to estimate:
- Linear acceleration of the robot body along one or more orthogonal axes
- Static tilt (inclination) with respect to the gravity vector
- Vibration signatures of motors, wheels, and terrain interaction

> [!NOTE]
> **Proper Acceleration vs. Coordinate Acceleration**
> Proper acceleration is the acceleration felt by the object (the "felt" weight per unit mass), measured in **m/s²** or normalized in **g** (where **1 g = 9.80665 m/s²**). A sensor in free-fall records **0 g** even though its coordinate acceleration is **g** downward.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Coffee Cup Test"
Imagine holding a sealed cup of coffee. The liquid surface tilts whenever the cup accelerates. The accelerometer is essentially a microscopic, electronic version of that coffee cup:
- A tiny **proof mass** is suspended on flexible springs inside the sensor.
- When the host robot accelerates, inertia causes the proof mass to **deflect** relative to the sensor frame.
- This deflection is converted into a voltage by a transduction mechanism (usually **capacitive** in MEMS units).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Accelerometers are Critical in Mobile Robotics**
> - **Inertial Navigation (IMU)**: Combined with gyroscopes, accelerometers enable dead-reckoning when GPS is denied.
> - **Terrain-Adaptive Locomotion**: Vibration spectra reveal surface type (concrete vs. grass vs. gravel).
> - **Fall Detection & Tilt Safety**: Quadrupeds and humanoids use static g-vector measurement to detect unsafe postures.
> - **Gesture & Vibration Control**: Useful in HRI and robotic manipulator compliance.

### 1.3 Classification of Accelerometers (by Transduction)

| Transduction Principle | Physical Quantity Measured | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacitive (MEMS)** | Change in plate gap → ΔC | Mobile robotics, IMUs, smartphones |
| **Piezoelectric** | Stress-induced charge Q | High-frequency vibration, industrial |
| **Piezoresistive** | Strain-induced ΔR | High-g shock, crash sensing |
| **Thermal (Convective)** | Heated gas bubble displacement | Ultra-stable, harsh environments |
| **Tunneling Current** | Quantum tunneling current | Sub-nano-g resolution lab sensors |
| **Hall Effect / Lorentz Force** | Magnetic field change | Specialized high-g ranges |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (with Typical Numerical Ranges)
- **Range**: ±2 g to ±200 g (mobile robots typically use ±2 g, ±4 g, ±8 g)
- **Sensitivity**: 100 mV/g to 1000 mV/g (analog), or LSB/g (digital)
- **Bandwidth**: DC to 1 kHz typical (DC response is the *defining feature*)
- **Noise Density**: 25 µg/√Hz to 500 µg/√Hz
- **Bias Stability**: 0.1 mg to 5 mg
- **Cross-axis Sensitivity**: 1% to 5%

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mass–Spring–Damper (MSD) Model — The Heart of the Accelerometer
Every accelerometer, regardless of transduction type, can be modeled as a **single degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mass–spring–damper system**:

- A **proof mass** `m` is suspended by a **flexure** of stiffness `k`
- Motion is opposed by a **damping coefficient** `b` (viscous air or fluid squeeze-film damping)
- The **case** is bonded to the robot body and moves with acceleration `a_in(t)`
- The **relative displacement** of the mass w.r.t. the case is `x(t)`

**Free-Body Diagram (Newton's 2nd Law applied to the proof mass):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inertial force:} \quad & F_i = m \cdot \ddot{x}_{\text{absolute}} \\
\text{Spring force:} \quad & F_s = -k \cdot x \\
\text{Damper force:} \quad & F_d = -b \cdot \dot{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the absolute acceleration of the mass is `a_in + ẍ` (case acceleration + relative acceleration), applying Newton's 2nd Law in the non-inertial sensor frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \cdot \ddot{x} + b \cdot \dot{x} + k \cdot x &= -m \cdot a_{in}(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **second-order linear ordinary differential equation with constant coefficients**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step
- **Inertial term `m·ẍ`**: The mass resists acceleration change (Newton's 1st law).
- **Damping term `b·ẋ`**: The fluid (usually air at micro-scale) dissipates energy, preventing perpetual oscillation.
- **Spring term `k·x`**: The flexure stores potential energy and provides a restoring force.
- **Right-hand side `−m·a_in`**: An "effective inertial force" appears in the non-inertial frame attached to the moving case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Right-Hand Side is the Key Insight**
> The *external driving function is proportional to the input acceleration*. This is precisely why we can recover `a_in(t)` by measuring `x(t)`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | Sets the usable bandwidth |
| 2 | Damping ratio | $\zeta$ | $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2 \sqrt{k m}} = \dfrac{b}{2 m \omega_n}$ | dimensionless | 0.6–0.7 typical for MEMS |
| 3 | Static sensitivity | $S_{dc}$ | $S_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}$ | m·s²/m | Displacement per unit a |
| 4 | DC gain (displacement/a) | $G_0$ | $G_0 = -\dfrac{m}{k} = -\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}$ | s² | Negative = opposite direction |
| 5 | Transfer function | $H(s)$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{-m}{m s^2 + b s + k}$ | s² | Output X(s) per input A(s) |
| 6 | Standard 2nd-order form | $H(s)$ | $\dfrac{-\omega_n^{-2}}{\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta s}{\omega_n} + 1}$ | s² | Bandwidth ≈ $\omega_n$ for $\zeta \le 0.7$ |
| 7 | Capacitive ΔC | $\Delta C$ | $\Delta C = 2 C_0 \cdot \dfrac{x}{d}$ | F | $C_0$ = rest capacitance, $d$ = gap |
| 8 | Capacitive sensitivity | $S_C$ | $S_C = \dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0 \cdot a_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{d \cdot \omega_n^2}$ | s²/m | Engineering figure of merit |
| 9 | Tilt-angle from accel. | $\theta$ | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a_y}{a_z}\right)$ | rad | 2-axis static tilt |
| 10 | Roll / Pitch | $\phi, \theta$ | $\phi = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{a_x}{g}\right),\ \theta = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{a_y}{g}\right)$ | rad | Static (no motion) only |
| 11 | Noise floor (RMS) | $a_{n,rms}$ | $a_{n,rms} = n_d \cdot \sqrt{BW}$ | m/s² | $n_d$ = noise density |
| 12 | Resolution | $a_{res}$ | $a_{res} = \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{S_{v}}$ | g | $S_v$ = voltage sensitivity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Autonomous Vehicle IMU**: MEMS accelerometers (e.g., Bosch BMI088, ST LSM6DSOX) provide ±24 g range and 6.7 kHz ODR for crash detection and eCall.
- **Quadruped Robots (MIT Mini Cheetah, ANYbotics)**: IMU at 1 kHz enables dynamic balancing and terrain adaptation.
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)**: Space-grade accelerometers measure gravity anomalies and seismic activity.
- **Drone Flight Controllers (Pixhawk)**: Used in complementary/Kalman filters for attitude estimation at 8 kHz.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function from Newton's 2nd Law

**Starting equation** (from Section 2.1):
$$
m \cdot \ddot{x}(t) + b \cdot \dot{x}(t) + k \cdot x(t) = -m \cdot a_{in}(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace Transform** (assuming zero initial conditions: $x(0)=0$, $\dot{x}(0)=0$).

Recall:
- $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = s X(s)$
- $\mathcal{L}\{\ddot{x}(t)\} = s^2 X(s)$
- $\mathcal{L}\{a_{in}(t)\} = A_{in}(s)$

Substituting:
$$
m \cdot s^2 X(s) + b \cdot s X(s) + k \cdot X(s) = -m \cdot A_{in}(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out X(s)** on the left-hand side:
$$
X(s) \cdot \left( m s^2 + b s + k \right) = -m \cdot A_{in}(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function** $H(s) = \dfrac{X(s)}{A_{in}(s)}$:
$$
H(s) = \frac{X(s)}{A_{in}(s)} = \frac{-m}{m s^2 + b s + k}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to the standard second-order form** by dividing numerator and denominator by $k$:

Since $k = m \omega_n^2$, we have $\omega_n^2 = k/m$. Dividing both num. and denom. by $k$ yields:
$$
H(s) = \frac{-1/\omega_n^2}{\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} s + 1}
$$

This is the canonical form used in control textbooks and datasheets. The **DC gain** is obtained by setting $s = 0$:

$$
H(0) = \frac{-1/\omega_n^2}{1} = -\frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = -\frac{m}{k}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The DC gain is negative because the proof mass moves **opposite** to the applied acceleration (inertia).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitive Readout from Mass Displacement

A MEMS accelerometer uses a **differential capacitor** structure: two fixed outer plates and one central movable plate attached to the proof mass.

**Step 1 — Resting capacitance** of one side:
$$
C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$
where:
- $A$ = plate overlap area
- $d$ = nominal gap
- $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity = $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (≈ 1 for air)

**Step 2 — When the proof mass deflects by $x$ (toward one plate):**
- Near plate gap: $d - x$ → capacitance $C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - x}$
- Far plate gap: $d + x$ → capacitance $C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + x}$

**Step 3 — Differential capacitance** (the quantity measured by the ASIC):
$$
\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \frac{1}{d - x} - \frac{1}{d + x} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator and simplification**:
$$
\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{(d + x) - (d - x)}{(d - x)(d + x)} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{2x}{d^2 - x^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Small-deflection approximation** ($x \ll d$, so $x^2 \approx 0$):
$$
\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot x}{d^2} = 2 C_0 \cdot \frac{x}{d}
$$

This is the **linearized capacitive sensitivity** used in all MEMS design handbooks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Static Tilt-Sensing Relationship

When the robot is **stationary**, the only proper acceleration present is the reaction to gravity. If a single-axis accelerometer is tilted by angle $\theta$ from the horizontal:

**Step 1 — Free-Body analysis in the static frame**:
The accelerometer measures the component of the reaction-to-gravity vector along its sensitive axis. If the axis makes angle $\theta$ with the horizontal, then the component along the axis is:
$$
a_{measured} = g \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\theta$**:
$$
\sin(\theta) = \frac{a_{measured}}{g} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \theta = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a_{measured}}{g}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Two-axis (pitch & roll) using a 2-axis accelerometer**:
With the sensor's y-axis horizontal and z-axis vertical at rest:
$$
\theta_{pitch} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right)
$$

Using the small-angle approximation valid for small tilts ($\theta \ll 1$ rad):
$$
\theta_{pitch} \approx \frac{a_y}{a_z} \approx \frac{a_y}{g}
$$

**Validity range:** This derivation **only holds when the robot is not accelerating** (otherwise inertial acceleration adds to gravity and corrupts the tilt estimate). This is why pure accelerometer-based tilt fails during motion — hence the need for **sensor fusion with gyroscopes**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- Proof mass: $m = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ kg (1 µg)
- Spring constant: $k = 1$ N/m
- Damping coefficient: $b = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s/m
- Gap: $d = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m (2 µm)
- Plate area: $A = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m × $1 \times 10^{-6}$ m

**Compute (a) Natural frequency:**
$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{10^{6}} = 1000 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Frequency in Hz:
$$
f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Compute (b) Damping ratio:**
$$
\zeta = \frac{b}{2 \sqrt{k m}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \sqrt{(1)(1 \times 10^{-6})}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\zeta = 0.0025$ is **far below** the optimal value (0.6–0.7). In practice, designers add squeeze-film damping channels to raise $\zeta$.

**Compute (c) Static displacement per g:**
At $a_{in} = 1g = 9.81$ m/s², $s = 0$:
$$
x = H(0) \cdot a_{in} = \frac{-m}{k} \cdot a_{in} = \frac{-1 \times 10^{-6}}{1} \cdot 9.81 = -9.81 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = -9.81 \text{ µm}
$$

**Compute (d) Differential capacitance per g:**
$$
\Delta C = 2 C_0 \cdot \frac{x}{d} = 2 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \cdot \frac{x}{d}
$$
$$
\Delta C = 2 \cdot \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(1 \times 10^{-12})}{2 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$
$$
\Delta C = 2 \cdot (4.427 \times 10^{-18}) \cdot 4.905 = 4.343 \times 10^{-17} \text{ F} \approx 43.4 \text{ fF}
$$

This femto-farad-level signal is why **on-chip ASIC charge amplifiers** are mandatory in MEMS accelerometers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["ROBOT BODY"] --> node2["SENSOR CASE"]
    node2 --> node3["PROOF MASS m"]
    node3 --> node4["FLEXURE SPRING k"]
    node3 --> node5["DAMPER b"]
    node3 --> node6["MOVABLE CAPACITOR PLATE"]
    node6 --> node7["FIXED CAPACITOR PLATE 1"]
    node6 --> node8["FIXED CAPACITOR PLATE 2"]
    node7 --> node9["DIFFERENTIAL CAPACITANCE Delta C"]
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10["CHARGE AMPLIFIER ASIC"]
    node10 --> node11["DEMODULATOR AND FILTER"]
    node11 --> node12["ADC 16 OR 20 BIT"]
    node12 --> node13["DIGITAL TEMP COMPENSATION"]
    node13 --> node14["I2C OR SPI OUTPUT"]
    node14 --> node15["ROBOT MCU OR IMU FUSION FILTER"]
    node15 --> node16["ORIENTATION ESTIMATE"]
    node15 --> node17["LINEAR ACCELERATION ESTIMATE"]
```
