```mermaid
    node15 --> node18["VIBRATION SPECTRUM"]

    style node1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style node15 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style node16 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style node17 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style node18 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Sensor-Fusion Architecture (Accelerometer + Gyroscope → Orientation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["3-AXIS ACCELEROMETER"] --> nodeC["LOW-PASS FILTER"]
    nodeB["3-AXIS GYROSCOPE"] --> nodeD["INTEGRATION"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["COMPLEMENTARY FILTER OR KALMAN FILTER"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["ROBOT PITCH ANGLE THETA"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["ROBOT ROLL ANGLE PHI"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["ROBOT YAW ANGLE PSI FROM MAGNETOMETER"]

    style nodeA fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style nodeH fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sensor Fusion is Mandatory**
> - Accelerometer gives **absolute tilt** but is **noisy at high frequency** and **corrupted by linear acceleration**.
> - Gyroscope gives **smooth short-term rotation rate** but **drifts over time** (no DC reference).
> - The **complementary filter** (e.g., $\theta = \alpha(\theta_{prev} + \omega \cdot dt) + (1-\alpha)\theta_{accel}$) combines the best of both.

### 4.3 Frequency-Response Behavior of the MSD Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIn["INPUT ACCELERATION a_in"] --> nodeLow["BELOW omega_n: FLAT GAIN 1 over omega_n squared"]
    nodeLow --> nodeRes["AT omega_n: PEAK FACTOR 1 over 2 zeta"]
    nodeRes --> nodeHigh["ABOVE omega_n: ROLLOFF 40 dB per decade"]
    nodeHigh --> nodeOut["OUTPUT DISPLACEMENT x"]

    style nodeIn fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style nodeLow fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style nodeRes fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style nodeHigh fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style nodeOut fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** Below $\omega_n$, the displacement is proportional to the input acceleration (accelerometer operates correctly). Above $\omega_n$, the proof mass can no longer follow the input, and the output rolls off at $-40$ dB/decade. This defines the **usable bandwidth** of the sensor.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define "proper acceleration" as measured by a MEMS accelerometer. How does it differ from the acceleration computed by double-differentiating GPS coordinates of a mobile robot? Why does a freely-falling accelerometer read 0 g?** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Proper acceleration is the acceleration of a body **relative to a free-fall (inertial) reference frame**, i.e., the acceleration "felt" by an object. It is what an accelerometer mounted on a robot actually measures. In contrast, coordinate acceleration is the second time-derivative of position in an arbitrary (typically Earth-fixed) frame.
- When double-differentiating GPS coordinates, one obtains the *coordinate* acceleration (with respect to the rotating, accelerating Earth frame), which includes centripetal and Coriolis terms.
- A freely-falling accelerometer reads **0 g** because the proof mass and the case are in the *same* free-fall state; relative to the inertial frame, the mass experiences no "felt" force. This is the operational basis of Einstein's equivalence principle.

**Q2. List and briefly explain any four key specifications used to characterize an accelerometer for mobile-robot applications.** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Range** (±g): Maximum measurable acceleration before saturation; e.g., ±4 g for humanoid balance, ±16 g for crash detection.
2. **Sensitivity** (mV/g or LSB/g): Ratio of output change to input acceleration; higher sensitivity → better resolution.
3. **Bandwidth** (Hz): Frequency range over which the output faithfully follows the input; typically DC–1 kHz for MEMS IMUs.
4. **Noise Density** (µg/√Hz): Spectral density of internal electrical noise; lower is better for vibration analytics.
*(Acceptable alternatives: bias stability, cross-axis sensitivity, nonlinearity %, sample rate.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** (14 Marks)

**(a) Starting from Newton's 2nd law, derive the transfer function of a single-degree-of-freedom mass–spring–damper accelerometer. Express your result in the standard second-order form and identify the natural frequency, damping ratio, and DC gain.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 model]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the equation of motion** in the non-inertial sensor frame:
$$
m \ddot{x}(t) + b \dot{x}(t) + k x(t) = -m \, a_{in}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace transform** (zero initial conditions):
$$
(m s^2 + b s + k) X(s) = -m \, A_{in}(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function**:
$$
H(s) = \frac{X(s)}{A_{in}(s)} = \frac{-m}{m s^2 + b s + k}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide numerator and denominator by $k$**, using $k = m \omega_n^2$:
$$
H(s) = \frac{-1/\omega_n^2}{\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_n^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} s + 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the parameters**:
- Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
- Damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{km}}$
- DC gain: $H(0) = -1/\omega_n^2 = -m/k$

**Step 6 — Comment on the operating region**:
For accurate accelerometer operation, the input frequency must satisfy $\omega \ll \omega_n$ (typically $\omega \le 0.3 \omega_n$), ensuring a flat magnitude response.

---

**(b) A capacitive MEMS accelerometer has the following parameters: proof mass $m = 0.5 \,\mu$g, spring constant $k = 0.5$ N/m, damping coefficient $b = 1.9 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s/m, and nominal capacitive gap $d = 1.5 \,\mu$m with plate overlap area $A = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m². Calculate the natural frequency, damping ratio, displacement per g, and the differential capacitance per g.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 model]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Natural frequency**:
$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{10^6} = 1000 \text{ rad/s}
$$
$$
f_n = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Damping ratio**:
$$
\zeta = \frac{b}{2 \sqrt{k m}} = \frac{1.9 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \sqrt{(0.5)(0.5 \times 10^{-6})}} = \frac{1.9 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.9 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Static displacement per g** ($a_{in} = 9.81$ m/s², DC, $s = 0$):
$$
x = -\frac{m}{k} \cdot a_{in} = -\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5} \cdot 9.81 = -9.81 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = -9.81 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Resting capacitance** $C_0$:
$$
C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(5 \times 10^{-7})}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.951 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 2.95 \text{ pF}
$$

**Step 5 — Differential capacitance per g**:
$$
\Delta C = 2 C_0 \cdot \frac{|x|}{d} = 2 \times 2.951 \times 10^{-12} \times \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$
$$
\Delta C = 2 \times 2.951 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.54 = 3.86 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} \approx 38.6 \text{ pF}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check / interpretation**:
- The natural frequency is 159 Hz — a typical MEMS IMU value.
- $\zeta = 0.0019$ is far below 0.7, indicating this design would ring severely near resonance; the bandwidth must be limited well below 159 Hz.
- 38.6 pF is comfortably measurable by a charge-amplifier ASIC.

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a) With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the working principle of a capacitive MEMS accelerometer. Discuss three advantages it offers over a piezoelectric accelerometer for mobile-robot applications.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 model]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (Block Diagram from Section 4.1):**

1. The **proof mass** `m` is suspended by flexure springs of stiffness `k` inside a sealed silicon case bonded to the robot body.
2. When the robot accelerates with $a_{in}(t)$, the case moves but inertia keeps the mass momentarily behind, producing a relative displacement $x(t)$ governed by the MSD equation.
3. The mass carries a **central movable plate** of a differential capacitor with two fixed outer plates. Deflection $x$ changes the gap on one side from $d$ to $(d-x)$ and the other from $d$ to $(d+x)$.
4. The two capacitances $C_1$ and $C_2$ are fed into a **switched-capacitor charge amplifier** on the ASIC. The differential output $\Delta C = C_1 - C_2$ is converted to a voltage, then digitized by a 16/20-bit $\Sigma$-$\Delta$ ADC.
5. **On-chip temperature compensation** and factory calibration convert the raw counts to a calibrated g-value, output over I²C/SPI to the robot MCU.

**Three Advantages over Piezoelectric Accelerometers:**

| # | Advantage | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DC (static) response** | Capacitive sensors respond to 0 Hz, measuring gravity and slow tilt; piezoelectric sensors only respond above ~1 Hz. |
| 2 | **Low power consumption** | MEMS ICs draw µA to mA; piezoelectric units need charge amplifiers with mA quiescent current. |
| 3 | **Small size & low cost** | Wafer-level batch fabrication gives $2 \times 2$ mm packages at <\$1; piezo sensors are bulky and expensive. |
| *(Optional 4th)* | Better temperature stability | On-chip digital compensation cancels bias drift. |

---

**(b) A 2-axis MEMS accelerometer is mounted on a stationary humanoid robot. The sensor reads $a_y = 0.4$ g along the y-axis and $a_z = 0.92$ g along the z-axis. Calculate the pitch and roll angles of the robot. Justify why this tilt estimate is unreliable if the robot is walking.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam — Module 2 model]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the gravity vector components**:
At rest, the accelerometer measures only the reaction to gravity. The gravity vector has magnitude $g$, so the components are $(a_x, a_y, a_z) = g \cdot (n_x, n_y, n_z)$ where $(n_x, n_y, n_z)$ is the unit downward direction in the sensor frame.

**Step 2 — Compute the pitch angle** (rotation about the y-axis):
$$
\theta_{pitch} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.4}{0.92}\right) = \arctan(0.4348) \approx 23.5^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the roll angle** (rotation about the x-axis):
Given $a_x = 0$ g (not stated; assume aligned with gravity plane):
$$
\phi_{roll} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_x}{a_z}\right) = 0^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Validity check** (magnitude of the measured vector must equal $g$):
$$
\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} = \sqrt{0^2 + 0.4^2 + 0.92^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.8464} = \sqrt{1.0064} \approx 1.003 \, g
$$

The slight deviation from 1 g is within the sensor's $\pm 1$% scale-factor error — confirms consistency.

**Step 5 — Why the estimate fails during walking**:
When the robot is in motion, the accelerometer measures **total proper acceleration**:
$$
\mathbf{a}_{measured} = \mathbf{a}_{gravity} + \mathbf{a}_{motion}
$$
The motion component (linear acceleration of the torso, foot impacts, arm swing) can easily reach $\pm 2$ to $\pm 5$ g during locomotion, completely swamping the $\pm 0.4$ g gravity component. The tilt estimate becomes useless.

**Solution used in practice:** Fuse the accelerometer with a gyroscope in a **complementary filter** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**. The gyro provides short-term accurate rotation, and the accelerometer provides the long-term DC reference. This is implemented in the **Madgwick filter**, **Mahony filter**, or generic EKF on virtually all modern IMU chipsets (BNO055, MPU-9250, etc.).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes on Accelerometer Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** on the transfer function. The proof mass moves *opposite* to the input acceleration, so $H(0) = -m/k$, not $+m/k$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing natural frequency with bandwidth.** The bandwidth is approximately $\omega_n$ only when $\zeta \le 0.7$. For underdamped designs, the bandwidth is set explicitly by the −3 dB point, not by $f_n$. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up g and m/s² units.** $1\,g = 9.81$ m/s². Always convert before substituting into formulas. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting gravity in static measurements.** A stationary accelerometer reads ±1 g, not 0 g, because the proof mass is pushed by the case's reaction to gravity. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Using coordinate acceleration instead of proper acceleration** in derivations. The MSD model is in the *sensor (non-inertial)* frame. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Wrong sign in the differential capacitance formula.** $C_1 - C_2$ yields a signed $\Delta C$; students often write $|C_1 - C_2|$ but lose the directional information needed for sign recovery.
> 7. **Not addressing sensor fusion.** When asked "How is the accelerometer used in robots?", a one-line answer of "to measure acceleration" will lose 3+ marks. Discuss IMU fusion, complementary filter, and EKF.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: Accelerometer = sensor of **proper acceleration** in m/s² or g; it responds from **DC to $\omega_n$**.
- **Physical model**: Single-DOF **mass–spring–damper** with proof mass $m$, stiffness $k$, damping $b$.
- **Governing equation**:
  $m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + k x = -m a_{in}(t)$.
- **Transfer function**:
  $H(s) = \dfrac{-m}{m s^2 + b s + k} = \dfrac{-1/\omega_n^2}{(s/\omega_n)^2 + 2\zeta (s/\omega_n) + 1}$.
- **Key parameters**:
  $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$,
  $\zeta = b/(2\sqrt{km})$,
  $H(0) = -m/k = -1/\omega_n^2$.
- **Operating regime**: $\omega \ll \omega_n$ for flat response; bandwidth $\approx 0.3\,\omega_n$ for underdamped case.
- **Capacitive readout**: $\Delta C = 2 C_0 \cdot (x/d)$ (small-deflection linearization).
- **Tilt sensing**: $\theta = \arcsin(a_y/g)$ or $\theta = \arctan(a_y/a_z)$ — **valid only when robot is stationary**.
- **Sensor fusion necessity**: Pure accelerometer tilt fails during motion; fuse with gyroscope via **complementary filter or EKF**.
- **Bias & noise**: Always check datasheet for **bias stability** (mg), **noise density** (µg/√Hz), and **cross-axis sensitivity** (%).
- **Most common in mobile robotics**: **MEMS capacitive 3-axis** (e.g., LSM6DS3, BMI270, ADXL345) — small, low-power, DC-responsive, I²C/SPI.
- **KTU must-know derivations**:
  (1) MSD → transfer function
  (2) Static sensitivity $= -m/k$
  (3) Tilt-angle from g-vector components
  (4) Capacitive ΔC linearization.
- **Engineering figures of merit**: $1/\omega_n^2$ (sensitivity), noise floor in µg/√Hz, range in g, sample rate in Hz/ODR.
- **Applications to remember**: IMU for AHRS, terrain vibration classification, fall detection in humanoids, seismic sensing in planetary rovers, eCall in autonomous vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gyroscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gyroscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **gyroscope** is a sensor device used to measure **angular velocity** (rate of rotation) about one or more axes. In mobile robotics, gyroscopes form a critical component of an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**, enabling the estimation of **orientation**, **heading**, and **angular position** of a robot by integrating the measured angular velocity over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEBRT522 - Mobile Robotics):**
> *A gyroscope is a device that uses the principles of angular momentum (mechanical) or wave interference (optical) to measure the rate of rotation of a body about a defined axis. In mobile robotics, gyroscopes provide the angular velocity vector $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z]^T$ expressed in the body frame, which is integrated to obtain roll, pitch, and yaw angles.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine spinning a bicycle wheel on a string. As long as the wheel is spinning fast, it strongly resists being tilted sideways — its axis of spin stubbornly points in the same direction. This is **angular momentum** in action. A mechanical gyroscope exploits this exact phenomenon: a fast-spinning rotor preserves its orientation in space, and any deviation between the spin axis and the robot's frame can be measured as rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> A gyroscope is essentially a "rotation speedometer" for a robot. Just as a speedometer in a car tells you how fast you are moving linearly, a gyroscope tells you how fast the robot is **turning or tilting**. By adding up (integrating) all these small turns over time, the robot can figure out its exact orientation — similar to how a blindfolded sailor might track their heading by counting each turn of their ship.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters characterize gyroscope performance in mobile robotics:

- **Bias ($\boldsymbol{b}_\omega$):** The constant offset error in the angular velocity reading, typically expressed in **$^\circ/\text{h}$** (degrees per hour) or **rad/s**.
- **Drift Rate:** The rate at which the integrated angle error grows over time, measured in **$^\circ/\text{h}$**.
- **Scale Factor Error:** The percentage deviation of the measured angular velocity from the true value.
- **Noise Density ($N_\omega$):** Random noise per unit bandwidth, measured in **$^\circ/\text{s}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$** or **$\text{rad/s}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$**.
- **Bandwidth:** The frequency range over which the gyroscope operates accurately, measured in **Hz**.
- **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The maximum measurable angular velocity, typically in **$^\circ/\text{s}$** (e.g., $\pm 2000^\circ/\text{s}$).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The Earth's rotation rate is approximately **$15^\circ/\text{h}$** or **$7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s**. High-precision gyroscopes (e.g., ring laser gyroscopes) are sensitive enough to detect this!

## 1.4 Role of Gyroscopes in Mobile Robotics

Modern mobile robots (drones, autonomous cars, warehouse AGVs, humanoid robots) use gyroscopes for:

1. **Attitude Estimation:** Determining roll, pitch, yaw angles of the robot.
2. **Stabilization:** Counteracting unwanted rotation (e.g., gimbal stabilization, drone flight control).
3. **Dead Reckoning Navigation:** Tracking heading changes when GPS or visual references are unavailable.
4. **Sensor Fusion:** Providing angular rate input to Kalman filters or complementary filters for orientation estimation.
5. **Control Loop Feedback:** Feeding $\omega$ back to PID controllers for trajectory tracking.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles of Different Gyroscope Types

### 2.1.1 Mechanical (Spinning Mass) Gyroscopes
- Based on the **conservation of angular momentum**: $\boldsymbol{L} = I\boldsymbol{\omega}$.
- A spinning rotor mounted in gimbals maintains its axis in inertial space.
- Any rotation of the mount produces a **precessional torque** perpendicular to the spin axis and the applied torque.
- The governing equation is **Euler's equation of motion** for a rigid body.

### 2.1.2 MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) Gyroscopes
- Based on the **Coriolis Effect**: when a mass $m$ moving with velocity $\boldsymbol{v}$ experiences a rotation $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$, it experiences a fictitious force:

$$\boldsymbol{F}_{Coriolis} = -2m(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \boldsymbol{v})$$

- A vibrating proof mass is driven at a primary mode; rotation causes a secondary mode displacement proportional to $\Omega$.
- Advantages: small, cheap (a few dollars), low power, integrated in IMUs.
- Disadvantages: higher bias drift and noise than optical gyroscopes.

### 2.1.3 Optical Gyroscopes (Fiber Optic / Ring Laser)
- Based on the **Sagnac Effect**: counter-propagating light beams in a rotating loop accumulate a path difference.
- The phase shift is:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{8\pi A \Omega}{c \lambda}$$

where $A$ is the loop area, $\Omega$ is the rotation rate, $c$ is the speed of light, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.
- Used in high-precision applications (aircraft, spacecraft, surveying).

## 2.2 Coordinate Frames in Mobile Robotics

Gyroscopes measure rotation in the **body frame** $\{B\}$ relative to the **inertial/world frame** $\{W\}$:

- **Body Frame $\{B\}$:** Fixed to the robot, axes: $x_b$ (forward), $y_b$ (left), $z_b$ (up).
- **World Frame $\{W\}$:** Fixed to Earth, axes: $x_w$ (North), $y_w$ (East), $z_w$ (Down or Up).

The transformation between frames uses **rotation matrices** built from **Euler angles** (roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$) or **quaternions**.

## 2.3 Why Gyroscopes? — The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use gyroscopes in robots?**
> 1. Wheel odometry gives translation but not heading — gyroscopes fill the gap.
> 2. Accelerometers cannot distinguish gravity from linear acceleration — gyroscopes decouple rotation from translation.
> 3. GPS fails indoors and during signal outage — gyroscopes enable **dead reckoning**.
> 4. Optical encoders measure motor rotation, not vehicle heading — gyroscopes measure the **true body rotation**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular Momentum | $\boldsymbol{L} = I\boldsymbol{\omega}$ | $I$: moment of inertia (kg·m²); $\omega$: angular velocity (rad/s) | Constant if no external torque |
| Euler's Equation | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \dfrac{d\boldsymbol{L}}{dt} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \boldsymbol{L}$ | $\tau$: torque (N·m); $\boldsymbol{\omega}$: body angular velocity | Applies in body frame |
| Precession Rate | $\Omega_p = \dfrac{\tau}{L} = \dfrac{\tau}{I\omega_s}$ | $\omega_s$: spin rate (rad/s) | $\omega_s \gg \Omega_p$ for gyroscopic stability |
| Coriolis Force | $\boldsymbol{F}_c = -2m(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \boldsymbol{v})$ | $m$: mass (kg); $v$: linear velocity (m/s) | Valid in rotating reference frame |
| Sagnac Phase Shift | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi A \Omega}{c \lambda}$ | $A$: loop area (m²); $c$: light speed (m/s); $\lambda$: wavelength (m) | Valid for $\Omega \ll c/\lambda$ |
| Angle Integration | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} \omega(\tau)\, d\tau$ | $\theta$: angle (rad); $\omega$: angular velocity (rad/s) | Discrete form: $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$ |
| Gyroscope Measurement Model | $\omega_{meas} = \omega_{true} + b_\omega + n_\omega$ | $b_\omega$: bias (rad/s); $n_\omega$: white noise | Bias drifts slowly (random walk) |
| Euler Angle Rates (ZYX) | $\begin{bmatrix}\dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & \sin\phi \tan\theta & \cos\phi \tan\theta \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi \sec\theta & \cos\phi \sec\theta\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}\omega_x \\ \omega_y \\ \omega_z\end{bmatrix}$ | $\phi, \theta, \psi$: roll, pitch, yaw; $\omega_{x,y,z}$: body rates | Singular at $\theta = \pm 90^\circ$ |
| Quaternion Update | $\dot{\boldsymbol{q}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{q} \otimes \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ \boldsymbol{\omega}\end{bmatrix}$ | $\boldsymbol{q}$: unit quaternion | $\|\boldsymbol{q}\|=1$, no singularities |
| MEMS Drift Equation | $b_\omega(t) = b_{\omega,0} + w_b t$ | $w_b$: bias random walk (rad/s²) | Drives integration error growth |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Inertial Navigation Systems (INS) in aircraft, spacecraft, and missiles use ring laser gyroscopes for high-accuracy heading.
- **Automotive:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC) and rollover detection use MEMS gyroscopes.
- **Robotics:** Drones (quadcopters) require gyroscopes at >1 kHz update rate for stable flight control.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones contain MEMS gyroscopes for screen rotation, gaming, and AR/VR.
- **Spacecraft:** Hubble Space Telescope uses rate gyroscopes for fine pointing (replaced in 2009 servicing mission).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gyroscope Angle from Angular Velocity Integration

The fundamental relationship between angular position and angular velocity is obtained by integration. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1: Define the rate of change of angle.**

The instantaneous angular velocity is defined as the time derivative of angular position:

$$\omega(t) = \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for the differential of angle.**

Multiply both sides by $dt$:

$$d\theta(t) = \omega(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3: Integrate both sides over a finite time interval $[0, t]$.**

The change in angle over the interval is the integral of angular velocity:

$$\int_{\theta(0)}^{\theta(t)} d\theta(\tau) = \int_{0}^{t} \omega(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the left-hand side (fundamental theorem of calculus).**

The integral of a differential gives the function evaluated at the bounds:

$$\theta(t) - \theta(0) = \int_{0}^{t} \omega(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 5: Solve explicitly for $\theta(t)$.**

Rearranging to isolate the final angle:

$$\theta(t) = \theta(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \omega(\tau)\, d\tau$$

This is the **continuous-time** angle integration formula. In discrete digital systems, this becomes a summation.

**Step 6: Derive the discrete-time form (sampled at interval $\Delta t$).**

Approximating the integral as a left Riemann sum:

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$$

where $\theta_k$ is the angle at time step $k$, and $\omega_k$ is the sampled angular velocity. This is the **Euler integration** form used in real-time robot controllers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Euler's Equation for a Spinning Gyroscope

We derive the **precession equation** of a mechanical gyroscope from Newton's second law for rotation.

**Step 1: Define angular momentum of a symmetric spinning rotor.**

For a symmetric rotor with moment of inertia $I_s$ about the spin axis, spinning at $\omega_s$:

$$L = I_s \omega_s$$

The angular momentum vector points along the spin axis.

**Step 2: Apply an external torque perpendicular to the spin axis.**

Let the torque $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ be applied perpendicular to $\boldsymbol{L}$. By Euler's equation:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \frac{d\boldsymbol{L}}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Express the change in angular momentum geometrically.**

When torque is applied, the spin axis **precesses** (rotates about a third perpendicular axis) at rate $\Omega_p$ rather than tilting. The tip of $\boldsymbol{L}$ sweeps a circle:

$$dL = L \sin(90^\circ)\, d\psi = L\, d\psi$$

where $d\psi$ is the angle of precession.

**Step 4: Equate the torque magnitude to the rate of change.**

The rate at which $L$ changes equals the applied torque:

$$\tau = \frac{dL}{dt} = L \frac{d\psi}{dt} = L \Omega_p$$

**Step 5: Solve for the precession rate $\Omega_p$.**

Substituting $L = I_s \omega_s$:

$$\tau = I_s \omega_s \Omega_p$$

Solving for $\Omega_p$:

$$\Omega_p = \frac{\tau}{I_s \omega_s}$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result.**

The precession rate is **inversely proportional** to the spin rate. A faster-spinning rotor precesses more slowly — this is why high-speed gyroscopes are stable. The direction of precession is **perpendicular** to both $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and $\boldsymbol{L}$, following the right-hand rule.

## 3.3 Derivation: MEMS Coriolis Gyroscope Sensitivity

We derive the relationship between Coriolis force and rotation rate for a MEMS gyroscope.

**Step 1: A proof mass is driven into primary oscillation.**

A MEMS comb-drive actuator drives the mass to oscillate along the $x$-axis:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_d t)$$

The velocity is:

$$v_x(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = -A \omega_d \sin(\omega_d t)$$

**Step 2: Apply a rotation $\Omega_z$ about the $z$-axis (out of plane).**

In the rotating frame, the Coriolis acceleration is:

$$\boldsymbol{a}_{Cor} = -2 \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \boldsymbol{v}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the cross product.**

With $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = \Omega_z \hat{k}$ and $\boldsymbol{v} = v_x \hat{i}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \boldsymbol{v} = \Omega_z v_x (\hat{k} \times \hat{i}) = \Omega_z v_x \hat{j}$$

So the Coriolis force is along the $y$-axis:

$$F_{Cor,y} = -2 m \Omega_z v_x(t) = 2 m \Omega_z A \omega_d \sin(\omega_d t)$$

**Step 4: Relate Coriolis force to secondary mode displacement.**

The secondary mode (sense mode) acts as a spring-mass-damper system:

$$m \ddot{y} + b \dot{y} + k y = F_{Cor,y}(t)$$

In steady state, the displacement amplitude is:

$$Y = \frac{2 m A \omega_d \Omega_z}{k} \cdot \frac{\omega_d/\omega_n}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

where $r = \omega_d/\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ is damping ratio. At resonance ($r=1$), this simplifies to:

$$Y = \frac{2 m A \omega_d \Omega_z}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{m A \omega_d \Omega_z}{k \zeta}$$

**Step 5: Express the sensitivity.**

The **scale factor** $S$ of the gyroscope (displacement per unit rotation rate) is:

$$S = \frac{Y}{\Omega_z} = \frac{m A \omega_d}{k \zeta}$$

Higher drive amplitude $A$, higher drive frequency $\omega_d$, lower stiffness $k$, and lower damping $\zeta$ all increase sensitivity.

## 3.4 Derivation: Sagnac Effect for Optical Gyroscopes

**Step 1: Consider a circular ring of radius $R$ rotating at angular velocity $\Omega$.**

Two light beams travel in opposite directions around the ring.

**Step 2: Compute the path length of the co-rotating beam.**

A point on the ring moves with tangential velocity $v = \Omega R$. The beam's effective path length increases:

$$L_{CW} = 2\pi R + \Delta L$$

**Step 3: The counter-rotating beam has a shorter path.**

$$L_{CCW} = 2\pi R - \Delta L$$

**Step 4: Compute the path difference.**

The time for the ring to complete one rotation is $T = 2\pi/\Omega$. During this time, the ring moves a distance $2\pi R$ in the direction of rotation. The path difference is:

$$\Delta L = 2\pi R \cdot \frac{v}{c} = 2\pi R \cdot \frac{\Omega R}{c} = \frac{2\pi R^2 \Omega}{c}$$

**Step 5: Convert path difference to phase difference.**

The phase shift is $\phi = 2\pi L/\lambda$, so:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi \Delta L}{\lambda} = \frac{4\pi^2 R^2 \Omega}{c \lambda}$$

**Step 6: Generalize to a loop of arbitrary area $A = \pi R^2$.**

Substituting $R^2 = A/\pi$:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{4\pi A \Omega}{c \lambda}$$

This is the **Sagnac phase shift**. For a fiber-optic gyroscope with $N$ turns, the effect is amplified:

$$\Delta\phi_{FOG} = \frac{4\pi N A \Omega}{c \lambda}$$

This derivation explains why high-performance inertial navigation uses ring laser or fiber-optic gyroscopes — the Sagnac effect provides a direct, drift-free measure of rotation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Gyroscope Types Comparison

| **Property** | **Mechanical** | **MEMS** | **Fiber-Optic** | **Ring Laser** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Angular momentum | Coriolis force | Sagnac effect | Sagnac effect |
| **Bias Drift** | 0.01–1 °/h | 1–100 °/h | 0.001–0.1 °/h | < 0.001 °/h |
| **Size** | Large (cm) | Tiny (mm) | Medium (cm) | Large (cm) |
| **Power** | High (W) | Low (mW) | Medium | High (W) |
| **Cost** | High | Low (~$1) | High | Very High |
| **Max Rate** | ~10⁴ °/s | ~2000 °/s | ~500 °/s | ~10⁶ °/s |
| **Shock Tolerance** | Low | High | Medium | Low |
| **Mobile Robot Use** | Rare | **Very Common** | High-end AGVs | Aerospace/Defense |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | Conceptual | **High** | Conceptual | Rare |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gyroscope Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["ANGULAR MOTION INPUT"] --> nodeB["PROOF MASS / SPIN ROTOR / LIGHT BEAM"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TRANSDUCTION MECHANISM"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CORIOLIS FORCE / PRECESSION / SAGNAC SHIFT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERSION"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["BIAS COMPENSATION AND CALIBRATION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["INTEGRATION ALGORITHM"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ORIENTATION OUTPUT ROLL PITCH YAW"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ROBOT CONTROLLER"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["ACTUATOR COMMAND VELOCITY STEERING"]
```

## 4.2 MEMS Gyroscope Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["DRIVE OSCILLATOR"] --> nodeB["COMB DRIVE ACTUATOR"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PROOF MASS X OSCILLATION"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ROTATION OMEGA Z APPLIED"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CORIOLIS FORCE Y DIRECTION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["SENSE COMB FINGERS"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["CAPACITANCE CHANGE DELTA C"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["CHARGE AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["DEMODULATOR REFERENCE"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["LOW PASS FILTER"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["ADC"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["DIGITAL OUTPUT OMEGA Z RAD PER S"]
```

## 4.3 Gyroscope Data Processing Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["RAW GYROSCOPE MEASUREMENT"] --> nodeB["BIAS ESTIMATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["NOISE FILTERING"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["CALIBRATED ANGULAR RATE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["COORDINATE TRANSFORMATION BODY TO WORLD"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["INTEGRATION EULER OR RUNGE KUTTA"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["EULER ANGLES OR QUATERNION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["KALMAN FILTER FUSION WITH ACCELEROMETER"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["FINAL ORIENTATION ESTIMATE"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ROBOT POSE UPDATE"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Gyroscopes (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph subgraphA["OPEN LOOP MEMS GYRO"]
        nodeA1["INPUT OMEGA"] --> nodeA2["CORIOLIS TRANSDUCTION"]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3["DIRECT VOLTAGE OUTPUT"]
        nodeA3 --> nodeA4["ADC"]
    end
    subgraph subgraphB["CLOSED LOOP MEMS GYRO FORCE BALANCE"]
        nodeB1["INPUT OMEGA"] --> nodeB2["CORIOLIS TRANSDUCTION"]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3["SENSE ELEMENT"]
        nodeB3 --> nodeB4["CONTROL LOOP"]
        nodeB4 --> nodeB5["FEEDBACK ACTUATOR"]
        nodeB5 --> nodeB6["NULL FORCE CONDITION"]
        nodeB6 --> nodeB7["ADC"]
        nodeB4 -. feedback .-> nodeB5
    end
```

