## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Bayes' Rule to the Bayes Filter

**Goal**: Derive the recursive Bayesian update for a mobile robot operating in a Markovian environment.

**Assumptions**:
- The environment is **Markovian**: the future state depends only on the current state and control, not on history.
- The measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$.

#### Step 1 — Start with the desired posterior
We want $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, the probability distribution over the current state given all measurements and controls up to time $t$.

#### Step 2 — Apply the definition of conditional probability
By definition of conditional probability, conditioning on $z_t$ and $u_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \\
&= p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_t, u_{1:t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem
Bayes' theorem separates the likelihood from the prior:

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Markov assumption
The measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ (not on past measurements or controls):

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

#### Step 5 — Simplify the prior term
The prior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ can be integrated over the previous state $x_{t-1}$ using the **law of total probability**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Apply Markov again and recognize the recursion
By the Markov property, $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ (the **motion model**).
The second term $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is simply the previous belief $bel(x_{t-1})$.

#### Step 7 — Assemble the final recursion
Combining everything and letting $\eta = 1/p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ be the normalization constant:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &= \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \quad \text{(Prediction)} \\
bel(x_t) &= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) \quad \text{(Correction)}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is the **Bayes Filter** — the mathematical foundation of all data interpretation in mobile robotics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Kalman Filter as a Special Case of the Bayes Filter

**Goal**: Show that the Kalman Filter arises from the Bayes Filter when all distributions are Gaussian and the models are linear.

#### Step 1 — State the assumptions
- **Motion model**: $x_t = A x_{t-1} + B u_t + w_t$, where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$.
- **Measurement model**: $z_t = H x_t + v_t$, where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.
- **Initial belief**: $bel(x_0) \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{x}_0, P_0)$.

A multivariate Gaussian is parameterized by mean $\mu$ and covariance $\Sigma$:

$$
p(x) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)\right)
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the prediction (motion update)
The convolution of two Gaussians is a Gaussian. Mean and covariance propagate as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_t^{-} &= A \hat{x}_{t-1} + B u_t \\
P_t^{-} &= A P_{t-1} A^T + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-term $A P_{t-1} A^T$ captures how the previous state's uncertainty is **amplified** by the dynamics. The additive $Q$ represents new uncertainty introduced by the process noise $w_t$.

#### Step 3 — Compute the innovation (measurement residual)
The **innovation** is the difference between the actual measurement and the predicted measurement:

$$
\nu_t = z_t - H \hat{x}_t^{-}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the innovation covariance
The covariance of the innovation is:

$$
S_t = H P_t^{-} H^T + R
$$

The two terms add: $H P_t^{-} H^T$ is the predicted measurement uncertainty propagated through the measurement matrix, and $R$ is the sensor's intrinsic noise.

#### Step 5 — Compute the Kalman gain
The Kalman gain $K_t$ is chosen to **minimize the posterior covariance** $P_t$. Solving $\partial P_t / \partial K_t = 0$ yields:

$$
K_t = P_t^{-} H^T S_t^{-1} = P_t^{-} H^T (H P_t^{-} H^T + R)^{-1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for $K_t$**: When $R$ is small (trustworthy sensor), $K_t \to P_t^{-} H^T (H P_t^{-} H^T)^{-1}$, meaning we trust the measurement fully. When $R$ is large (noisy sensor), $K_t \to 0$, meaning we ignore the measurement and rely on prediction. **The Kalman gain is the optimal weighting between prediction and measurement.**

#### Step 6 — State update
The posterior mean is the prior mean plus a correction proportional to the innovation:

$$
\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^{-} + K_t \nu_t = \hat{x}_t^{-} + K_t (z_t - H \hat{x}_t^{-})
$$

#### Step 7 — Covariance update
The posterior covariance shrinks after incorporating the measurement:

$$
P_t = (I - K_t H) P_t^{-}
$$

**Final Kalman Filter Equations (boxed for KTU recall):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Prediction:} \\
&\hat{x}_t^{-} = A \hat{x}_{t-1} + B u_t \\
&P_t^{-} = A P_{t-1} A^T + Q \\
&\textbf{Correction:} \\
&K_t = P_t^{-} H^T (H P_t^{-} H^T + R)^{-1} \\
&\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^{-} + K_t (z_t - H \hat{x}_t^{-}) \\
&P_t = (I - K_t H) P_t^{-}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A mobile robot has state $x = [p, v]^T$ (position, velocity). The motion model is $x_t = A x_{t-1}$ with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. A GPS sensor measures only position: $z_t = H x_t$ with $H = [1, 0]$. Given: $\hat{x}_0 = [0, 0]^T$, $P_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, $R = [0.5]$, and $z_1 = 1.2$ m.

**Step 1 — Prediction**:

$$
\hat{x}_1^{-} = A \hat{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P_1^{-} = A P_0 A^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P_1^{-} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation covariance**:

$$
S_1 = H P_1^{-} H^T + R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 = 2.1 + 0.5 = 2.6
$$

**Step 3 — Kalman gain**:

$$
K_1 = P_1^{-} H^T S_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2.6} = \frac{1}{2.6}\begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — State update**:

$$
\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_1^{-} + K_1 (z_1 - H \hat{x}_1^{-}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix}(1.2 - 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.969 \\ 0.462 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Covariance update**:

$$
P_1 = (I - K_1 H) P_1^{-} = \left(\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\right)\begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.192 & 0 \\ -0.385 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.404 & 0.192 \\ -0.194 & 0.675 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The estimated position after one update is **0.969 m** with a position variance of **0.404 m²** — substantially less uncertain than the initial variance of **1.0 m²**, confirming that the GPS measurement reduced our uncertainty.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Data Interpretation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sensor Data] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction]
    C --> D[Sensor Model p(z|x)]
    D --> E[Bayesian Inference Engine]
    F[Prior Belief bel xt-1] --> E
    E --> G[Posterior Belief bel xt]
    G --> H{Output Decision}
    H -->|High Confidence| I[Action / Map Update]
    H -->|Low Confidence| J[Request More Data]
    G --> K[State Estimate x hat]
    K --> L[Robot Controller]
```

### 4.2 Kalman Filter Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Motion Model A, B, Q] --> P1[State Prediction x hat t neg]
    M2[Previous Covariance P t-1] --> P2[Covariance Prediction P t neg]
    M1 --> P1
    P1 --> C1[Innovation nu t]
    S1[Sensor Measurement z t] --> C1
    P2 --> K1[Kalman Gain K t]
    M3[Measurement Model H, R] --> K1
    K1 --> U1[State Update x hat t]
    C1 --> U1
    K1 --> U2[Covariance Update P t]
    P2 --> U2
    U1 --> OUT1[Optimal State Estimate]
    U2 --> OUT2[Updated Uncertainty]
```

### 4.3 Multi-Sensor Data Fusion Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[LIDAR Sensor] --> F1[Range Data Processing]
    L2[Camera Sensor] --> F2[Image Feature Extraction]
    L3[IMU Sensor] --> F3[Inertial Data Processing]
    L4[GPS Sensor] --> F4[Position Data Processing]
    F1 --> LL1[Low-Level Fusion Layer]
    F2 --> LL1
    LL1 --> FF1[Feature-Level Fusion]
    F3 --> FF1
    F4 --> FF1
    FF1 --> DD1[Decision-Level Fusion]
    DD1 --> OO1[Final Interpreted State]
    OO1 --> RR1[Robot Decision Module]
```

### 4.4 Bayesian Update Cycle (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Start with Prior bel x0] --> ST2[Receive Control u t]
    ST2 --> ST3[Predict bel bar x t via Motion Model]
    ST3 --> ST4[Receive Measurement z t]
    ST4 --> ST5[Apply Sensor Model p z t given x t]
    ST5 --> ST6[Compute Posterior bel x t]
    ST6 --> ST7{Loop Continue}
    ST7 -->|Yes| ST2
    ST7 -->|No| ST8[Output Final Belief]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note**: All diagrams above are functional block-architecture flows. They map the *information flow* and *computational interactions* between modules, which is the focus of KTU Module 2 "Data Interpretation" rather than the physical wiring of a robot.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define sensor measurement model and explain the Gaussian noise model for a range sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
A sensor measurement model describes the probabilistic relationship between the true world state $x$ and the measurement $z$ returned by the sensor, expressed as the conditional probability $p(z \mid x)$. **(1 mark)**

For a range sensor measuring a true distance $d$, the measurement $z$ is corrupted by additive Gaussian noise $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$: **(1 mark)**

$$p(z \mid d) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z - d)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

The parameter $\sigma$ represents the standard deviation of the sensor's noise in meters. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Kalman gain? Explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The Kalman gain $K_t$ is a weighting matrix that determines how much the new measurement $z_t$ influences the updated state estimate relative to the prediction. It is computed as: **(1 mark)**

$$K_t = P_t^{-} H^T (H P_t^{-} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

**Physical significance (2 marks)**:
- When the measurement noise $R$ is small (sensor is accurate), $K_t$ is large, and the filter trusts the measurement heavily.
- When $R$ is large (sensor is noisy), $K_t$ is small, and the filter relies more on the prediction from the motion model.
- $K_t$ optimally minimizes the posterior covariance $P_t$, making the Kalman filter the **best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often forget to include the process noise $Q$ in the covariance prediction. ($P_t^{-} = A P_{t-1} A^T + Q$ — omitting $Q$ loses 1–2 marks.)
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing the **prior** $p(x)$ with the **likelihood** $p(z \mid x)$ in Bayes' theorem applications. Remember: the **measurement model is the likelihood**.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Writing the Kalman Filter state update as $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^{-} + K_t z_t$ instead of the correct $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^{-} + K_t (z_t - H \hat{x}_t^{-})$. The **innovation** $z_t - H\hat{x}_t^{-}$ is critical.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Failing to specify the units of all variables in numerical problems. KTU examiners award marks for unit consistency.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the recursive Bayes Filter equations from first principles, clearly stating the Markov assumption and the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot uses a 1D Kalman filter to estimate its position $p$ and velocity $v$. The state is $x = [p, v]^T$, the motion model is $x_t = A x_{t-1}$ with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ where $\Delta t = 1$ s, the measurement model is $z_t = H x_t$ with $H = [1, 0]$, and $R = 0.2$. Given initial conditions $\hat{x}_0 = [0, 0]^T$, $P_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}$, and the measurement $z_1 = 1.0$ m, compute the full Kalman filter update (state and covariance) for $t = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A**:

**(a) Derivation of Bayes Filter (7 marks):**

**Step 1** — Define belief as the posterior over state: $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2** — Apply Bayes' theorem:

$$
bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3** — Apply the **Markov assumption** (measurement depends only on current state): $p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4** — Expand the prior using the law of total probability over $x_{t-1}$:

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5** — Combine and define $\eta$ as the normalizer. Final recursion:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}
$$
$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(b) Kalman Filter Numerical Update (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Prediction with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\hat{x}_1^{-} = A \hat{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Covariance prediction:** **(1 mark)**

$$
P_1^{-} = A P_0 A^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance:** **(1 mark)**

$$
S_1 = H P_1^{-} H^T + R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.2 = 2.01 + 0.2 = 2.21
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain:** **(1 mark)**

$$
K_1 = P_1^{-} H^T S_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.01 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2.21} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.910 \\ 0.452 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — State update:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\hat{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.910 \\ 0.452 \end{bmatrix}(1.0 - 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.910 \\ 0.452 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Covariance update:** **(2 marks)**

$$
P_1 = (I - K_1 H) P_1^{-} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.910 & 0 \\ -0.452 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.090 & 0 \\ -0.452 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2.01 & 1 \\ 1 & 1.01 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.181 & 0.090 \\ -0.397 & 0.558 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final Result**: $\hat{x}_1 = [0.910, 0.452]^T$, $P_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.181 & 0.090 \\ -0.397 & 0.558 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three levels of sensor data fusion with suitable mobile robotics examples. Compare the advantages and limitations of each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **Particle Filter** for data interpretation. Derive the importance weight update equation and discuss the **particle degeneracy** problem with its standard solution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B**:

**(a) Three Levels of Sensor Data Fusion (7 marks):**

**1. Low-Level (Raw Data) Fusion (2.5 marks):**
Raw data from multiple sensors is combined at the signal level before any feature extraction. Example: projecting lidar point cloud onto camera image pixels to create a colored 3D representation. **Advantage**: Maximum information preservation. **Limitation**: Requires precise sensor calibration and synchronization; high computational cost.

**2. Feature-Level Fusion (2.5 marks):**
Each sensor independently extracts features (e.g., edges from camera, planes from lidar), and these feature sets are merged. Example: combining SIFT features from a camera with planar segments from a lidar for object recognition. **Advantage**: Balanced trade-off between information retention and computational cost. **Limitation**: Loss of information during feature extraction; requires robust feature association.

**3. Decision-Level (High-Level) Fusion (2 marks):**
Each sensor produces an independent decision (e.g., object classification), and these are combined via voting or Bayesian methods. Example: two independent classifiers (camera-based and lidar-based) voting on whether an obstacle is a pedestrian. **Advantage**: Modular, robust to single-sensor failure. **Limitation**: Throws away inter-sensor correlations; less accurate than low-level fusion.

---

**(b) Particle Filter Working (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Basis (1 mark):** A particle filter represents the belief $bel(x_t)$ as a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, each particle being a hypothesis about the true state.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Prediction**: For each particle, sample from the motion model: $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$.
2. **Weight Update**: Compute importance weight based on measurement likelihood: $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$.
3. **Normalization**: $w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow w_t^{[i]} / \sum_j w_t^{[j]}$.
4. **Resampling**: Draw $N$ new particles with replacement, with probability proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$.

**Derivation of Weight Update (2 marks):** Since sampling directly from $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t})$ is intractable, we sample from a **proposal distribution** $\pi(x_t)$ and correct via importance sampling:

$$
w_t^{[i]} \propto \frac{p(x_t^{[i]} \mid z_{1:t})}{\pi(x_t^{[i]})} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}^{[i]})
$$

For the common choice $\pi = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, this simplifies to $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$.

**Particle Degeneracy and Solution (1 mark):** After several iterations, most particles have negligible weights while a few carry almost all the probability mass — this is **particle degeneracy**. The standard solution is **resampling** (e.g., systematic resampling), triggered when the effective particle count $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ falls below a threshold (typically $N/2$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Data Interpretation**

- **Data Interpretation** converts raw, noisy sensor measurements into structured, actionable information about the robot and its environment.
- The **Bayes Filter** is the foundational recursive estimator: it has a **prediction** step (using the motion model) and a **correction** step (using the sensor model).
- The **Gaussian sensor model** is $p(z \mid x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-(z-x)^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ — write this from memory.
- The **Markov assumption** is critical: the future depends only on the present, not the entire history.
- The **Kalman Filter** is a special case of the Bayes Filter under **linear-Gaussian assumptions**. Its five core equations are: state prediction, covariance prediction, Kalman gain, state update, covariance update.
- The **Kalman gain** $K_t = P_t^{-} H^T (H P_t^{-} H^T + R)^{-1}$ acts as an optimal weighting between prediction and measurement.
- For **non-linear systems**, the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes via Jacobians, and the **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** uses the unscented transform.
- The **Particle Filter** handles **non-Gaussian, non-linear** systems using weighted samples; weight $\propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$.
- **Particle degeneracy** is solved by **resampling** based on the **effective sample size** $N_{\text{eff}}$.
- **Data fusion** occurs at three levels: **low-level** (raw), **feature-level**, and **decision-level** — each with distinct trade-offs.
- The **Mahalanobis distance** is used for outlier rejection and data association in multi-target tracking.
- The **innovation** (or measurement residual) $\nu_t = z_t - H\hat{x}_t^{-}$ is the driving force of the Kalman update.
- **Process noise $Q$** and **measurement noise $R$** must always be specified; omitting $Q$ is a common KTU mark-loss pitfall.
- Real-world applications include **SLAM**, **autonomous vehicles**, **drone stabilization**, and **medical robotics**.
- Always carry **units** through every numerical problem; KTU examiners check for dimensional consistency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Range Finders - Data Fusion: Kalman Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laser Range Finders - Data Fusion: Kalman Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Laser Range Finders (LRF)

A **Laser Range Finder (LRF)** is an active electro-optical sensing device that determines the distance to a target object by measuring the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** of a short, coherent laser pulse emitted toward the target and reflected back to a photodetector. In mobile robotics, 2D LiDARs (Light Detection and Ranging) are most prevalent, producing a 2D scan of the environment in a single horizontal plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** "A Laser Range Finder is a non-contact distance measurement sensor that exploits the constant propagation velocity of an emitted laser beam in free space ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) to compute range from the round-trip propagation delay."

**Principle of Range Computation:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

Where:
- $d$ = measured range to obstacle (m)
- $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- $\Delta t$ = round-trip time (s)
- Division by 2 accounts for the **two-way (outward and return) propagation path**.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Bats and Echo-Location:** Imagine you are blindfolded in a dark room and you shout toward a wall. The time gap between your shout and its returning echo tells you how far the wall is. A Laser Range Finder does the **exact same thing** but with light waves (laser) instead of sound, and at roughly **a million times faster** speed. Since light travels at $3 \times 10^8$ m/s, the time intervals are in nanoseconds — which is why LRF electronics must operate at extremely high speed.

**Common LRF Architectures Used in Mobile Robots:**

| Architecture | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1D LRF (ToF) | Single beam, stationary | Distance to single obstacle |
| 2D Scanner | Rotating mirror + ToF | Indoor mapping, SLAM |
| 3D LiDAR | Multi-beam or oscillating unit | Autonomous vehicles, terrain mapping |
| Phase-shift LRF | Measures phase of modulated beam | Short-range high precision |

---

## 1.2 Data Fusion

**Data Fusion** is the systematic process of integrating data from multiple heterogeneous sensors to produce a single, more accurate, more reliable, and more complete estimate of the system state than any individual sensor could provide alone. In mobile robotics, the platform's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ is typically estimated by fusing wheel odometry, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit), GPS, and **Laser Range Finder** scans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** "Data fusion is the probabilistic combination of redundant or complementary sensory information to obtain a consistent, low-uncertainty representation of the robot's state and environment."

### Intuitive Analogy for Data Fusion

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Doctor's Diagnosis:** When a doctor diagnoses a patient, they do not rely on a single test. They combine results from a stethoscope, blood test, X-ray, and patient history. Each test has a different uncertainty profile. By intelligently **weighting each test's reliability**, the doctor arrives at a more confident diagnosis. **Sensor Data Fusion does the same thing for robots** — it weighs each sensor's measurement by its uncertainty (covariance) to produce a final, more confident estimate.

---

## 1.3 The Kalman Filter

The **Kalman Filter (KF)** is an optimal, recursive, linear, discrete-time state estimator developed by **Rudolf E. Kálmán in 1960**. It estimates the hidden state of a linear dynamic system perturbed by Gaussian white noise by using a mathematical model of the system combined with noisy measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** "The Kalman Filter is an optimal recursive data processing algorithm that minimizes the mean-squared estimation error of a linear system under Gaussian noise assumptions, by combining a *predictive model* with *noisy measurements* using a weighted blending factor called the Kalman Gain."

### Intuitive Analogy for the Kalman Filter

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — GPS While Driving:** Imagine driving a car with two sources of position information: (1) your **speedometer** integrated over time (a prediction model), and (2) a **GPS** receiver (noisy measurement). The speedometer drifts over time (cumulative error), and the GPS jitters around the true position (random error). The Kalman Filter intelligently **fuses these two** — trusting the speedometer for short-term motion, trusting the GPS for long-term accuracy — and outputs a single smooth, accurate position estimate. **The Kalman Gain decides which one to trust more, in real time.**

### Why the Kalman Filter is "Optimal"

The Kalman Filter is mathematically **optimal** under three simultaneous conditions:
1. The system is **linear**.
2. The process and measurement noises are **white, zero-mean, and Gaussian**.
3. The performance metric is the **mean squared error (MSE)**.

When these conditions are met, the Kalman Filter produces estimates that are **unbiased** and of **minimum variance** — no other linear filter can do better.

---

## 1.4 Why Combine LRF Data with Kalman Filter?

A mobile robot's environment is sensed by multiple sensors, each with unique noise characteristics:

| Sensor | Strength | Weakness |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Wheel Odometry | Short-term, high-frequency accuracy | Unbounded drift, slip errors |
| IMU | High-frequency dynamics | Bias drift, integration error |
| GPS | Globally bounded error | Low frequency, indoor blind spots |
| LRF | Accurate geometric landmarks | Sparse feature dependency |

The **Kalman Filter** provides the **mathematical scaffold** to combine these heterogeneous data sources, with the LRF providing geometric constraints that correct odometric drift.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Laser Range Finder — Operational Pipeline

A 2D laser range finder executes the following logical steps in each measurement cycle:

1. **Pulse Emission:** A semiconductor laser diode (typically $\lambda = 905$ nm or $1550$ nm) emits a short nanosecond pulse.
2. **Beam Steering:** A rotating mirror or MEMS device deflects the beam to a new azimuth angle $\theta_i$.
3. **Time-of-Flight Measurement:** A high-speed photodiode detects the return pulse; a TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) measures $\Delta t_i$.
4. **Range Computation:** Using $d_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i / 2$.
5. **Scan Assembly:** All $(d_i, \theta_i)$ pairs are concatenated into a **scan vector** $Z_k = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n\}$ at scan $k$.
6. **Coordinate Transformation:** Each range reading is mapped to Cartesian: $x_i = d_i \cos \theta_i$, $y_i = d_i \sin \theta_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> The LRF provides a point cloud that, in the context of SLAM and data fusion, serves as the **measurement input $z_k$** to the Kalman Filter.

---

## 2.2 The Kalman Filter — The Five Recursive Equations

The Kalman Filter operates in a continuous **predict–measure–update** cycle. Here is the complete theoretical breakdown.

### A. The Linear Dynamic System Model

A discrete-time linear system is described by two equations:

**State Equation (Predictive Model):**
$$x_k = F_k \, x_{k-1} + B_k \, u_k + w_{k-1}$$

**Measurement Equation (Observation Model):**
$$z_k = H_k \, x_k + v_k$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimensions |
|--------|---------|------------|
| $x_k$ | System state vector at time $k$ | $n \times 1$ |
| $F_k$ | State transition matrix | $n \times n$ |
| $B_k$ | Control input matrix | $n \times m$ |
| $u_k$ | Control input vector | $m \times 1$ |
| $w_{k-1}$ | Process noise (Gaussian, $Q$) | $n \times 1$ |
| $z_k$ | Measurement vector | $m \times 1$ |
| $H_k$ | Observation matrix | $m \times n$ |
| $v_k$ | Measurement noise (Gaussian, $R$) | $m \times 1$ |

**Noise Assumptions:**
$$w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k), \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$$

$$E[w_k w_j^T] = Q_k \delta_{kj}, \quad E[v_k v_j^T] = R_k \delta_{kj}, \quad E[w_k v_j^T] = 0$$

---

### B. The Two-Phase Recursive Algorithm

**Phase 1 — Time Update (Prediction):** Project state and covariance forward in time.

$$\boxed{\hat{x}_k^{-} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1} + B_k u_k}$$

$$\boxed{P_k^{-} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k}$$

**Phase 2 — Measurement Update (Correction):** Refine the prediction using sensor data.

$$\boxed{K_k = P_k^{-} H_k^T \left( H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k \right)^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^{-} + K_k \left( z_k - H_k \hat{x}_k^{-} \right)}$$

$$\boxed{P_k = \left( I - K_k H_k \right) P_k^{-}}$$

---

### C. Terminology Table — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these definitions for the university exam.**

| Term | Mathematical Form | Plain-English Meaning |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Prior estimate** | $\hat{x}_k^{-}$ | State estimate **before** using measurement at $k$ |
| **Posterior estimate** | $\hat{x}_k$ | State estimate **after** using measurement at $k$ |
| **Prior covariance** | $P_k^{-}$ | Uncertainty **before** measurement |
| **Posterior covariance** | $P_k$ | Uncertainty **after** measurement |
| **Innovation / Residual** | $\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H_k \hat{x}_k^{-}$ | New information brought by the measurement |
| **Innovation covariance** | $S_k = H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k$ | Total uncertainty of the residual |
| **Kalman Gain** | $K_k$ | Optimal weighting factor: $K \to 1$ trusts measurement, $K \to 0$ trusts model |
| **Process noise covariance** | $Q_k$ | Model uncertainty |
| **Measurement noise covariance** | $R_k$ | Sensor noise level |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight on Kalman Gain:** As $R_k \to 0$ (sensor very accurate), $K_k \to H_k^{-1}$ (fully trust measurement). As $Q_k \to 0$ (model very accurate), $K_k \to 0$ (fully trust prediction). This is the **intelligent blending** property.

---

## 2.3 LRF-Driven Kalman Filter for Mobile Robot Localization

Consider a differential-drive robot localized in a 2D plane. The state vector is:

$$x_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \theta_k \end{bmatrix}$$

### Linearized Discrete Motion Model

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \theta_k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_{k-1} \\ y_{k-1} \\ \theta_{k-1} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \Delta s \cos\theta_{k-1} \\ \Delta s \sin\theta_{k-1} \\ \Delta\theta \end{bmatrix} + w_{k-1}$$

The Jacobian of the motion model w.r.t. state is:

$$F_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin\theta_{k-1} \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta s \cos\theta_{k-1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### LRF Measurement Model

When the LRF observes a known landmark at map position $(l_x, l_y)$ from robot pose $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$ with bearing angle $\phi$:

$$z_k = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_x - x_k)^2 + (l_y - y_k)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_y - y_k, l_x - x_k) - \theta_k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_k \\ \phi_k \end{bmatrix} + v_k$$

The corresponding Jacobian (observation matrix) is:

$$H_k = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x_k - l_x}{r_k} & \frac{y_k - l_y}{r_k} & 0 \\[4pt] \frac{l_y - y_k}{r_k^2} & \frac{x_k - l_x}{r_k^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because the measurement model is **nonlinear** (square root, atan2), a direct Kalman Filter is insufficient. We must use the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, which linearizes about the current estimate using Jacobians. This is a **high-yield KTU 2024 concept**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of LRF + Kalman Fusion |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | LiDAR scan matching corrects GPS/IMU drift |
| **Warehouse AGVs** | LRF landmarks correct wheel-odometry cumulative error |
| **Search & Rescue Drones** | LiDAR + IMU fusion through EKF for GPS-denied navigation |
| **SLAM** | LRF scans sequentially fused to build and localize on a map |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kalman Filter is the **mathematical heart** of nearly every modern robotic localization system. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the intersection of **Laser Range Finders** and **Kalman-based Data Fusion** is a **high-weightage Module 2 topic**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Gain $K_k$

The Kalman Filter minimizes the **posterior error covariance trace** $E[\|x_k - \hat{x}_k\|^2]$. Let us derive the optimal $K_k$.

### Step 1 — Define the Posterior Error

The error between true and estimated state is:
$$e_k = x_k - \hat{x}_k$$

Substituting the update equation:
$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^{-} + K_k (z_k - H_k \hat{x}_k^{-})$$

Using $z_k = H_k x_k + v_k$:

$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^{-} + K_k (H_k x_k + v_k - H_k \hat{x}_k^{-})$$

$$= \hat{x}_k^{-} + K_k H_k (x_k - \hat{x}_k^{-}) + K_k v_k$$

So the error is:
$$e_k = x_k - \hat{x}_k = (I - K_k H_k)(x_k - \hat{x}_k^{-}) - K_k v_k$$

### Step 2 — Posterior Covariance

$$P_k = E[e_k e_k^T]$$

Expanding:
$$P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-} (I - K_k H_k)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

(The cross-term $E[(x_k - \hat{x}_k^{-})v_k^T] = 0$ because $v_k$ is independent and zero-mean.)

### Step 3 — Minimize the Trace

Expanding the first term:
$$P_k = P_k^{-} - K_k H_k P_k^{-} - P_k^{-} H_k^T K_k^T + K_k (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k) K_k^T$$

Taking the trace and differentiating w.r.t. $K_k$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \text{tr}(P_k)}{\partial K_k} = -2 (P_k^{-} H_k^T)^T + 2 K_k (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k) = 0$$

Solving for $K_k$:

$$K_k (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k) = P_k^{-} H_k^T$$

$$\boxed{K_k = P_k^{-} H_k^T (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **optimal Kalman Gain** — it minimizes $E[\|x_k - \hat{x}_k\|^2]$ under the linear-Gaussian assumptions. Derivation appears regularly in KTU Module 2 long-answer questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Posterior Covariance Simplification

A widely-used KTU exam result is the simplified form of $P_k$.

Starting from:
$$P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-} (I - K_k H_k)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

Substitute $K_k = P_k^{-} H_k^T S_k^{-1}$ where $S_k = H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k$:

$$P_k = P_k^{-} - P_k^{-} H_k^T S_k^{-1} H_k P_k^{-} - P_k^{-} H_k^T S_k^{-1} H_k P_k^{-} + P_k^{-} H_k^T S_k^{-1} (S_k - H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T) S_k^{-1} H_k P_k^{-}$$

The last term simplifies to $P_k^{-} H_k^T S_k^{-1} H_k P_k^{-}$. Thus:

$$P_k = P_k^{-} - P_k^{-} H_k^T S_k^{-1} H_k P_k^{-}$$

$$\boxed{P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-}}$$

This is the **numerically stable** and **commonly tested** form.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: 1D Robot Tracking with LRF and Odometry

A robot moves along the x-axis. State: $x_k$ (position). It has two sources of information:

- **Odometry model** (process): $x_k = x_{k-1} + u_k + w_k$, where $u_k = 1$ m, $Q = 0.1$.
- **LRF measurement** (sensor): $z_k = x_k + v_k$, where $H = 1$, $R = 0.5$.

**Initial conditions:** $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1.0$.

We will run **two KF iterations** by hand.

### Iteration 1 (k = 1)

**Step 1 — Predict:**

$$\hat{x}_1^{-} = \hat{x}_0 + u_1 = 0 + 1 = 1$$

$$P_1^{-} = P_0 + Q = 1.0 + 0.1 = 1.1$$

**Step 2 — Compute Kalman Gain:**

$$K_1 = \frac{P_1^{-}}{P_1^{-} + R} = \frac{1.1}{1.1 + 0.5} = \frac{1.1}{1.6} = 0.6875$$

**Step 3 — Update with LRF measurement** $z_1 = 1.2$ m:

$$\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_1^{-} + K_1 (z_1 - \hat{x}_1^{-}) = 1 + 0.6875 \cdot (1.2 - 1) = 1 + 0.6875 \cdot 0.2 = 1.1375 \text{ m}$$

$$P_1 = (1 - K_1) P_1^{-} = (1 - 0.6875) \cdot 1.1 = 0.3125 \cdot 1.1 = 0.34375$$

> [!NOTE]
> The estimate $1.1375$ lies **between the prediction ($1.0$) and the measurement ($1.2$)**, weighted by the Kalman Gain $0.6875$. This is the **optimal blending**.

### Iteration 2 (k = 2)

**Step 1 — Predict:**

$$\hat{x}_2^{-} = \hat{x}_1 + u_2 = 1.1375 + 1 = 2.1375$$

$$P_2^{-} = P_1 + Q = 0.34375 + 0.1 = 0.44375$$

**Step 2 — Compute Kalman Gain:**

$$K_2 = \frac{0.44375}{0.44375 + 0.5} = \frac{0.44375}{0.94375} = 0.4702$$

**Step 3 — Update with LRF measurement** $z_2 = 2.5$ m:

$$\hat{x}_2 = 2.1375 + 0.4702 \cdot (2.5 - 2.1375) = 2.1375 + 0.1702 = 2.3077 \text{ m}$$

$$P_2 = (1 - 0.4702) \cdot 0.44375 = 0.5298 \cdot 0.44375 = 0.2350$$

### Iteration Summary Table

| $k$ | $\hat{x}_k^{-}$ | $P_k^{-}$ | $K_k$ | $z_k$ | $\hat{x}_k$ | $P_k$ |
|-----|-----------------|-----------|-------|-------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | 1.0000 | 1.1000 | 0.6875 | 1.20 | 1.1375 | 0.3438 |
| 2 | 2.1375 | 0.4438 | 0.4702 | 2.50 | 2.3077 | 0.2350 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Notice how the covariance $P_k$ **decreases monotonically** ($1.0 \to 0.34 \to 0.24$). This represents the system's growing confidence in its estimates as more data is fused.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Filter?

| Scenario | Suitable Filter | Reason |
|----------|-----------------|--------|
| Linear dynamics, linear measurements, Gaussian noise | **Standard Kalman Filter (KF)** | Closed-form optimality holds |
| Nonlinear dynamics or measurements, mild nonlinearity | **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Linearized via Jacobians |
| Strong nonlinearity, non-Gaussian noise | **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** or **Particle Filter** | Captures higher-order moments or non-Gaussian densities |
| LRF scan matching for SLAM | **EKF-SLAM or FastSLAM** | Landmark-based probabilistic map |
| 2D LRF + IMU/odometry indoor | **EKF** | Industry standard, computationally efficient |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Laser Range Finder — Operational Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Laser Diode Driver] --> B[Pulse Generation at wavelength 905 nm or 1550 nm]
    B --> C[Rotating MEMS Mirror or Polygon Scanner]
    C --> D[Beam Projects to Environment at Angle theta]
    D --> E[Target Object Reflects Beam]
    E --> F[Photodiode Detector Receives Return Pulse]
    F --> G[Time to Digital Converter TDC measures delta t]
    G --> H[Range Computation Module d equals c times delta t over 2]
    H --> I[Scan Assembly Forms Vector z k of n range readings]
    I --> J[Coordinate Transform to x y Cartesian]
    J --> K[LRF Output Point Cloud]

    K --> L[Input to Kalman Filter Measurement Model]
```

## 4.2 Kalman Filter — Recursive Predict-Measure-Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Initial State x0 and Covariance P0] --> P2[State Prediction xk minus equals F xk minus 1 plus B uk]
    P3[Process Noise Q] --> P4[Covariance Prediction Pk minus equals F Pk minus 1 FT plus Q]
    P2 --> P5[Innovation y tilde equals zk minus H xk minus]
    P4 --> P6[Kalman Gain Kk equals Pk minus HT times HPk minus HT plus R inverse]
    P5 --> P7[State Update xk equals xk minus plus Kk times y tilde]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Covariance Update Pk equals I minus Kk H Pk minus]
    P8 --> P9[Output Posterior Estimate xk and Pk]
    P9 --> P10{Next Time Step k plus 1}
    P10 --> P2

    S1[LRF Scan zk] --> P5
    S2[Measurement Noise R] --> P6
```

## 4.3 Mobile Robot Localization — LRF + Odometry Fusion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Encoders] --> B[Odometry Model u k]
    C[IMU] --> D[Inertial Prediction]
    E[Laser Range Finder] --> F[Feature Extraction and Scan Matching]
    F --> G[Landmark Observation z k with Range and Bearing]
    B --> H[Kalman Filter State Predictor]
    D --> H
    H --> I[Prior State Estimate xk minus]
    H --> J[Prior Covariance Pk minus]
    G --> K[Innovation Computation y tilde]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Kalman Gain Computation Kk]
    K --> M[Posterior State Update xk]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Robot Pose Output xk yk theta k]
    M --> O[Posterior Covariance Pk]
    O --> P{Feedback to Next Cycle}
    P --> H
```

## 4.4 Kalman Filter — Innovation and Gain Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurement Noise R] --> B{Is R small}
    B -- Yes --> C[Sensor is Accurate]
    C --> D[Kalman Gain K approaches 1]
    D --> E[Posterior Estimate xk approximately equals zk]
    B -- No --> F[Sensor is Noisy]
    F --> G[Kalman Gain K approaches 0]
    G --> H[Posterior Estimate xk approximately equals Prior Estimate xk minus]

    I[Process Noise Q] --> J{Is Q small}
    J -- Yes --> K[Model is Accurate]
    K --> L[Kalman Gain K approaches 0]
    L --> H
    J -- No --> M[Model is Uncertain]
    M --> N[Kalman Gain K approaches H inverse]
    N --> E
```

## 4.5 Data Fusion Hierarchy in Mobile Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Level 0 Signal Level Fusion] --> B[Level 1 Feature Level Fusion]
    B --> C[Level 2 Decision Level Fusion]
    C --> D[Level 3 Knowledge Level Fusion]

    A1[Raw LRF Scans] --> A
    A2[Raw IMU Streams] --> A
    A3[Raw Odometry Pulses] --> A

    B1[Extracted Edges and Corners] --> B
    B2[Landmark Features] --> B
    B3[Scan Matching Pose] --> B

    C1[Kalman Filter Fused Pose] --> C
    C2[Particle Filter Localization] --> C

    D1[Semantic Map Belief] --> D
    D2[Mission Level Decision Making] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The Kalman Filter operates at **Level 1 (Feature-Level) Fusion**, where geometric features extracted from LRF scans are combined with kinematic and inertial data. This is the most common fusion level in mobile robotics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Laser Range Finder. With a neat diagram, explain its working using the Time-of-Flight principle.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Laser Range Finder (LRF)** is a non-contact electro-optical distance sensor used in mobile robots to determine the range to objects in the environment.

**Working Principle (Time-of-Flight):**

The LRF emits a short, coherent laser pulse of wavelength typically 905 nm or 1550 nm toward a target. The pulse propagates to the target, reflects from its surface, and returns to a photodetector. The round-trip propagation time $\Delta t$ is measured using a high-speed Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC). The range is computed as:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light. The factor 2 accounts for the **two-way propagation path**. In a 2D scanner, a rotating mirror sweeps the beam across a horizontal plane, producing a range scan $\{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n\}$ at angular positions $\{\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n\}$.

> **Key Points to Score Full Marks:**
> - State the equation explicitly.
> - Mention speed of light value.
> - Describe scan assembly for 2D LRF.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the five recursive equations of the Kalman Filter and state the role of the Kalman Gain.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Kalman Filter executes the following **five recursive equations**:

| # | Equation | Phase |
|---|----------|-------|
| 1 | $\hat{x}_k^{-} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1} + B_k u_k$ | Predict — State |
| 2 | $P_k^{-} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$ | Predict — Covariance |
| 3 | $K_k = P_k^{-} H_k^T (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$ | Gain |
| 4 | $\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^{-} + K_k (z_k - H_k \hat{x}_k^{-})$ | Update — State |
| 5 | $P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-}$ | Update — Covariance |

**Role of Kalman Gain:**

The Kalman Gain $K_k$ is the **optimal weighting factor** that determines how much the new measurement $z_k$ influences the corrected state estimate. It ranges between 0 and 1 (or a matrix of such values):

- When $R_k$ is small (sensor is accurate), $K_k \to 1$, meaning the filter **trusts the measurement** more.
- When $Q_k$ is small (model is accurate), $K_k \to 0$, meaning the filter **trusts the prediction** more.

It is computed to **minimize the posterior error covariance**, making the KF optimal in the mean-squared-error sense.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) Derive the Kalman Filter update equations from first principles. (7 Marks)**

**(b) For a mobile robot with state $x_k = [x_k, y_k, \theta_k]^T$, set up the linearized Kalman Filter state prediction model using wheel odometry. Define the LRF measurement model when observing a known landmark. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation of Kalman Update Equations (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the linear system.**

The discrete-time linear dynamic system is given by:

$$x_k = F_k x_{k-1} + B_k u_k + w_{k-1}, \quad w_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$$

$$z_k = H_k x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$$

**Step 2: Posterior state update form.**

The filter forms the posterior estimate as a weighted blend of the prior and the measurement:

$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^{-} + K_k (z_k - H_k \hat{x}_k^{-})$$

where $K_k$ is the Kalman Gain to be determined optimally.

**Step 3: Express the posterior error.**

Let the prior error be $e_k^{-} = x_k - \hat{x}_k^{-}$ and the measurement noise be $v_k$. Then:

$$e_k = x_k - \hat{x}_k = (I - K_k H_k) e_k^{-} - K_k v_k$$

**Step 4: Posterior covariance.**

Taking the expectation of the outer product and using independence of $e_k^{-}$ and $v_k$:

$$P_k = E[e_k e_k^T] = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-} (I - K_k H_k)^T + K_k R_k K_k^T$$

**Step 5: Minimize the trace w.r.t. $K_k$.**

Expanding and differentiating $tr(P_k)$ with respect to $K_k$:

$$\frac{\partial \, tr(P_k)}{\partial K_k} = -2 P_k^{-} H_k^T + 2 K_k (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k) = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve for $K_k$.**

$$K_k (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k) = P_k^{-} H_k^T$$

$$\boxed{K_k = P_k^{-} H_k^T (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}}$$

**Step 7: Simplified $P_k$ formula.**

Substituting $K_k$ back into $P_k$ yields the Joseph form simplification:

$$\boxed{P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-}}$$

This completes the derivation of the Kalman update equations.

---

### Part (b) — Mobile Robot Kalman Filter Setup (7 Marks)

**State Vector Definition:**

$$x_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \theta_k \end{bmatrix}$$

**Wheel Odometry Motion Model:**

Given odometry inputs $\Delta s$ (translation) and $\Delta \theta$ (rotation):

$$\hat{x}_k^{-} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{k-1} + \Delta s \cos\theta_{k-1} \\ y_{k-1} + \Delta s \sin\theta_{k-1} \\ \theta_{k-1} + \Delta \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Linearized State Transition Matrix $F_k$:**

Taking the Jacobian of the motion model w.r.t. the state:

$$F_k = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin\theta_{k-1} \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta s \sin\theta_{k-1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Covariance Prediction:**

$$P_k^{-} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$

**LRF Measurement Model (Observing Landmark at $(l_x, l_y)$):**

$$z_k = h(x_k) + v_k = \begin{bmatrix} r_k \\ \phi_k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_x - x_k)^2 + (l_y - y_k)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_y - y_k, l_x - x_k) - \theta_k \end{bmatrix} + v_k$$

**Linearized Observation Matrix $H_k$:**

$$H_k = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x_k - l_x}{r_k} & \frac{y_k - l_y}{r_k} & 0 \\ \frac{l_y - y_k}{r_k^2} & \frac{l_k - x_k}{r_k^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Update:**

$$K_k = P_k^{-} H_k^T (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^{-} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_k^{-}))$$

$$P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly **lose marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting the **Joseph stabilized form** $P_k = (I - K_k H_k) P_k^{-}$ — using the expanded three-term form without simplification loses 1 mark.
> 2. Failing to **linearize** the LRF measurement model via Jacobians — without this, the system is the **EKF**, not the basic KF.
> 3. Confusing **prior and posterior** notations — using $\hat{x}_k^{-}$ for posterior loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. Not stating the assumption $E[w_k v_k^T] = 0$ explicitly — KTU expects this independence assumption.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a) Explain the conceptual difference between a Laser Range Finder and a sonar sensor. Why is the Kalman Filter preferred over direct measurement integration for LRF-based localization? (7 Marks)**

**(b) Consider a 1D robot tracking problem where the state equation is $x_k = x_{k-1} + u_k + w_k$ with $Q = 0.2$, and measurement $z_k = x_k + v_k$ with $R = 0.4$. Initial state $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1.0$. Given $u_1 = u_2 = 1$ m, $z_1 = 1.5$ m, and $z_2 = 2.2$ m, compute $\hat{x}_1$, $P_1$, $\hat{x}_2$, and $P_2$ using the Kalman Filter. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — LRF vs Sonar and Justification for Kalman Filter (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | LRF (Laser) | Sonar (Ultrasonic) |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Wave type | Electromagnetic (light) | Mechanical (sound) |
| Speed | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s | $\approx 343$ m/s |
| Beam width | Very narrow (~0.5°) | Wide (~15–30°) |
| Range | 0.1 m to 100+ m | 0.1 m to 10 m |
| Resolution | Millimeter | Centimeter |
| Reflectivity issues | Specular surfaces problematic | Soft surfaces absorb |
| Update rate | 10–100 Hz | 5–20 Hz |
| Cost | High | Low |

**Why Kalman Filter is preferred (4 Marks):**

1. **Uncertainty Quantification:** LRF measurements have a stochastic error model (Gaussian noise). The Kalman Filter provides a **probabilistic estimate** with explicit covariance — direct integration does not.
2. **Sensor Fusion:** The KF can fuse LRF data with odometry and IMU in a single unified framework, exploiting the strengths of each.
3. **Recursive Efficiency:** The KF is recursive — it only needs the previous estimate, not the entire measurement history. This gives $O(n^2)$ complexity, suitable for real-time embedded robotics.
4. **Optimality:** Under linear-Gaussian assumptions, the KF is provably **minimum mean-squared error (MMSE)** optimal.
5. **Drift Correction:** Odometric integration suffers from **unbounded drift**. The KF uses LRF landmark observations to **reset and bound** this drift.
6. **Smoothness:** The KF produces **smooth, denoised state trajectories** free of measurement jitter.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution of 1D Kalman Filter (7 Marks)

**Given:** $F = 1$, $H = 1$, $B = 1$, $Q = 0.2$, $R = 0.4$, $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1.0$, $u_1 = u_2 = 1$ m, $z_1 = 1.5$ m, $z_2 = 2.2$ m.

### Iteration 1

**Predict:**

$$\hat{x}_1^{-} = \hat{x}_0 + u_1 = 0 + 1 = 1$$

$$P_1^{-} = P_0 + Q = 1.0 + 0.2 = 1.2$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$K_1 = \frac{P_1^{-}}{P_1^{-} + R} = \frac{1.2}{1.2 + 0.4} = \frac{1.2}{1.6} = 0.75$$

**Update:**

$$\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_1^{-} + K_1 (z_1 - \hat{x}_1^{-}) = 1 + 0.75 (1.5 - 1) = 1 + 0.375 = 1.375 \text{ m}$$

$$P_1 = (1 - K_1) P_1^{-} = 0.25 \times 1.2 = 0.30$$

### Iteration 2

**Predict:**

$$\hat{x}_2^{-} = \hat{x}_1 + u_2 = 1.375 + 1 = 2.375$$

$$P_2^{-} = P_1 + Q = 0.30 + 0.2 = 0.50$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$K_2 = \frac{P_2^{-}}{P_2^{-} + R} = \frac{0.50}{0.50 + 0.4} = \frac{0.50}{0.90} = 0.5556$$

**Update:**

$$\hat{x}_2 = \hat{x}_2^{-} + K_2 (z_2 - \hat{x}_2^{-}) = 2.375 + 0.5556 (2.2 - 2.375) = 2.375 + 0.5556 \times (-0.175)$$

$$= 2.375 - 0.0972 = 2.2778 \text{ m}$$

$$P_2 = (1 - K_2) P_2^{-} = 0.4444 \times 0.50 = 0.2222$$

**Final Results:**

$$\boxed{\hat{x}_1 = 1.375 \text{ m}, \quad P_1 = 0.30, \quad \hat{x}_2 = 2.2778 \text{ m}, \quad P_2 = 0.2222}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Rounding:** Students frequently make errors in:
> 1. Forgetting to update the state before computing the next prediction (must use $\hat{x}_1$, not $\hat{x}_1^{-}$).
> 2. Using $P_k$ instead of $P_k^{-}$ in the next iteration's gain — KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 3. Sign errors in the innovation $(z_k - \hat{x}_k^{-})$ — the order matters; it is measurement minus prediction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Laser Range Finders
- LRF works on **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle: $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — **memorize**.
- 2D LRF = rotating mirror + ToF unit, producing scan $\{d_i, \theta_i\}$.
- Common wavelengths: **905 nm** (silicon-safe) and **1550 nm** (eye-safe).
- LRF advantages: high accuracy (mm), long range, narrow beam.
- LRF limitations: cost, specular reflections, transparent objects.

### Data Fusion
- Data fusion combines multiple sensors for a **better-than-any-single** estimate.
- Levels: signal → feature → decision → knowledge.
- The Kalman Filter is a **feature-level fusion** algorithm.

### Kalman Filter — Core Theory
- 5 equations: 2 predict + 3 update.
- **State equation:** $x_k = F x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_k$.
- **Measurement equation:** $z_k = H x_k + v_k$.
- $Q$ = process noise covariance, $R$ = measurement noise covariance.
- $\hat{x}_k^{-}$ = prior, $\hat{x}_k$ = posterior.
- **Innovation:** $\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H_k \hat{x}_k^{-}$.
- **Kalman Gain $K_k$** minimizes posterior error covariance.

### Key Properties
- Optimal for **linear-Gaussian** systems in **MMSE** sense.
- **Recursive** — $O(n^2)$ per step.
- **Convergent** — $P_k$ decreases monotonically (in steady state).
- For nonlinear systems, use **EKF** (Jacobian linearization) or **UKF**.

### LRF + KF in Mobile Robots
- LRF provides **range and bearing to landmarks**.
- Jacobians of measurement model w.r.t. state give $H_k$.
- Motion model Jacobians give $F_k$.
- Combined with odometry and IMU, the KF delivers a drift-corrected robot pose estimate.

### Common Exam Traps
- Confusing $P_k$ and $P_k^{-}$.
- Forgetting to linearize in EKF context.
- Sign errors in innovation term.
- Using $H$ transpose incorrectly in gain formula.
- Forgetting the "$/2$" in range equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** When asked "Why Kalman Filter?", always answer: **optimality under linear-Gaussian assumptions**, **real-time recursive efficiency**, and **explicit uncertainty quantification**. These three phrases score full conceptual marks in KTU viva and written exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Sensors: Perspective Camera
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Visual Sensors: Perspective Camera

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Perspective Camera** in the context of mobile robotics is a vision sensor that models the geometric projection of 3D world points onto a 2D image plane using the principles of **central (pinhole) projection**. It is the canonical mathematical model used in computer vision to describe how a robot perceives its environment through a monocular camera. The perspective camera obeys the **collinearity principle**: every point in the 3D world, its corresponding image point, and the camera's center of projection (the optical center) lie on a single straight line in space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The perspective camera model is the **foundation** for all vision-based mobile robot tasks, including visual odometry, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), structure-from-motion, and object detection. Mastering its projection mathematics is non-negotiable for Module 2.

The mathematical model is defined by two parameter sets:

- **Intrinsic Parameters (K)** — internal to the camera: focal length $(f_x, f_y)$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, and sometimes skew $(\gamma)$.
- **Extrinsic Parameters $[R \mid t]$** — relating the world coordinate frame to the camera coordinate frame: a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix $R$ and a $3 \times 1$ translation vector $t$.

The standard aperture angle (horizontal Field of View) of a typical mobile robot camera is in the range of **60° to 90°**, with focal lengths varying from **2.8 mm to 12 mm** for common CMOS sensors (e.g., OV5640, IMX219).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Camera as a Human Eye":**
> Imagine closing one eye and looking at a long straight road. The two parallel lines of the road appear to **converge** at a point far away on the horizon. This is *perspective foreshortening*. The perspective camera models exactly this: parallel lines in 3D world space **converge to a vanishing point** on the 2D image plane. The closer an object is to the camera's optical center, the larger it appears; the farther away, the smaller it appears — in **inverse proportion** to its depth $Z$.

A simpler analogy is the **"Pinhole Camera" or Camera Obscura**:

1. Take a closed box and make a tiny hole on one side.
2. On the opposite inside wall (the *image plane*), an upside-down image of the outside world is projected.
3. The smaller the hole, the sharper (but dimmer) the image.
4. The hole acts as the **optical center** $O_c$, and the image plane is at distance $f$ (the **focal length**) from it.

This is precisely the geometry that every perspective camera emulates — and engineers use a lens (instead of a tiny hole) to gather more light while maintaining a sharp image.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in Mobile Robotics:** A mobile robot navigating a corridor, lane, or hallway relies on exactly this perspective geometry to estimate **depth**, **distance to obstacles**, and **self-motion (egomotion)**. Without the perspective model, visual odometry and SLAM are mathematically impossible.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focal Length (X-axis) | $f_x$ | **500 – 1500** | pixels |
| Focal Length (Y-axis) | $f_y$ | **500 – 1500** | pixels |
| Principal Point | $(c_x, c_y)$ | **$(w/2,\ h/2)$** | pixels |
| Sensor Pixel Size | $s_x,\ s_y$ | **1.4 – 3.0** | $\mu m$ |
| Field of View (Horizontal) | $\theta_H$ | **60° – 90°** | degrees |
| Depth of Field | DOF | **0.5 – $\infty$** | meters |
| Sensor Format (1/3") | Diagonal | **6.0** | mm |
| Frame Rate | $fps$ | **30 – 60** | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> All values listed in **bold** are the **industry-standard reference figures** used by KTU examiners. If a numerical problem provides different values, always **use the given data**; these are default benchmarks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pinhole Camera Model — Step-by-Step Logic

The perspective camera is fundamentally governed by the **pinhole projection geometry**. Here is a structured breakdown of how a 3D world point becomes a 2D pixel:

### Step 1: World Point Definition
A point $P$ in the 3D world is expressed in **world coordinates** as:
$$P_w = (X_w,\ Y_w,\ Z_w)$$

### Step 2: World-to-Camera Transformation (Extrinsic)
The point is first transformed into the **camera coordinate frame** (origin at optical center $O_c$, $Z$-axis along the optical axis):
$$P_c = R \cdot P_w + t$$

Here, $R \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ is the rotation matrix and $t \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$ is the translation vector. Together they form the extrinsic matrix $E = [R \mid t]$.

> **Intuition — "Why extrinsic?"**: A robot may have its camera mounted at a tilted angle on top, or even on a moving pan-tilt unit. The extrinsics tell us *where the camera is and how it is oriented* relative to the robot's body frame.

### Step 3: Perspective Projection (Intrinsic Geometry)
Using similar triangles in the pinhole geometry, a camera-frame point $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ projects to the **normalized image plane** (at $Z = 1$) as:
$$x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

> **Intuition — "Why divide by Z?"**: This is the *foreshortening effect*. An object twice as far appears half the size.

### Step 4: Scaling to Pixel Coordinates (Intrinsic Matrix)
The projected point is then scaled by the focal length and translated to the principal point:
$$u = f_x \cdot x_n + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \cdot y_n + c_y$$

The complete projection is captured by the **camera intrinsic matrix** $K$:
$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

For most modern cameras, the skew $\gamma \approx 0$.

### Step 5: Complete Pipeline
The full 3D-to-2D projection is:
$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \cdot [R \mid t] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The symbol $\sim$ denotes **equality up to a non-zero scalar** (homogeneous coordinates).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every numerical problem in KTU Module 2 visuals will directly use one or more of these equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$ | X-pixel projection (perspective) | pixels |
| 2 | $v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$ | Y-pixel projection (perspective) | pixels |
| 3 | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic matrix (3×3) | pixels |
| 4 | $P_c = R \cdot P_w + t$ | World to camera transformation | meters |
| 5 | $\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right)$ | Horizontal Field of View | radians |
| 6 | $f = \frac{w}{2\tan(\theta/2)}$ | Focal length from FOV & image width | pixels |
| 7 | $\text{Depth} = \frac{f \cdot H_{\text{real}}}{h_{\text{pixel}}}$ | Monocular depth from known object size | meters |
| 8 | $\text{Disparity} = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}$ | Stereo depth relation (referenced) | pixels |
| 9 | $r^2 = x_n^2 + y_n^2$ | Radial distance squared (distortion) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $x_d = x_n (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + 2p_1 x_n y_n + p_2(r^2 + 2x_n^2)$ | Radial + tangential distortion (X) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $y_d = y_n (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + p_1(r^2 + 2y_n^2) + 2p_2 x_n y_n$ | Radial + tangential distortion (Y) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \cdot [R \mid t] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Full projection in homogeneous form | $\lambda$ is scale |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** This projection pipeline is the literal backbone of every autonomous vehicle, drone (e.g., DJI Mavic), and AR/VR headset. A self-driving car's lane-detection module uses the inverse of this projection to reconstruct 3D lane geometry from 2D image data.

---

## 2.3 Lens Distortion — The "Why"

Real cameras have **lenses**, not pinholes. Lenses introduce radial and tangential distortion. In mobile robotics, **camera calibration** (e.g., using Zhang's checkerboard method) estimates distortion coefficients $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$ which are then used to *undistort* images before any geometric computation.

- **Radial distortion** ($k_1, k_2, k_3$): Causes straight lines to appear curved ("barrel" or "pincushion" effect).
- **Tangential distortion** ($p_1, p_2$): Caused by misalignment of the lens with the sensor plane.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the Perspective Projection Equations from Similar Triangles

> **Goal:** Derive $u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$ and $v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$ from first principles.

### Setup
Consider a 3D point $P_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ in the camera coordinate frame. The camera's optical center $O_c$ is at the origin, and the image plane is positioned at $Z = f$ (where $f$ is the focal length, in meters initially). The principal point $P_0$ is located at $(0, 0, f)$ in camera coordinates.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Draw the similar triangles.**
The optical center, the 3D point $P_c$, and its image $p$ are collinear. The triangle formed by $(0,0,0)$, $(X_c, 0, Z_c)$, and $(0, 0, Z_c)$ is similar to the triangle formed by $(0,0,0)$, $(x, 0, f)$, and $(0, 0, f)$ in the image plane.

**Step 2 — Apply the similar-triangle ratio.**
For the X-axis projection:
$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}$$

Solving for $x$:
$$x = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}$$

**Step 3 — Convert from meters to pixels.**
The image is sampled on a discrete pixel grid. Let $s_x$ (pixels/meter) be the horizontal pixel density. Then:
$$u - c_x = s_x \cdot x = s_x \cdot f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}$$

**Step 4 — Define focal length in pixels.**
Combine $f$ (meters) and $s_x$ (pixels/meter) into a single quantity $f_x$ (pixels):
$$f_x = s_x \cdot f$$

Substituting:
$$u - c_x = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $u$.**
$$u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$$

**Step 6 — Repeat for the Y-axis** (by symmetry, using $f_y = s_y \cdot f$):
$$v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

**Step 7 — Express in matrix form (homogeneous).**
$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c / Z_c \\ Y_c / Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying by $Z_c$ to recover the homogeneous form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the canonical derivation. Every KTU numerical problem on perspective projection begins from these equations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Field of View from Focal Length and Image Width

> **Goal:** Prove $\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right)$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup.**
The image plane has a horizontal width of $w$ pixels and lies at a distance $f$ (in same units) from the optical center. The leftmost image point is at horizontal coordinate $x = -w/2$ and the rightmost at $x = +w/2$, both measured from the principal axis.

**Step 2 — Use the tangent of the half-angle.**
The half-angle of the FOV is the angle between the principal axis and the line from $O_c$ to the corner of the image plane:
$$\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{w/2}{f}$$

**Step 3 — Invert to solve for $\theta$:**
$$\frac{\theta}{2} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by 2:**
$$\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU standard).**
For a camera with $w = 1280$ pixels and $f = 800$ pixels:
$$\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{1280}{1600}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.8) = 2 \times 0.6747 = 1.3494\ \text{rad}$$

Converting to degrees:
$$\theta \approx 77.32°$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Monocular Depth from Known Object Size

> **Goal:** Derive $Z = \frac{f \cdot H_{\text{real}}}{h_{\text{pixel}}}$ — a frequently tested KTU formula.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Similar-triangle setup.**
A real-world object of height $H_{\text{real}}$ is placed at depth $Z$ from the camera. Its image has a pixel height of $h_{\text{pixel}}$.

**Step 2 — Set up the ratio.**
Using the pinhole model:
$$\frac{h_{\text{pixel}}}{f} = \frac{H_{\text{real}}}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Z$:**
$$Z = \frac{f \cdot H_{\text{real}}}{h_{\text{pixel}}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
A mobile robot observes a road sign of real height $H = 2.0$ m. The camera focal length is $f = 1000$ pixels, and the sign appears $h = 200$ pixels tall in the image. The depth is:
$$Z = \frac{1000 \times 2.0}{200} = 10\ \text{meters}$$

This is a **classic KTU 2-mark short problem** in Module 2.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Theory to Practice

| Concept | Mathematical Tool | Robotic Application |
|---|---|---|
| Perspective Projection | $u = f_x X_c/Z_c + c_x$ | Image formation |
| FOV | $\theta = 2 \arctan(w/2f)$ | Camera lens selection |
| Depth Recovery | $Z = f H / h$ | Obstacle distance |
| Calibration | $K$, $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2$ | Pre-processing |
| Extrinsics | $[R \mid t]$ | Multi-sensor fusion |
| Epipolar Geometry | $F$ matrix (not covered here) | Stereo vision setup |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pinhole Projection Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the **functional block architecture** of the perspective camera pipeline. It maps the data flow from 3D world coordinates all the way to 2D pixel coordinates, including the role of intrinsic and extrinsic parameters and the lens distortion correction block.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[WORLD POINT Pw Xw Yw Zw] --> B[EXTRINSIC TRANSFORMATION R t]
    B --> C[CAMERA FRAME POINT Pc Xc Yc Zc]
    C --> D[PERSPECTIVE DIVISION Xc/Zc Yc/Zc]
    D --> E[NORMALIZED IMAGE PLANE xn yn]
    E --> F[LENS DISTORTION MODEL k1 k2 p1 p2]
    F --> G[DISTORTED IMAGE COORDINATES xd yd]
    G --> H[INTRINSIC MATRIX K fx fy cx cy]
    H --> I[PIXEL COORDINATES u v]
    I --> J[FINAL 2D IMAGE]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style I fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style J fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

### Diagram Interpretation (Step-by-Step)

1. **A → B:** A 3D world point $P_w$ is captured.
2. **B:** The extrinsic transformation (rotation $R$ + translation $t$) converts the world frame to the camera frame. This accounts for the camera's position and orientation on the robot.
3. **B → C:** Result is the camera-frame coordinates $P_c$.
4. **C → D:** Perspective division — dividing by $Z_c$ gives the **foreshortening** effect (this is the *key step* that distinguishes perspective from orthographic).
5. **D → E:** The result lies on the **normalized image plane** (a virtual plane at $Z = 1$).
6. **E → F:** The lens distortion block applies the **Brown–Conrady** distortion model with coefficients $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$.
7. **F → G:** Distorted coordinates are obtained.
8. **G → H:** The intrinsic matrix $K$ scales and shifts the coordinates to pixels.
9. **H → I:** Final pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ are produced.
10. **I → J:** These pixels constitute the final digital image.

---

## 4.2 Coordinate System Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W[WORLD FRAME Xw Yw Zw] -- R t --> C[CAMERA FRAME Xc Yc Zc]
    C -- K --> I[IMAGE FRAME u v PIXELS]
    I -- INVERSE K --> N[NORMALIZED PLANE xn yn]
    N -- TRIANGULATION --> D[3D DEPTH Z]
    style W fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style I fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style N fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style D fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

This block diagram summarizes the **forward and inverse** transformations central to mobile robot vision.

---

## 4.3 Distortion Types (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L[LENS] --> RD[RADIAL DISTORTION k1 k2 k3]
    L --> TD[TANGENTIAL DISTORTION p1 p2]
    RD --> BD[BARREL OR PINCUSHION EFFECT]
    TD --> AD[ASYMMETRIC SHEARING EFFECT]
    BD --> OUT[UNDISTORTED IMAGE AFTER CORRECTION]
    AD --> OUT
    style L fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style RD fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c
    style TD fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c
    style BD fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c
    style AD fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c
    style OUT fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Question A1
**Define the intrinsic matrix $K$ of a perspective camera. List all its components and their units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **intrinsic matrix $K$** is a $3 \times 3$ upper-triangular matrix that encapsulates the internal geometric and optical characteristics of the camera. It is given by:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Components and Units:**

- $f_x, f_y$ — **Focal lengths** in pixels (along the X and Y image axes respectively). For most cameras, $f_x \approx f_y$ if the pixels are square. **(1 mark)**
- $c_x, c_y$ — **Principal point coordinates** (in pixels), the point where the optical axis intersects the image plane. Typically near $(w/2,\ h/2)$ for a centered camera. **(1 mark)**
- $\gamma$ — **Skew coefficient** (dimensionless), accounting for non-rectangular pixels. For most modern cameras, $\gamma \approx 0$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2
**What is perspective division? Why is it necessary in a perspective camera model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Perspective division is the mathematical operation of dividing the X and Y camera-frame coordinates by the depth $Z_c$:

$$x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

It is necessary because, in a perspective camera, the size of a projected object is **inversely proportional** to its depth — a fundamental geometric property of central projection. **(1 mark)**

Without perspective division, parallel 3D lines would project to parallel 2D lines, which is physically incorrect for any real camera. With perspective division, the **foreshortening effect** is correctly modeled, and 3D parallel lines (e.g., railway tracks) correctly converge to vanishing points in the 2D image. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style: Module 2, Question with internal choice]**

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the perspective projection equations for a pinhole camera model. Clearly explain each variable and the role of similar triangles in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot's camera has a focal length of $f = 800$ pixels in both X and Y directions, a principal point at $(c_x, c_y) = (640, 360)$, and zero skew. The camera is mounted such that its optical axis is aligned with the world Z-axis, and the camera origin is at the world origin (i.e., $R = I$, $t = 0$). A 3D world point is at $P_w = (2.0,\ 1.5,\ 5.0)$ meters.

Compute the pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ at which this point will appear in the image. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Setup the pinhole geometry:**
Define a 3D point $P_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ in the camera coordinate frame. The optical center $O_c$ is the origin, and the image plane is located at $Z = f$ (focal length in meters).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply similar triangles:**
For the X-direction projection, the triangle $(O_c, X_c, Z_c)$ in 3D is similar to the triangle $(O_c, x, f)$ in the image plane:
$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X_c}{Z_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}$$

Similarly, for Y:
$$y = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Convert meters to pixels:**
Let $s_x$ (pixels/m) and $s_y$ (pixels/m) be the pixel densities. Define $f_x = s_x \cdot f$ and $f_y = s_y \cdot f$ (both in pixels):
$$u - c_x = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} \qquad v - c_y = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Final form and intrinsic matrix:**
Rearranging:
$$u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \qquad v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

In homogeneous form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify the parameters:**
Given:
- $f_x = f_y = 800$ pixels
- $c_x = 640,\ c_y = 360$ pixels
- $R = I$ (identity), $t = 0$
- $P_w = (2.0,\ 1.5,\ 5.0)$ m

Since $R = I$ and $t = 0$, we have $P_c = P_w = (2.0,\ 1.5,\ 5.0)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply perspective division:**
$$x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c} = \frac{2.0}{5.0} = 0.4$$
$$y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} = \frac{1.5}{5.0} = 0.3$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Scale to pixel units:**
$$u = f_x \cdot x_n + c_x = 800 \times 0.4 + 640 = 320 + 640 = 960$$
$$v = f_y \cdot y_n + c_y = 800 \times 0.3 + 360 = 240 + 360 = 600$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Final answer:**
$$\boxed{(u, v) = (960,\ 600)\ \text{pixels}}$$

**Verification:** The point is at the right side of the image (since $u = 960 > c_x = 640$) and slightly below the center (since $v = 600 > c_y = 360$), which is geometrically consistent with the positive X and Y world coordinates.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the pinhole camera model. Define intrinsic and extrinsic parameters, and write the complete projection equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot-mounted camera has the following intrinsic parameters: $f_x = 1200$ pixels, $f_y = 1200$ pixels, $c_x = 640$, $c_y = 480$. The image resolution is $1280 \times 960$. The robot observes a vertical pole of real height $H = 1.8$ m, and the pole's image height is $h = 240$ pixels.

Compute:
(i) The distance of the pole from the camera. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The horizontal Field of View (in degrees) of the camera. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Diagram and Pinhole Model:**
The pinhole camera consists of a closed box with a tiny aperture (the optical center $O_c$) on one side and an image plane on the opposite side at distance $f$ (focal length). Light rays from a 3D world point $P$ pass through $O_c$ and project onto the image plane at point $p$, forming an upside-down image.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Intrinsic and Extrinsic Parameters:**
- **Intrinsic parameters ($K$):** Internal camera properties — focal length $(f_x, f_y)$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, and skew $\gamma$. These are constant for a given camera and are determined by calibration.
- **Extrinsic parameters ($[R \mid t]$):** The 6-DoF pose (3 for rotation, 3 for translation) of the camera in the world frame. These change as the robot moves.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Complete projection equation:**
