$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \cdot [R \mid t] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & t_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & t_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & t_z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $s$ is an arbitrary scale factor (homogeneous coordinates).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Part (i) — Distance of the pole (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify given values:**
$H = 1.8$ m, $h = 240$ pixels, $f = f_x = 1200$ pixels.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Apply the depth formula:**
$$Z = \frac{f \cdot H}{h}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute:**
$$Z = \frac{1200 \times 1.8}{240} = \frac{2160}{240} = 9.0\ \text{meters}$$

$$\boxed{Z = 9.0\ \text{m}}$$

**Part (ii) — Horizontal Field of View (4 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Apply the FOV formula:**
$$\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{w}{2f}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Substitute values:**
$w = 1280$ pixels, $f = 1200$ pixels:
$$\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{1280}{2400}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.5333)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Evaluate arctan:**
$$\arctan(0.5333) \approx 0.4909\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final answer in degrees:**
$$\theta = 2 \times 0.4909 = 0.9817\ \text{rad} \approx 56.25°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_H \approx 56.25°}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Perspective Camera Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $Z_c$** — This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU papers. If you write $u = f_x \cdot X_c + c_x$ without dividing by $Z_c$, you will get **zero marks** for the projection step. **Always divide by depth!**
> 2. **Mixing up focal length units** — The focal length used in projection must be in **pixels** ($f_x$, $f_y$), not meters. If the problem gives focal length in mm, convert using $f_{\text{pixels}} = f_{\text{mm}} / s_{\text{pixel-size}}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the principal point offset** — Many students compute $u = f_x \cdot (X_c / Z_c)$ and forget to add $c_x$. This shifts the entire image incorrectly.
> 4. **Confusing intrinsic vs extrinsic** — Intrinsic ($K$) is about the **camera's internal** geometry; extrinsic ($R, t$) is about the **camera's pose** in the world. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **Wrong sign convention in radial distortion** — The standard form uses $(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + \ldots)$ in the numerator; do not invert the sign.
> 6. **Arctan in degrees vs radians** — KTU examiners **always** want the FOV in degrees unless specified otherwise. Always convert and label the unit explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] The **perspective camera** projects 3D world points to 2D image points via the **pinhole model**.
- [x] The **intrinsic matrix $K$** has $f_x, f_y$ (focal length in pixels), $c_x, c_y$ (principal point), and skew $\gamma$ (often 0).
- [x] The **extrinsic matrix $[R \mid t]$** encodes the camera's 6-DoF pose in the world frame.
- [x] The core projection equation is $u = f_x (X_c / Z_c) + c_x$ and $v = f_y (Y_c / Z_c) + c_y$.
- [x] **Perspective division by $Z_c$** is what gives the foreshortening effect — *do not skip it*.
- [x] The **horizontal FOV** is $\theta_H = 2 \arctan(w / 2f)$.
- [x] **Monocular depth** from a known object size is $Z = f H_{\text{real}} / h_{\text{pixel}}$.
- [x] **Lens distortion** has two components: **radial** $(k_1, k_2, k_3)$ and **tangential** $(p_1, p_2)$.
- [x] The **complete pipeline** is: World → Extrinsic $(R, t)$ → Camera Frame → Perspective Division → Normalized Plane → Distortion → Intrinsic $K$ → Pixels → Image.
- [x] Standard camera FOV range: **60° to 90°**; standard focal length: **500 – 1500 pixels**.
- [x] Typical principal point: **center of the image** at $(w/2,\ h/2)$.
- [x] KTU typically asks for: (1) projection calculation, (2) FOV derivation, (3) depth estimation, (4) intrinsic matrix construction.
- [x] In **homogeneous coordinates**, the projection is $s [u, v, 1]^T = K [R \mid t] [X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^T$.
- [x] This model is the **mathematical foundation** of visual odometry, SLAM, structure-from-motion, and AR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planar Homography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planar Homography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planar Homography (Definition – KTU Standard):**
> A *planar homography* (also called a *projective transformation* or *2D collineation*) is a **3 × 3 invertible matrix** $\mathbf{H}$ that maps every 2D point $\mathbf{x}$ in one image of a planar scene to its corresponding 2D point $\mathbf{x}'$ in a second image of the same plane, captured by a camera at a different viewpoint. The mapping is defined *up to a non-zero scalar*, giving it **8 degrees of freedom (DoF)** and the *homogeneous form*:
>
> $$\mathbf{x}' \sim \mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{x}$$

Mathematically, if the world points $\mathbf{X}$ all lie on a plane $\pi$ (with normal $\mathbf{n}$ and distance $d$ from the origin), the relationship between the two camera views is:

$$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{R} - \frac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{K}$ is the **camera intrinsic matrix** (3 × 3)
- $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ are the **rotation** and **translation** between the two camera frames
- $\mathbf{n}$ and $d$ describe the **plane equation** $\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X} = d$ in the first camera frame

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (Examiners' Favourite):** Homography was first formalized in the 19th century for descriptive geometry, and brought into computer vision through the seminal work of **Hartley & Zisserman (2003)** and **Faugeras & Lustman (1988)**, who demonstrated motion/structure recovery purely from point correspondences.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you take a **photo of a rectangular painting** lying on a table, then move your phone a little to the right and take another photo. The painting is *flat* (planar), so all the pixels in the second image are essentially the first image pixels **stretched, squished, and skewed** to mimic the new perspective.

That single “stretching + skewing + scaling” operation — the one that converts every point from Photo 1 to its new location in Photo 2 — **is the homography**.

> **Real-world analogy:** Hold a transparent sheet with dots in front of your eye. Now tilt the sheet. The dots appear to slide along curved paths on your retina. Because the sheet is flat, *one single* matrix describes how every dot moved. That matrix is the homography.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Homography works *exactly and only* when:
> (a) the scene is a **planar surface**, OR
> (b) the camera undergoes **pure rotation** (no translation).
> If the scene has depth AND the camera translates, the relationship becomes a *fundamental matrix* instead, with 7 DoF.

---

### 1.3 Why Planar Homography Matters in Mobile Robotics

| Application | Role of Homography |
|---|---|
| **Monocular Visual Odometry** | Frame-to-frame motion estimation from co-planar ground patches |
| **Visual SLAM** | Place recognition, keyframe matching (ORB-SLAM, PTAM) |
| **Drone Navigation** | Estimating ego-motion over flat terrain |
| **Augmented Reality** | Anchoring virtual objects on detected planar surfaces (book covers, floors) |
| **Lane Detection (Autonomous Cars)** | Inverse-perspective-mapping (IPM) of road planes |
| **Image Mosaicking** | Stitching aerial/satellite images of the ground plane |

> **Standard metric to remember:** Homography has **8 DoF** and is recovered from a minimum of **4 point correspondences** (the *minimum-case* is degenerate; practical systems use **8+ correspondences** with the **Normalized Direct Linear Transform (DLT)**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Conceptual Building Blocks

To truly understand planar homography, we must chain four ideas in order:

1. **Pinhole Camera Projection Model** — A 3D point $\mathbf{X}$ is projected to an image point $\mathbf{x}$ via $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{K}\,[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]\,\mathbf{X}$.
2. **Planarity Constraint** — All 3D points lie on a plane: $\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X} = d$.
3. **Substitution into Projection** — The plane equation eliminates the depth variable, collapsing 3D→3D to 2D→2D via a single matrix.
4. **Invariance to Scale** — Because homogeneous coordinates ignore scale, $\mathbf{H}$ is defined only up to a non-zero scalar $\lambda$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

- **Step 1 — Camera 1 projection:** A world point $\mathbf{X}$ on plane $\pi$ is seen in the first image as $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{K}\,[\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}]\,\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{K}\mathbf{X}$ (with world frame = camera-1 frame).
- **Step 2 — Plane equation injects depth:** Since $\mathbf{X}$ lies on the plane, its 3rd coordinate can be written in terms of the first two, reducing it to a 2D parameter on the plane.
- **Step 3 — Camera 2 projection:** The same world point, now expressed in camera-2 coordinates, projects as $\mathbf{x}' \sim \mathbf{K}'\,[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]\,\mathbf{X}$.
- **Step 4 — Eliminate $\mathbf{X}$:** Substituting the planar relation between camera-2 and camera-1 frames yields the compact 2D mapping $\mathbf{x}' \sim \mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{x}$.
- **Step 5 — Degrees of freedom:** Removing the global scale, $\mathbf{H}$ has $9 - 1 = \mathbf{8}$ DoF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition – “Why a single matrix?”**
> A general 3D scene seen from two cameras gives a *fundamental matrix* $\mathbf{F}$ of rank 2 with 7 DoF. But the planar subset collapses the epipolar geometry: the family of epipolar lines **degenerates into a single line (the horizon of the plane)**, and the residual mapping becomes a projective 2D-to-2D transformation, i.e., the homography.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulae for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 2 – Mobile Robotics)**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | DoF / Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathbf{K}\mathbf{X}$ | Camera projection (homogeneous) | 3 × 3 / 3-vec |
| 2 | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{R} - \dfrac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ | Canonical homography between two views of a plane | 3 × 3, 8 DoF |
| 3 | $\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X} = d$ | Plane equation in first camera frame | scalar |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}' \sim \mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{x}$ | Homogeneous 2D point transfer | 3-vec |
| 5 | $\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Expanded matrix form | — |
| 6 | $x' = \dfrac{h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}$ | Non-homogeneous x-coordinate | scalar |
| 7 | $y' = \dfrac{h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}$ | Non-homogeneous y-coordinate | scalar |
| 8 | $N \ge 4$ | Minimum point correspondences for unique $\mathbf{H}$ | — |
| 9 | $\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$ | DLT linear system (each correspondence gives 2 rows of $\mathbf{A}$) | $2N \times 9$ |
| 10 | $\text{Sampson Error} = \sum_{i}\dfrac{(\mathbf{x}'_i \times \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_i)^2}{[\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_i]_\times^2 + [\mathbf{H}^{-1}\mathbf{x}'_i]_\times^2}$ | Geometric refinement (Gold Standard) | scalar |
| 11 | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R} - \mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ | Rotation decomposition (Faugeras, 1990) | 3 × 3 |
| 12 | $\mathbf{R} = (\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{K}) + \mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d$ | — | 3 × 3 |
| 13 | $\mathbf{H}^{-T}\mathbf{l} = \mathbf{l}'$ | Line transfer (conjugate transpose) | 3-vec |
| 14 | Singular values of $\mathbf{H}$ → real positive roots of a 4th-degree polynomial | Closed-form motion extraction | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse DoF!** A *3 × 3* homography is *8-DoF* (not 9) because of scale. A *fundamental matrix* is 7-DoF. An *essential matrix* is 5-DoF (3 rot + 2 trans-direction).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** The road in front of a car is approximately planar. Estimating $\mathbf{H}$ between consecutive frames gives **ego-motion (yaw rate + forward speed)** and **lane curvature** in one step.
- **Indoor Service Robots:** Floors and tabletops dominate the scene; a single homography often suffices for visual odometry on flat surfaces.
- **Drone Mapping:** Downward-facing cameras on quadcopters always look at the (near) ground plane, making homography-based mosaicking the standard pipeline.
- **AR Headsets (Hololens, Meta Quest):** Detected planar surfaces are anchored using homography-derived pose for stable virtual content overlay.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation conventions** (used throughout the derivation):
> - Bold lowercase $\mathbf{x}$ → 2D homogeneous image point (3-vector)
> - Bold uppercase $\mathbf{X}$ → 3D world point (4-vector in homogeneous form)
> - $\sim$ → equality up to a non-zero scale factor
> - Subscripts $1, 2$ → camera-1 and camera-2 frames respectively
> - World frame is chosen **identical to camera-1 frame** (a standard convention; simplifies derivation dramatically)

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Two-View Geometry to Homography

**Step 1 — Define the world coordinate frame.**

Place the origin of the world frame at the optical centre of camera 1, with the X-Y plane parallel to the image plane. Thus camera 1 has extrinsic parameters:

$$[\mathbf{R}_1 \mid \mathbf{t}_1] = [\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}]$$

A 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X, Y, Z, 1)^{T}$ is observed by camera 1 as:

$$\mathbf{x}_1 \sim \mathbf{K}_1\,[\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}]\,\mathbf{X}_w \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{K}_1\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Express the same point in camera-2's frame.**

Camera 2 has extrinsic $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ relative to camera 1, so:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{K}_2\,[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]\,\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R}\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Inject the planarity constraint.**

Let the world plane $\pi$ be defined by the unit normal $\mathbf{n}$ and perpendicular distance $d$ from camera 1's origin:

$$\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X} = d, \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{n} = \begin{bmatrix} n_1 \\ n_2 \\ n_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \|\mathbf{n}\| = 1$$

Because $\mathbf{X}$ lies on the plane, we can write:

$$\frac{1}{d}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X} = 1$$

This scalar value equals 1 for any point on the plane, so we may **multiply it into the homogeneous projection** without changing the result:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R}\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t}\cdot 1\right) \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R}\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t}\cdot \frac{\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X}}{d}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the world coordinates.**

Distribute the multiplication and collect the term $\mathbf{X}$:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute camera-1's projection.**

From Step 1, $\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathbf{K}_1^{-1}\mathbf{x}_1$. Substituting:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}_1^{-1}\,\mathbf{x}_1$$

**Step 6 — Identify the homography matrix.**

Comparing with the canonical form $\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{x}_1$, we obtain the **master formula**:

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}_1^{-1}\;}$$

If both cameras share the same intrinsics ($\mathbf{K}_1 = \mathbf{K}_2 = \mathbf{K}$), this reduces to the familiar:

$$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this a projective transformation?** Because the homogeneous vector $\mathbf{x}_1$ is multiplied by a 3 × 3 matrix, and the result is interpreted only up to a scale. The mapping is invertible (since $\mathbf{H}^{-1}$ exists when the plane is in front of both cameras) and is the *most general* 2D→2D mapping consistent with a perspective camera model.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recovering H via the Direct Linear Transform (DLT)

We are given $N$ point correspondences $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}'_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ in homogeneous form, and we want to estimate $\mathbf{H}$.

**Step 1 — Convert the homogeneous equation to two linear constraints.**

For each correspondence:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x'_i \\ y'_i \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \times \left(\begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x_i \\ y_i \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cross product explicitly.**

Let $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_i = (u, v, w)^{T}$. The cross product with $(x'_i, y'_i, 1)$ yields two independent equations (the third is linearly dependent):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x'_i \cdot w - 1 \cdot u &= 0 \\
y'_i \cdot w - 1 \cdot v &= 0 \\
1 \cdot u - x'_i \cdot w &= 0 \quad (\text{duplicate})
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $u, v, w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x'_i (h_{31}x_i + h_{32}y_i + h_{33}) - (h_{11}x_i + h_{12}y_i + h_{13}) &= 0 \\
y'_i (h_{31}x_i + h_{32}y_i + h_{33}) - (h_{21}x_i + h_{22}y_i + h_{23}) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as $\mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$.**

Let $\mathbf{h} = (h_{11}, h_{12}, h_{13}, h_{21}, h_{22}, h_{23}, h_{31}, h_{32}, h_{33})^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^9$. Then:

$$\begin{bmatrix} -x_i & -y_i & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & x'_i x_i & x'_i y_i & x'_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & -x_i & -y_i & -1 & y'_i x_i & y'_i y_i & y'_i \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Stack $N$ correspondences.**

For $N \ge 4$ (with non-degenerate configuration), we assemble the full system:

$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{2N \times 9}$$

**Step 5 — Solve via SVD (Hartley normalization recommended).**

The solution is the **right singular vector of $\mathbf{A}$ corresponding to its smallest singular value** $\sigma_9$. In MATLAB-style pseudocode:

```
[U, S, V] = svd(A);
H = reshape(V(:,9), 3, 3);     % H defined up to scale
H = H / H(3,3);                % optional normalization
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hartley Normalization (Pre-DLT step — boosts accuracy by 10×):**
> (a) Translate all points so their centroid is the origin.
> (b) Scale so the average distance from the origin is $\sqrt{2}$.
> (c) Solve DLT in normalized coordinates, then **unnormalize**:
> $$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{T}'^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{\text{norm}}\mathbf{T}$$
> where $\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{T}'$ are the 3 × 3 similarity transforms applied to image 1 and image 2.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Decomposing H into R, t, n (Faugeras 1990)

Given $\mathbf{H}$ and known $\mathbf{K}$, we want to recover the motion $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ and plane $(\mathbf{n}, d)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{K}$.**

This removes the intrinsic distortion, leaving a Euclidean homography:

$$\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Notice that columns of $\mathbf{H}'$ are sums of a rotated basis plus a scaled normal.**

$$\mathbf{H}' = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{r}_1 + \alpha\,\mathbf{n} & \mathbf{r}_2 + \alpha\,\mathbf{n} & \mathbf{r}_3 + \alpha\,\mathbf{n} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \alpha = t/d$$

Wait — more carefully, $\mathbf{R} = [\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \mathbf{r}_3]$, so:

$$\mathbf{H}' = \left[\mathbf{r}_1 + \alpha n_1 \mathbf{t}, \; \mathbf{r}_2 + \alpha n_2 \mathbf{t}, \; \mathbf{r}_3 + \alpha n_3 \mathbf{t}\right]$$

This is awkward; the standard trick is to **solve for $\mathbf{t}$ direction only**, treating $d$ and $\mathbf{n}$ as unknowns.

**Step 3 — SVD trick.**

Let $\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$. Define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_1 &= +\frac{\sqrt{1 + \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \sigma_3 - \sigma_1^2 - \sigma_2^2 - \sigma_3^2}}{\sqrt{2}}\,\mathbf{u}_3 \times \mathbf{v}_3 \\
\mathbf{v}_2 &= \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2 \sigma_3 - 1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\mathbf{v}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

There are **four algebraic solutions** (sign + permutation combinations). Geometric validation (positive depth, $|\mathbf{R}| = 1$, etc.) retains the **physically valid one**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Favourite Derivation:** You are *not* expected to reproduce the SVD trick in full; the standard expectation is to know the **master formula** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R} + \mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ and explain it qualitatively. The 4-solution branch structure is sufficient for 14-mark answers.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A robot views a planar floor patch. Two successive images give 4 point correspondences (in pixels, with $\mathbf{K} = \text{diag}(500, 500, 1)$, zero skew, principal point at origin):

| $i$ | $(x, y)$ (Image 1) | $(x', y')$ (Image 2) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (0, 0) | (10, 5) |
| 2 | (100, 0) | (115, 4) |
| 3 | (100, 100) | (120, 110) |
| 4 | (0, 100) | (5, 115) |

**Step 1 — Build the DLT matrix $\mathbf{A}$ (first correspondence).**

From $(x, y, x', y') = (0, 0, 10, 5)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 10 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Stacking all 4 correspondences** gives an $8 \times 9$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Hartley normalization:** centroids at $(50, 50)$, mean radius $\sqrt{2}\cdot 50\sqrt{2}$ — actually $50\sqrt{2}$. Scale factor $s = \sqrt{2}/(50\sqrt{2}) = 1/50$.

**Step 4 — SVD of $\mathbf{A}_{\text{norm}}$** gives the smallest singular vector, reshaped into:

$$\mathbf{H}_{\text{norm}} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.05 & 0.00 & 0.10 \\ -0.01 & 1.10 & 0.05 \\ 0.0001 & 0.0002 & 1.00 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Unnormalize:** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{T}'^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{\text{norm}}\mathbf{T}$ recovers the pixel-space homography. (Detailed numerical continuation omitted here for brevity; the procedure is exactly as described in Hartley-Zisserman §4.1.)

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, you will *not* be asked to compute a full SVD. You **will** be asked to:
> 1. Write the DLT linear system.
> 2. State the number of correspondences needed (≥ 4).
> 3. Mention Hartley normalization.
> 4. Justify the 8-DoF count.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Planar Homography Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3D Points X on Plane pi] --> B[Camera 1 Frame]
    B --> C[Camera 1 Projection K1]
    C --> D[Image 1 Points x]
    A --> E[Camera 2 Frame R t]
    E --> F[Camera 2 Projection K2]
    F --> G[Image 2 Points xp]
    D --> H[Estimate H via DLT]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply Hartley Normalization]
    I --> J[Recover R t n d]
    J --> K[Robot Ego Motion Update]
    K --> L[SLAM or Visual Odometry]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DLT Algorithm Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Input N Point Correspondences] --> S2[Hartley Normalization T and Tp]
    S2 --> S3[Build 2N x 9 Matrix A]
    S3 --> S4[SVD of A equals U S V transpose]
    S4 --> S5[Extract V column 9 as h]
    S5 --> S6[Reshape to 3x3 H norm]
    S6 --> S7[Unnormalize H equals Tp inverse H norm T]
    S7 --> S8[Refine via Sampson or Levenberg]
    S8 --> S9[Final H Matrix]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Use-Case Map in Mobile Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Planar Homography H] --> U1[Monocular Visual Odometry]
    P --> U2[ORB SLAM Place Recognition]
    P --> U3[AR Pose Estimation]
    P --> U4[Drone Aerial Mosaicking]
    P --> U5[Autonomous Car Lane Detection IPM]
    P --> U6[Robot Floor Mapping]
    U1 --> M1[Pose Graph Optimization]
    U2 --> M1
    U3 --> M2[Virtual Object Overlay]
    U4 --> M3[Ortho Photo Generation]
    U5 --> M4[Bird Eye View]
    U6 --> M5[Occupancy Grid Update]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Degeneracy Conditions (What Breaks H?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Scene and Motion} --> R[Planar and Translating]
    Q --> S[Non planar and Translating]
    Q --> T[Planar and Pure Rotation]
    R --> U[Homography H valid 8 DoF]
    S --> V[Fundamental Matrix F 7 DoF]
    T --> W[Homography H valid special case]
    U --> X[Recover R t n d]
    V --> Y[Recover R t up to scale]
    W --> Z[Recover R only]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "When does homography **degenerate**?", the answer is: when three or more of the four (or more) point correspondences are **collinear**, or when the plane is parallel to the camera baseline. In these cases $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}$ loses rank, and the DLT fails to give a unique $\mathbf{H}$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Q1. Define planar homography. State its degrees of freedom.**

**Model Answer:**
> Planar homography is a 3 × 3 invertible projective transformation matrix $\mathbf{H}$ that maps every 2D point in one image of a planar scene to its corresponding point in another image of the same plane, expressed in homogeneous coordinates as $\mathbf{x}' \sim \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$. It has **8 degrees of freedom** because the 9 entries of the matrix are defined only up to a non-zero scale factor.
>
> *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for DoF justification.)*

---

**Q2. State the canonical equation relating homography to camera intrinsics, rotation, translation, and plane parameters.**

**Model Answer:**
> $$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\,\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$$
>
> where $\mathbf{K}$ is the intrinsic matrix, $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ are the rotation and translation between two camera views, and $\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X} = d$ is the equation of the planar surface.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for the formula, 1 for variable definitions, 1 for the planar constraint.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *“Derivation + DLT” Type*

**(a)** Starting from the pinhole camera projection model, derive the canonical homography equation
$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R} + \mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ for two views of a planar scene. Clearly state the assumption that the world frame coincides with camera-1's frame. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the Direct Linear Transform (DLT) algorithm for computing $\mathbf{H}$ from $N \ge 4$ point correspondences. Include the role of **Hartley normalization** and the **SVD** step. State the minimum number of correspondences and the rationale for the 8-DoF count. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Place the world frame at camera-1's optical centre. Then the projection of a 3D point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)^{T}$ onto image 1 is:

$$\mathbf{x}_1 \sim \mathbf{K}_1\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Camera 2 has extrinsic $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ relative to camera 1:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{K}_2(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{t})$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Apply the plane constraint $\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X} = d$ by multiplying by the scalar $\frac{\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X}}{d} = 1$:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{t}\cdot\frac{\mathbf{n}^{T}\mathbf{X}}{d}\right) = \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{X}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Substitute $\mathbf{X} \sim \mathbf{K}_1^{-1}\mathbf{x}_1$:

$$\mathbf{x}_2 \sim \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}_1^{-1}\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{x}_1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}_2\left(\mathbf{R} + \frac{\mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}}{d}\right)\mathbf{K}_1^{-1}$$

When $\mathbf{K}_1 = \mathbf{K}_2 = \mathbf{K}$, this simplifies to the canonical form.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For each correspondence $(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}'_i)$, the homogeneous equation $\mathbf{x}'_i \times \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}$ yields 2 linear equations in the 9 entries of $\mathbf{H}$. Assembling all $N$ correspondences produces:

$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{2N \times 9}, \quad \mathbf{h} \in \mathbb{R}^9$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Hartley Normalization** translates the centroid of points in each image to the origin and scales them so the mean radius is $\sqrt{2}$. This conditioning **dramatically improves numerical conditioning** (Hartley showed ~10× accuracy gain) and is applied via similarity transforms $\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{T}'$.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Solve $\mathbf{A}_{\text{norm}}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$ by **SVD**: $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$. The solution is the right singular vector $\mathbf{V}_{:,9}$ corresponding to the **smallest singular value** $\sigma_9$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Reshape to $3\times 3$ and **unnormalize**: $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{T}'^{-1}\mathbf{H}_{\text{norm}}\mathbf{T}$.

**Step 5 (0.5 marks):** Minimum correspondences = **4** (since 8 DoF / 2 equations per point = 4). 8 DoF arises because a 3 × 3 matrix has 9 entries, but homogeneous scale removes 1, leaving **8**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *“Properties + Application” Type*

**(a)** Discuss the geometric properties of planar homography. Explain (i) line preservation, (ii) the transfer of the line at infinity, and (iii) the concept of affine vs. projective homography with the condition for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain how planar homography is used in **monocular visual odometry** for a mobile robot. Describe the workflow from feature detection, through RANSAC-based homography estimation, to motion extraction using the **Faugeras decomposition**. State one situation where the homography model becomes invalid. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Line Preservation.** A homography maps lines to lines. If a line in image 1 is $\mathbf{l}$, then the corresponding line in image 2 is $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{H}^{-T}\mathbf{l}$ (conjugate-transpose transfer). Collinearity is preserved.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Line at Infinity Transfer.** The line at infinity $\mathbf{l}_\infty = (0, 0, 1)^{T}$ transfers to $\mathbf{l}'_\infty = \mathbf{H}^{-T}\mathbf{l}_\infty$. This is non-zero and finite, meaning **parallel lines in image 1 generally become non-parallel in image 2** — the hallmark of projective transformation.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Affine vs. Projective Homography.** A homography is **affine** iff its last row is $(0, 0, 1)$ (i.e., $h_{31} = h_{32} = 0$). It is **pure translation** iff all off-diagonal entries are zero. It is **Euclidean** iff additionally $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}$ (rigid).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** A homography preserves **cross-ratios** of four collinear points, even though lengths and angles are not preserved.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** In monocular visual odometry, **ORB / SIFT / SURF features** are detected in two consecutive frames. Matches are pruned by descriptor distance, then **RANSAC** is run with the homography model — correspondences consistent with $\mathbf{H}$ are inliers.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The best homography (with the most inliers) is re-estimated using **Normalized DLT** and refined via the **Sampson error** or **Levenberg-Marquardt**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Faugeras Decomposition** computes $\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{K}$, performs SVD $\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T}$, and extracts four candidate solutions for $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}, \mathbf{n}, d)$. The physically valid one is selected by enforcing positive depth.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The recovered rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation direction $\mathbf{t}/\|\mathbf{t}\|$ update the robot's pose estimate in a sliding-window filter (EKF / pose-graph).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Invalidation condition:** Homography fails when the scene is **non-planar AND the camera translates** (general 3D motion). In that case the fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$ is the correct model.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the scale invariance.** Writing $\mathbf{H}$ as a 9-DoF matrix is a *direct loss of 1 mark*. Always state "$\mathbf{H}$ is defined up to a non-zero scalar, hence 8 DoF."
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{R} + \mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d$ with $\mathbf{R} - \mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d$.** The **+** sign is correct *only* when the world frame is camera-1's frame. The **−** sign is used when the world frame is *behind* camera 2 (Faugeras' original formulation). Examiners will not penalize if you state your convention explicitly.
> 3. **Stating "DLT needs 4 correspondences" without justification.** Always add: "because homography has 8 DoF and each correspondence gives 2 independent equations."
> 4. **Skipping Hartley normalization in the DLT solution.** Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for explicitly mentioning conditioning.
> 5. **Writing $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{KRK}^{-1}$ for the general case.** This is true **only** if $\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0}$ (pure rotation). The plane-induced term $\mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d$ must appear in the general answer.
> 6. **Mixing fundamental matrix terminology with homography.** A fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$ has 7 DoF and is rank-2; a homography $\mathbf{H}$ has 8 DoF and is full rank. Confusing these is a recurring high-mark-loss trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Planar Homography**

- **Definition:** $\mathbf{x}' \sim \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$, a 2D projective transformation between two images of a planar (or pure-rotation) scene.
- **Master Equation:** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R} + \mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^{T}/d)\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ — *memorize verbatim*.
- **DoF:** 8 (3 × 3 matrix minus 1 for scale).
- **Minimum correspondences:** 4 (giving 8 equations).
- **Computation:** DLT = build $2N \times 9$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ → SVD → smallest right singular vector → reshape → unnormalize.
- **Hartley Normalization:** Translate centroid to origin, scale mean radius to $\sqrt{2}$, then solve in normalized coordinates, then invert the transform.
- **Properties:** Maps lines to lines, preserves cross-ratios, transfers line at infinity via $\mathbf{H}^{-T}$.
- **Special Cases:**
  - $h_{31} = h_{32} = 0$ → affine (parallelism preserved).
  - Pure rotation ($\mathbf{t} = 0$) → still a valid homography even for non-planar scenes.
  - Pure translation with planar scene → still a valid homography.
- **Decomposition (Faugeras 1990):** SVD of $\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{K}$ gives 4 solutions for $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}/\|\mathbf{t}\|, \mathbf{n}/\|\mathbf{n}\|, 1/d)$.
- **Degeneracies:** Collinear point configurations, or plane parallel to the camera baseline.
- **Applications:** Visual odometry, SLAM loop closure, AR pose, drone mosaicking, lane IPM.
- **Versus Fundamental Matrix:** $\mathbf{F}$ has 7 DoF, rank 2; valid for any 3D scene with translational motion. $\mathbf{H}$ has 8 DoF, full rank; valid for planar scenes or pure rotation only.
- **Order of derivation (always):** Projection → Planarity → Substitution → Factorization → Identification of $\mathbf{H}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Camera Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Camera Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Camera Calibration** is the process of determining the **internal geometric and optical characteristics (intrinsic parameters)** of a camera, together with its **position and orientation (extrinsic parameters)** in a known world coordinate frame, by using images of a reference object (typically a planar checkerboard or a 3D calibration target) of precisely known geometry.

In more rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, camera calibration establishes the mathematical mapping between:
- **3D world coordinates** $(X, Y, Z)$ of physical points in the scene, and
- **2D image coordinates** $(u, v)$ of the corresponding pixels captured by the camera sensor.

This mapping is essential in mobile robotics for tasks such as **visual odometry, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), obstacle detection, and 3D scene reconstruction**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Camera Fingerprint"

Imagine you buy a new pair of prescription eyeglasses. Before they can help you see clearly, an optometrist must **measure your eyes** (focal length, lens curvature, alignment of the optical center). Only after this "eye calibration" can the glasses convert the real world into a sharp image for *your* specific eyes.

A camera is the same! Each camera has its **own unique optical fingerprint**:
- The lens is never perfectly centered on the sensor.
- The focal length is slightly different from what the manufacturer claims.
- The lens introduces subtle bending of light (distortion), especially near the edges.

**Camera calibration is essentially writing the "prescription" of the camera** so that the robot's software can convert raw pixels into mathematically accurate 3D measurements of the real world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Mobile Robotics:** A mobile robot using an uncalibrated camera cannot measure distances, cannot localize itself, and cannot build a map. Calibration is the *first prerequisite* before any computer vision algorithm can be trusted.

## 1.3 Key Parameters Identified by Calibration

| Category | Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic | $f_x, f_y$ | Focal length in pixels (x and y directions) |
| Intrinsic | $c_x, c_y$ | Principal point (optical center) in pixels |
| Intrinsic | $\gamma$ (gamma) | Skew coefficient between sensor axes |
| Intrinsic | $k_1, k_2, k_3$ | Radial distortion coefficients |
| Intrinsic | $p_1, p_2$ | Tangential distortion coefficients |
| Extrinsic | $R$ (3×3) | Rotation from world to camera frame |
| Extrinsic | $t$ (3×1) | Translation from world to camera frame |

## 1.4 The Pinhole Camera Model (Foundation Concept)

A pinhole camera is the ideal mathematical model assumed during calibration. Light from a 3D point $P$ passes through a single infinitesimally small aperture and projects onto an image plane at a corresponding point $p$.

The fundamental pinhole projection equation is:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $s$ is a scalar (depth)
- $(u, v)$ are pixel coordinates
- $(X, Y, Z)$ are world coordinates
- $K$ is the **intrinsic matrix** (3×3)
- $[R \mid t]$ is the **extrinsic matrix** (3×4)
- The right-hand side is a **homogeneous coordinate** in 3D

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Calibration is essentially *solving for* $K$, $R$, $t$, and the distortion coefficients so that the above equation holds true for a set of known 3D-2D correspondences.

## 1.5 Types of Calibration Targets

| Target Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Planar Checkerboard** | 2D grid of black/white squares with known size | Most common (Zhang's method) |
| **Circular Grid** | Array of circles with known spacing | High-precision industrial setups |
| **3D Calibration Object** | Cube/trihedron with markers at known 3D points | High-accuracy lab calibration |
| **Self-Calibration** | No target — uses scene features across multiple views | Online SLAM, structure from motion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Coordinate Frames in Camera Calibration

To understand calibration, you must first understand the **three coordinate systems** involved:

1. **World Frame** $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ — A fixed reference frame attached to the scene or calibration target.
2. **Camera Frame** $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ — Attached to the camera, with origin at the optical center and $Z_c$ axis pointing along the optical axis.
3. **Image Frame** $(u, v)$ — 2D pixel coordinates on the sensor/retina.

The full projection chain is:
$$\text{World} \xrightarrow{[R \mid t]} \text{Camera} \xrightarrow{K, \text{distortion}} \text{Image}$$

## 2.2 Intrinsic Parameters — What They Mean

### Focal Length ($f_x, f_y$)
The focal length is the distance from the optical center to the image plane. In pixel units, it is given by:
$$f_x = \frac{f}{s_x}, \quad f_y = \frac{f}{s_y}$$
where $f$ is the physical focal length (in mm) and $s_x, s_y$ are the physical pixel sizes (in mm/pixel). Modern CMOS sensors often have $f_x \neq f_y$ because pixels are not perfectly square.

### Principal Point $(c_x, c_y)$
The principal point is the pixel coordinate where the optical axis pierces the image plane. For an ideal camera, it should be at the **center** of the image, but manufacturing tolerances push it slightly off-center. Hence, calibration estimates:
$$c_x \approx \frac{\text{width}}{2}, \quad c_y \approx \frac{\text{height}}{2}$$

### Skew Coefficient ($\gamma$)
Modern sensors are aligned, so $\gamma \approx 0$. However, calibration allows a non-zero skew for completeness.

### The Intrinsic Matrix $K$
$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The intrinsic matrix $K$ encodes *everything* about the camera's internal geometry — it is the camera's **mathematical identity card**.

## 2.3 Extrinsic Parameters — Camera Pose

The extrinsic parameters describe **where the camera is** and **where it is looking** relative to the world frame:
$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $R$ is a **3×3 rotation matrix** (orthonormal, $R^T R = I$, $\det R = +1$).
- $t$ is a **3×1 translation vector**.
- Together, $[R \mid t]$ has **6 degrees of freedom** (3 for rotation + 3 for translation).

## 2.4 Lens Distortion — The Real-World Imperfection

Real lenses do not behave like an ideal pinhole. They introduce **distortion**, which calibration must also estimate.

### Radial Distortion (barrel/pincushion)
Light rays bend more as they move away from the optical center. Modeled as a Taylor series in $r^2$:

$$x_{distorted} = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

$$y_{distorted} = y(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.

### Tangential Distortion (decentering)
Caused by the lens not being perfectly aligned with the sensor plane:

$$x_{distorted} = x + 2 p_1 x y + p_2 (r^2 + 2 x^2)$$

$$y_{distorted} = y + p_1 (r^2 + 2 y^2) + 2 p_2 x y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total distortion = Radial + Tangential.** This is the **Brown–Conrady model** used in OpenCV's `calibrateCamera()` function.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $s \tilde{p} = K [R \mid t] \tilde{P}$ | Full projection equation | Core calibration equation |
| $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic matrix | 5 DOF intrinsic model |
| $X_c = R X_w + t$ | World-to-camera transform | Extrinsic model |
| $x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$ | Radial distortion (x) | Wide-angle lens correction |
| $y_d = y(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$ | Radial distortion (y) | Wide-angle lens correction |
| $x_d = x + 2 p_1 x y + p_2(r^2 + 2 x^2)$ | Tangential distortion (x) | Decentering correction |
| $y_d = y + p_1(r^2 + 2 y^2) + 2 p_2 x y$ | Tangential distortion (y) | Decentering correction |
| $H = K [r_1 \; r_2 \; t]$ | Planar homography (Zhang) | Calibration from planar target |
| $B = K^{-T} K^{-1}$ | Image of absolute conic | Zhang's constraint |
| $r_1, r_2$ orthonormal; $r_3 = r_1 \times r_2$ | Rotation orthonormality | Extrinsic recovery |
| $h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0$ | Zhang's constraint #1 | Solving for $K$ |
| $(h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_1) = (h_2^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2)$ | Zhang's constraint #2 | Solving for $K$ |
| Reprojection error $= \sum_i \vert p_i - \hat{p}_i \vert^2$ | Calibration quality metric | Optimization objective |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in formulas. Do not use the pipe symbol `|` inside tables — it breaks the markdown table structure.

## 2.6 Why Camera Calibration is Crucial in Mobile Robotics

A mobile robot equipped with a camera needs to:
1. **Measure distances** to obstacles (depth from stereo or monocular vision).
2. **Localize itself** in an environment (visual odometry).
3. **Build 3D maps** of surroundings (visual SLAM).
4. **Detect and track objects** (lane lines, pedestrians, AR markers).

All these tasks assume the camera's projection model is known. An **uncalibrated camera** is like a **ruler with unknown scale** — you can see *shapes*, but you cannot measure *quantities*. Calibration turns a camera into a **quantitative measurement device**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pinhole Projection Equation

### Starting Point: 3D World Point → Camera Frame
Let a 3D point in the world be $P_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$. To express it in the camera's coordinate system, we apply a rigid-body transformation (rotation $R$ and translation $t$):

$$P_c = R P_w + t$$

In homogeneous form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- $R$ rotates the world axes so they align with the camera axes.
- $t$ shifts the origin of the world to the camera's optical center.
- The $0^T$ row keeps the homogeneous coordinate equal to 1.

### Step 2: Camera Frame → Normalized Image Plane
The pinhole model projects a 3D point onto the image plane by **perspective division** (dividing by depth $Z_c$):

$$x_{norm} = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_{norm} = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

This is the **normalized image coordinate** (a dimensionless ratio).

### Step 3: Apply Intrinsic Parameters
The normalized coordinates are converted to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ by applying the intrinsic matrix $K$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} x_{norm} \\ y_{norm} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c / Z_c \\ Y_c / Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Combine into the Full Projection Equation
Substituting back, we get the **single unified equation**:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $s = Z_c$ is the depth scaling factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $\sim$ (similarity) symbol?** Pixel coordinates are defined up to a non-zero scale factor because they are expressed in homogeneous form. We can multiply both sides by any non-zero scalar and still represent the same pixel.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Homography for a Planar Target (Zhang's Method)

### Setup
Place a planar calibration target (e.g., checkerboard) on the $Z_w = 0$ plane. Then for every point on the target, $Z_w = 0$. The world coordinates become $(X_w, Y_w, 0, 1)^T$.

### Step 1: Simplify the Projection
The extrinsic matrix columns are $r_1, r_2, r_3$ (the columns of $R$) and $t$. For $Z_w = 0$:

$$K \begin{bmatrix} R & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & r_3 & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The third column $r_3$ multiplies $Z_w = 0$, so it drops out:

$$= K \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Define the Homography
The 3×3 matrix that maps a 2D point on the calibration plane to its image is called the **homography**:

$$H = K \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & t \end{bmatrix}$$

So the projection becomes:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = H \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Homography from a Single View
Given a checkerboard image, we can find $H$ by detecting corner points and solving a linear system (e.g., Direct Linear Transform). However, $H$ is a 3×3 matrix with 8 DOF (scale is irrelevant in homogeneous coordinates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Each image of the planar target gives one homography $H$. To solve for the 5 intrinsic DOF, we need at least **3 views** (each provides 2 constraints), giving 6 equations for 5 unknowns.

## 3.3 Derivation of Zhang's Constraints on the Intrinsic Matrix

### Step 1: Express $H$ in Terms of Its Columns
Let $H = \begin{bmatrix} h_1 & h_2 & h_3 \end{bmatrix}$ (three 3×1 column vectors). Then:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} h_1 & h_2 & h_3 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda K \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & t \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\lambda$ is a non-zero scalar. Therefore:

$$r_1 = \frac{1}{\lambda} K^{-1} h_1, \quad r_2 = \frac{1}{\lambda} K^{-1} h_2, \quad t = \frac{1}{\lambda} K^{-1} h_3$$

### Step 2: Use Orthonormality of Rotation
Since $R$ is a rotation matrix, its columns are orthonormal: $r_1^T r_2 = 0$ and $r_1^T r_1 = r_2^T r_2$.

This gives two constraints per image:

**Constraint 1** ($r_1 \perp r_2$):

$$h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0$$

**Constraint 2** ($\vert r_1 \vert = \vert r_2 \vert$):

$$h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_1 = h_2^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2$$

### Step 3: Define the Image of the Absolute Conic
Let $B = K^{-T} K^{-1}$. This is a symmetric 3×3 matrix with 6 unknowns. Each image provides 2 constraints. So **3 images** give 6 equations — enough to solve for $B$ (up to scale).

### Step 4: Recover $K$ from $B$
Once $B$ is known, $K$ is obtained via Cholesky decomposition of $B^{-1}$ (since $B = K^{-T} K^{-1}$ implies $B^{-1} = K K^T$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Zhang's 2000 paper ("A Flexible New Technique for Camera Calibration") is the most cited calibration method. The above derivation is the **expected answer** for any 14-mark derivation question.

## 3.4 Derivation of Reprojection Error (Optimization Objective)

After an initial estimate of $K$, $R_i$, $t_i$ for each view $i$, calibration refines them by **minimizing the reprojection error**:

$$E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \vert p_{ij} - \hat{p}_{ij}(K, R_i, t_i, P_j) \vert^2$$

where:
- $N$ = number of calibration images
- $M$ = number of corner points per image
- $p_{ij}$ = observed pixel location of corner $j$ in image $i$
- $\hat{p}_{ij}$ = pixel location predicted by the current estimate of $K, R_i, t_i$
- $P_j$ = known 3D world coordinate of corner $j$

This is solved using **Levenberg–Marquardt non-linear least squares**.

**Quality benchmark:** A "good" calibration typically achieves a **reprojection error below 0.3 pixels**.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A camera has focal length $f = 8$ mm, pixel size $s = 0.004$ mm, and an image sensor of resolution $1280 \times 960$ pixels. The principal point is at the image center. Compute the intrinsic matrix $K$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute focal length in pixels:

$$f_x = f_y = \frac{f}{s} = \frac{8 \text{ mm}}{0.004 \text{ mm/pixel}} = 2000 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2:** Compute principal point in pixels:

$$c_x = \frac{1280}{2} = 640, \quad c_y = \frac{960}{2} = 480$$

**Step 3:** Assume skew $\gamma = 0$. Then:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & 0 & 640 \\ 0 & 2000 & 480 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (KTU Exam Style):** A 3D point at $P_w = (0.5, 0.3, 2.0)^T$ meters is observed. With $R = I$ and $t = 0$, the projection is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Normalized:

$$x_{norm} = \frac{0.5}{2.0} = 0.25, \quad y_{norm} = \frac{0.3}{2.0} = 0.15$$

Pixel coordinates:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_{norm} + c_x = 2000 \cdot 0.25 + 640 = 500 + 640 = 1140$$

$$v = f_y \cdot y_{norm} + c_y = 2000 \cdot 0.15 + 480 = 300 + 480 = 780$$

**Answer:** The 3D point projects to pixel $(u, v) = (1140, 780)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Camera Calibration Workflow (Zhang's Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect N>=10 images of checkerboard from different angles] --> B[Detect corner points in each image using OpenCV findChessboardCorners]
    B --> C[Refine corner locations to sub-pixel accuracy using cornerSubPix]
    C --> D[Generate 3D world coordinates for checkerboard corners]
    D --> E[Compute homography H for each image using DLT]
    E --> F[Form linear system from Zhang constraints h1T B h2 equals 0 and h1T B h1 equals h2T B h2]
    F --> G[Solve for symmetric matrix B = KinvT times Kinv]
    G --> H[Cholesky decomposition of Binverse to recover K]
    H --> I[Compute initial extrinsic parameters R and t for each view]
    I --> J[Minimize reprojection error using Levenberg Marquardt]
    J --> K[Output: K matrix, distortion coefficients, R and t per view]
    K --> L[End: Save calibration as YAML or XML file]
```

## 4.2 Coordinate Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[World Point P_w in 3D space] --> T1[Apply rotation R and translation t]
    T1 --> P2[Camera Frame Point P_c]
    P2 --> T2[Perspective division by Z_c]
    T2 --> P3[Normalized image coordinates]
    P3 --> T3[Apply intrinsic matrix K]
    T3 --> P4[Pixel coordinates u and v]
    P4 --> T4[Apply distortion model radial plus tangential]
    T4 --> P5[Distorted pixel coordinates u_d and v_d]
    P5 --> P6[Final observed pixel location]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Calibration System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Calibration Target Checkerboard]
        A2[Multiple View Captures N images]
    end
    subgraph DetectionLayer[FEATURE DETECTION LAYER]
        B1[Corner Detection Module]
        B2[Sub pixel Refinement Module]
        B3[3D World Point Generator]
    end
    subgraph EstimationLayer[PARAMETER ESTIMATION LAYER]
        C1[Homography Solver DLT]
        C2[Linear Solver for Intrinsic Matrix K]
        C3[Extrinsic Parameter Extractor R and t]
    end
    subgraph OptimizationLayer[NON LINEAR REFINEMENT LAYER]
        D1[Reprojection Error Function]
        D2[Levenberg Marquardt Optimizer]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[OUTPUT LAYER]
        E1[Intrinsic Matrix K]
        E2[Distortion Coefficients k1 k2 p1 p2 k3]
        E3[Per View Extrinsics R and t]
        E4[Reprojection Error Statistic]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D2 --> E3
    D2 --> E4
```

## 4.4 Distortion Geometry Visualization (Barrel vs Pincushion)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IdealGrid[IDEAL STRAIGHT GRID]
        G1[Lines should be perfectly straight]
    end
    subgraph Barrel[BARREL DISTORTION positive k1]
        B1[Lines bulge outward]
        B2[Common in wide angle lenses]
        B3[Correction k1 negative]
    end
    subgraph Pincushion[PINCUSHION DISTORTION negative k1]
        P1[Lines pinch inward]
        P2[Common in telephoto lenses]
        P3[Correction k1 positive]
    end
    subgraph Tangential[TANGENTIAL DISTORTION p1 p2 nonzero]
        T1[Lines appear sheared or tilted]
        T2[Caused by lens decentering]
        T3[Correction adjust p1 and p2]
    end
    IdealGrid --> Barrel
    IdealGrid --> Pincushion
    IdealGrid --> Tangential
```

## 4.5 Zhang's Algorithm — Closed-Form vs Refinement Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Closed Form Initial Guess] --> B[Collect 3 to 5 checkerboard views]
    B --> C[Find homography Hi for each view]
    C --> D[Build Zhang constraint matrix V]
    D --> E[Singular Value Decomposition of V]
    E --> F[Recover b vector and thus B matrix]
    F --> G[Cholesky decomposition for initial K]
    G --> H[Recover R and t for each view]
    H --> I[Stage 2: Non Linear Refinement]
    I --> J[Define reprojection error objective]
    J --> K[Levenberg Marquardt iteration]
    K --> L{Converged}
    L -->|No| K
    L -->|Yes| M[Final calibrated parameters]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define intrinsic and extrinsic parameters of a camera. Why are both needed for calibration? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Intrinsic parameters** are the internal characteristics of the camera: focal length $(f_x, f_y)$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, skew coefficient $\gamma$, and lens distortion coefficients $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$. They describe *how* the camera forms an image internally.
- **Extrinsic parameters** describe the camera's position and orientation in the world: the 3×3 rotation matrix $R$ and 3×1 translation vector $t$.
- **Why both?** Intrinsics convert normalized coordinates to pixels, while extrinsics convert world coordinates to camera coordinates. Without *both*, the projection from 3D world to 2D pixels cannot be computed.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. What is radial distortion in a camera lens? Mention the mathematical model used to correct it. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- Radial distortion is the bending of light rays as they move away from the optical center of the lens, causing straight lines in the real world to appear curved in the image (barrel or pincushion shape).
- It is corrected using the polynomial model:

$$x_{corrected} = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

$$y_{corrected} = y(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ and $k_1, k_2, k_3$ are radial distortion coefficients estimated during calibration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of the pinhole camera model, derive the full camera projection equation relating 3D world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ to 2D image pixel coordinates $(u, v)$. Clearly define the intrinsic matrix $K$ and extrinsic parameters $[R \mid t]$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — World to Camera Frame Transformation:**
A 3D world point $P_w = (X, Y, Z)^T$ is first transformed into the camera coordinate frame using a rigid body transformation. The camera may be rotated by $R$ and translated by $t$ relative to the world:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Perspective Projection (Pinhole Geometry):**
The pinhole model projects a 3D point onto the image plane by dividing by the depth (the $Z_c$ coordinate):

$$x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

where $(x_n, y_n)$ are the **normalized image coordinates** in a unit-less image plane at unit focal length.

**Step 3 — Apply Intrinsic Parameters to Get Pixel Coordinates:**
The pixel coordinates are obtained by applying the intrinsic matrix $K$, which includes focal length in pixels, principal point, and skew:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim K \begin{bmatrix} x_n \\ y_n \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting $x_n, y_n$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c / Z_c \\ Y_c / Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into One Equation:**
Defining the depth scalar $s = Z_c$ and combining with the world-to-camera step, we get the unified pinhole projection equation:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Definitions:**
- $K$ = **intrinsic matrix** (3×3), encodes internal camera geometry.
- $[R \mid t]$ = **extrinsic matrix** (3×4), encodes camera pose in the world.

---

**(b)** Explain the Brown–Conrady distortion model with both radial and tangential components. A camera has $k_1 = -0.25$, $k_2 = 0.12$, and a normalized image point $(x, y) = (0.4, 0.3)$. Compute the corrected (distorted) coordinates $x_d$ and $y_d$ (ignore $k_3$, $p_1$, $p_2$). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Brown–Conrady Model:**
The total distortion is the sum of radial and tangential distortion components. The **radial distortion** model is:

$$x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

$$y_d = y(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r^2$:**
Given $(x, y) = (0.4, 0.3)$:

$$r^2 = x^2 + y^2 = (0.4)^2 + (0.3)^2 = 0.16 + 0.09 = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute $r^4$:**
$$r^4 = (r^2)^2 = (0.25)^2 = 0.0625$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Radial Factor:**
With $k_1 = -0.25$, $k_2 = 0.12$:

$$1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 = 1 + (-0.25)(0.25) + (0.12)(0.0625)$$

$$= 1 - 0.0625 + 0.0075 = 0.945$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Distortion:**
$$x_d = x \cdot 0.945 = 0.4 \cdot 0.945 = 0.378$$

$$y_d = y \cdot 0.945 = 0.3 \cdot 0.945 = 0.2835$$

**Final Answer:** $(x_d, y_d) = (0.378, 0.2835)$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *which* point is undistorted and *which* is distorted. **Always remember:** $x$ and $y$ are the **ideal (undistorted) normalized coordinates**, and the equations give the **observed (distorted) coordinates** $x_d, y_d$. During rectification, you must invert this mapping.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe **Zhang's planar calibration method**. Why is it more practical than 3D target-based calibration? State the two key constraints derived from homography. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Method Overview:**
Zhang's method (2000) uses a **planar calibration target** (e.g., a printed checkerboard) photographed from **at least 3 different orientations** by the camera. Corners on the checkerboard are detected automatically.

**Step 2 — Why It Is Practical:**
- It does not require expensive 3D calibration rigs.
- A printed checkerboard on paper is sufficient.
- It is highly accurate and forms the basis of OpenCV's `calibrateCamera()` function.
- It can be performed by any end-user in the field.

**Step 3 — Procedure:**
1. Capture $N \geq 10$ images of the checkerboard at different angles.
2. Detect corner points $(u_i, v_i)$ in each image.
3. Compute the homography $H_i$ for each view via Direct Linear Transform (DLT).
4. Set up Zhang's two constraints and solve linearly for the intrinsic matrix $K$.
5. Recover extrinsics $(R_i, t_i)$ for each view.
6. Refine all parameters by minimizing reprojection error.

**Step 4 — Zhang's Two Constraints:**
Let $B = K^{-T} K^{-1}$ and let $H = [h_1 \; h_2 \; h_3]$. The two constraints are:

**Constraint 1** (orthogonality of $r_1$ and $r_2$):

$$h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0$$

**Constraint 2** (equal magnitude of $r_1$ and $r_2$):

$$h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_1 = h_2^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2$$

Each image gives 2 such constraints. With 3 images, we have 6 equations to solve for the 5 intrinsic DOF.

---

**(b)** A camera has an intrinsic matrix:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

A 3D world point is at $(X, Y, Z) = (0.4, 0.2, 2.0)$ m. Given $R = I$ and $t = 0$, compute the pixel coordinates $(u, v)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — World to Camera Frame:**
With $R = I$ (identity, no rotation) and $t = 0$ (no translation):

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.2 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Perspective Division (Normalize by Depth):**
$$x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c} = \frac{0.4}{2.0} = 0.20$$

$$y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} = \frac{0.2}{2.0} = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Apply Intrinsic Matrix K:**
Substitute into the projection equation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.20 \\ 0.10 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Matrix Multiplication:**
- $u = 800 \cdot 0.20 + 0 \cdot 0.10 + 320 \cdot 1 = 160 + 0 + 320 = 480$
- $v = 0 \cdot 0.20 + 800 \cdot 0.10 + 240 \cdot 1 = 0 + 80 + 240 = 320$

**Final Answer:** Pixel coordinates are $(u, v) = (480, 320)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting perspective division** — students often directly multiply $K$ by $(X, Y, Z)$ without first dividing by $Z$. This is the **#1 most common error** in calibration questions. Always normalize by depth first.
> 2. **Mixing up the order of transformations** — the correct order is World → Camera (using $[R \mid t]$) → Normalize by $Z_c$ → Apply $K$. The reverse order is wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — $K$ contains focal length in **pixels**, but world points are in **meters**. The matrix $K$ handles this via $f_x, f_y$ which include the unit conversion.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of constraints** — Zhang's constraints MUST be derived from the orthonormality of $R$, not just stated. A 7-mark question expects at least the constraint equations to be shown.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Before the Exam:**

- **Camera calibration = finding $K$, $R$, $t$, and distortion coefficients** that map 3D world points to 2D image pixels.
- **Pinhole equation to memorize:**
  $$s \tilde{p} = K [R \mid t] \tilde{P}$$
- **Intrinsic matrix $K$** has 5 DOF: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$.
- **Extrinsic matrix $[R \mid t]$** has 6 DOF: 3 rotation + 3 translation.
- **Lens distortion = radial (k1, k2, k3) + tangential (p1, p2)** — Brown–Conrady model.
- **Zhang's method** uses a **planar checkerboard** with $\geq 3$ views (recommended $\geq 10$).
- **Two Zhang constraints** per image:
  1. $h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2 = 0$
  2. $h_1^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_1 = h_2^T K^{-T} K^{-1} h_2$
- **Reprojection error** is the gold standard for calibration quality. Aim for **$< 0.3$ pixels**.
- **Levenberg–Marquardt** is used to refine parameters non-linearly.
- **Distortion coefficients:** $k_1 < 0$ → pincushion, $k_1 > 0$ → barrel (sometimes opposite depending on convention — verify with OpenCV docs).
- **Homography $H$** for a planar target simplifies the projection to a 2D-to-2D mapping.
- **In mobile robotics**, calibration is the prerequisite for visual odometry, SLAM, and 3D reconstruction.
- **Practical tip:** OpenCV provides `cv2.calibrateCamera()` which implements Zhang's method with LM refinement.
- **Units check:** Pixel coordinates are dimensionless; world coordinates are typically in meters; $K$ bridges the two via $f_x = f / s$ where $s$ is pixel pitch in meters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Appearance and Shading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Object Appearance and Shading # MODULE 2 — OBJECT APPEARANCE AND SHADING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Object Appearance and Shading** refers to the set of photometric, geometric, and radiometric properties of a surface that determine how light interacts with the surface and how the resulting reflected radiance is captured by a vision sensor. In mobile robotics, shading analysis enables a robot to infer **3-D shape, surface orientation, material type, and illumination conditions** from 2-D images — a foundational capability for visual navigation, SLAM, and object recognition. Mathematically, *appearance* is described by the **radiance** $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\omega})$ leaving a surface point $\mathbf{x}$ in direction $\boldsymbol{\omega}$, which depends on: - the **Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF)** $f_r(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i, \boldsymbol{\omega}_r)$ of the material, - the **incident illumination** $L_i(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$, - the **surface normal** $\mathbf{n}(\mathbf{x})$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2, Unit 2.3:** The course outcome mapping requires students to derive the image irradiance equation, classify reflection models (diffuse, specular, glossy), and apply Lambert's Cosine Law to estimate surface normals — all of which fall under Object Appearance and Shading. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you walk into a room at night and switch on a single lamp. A white ball, a shiny metal sphere, and a piece of black velvet all sit on the table. Even though the *light source is identical*, the three objects look completely different. Why? - The **white ball** appears uniformly bright from every angle → *diffuse reflection* (Lambertian). - The **metal sphere** shows a bright *highlight* that moves as you move → *specular reflection* (mirror-like). - The **black velvet** appears almost uniformly dark → *low albedo / high absorption*. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Hook for Robotics Students:** A mobile robot's camera is essentially "the eye walking into the room." By *mathematically inverting* the shading equation, the robot can recover surface normals (a clue to 3-D shape) and material properties (a clue to *what* the object is). This is the foundation of *photometric stereo*, *shape-from-shading*, and *BRDF-based object recognition* in robotics. ### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Units (SI) | Typical Range in Robotics | |---|---|---|---| | $L$ | Radiance | $\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{sr}^{-1}$ | $0.1 - 10^4$ | | $E$ | Irradiance | $\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}$ | $0.01 - 10^3$ | | $I$ | Radiant Intensity | $\text{W}\cdot\text{sr}^{-1}$ | $0.1 - 10^2$ | | $f_r$ | BRDF | $\text{sr}^{-1}$ | $0 - \infty$ | | $\rho$ | Albedo (reflectivity) | dimensionless | $0 - 1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wavelength:** Visible light in robotics vision is typically $400\,\text{nm} \le \lambda \le 700\,\text{nm}$. Standard color cameras sample the spectrum into **R, G, B** channels with peak sensitivities near $\mathbf{700\,nm}$, $\mathbf{546\,nm}$, and $\mathbf{436\,nm}$ respectively. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Image Formation Pipeline Object appearance is the end-product of a chain of physical events. The chain for a robotic vision system is: 1. **Light Source Emission** → produces a spectral power distribution $L_i(\lambda)$. 2. **Light Transport** → light travels and may be reflected/scattered by other surfaces. 3. **Surface Interaction** → incident light strikes a surface point $\mathbf{x}$ with normal $\mathbf{n}$. 4. **Reflection / Scattering** → the BRDF $f_r$ redistributes the energy into outgoing directions. 5. **Sensor Capture** → a lens projects the reflected radiance onto a CCD/CMOS pixel. 6. **Image Formation** → pixel value $I(\mathbf{u})$ is proportional to scene radiance. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for mobile robots:** A mobile robot cannot separate the above steps by *just looking* at a single image. However, with multiple images (from different viewpoints, under different lights, or with known priors), the chain becomes *invertible* — this is the basis of **multi-view stereo, photometric stereo, NeRF, and SLAM**. ## 2.2 Light Source Models in Robotics | Type | Description | Irradiance at Distance $d$ | When Used in Robotics | |---|---|---|---| | **Point source** | Originates from a single point | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Indoor lamps, structured light projectors | | **Directional source** | Rays parallel, from $\infty$ | $E = E_\infty \cos\theta$ (constant) | Sunlight, controlled lab illumination | | **Area source** | Extended emitter | Requires integration over the area | Diffuse ceiling lights, softboxes | | **Ambient** | Uniform everywhere | $E = E_a$ (constant) | Background "fill" light approximation | > For a directional source, the irradiance is **independent of distance** — this is why outdoor robots treat the sun as a directional source. Indoor robots typically treat room lights as **point or area sources**. ## 2.3 Three Fundamental Reflection Types ### (a) Diffuse (Lambertian) Reflection Light is scattered **equally in all outgoing directions**. The surface appears equally bright from every viewing angle. A matte chalk wall is a near-perfect example. ### (b) Specular Reflection Light is reflected at an angle equal to the incidence angle relative to the surface normal (law of reflection). A polished metal mirror is the ideal case. ### (c) Glossy (Hybrid) Reflection A combination of diffuse and specular — most real materials. Polished wood, plastic, and human skin are glossy. ## 2.4 Lambert's Cosine Law (KTU High-Yield Topic) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lambert's Cosine Law** states that the observed brightness of a Lambertian surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle between the **surface normal** $\mathbf{n}$ and the **direction of illumination** $\boldsymbol{\ell}$. This is the single most-examined result in shading problems. For a Lambertian surface with albedo $\rho$ under incident light $L_i$: $$I(\mathbf{x}) = \rho \, (\mathbf{n} \cdot \boldsymbol{\ell}) \, L_i$$ provided $\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell} > 0$ (else the surface is in self-shadow and $I=0$). - $\mathbf{n}$ = unit surface normal. - $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ = unit vector pointing *from* the surface *toward* the light. - $\rho$ = diffuse albedo, $0 \le \rho \le 1$. ## 2.5 The Phong Reflection Model The **Phong model** approximates a glossy surface as the sum of three terms: ambient + diffuse + specular. It is the most widely used model in computer graphics and appears frequently in KTU exam questions on shading. $$I(\mathbf{x}) = k_a L_a + k_d (\mathbf{n} \cdot \boldsymbol{\ell}) L_d + k_s (\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{v})^\alpha L_s$$ - $k_a, k_d, k_s$ = ambient, diffuse, specular reflection coefficients ($k_a+k_d+k_s \le 1$ for energy conservation). - $L_a, L_d, L_s$ = light intensities. - $\mathbf{r}$ = mirror reflection direction of $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ about $\mathbf{n}$. - $\mathbf{v}$ = unit vector from surface to viewer (camera). - $\alpha$ = specular exponent (shininess). Larger $\alpha$ = sharper highlight. The reflection vector $\mathbf{r}$ is: $$\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{n} \cdot \boldsymbol{\ell})\mathbf{n} - \boldsymbol{\ell}$$ ## 2.6 The Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF) The **BRDF** is the *complete* description of a material's reflective behavior — it is the most general formulation and subsumes Lambert, Phong, Cook-Torrance, etc. $$f_r(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i, \boldsymbol{\omega}_r) = \frac{dL_r(\boldsymbol{\omega}_r)}{dE_i(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i)} \quad \left[\text{sr}^{-1}\right]$$ The **rendering equation** (Kajiya, 1986) then gives outgoing radiance: $$L_r(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_r) = \int_{\Omega^+} f_r(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i, \boldsymbol{\omega}_r) \, L_i(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_i) \, (\mathbf{n} \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega}_i) \, d\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$$ The integral is over the upper hemisphere $\Omega^+$ of incoming directions. > [!NOTE] > **Reciprocity (Helmholtz):** $f_r(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i, \boldsymbol{\omega}_r) = f_r(\boldsymbol{\omega}_r, \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$. This is a strict physical constraint and is used by mobile robots in novel-view-synthesis to validate reflectance estimates. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The table below is your revision sheet.** All KTU problems on shading reduce to one or more of these equations. | # | Equation | Description | Key Constraints | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Point source irradiance | $d > 0$ | | 2 | $E = E_\infty \cos\theta$ | Directional source irradiance | $\cos\theta \ge 0$ | | 3 | $I = \rho(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})L_i$ | Lambertian shading | $\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell} > 0$ | | 4 | $\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})\mathbf{n} - \boldsymbol{\ell}$ | Reflection vector | $\vert\mathbf{n}\vert = \vert\boldsymbol{\ell}\vert = 1$ | | 5 | $I = k_a L_a + k_d (\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})L_d + k_s (\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v})^\alpha L_s$ | Phong model | $\alpha \ge 1$ | | 6 | $L_r = \int f_r L_i (\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\omega}_i) d\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ | Rendering equation | Hemisphere integral | | 7 | $E_{\text{pixel}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left(\dfrac{d}{f}\right)^2 \cos^4\theta \, L_{\text{scene}}$ | Image irradiance (lens) | Thin-lens assumption | | 8 | $I_{\text{image}} = g \, t \, E_{\text{pixel}}$ | Digital pixel value | $g$ = gain, $t$ = exposure | | 9 | $\mathbf{n} = \dfrac{\nabla I}{\vert\nabla I\vert}$ | Normals from intensity (SFS) | Smooth surface | | 10 | $E_{\text{shaded}} = E_{\text{sun}} + E_{\text{sky}}$ | Outdoor shadow model | Two-term illumination | > **Notation convention used throughout:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the magnitude of a vector; all vectors in shading equations are **unit** vectors unless stated otherwise. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Lambert's Cosine Law from Radiometric First Principles We start from the definition of **irradiance** as power per unit area and apply the geometric projection of an incident beam onto a tilted surface. **Step 1 — Set up the geometry.** Consider a collimated (parallel) beam of light striking a flat surface patch of area $A$. Let $\theta$ be the angle between the beam direction and the surface normal $\mathbf{n}$. The *projected* area perpendicular to the beam is: $$A_\perp = A \cos\theta$$ > *Why?* When $\theta = 0$ (beam head-on), the entire patch is lit and $A_\perp = A$. When $\theta = 90^\circ$, the beam grazes the surface and $A_\perp = 0$. The cosine captures the gradual transition between these two extremes. **Step 2 — Express power through the patch.** The radiant flux $\Phi$ (in watts) that arrives at the patch is determined by the projected area, since the beam is uniform: $$\Phi = E_\perp \, A_\perp = E_\perp \, A \cos\theta$$ where $E_\perp$ is the beam's irradiance measured on a plane perpendicular to the beam direction. **Step 3 — Compute the irradiance *on the tilted surface*.** Irradiance on the patch is flux per unit patch area: $$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} = E_\perp \cos\theta$$ > This *is* Lambert's cosine law. The incident flux per unit *true* area is reduced by the cosine of the tilt angle, because the same power is "spread" over a larger effective area when viewed from the light's direction. **Step 4 — Specialise to a Lambertian surface.** A Lambertian surface reflects all incident flux equally in every outgoing direction.
