Its radiance $L$ in any outgoing direction is therefore: $$L = \frac{\rho}{\pi} \, E = \frac{\rho}{\pi} \, E_\perp \cos\theta$$ The factor $\frac{1}{\pi}$ ensures energy conservation when the radiance is integrated over the hemisphere. **Step 5 — Express in vector form.** With $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ as the unit vector *toward* the light and $\mathbf{n}$ as the unit surface normal, $\cos\theta = \mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}$. So the final Lambertian shading equation is: $$I(\mathbf{x}) = \rho \, (\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}) \, E_\perp$$ This is the workhorse equation for photometric stereo and shape-from-shading in mobile robots. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Phong Specular Term **Step 1 — Compute the reflection vector $\mathbf{r}$.** The law of reflection states that the incident direction $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ and the reflected direction $\mathbf{r}$ are symmetric about $\mathbf{n}$. The reflected vector is therefore twice the projection of $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ onto $\mathbf{n}$, minus the original vector: $$\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})\mathbf{n} - \boldsymbol{\ell}$$ **Step 2 — Express specular intensity as the cosine of the angle between $\mathbf{r}$ and the viewer direction $\mathbf{v}$.** The brightest specular highlight occurs when $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{r}$ (i.e., the viewer is along the mirror direction). The falloff as the viewer moves away from the mirror direction is modelled as a power of cosine: $$I_s = k_s (\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v})^\alpha$$ > *Why raise to the power $\alpha$?* A higher $\alpha$ produces a sharper, smaller highlight (like polished metal); $\alpha = 1$ gives a broad soft glow (like satin). Physically, $\alpha$ is related to surface micro-roughness. **Step 3 — Add ambient and diffuse terms.** The full Phong model is: $$I = k_a L_a + k_d (\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})L_d + k_s (\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v})^\alpha L_s$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Image Irradiance from a Thin Lens A mobile robot's camera lens projects scene radiance onto a sensor. The image-plane irradiance $E_{\text{image}}$ is: **Step 1 — Irradiance at the lens.** If the lens has diameter $D$ and the scene radiance from direction $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is $L$, the total flux entering the lens is: $$\Phi_{\text{lens}} = L \, A_{\text{lens}} \, \cos\theta = L \, \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cos\theta$$ **Step 2 — Image-plane area.** The lens of focal length $f$ forms an image at distance $v \approx f$ (for far objects). The patch in the image plane corresponding to solid angle $\delta\omega$ has area: $$A_{\text{image}} = \frac{v^2}{\cos^2\theta} \, \delta\omega$$ **Step 3 — Image-plane irradiance.** Dividing flux by image area: $$E_{\text{image}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{lens}}}{A_{\text{image}}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(\frac{D}{f}\right)^2 \cos^4\theta \, L$$ > This is the **image-irradiance equation**, and it shows that a robot's measured pixel value is proportional to scene radiance multiplied by a vignetting factor $\cos^4\theta$. Mobile robots must correct for this $\cos^4\theta$ term when doing photometric stereo from wide-field cameras. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) > **Problem.** A mobile robot's camera observes a flat matte surface patch whose normal makes an angle of $60^\circ$ with the illumination direction. The albedo of the surface is $\rho = 0.7$ and the incident irradiance on a perpendicular plane is $E_\perp = 100\,\text{W/m}^2$. Compute the radiance $L$ leaving the surface. **Solution.** Step 1 — Compute the dot product $\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}$: $$\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell} = \cos 60^\circ = 0.5$$ Step 2 — Apply Lambert's law to get the irradiance on the tilted surface: $$E = E_\perp \cos\theta = 100 \times 0.5 = 50\,\text{W/m}^2$$ Step 3 — Compute the reflected radiance (Lambertian case): $$L = \frac{\rho}{\pi} E = \frac{0.7}{\pi} \times 50 \approx 11.14\,\text{W/m}^2/\text{sr}$$ > **Interpretation for robotics:** If the robot's camera is calibrated to convert pixel values to radiance, the above $L$ is what the sensor "sees." If $L$ is too high or too low relative to the *known* $E_\perp$ and geometry, the robot can flag an anomaly (e.g., a wet or oily patch that has changed albedo). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Shading Model | Scenario in Robotics | Recommended Model | Justification | |---|---|---| | Indoor robot, matte walls (offices) | **Lambertian** | Paint is approximately diffuse; fast, closed-form | | Autonomous car, metallic road signs | **Phong (low $\alpha$)** | Specular highlights affect sign readability | | Industrial bin-picking, shiny plastic parts | **Phong (high $\alpha$ + diffuse)** | Glossy surface with some highlight | | Outdoor robot, weathered stone surfaces | **Oren-Nayar** | Rough diffuse (not perfectly Lambertian) | | Robot inspecting polished metal | **Cook-Torrance (microfacet)** | Physically based, models micro-roughness | | Robot estimating normals from 1 image | **Lambert + shape-from-shading** | Invertible under known light | | Robot estimating normals from N images | **Photometric stereo (Lambert)** | Robust, linear, closed-form | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Image Formation and Shading Pipeline The following Mermaid block captures the complete flow of physical and computational events from a light source in a robot's environment to a digital pixel value in the robot's memory. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Light Source
Point or Directional] --> B1[Light Transport
Free space propagation] B1 --> C1[Surface Interaction
at point x with normal n] C1 --> D1[BRDF Evaluation
f_r omega_i omega_r] D1 --> E1[Reflection Operator
Lambert or Phong] E1 --> F1[Outgoing Radiance
L_r x omega_r] F1 --> G1[Lens System
Aperture and focal length f] G1 --> H1[Image Plane
Irradiance E_image] H1 --> I1[Sensor Array
CCD or CMOS pixels] I1 --> J1[ADC and Gain
Convert to digital value] J1 --> K1[Robot Vision Buffer
I u as 8 or 16 bit integer] K1 --> L1[Shading Analysis
Estimate n, rho, f_r] style A1 fill:#ffe5b4 style C1 fill:#ffd1dc style D1 fill:#c1f0c1 style E1 fill:#c1e1f0 style L1 fill:#f0c1e1 ``` > **Read this diagram left-to-right.** Each block transforms the light signal. The robot's *vision algorithm* (rightmost block) must mathematically **invert** the entire chain. Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow is used here because a physical drawing of light rays, lenses, and sensors would not fit in a Mermaid schematic and would not aid the shading mathematics. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Taxonomy of Reflection Models ```mermaid flowchart TB R[Reflection Models for Mobile Robotics] --> A[Diffuse
Lambertian] R --> B[Specular
Ideal mirror] R --> C[Glossy
Phong and Blinn-Phong] R --> D[Anisotropic
Brushed metal and hair] R --> E[Microfacet
Cook-Torrance and GGX] A --> A1[Surface appears uniform
no view dependence] B --> B1[Single mirror direction
law of reflection] C --> C1[Highlight with cosine falloff
controlled by exponent alpha] D --> D1[Different BRDF along different tangents
common for road surfaces] E --> E1[Physically based BRDF
used for accurate rendering and sim2real] style R fill:#fff4cc style A fill:#c1f0c1 style B fill:#f0c1c1 style C fill:#c1e1f0 style D fill:#e1c1f0 style E fill:#f0e1c1 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Shadow Decomposition in a Mobile Robot's View ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Observed Pixel Intensity I] --> M1{Is point x in shadow?} M1 -- No --> LIT[Lit Region
I = rho n dot ell times L] M1 -- Yes --> SH[Shadow Region
I = rho n dot ell_s times L_s] LIT --> OUT[Final Image] SH --> OUT style S fill:#ffe5b4 style M1 fill:#ffd1dc style LIT fill:#c1f0c1 style SH fill:#d3d3d3 style OUT fill:#fff4cc ``` > **Why this matters for mobile robots:** Shadows are *not just darkness* — they carry *geometric* information. A robot that detects a shadow boundary can infer the *outline* of the occluding object, which is critical for obstacle detection and tracking. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Lambertian reflection. State Lambert's cosine law and explain its significance in robotic perception.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A Lambertian surface is one whose radiance is *independent of the viewing direction* and depends only on the cosine of the angle between the surface normal and the direction of illumination. - **Statement of Law (1 mark):** The brightness $I(\mathbf{x})$ at a surface point is $I = \rho(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})L_i$, where $\rho$ is the albedo, $\mathbf{n}$ is the unit normal, $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ is the unit direction toward the light, and $L_i$ is the incident light intensity. - **Significance (1 mark):** It is the *only* shading model that yields a *linear* relationship between image intensity and surface orientation. This linearity is what allows mobile robots to recover 3-D surface normals by **photometric stereo** and **shape-from-shading**, both of which are core algorithms in the KTU Mobile Robotics syllabus. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Distinguish between diffuse, specular, and glossy reflection. Give one real-world example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Type | Behaviour | Example | Directional Dependence | |---|---|---|---| | **Diffuse** | Equal radiance in all directions | Matte chalk, unfinished wood | None | | **Specular** | Mirror-like, law of reflection | Polished silver, calm water | Highly directional | | **Glossy** | Mixture of diffuse and specular | Polished wood, plastic toys | Softly directional | - One mark each for distinction + example. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions in the End-Semester Examination (ESE) carry **14 marks each** and offer an **internal choice** — i.e., the question paper prints *two* alternatives (Q A and Q B) and the student answers *one*. Both alternatives below are written to be self-contained and follow the standard KTU sub-part pattern of **7 + 7** marks. ### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — Lambertian Model and Photometric Stereo **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q3A]** **(a)** Derive the **Lambertian shading equation** starting from the definition of irradiance. Show clearly how the cosine term arises from the projected area of a tilted surface. (7 marks) **(b)** A mobile robot is fitted with a camera and three light sources at known positions. The camera observes a flat matte patch of unknown orientation. The three light sources, when switched on *one at a time*, produce pixel intensities $I_1 = 120$, $I_2 = 80$, and $I_3 = 40$ at the same patch location. The light directions (in robot frame) are $\boldsymbol{\ell}_1 = (0, 0, 1)$, $\boldsymbol{\ell}_2 = (1, 0, 0)/\sqrt{1}$, $\boldsymbol{\ell}_3 = (0, 1, 0)/\sqrt{1}$. Assuming unit incident intensity and Lambertian shading, **recover the surface normal $\mathbf{n}$ and the albedo $\rho$** at this patch. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to Q3A(a): **Step 1 — Set up the geometry (2 marks).** Consider a parallel light beam striking a tilted surface patch. Let $\theta$ be the angle between the beam direction and the surface normal $\mathbf{n}$. The patch has true area $A$. The *projected* area perpendicular to the beam is $A_\perp = A \cos\theta$ (this is the fundamental geometric relation between an actual surface and its projection onto a plane normal to the beam). **Step 2 — Irradiance on the patch (2 marks).** The flux through the patch is $\Phi = E_\perp A \cos\theta$, where $E_\perp$ is the beam's irradiance on a perpendicular plane. The irradiance *on the patch itself* is: $$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} = E_\perp \cos\theta$$ This is the cosine law. **Step 3 — Lambertian reflection (2 marks).** For a Lambertian surface, the reflected radiance $L$ in any outgoing direction is constant and equals the reflected flux per unit area per steradian. With albedo $\rho$: $$L = \frac{\rho}{\pi} E = \frac{\rho}{\pi} E_\perp \cos\theta$$ The $\frac{1}{\pi}$ factor comes from integrating radiance over the hemisphere to retrieve total flux. **Step 4 — Vector form (1 mark).** Replacing $\cos\theta = \mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}$: $$\boxed{I = \rho(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}) E_\perp}$$ #### Model Solution to Q3A(b): **Step 1 — Write three Lambertian equations (2 marks).** With $E_\perp = 1$ for each source: $$I_1 = \rho \, n_z = 120$$ $$I_2 = \rho \, n_x = 80$$ $$I_3 = \rho \, n_y = 40$$ **Step 2 — Express each component of $\mathbf{n}$ in terms of $\rho$ (1 mark):** $$n_x = \frac{80}{\rho}, \quad n_y = \frac{40}{\rho}, \quad n_z = \frac{120}{\rho}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the unit-normal constraint (3 marks).** The normal must satisfy $n_x^2 + n_y^2 + n_z^2 = 1$: $$\left(\frac{80}{\rho}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{40}{\rho}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{120}{\rho}\right)^2 = 1$$ $$\frac{6400 + 1600 + 14400}{\rho^2} = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{22400}{\rho^2} = 1$$ $$\rho^2 = 22400 \;\Longrightarrow\; \rho = \sqrt{22400} \approx 149.67$$ **Step 4 — Recover the normal (1 mark):** $$n_x = \frac{80}{149.67} \approx 0.5345, \quad n_y \approx 0.2673, \quad n_z \approx 0.8018$$ > **Final Answer:** $\rho \approx 149.67$, $\mathbf{n} \approx (0.5345, 0.2673, 0.8018)$. > **Verification:** Compute $\vert\mathbf{n}\vert = \sqrt{0.286 + 0.0714 + 0.643} \approx \sqrt{1.0004} \approx 1.000$ ✓. > **Note on physical units:** A $\rho > 1$ indicates that the assumed $E_\perp$ in the question was not actually unit intensity — in a real KTU exam, the student is expected to flag this. KTU examiners usually allow such answers *as long as the student notes the issue*. The normal estimate is unaffected. --- ### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Phong Model and Reflection Geometry **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q3B]** **(a)** Derive the **Phong reflection model** for a glossy surface. Clearly explain the role of each term (ambient, diffuse, specular) and the significance of the shininess exponent $\alpha$. (7 marks) **(b)** Consider a glossy surface patch with normal $\mathbf{n} = (0, 0, 1)$ illuminated by a directional light coming from the direction $\boldsymbol{\ell} = (1, 1, 1)/\sqrt{3}$. The camera is positioned along the direction $\mathbf{v} = (-1, -1, 2)/\sqrt{6}$. The Phong parameters are $k_a = 0.1$, $k_d = 0.6$, $k_s = 0.3$, $\alpha = 50$, and the light intensities are $L_a = L_d = L_s = 1$. **Compute the observed intensity** $I$ at this patch using the Phong model. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to Q3B(a): **Step 1 — Ambient term (1 mark).** The ambient term $k_a L_a$ accounts for uniform background illumination that is *not* from any specific source. It prevents surfaces in shadow from appearing completely black. **Step 2 — Diffuse term (2 marks).** The diffuse term $k_d (\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})L_d$ is the Lambertian component, derived as in §3.1. It produces the soft, even shading observed on matte portions of the object. The $(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})$ term vanishes when the light is behind the surface, modelling self-shadowing. **Step 3 — Specular term (3 marks).** The specular term $k_s(\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v})^\alpha L_s$ models the bright highlight visible on glossy surfaces. The reflection vector is $\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})\mathbf{n} - \boldsymbol{\ell}$. The cosine $\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v}$ measures how close the viewer is to the *mirror* direction. Raising it to the power $\alpha$ makes the highlight fall off rapidly as the viewer deviates from the mirror direction. **Step 4 — Role of $\alpha$ (1 mark).** $\alpha$ is the shininess exponent. $\alpha = 1$ produces a broad soft glow (e.g., satin). $\alpha \ge 100$ produces a sharp small highlight (e.g., polished metal). Physically, $\alpha$ is inversely related to surface micro-roughness. **Final boxed model (for 1 mark, included in any of the above):** $$\boxed{I = k_a L_a + k_d(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})L_d + k_s(\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v})^\alpha L_s}$$ #### Model Solution to Q3B(b): **Step 1 — Verify that $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are unit vectors (1 mark).** $$\vert\boldsymbol{\ell}\vert = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}} = 1 \quad\checkmark$$ $$\vert\mathbf{v}\vert = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{6}+\tfrac{1}{6}+\tfrac{4}{6}} = \sqrt{1} = 1 \quad\checkmark$$ **Step 2 — Compute the dot product $\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}$ (1 mark).** $$\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell} = (0)(1/\sqrt{3}) + (0)(1/\sqrt{3}) + (1)(1/\sqrt{3}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$$ **Step 3 — Compute the reflection vector $\mathbf{r}$ (2 marks).** $$\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})\mathbf{n} - \boldsymbol{\ell} = 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot (0,0,1) - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(-1, -1, 1)$$ Check: $\vert\mathbf{r}\vert = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}+\tfrac{1}{3}} = 1 \quad\checkmark$. **Step 4 — Compute the dot product $\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v}$ (1 mark).** $$\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\tfrac{-1}{\sqrt{6}} + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\tfrac{-1}{\sqrt{6}} + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{6}} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{18}}(-1 - 1 + 2) = 0$$ **Step 5 — Compute each Phong term and sum (2 marks).** $$I_a = 0.1 \times 1 = 0.1$$ $$I_d = 0.6 \times 0.5774 \times 1 = 0.3464$$ $$I_s = 0.3 \times 0^{50} \times 1 = 0$$ $$I = 0.1 + 0.3464 + 0 = \mathbf{0.4464}$$ > **Final Answer:** $I \approx 0.4464$. The zero specular term indicates that the viewer is *not* aligned with the mirror direction — the highlight is in a different viewing direction. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions in Shading Problems (avoid these!):** > 1. **Forgetting the unit-normal constraint** in photometric stereo — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for not verifying $\vert\mathbf{n}\vert = 1$ when solving for $\rho$. > 2. **Using non-unit vectors** in $\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}$ without normalising — the cosine law *only* holds for unit vectors. A 1-mark penalty is routine. > 3. **Skipping the derivation steps** in 7-mark derivation parts. KTU 2024 scheme mandates step-by-step working; a "plug and chug" answer gets at most 3 of 7 marks. > 4. **Confusing the reflection vector $\mathbf{r}$ with the incidence direction $\boldsymbol{\ell}$** — $\mathbf{r}$ is reflected *about* $\mathbf{n}$, not equal to $\boldsymbol{\ell}$. This is a 1-mark trap. > 5. **Computing $I_s$ with a wrong sign** in $(\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{v})^\alpha$ — for negative arguments, the result should be clamped to 0 (no negative intensities). Many students omit this clamp. > 6. **Ignoring self-shadowing** ($\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell} < 0$) — the diffuse term must be clamped to zero. Failing to do so is a 1-mark deduction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2** - ✅ **Lambert's Cosine Law** $I = \rho(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})L_i$ is the *single most important* equation in shading. - ✅ **Phong Model** has three terms: ambient (no geometry), diffuse (cosine with normal), specular (cosine with reflection vector, raised to $\alpha$). - ✅ **Reflection vector** $\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell})\mathbf{n} - \boldsymbol{\ell}$ is symmetric to $\boldsymbol{\ell}$ about $\mathbf{n}$. - ✅ **BRDF** $f_r(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i, \boldsymbol{\omega}_r)$ is the most general reflectance descriptor; obeys **Helmholtz reciprocity**. - ✅ **Rendering equation** is the integral form: $L_r = \int f_r L_i (\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\omega}_i) d\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$. - ✅ **Image irradiance** has a $\cos^4\theta$ vignetting factor — must be corrected in photometric stereo. - ✅ **Photometric stereo** with N ≥ 3 lights yields a closed-form solution for $(\mathbf{n}, \rho)$ via linear equations. - ✅ **Albedo** $\rho$ is dimensionless, $0 \le \rho \le 1$ for physical surfaces (values > 1 indicate uncalibrated lighting). - ✅ **Self-shadowing rule:** if $\mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell} < 0$, the diffuse term = 0. - ✅ **Shading ≠ Shadowing:** shading = variation in intensity from geometry; shadowing = blocking of light by another object. - ✅ **KTU high-yield keywords to memorise:** Lambertian, Phong, BRDF, rendering equation, Helmholtz reciprocity, photometric stereo, shape-from-shading, albedo, radiance, irradiance. - ✅ **Conversion traps to avoid:** $1\,\text{sr}$ for solid angle; $\cos\theta = \mathbf{n}\cdot\boldsymbol{\ell}$ *only* for unit vectors; $\alpha$ is *not* a physical angle but a shininess exponent. --- > **End of Module 2 — Object Appearance and Shading | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**

topics.title: Signals and Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Signals and Sampling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Signal:** A signal is any physical quantity that varies with time, space, or any other independent variable and conveys information about the behavior or nature of a phenomenon. In the context of **Mobile Robotics**, signals represent sensor measurements (LiDAR returns, camera pixels, IMU readings, encoder pulses), communication data between modules, and control commands sent to actuators. Mathematically, a signal is a function that maps an independent variable (most commonly time, $t$) to a dependent variable (the signal value, $x$): $$x(t): \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$ ### Classification of Signals | Signal Type | Definition | Variable Domain | Mobile Robotics Example | |-------------|------------|-----------------|--------------------------| | **Continuous-time (Analog)** | Defined for every instant of time | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Analog output of a Hall-effect wheel encoder | | **Discrete-time** | Defined only at specific instants $t = nT_s$ | $t \in \{nT_s\}$ | Sampled IMU accelerometer reading | | **Analog (Continuous-Amplitude)** | Continuous in both time and amplitude | Both continuous | Direct microphone capture of a motor's acoustic signature | | **Digital (Discrete in both)** | Discrete time AND discrete amplitude | Both discrete | PWM duty cycle command stored in a microcontroller register | | **Deterministic** | Completely described by a mathematical expression | Predictable | Computed odometry trajectory | | **Random/Stochastic** | Cannot be exactly predicted; needs probability models | Statistical | LiDAR measurement noise, wheel slip disturbances | > [!NOTE] > In **Mobile Robotics**, a robot almost never works with true continuous-time analog signals at the CPU level. The onboard computer samples, quantizes, and processes all sensor data as discrete-time digital signals before any control algorithm (PID, SLAM, MPC) can act upon them. ## 1.2 The Concept of Sampling > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by recording the amplitude of the signal at uniformly spaced intervals of time, $T_s$ (the **sampling period**). The mathematical operator is: $$x[n] = x(t)\Big\vert_{t = nT_s} = x(nT_s), \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$ where $T_s$ is the sampling period (in **seconds**) and $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency (in **Hertz**). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The Stroboscope Effect:** > Imagine filming a spinning ceiling fan with a normal camera (continuous sampling, 30+ fps). The blades appear blurred, but you clearly see rotation. Now film the same fan at a low frame rate (say, 1 fps). The blades may look stationary, rotate slowly in the wrong direction, or even appear to rotate forward at impossible speeds. The fan's true rotation is the **continuous signal**; each captured video frame is a **sample**; the misleading visual is **aliasing**. > - If the camera is *fast enough* relative to the fan's rotation, you reconstruct the true motion. This is the essence of the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**. A second, simpler analogy for *quantization*: think of a digital weighing scale that only displays weights rounded to the nearest **0.1 kg**. An object weighing **0.873 kg** will be displayed as **0.9 kg**. The display is *quantized*, and the rounding error is the **quantization error**. ## 1.4 Why Sampling Matters in Mobile Robotics - **Sensor Fusion:** Combining LiDAR (typically 10–20 Hz), cameras (30–60 Hz), and IMUs (100–1000 Hz) requires understanding different sampling rates. - **Control Loops:** Digital PID/MPC controllers must execute at a known, fixed sampling frequency. - **Communication:** Wireless links (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 5G) transmit discrete packets. - **Computation Limits:** Onboard processors (Jetson, Raspberry Pi, microcontrollers) have finite throughput — oversampling wastes CPU, undersampling loses information. ## 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted) - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, the number of samples taken per second. - **Nyquist Frequency ($f_N$):** $f_N = f_s / 2$ in **Hz** — the highest baseband frequency that can be reconstructed. - **Bit Depth ($B$):** number of bits used per sample — unitless. - **Quantization Step ($\Delta$):** in **Volts (V)** for voltage-domain signals. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Sampling Operation — Step-by-Step Logic Sampling is equivalent to multiplying the continuous signal $x(t)$ by a periodic impulse train (Dirac comb), $p(t)$: $$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$ The sampled signal is therefore: $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$ ### Logical Breakdown of the "Why" and "How" 1. **Why a Dirac Comb?** A Dirac impulse $\delta(t)$ has unit area and is zero everywhere except at $t=0$. Multiplying $x(t)$ by $\delta(t - nT_s)$ "picks out" the value of $x$ at exactly $t = nT_s$. 2. **The Spectrum Effect:** In the frequency domain, multiplication in time becomes convolution in frequency. The Fourier Transform of $p(t)$ is another impulse train in frequency: $$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(f - \frac{k}{T_s}\right)$$ 3. **Resulting Spectrum:** The spectrum $X_s(f)$ of the sampled signal is a periodic replication of $X(f)$ centered at multiples of $f_s$: $$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(f - kf_s\right)$$ 4. **Reconstruction Possibility:** If the spectral replicas do NOT overlap, the original signal can be recovered by a **low-pass filter** (the ideal reconstruction filter). ## 2.2 The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem Statement:** > A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$ (also called bandwidth $B$) can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies: > $$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$ > The minimum acceptable sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ is the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2 f_{\max}$ is the **Nyquist Frequency**. ### Consequences of the Theorem - **Undersampling ($f_s < 2f_{\max}$)** → spectral overlap → **aliasing** (irrecoverable distortion). - **Nyquist-rate sampling ($f_s = 2f_{\max}$)** → marginal; the replicas just touch at $f_{\max}$; in practice impractical. - **Oversampling ($f_s > 2f_{\max}$)** → guard band, easier reconstruction, lower anti-aliasing filter requirements. - **Sub-Nyquist sampling** is only possible with **compressed sensing** (a specialized advanced topic). ## 2.3 Aliasing — The Critical Distortion > [!WARNING] > **Aliasing** is the phenomenon where high-frequency components of a signal masquerade as lower-frequency components after sampling because the sampling rate is too low to resolve them. The aliased frequency is given by: > $$f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - k f_s \vert, \quad k = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f}{f_s}\right)$$ A simple example: a $90$ **Hz** signal sampled at $f_s = 100$ **Hz** appears as $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert 90 - 1 \cdot 100 \vert = 10$ **Hz**. The original $90$ Hz content is irretrievably lost and appears as a phantom $10$ Hz tone. ## 2.4 Quantization — Discretizing the Amplitude In any real **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, the continuous amplitude of the sampled signal is mapped to one of $2^B$ discrete levels, where $B$ is the **bit depth**. **Quantization Step:** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^B}$$ where $V_{\text{FS}}$ is the full-scale voltage range of the ADC. **Quantization Error (uniform midtread quantizer):** $e_q \in \left[-\tfrac{\Delta}{2}, +\tfrac{\Delta}{2}\right]$ **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB** (for a uniform quantizer with sinusoidal input): $$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02\,B + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rule-of-Thumb:** Every additional bit of ADC resolution provides approximately **6 dB** (a factor of 2 in amplitude, or 4× in power) of dynamic range. ## 2.5 Anti-Aliasing Filter An **anti-aliasing filter** is a low-pass analog filter placed *before* the sampler, designed to remove all frequency components above $f_s/2$ (the Nyquist frequency) so that no aliasing can occur. In practice, it is a Butterworth, Chebyshev, or Bessel filter with cutoff slightly below $f_s/2$. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Sampled signal value | $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ | Dimensionless index $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | 2 | Sampling period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | **seconds (s)** | | 3 | Nyquist condition | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | **Hz** | | 4 | Nyquist frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | **Hz** | | 5 | Aliased frequency | $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | **Hz**; $k$ = nearest integer to $f/f_s$ | | 6 | Spectrum of sampled signal | $X_s(f) = \tfrac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(f - kf_s)$ | Fourier series of replicas | | 7 | Ideal reconstruction filter | $H_r(f) = T_s \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\tfrac{f}{f_s}\right)$ | Unity gain over $\vert f \vert < f_s/2$ | | 8 | Quantization step | $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^B$ | **Volts (V)** | | 9 | Quantization noise variance | $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ | $\text{V}^2$ (uniform) | | 10 | SQNR (sinusoidal input) | $6.02 B + 1.76$ | **decibels (dB)** | | 11 | Reconstruction sinc-formula (Whittaker–Shannon) | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\tfrac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Ideal bandlimited interpolation | | 12 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^B$ | Dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > The **sinc function** is defined as $\text{sinc}(u) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi u)}{\pi u}$. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **LiDAR / RGB-D Cameras:** Velodyne LiDAR samples at 10–20 Hz; the Nyquist criterion determines the maximum resolvable angular velocity of detected objects. - **Motor Encoders:** Quadrature encoders produce pulse trains; sampling them at the microcontroller's input-capture frequency determines wheel-velocity resolution. - **IMUs:** MEMS gyroscopes internally sample at kHz rates; the host robot reads them at much lower rates, so internal anti-aliasing filters are crucial. - **Vision-based control:** Camera frame rate (FPS) sets the sampling rate for visual servoing loops. - **Wireless comms:** Symbol rate is the sampling rate in digital communication links (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Spectrum of a Sampled Signal **Goal:** Starting from the time-domain definition of sampling, derive the frequency-domain expression showing the replicated spectrum. ### Step 1 — Define the Impulse Train We model the sampling operation as a periodic train of Dirac impulses spaced by $T_s$: $$ p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) $$ This impulse train is itself periodic with period $T_s$, hence its fundamental angular frequency is $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_s$. ### Step 2 — Fourier Series Expansion of $p(t)$ A periodic signal admits a Fourier series representation. Using the sifting property of the Dirac delta and the standard result for a Dirac comb, the complex Fourier series coefficients are: $$ c_k = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} \delta(t)\,e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\,dt = \frac{1}{T_s} $$ Therefore, the impulse train in time can be written as: $$ p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} c_k\,e^{jk\omega_0 t} = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k t / T_s} $$ ### Step 3 — Form the Sampled Signal The sampled signal is the pointwise product: $$ x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{j 2\pi k t / T_s} $$ ### Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform Apply $\mathcal{F}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides. Recall that multiplication by $e^{j2\pi f_0 t}$ in time shifts the spectrum by $f_0$: $$ \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\,e^{j 2\pi k t / T_s}\} = X\!\left(f - \frac{k}{T_s}\right) = X(f - kf_s) $$ Therefore: $$ X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(f - kf_s\right) $$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation The original spectrum $X(f)$ is replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s = 1/T_s$, and each replica is scaled by $1/T_s$. This is the **spectral replication** property and the basis for understanding aliasing. > [!NOTE] > The replication spacing is exactly $f_s$. Hence, if $X(f)$ has support only on $[-f_{\max}, +f_{\max}]$ and $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$, the replicas do NOT overlap — and the original signal can be recovered via an ideal low-pass filter of cutoff $f_s/2$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Alias Frequency Formula **Goal:** Show mathematically that a tone at frequency $f_0$ exceeding $f_s/2$ appears at an aliased frequency $f_a$ after sampling. ### Step 1 — Consider a Single-Tone Input Let $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$. Its spectrum is: $$ X(f) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)\right] $$ ### Step 2 — Replicate the Spectrum at Multiples of $f_s$ By the result of §3.1: $$ X_s(f) = \frac{1}{2T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \Big[\delta(f - f_0 - kf_s) + \delta(f + f_0 - kf_s)\Big] $$ ### Step 3 — Identify the Dominant Low-Frequency Component After low-pass filtering with cutoff $f_s/2$, only the impulse in the fundamental band $[-f_s/2, +f_s/2]$ survives. Choosing the integer $k$ that places $f_0 - kf_s$ inside this band: $$ f_a = f_0 - kf_s, \quad \text{where } k = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f_0}{f_s}\right) $$ This $f_a$ satisfies $\vert f_a \vert \leq f_s/2$ and represents the perceived frequency of the original tone after sampling. ### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example Let $f_0 = 7$ **kHz**, $f_s = 10$ **kHz**. - $f_s/2 = 5$ **kHz** → $f_0 = 7$ kHz lies above Nyquist → aliasing. - $k = \text{round}(7/10) = 1$ - $f_a = 7 - 1 \cdot 10 = -3$ **kHz** (magnitude $3$ **kHz**). Hence the $7$ kHz tone appears as a $3$ kHz tone after sampling. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: SQNR for a Uniform Quantizer **Goal:** Derive the well-known $6.02B + 1.76$ dB rule. ### Step 1 — Quantization Error Model Assume a uniform midtread quantizer with step $\Delta$ and bit depth $B$. The quantization error $e_q = x_q - x$ is assumed to be uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$ and statistically independent of $x$. ### Step 2 — Variance of the Quantization Error For a uniform distribution on $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$: $$ \sigma_q^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\,de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} $$ ### Step 3 — Signal Power (Sinusoidal Input) Let $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f_0 t)$. RMS value of $x$ is $A/\sqrt{2}$, so the signal power is: $$ P_x = \frac{A^2}{2} $$ For a full-scale sine, the peak-to-peak range is $2A = V_{\text{FS}} = 2^B \Delta$, hence $A = 2^{B-1}\Delta$. Substituting: $$ P_x = \frac{(2^{B-1}\Delta)^2}{2} = \frac{2^{2B-2}\Delta^2}{2} = \frac{2^{2B-1}\Delta^2}{2} = 2^{2B-3}\Delta^2 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute SQNR in Linear and dB Scales $$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{P_x}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{2^{2B-3}\Delta^2}{\Delta^2/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2B-3} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2B} = \frac{3}{4} \cdot 4^B $$ Converting to decibels: $$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2B}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 20B\log_{10}(2) $$ Using $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$ and $10\log_{10}(3/2) \approx 1.76$: $$ \boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02\,B + 1.76 \text{ dB}} $$ ### Step 5 — Sanity Check For $B = 8$ bits: $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 49.92$ dB (≈ 316:1). This matches practical 8-bit ADC specifications. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sampling-Theorem Scenarios | Scenario | $f_{\max}$ (Hz) | Nyquist Rate $2f_{\max}$ (Hz) | Chosen $f_s$ (Hz) | Verdict | |----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|--------------------|---------| | Human voice (telephony) | 3,400 | 6,800 | 8,000 | OK — telephony standard | | Audio CD (music) | 20,000 | 40,000 | 44,100 | OK — slight oversampling guard band | | ECG (medical) | 150 | 300 | 500 | OK — comfortable guard band | | Wheel encoder (slow robot) | 50 | 100 | 200 | OK | | IMU gyroscope (consumer) | 250 | 500 | 1,000–8,000 | OK — typically oversampled internally | | Vibration sensor on a motor | 5,000 | 10,000 | 10,000 | Marginal — replicas touch | | Same vibration sensor | 5,000 | 10,000 | 8,000 | **Aliasing** — data corrupt | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Signal Acquisition Pipeline in a Mobile Robot This block-level functional architecture flow maps every step from a physical phenomenon to a digital control command inside a mobile robot. It captures the **exact** path that a sensor reading takes through sampling, quantization, and digital processing before influencing motion. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Phenomenon
Light, Sound, Acceleration] --> B[Analog Transducer
Sensor Element] B --> C[Analog Signal
Conditioning Amplifier] C --> D[Anti-Aliasing
Low-Pass Filter] D --> E[Sample and Hold
Circuit] E --> F[Analog to Digital
Converter ADC] F --> G[Digital Sample Stream
x n = x nTs] G --> H[Microprocessor
DSP / MCU / SoC] H --> I[Digital Signal
Processing Block] I --> J[Control Algorithm
PID SLAM MPC] J --> K[Digital Command
u n] K --> L[Digital to Analog
Converter DAC] L --> M[Actuator Driver
Motor H-Bridge] M --> N[Physical Motion
Wheel or Joint] N -. feedback via sensors .-> A ``` ### Functional Description of Each Block | Block | Function | Mobile Robotics Example | |-------|----------|--------------------------| | Physical Phenomenon | Source of information | Obstacle distance, wheel rotation | | Analog Transducer | Converts energy domain to voltage | Photodiode, encoder, MEMS accelerometer | | Signal Conditioning | Amplifies / offsets / linearises | Instrumentation amplifier for ECG signal | | Anti-Aliasing Filter | Removes $f > f_s/2$ components | 2nd-order Butterworth LPF before ADC | | Sample and Hold | Freezes voltage for ADC conversion | Switched capacitor in MCU ADC front-end | | ADC | Quantizes amplitude into $B$-bit code | 12-bit SAR ADC on STM32 | | Digital Sample Stream | $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ | UART/SPI/I2C data packets | | Microprocessor | Executes algorithms | Jetson Nano, Raspberry Pi, ESP32 | | DSP Block | Filtering, FFT, feature extraction | Complementary filter, EKF | | Control Algorithm | Computes actuator commands | PID, MPC, behavior trees | | DAC / PWM | Reconstructs analog or pulse command | PWM duty cycle to motor driver | | Actuator Driver | Power amplification | BTS7960 motor driver, servo amplifier | | Physical Motion | Wheel rotation, arm joint | DC gearmotor, servo | ## 4.2 Spectral Replication and Aliasing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CASE_A[Case A: f s greater than 2 f max - SAFE] A1[Original Spectrum X f
Support minus fmax to plus fmax] --> A2[Replicas at k fs
NO overlap] A2 --> A3[Low Pass Filter
Cutoff f s over 2] A3 --> A4[Reconstructed X f
Lossless] end subgraph CASE_B[Case B: f s less than 2 f max - ALIASING] B1[Original Spectrum X f
Wide support] --> B2[Replicas at k fs
OVERLAP occurs] B2 --> B3[Low Pass Filter
Cannot separate] B3 --> B4[Corrupted X prime f
Information lost] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The **guard band** (the difference between $f_s/2$ and $f_{\max}$) is what makes Case A practical. Without it, the reconstruction low-pass filter would need an infinitely sharp roll-off, which is physically unrealizable. ## 4.3 Quantization Characteristic (Midtread Uniform Quantizer) ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Continuous Input x t] --> Q[Quantizer
Step Delta
B bits] --> OUT[Digital Code x q n] Q -. error .-> E[e q = x q minus x] E --> EB[Uniform Distribution
Minus Delta over 2 to Plus Delta over 2] ``` For a 3-bit ($B=3$, $L=8$) midtread quantizer with full-scale $\pm 4\Delta$, the input is mapped to integer codes $\{-4, -3, -2, -1, 0, +1, +2, +3\}$ and the maximum quantization error is $\pm \Delta/2$. ## 4.4 Sampling Clock Timing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR CLK[Master Clock f s] --> SH[Sample and Hold Trigger] SH --> ADC[ADC Conversion Start] ADC --> EOC[End of Conversion Flag] EOC --> DMA[DMA Transfer to RAM] DMA --> ISR[Interrupt Service Routine] ISR --> CTRL[Control Loop Body Executes] CTRL -. next period .-> CLK ``` This sequence is critical in real-time robot control: the total loop time (sample + convert + transfer + compute) must fit within $T_s = 1/f_s$, otherwise samples are dropped or processing lags behind reality. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 3 Marks **State the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. What is the minimum sampling rate required for a signal with maximum frequency $4$ kHz?** **Model Answer:** The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{\max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$. For $f_{\max} = 4$ kHz, the minimum (Nyquist) sampling rate is: $$ f_s = 2 f_{\max} = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ kHz} $$ The corresponding sampling period is $T_s = 1/f_s = 125$ µs. In practice, the signal is oversampled (e.g., $f_s = 10$ kHz) to provide a guard band and relax the anti-aliasing filter design. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks **Define aliasing. How can it be prevented in a practical data acquisition system?** **Model Answer:** **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a continuous-time signal appear as spurious lower-frequency components after sampling, due to insufficient sampling rate. Mathematically, if a component at frequency $f > f_s/2$ is sampled at $f_s$, it manifests as a phantom frequency $f_a = \vert f - kf_s \vert$ for an appropriate integer $k$. **Prevention methods:** 1. Sample at a rate $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$ (Nyquist criterion). 2. Place an **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** with cutoff $\leq f_s/2$ before the sampler. 3. Choose a sampling rate comfortably higher than the Nyquist rate to provide a practical guard band. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal $X_s(f)$ in terms of the spectrum $X(f)$ of the continuous signal and the sampling frequency $f_s$. Clearly explain the role of the impulse train. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A continuous-time signal $x(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 200\,t) + 3\cos(2\pi \cdot 350\,t)$ (with $t$ in seconds, so frequencies in Hz) is sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz. Determine whether aliasing occurs. If it does, find the aliased frequencies. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Model the sampling operation as multiplication by a periodic Dirac impulse train (Dirac comb): $$ p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s), \quad T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} $$ The **role of the impulse train** is to "pick out" the value of $x(t)$ at the discrete instants $t = nT_s$ via the sifting property of the Dirac delta: $\int x(\tau)\delta(\tau - nT_s)\,d\tau = x(nT_s)$. **Step 2:** The sampled signal is the product: $$ x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s) $$ **Step 3:** The impulse train is itself periodic with period $T_s$, so it admits a complex Fourier series. The Fourier series coefficients are $c_k = 1/T_s$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}$: $$ p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t} $$ **Step 4:** Substituting and applying the frequency-shift property of the Fourier Transform: $$ X_s(f) = \mathcal{F}\{x_s(t)\} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s) $$ **Conclusion:** The spectrum of the sampled signal is a periodic replication of $X(f)$, centered at $f = kf_s$ for all integers $k$, with each replica scaled by $1/T_s$. This is the key insight behind the Nyquist–Shannon theorem and the cause of aliasing when replicas overlap. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** $f_1 = 200$ Hz, $f_2 = 350$ Hz, $f_s = 500$ Hz. **Step 1 — Compute Nyquist Frequency:** $$ f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Compare Each Component:** - $f_1 = 200$ Hz $\leq 250$ Hz → **no aliasing**. - $f_2 = 350$ Hz $> 250$ Hz → **aliasing occurs**. **Step 3 — Compute Aliased Frequency for $f_2$:** $$ k = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f_2}{f_s}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{350}{500}\right) = \text{round}(0.7) = 1 $$ $$ f_{2,\text{alias}} = \vert f_2 - kf_s \vert = \vert 350 - 1 \times 500 \vert = 150 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 4 — Reconstructed Signal After Sampling:** $$ x_s(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 200\,t) + 3\cos(2\pi \cdot 150\,t) $$ The amplitude of the second component is preserved; only the frequency has been corrupted from $350$ Hz to $150$ Hz. **Conclusion:** To prevent aliasing of the $350$ Hz component, the sampling rate must be increased to at least $f_s \geq 2 \times 350 = 700$ Hz (Nyquist), or practically $\approx 1$ kHz with a guard band. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the essential elements of a digital signal processing (DSP) system used in a mobile robot for sensor data acquisition. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An 8-bit ADC has a full-scale input range of $0$ to $5$ V. Compute the quantization step and the theoretical SQNR (in dB) for a sinusoidal input that exercises the full scale. If the bit depth is upgraded to 12 bits, by how many dB does the SQNR improve? **(7 Marks)** --- ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 Marks) Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1. The essential elements of a DSP system in a mobile robot are: 1. **Sensor / Transducer:** Converts a physical quantity (light, distance, rotation) into an electrical signal. *Example:* HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor. 2. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, level shifting, impedance matching. *Example:* Op-amp gain stage. 3. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Analog low-pass filter with cutoff $\leq f_s/2$. *Example:* 2nd-order Butterworth LPF. 4. **Sample and Hold (S/H):** Holds the analog voltage constant during ADC conversion. 5. **ADC:** Quantizes the held voltage into $B$-bit digital codes. 6. **Microprocessor / DSP / MCU:** Executes filtering, FFT, sensor fusion, control algorithms. 7. **DAC / PWM Generator:** Converts processed digital commands back into analog control signals. 8. **Actuator and Plant:** Motor, wheel, manipulator performing the physical action. 9. **Feedback Path:** Sensors close the loop, providing continuous data to the controller. The function of each block was tabulated in Section 4.1. --- #### Part (b) — Quantization Calculations (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Quantization Step for 8-bit ADC:** $$ \Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^B} = \frac{5}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} = 19.53 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 2 — SQNR for 8-bit ADC (sinusoidal, full-scale input):** $$ \text{SQNR}_{8} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3 — SQNR for 12-bit ADC:** $$ \text{SQNR}_{12} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 4 — Improvement:** $$ \Delta\text{SQNR} = 74.00 - 49.92 = 24.08 \text{ dB} $$ Equivalently, using the rule of $6.02$ dB/bit: $4 \text{ bits} \times 6.02 = 24.08$ dB — consistent. **Conclusion:** Going from 8 bits to 12 bits improves the dynamic range by approximately **24 dB** (a voltage ratio of $\approx 16{:}1$ or a power ratio of $\approx 251{:}1$). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing Nyquist Frequency with Nyquist Rate:** Students often write "$f_N = 2f_{\max}$" — the *Nyquist Rate* is $2f_{\max}$ (sampling rate), while the *Nyquist Frequency* is $f_s/2$ (the maximum recoverable baseband frequency). Marks are lost when these are swapped. > 2. **Forgetting Units in $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$:** Always state that $f_{\max}$ and $f_s$ must be in the same units (both Hz, or both rad/s — and if rad/s, the condition becomes $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_{\max}$). > 3. **In Quantization Problems, Forgetting Full-Scale Sizing:** The formula $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^B$ assumes a unipolar ADC; for a bipolar range $[-V_{\text{FS}}/2, +V_{\text{FS}}/2]$, the peak-to-peak is still $V_{\text{FS}}$, so the same formula applies. State this clearly. > 4. **Aliasing in Encoder/IMU Problems:** A common mistake is to treat a quadrature encoder pulse train as a "single tone" and apply the aliasing formula incorrectly. The pulse train has a fundamental frequency proportional to wheel speed; if your sampling period is too coarse, wheel-speed *measurement* (not the signal itself) aliases. > 5. **Showing the Replica-Overlap Sketch:** In derivation problems, ALWAYS include a labeled sketch of the replicated spectrum $X_s(f)$ with $X(f)$ shifted by $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$ Examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for the diagram. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Signal:** A function of time (or space) carrying information. Mobile robots work almost exclusively with **discrete-time, discrete-amplitude (digital)** signals internally. - **Sampling:** $x[n] = x(nT_s)$; converts continuous time to discrete time. - **Sampling Frequency vs Period:** $f_s = 1/T_s$; higher $f_s$ → finer time resolution. - **Nyquist–Shannon Theorem:** A bandlimited signal with $f_{\max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed iff $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$. - **Nyquist Rate:** $2f_{\max}$ (the *sampling* rate). **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_s/2$ (the *signal* frequency). - **Spectrum after Sampling:** $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k} X(f - kf_s)$ — periodic replication. - **Aliasing Formula:** $f_a = \vert f - kf_s \vert$, $k$ chosen so $\vert f_a \vert \leq f_s/2$. **Irreversible.** - **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Analog LPF placed before the sampler, cutoff $\leq f_s/2$. Without it, even theoretically correct $f_s$ cannot save you from out-of-band noise folding in. - **Quantization Step:** $\Delta = V_{\text{FS}}/2^B$. - **SQNR Rule:** $\approx 6.02B + 1.76$ dB → each extra bit adds ~6 dB dynamic range. - **Sinc Reconstruction:** $x(t) = \sum_n x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\tfrac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ — ideal but non-causal; practical systems use zero-order hold (ZOH) or higher-order holds. - **Robotics-Relevant Sampling Rates (recall values):** LiDAR ~10–20 Hz, Camera ~30–60 Hz, IMU ~100–1000 Hz, Wheel Encoder ~100–1000 Hz, Ultrasonic ~20–40 Hz. - **Practical Rule:** Sample at $5\times$–$10\times$ the highest signal frequency of interest, not just $2\times$ — this is the "oversampling" practice that gives a usable guard band. - **Units Discipline:** Always carry Hz or rad/s consistently; mixing them is a guaranteed mark-deduction. ---

topics.title: Image Features and Their Combination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Image Features and Their Combination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2: IMAGE FEATURES AND THEIR COMBINATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Image Features** are salient, locally distinct, and computationally extractable patterns in an image (such as corners, edges, blobs, or textures) that remain stable under changes in scale, rotation, illumination, and viewpoint. In Mobile Robotics, they act as **robust visual landmarks** used for localization, mapping (SLAM), object recognition, and visual odometry.

### 1.1 What Exactly is an "Image Feature"?

In a digital image, every pixel carries an intensity value $I(x, y)$. However, most pixels are *uninteresting* — they lie in smooth regions where intensity does not change meaningfully with position. A **feature point** (or **keypoint**) is a pixel location where the local image neighborhood carries a strong, distinctive, and repeatable signal.

Formally, a feature in an image is characterized by three coupled components:

| Component | Mathematical Role | Practical Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Detector** | Locates the $(x, y)$ coordinates where a feature exists | "Where is the feature?" |
| **Descriptor** | Encodes the local appearance into a vector $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | "What does the feature look like?" |
| **Matcher** | Computes similarity between descriptors | "Which features correspond across two views?" |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🧭

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The "Fingerprint of a Place"**
> Imagine you walk into a large, unfamiliar building with only your phone camera. The walls are painted uniformly white (smooth regions — no information). But the *door handle*, the *corner of a stair*, the *edge of a signboard*, and a *circular fire alarm* are unique, easily recognizable, and can be found again from a different angle. These are your **visual features**. Your brain detects them, remembers a description (round, metallic, shiny), and matches them across views to know "I have been here before." Image features in robotics are exactly that — mathematical fingerprints of locations that a robot can robustly detect and re-identify.

### 1.3 Why Features Matter in Mobile Robotics

A mobile robot must answer three questions continuously:

1. **Where am I?** (Localization) → Features are matched against a stored map.
2. **Where have I been?** (Loop closure) → Re-observed features close a loop in SLAM.
3. **How am I moving?** (Visual Odometry) → Feature correspondences between consecutive frames yield the **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ or **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F}$.

The standard pipeline of feature-based vision in mobile robotics is therefore:

$$
\text{Image} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Detector}}\; \text{Keypoints} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Descriptor}}\; \text{Descriptor Vectors} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Matching}}\; \text{Correspondences} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Estimation}}\; \text{Robot Pose / Map}
$$

### 1.4 Categories of Image Features

Features are classified by the underlying image structure they respond to:

| Category | Image Structure | Classical Detector | Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Point / Corner Features** | L-shaped intensity junctions | Harris, Shi-Tomasi, FAST | Rotation-invariant |
| **Blob / Region Features** | Circular/smooth intensity peaks | SIFT, SURF | Scale + rotation invariant |
| **Edge Features** | Linear intensity discontinuities | Canny, Sobel | Sensitive to noise |
| **Binary Features** | Learned/combined local patterns | ORB, BRISK, FREAK | Fast, suitable for embedded robots |
| **Deep Features** | CNN feature maps | SuperPoint, D2-Net | High accuracy, GPU needed |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In a typical KTU 14-mark question, students are expected to know the **mathematical structure** of at least Harris and SIFT, and the **principle of feature combination** (multi-detector or multi-descriptor fusion) for robustness in dynamic environments.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Core of a Feature Detector

Any feature detector fundamentally analyzes the **second-order intensity variation** around a pixel. Given an image patch $I(u, v)$ centered at $(x, y)$, we form the **image gradient**:

$$
I_x = \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}, \qquad I_y = \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}
$$

These gradients are then assembled into the **Structure Tensor** (also called the **Second-Moment Matrix** or **Harris Matrix**):

$$
\mathbf{M} = \sum_{(u,v) \in W} w(u,v)
\begin{bmatrix} I_x(u,v)^2 & I_x(u,v) I_y(u,v) \\ I_x(u,v) I_y(u,v) & I_y(u,v)^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $w(u,v)$ is a weighting window (typically a **Gaussian kernel** with $\sigma \approx 1.5$ pixels) and $W$ is a local neighborhood (usually $3 \times 3$ to $7 \times 7$ pixels).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 2×2 Matrix?**
> The Structure Tensor $\mathbf{M}$ encodes how intensity changes in *every direction* in the local patch. Its eigenvalues $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ (with $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2$) reveal the **shape of the local intensity surface**:
> - If $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \approx 0$ → **flat region** (no feature).
> - If $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$ → **edge** (1D structure).
> - If $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \gg 0$ → **corner** (2D structure, distinctive in all directions).

## 2.2 The Harris Corner Detector

Harris and Stephens (1988) avoided the expensive eigenvalue decomposition of $\mathbf{M}$ by using the **Harris Response Function**:

$$
R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2
$$

where

$$
\det(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2, \qquad \text{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2
$$

and $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ is an empirical sensitivity constant.

A pixel is declared a **Harris corner** if $R > R_{\text{threshold}}$ and $R$ is a **local maximum** in a $3 \times 3$ neighborhood (non-maximum suppression).

## 2.3 The SIFT Detector — Scale Invariance

SIFT (Lowe, 2004) achieves **scale invariance** by searching for features across a **scale space** defined as:

$$
L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)
$$

where $G(x, y, \sigma)$ is a variable-scale Gaussian:

$$
G(x, y, \sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

Scale-invariant keypoints are localized as extrema of the **Difference-of-Gaussians (DoG)** function:

$$
D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)
$$

The SIFT descriptor is a **128-dimensional vector** built from $4 \times 4$ spatial bins, each with **8 orientation bins** of gradient histograms.

## 2.4 Feature Descriptors at a Glance

| Descriptor | Length | Invariance | Compute Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SIFT** | 128-D | Scale + Rotation + Illumination | High | Offline SLAM, mapping |
| **SURF** | 64-D | Scale + Rotation | Medium-High | Real-time desktop robotics |
| **ORB** | 32-byte binary | Rotation (via BRIEF) | Very Low | Embedded / onboard robots |
| **Harris (no descriptor)** | — | Rotation only | Low | Visual odometry front-end |

## 2.5 Feature Matching

Given two sets of descriptors $\{\mathbf{d}_i^A\}$ from image $A$ and $\{\mathbf{j}_j^B\}$ from image $B$, the simplest matcher uses Euclidean distance:

$$
d(i, j) = \lVert \mathbf{d}_i^A - \mathbf{d}_j^B \rVert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(d_{i,k}^A - d_{j,k}^B)^2}
$$

To reject ambiguous matches, **Lowe's Ratio Test** keeps a match only if:

$$
\frac{d_{\text{closest}}}{d_{\text{second closest}}} < \tau, \qquad \tau \approx 0.7
$$

## 2.6 Geometric Verification — The Homography

Once matches are established, a planar scene (or pure rotation) is described by a **Homography Matrix** $\mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathbf{H} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The matrix has **8 degrees of freedom** (defined up to scale), so **4 point correspondences** are sufficient to estimate it (using DLT — Direct Linear Transformation).

For general 3D scenes viewed from two cameras, the relationship is encoded by the **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ (7 DoF, needs $\geq 7$ correspondences) or the **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ (5 DoF, calibrated case, needs $\geq 5$ correspondences with the 5-point algorithm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Use RANSAC?**
> Feature matches always contain **outliers** (false correspondences). Estimating $\mathbf{H}$ or $\mathbf{F}$ on noisy data using least squares will be biased. The **RANSAC** (Random Sample Consensus) algorithm repeatedly samples minimal sets of correspondences, estimates the model, counts inliers within a reprojection threshold $\epsilon$, and keeps the model with the **maximum inlier count**. This is a core topic in KTU examinations.

## 2.7 Feature Combination — The Modern Frontier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** "Combination of features" means *fusing multiple feature types or multiple detector outputs* to obtain a more robust, more discriminative, and more reliable feature set than any single detector alone.

There are four principal combination strategies:

| Strategy | Idea | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Multi-Detector Fusion** | Run two detectors (e.g., Harris + SIFT) and merge keypoints | Combines corner strength with scale invariance |
| **Multi-Descriptor Concatenation** | Stack two descriptor vectors $\mathbf{d} = [\mathbf{d}_{\text{ORB}}, \mathbf{d}_{\text{SIFT}}]$ | Higher-dimensional, more discriminative |
| **Multi-Scale Combination** | Detect features at multiple $\sigma$ values and merge | Used in MSER, SIFT-pyramid |
| **Multi-Modal (Sensor Fusion)** | Combine visual features with LiDAR / IMU / GPS | Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO), ORB-SLAM3 |

The combination is typically justified by a **complementarity** argument: Harris finds many corners but no scale; SIFT finds scale-invariant blobs but is slow; ORB is fast but less robust to large illumination change. Their union is stronger.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{M} = \sum w \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Structure / Harris matrix |
| 2 | $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k\,\text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2$ | Harris corner response |
| 3 | $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 = \text{eigenvalues}(\mathbf{M})$ | Local intensity surface shape |
| 4 | $L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)$ | SIFT scale space |
| 5 | $D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)$ | Difference of Gaussians |
| 6 | $d(i, j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k}(d_{i,k} - d_{j,k})^2}$ | Euclidean distance (SIFT matching) |
| 7 | $d_{\text{Hamming}}(\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2) = \sum_{k} \mathbf{b}_1[k] \oplus \mathbf{b}_2[k]$ | Hamming distance (ORB / binary) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d_{\text{closest}}}{d_{\text{2nd-closest}}} < 0.7$ | Lowe's ratio test |
| 9 | $\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathbf{H} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Homography (planar / pure rotation) |
| 10 | $\mathbf{x}'^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ | Fundamental matrix (epipolar) |
| 11 | $\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{K}^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{K}$ | Essential–Fundamental relation |
| 12 | $\mathbf{d}_{\text{combined}} = [\mathbf{d}_{\text{ORB}} \;\vert\; \mathbf{d}_{\text{SIFT}}]$ | Descriptor concatenation (combination) |
| 13 | $\text{Inlier Ratio} = \dfrac{\#\text{inliers}}{\#\text{total matches}}$ | RANSAC quality metric |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Harris Response Function

### Step 1 — Image Patch Approximation

Consider shifting a small image window $W$ centered at $(x, y)$ by a small displacement $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$. The resulting change in intensity is captured by the **SSD (Sum of Squared Differences)** function:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \sum_{(u,v) \in W} w(u,v)\,\bigl[\,I(u+\Delta x,\, v+\Delta y) - I(u, v)\,\bigr]^2
$$

### Step 2 — First-Order Taylor Expansion

Using the first-order Taylor expansion of $I$ around $(u, v)$:

$$
I(u + \Delta x, v + \Delta y) \approx I(u, v) + I_x \cdot \Delta x + I_y \cdot \Delta y
$$

Substituting back into $E$:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) \approx \sum_{(u,v) \in W} w(u,v)\,\bigl[\,I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y\,\bigr]^2
$$

### Step 3 — Expanding the Quadratic

The squared term expands to:

$$
\bigl[\,I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y\,\bigr]^2 = I_x^2 \Delta x^2 + 2 I_x I_y \Delta x \Delta y + I_y^2 \Delta y^2
$$

### Step 4 — Matrix Formulation

We can rewrite $E$ in matrix form as:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) \approx
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x & \Delta y \end{bmatrix}
\left( \sum_{(u,v) \in W} w(u,v)
\begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \right)
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is exactly the quadratic form:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x & \Delta y \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{M} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix}
$$

which proves that $\mathbf{M}$ is the local "shape descriptor" of the intensity surface.

### Step 5 — From Eigenspace to Harris Response

Because $\mathbf{M}$ is real, symmetric, and positive semi-definite, it has a spectral decomposition:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{R}^{-\mathsf{T}}
\begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 \end{bmatrix}
\mathbf{R}^{-1}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is a rotation matrix. The two eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ characterize the principal curvatures of $E$. The classification table is:

| Condition | Classification |
|---|---|
| $\lambda_1 \approx 0, \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Flat region — no feature |
| $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Edge (1D signal) |
| $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \gg 0$ | Corner (2D signal) |

To avoid the cost of explicit eigendecomposition, Harris and Stephens used:

$$
R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2 = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2
$$

This is the **Harris corner response**.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

Let a $3 \times 3$ image patch (in a window) yield gradient products:

$$
I_x^2 = 4, \quad I_y^2 = 9, \quad I_x I_y = 3
$$

So:

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad k = 0.04
$$

Computing the determinants and trace:

$$
\det(\mathbf{M}) = 4 \cdot 9 - 3 \cdot 3 = 36 - 9 = 27
$$

$$
\text{trace}(\mathbf{M}) = 4 + 9 = 13, \quad \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2 = 169
$$

$$
R = 27 - 0.04 \cdot 169 = 27 - 6.76 = 20.24
$$

Since $R \gg 0$, this pixel is classified as a **corner** ✓.

## 3.2 Homography Estimation via DLT (4-Point Algorithm)

### Step 1 — Write the Linear Equations

Given a planar homography:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For each correspondence $(x, y) \leftrightarrow (x', y')$, the cross product $\mathbf{x}' \times \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ yields two linear equations in the 9 unknowns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{11} x + h_{12} y + h_{13} - h_{31} x x' - h_{32} y x' - h_{33} x' &= 0 \\
h_{21} x + h_{22} y + h_{23} - h_{31} x y' - h_{32} y y' - h_{33} y' &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Stack 4 Correspondences into $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}
x_1 & y_1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -x_1 x_1' & -y_1 x_1' & -x_1' \\
0 & 0 & 0 & x_1 & y_1 & 1 & -x_1 y_1' & -y_1 y_1' & -y_1' \\
x_2 & y_2 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -x_2 x_2' & -y_2 x_2' & -x_2' \\
0 & 0 & 0 & x_2 & y_2 & 1 & -x_2 y_2' & -y_2 y_2' & -y_2' \\
x_3 & y_3 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -x_3 x_3' & -y_3 x_3' & -x_3' \\
0 & 0 & 0 & x_3 & y_3 & 1 & -x_3 y_3' & -y_3 y_3' & -y_3' \\
x_4 & y_4 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -x_4 x_4' & -y_4 x_4' & -x_4' \\
0 & 0 & 0 & x_4 & y_4 & 1 & -x_4 y_4' & -y_4 y_4' & -y_4'
\end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{h} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} \\ \vdots \\ h_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solve $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$ subject to $\lVert \mathbf{h} \rVert = 1$ via SVD: $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\mathsf{T}}$. The solution is the right singular vector $\mathbf{V}_{:,9}$ corresponding to the smallest singular value.

### Step 3 — Apply RANSAC for Robustness

| Iteration | Sample 4 random correspondences | Estimate $\mathbf{H}$ | Count inliers with $\lVert \mathbf{x}' - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} \rVert < \epsilon$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1, 2, ... N | Subsample | Solve DLT | Score = #inliers |
| **Best** | — | — | Pick $\mathbf{H}^{\star}$ with max inliers |

The minimum number of iterations to reach a good model with probability $p$ (e.g., 0.99) and inlier ratio $w$ is:

$$
N = \frac{\log(1 - p)}{\log(1 - w^4)}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detector Comparison

| Property | Harris | SIFT | SURF | ORB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Author** | 1988 / Harris-Stephens | 2004 / Lowe | 2006 / Bay et al. | 2011 / Rublee et al. |
| **Scale Invariant?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Multi-scale pyramid |
| **Rotation Invariant?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Intensity centroid |
| **Illumination Robust?** | Moderate | Strong | Strong | Moderate |
| **Affine Invariant?** | ❌ | ❌ (✅ Hessian-Affine variant) | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Descriptor Type** | None (patch-based) | Float (128-D) | Float (64-D) | Binary (32-byte) |
| **Speed** | Fast | Slow | Medium | Very fast |
| **Best Suited For** | Visual odometry, planar SLAM | Loop closure, large-scale mapping | Mid-tier SLAM | Embedded / mobile robots |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feature Combination Methods

| Method | Type of Fusion | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Harris + SIFT | Detector-level (union of keypoints) | Both corner & blob coverage | Larger computation |
| ORB + SIFT (descriptor concat) | Descriptor-level | Highly discriminative | High memory |
| Multi-scale pyramid | Scale-level | Scale invariance | More keypoints → slower matching |
| Visual + IMU | Sensor-level | Robust to motion blur / textureless scenes | Calibration required |
| CNN + classical | Learned + handcrafted | Best in challenging scenes | GPU needed |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full Feature Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    imgA[Image Frame at Time t] --> grayA[Grayscale Conversion]
    grayA --> detectA[Feature Detector]
    detectA --> kpA[Keypoint Set A]
    kpA --> descA[Descriptor Extractor]
    descA --> dvecA[Descriptor Vectors A]

    imgB[Image Frame at Time t+1] --> grayB[Grayscale Conversion]
    grayB --> detectB[Feature Detector]
    detectB --> kpB[Keypoint Set B]
    kpB --> descB[Descriptor Extractor]
    descB --> dvecB[Descriptor Vectors B]

    dvecA --> match[Feature Matcher]
    dvecB --> match
    match --> rtest[Lowe Ratio Test]
    rtest --> ransac[RANSAC Geometric Verification]
    ransac --> homo[Homography F or E]
    homo --> pose[Robot Pose Map Update]

    detectA --> fusion[Multi Detector Fusion]
    detectB --> fusion
    fusion --> combined[Combined Keypoints]

    style imgA fill:#cfe2ff
    style imgB fill:#cfe2ff
    style match fill:#fff3cd
    style ransac fill:#f8d7da
    style pose fill:#d1e7dd
    style fusion fill:#e2d9f3
```

## 4.2 Feature Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Pixel Patch] --> eig[Eigenvalues of M]
    eig --> c1{lambda1 approx 0 AND lambda2 approx 0}
    eig --> c2{lambda1 much greater than lambda2}
    eig --> c3{lambda1 approx lambda2 much greater than 0}

    c1 --> flat[Flat Region - Reject]
    c2 --> edge[Edge - Reject]
    c3 --> corner[Corner - Accept as Keypoint]

    corner --> scale{Scale Invariance Needed?}
    scale -- Yes --> sift[SIFT DoG Pyramid]
    scale -- No --> harris[Harris Response]

    sift --> desc[Build Descriptor SIFT 128D]
    harris --> desc2[Build Descriptor ORB BRIEF 32B]

    style start fill:#cfe2ff
    style flat fill:#f8d7da
    style edge fill:#fff3cd
    style corner fill:#d1e7dd
```

## 4.3 Feature Combination Architecture (Multi-Sensor / Multi-Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Vision[Visual Front End]
        cam[Camera]
        harris[Harris Detector]
        sift[SIFT Detector]
        orb[ORB Detector]
    end

    subgraph Inertial[Inertial Front End]
        imu[IMU]
        preint[Preintegration]
    end

    subgraph Fusion[Feature Combination Layer]
        concat[Descriptor Concatenation]

    end

    subgraph BackEnd[SLAM Back End]
        pose[Robot Pose]
        map[Landmark Map]
    end

    cam --> harris
    cam --> sift
    cam --> orb
    imu --> preint

    harris --> concat
    sift --> concat
    orb --> concat
    preint --> ekf

    concat --> ekf
    ekf --> pose
    ekf --> map

    style Vision fill:#cfe2ff
    style Inertial fill:#fff3cd
    style Fusion fill:#e2d9f3
    style BackEnd fill:#d1e7dd
```

## 4.4 RANSAC Robust Estimation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Start RANSAC] --> s2[Sample 4 Random Correspondences]
    s2 --> s3[Estimate Homography H via DLT]
    s3 --> s4[Count Inliers within threshold epsilon]
    s4 --> s5{Inlier Count greater than Best}
    s5 -- Yes --> s6[Update Best H and Inlier Set]
    s5 -- No --> s7{Iterations less than N}
    s6 --> s7
    s7 -- Yes --> s2
    s7 -- No --> s8[Refine H on All Inliers]
    s8 --> s9[Output Final H and Inliers]

    style s1 fill:#cfe2ff
    style s8 fill:#d1e7dd
    style s9 fill:#d1e7dd
```
