# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**Define an image feature. List any two classical feature detectors and state one key property of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **image feature** is a salient, locally distinct, and repeatable image pattern (typically a point, corner, edge, or blob) that can be reliably detected and described in terms of its local intensity neighborhood. Features serve as visual landmarks for tasks such as localization, mapping, and visual odometry in mobile robotics. **(1 mark)**
>
> Two classical detectors:
> - **Harris Corner Detector (1988):** Rotation-invariant, based on the eigenvalues of the structure tensor $\mathbf{M}$. It uses the response $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k\cdot\text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2$, where large $R$ indicates a corner. **(1 mark)**
> - **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform, 2004):** Detects keypoints as extrema of the **Difference-of-Gaussians (DoG)** across a Gaussian scale-space, giving **scale and rotation invariance**, and produces a 128-D descriptor. **(1 mark)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**What is Lowe's ratio test and why is it essential in feature matching?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Lowe's ratio test is a filtering rule applied after computing the Euclidean distances between a query descriptor and all database descriptors. **(1 mark)** A match between descriptor $A_i$ and $B_j$ is accepted only if:
>
> $$\frac{d(A_i, B_{\text{closest}})}{d(A_i, B_{\text{2nd closest}})} < \tau, \quad \tau \approx 0.7$$
>
> This rejects **ambiguous matches** — descriptors that look almost equally similar to two different database points, indicating an unreliable correspondence. **(1 mark)** It is essential because raw nearest-neighbor matching in high-dimensional descriptor spaces produces many false matches due to the curse of dimensionality, and the ratio test dramatically improves precision (often to >95%) without sacrificing recall. **(1 mark)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Module Internal Choice):** Students must answer ONE full question from a pair. Each 14-mark question typically splits into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the **Harris corner response** $R$ from the second-moment matrix $\mathbf{M}$. Show explicitly the role of eigenvalues $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ in classifying image regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **SIFT feature detection pipeline**. State the scale-space equation, the DoG function, and the role of keypoint stabilization. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Intensity Change Function** (1 mark)

For a small window $W$ shifted by $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$, the intensity change is:

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \sum_{(u,v) \in W} w(u,v)\,\bigl[\,I(u+\Delta x, v+\Delta y) - I(u,v)\,\bigr]^2
$$

**Step 2 — Taylor Expansion** (1 mark)

$$
I(u+\Delta x, v+\Delta y) \approx I + I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y
$$

Substituting and expanding:

$$
E \approx \sum w \bigl[\,I_x \Delta x + I_y \Delta y\,\bigr]^2
$$

**Step 3 — Quadratic Form** (1 mark)

$$
E(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x & \Delta y \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{M} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix}
$$

with

$$
\mathbf{M} = \sum w \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigen Decomposition** (1 mark)

Since $\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric PSD, it has real non-negative eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq 0$ with orthonormal eigenvectors. So:

$$
E \approx \lambda_1 \Delta x^2 + \lambda_2 \Delta y^2 \quad \text{(in eigen-frame)}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification** (1 mark)

- $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ both small → flat region ($E \approx 0$ for any shift).
- $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$ → edge (shift along $\lambda_2$ direction causes no change).
- $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ both large → corner (any shift causes large $E$).

**Step 6 — Harris Response** (2 marks)

To avoid the cost of eigendecomposition, use:

$$
R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2 = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2
$$

with $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$. Pixels with $R > R_{\text{threshold}}$ and a local-maximum $R$ are corners. The factor $k$ penalizes edges, since for an edge $\lambda_1 \lambda_2$ is small and $(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2$ is large, giving strongly negative $R$.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

**Block Diagram of SIFT Pipeline** (3 marks)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image] --> B[Scale Space Construction]
    B --> C[DoG Computation]
    C --> D[Extrema Detection 3x3x3]
    D --> E[Keypoint Stabilization]
    E --> F[Orientation Assignment]
    F --> G[Descriptor Building 128D]
    G --> H[Output SIFT Features]
```

**Scale-Space Equation** (1 mark)

$$
L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y), \qquad G(x,y,\sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

Multiplied across octaves doubling $\sigma$.

**DoG Function** (1 mark)

$$
D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)
$$

**Extrema Detection** (1 mark)

Each pixel is compared with its 8 spatial neighbours and 9 neighbours above and 9 below in the scale pyramid — total 26 comparisons. Only the maxima and minima of $D$ are retained.

**Keypoint Stabilization** (1 mark)

Candidate keypoints are refined by:
- **Sub-pixel localization** via Taylor expansion of $D$ up to quadratic terms.
- **Rejecting low-contrast** keypoints ($|D| < 0.03$).
- **Rejecting edge responses** using a Hessian-ratio test (similar to Harris).
This yields stable, repeatable keypoints invariant to scale and rotation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** What is meant by **combination of features** in a mobile robotics vision pipeline? Discuss **multi-detector fusion** and **descriptor concatenation** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **RANSAC algorithm** for robust homography estimation from feature correspondences. Derive the iteration-count formula. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a)

**Definition of Feature Combination** (1 mark)

Feature combination refers to the practice of **fusing multiple feature detectors, descriptors, scales, or sensor modalities** to obtain a more robust, discriminative, and viewpoint-invariant feature representation than any single method alone. In a mobile robot operating in a dynamic and uncertain environment, no single detector is universally best — Harris finds corners but not blobs; SIFT is robust but slow; ORB is fast but less accurate. Combining them exploits **complementarity**.

**Multi-Detector Fusion** (2 marks)

In this strategy, two or more independent detectors are run on the same image, and their keypoint sets are merged (often with deduplication by spatial proximity). Example:

- Run **Harris** (corner-rich) + **SIFT** (blob-rich) → take the union.
- Apply a confidence threshold (e.g., $R > R_{\text{Harris}}$ and DoG response $> T_{\text{SIFT}}$).
- Result: more keypoints covering both corner and blob structures, increasing the chance of at least 4 inliers for homography estimation.

**Descriptor Concatenation** (2 marks)

When two descriptors are computed for the same keypoint, they can be **concatenated** to form a higher-dimensional descriptor:

$$
\mathbf{d}_{\text{combined}} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{d}_{\text{ORB}} \\ \mathbf{d}_{\text{SIFT}} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{32 + 128} = \mathbb{R}^{160}
$$

This combines the **speed of ORB** with the **discriminative power of SIFT**. Matching then uses a weighted distance, e.g.:

$$
d_{\text{combined}} = \alpha \cdot d_{\text{ORB}}^{\text{Hamming}} + (1-\alpha)\cdot d_{\text{SIFT}}^{\text{Euclidean}}
$$

**Other Strategies (briefly)** (2 marks)

- **Multi-scale combination:** detect at multiple $\sigma$, merge.
- **Multi-modal:** combine visual features with IMU / LiDAR / GPS for Visual-Inertial SLAM (e.g., ORB-SLAM3).
- **Deep + classical:** SuperPoint CNN features combined with handcrafted geometry.

**Why combination is critical in mobile robotics:** it improves reliability under lighting changes, dynamic obstacles, and textureless scenes where a single detector fails.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b)

**Problem Statement** (1 mark)

Given $N$ feature correspondences $\{(x_i, y_i) \leftrightarrow (x_i', y_i')\}$ between two images, some of which are **outliers** (false matches), estimate the **homography matrix** $\mathbf{H}$ that best explains the inlier correspondences.

**RANSAC Algorithm Steps** (3 marks)

1. Randomly sample a **minimal set** of 4 correspondences (since homography has 8 DoF).
2. Estimate a candidate $\mathbf{H}$ using the **DLT (Direct Linear Transformation)**.
3. Apply $\mathbf{H}$ to all $N$ correspondences and count **inliers** — those with reprojection error:
   $$\lVert \mathbf{x}' - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} \rVert_2 < \epsilon$$ where $\epsilon \approx 1$–$3$ pixels.
4. If the inlier count exceeds the current best, store $(\mathbf{H}^{\star}, \text{inlier set})$.
5. Repeat for $N$ iterations (or until probability of finding a clean sample is high).
6. **Final refinement:** re-estimate $\mathbf{H}^{\star}$ using **all inliers** via least-squares DLT.

**Iteration Count Derivation** (3 marks)

Let:
- $p$ = desired probability of success (e.g., $0.99$).
- $w$ = inlier ratio = $\dfrac{\#\text{inliers}}{\#\text{total}}$.
- $s = 4$ = sample size for homography.

The probability that **one** random sample of 4 is all-inlier is $w^4$. The probability that **one** sample is contaminated is $1 - w^4$. After $N$ iterations, the probability that **all** $N$ samples are contaminated is $(1 - w^4)^N$. For success:

$$
1 - (1 - w^4)^N \geq p
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
(1 - w^4)^N \leq 1 - p
$$

Take the natural log of both sides (note $\ln(1-x) \leq -x$ for $x \in [0,1)$):

$$
N \cdot \ln(1 - w^4) \leq \ln(1 - p)
$$

$$
N \geq \frac{\ln(1 - p)}{\ln(1 - w^4)}
$$

**Example:** if $w = 0.5$ and $p = 0.99$:

$$
N \geq \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(1 - 0.5^4)} = \frac{-4.605}{\ln(0.9375)} = \frac{-4.605}{-0.0645} \approx 72 \text{ iterations}
$$

If $w = 0.3$ (heavy outliers), $N$ rises to $\approx 567$ iterations. This is why **good feature matchers** (high $w$) drastically reduce RANSAC runtime.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $\mathbf{F}$ and $\mathbf{H}$:** Students often write the homography equation when the question asks for the fundamental matrix (and vice versa). Remember: $\mathbf{H}$ is for **planar scenes or pure rotation**; $\mathbf{F}$ is for **general 3D scenes**.
> 2. **Forgetting scale ambiguity:** Homography has only **8 DoF** (not 9); always normalize $h_{33} = 1$ or $\lVert \mathbf{h} \rVert = 1$.
> 3. **RANSAC iteration count:** Skipping the derivation step and directly writing the formula loses 2–3 marks. Always show $\ln(1-p)/\ln(1-w^s)$.
> 4. **Feature combination ≠ simple averaging:** Examiners expect students to discuss **concatenation, fusion, multi-scale**, not "I take the average of SIFT and ORB." Be precise.
> 5. **Lowe's ratio test threshold:** $\tau = 0.7$ is the standard. Writing 0.5 or 1.0 loses credit unless justified.
> 6. **Eigenvalue classification:** Many students write only "if both eigenvalues are large → corner" but fail to mention the **edge case** ($\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$) and the role of $k$ in suppressing edges.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Image feature** = salient, repeatable, locally distinct image pattern (corner, blob, edge).
- ✅ **Three components:** Detector (where), Descriptor (what), Matcher (correspondence).
- ✅ **Harris Matrix** = structure tensor from gradients; **eigenvalues** classify flat / edge / corner.
- ✅ **Harris Response:** $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - k \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2$ with $k \approx 0.04$–$0.06$.
- ✅ **SIFT** uses a Gaussian scale space + **DoG** to achieve scale invariance; descriptor is **128-D**.
- ✅ **ORB** is the **fast, binary** choice for embedded mobile robots; descriptor is **32-byte** BRIEF.
- ✅ **Lowe's Ratio Test:** keep match only if $d_{\text{1st}}/d_{\text{2nd}} < 0.7$ — rejects ambiguous matches.
- ✅ **Homography $\mathbf{H}$** relates two views of a **planar** scene: 8 DoF, 4-point DLT.
- ✅ **RANSAC** is mandatory for robust estimation under outlier-contaminated correspondences.
- ✅ **Iteration count:** $N = \dfrac{\ln(1-p)}{\ln(1-w^s)}$ — derive, do not just state.
- ✅ **Feature combination** strategies: **multi-detector fusion**, **descriptor concatenation**, **multi-scale**, **multi-modal (visual+IMU)**.
- ✅ **Why combine?** No single detector is universally robust — complementarity is the engineering motivation.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** ORB-SLAM, Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO), structure-from-motion, autonomous vehicle localization, drone navigation.
- ✅ **KTU-typical 14-mark answer** should contain: derivation + diagram + comparison table + numerical example.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Tip (3 minutes before exam):**
> Memorize the **Harris response formula**, the **RANSAC iteration count formula**, the **SIFT scale-space equation**, and the **homography equation**. These four equations cover >70% of typical Module-2 image-features questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring Depth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measuring Depth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — MEASURING DEPTH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Depth Measurement** in mobile robotics is the process of estimating the perpendicular distance from a robot's onboard sensor (camera, laser, ultrasonic, or infrared projector) to points on the surfaces of objects in the surrounding environment, producing a 2.5-D or 3-D spatial representation of the scene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Syllabus Highlight)**
> Depth measurement is the determination of the third coordinate (range, $Z$) of every pixel/point in the scene, given the 2-D image coordinates $(x, y)$ produced by an imaging sensor. It is the foundation of *obstacle avoidance*, *localization*, *mapping (SLAM)*, and *scene reconstruction* in autonomous mobile robots.

Mathematically, the generic depth-sensing problem is to recover $Z$ from the equation

$$
Z = f(\text{sensor reading}, \text{sensor geometry}, \text{scene reflectance})
$$

where the function $f$ depends on the chosen physical principle (triangulation, time-of-flight, or interferometry).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — How Humans "Measure Depth"**
> Your two eyes see the same object from slightly different angles. The brain fuses these two images and uses the tiny horizontal shift (called *binocular disparity*) between them to judge how far the object is — the larger the shift, the closer the object. **Stereo vision in robots works on exactly the same principle.** Alternatively, bats emit a chirp and listen for the echo; the time between emission and reception tells them how far the wall is. **Time-of-Flight sensors use the same idea** with light pulses instead of sound.

In short, there are only **two physical tricks** that almost every depth sensor uses:

1. **Triangulation** — use geometry (two viewpoints, or a known light pattern).
2. **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** — use the speed of light (or sound) and a precise timer.

| Method Class | Physical Trick | Typical Range | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stereo Vision | Triangulation (parallax) | $0.5$ m – $30$ m | mm – cm |
| Structured Light | Triangulation (known pattern) | $0.2$ m – $5$ m | mm |
| Laser Triangulation | Triangulation (sheet of light) | $0.05$ m – $2$ m | $\mu$m – mm |
| LIDAR (ToF) | Speed of light, $c$ | $0.1$ m – $200$ m | cm |
| Ultrasonic | Speed of sound, $v_s$ | $0.02$ m – $10$ m | cm |
| ToF Camera (PMD) | Phase shift of modulated IR | $0.1$ m – $5$ m | cm |

### 1.3 Why Depth Measurement Matters for Mobile Robots

A mobile robot without depth perception is *blind to obstacles*. Depth information enables:

- **Obstacle detection & avoidance** (front, rear, side scans).
- **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) — building a 2-D or 3-D occupancy grid.
- **Object recognition with pose** — knowing *where* an object is, not just *what* it is.
- **Path planning** — computing free-space corridors.
- **Manipulation** — grasping requires cm-level depth at the gripper.

The standard unit in robotics is **meters (m)**, while sensor-level measurements are often reported in **millimeters (mm)** or **disparity pixels (px)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Depth Measurement

### Pillar 1 — Triangulation-Based Depth

The principle of **triangulation** dates back to antiquity. If you know the length of one side of a triangle and the two adjacent angles, you can compute every other side and angle. In robotics, this becomes the **stereo vision** and **structured-light** principle.

**Operational Steps (Bullet Logic):**

1. Two viewpoints (real cameras, or one camera + one projector) are separated by a known **baseline $B$**.
2. Both viewpoints observe the same scene point $P = (X, Y, Z)$.
3. The optical axes of the two viewpoints are made **parallel** (rectified stereo) so that the two image points $p_L$ and $p_R$ lie on the *same horizontal scanline*.
4. The **horizontal pixel difference** between the two image points is called the **disparity $d$**:
   $$
   d = x_L - x_R
   $$
5. Through similar triangles in the camera geometry, the depth $Z$ is recovered as
   $$
   Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
   $$
6. The 3-D coordinates of $P$ are then:
   $$
   X = \frac{B \cdot x_L}{d}, \qquad Y = \frac{B \cdot y}{d}, \qquad Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
   $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $Z$ is *inversely* proportional to $d$:**
> When a point is **far away**, the two camera rays are almost parallel, so the disparity is **small**, hence $Z$ is **large**. When the point is **close**, the rays diverge strongly, $d$ is **large**, so $Z$ is **small**. This inverse relationship is the signature of all triangulation-based depth sensors.

---

### Pillar 2 — Time-of-Flight (ToF) Depth

A **Time-of-Flight** sensor emits a short pulse of light (or a continuous modulated wave) and measures the round-trip time $t$ taken for the light to travel to the object and back.

**Operational Steps:**

1. The emitter (laser diode or LED) sends out a pulse at time $t = 0$.
2. The pulse travels at the speed of light $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.
3. The pulse reflects off the target surface.
4. The receiver detects the return pulse at time $t$.
5. The round-trip distance is
   $$
   D_{round} = c \cdot t
   $$
6. Since the light has travelled *twice* the depth, the actual depth is
   $$
   Z = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
   $$

**Continuous-Wave (CW) Variant:** A sinusoidally modulated light beam is emitted; the reflected wave is phase-shifted by $\phi$ relative to the emitted wave. Depth is then recovered as
$$
Z = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}
$$
where $f_{mod}$ is the modulation frequency (typically $10$–$50$ MHz).

---

### Pillar 3 — Structured-Light Depth

A **structured-light** sensor projects a **known pattern** (a grid of dots, lines, or a coded sequence) onto the scene using an IR projector. A single IR camera observes how the pattern deforms over the scene's surfaces. By matching observed pattern features to the projected pattern codebook, the system performs **dense per-pixel matching** without needing a second physical camera.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic example — Microsoft Kinect v1**: Projects a fixed speckle pattern of IR dots and uses a single IR CMOS camera. The shift of each dot relative to a reference plane gives the depth at that pixel. Operating range: $0.8$ m – $4.0$ m with cm-level accuracy.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU End-Semester paper on this module.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinhole projection | $x = \dfrac{f X}{Z}, \quad y = \dfrac{f Y}{Z}$ | $f$ in px or mm; $X,Y,Z$ in m | Maps 3-D to 2-D |
| 2 | Disparity | $d = x_L - x_R$ | pixels | Horizontal shift between stereo pair |
| 3 | Depth from disparity | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $f$ in px, $B$ in m, $d$ in px, $Z$ in m | Core stereo equation |
| 4 | 3-D reconstruction | $X = \dfrac{B \, x_L}{d}, \; Y = \dfrac{B \, y}{d}$ | world units | Per-pixel triangulation |
| 5 | Depth resolution | $\Delta Z = \dfrac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot B} \, \Delta d$ | $Z$ in m | Worse (larger) for far points |
| 6 | Field of View (FOV) | $\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2 f}\right)$ | radians or degrees | $w$ = sensor width |
| 7 | ToF pulsed | $Z = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ | $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | $t$ in s |
| 8 | ToF CW phase | $Z = \dfrac{c \, \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}$ | $\phi$ in rad, $f_{mod}$ in Hz | Limited to $\lambda/2$ ambiguity |
| 9 | Maximum unambiguous range | $Z_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$ | metres | For CW ToF |
| 10 | Laser triangulation | $Z = \dfrac{f \, B}{x - x^{\prime}}$ | $x^{\prime}$ = laser image shift | $B$ = camera–laser baseline |
| 11 | Ultrasonic range | $Z = \dfrac{v_s \, t}{2}$ | $v_s \approx 343$ m/s in air | Cheap but wide beam |
| 12 | Lens equation (focus) | $\dfrac{1}{Z} + \dfrac{1}{Z^{\prime}} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | thin-lens rule | Sets camera focus |
| 13 | Baseline selection | $B \approx \dfrac{Z_{target}^{\,2} \cdot \Delta d}{f \cdot \Delta Z}$ | trade-off | Bigger $B$ = better far-depth |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit consistency is the #1 KTU mark-loss cause.** When $f$ is given in pixels and $B$ in metres, the product $f \cdot B$ yields **pixel·metres**, and the disparity $d$ is in **pixels** — they cancel cleanly only if the same convention is used. Always check whether $f$ is the *focal length in mm* (then divide by pixel size $p_x$ to get pixel units) or the *focal length in pixels* (then $B$ must be in metres).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla, Mobileye):** Stereo cameras + spinning LIDAR for $360°$ depth.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Locus Robotics):** 2-D LIDAR for SLAM and obstacle avoidance.
- **Agricultural Robots:** Solid-state 3-D ToF cameras for crop-height mapping.
- **Surgical Robots (da Vinci):** Stereo endoscopes for sub-mm depth perception in tissue.
- **Drones (DJI):** Downward ToF + stereo vision pairs for terrain-relative navigation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depth from Stereo Disparity (Pinhole Model)

We will rigorously derive the **canonical stereo depth equation** $Z = fB/d$ from first principles using the pinhole camera model.

### Setup

- Two identical cameras, $C_L$ (left) and $C_R$ (right), with parallel optical axes.
- The two camera centres are separated along the $X$-axis by a distance $B$ (the **baseline**).
- Each camera has focal length $f$ (in pixels for now).
- A 3-D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to image points $p_L = (x_L, y_L)$ and $p_R = (x_R, y_R)$ on the two image planes.

### Step 1 — Apply the Pinhole Projection Equation

For the **left camera** (origin at $C_L$):

$$
x_L = \frac{f \cdot X}{Z}, \qquad y_L = \frac{f \cdot Y}{Z}
$$

For the **right camera** (origin at $C_R$, shifted by $-B$ along the world $X$-axis), the world coordinates relative to $C_R$ are $(X - B, Y, Z)$:

$$
x_R = \frac{f \cdot (X - B)}{Z}, \qquad y_R = \frac{f \cdot Y}{Z}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Disparity

By definition, disparity is the horizontal pixel difference between the left and right image points:

$$
d \;=\; x_L - x_R
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Projection Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{f \cdot X}{Z} - \frac{f \cdot (X - B)}{Z} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{f \cdot X}{Z} - \frac{f \cdot X}{Z} + \frac{f \cdot B}{Z} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $X$ terms cancel perfectly — this is the geometric magic of stereo vision.

### Step 4 — Solve for the Depth $Z$

Inverting the relation:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

This is the **fundamental stereo depth equation**, the single most-tested formula in the KTU Mobile Robotics exam.

### Step 5 — Recover the Other 3-D Coordinates

Substituting $Z = fB/d$ back into the left-camera projection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{Z \cdot x_L}{f} = \frac{(fB/d) \cdot x_L}{f} = \frac{B \, x_L}{d} \\[4pt]
Y &= \frac{Z \cdot y}{f} = \frac{B \, y}{d} \\[4pt]
Z &= \frac{f \, B}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive the Depth Resolution $\Delta Z$

Suppose the disparity estimate has an uncertainty $\Delta d$ (typically $\pm 1$ pixel for integer-pel matching). Taking the differential of $Z = fB/d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dZ}{dd} &= -\frac{f \cdot B}{d^{2}} \\[4pt]
\Delta Z &\approx \frac{f \cdot B}{d^{2}} \, \Delta d
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute $d = fB/Z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{\left(\dfrac{fB}{Z}\right)^{2}} \, \Delta d \\[4pt]
         &= \frac{f \cdot B \cdot Z^{2}}{(fB)^{2}} \, \Delta d \\[4pt]
         &= \frac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot B} \, \Delta d
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **depth-resolution equation**. It says that depth error grows as the **square** of distance and falls as the product $fB$ grows. **Doubling the baseline halves the depth error** — a critical design rule for stereo rigs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-of-Flight Range from Pulse Round-Trip

### Step 1 — State the Physical Model

A pulse of light is emitted at $t = 0$, reflects from a target at distance $Z$, and returns at time $t$. Light travels at constant speed $c = 299\,792\,458$ m/s in vacuum (approximately $c$ in air for robotics).

### Step 2 — Total Path Length

The light travels **to** the target and **back**, so the total path length is

$$
L_{total} = 2 Z
$$

### Step 3 — Use Distance = Speed × Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= c \cdot t \\[4pt]
2 Z &= c \cdot t \\[4pt]
Z &= \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (Worked)

A LIDAR measures $t = 33.4$ ns. Find the target distance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{(3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}) \cdot (33.4 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{s})}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{10.02 \; \text{m}}{2} \\[4pt]
  &= 5.01 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to a 5-m distant wall — exactly the kind of numerical expected in a KTU problem.

### Step 5 — Continuous-Wave (Phase-Shift) Variant

If the emitted wave is $E(t) = A \cos(2 \pi f_{mod} t)$ and the received wave is $R(t) = A' \cos(2 \pi f_{mod} t - \phi)$, then the round-trip time relates to phase shift by

$$
t = \frac{\phi}{2 \pi f_{mod}}
$$

Substituting into the ToF equation:

$$
Z = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \frac{\phi}{2 \pi f_{mod}} = \frac{c \, \phi}{4 \pi f_{mod}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ambiguity Limit:** The phase is only defined modulo $2\pi$, so the **maximum unambiguous range** is when $\phi = 2\pi$:
> $$
> Z_{max} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}}
> $$
> For $f_{mod} = 20$ MHz, $Z_{max} = 7.5$ m. This is why many ToF cameras are designed for indoor (< 5 m) use only.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Laser Triangulation Range

A **laser triangulation sensor** uses a single camera and a laser line projector offset by baseline $B$. The laser projects a sheet of light at angle $\theta_0$ onto the scene; the camera sees the laser line at a pixel position $x^{\prime}$ shifted from its reference at $x_0$.

For a small-angle geometry, similar triangles give

$$
Z = \frac{f \, B}{x^{\prime} - x_0}
$$

Derivation: The laser line position on the image plane encodes the angle of the return ray. As the target moves by $\Delta Z$, the angle changes by $\Delta \theta = B/Z^{2} \cdot \Delta Z$, and the pixel shift is $\Delta x^{\prime} = f \cdot \Delta \theta$. Inverting and integrating gives the standard form above. This sensor is widely used for **bin-picking** and **road-surface profiling** because of its sub-mm precision at short range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Depth-Sensing Methods

| Property | Stereo Camera | Structured Light | LIDAR (ToF) | ToF Camera | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $0.5$–$30$ m | $0.2$–$5$ m | $0.1$–$200$ m | $0.1$–$5$ m | $0.02$–$10$ m |
| **Accuracy** | mm – cm | mm (short) | cm | cm | cm |
| **Update Rate** | $30$–$120$ Hz | $30$–$60$ Hz | $5$–$20$ Hz (rotating) | $30$–$60$ Hz | $10$–$50$ Hz |
| **Cost** | Medium | Medium | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Outdoor Use** | Yes (with issues) | Poor (sunlight kills IR pattern) | Excellent | Poor | Good |
| **Output** | Dense 2.5-D | Dense 2.5-D | Sparse 2-D/3-D points | Dense 2.5-D | Sparse 1-D point |
| **Failure Mode** | Textureless walls | Sunlight, multi-path | Rain, fog, mirror surfaces | Multi-path interference | Specular surfaces, narrow beam |
| **Power** | Low | Low | High (laser) | Low | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
All node IDs are alphanumeric + prefixed; no reserved keywords; no markdown formatting inside labels.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Depth-Sensing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[sceneObject3D] --> B[emitterModule]
    B --> C[lightOrSoundPulse]
    C --> D[sceneSurface]
    D --> E[reflectedSignal]
    E --> F[receiverModule]
    F --> G[signalConditioning]
    G --> H[principleSelector]
    H --> I[triangulationPath]
    H --> J[timeOfFlightPath]
    H --> K[structuredLightPath]
    I --> L[disparityMap]
    J --> M[rangeProfile]
    K --> N[patternDecode]
    L --> O[depthMap]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[motionPlanner]
    P --> Q[motorCommands]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **scene object** reflects a **pulse or pattern** from the **emitter**.
- The **receiver** captures the returned signal.
- A **principle selector** (chosen at design time) routes the signal to one of three processing paths.
- Each path produces a different intermediate representation: **disparity map** (stereo), **range profile** (LIDAR), or **decoded pattern** (structured light).
- All three paths fuse into a common **depth map**, which feeds the **motion planner** that issues **motor commands** to the wheels.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stereo Rectification and Triangulation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CamL[leftCamera] --> GrabL[leftImageGrab]
    CamR[rightCamera] --> GrabR[rightImageGrab]
    GrabL --> RectL[rectificationLeft]
    GrabR --> RectR[rectificationRight]
    RectL --> Match[stereoMatcher]
    RectR --> Match
    Match --> Disp[disparityMap]
    Disp --> Tri[triangulationXYZ]
    Tri --> Cloud[pointCloudXYZRGB]
    Cloud --> Viz[rviz3DViewer]
    Disp --> Filt[medianFilter]
    Filt --> Obst[obstacleDetector]
    Obst --> Stop[motionStopCmd]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Two cameras produce two **rectified** images (epipolar lines aligned horizontally).
- The **stereo matcher** finds corresponding pixels and outputs a **disparity map**.
- The disparity map is the input to **triangulation**, which produces a colored **point cloud**.
- A **median filter** cleans noise; an **obstacle detector** decides whether to halt the robot.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Time-of-Flight LIDAR Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Trigger[controllerTrigger] --> Laser[laserDiodeEmits]
    Laser --> OutPulse[outgoingPhotonPulse]
    OutPulse --> Target[sceneTarget]
    Target --> BackPulse[backscatteredPhotonPulse]
    BackPulse --> APD[avalanchePhotodiode]
    APD --> TDC[timeToDigitalConverter]
    TDC --> DistCalc[rangeCalculatorEquation]
    DistCalc --> FilterOut[outlierRejectionMedian]
    FilterOut --> Scan[azimuthEncoder]
    Scan --> Cloud2D[rangeScan360deg]
    Cloud2D --> Grid[occupancyGridSLAM]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **controller** triggers the **laser diode** to emit a short IR pulse.
- The **APD (avalanche photodiode)** detects the faint returned photons.
- The **TDC (time-to-digital converter)** measures the time interval with picosecond resolution.
- The **range calculator** applies $Z = ct/2$.
- An **azimuth encoder** gives the angular position, producing a **2-D range scan**.
- The scan is fed into a **SLAM module** to build an **occupancy grid**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define depth measurement in mobile robotics. List any four depth-sensing methods and state the underlying physical principle of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Depth measurement is the process of determining the distance from a robot's onboard sensor to objects in its environment, producing a 2.5-D or 3-D spatial map essential for navigation and obstacle avoidance.

| Method | Physical Principle |
|---|---|
| Stereo Vision | Triangulation using two camera viewpoints (parallax) |
| Structured Light | Triangulation using a known projected IR pattern |
| LIDAR (ToF) | Round-trip time of a laser pulse, $Z = ct/2$ |
| Ultrasonic Sensor | Round-trip time of a sound pulse, $Z = v_s t/2$ |
| ToF Camera (PMD) | Phase shift of an amplitude-modulated IR wave |
| Laser Triangulation | Triangulation using a single camera + laser line |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for the table — partial credit for fewer entries.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**What is disparity in stereo vision? A stereo rig has focal length $f = 800$ pixels and baseline $B = 12$ cm. For a point with disparity $d = 40$ pixels, compute the depth $Z$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Disparity is the horizontal pixel difference between the projections of a 3-D point in the left and right images of a stereo pair, defined as $d = x_L - x_R$.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{40} = \frac{96}{40} = 2.4 \;\text{m}
$$

The object is **2.4 m** away from the camera.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** (or split as 5+5+4 in some question patterns). Students answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B** (internal choice).

---

### Question A — Stereo Vision and Triangulation

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks]
With the help of a neat diagram, derive the stereo depth equation $Z = fB/d$ using the pinhole camera model. Define disparity and state the assumption of rectified stereo geometry.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **[1 mark]** *Setup:* Two identical cameras $C_L$ and $C_R$ with parallel optical axes, separated by baseline $B$ along the world $X$-axis. Each has focal length $f$. A 3-D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to $p_L = (x_L, y_L)$ in the left image and $p_R = (x_R, y_R)$ in the right image.

2. **[1 mark]** *Pinhole projection:*
   $$
   x_L = \frac{fX}{Z}, \qquad x_R = \frac{f(X - B)}{Z}
   $$

3. **[1 mark]** *Disparity definition:* $d = x_L - x_R$ (horizontal pixel difference on rectified scanline).

4. **[2 marks]** *Algebraic derivation:*
   $$
   \begin{aligned}
   d &= \frac{fX}{Z} - \frac{f(X-B)}{Z} = \frac{fX - fX + fB}{Z} = \frac{fB}{Z}
   \end{aligned}
   $$
   Inverting: $Z = fB/d$.

5. **[1 mark]** *Rectified-stereo assumption:* Both camera optical axes are parallel; the two image planes are coplanar; corresponding epipolar lines coincide with horizontal scanlines so that $y_L = y_R$.

6. **[1 mark]** *Sketch:* Pinhole camera pair with parallel axes, baseline $B$, focal planes, point $P$ and image points $p_L$, $p_R$, plus the disparity $d$ marked on the image plane.

---

**Q. (b)** [7 Marks]
A mobile robot carries a stereo rig with focal length $f = 600$ pixels, baseline $B = 15$ cm, and a sub-pixel disparity estimator with uncertainty $\Delta d = 0.25$ pixels. For a target at $Z = 5$ m:
(i) compute the expected disparity;
(ii) compute the depth resolution $\Delta Z$;
(iii) if the required depth resolution is $\Delta Z \le 0.05$ m, by what factor must the baseline be increased (assuming $f$ and $\Delta d$ fixed)?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Disparity at $Z = 5$ m [2 marks]:**
$$
d = \frac{fB}{Z} = \frac{600 \times 0.15}{5} = \frac{90}{5} = 18 \;\text{pixels}
$$

**(ii) Depth resolution [2 marks]:**
$$
\Delta Z = \frac{Z^{2}}{fB} \cdot \Delta d = \frac{25}{600 \times 0.15} \times 0.25 = \frac{25}{90} \times 0.25 = 0.0694 \;\text{m} \approx 6.94 \;\text{cm}
$$

**(iii) Required baseline [3 marks]:**
We need $\Delta Z \le 0.05$ m:
$$
B_{new} \ge \frac{Z^{2} \cdot \Delta d}{f \cdot \Delta Z_{req}} = \frac{25 \times 0.25}{600 \times 0.05} = \frac{6.25}{30} \approx 0.2083 \;\text{m}
$$

The current baseline is $0.15$ m, so the required scaling factor is
$$
k = \frac{B_{new}}{B_{old}} = \frac{0.2083}{0.15} \approx 1.389
$$

**Answer:** Baseline must be increased by a factor of about **1.39** (i.e., from 15 cm to ≈ 20.8 cm). The new baseline satisfies the depth-resolution requirement.

---

### Question B — Time-of-Flight and Sensor Comparison

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** [7 Marks]
Explain the working of a pulsed Time-of-Flight (ToF) depth sensor. Derive the range equation $Z = ct/2$ and compute the round-trip time for a target at $Z = 30$ m. Mention two advantages and two limitations of LIDAR over stereo vision.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **[1 mark]** *Working:* A laser diode emits a short IR pulse (typically $5$–$10$ ns). The pulse travels to the target, scatters off the surface, and a fraction returns to the receiver. An avalanche photodiode (APD) detects the faint return, and a time-to-digital converter (TDC) measures the interval $t$ between emission and reception with picosecond resolution.

2. **[1 mark]** *Derivation:* Light travels at $c$ in air; total path $= 2Z$ (round trip); hence $c \cdot t = 2Z$, giving
   $$
   Z = \frac{c \, t}{2}
   $$

3. **[1 mark]** *Numerical:*
   $$
   t = \frac{2Z}{c} = \frac{2 \times 30}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{60}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{s} = 200 \;\text{ns}
   $$

4. **[2 marks]** *Advantages of LIDAR over stereo vision:*
   - Works in **darkness and low-texture scenes** (stereo fails on blank walls).
   - **Direct range measurement**, not dependent on image correspondence.
   - Long range ($> 100$ m) without needing wide baseline.
   - Robust to ambient lighting changes.

5. **[2 marks]** *Limitations of LIDAR:*
   - **Expensive** (mechanically scanned LIDARs cost thousands of dollars).
   - Generates only **sparse 2-D or 3-D points**, not a dense depth map.
   - Struggles in **rain, fog, and snow** (water droplets scatter IR).
   - **Eye-safety** regulations limit peak power in some bands.

---

**Q. (b)** [7 Marks]
(i) A continuous-wave ToF camera operates at a modulation frequency of $f_{mod} = 25$ MHz. Find the maximum unambiguous range $Z_{max}$.
(ii) An ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of $t = 8.7$ ms. If the speed of sound in air is $v_s = 343$ m/s, what is the target distance?
(iii) Compare structured-light depth sensors with stereo cameras in terms of accuracy, cost, range, and sunlight performance.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) CW ToF maximum range [2 marks]:**
$$
Z_{max} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 25 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{5 \times 10^{7}} = 6 \;\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Ultrasonic range [2 marks]:**
$$
Z = \frac{v_s \, t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 8.7 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.9841}{2} \approx 1.492 \;\text{m}
$$

**(iii) Comparison [3 marks]:**

| Aspect | Structured Light | Stereo Camera |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy at short range** | Very high (mm) — pre-calibrated projector | Moderate (cm), depends on matching |
| **Cost** | Medium (needs IR projector + IR camera + ASIC) | Low to medium (two commodity cameras) |
| **Range** | Short ($0.2$–$5$ m) | Medium ($0.5$–$30$ m) |
| **Sunlight performance** | Poor — IR pattern drowned by sunlight | Good — uses ambient visible light |
| **Textureless surfaces** | Excellent — pattern itself provides texture | Poor — matching fails on blank walls |
| **Outdoor use** | Limited (indoor dominant) | Suitable |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Depth-Measuring Questions**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $Z = fB/d$** — mixing focal length in mm with baseline in metres without converting pixel size. *Always write out the unit cancellation explicitly.*
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF** — the pulse travels *twice* the distance. Many students write $Z = c \cdot t$ instead of $Z = ct/2$. This costs **1 full mark** in the calculation step.
> 3. **Confusing disparity with depth** — disparity is in *pixels* (image space); depth is in *metres* (world space). They are inversely related.
> 4. **Not stating the rectified-stereo assumption** before deriving $Z = fB/d$. Examiners expect this in a 7-mark derivation question.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in derivation questions. A correctly labelled pinhole stereo diagram is worth at least **1 mark** by itself.
> 6. **Ignoring the depth-resolution equation** $\Delta Z = Z^{2} \Delta d / (fB)$ when asked "which sensor is better at long range?" The squared dependence on $Z$ is the answer's key insight.
> 7. **Mixing up LIDAR and ToF-camera terminology** — LIDAR is usually a *scanned point* sensor; ToF cameras give a *full depth image* per frame.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Measuring Depth" — Module 2**

- [ ] Depth measurement = recovering the **range $Z$** for points in the robot's field of view.
- [ ] The **two physical tricks** are **triangulation** and **time-of-flight**.
- [ ] **Pinhole projection:** $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$ — used to derive all stereo math.
- [ ] **Disparity:** $d = x_L - x_R$ (pixels), measured along rectified scanlines.
- [ ] **Stereo depth equation:** $Z = fB/d$ — the single most important formula in this module.
- [ ] **Depth resolution:** $\Delta Z = Z^{2}\Delta d / (fB)$ — degrades as $Z^{2}$, improves linearly with $B$ and $f$.
- [ ] **Rectified stereo assumption:** parallel optical axes + coplanar image planes ⇒ epipolar lines = horizontal scanlines.
- [ ] **Time-of-Flight pulsed:** $Z = ct/2$. Speed of light $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.
- [ ] **Time-of-Flight CW phase:** $Z = c\phi / (4\pi f_{mod})$, with $Z_{max} = c / (2 f_{mod})$.
- [ ] **Structured light** projects a known pattern (e.g., IR dot grid) and observes its deformation; ideal for indoor short-range.
- [ ] **Laser triangulation** uses one camera + one laser line offset by baseline $B$: $Z = fB/(x^{\prime} - x_0)$, sub-mm precision.
- [ ] **Ultrasonic:** $Z = v_s t / 2$, $v_s \approx 343$ m/s in air; wide beam, poor directionality, specular-reflection failure.
- [ ] **LIDAR** is the workhorse for outdoor mobile robots (autonomous cars, AMRs); works in dark, gives direct range, but is sparse and costly.
- [ ] **Baseline trade-off:** bigger $B$ improves depth resolution and maximum range, but increases the minimum usable range (objects closer than $Z_{min} \approx fB / d_{max}$ produce disparities larger than the image width).
- [ ] **Failure modes by sensor:** stereo → textureless walls; ToF → multi-path interference; structured light → sunlight; ultrasonic → specular surfaces; LIDAR → rain/fog/dust.
- [ ] **Dense vs sparse:** stereo and structured light give *dense* 2.5-D depth maps; LIDAR and ultrasonic give *sparse* 1-D or 2-D point clouds.
- [ ] For KTU numerical problems, always carry **3 significant figures** in intermediate steps and report the final answer to **2 decimal places** when the unit is metres.
- [ ] Remember to draw the **pinhole stereo diagram** in any derivation question — it is a guaranteed 1-mark gain.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Vision
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Active Vision

# 👁️ Active Vision in Mobile Robotics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Vision** is a paradigm in computer vision and mobile robotics where the visual sensor (camera) is treated as an **actively controllable agent** rather than a passive data collector. The system dynamically manipulates camera parameters — **pose (pan, tilt, roll)**, **intrinsic settings (focus, zoom, aperture, exposure)** and **viewing geometry (vergence, baseline)** — to optimize the acquisition of task-relevant visual information from the environment.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for PEBRT522, Active Vision sits at the intersection of three pillars:

1. **Perception** — extracting meaningful information from imagery.
2. **Control** — closing the loop between sensing and actuation.
3. **Cognition** — deciding *where*, *what*, and *when* to look next.

This paradigm was formally articulated by **Bajcsy (1988)** and **Aloimonos, Weiss & Bandyopadhyay (1988)**, who proved that many ill-posed passive vision problems become **well-posed** when the observer is allowed to move.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a vast, dark library searching for a specific red book.

- A **passive vision system** would be like a person tied to one spot, holding a fixed flashlight, struggling to spot the book because the lighting never changes.
- An **active vision system** is like a person free to walk around, tilt their head, squint, lean closer (zoom), shift their gaze, and refocus their eyes whenever they see a promising hint. They **actively change viewpoint and attention** to gather better clues.

In a mobile robot context, the "head" of the robot (with its cameras) can pan, tilt, and translate. By intelligently directing its gaze — just as humans do with **saccades**, **smooth pursuit**, and **vergence** — the robot overcomes limitations like occlusion, depth ambiguity, and motion blur.

### 🧠 Key Enabling Behaviours of an Active Vision System

- **Foveation:** Variable spatial resolution (high resolution at the gaze point, lower at periphery).
- **Vergence:** Independent rotation of the two eyes/cameras to fixate on the same 3D point.
- **Saccades:** Rapid, ballistic gaze shifts between fixation points.
- **Smooth Pursuit:** Continuous tracking of a moving object.
- **Gaze Stabilization:** Compensating for ego-motion using vestibular/IMU feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Active Vision in Module 2 is positioned as the bridge between *low-level image processing* (Module 1) and *high-level SLAM/decision making* (Module 3). Expect questions linking camera actuation to perceptual robustness.

### 🔑 Standard Metrics Used (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Field of View (FOV):** Typically **45°–90°** for standard cameras; **≥ 180°** for fisheye/panoramic rigs.
- **Frame Rate:** Standard **30 Hz**; high-speed active tracking up to **1000 Hz**.
- **Vergence Angle:** **5°–15°** for far-to-near fixation transitions.
- **Baseline (inter-ocular distance):** Human ≈ **6.5 cm**; stereo robot rigs **10–30 cm**.
- **Depth of Field:** Determined by aperture $f$-number $N = f/D$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Active vs. Passive Vision — A Structured Comparison

| **Aspect** | **Passive Vision** | **Active Vision** |
|---|---|---|
| Camera State | Static, fixed parameters | Dynamic, controllable parameters |
| Pose | Fixed during observation | Controllable (pan/tilt/translate) |
| Intrinsics | Fixed (focus, zoom, aperture) | Adjustable in real-time |
| Problem Well-Posedness | Often ill-posed | Often well-posed |
| Computational Load | Higher (recover all from one image) | Lower (data tailored to task) |
| Biological Analogy | Invertebrate compound eye | Human/vertebrate foveal vision |
| Typical Application | Surveillance, archival imaging | Mobile robots, autonomous vehicles |

## 2.2 The Three Operational Pillars of Active Vision

### 🔹 Pillar I — **Viewpoint Control (Gaze Control)**
The robot actively chooses *where* to look. This is governed by a **gaze control law**:

$$
\dot{q}_{\text{cam}} = K_p (q_{\text{desired}} - q_{\text{current}})
$$

where $q_{\text{cam}}$ is the joint configuration of the camera head, and $K_p$ is a proportional gain matrix.

### 🔹 Pillar II — **Sensor Parameter Adjustment**
The camera adjusts its **intrinsics** (focus $f$, zoom $z$, aperture $A$) based on scene demand:

- **Focus change** solves the **Depth-from-Focus (DFF)** problem.
- **Zoom change** controls the trade-off between field-of-view and resolution.
- **Aperture change** controls the depth-of-field for segmentation cues.

### 🔹 Pillar III — **Attention / Gaze Planning**
A high-level **saliency map** $S(x,y)$ is computed to decide the next fixation point:

$$
(x^*, y^*) = \arg\max_{(x,y)} S(x,y)
$$

Common saliency models use bottom-up features (color, intensity, orientation) and top-down task priors.

## 2.3 Mathematical Foundation — The Pinhole Projection

A 3D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)^T$ projects onto the image plane at $(u, v)$ as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \\[4pt]
v &= f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $f_x, f_y$ = focal lengths in pixel units (mm × pixels/mm),
- $c_x, c_y$ = principal point coordinates,
- $Z$ = depth of the point along the optical axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Active?** In the passive case, $Z$ is unknown and the equation is ill-posed (one equation, two unknowns). If the camera **actively translates by a known $\Delta Z$**, depth becomes directly recoverable via **structure-from-motion**.

## 2.4 The Image Jacobian (Interaction Matrix)

For visual servoing, the velocity of image features $\dot{s}$ is related to camera twist $\xi = (v, \omega)$ via:

$$
\dot{s} = L_s \cdot \xi
$$

where $L_s$ is the **image Jacobian** (also called the **interaction matrix**). For a single point feature $s = (u, v)$:

$$
L_s = \begin{bmatrix}
-\frac{f}{Z} & 0 & \frac{u}{Z} & \frac{uv}{f} & -\frac{f^2+u^2}{f} & v \\[4pt]
0 & -\frac{f}{Z} & \frac{v}{Z} & \frac{f^2+v^2}{f} & -\frac{uv}{f} & -u
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The depth $Z$ appears in the Jacobian. Active vision (e.g., stereo or active ranging) directly improves the conditioning of $L_s$, leading to faster convergence of the visual servoing controller.

## 2.5 Vergence Geometry for Stereo Active Vision

When two cameras with baseline $b$ verge (rotate) inward by angle $\theta_v$ to fixate on a common 3D point at depth $Z$:

$$
\theta_v = \arctan\!\left(\frac{b}{2Z}\right)
$$

The convergence (fixation) depth as a function of the vergence angle:

$$
Z_{\text{fix}} = \frac{b}{2 \tan \theta_v}
$$

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Pinhole Projection | $u = f X/Z + c_x$, $\ v = f Y/Z + c_y$ | $f$ [px], $X,Y,Z$ [m], $c$ [px] |
| Lens (Thin Lens) Equation | $\tfrac{1}{f} = \tfrac{1}{u_o} + \tfrac{1}{v_i}$ | $f$ [m], $u_o$ object dist., $v_i$ image dist. |
| Field of View | $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\tfrac{h}{2f}\right)$ | $h$ sensor height, $f$ focal length |
| Depth from Stereo (Disparity) | $Z = \tfrac{f \cdot b}{d}$ | $b$ baseline, $d$ disparity [px] |
| Vergence Angle | $\theta_v = \arctan\!\left(\tfrac{b}{2Z}\right)$ | $b$ baseline, $Z$ depth |
| Image Jacobian for Point | $\dot{s} = L_s \xi$ | $L_s \in \mathbb{R}^{2\times 6}$, $\xi \in \mathbb{R}^6$ |
| Visual Servo Control Law | $\xi = -\lambda \, L_s^{+} (s - s^*)$ | $\lambda$ gain, $L_s^{+}$ pseudo-inverse |
| Optical Flow (Lucas–Kanade) | $I_x v_x + I_y v_y + I_t = 0$ | $I_x, I_y$ spatial grads, $I_t$ temporal grad |
| Ego-motion Optical Flow | $\omega_x = \tfrac{v_x}{Z}$ (approx.) | $\omega$ angular vel., $v$ pixel vel. |
| Saliency (Itti-Koch) | $S = w_1 C + w_2 O + w_3 I$ | $C$ color, $O$ orientation, $I$ intensity |

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., Waymo):** Active camera heads tilt down before parking, up before highway merging.
- **Aerial Drones:** Gimbal-stabilized cameras actively cancel UAV vibration (gaze stabilization).
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Endoscopic cameras actively zoom on the suturing region.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Mast-mounted cameras pan/tilt to follow terrain of interest — a textbook active vision system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Depth Recovery via Active Camera Translation (Structure-from-Motion in Active Vision)

**Setup:** A single camera translates by a known baseline $b$ along the optical axis $Z$. Two images of a 3D point $P$ are captured at $t_1$ and $t_2$.

**Step 1:** At time $t_1$, the projection gives:

$$
u_1 = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}
$$

**Step 2:** After translation, the depth changes to $Z - b$, so:

$$
u_2 = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z - b}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide the two equations to eliminate $X$:

$$
\frac{u_2}{u_1} = \frac{Z}{Z - b}
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply:

$$
u_2 (Z - b) = u_1 Z
$$

**Step 5:** Expand:

$$
u_2 Z - u_2 b = u_1 Z
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate $Z$:

$$
Z (u_2 - u_1) = u_2 b
$$

**Step 7:** Final closed-form expression:

$$
\boxed{\,Z = \frac{u_2 \cdot b}{u_2 - u_1}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The denominator $u_2 - u_1$ is the **disparity**. When the active camera translation $b$ is known precisely, depth is recovered linearly. This is the active-vision analog of stereo disparity.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Vergence Geometry for Stereo Active Head

**Setup:** Two cameras separated by baseline $b$ rotate inward by angle $\theta_v$ to fixate on point $P$ at depth $Z$.

**Step 1:** Consider the right eye. The optical axis makes angle $\theta_v$ with the forward direction. The projection of $P$ onto the forward axis has length $Z$, and the perpendicular offset is $b/2$.

**Step 2:** Form the right triangle:

$$
\tan \theta_v = \frac{b/2}{Z}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $Z$:

$$
\boxed{\,Z = \frac{b}{2 \tan \theta_v}\,}
$$

**Step 4:** For a vergence-controlled active head, define **vergence error** as:

$$
e_v = \theta_{v,\text{desired}} - \theta_{v,\text{measured}}
$$

**Step 5:** A simple proportional control law drives vergence to zero:

$$
\dot{\theta}_v = K_v \cdot e_v
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting the depth expression, the **controller maps pixel disparity $d$ directly to motor commands**:

$$
\dot{\theta}_v = K_v \cdot \left[ \arctan\!\left(\frac{b}{2 Z_d}\right) - \arctan\!\left(\frac{b}{2 Z_m}\right) \right]
$$

where $Z_d$ is desired depth and $Z_m$ is the current (measured) depth.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS) Control Law

**Goal:** Move the camera so that the current image features $s$ converge to the desired features $s^*$.

**Step 1:** Define the feature error:

$$
e(t) = s(t) - s^{*}
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. time:

$$
\dot{e} = \dot{s} = L_s \, \xi
$$

**Step 3:** Apply an exponentially decaying error dynamics:

$$
\dot{e} = -\lambda \, e
$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $\dot{e}$:

$$
L_s \, \xi = -\lambda \, e
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for camera twist $\xi$ using the pseudo-inverse $L_s^{+}$ (assuming $L_s$ has full row rank):

$$
\xi = -\lambda \, L_s^{+} \, e
$$

**Step 6:** When $L_s$ is square ($6 \times 6$ for 3 points), the pseudo-inverse is a regular inverse:

$$
\boxed{\,\xi = -\lambda \, L_s^{-1} \, (s - s^{*})\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU Note:** In mobile robots, IBVS is often used with **4 points (8 features)** to over-constrain the system and reject noise via least squares.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Depth from Focus (Active Vision Using Lens Adjustment)

**Setup:** A camera with adjustable focus lens captures images at several focus distances $f_i$. The **Tenengrad** focus measure $T(f_i)$ peaks when the lens is focused on the object.

**Step 1:** Tenengrad focus measure:

$$
T(f_i) = \sum_{(x,y) \in \Omega} \left[ I_x(x,y)^2 + I_y(x,y)^2 \right]
$$

where $I_x, I_y$ are Sobel gradients and $\Omega$ is a small neighborhood.

**Step 2:** Lens equation relates focus setting $f_i$ to object distance $u_o$:

$$
\frac{1}{f_i} = \frac{1}{u_o} + \frac{1}{v_i}
$$

**Step 3:** As the active system sweeps $f_i$ and records $T(f_i)$, the maximum occurs at:

$$
f_i^{*} = \arg\max_{f_i} T(f_i)
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $f_i^{*}$ into the lens equation to recover depth $u_o$:

$$
\boxed{\,u_o = \left( \frac{1}{f_i^{*}} - \frac{1}{v_i} \right)^{-1}\,}
$$

**Step 5:** Search optimization: Use **Fibonacci search** or **Hill climbing** on $T(f_i)$ for faster convergence than brute-force scanning.

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Active Strategy?

| **Scenario** | **Recommended Active Strategy** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Object tracking at high speed | Smooth pursuit + predictive saccades | Compensates for object motion |
| Depth ambiguity in clutter | Active camera translation (SfM) | Solves scale ambiguity |
| Distant object inspection | Active zoom + focus stacking | Boosts resolution |
| Binocular fixated point | Vergence + stereo fusion | Solves correspondence at fixation |
| Unknown object search | Top-down saliency + saccades | Directed exploration |
| Self-motion in cluttered space | Gaze stabilization (gyro feedforward) | Maintains stable retinal image |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of an Active Vision System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERCEPTION["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        IMGCAP[Image Capture]
        SAL[S Saliency Map Computation]
        FEAT[Feature Extraction]
        DEPTH[Depth Estimation]
    end

    subgraph COGNITION["COGNITION LAYER"]
        DECIDE[Task Driven Attention Selector]
        PLAN[Gaze Planner]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL LAYER"]
        GAZECTRL[Vergence Controller]
        SERVO[Visual Servo Controller]
        FOCUS[Focus and Zoom Controller]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        PAN[PAN Motor]
        TILT[TILT Motor]
        LENSM[Lens Motor]
        TRANS[Mobile Base Drive]
    end

    IMGCAP --> SAL
    IMGCAP --> FEAT
    IMGCAP --> DEPTH

    SAL --> DECIDE
    FEAT --> DECIDE
    DEPTH --> DECIDE

    DECIDE --> PLAN
    PLAN --> GAZECTRL
    PLAN --> SERVO
    PLAN --> FOCUS

    GAZECTRL --> PAN
    GAZECTRL --> TILT
    SERVO --> TRANS
    FOCUS --> LENSM

    PAN --> IMGCAP
    TILT --> IMGCAP
    LENSM --> IMGCAP
    TRANS --> IMGCAP
```

## 4.2 The Active Perception Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SCENE[World Scene] --> SENS[Sensor and Camera]
    SENS --> PROC[Perception Module]
    PROC --> DEC[Decision and Saliency]
    DEC --> ACT[Actuation Command]
    ACT --> CAM[Cameras Pan Tilt Zoom Translate]
    CAM --> SENS
    DEC -.task prior.-> DEC
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Passive vs Active Vision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PAS["PASSIVE VISION PIPELINE"]
        P1[Fixed Camera] --> P2[Single Image Capture]
        P2 --> P3[Recover Geometry Optics and Motion]
        P3 --> P4[Often Ill Posed Output]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTIVE VISION PIPELINE"]
        A1[Controllable Camera] --> A2[Multiple Viewpoints and Settings]
        A2 --> A3[Selective Information Acquisition]
        A3 --> A4[Well Posed Task Driven Output]
        A1 --> A2
    end
```

## 4.4 Stereo Active Vergence Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L["LEFT EYE"]
        LOPT[Left Optical Axis]
        LP[Left Camera Lens]
    end

    subgraph R["RIGHT EYE"]
        ROPT[Right Optical Axis]
        RP[Right Camera Lens]
    end

    FIX[Fixation Point P at Depth Z]
    BASE[Baseline b between cameras]

    LOPT --> FIX
    ROPT --> FIX
    LP --- BASE
    RP --- BASE
    BASE -->|verges inward| FIX
```

## 4.5 Module Mapping — Where Active Vision Sits in Mobile Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Module 1 Image Processing Basics]
    M2[Module 2 Active Vision]
    M3[Module 3 SLAM and Navigation]
    M4[Module 4 Decision and Planning]

    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M2 -.informs.-> M3
    M2 -.feeds saliency.-> M4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Every Mermaid block above renders as a self-contained architecture. The *closed loop* in Section 4.2 is the **defining signature** of active vision — perception continuously drives actuation, which reshapes perception.
