## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Discrete Search Space Construction Methods

The construction of a discrete search space is canonically classified into **four families** that are mandatory in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

#### 2.1.1 Cell Decomposition Methods

The free space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is partitioned into a finite set of non-overlapping cells.

- **Exact Cell Decomposition:** The decomposition boundaries align with obstacle boundaries, producing convex cells. An adjacency graph $G_{adj}$ connects cells sharing a common boundary edge.
- **Approximate (Grid-Based) Cell Decomposition:** The workspace is overlaid with a uniform grid of resolution $r$. Each cell is labelled *free* or *occupied* by a ray-casting or occupancy-grid test.

**Step-by-step Construction Logic:**

1. Define workspace bounds: $x \in [x_{min}, x_{max}]$, $y \in [y_{min}, y_{max}]$.
2. Choose grid resolution $r$ (meters per cell).
3. Number of cells: $N_x = \lceil (x_{max}-x_{min})/r \rceil$, $N_y = \lceil (y_{max}-y_{min})/r \rceil$.
4. For each cell, perform collision check by sampling the robot's footprint at the cell center and corners.
5. Connect 4- or 8-neighbour free cells by edges weighted with traversal cost.

#### 2.1.2 Skeletonization (Roadmap) Methods

The free space is reduced to a 1-D network of curves. Three canonical roadmaps:

- **Visibility Graph:** Nodes = start, goal, and all obstacle vertices. Edges connect mutually visible nodes. Edge weights are Euclidean distances. Optimal for finding shortest Euclidean path in a polygonal C-space, but paths hug obstacles (vulnerable to localization error).
- **Voronoi Diagram:** Set of points equidistant to the nearest two obstacles. Produces the *maximum clearance* path, ideal when localization noise is high, but the resulting path is generally longer.
- **Silhouette Method:** Generalized roadmap for high-dimensional C-spaces; edges trace the boundary of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

#### 2.1.3 Sampling-Based Methods

Random or deterministic samples are drawn from $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ and connected to form a probabilistic roadmap (PRM) or an exploring/exploring random tree (RRT). These dominate high-dimensional planning (e.g., robotic arms) where exact methods explode combinatorially.

#### 2.1.4 Potential Field Methods (Continuous, but discretized for evaluation)

A scalar potential $U(q)$ is constructed such that the robot is attracted to the goal and repelled by obstacles. Gradient descent is performed, but discretization is needed for stability and discretization of the force vector field.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **canonical KTU 2024 reference** for Module 3 path-planning formulas. Memorize each entry — questions are routinely derived directly from these.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Configuration tuple | $q = (x, y, \theta)$ | $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ rad |
| 2 | Free space | $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Set difference |
| 3 | Grid resolution vs. cells | $N = \lceil L_x/r \rceil \cdot \lceil L_y/r \rceil$ | cells, $r$ in m |
| 4 | Manhattan distance | $d_M = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ | 4-connected grid |
| 5 | Euclidean distance | $d_E = \sqrt{(x_1-x_2)^2 + (y_1-y_2)^2}$ | 8-connected grid |
| 6 | Chebyshev distance | $d_C = \max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert, \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert)$ | King-move on grid |
| 7 | A* cost function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g$: cost-so-far, $h$: heuristic |
| 8 | Admissible heuristic | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | $h^*$ = true cost to goal |
| 9 | Consistent heuristic | $h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$ | Triangle inequality |
| 10 | Dijkstra's optimality | $f(n) = g(n)$ | A* with $h \equiv 0$ |
| 11 | Visibility-graph edge weight | $w_{ij} = d_E(v_i, v_j)$ | Polygon-vertex pair |
| 12 | Voronoi clearance | $d_{vor}(p) = \min_i d(p, \partial O_i)$ | Distance to nearest obstacle |
| 13 | Cell-decomposition convexity | $C_i$ convex $\Leftrightarrow$ every pair $p,q \in C_i$ has segment $\overline{pq} \subset C_i$ | Definition |
| 14 | Potential field force | $F(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$ | Newton units |
| 15 | Attractive potential | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\xi \, d^2(q, q_{goal})$ | Quadratic, $\xi > 0$ |
| 16 | Repulsive potential | $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$ | $\eta > 0$, $\rho_0$ influence radius |

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of Discrete Search Space Construction:**
> - **Warehouse robotics (Amazon Kiva, GreyOrange):** Grid decomposition over a known warehouse map, A* search under the hood.
> - **Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla FSD):** Lanelet/roadmap structures are essentially roadmaps on a discretized high-definition map.
> - **Drone swarm planning:** Visibility graphs on 2-D obstacle polygons augmented with altitude layers.
> - **Last-mile delivery robots:** Voronoi diagrams maximize clearance from pedestrians and curbs.
> - **Underwater AUVs:** Sampling-based PRMs handle the high-dimensional 6-DOF pose of the vehicle in cluttered 3-D seabed maps.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: A* Optimality from Admissibility and Consistency

We will prove that when the heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** and **consistent**, A* returns an optimal path on any finite discrete graph $G(V,E)$ with non-negative edge weights $c(n, n')$.

**Definitions:**

$$g(n) \;=\; \text{lowest known cost from start } s \text{ to node } n$$

$$h(n) \;=\; \text{estimated cost from } n \text{ to goal } t$$

$$f(n) \;=\; g(n) + h(n)$$

**Claim 1 (Admissibility).** If $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n \in V$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true optimal cost from $n$ to $t$, then A* expands a node $n$ on an optimal path before terminating.

*Proof by expansion sequence argument:*

Let $n^*$ be the first node on an optimal path that A* expands. At any point during search, the OPEN list contains frontier nodes. For every node $n$ on OPEN:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \le g(n) + h^*(n)$$

For the goal node $t$ on the optimal path, when the algorithm terminates via goal expansion, we have $f(t) = g(t) = C^*$ (the optimal cost). For any unexpanded node $n$ on OPEN, $f(n) \le C^*$. Since $h(n) \le h^*(n)$, the algorithm never returns a path more expensive than $C^*$. $\blacksquare$

**Claim 2 (Consistency).** A heuristic is consistent if for every edge $(n, n')$:

$$h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$$

This implies that $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path:

$$\begin{aligned}
f(n') &= g(n') + h(n') \\
      &= g(n) + c(n,n') + h(n') \\
      &\ge g(n) + h(n) \;=\; f(n)
\end{aligned}$$

**Why both are required for KTU-level analysis:**

- *Admissibility alone* guarantees optimality but not that $f$ along a path is monotonic — re-expansions may occur.
- *Consistency* (also called monotonicity) ensures that once a node is expanded (CLOSED), its $g$-value is final, reducing the search to **O(V)** expansions in the worst case.

### 3.2 Derivation: Grid Resolution vs. Path Length Error

Given a uniform grid of resolution $r$, what is the maximum deviation of the A*-computed path from the true shortest path in continuous free space?

**Setup:** Consider a free straight corridor of width $W$ and length $L$. The grid discretization forces a staircase approximation. The true length is $L$; the grid-path length is:

$$L_{grid} \;=\; (N_x \cdot r) \cdot \sec(\alpha)$$

where $N_x = L/r$ cells horizontally and $\alpha$ is the staircase tilt. The excess length is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= L_{grid} - L \\
         &= \frac{L}{r} \cdot r \cdot \sec(\alpha) - L \\
         &= L(\sec(\alpha) - 1)
\end{aligned}$$

For 8-connected grids, the worst case tilt is $\alpha = 45°$, so $\sec(45°) = \sqrt{2}$:

$$\Delta L_{max} \;=\; L(\sqrt{2} - 1) \;\approx\; 0.4142 \, L$$

This is the **isotropy error** of an 8-connected grid, and is the reason weighted A* or interpolation techniques are used in production.

### 3.3 Derivation: Voronoi Edge Equation for Two Point Obstacles

For two point obstacles $O_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $O_2 = (x_2, y_2)$, the Voronoi edge is the locus of points equidistant from both:

$$d(p, O_1) \;=\; d(p, O_2)$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 &= (x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 \\
x^2 - 2x x_1 + x_1^2 + y^2 - 2y y_1 + y_1^2 &= x^2 - 2x x_2 + x_2^2 + y^2 - 2y y_2 + y_2^2 \\
-2x x_1 + x_1^2 - 2y y_1 + y_1^2 &= -2x x_2 + x_2^2 - 2y y_2 + y_2^2 \\
2x(x_2 - x_1) + 2y(y_2 - y_1) &= x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\
x(x_2 - x_1) + y(y_2 - y_1) &= \tfrac{1}{2}(x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** — the perpendicular bisector of segment $\overline{O_1 O_2}$. For polygonal obstacles, Voronoi edges become line segments and parabolic arcs.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Choosing the right discrete search space method is a recurring KTU 14-mark question.** Use this comparison matrix in your answer to score full marks.

| Method | Path Optimality | Clearance from Obstacles | Computational Cost | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Visibility Graph | Shortest Euclidean path | Low (hugs obstacles) | $O(n^2)$ to build, $n$ = vertices | Indoor, sparse polygons |
| Voronoi Diagram | Sub-optimal but safe | Maximum | $O(n \log n)$ | Noisy localization |
| Grid + A* | Resolution-dependent | Configurable via cost | $O(V \log V)$ | Warehouse, occupancy grids |
| Exact Cell Decomposition | Optimal in cell sense | Cell-boundary based | $O(n \log n)$ | Known polygonal maps |
| PRM (sampling) | Probabilistically optimal | Statistical | $O(n \log n)$ | High-DOF arms |
| RRT (sampling) | Sub-optimal, fast | Configurable | $O(n \log n)$ | Real-time, dynamic env. |
| Potential Field | Local minima possible | Smooth | $O(1)$ per step | Reactive obstacle avoidance |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Full Path Planning Pipeline

The end-to-end pipeline that takes a raw map and produces a discrete search space is a **block-level functional architecture flow**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Workspace W] --> B[Sensor Data and Prior Map]
    B --> C[Map Representation Module]
    C --> D{Map Type Decision}
    D -- Metric Grid --> E[Occupancy Grid Construction]
    D -- Polygonal --> F[Polygon Map Construction]
    D -- Topological --> G[Topological Map Construction]
    E --> H[Cell Labelling Free or Occupied]
    F --> I[Vertex Extraction from Polygons]
    G --> J[Place Recognition Node Detection]
    H --> K[Graph Builder]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Discrete Search Space G of V and E]
    L --> M[Edge Weight Assignment]
    M --> N[A Star or Dijkstra Search]
    N --> O[Continuous Path Reconstruction]
    O --> P[Executable Trajectory for Robot]
    P --> Q[Feedback from Localization]
    Q --> C
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Visibility Graph Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify all obstacle vertices V] --> T[Add Start Vertex S to V]
    T --> U[Add Goal Vertex G to V]
    U --> V[For each pair of vertices v_i and v_j]
    V --> W{Is edge v_i v_j collision free and within sensor range}
    W -- Yes --> X[Add edge v_i v_j to E]
    W -- No --> Y[Skip edge]
    X --> Z[More vertex pairs]
    Y --> Z
    Z -- Yes --> V
    Z -- No --> AA[Output Visibility Graph G]
    AA --> AB[Apply Dijkstra or A Star to find shortest path]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Configuration Space Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workspace Obstacle O in W] --> B[Minkowski Sum with Robot Footprint]
    B --> C[C space Obstacle Cobs]
    D[Robot Reference Point q in W] --> E[Configuration q in C space]
    C --> F[Free Configuration Space Cfree]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Discretization via Grid or Sampling]
    G --> H[Discrete Search Graph G of V and E]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discrete Search Space Construction] --> B[Combinatorial Methods]
    A --> C[Sampling Based Methods]
    A --> D[Grid Based Methods]
    A --> E[Roadmap Methods]
    B --> B1[Exact Cell Decomposition]
    B --> B2[Skel etonization]
    C --> C1[Probabilistic Roadmap PRM]
    C --> C2[Rapidly Exploring Random Tree RRT]
    D --> D1[Uniform Grid 4 or 8 connected]
    D --> D2[Adaptive Grid]
    E --> E1[Visibility Graph]
    E --> E2[Voronoi Diagram]
    E --> E3[Silhouette Method]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024] Q1. Define the configuration space of a mobile robot and explain the significance of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ in path planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ of a planar mobile robot is the set of all possible robot poses $q = (x, y, \theta)$ where $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$. The **C-space obstacles** $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ are the sets of configurations where the robot collides with workspace obstacles. The **free configuration space** is defined as $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$. Its significance is that path planning reduces to finding a continuous curve $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ with $\tau(0) = q_{start}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{goal}$. Operating in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ guarantees collision avoidance, which is the central constraint of every planning algorithm. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Q2. Differentiate between a Visibility Graph and a Voronoi Diagram as discrete search space methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Visibility Graph** connects the start, goal, and all obstacle vertices using straight-line edges that do not intersect any obstacle interior. It produces the **shortest Euclidean path** but the path lies arbitrarily close to obstacle boundaries, making it sensitive to localization error. A **Voronoi Diagram** connects points equidistant to two or more obstacles, producing a **maximum-clearance path** that is robust to localization noise but typically longer. Visibility graphs are best for short, optimal paths in polygonal environments; Voronoi diagrams are best when safety margin and robustness dominate over path length. (3 marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction of an **occupancy grid** as a discrete search space for a differential-drive mobile robot. Derive the relationship between grid resolution $r$ and the resulting isotropy error in an 8-connected grid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 6×6 grid (origin at top-left, rows increasing downward, columns rightward) with the following occupancy map — `0 = free`, `1 = obstacle` — find the **shortest path** from $S = (1,1)$ to $G = (5,5)$ using **A* search** with the **Manhattan heuristic**. Show the OPEN/CLOSED expansions step by step. **(7 marks)**

| | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 | C6 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| R1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** | 0 | 0 |
| R2 | 0 | **1** | 0 | **1** | 0 | 0 |
| R3 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| R4 | 0 | **1** | **1** | **1** | 0 | 0 |
| R5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| R6 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** | 0 |

Where $S$ sits at (R1, C1) and $G$ at (R6, C6). Diagonal moves are **not** allowed.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A:**

**(a) Part (a) – 7 marks:**

An **occupancy grid** partitions the 2-D workspace into a uniform array of square cells of side $r$. Each cell stores a probabilistic value $P(m \mid z_{1:t})$ representing the probability of occupancy given all sensor measurements up to time $t$.

**Construction Steps:**

1. **Define extent:** Choose $x \in [x_{min}, x_{max}]$, $y \in [y_{min}, y_{max}]$.
2. **Discretize:** Set cell size $r$. Number of cells:
$$N_x = \left\lceil \frac{x_{max} - x_{min}}{r} \right\rceil, \quad N_y = \left\lceil \frac{y_{max} - y_{min}}{r} \right\rceil$$
3. **Initialize:** All cells to $P = 0.5$ (unknown).
4. **Sensor update:** For each measurement $z_t$ (e.g., a lidar ray), use the inverse sensor model:
$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}) = \left[1 + \frac{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t)}{P(m_i \mid z_t)} \cdot \frac{1 - P(m_i)}{P(m_i)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})} \right]^{-1}$$
5. **Threshold:** Cells with $P > 0.65$ are marked occupied; $P < 0.35$ are free; in-between is unknown and conservatively treated as occupied.
6. **Graph build:** Each free cell becomes a node. Connect to 4-neighbours (or 8-neighbours) with edge weight equal to traversal cost (1 for cardinal, $\sqrt{2}$ for diagonal).

**Isotropy error derivation** (already in Section 3.2, restated):

For an 8-connected grid, the path must be approximated as horizontal/vertical or 45°-tilted segments. The worst case is when true path is at 45° to axes:

$$\Delta L_{max} = L \cdot (\sec 45° - 1) = L(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.4142 L$$

To reduce this, either use a finer grid (halving $r$ roughly halves $\Delta L$) or use weighted A* with diagonal costs exactly $\sqrt{2}$.

**(b) Part (b) – 7 marks:**

A* on 4-connected grid with **Manhattan heuristic** $h(n) = \vert x - x_G \vert + \vert y - y_G \vert$ (using R/C indices). Note: rows increase downward, columns rightward. Diagonals are forbidden, so we move R±1, C±1 only.

| 23 | (1,6) f=10 | (6,4,8,2,10), (5,5,8,2,10) | + (1,6) |
| 24 | (6,4) f=10 | (5,5,8,2,10) | + (6,4) |
| 25 | (5,5) f=10 | (5,6,9,1,10), (6,5,9,1,10) | + (5,5) |
| 26 | (5,6) f=10 | (6,5,9,1,10) | + (5,6) |
| 27 | (6,5) f=10 | (6,6,10,0,10) | + (6,5) |
| 28 | (6,6) **GOAL** | — | + (6,6) |

**Path Reconstruction** (parent pointers):

$$S(1,1) \to (1,2) \to (2,2) \to (2,3) \to (3,3) \to (3,4) \to (3,5) \to (3,6) \to (4,6) \to (5,6) \to (6,6) = G$$

**Path length = 10 cells** (matches $h(S) = 10$, confirming optimality).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the construction of a **Visibility Graph** as a discrete search space. For a workspace with two convex polygonal obstacles having a total of 8 vertices (combined) plus a start and goal, state the number of nodes $N$ in the graph and the maximum number of edges before the visibility test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Voronoi Diagram** approach to discrete search space construction. For two point obstacles at $O_1 = (0, 0)$ and $O_2 = (6, 0)$, derive the equation of the Voronoi edge and identify the point on it that is **farthest** from the line segment $\overline{O_1 O_2}$. State its clearance from each obstacle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Q.B:**

**(a) Part (a) – 7 marks:**

**Construction of Visibility Graph:**

1. **Node set $V$** = $\{q_{start}\} \cup \{q_{goal}\} \cup \{\text{all reflex and convex vertices of all obstacles}\}$.
2. **Edge set $E$:** For every pair $(v_i, v_j)$ with $v_i \ne v_j$, draw a straight line segment.
3. **Visibility test:** The edge is retained in $E$ **if and only if** the entire open segment $\overline{v_i v_j}$ lies entirely in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ (i.e., it does not pass through the interior of any obstacle).
4. **Edge weights:** Euclidean distance $w_{ij} = d_E(v_i, v_j)$.
5. **Search:** Apply Dijkstra or A* on the resulting graph.

**Node and edge count:**

- Total nodes: $N = 2 + 8 = 10$ (start + goal + 8 obstacle vertices).
- Total possible edges before visibility test: $\binom{N}{2} = \binom{10}{2} = 45$.
- After visibility test, the actual edge count $|E|$ is generally much smaller than 45 because most inter-vertex segments are blocked by obstacle interiors.

**Why visibility graphs give shortest Euclidean path:**

Any shortest Euclidean path in a polygonal $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is a polygonal path whose vertices are either $q_{start}$, $q_{goal}$, or obstacle vertices (this is the classical result from the "rubber-band" taut-string argument). Hence the shortest path must be a path in the visibility graph, and Dijkstra's algorithm on the visibility graph with Euclidean weights provably returns the global optimum.

**(b) Part (b) – 7 marks:**

**Voronoi Diagram Construction:**

The Voronoi diagram of a set of obstacle sites $S = \{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n\}$ is the partition of the plane into cells $V_i = \{p \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid d(p, O_i) \le d(p, O_j) \; \forall j\}$. The **Voronoi edge** is the boundary between two adjacent cells, and consists of points equidistant to the two nearest obstacles.

For obstacle boundaries that are line segments, Voronoi edges consist of straight-line segments and parabolic arcs.

**Derivation for two point obstacles** $O_1 = (0,0)$ and $O_2 = (6,0)$:

Setting $d(p, O_1) = d(p, O_2)$:

$$(x-0)^2 + (y-0)^2 = (x-6)^2 + (y-0)^2$$

$$x^2 + y^2 = x^2 - 12x + 36 + y^2$$

$$0 = -12x + 36 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 3$$

So the Voronoi edge is the vertical line $x = 3$, defined for all $y \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Farthest point from $\overline{O_1 O_2}$:** The segment $\overline{O_1 O_2}$ lies on the $x$-axis from $x=0$ to $x=6$. The point on the Voronoi edge $x=3$ that is farthest from this segment is at $y = \pm \infty$ in the unbounded case, but within any bounded workspace, the farthest point is the point $(3, y_{max})$ or $(3, y_{min})$ depending on bounds.

If the workspace is bounded with $y \in [0, 4]$, then the farthest point is $(3, 4)$ (or $(3, 0)$, but the latter coincides with $\overline{O_1 O_2}$).

**Clearance from each obstacle** at $(3, 4)$:

$$d((3,4), O_1) = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ units}$$

$$d((3,4), O_2) = \sqrt{(3-6)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5 \text{ units}$$

This confirms equidistance — by construction of the Voronoi edge. **Maximum clearance = 5 units** (using the classic 3-4-5 right triangle).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing $\theta$ into the cost:** For a holonomic robot, $\theta$ is irrelevant to grid cost. For non-holonomic, students often wrongly add $\theta$ into the heuristic, breaking admissibility.
> 2. **Forgetting admissibility check:** Many students write a heuristic like Euclidean distance divided by 2 and call it admissible — but only the unnormalized Euclidean is admissible on a 4-connected grid. Always verify $h(n) \le h^*(n)$.
> 3. **Visibility graph optimality caveat:** Visibility graphs give the *shortest Euclidean path*, **not** the *smoothest* or *kinodynamically feasible* path. State this explicitly or lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Voronoi obstacle-shape confusion:** The Voronoi diagram of *point* obstacles gives straight perpendicular bisectors. The Voronoi diagram of *polygonal* obstacles gives line segments **and parabolic arcs** (where the nearest point on an obstacle is in its interior). Examiners test for this distinction.
> 5. **A* tie-breaking:** Ties on $f$-value can lead to different paths of the same cost; examiners may deduct marks if you don't mention tie-breaking criteria.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Constructing a Discrete Search Space**

- The **configuration space** is $\mathcal{C}$; **free space** is $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$; the planning problem is to find a continuous curve inside $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
- **Four pillars** of discrete search space construction: (i) **Cell decomposition** (exact & grid), (ii) **Roadmap** (visibility, Voronoi, silhouette), (iii) **Sampling-based** (PRM, RRT), (iv) **Potential field**.
- A **Visibility Graph** uses obstacle vertices as nodes and collision-free straight-line edges; produces the **shortest Euclidean path**; classical complexity $O(n^2 \log n)$ to build.
- A **Voronoi Diagram** uses the locus of points equidistant to two obstacles; produces the **maximum clearance path**; complexity $O(n \log n)$ via Fortune's sweep.
- **Occupancy grids** discretize the workspace into uniform cells of resolution $r$; 8-connected grids suffer up to $(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 41.4\%$ isotropy error; remedy with weighted diagonal costs or finer $r$.
- **A\* heuristic conditions:** *Admissible* ⇒ $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ (optimality); *Consistent* ⇒ $h(n) \le c(n,n') + h(n')$ (monotonicity, no re-expansion).
- **Heuristic metrics:** Manhattan for 4-connected grids; Euclidean for 8-connected; Chebyshev for king-move; **always divide by max edge cost** to keep admissible.
- **Potential field** $F = -\nabla U_{att} - \nabla U_{rep}$; attractive is quadratic in distance to goal, repulsive is inverse-distance squared within influence radius $\rho_0$.
- **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM):** Sample $n$ points in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, connect to $k$ nearest neighbours; complexity $O(n \log n)$ with a KD-tree.
- **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT):** Incrementally builds a tree biased toward unexplored regions via random sampling; biased growth toward goal gives RRT*.
- **Discretization trade-off:** Finer resolution ⇒ more accurate path, more memory, slower search. Use **adaptive grids** or **multi-resolution** in production systems.
- **Algorithm pick-rule (exam answer):** Visibility graph → shortest path in static polygonal map. Voronoi → noisy localization. Grid + A* → general known map. PRM → high-DOF. RRT → real-time / kinodynamic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retraction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Retraction Methods # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Retraction (Topological Definition):** A *retraction* is a continuous mapping $\mathbf{r}: \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ from the free configuration space of the robot onto a lower-dimensional subset $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, such that $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}$. The subset $\mathcal{X}$ is called a *retract* of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. In **Mobile Robotics** and specifically in **roadmap-based path planning**, retraction is the mathematical operation of *projecting* every point in the robot's free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ onto a one-dimensional network (the **Generalized Voronoi Diagram / GVD**). The result is a skeleton-like roadmap that: - Encodes maximum clearance from obstacles - Preserves topological connectivity of the free space - Reduces the 2D planning problem to 1D graph search The standard metric used for retraction is the **Euclidean distance to the nearest obstacle boundary**, denoted by the distance function: $$d(\mathbf{x}) = \min_{q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}} \lVert \mathbf{x} - q \rVert$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Hiker and the River Network"** > Imagine a hiker lost in a forest with many circular lakes. The hiker needs a safe path. Notice that the **shoreline points exactly halfway between two lakes** are the *safest* points — they maximize the distance to any obstacle. If we connect all these "midway points" together, they form a **river-like network** that threads between all the lakes. This network is the **Voronoi Diagram**. A *retraction* is the act of pulling the entire forest map (free space) onto this river network — every tree and clearing gets projected onto the nearest point on the river. After retraction, navigation becomes as simple as walking along the river. **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used:** - Standard clearance metric: **Euclidean distance** $d(\mathbf{x})$ - Default grid resolution for implementation: **1 pixel = 1 cm** (or $\Delta = 0.01$ m) - Default safety threshold: **robot radius + safety margin** = $r_{\text{robot}} + \delta$ ## 1.3 Why Retraction? The Engineering Motivation | Problem Without Retraction | Solution Provided by Retraction | |---|---| | Path planning in 2D continuous space is expensive | Problem reduces to 1D graph search | | Many narrow passages cause failure | Retract to GVD avoids narrow regions | | Direct A* on grid is memory-intensive | Roadmap compresses free space | | No clearance guarantees | GVD guarantees **max-clearance** paths | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Retraction methods belong to the family of *roadmap path planners* (introduced by Takahashi and Schilling, 1989). They are essential for the **Generalized Voronoi Diagram (GVD)** approach and the **Brushfire Algorithm** — both expected in KTU Module 3 outcomes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Retraction Pipeline — Structured Logic The complete retraction methodology consists of **four sequential stages**: ### Stage 1 — Distance Transform (Brushfire Algorithm) - Initialize grid with obstacle cells marked. - Propagate a wavefront outward; assign to each cell the **distance to the nearest obstacle**. - Result: scalar field $d(\mathbf{x}) = \lVert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{obs}} \rVert_2$. ### Stage 2 — Compute the Gradient Field - Compute $\nabla d(\mathbf{x}) = \left( \dfrac{\partial d}{\partial x},\ \dfrac{\partial d}{\partial y} \right)$. - This gradient points in the direction of **steepest ascent in clearance** (away from obstacles). ### Stage 3 — Retraction (Projection onto GVD) - At every point $\mathbf{x}$, follow the negative gradient $-\nabla d$ until a **local maximum** of $d$ is reached. - The set of all such local maxima forms the **Generalized Voronoi Diagram (GVD)**. - The retraction map is: $$\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \int_0^{T} -\nabla d(\mathbf{x}(s))\ ds$$ where $T$ is the time when $\|\nabla d(\mathbf{x}(T))\| = 0$ (ridge point reached). ### Stage 4 — Roadmap Construction - Extract nodes at **GVD junctions** (points with 3+ GVD branches). - Extract edges by tracing the ridges. - Connect **start** and **goal** to the nearest GVD points via gradient ascent. - Apply **A\*** or **Dijkstra** on the resulting graph. ## 2.2 The Generalized Voronoi Diagram (GVD) — Mathematical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (GVD):** Given a set of obstacles $O = \{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n\}$ in a 2D workspace, the GVD is the locus of all points in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ that are **equidistant from at least two distinct obstacle boundaries** and strictly farther from all others: > $$\mathcal{V} = \left\{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \;\Big|\; \exists\, i \neq j:\ d(\mathbf{x}, O_i) = d(\mathbf{x}, O_j) \leq d(\mathbf{x}, O_k)\ \forall k \right\}$$ For polygonal obstacles with $n$ total edges, the GVD consists of: - **Voronoi edges** — parabolic arcs (between two line segments) and straight-line segments (between two points). - **Voronoi vertices** — junctions of $\geq 3$ edges. ## 2.3 The Brushfire Algorithm (Wavefront Distance Transform) The brushfire algorithm builds the distance field $d(\mathbf{x})$ using a **multi-source BFS on the grid**: 1. **Initialize:** All obstacle cells → distance $= 0$, push into queue $\mathcal{Q}$. 2. **Pop** cell $c$ from $\mathcal{Q}$. For each 4-neighbor $n$: - If $n$ is unvisited: set $d(n) = d(c) + 1$, push $n$ into $\mathcal{Q}$. 3. **Repeat** until $\mathcal{Q}$ is empty. 4. Result: a discrete distance map $d: \mathbb{Z}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$. **Variants:** - **4-connectivity brushfire** (Manhattan distance) — faster but less accurate. - **8-connectivity brushfire** (Chebyshev distance) — slightly better. - **Exact Euclidean brushfire** — uses $\sqrt{dx^2+dy^2}$ for propagation, more expensive. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **The following table contains all KTU-exam-ready formulas. Memorize before going to the exam hall.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Distance to obstacle | $d(\mathbf{x}) = \min_{q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}} \lVert \mathbf{x} - q \rVert_2$ | meters (m) | | 2 | Gradient of distance field | $\nabla d = \left(\partial d / \partial x,\ \partial d / \partial y \right)$ | unitless direction | | 3 | Retraction ODE | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(s) = -\nabla d(\mathbf{x}(s))$ | flow toward ridge | | 4 | GVD condition | $d(\mathbf{x}, O_i) = d(\mathbf{x}, O_j) \leq d(\mathbf{x}, O_k)$ | $\forall i \neq j$ | | 5 | Clearance along GVD | $d_{\text{clear}}(\mathbf{x}) = d(\mathbf{x})\ \big\vert_{\mathbf{x}\in\mathcal{V}}$ | max-clearance path | | 6 | Brushfire update | $d(n) = d(c) + 1$ | integer grid units | | 7 | Path length (GVD) | $L = \int_{\gamma \subset \mathcal{V}} ds$ | path cost for A* | | 8 | Connectivity condition | $\mathcal{V}$ is connected iff $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is connected | topological | | 9 | H-signature (Voronoi edge) | Parabola: $\lVert \mathbf{x} - f_1 \rVert^2 = \lVert \mathbf{x} - f_2 \rVert^2$ | $f_1, f_2$ are foci | | 10 | Number of Voronoi edges (upper bound) | $E_{\max} = \mathcal{O}(n)$ | $n$ = total obstacle edges | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Retraction Methods Retraction methods are deployed in real-world applications including: - **Autonomous vehicles** — lane selection on highways (GVD over dynamic obstacles). - **Warehouse AGVs** — KIVA/Symbley robots using max-clearance aisles. - **Surgical robots** — da Vinci system retraction to avoid tissue damage. - **Planetary rovers** — Mars Exploration Rovers used GVD for hazard avoidance. - **Underwater ROVs** — AUV navigation around coral reefs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Gradient Ascent Converges to the GVD **Claim:** For any $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, the flow $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \nabla d(\mathbf{x})$ converges asymptotically to the GVD. **Proof (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** Step 1: Consider the **continuous distance function** $d: \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$. By definition, $d$ is **1-Lipschitz**: $$\vert d(\mathbf{x}_1) - d(\mathbf{x}_2) \vert \leq \lVert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2 \rVert_2$$ This means $d$ is differentiable almost everywhere (Rademacher's theorem) and the gradient is bounded: $$\lVert \nabla d(\mathbf{x}) \rVert_2 \leq 1$$ Step 2: Define the candidate Lyapunov function: $$V(\mathbf{x}) = d(\mathbf{x})$$ We want to show $V$ is non-decreasing along the flow $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \nabla d(\mathbf{x})$. Step 3: Compute the time derivative of $V$ along trajectories: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \nabla d(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \nabla d(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \nabla d(\mathbf{x}) = \lVert \nabla d(\mathbf{x}) \rVert_2^2$$ Step 4: By Cauchy–Schwarz and the Lipschitz condition: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \lVert \nabla d(\mathbf{x}) \rVert_2^2 \geq 0$$ with equality if and only if $\nabla d(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$ (a critical point of $d$). Step 5: Critical points of $d$ occur precisely when: $$\nabla d(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{V}$$ This is because $\nabla d = 0$ implies the point has **locally maximum distance from the nearest obstacle**, i.e., it is equidistant from at least two obstacle boundaries — the GVD definition. Step 6: By LaSalle's Invariance Principle, every trajectory converges to the largest invariant set contained in $\{\mathbf{x} \mid \dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = 0\} = \mathcal{V}$. Therefore, $\mathbf{x}(t) \to \mathbf{x}^* \in \mathcal{V}$ as $t \to \infty$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation II: Parabolic Voronoi Edge Between Two Line Segments **Setup:** Let $S_1$ and $S_2$ be two non-parallel line segments with endpoints defining foci $f_1, f_2$ of an equidistant parabola. **Step-by-step derivation:** Step 1: A point $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)$ lies on the bisector iff: $$\lVert \mathbf{x} - f_1 \rVert_2 = \lVert \mathbf{x} - f_2 \rVert_2$$ Step 2: Square both sides: $$(x - f_{1x})^2 + (y - f_{1y})^2 = (x - f_{2x})^2 + (y - f_{2y})^2$$ Step 3: Expand: $$x^2 - 2xf_{1x} + f_{1x}^2 + y^2 - 2yf_{1y} + f_{1y}^2 = x^2 - 2xf_{2x} + f_{2x}^2 + y^2 - 2yf_{2y} + f_{2y}^2$$ Step 4: Cancel $x^2$ and $y^2$: $$- 2xf_{1x} + f_{1x}^2 - 2yf_{1y} + f_{1y}^2 = - 2xf_{2x} + f_{2x}^2 - 2yf_{2y} + f_{2y}^2$$ Step 5: Group: $$2x(f_{2x} - f_{1x}) + 2y(f_{2y} - f_{1y}) = f_{2x}^2 + f_{2y}^2 - f_{1x}^2 - f_{1y}^2$$ Step 6: Recognize this as a **linear equation** in $(x, y)$ — a straight line (the perpendicular bisector of $f_1 f_2$). However, when the closest points on the obstacle boundaries are **interior points of line segments** (not endpoints), the bisector is a **parabolic arc** whose equation in rotated coordinates is: $$u = \frac{(v - v_0)^2}{2p} + u_0$$ where $p$ is the focal parameter. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Derivation III: Brushfire Algorithm Complexity **Claim:** Brushfire on a $N \times N$ grid runs in $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ time. **Derivation:** Step 1: Total cells in grid: $N^2$. Step 2: Each cell is **enqueued exactly once** (when first discovered). Step 3: Each cell is **dequeued exactly once**. Step 4: For each dequeue, we examine at most 4 neighbors (4-connectivity) or 8 (8-connectivity) — a **constant** number independent of $N$. Step 5: Total operations: $$T(N) = N^2 \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(N^2)$$ Step 6: Memory complexity: $N^2$ cells × constant storage per cell = $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Retraction vs. Other Roadmap Methods | Property | Retraction (GVD) | Visibility Graph | Cell Decomposition | RRT | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Optimality** | Max-clearance | Shortest | Depends on cells | Probabilistic | | **Completeness** | Resolution-complete | Resolution-complete | Complete | Probabilistically complete | | **Narrow passages** | Avoids them | Goes through | May need fine cells | Struggles | | **Computational cost** | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ brushfire | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | | **Smoothness of path** | Smooth (gradient flow) | Piecewise-linear | Piecewise-linear | Not smooth | | **Dynamic env.** | Needs recompute | Needs recompute | Needs recompute | Replans easily | | **Memory** | High ($N^2$ grid) | Low | Medium | Low | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Retraction Pipeline The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the entire retraction-based path planning architecture as required by KTU Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Workspace Input
Polygon Obstacles] --> B[Grid Discretization
Resolution delta] B --> C[Brushfire Algorithm
BFS Wavefront] C --> D[Distance Field
d x Map] D --> E[Gradient Computation
nabla d] E --> F[Retraction Flow
Follow Gradient] F --> G[GVD Extraction
Ridge Detection] G --> H[Roadmap Graph
Nodes and Edges] H --> I[Connect Start
and Goal] I --> J[Graph Search
A Star Algorithm] J --> K[Shortest Max Clearance
Path Output] D --> L[Local Maxima
Detection] L --> G H --> M[Path Smoothing
Optional] M --> K ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: GVD with Two Circular Obstacles ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph WorkspaceMap[Free Space W] O1((Obstacle 1)) O2((Obstacle 2)) O3((Obstacle 3)) V1((Voronoi
Vertex 1)) V2((Voronoi
Vertex 2)) V3((Voronoi
Vertex 3)) E1([Voronoi Edge
Arc 1-2]) E2([Voronoi Edge
Arc 1-3]) E3([Voronoi Edge
Arc 2-3]) S([Start]) --> V1 V1 --- E1 E1 --- V2 V2 --- E2 E2 --- V3 V3 --- E3 E3 --- V1 V2 --> G([Goal]) end O1 -.equidistant.-> E1 O2 -.equidistant.-> E1 O2 -.equidistant.-> E3 O3 -.equidistant.-> E3 ``` ## 4.3 Brushfire Propagation State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unvisited Unvisited --> Obstacle : Initialize
distance = 0 Obstacle --> Frontier : Push to Queue Frontier --> Propagated : Assign
distance = d plus 1 Propagated --> Propagated : Neighbor visited
with d plus 1 Propagated --> Converged : Queue empty Converged --> [*] : Distance Field Ready ``` ## 4.4 Retraction as Topological Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CF[Free Space C free - 2D] P1[Point p1]:::retracted P2[Point p2]:::retracted P3[Point p3]:::retracted end subgraph GVD[GVD - 1D Skeleton] V1[Voronoi Point v1]:::onroad V2[Voronoi Point v2]:::onroad V3[Voronoi Point v3]:::onroad end P1 -->|r p1| V1 P2 -->|r p2| V2 P3 -->|r p3| V3 classDef retracted fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef onroad fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q1.** Define the *Generalized Voronoi Diagram (GVD)* in the context of mobile robot path planning. State its key property. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The GVD $\mathcal{V}$ is the locus of points in the free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ that are equidistant from at least two distinct obstacle boundaries, with this common distance being the **minimum** distance to any obstacle. Formally: $$\mathcal{V} = \left\{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \;\big|\; \exists\, i \neq j,\ d(\mathbf{x}, O_i) = d(\mathbf{x}, O_j) \leq d(\mathbf{x}, O_k)\ \forall k \right\}$$ **Key property:** The GVD provides the path of **maximum clearance** from obstacles, making it the safest trajectory for navigation. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for property.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q2.** What is *retraction* in topology-based path planning? Give a one-line expression for the retraction map. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *retraction* is a continuous map $\mathbf{r}: \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \to \mathcal{X}$ that projects every point in the free space onto a lower-dimensional subset $\mathcal{X}$ (the retract) such that $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}$. The standard expression is: $$\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}(T),\quad \text{where } \mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{x},\ \dot{\mathbf{x}}(s) = \nabla d(\mathbf{x}(s))$$ Here $T$ is the time at which $\mathbf{x}$ reaches the GVD ridge. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for retraction condition, 1 for map expression.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Paper 2]** ### Option A — Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete retraction-based path planning algorithm** for a mobile robot navigating in a 2D polygonal environment. Clearly state the role of the brushfire algorithm. **[7 marks]** **(b)** For the workspace shown below (two circular obstacles of radius $r_1 = 2$ m at center $(2, 3)$ and $r_2 = 3$ m at center $(8, 3)$, with a square obstacle of side 2 m at $(5, 7)$), determine the **equation of the Voronoi edge** between the two circular obstacles. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The retraction-based path planning algorithm consists of the following stages: 1. **Workspace discretization** *(1 mark)*: Partition the workspace into a uniform grid of resolution $\delta$ (typically 0.01 m to 0.1 m). 2. **Brushfire distance transform** *(2 marks)*: Initialize obstacle cells with distance $0$ and push them into a FIFO queue. Iteratively propagate the wavefront using BFS, assigning each visited cell a distance value: $$d(n) = d(c) + 1$$ This produces the distance field $d(\mathbf{x})$ for all free cells. 3. **Gradient ascent and retraction** *(2 marks)*: For every point $\mathbf{x}$, follow the gradient $\nabla d$ until convergence to a critical point. The set of all critical points forms the GVD $\mathcal{V}$. The retraction map is: $$\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \int_0^T \nabla d(\mathbf{x}(s))\, ds$$ 4. **Roadmap construction and A\* search** *(2 marks)*: Extract Voronoi vertices (junctions) and edges (ridges). Connect start and goal to the nearest GVD points. Apply A* with edge cost equal to path length. The result is the **maximum-clearance** path. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Identify foci of the parabola. *(1 mark)* — The two foci are the obstacle centers: $f_1 = (2, 3)$ and $f_2 = (8, 3)$. The closest boundary points to any point on the bisector are the obstacle surfaces; for the bisector running **vertically between the two circular obstacles**, the relevant focus-to-boundary distance is the radius, but the **bisector line** between the centers is: $$x_{\text{bisector}} = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2} = \frac{2 + 8}{2} = 5$$ **Step 2:** Since the two circular obstacles have **equal closest-point geometry** along the perpendicular bisector, the Voronoi edge is the **vertical line** $x = 5$ for $y$ values that remain equidistant and inside free space. *(2 marks)* **Step 3:** Verify the distance from any point $(5, y)$ on the bisector to both circles: *(2 marks)* - Distance to circle 1 boundary: $\sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (y-3)^2} - r_1 = \sqrt{9 + (y-3)^2} - 2$ - Distance to circle 2 boundary: $\sqrt{(5-8)^2 + (y-3)^2} - r_2 = \sqrt{9 + (y-3)^2} - 3$ These are **not equal** (since $r_1 \neq r_2$), so the bisector is a **parabolic arc**, not a straight line. **Step 4:** Equate distances and solve: *(2 marks)* $$\sqrt{9 + (y-3)^2} - 2 = \sqrt{9 + (y-3)^2} - 3$$ $$-2 = -3 \quad \text{(impossible)}$$ The bisector is the locus where the **distance to the boundary** of both circles is equal, accounting for the radius. Setting: $$d_1 = \sqrt{(x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2} - 2 = d_2 = \sqrt{(x-8)^2 + (y-3)^2} - 3$$ $$\sqrt{(x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2} - \sqrt{(x-8)^2 + (y-3)^2} = -1$$ Squaring and simplifying (or using the focus-directrix property), the locus is a **hyperbola-like arc** with foci at the obstacle centers and difference of distances $= r_2 - r_1 = 1$ m. > The exact equation is: $\dfrac{(x-5)^2}{0.25} - \dfrac{(y-3)^2}{8.75} = 1$ (a hyperbola branch), restricted to the free space. --- ### Option B — Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** Describe the **Brushfire Algorithm** with a flowchart. Explain why it runs in $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ time for an $N \times N$ grid. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A point robot is in a $10 \times 10$ grid with obstacles at cells $(3, 3)$, $(3, 4)$, $(4, 3)$, $(6, 6)$, $(6, 7)$, $(7, 6)$. Apply the **4-connectivity brushfire** algorithm to compute the distance field $d(\mathbf{x})$ and identify the **GVD ridge cells**. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Brushfire Algorithm Description:** *(4 marks)* 1. Initialize a grid array `dist[N][N] = INFINITY`. Mark all obstacle cells with `dist = 0` and push them into queue `Q`. 2. While `Q` is not empty: - Dequeue cell `c` at position $(i, j)$. - For each of the 4 neighbors $(i\pm 1, j)$ and $(i, j\pm 1)$ that is free and unvisited: - Set `dist[neighbor] = dist[c] + 1`. - Mark neighbor as visited; enqueue neighbor. 3. Return the populated `dist` array. **Time Complexity Analysis:** *(3 marks)* - **Cells processed:** Each of the $N^2$ cells is enqueued and dequeued exactly once. → $2N^2$ queue operations. - **Neighbor checks:** Each dequeue triggers at most 4 neighbor checks. → $4N^2$ neighbor operations. - **Total operations:** $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$. - **Space complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ for the distance array and queue. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Initialize the 10×10 grid. Mark obstacles: - Block A: $(3,3), (3,4), (4,3)$ (L-shape) → distance 0 - Block B: $(6,6), (6,7), (7,6)$ (L-shape) → distance 0 **Step 2:** First BFS layer (distance 1): All 4-neighbors of obstacle cells (excluding obstacles themselves). *(1 mark)* **Step 3:** Continue BFS layers 2, 3, ... until the queue is empty. The distance field yields a discrete map where values increase as we move away from obstacles. *(3 marks)* **Step 4:** GVD ridges are cells where the distance function has a **local maximum along at least one direction** — i.e., cells that are equidistant from at least two obstacle cells/blocks. Practically, GVD cells are identified by finding cells where the distance field has **at least two "tied" nearest obstacle cells**. In a discrete grid, these appear as **ridges in the contour plot of $d$** between the two obstacle clusters. *(3 marks)* The ridge runs approximately along the diagonal between Block A (around (3,3)) and Block B (around (6,6)) — the cell $(4.5, 4.5)$ region, discretely identifiable as cells like $(4, 4), (5, 5), (5, 4), (4, 5)$ with $d \geq 2$ and equidistant from both blocks. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Confusing *GVD* (equidistant from **boundaries**) with the *Voronoi Diagram of points* (equidistant from **point sites**). These are **different** diagrams! > - **Common Mistake #2 (–2 marks):** Treating the brushfire distance as **Euclidean** when 4-connectivity produces **Manhattan** distance. Always state which metric you are using. > - **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to project start/goal onto the GVD before applying A*. The retraction must be applied to **both endpoints** of the query. > - **Common Mistake #4 (–1 mark):** Writing $\nabla d$ without clarifying the sign — retraction flows along $+\nabla d$ (ascent), not $-\nabla d$ (descent). > - **Mark Distribution Tip:** A 14-mark question typically has (a) and (b) at 7 marks each. Sub-parts of each get 3-4 marks for formulas/diagrams and 3-4 marks for derivation/computation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Retraction Methods** - **Definition of Retraction:** Continuous map $\mathbf{r}: \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \to \mathcal{X}$ projecting free space onto a 1D skeleton (GVD). - **GVD Equation:** Points equidistant from at least two obstacle boundaries — $\mathcal{V} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \mid d(\mathbf{x}, O_i) = d(\mathbf{x}, O_j) \leq d(\mathbf{x}, O_k),\ \exists\, i \neq j\}$. - **Brushfire Algorithm:** BFS-based distance transform; complexity $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ on $N \times N$ grid; produces integer Manhattan distance (4-conn) or Chebyshev (8-conn). - **Retraction ODE:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \nabla d(\mathbf{x})$; converges to GVD by LaSalle's Invariance Principle (Lyapunov function $V = d$). - **GVD Properties:** (1) Max-clearance paths; (2) Connected iff $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is connected; (3) Avoids narrow passages; (4) Upper bound on edges: $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for $n$ obstacle edges. - **Voronoi Edges:** Straight lines (point-point or line-point foci) or parabolic arcs (line-line foci). - **Pipeline Order:** Grid → Brushfire → Distance Field → Gradient → Retraction → GVD → Graph → A* → Path. - **Comparison:** Retraction ≠ Visibility Graph. Retraction = max-clearance; Visibility = shortest path. - **Clearance Metric:** $d_{\text{clear}}(\mathbf{x}) = d(\mathbf{x})\vert_{\mathbf{x}\in\mathcal{V}}$ — the **safety margin** of the GVD path. - **Key Inventor:** Takahashi & Schilling (1989) — credited with the GVD retraction method for mobile robots. - **Limitations:** (1) Expensive in dynamic environments; (2) Brittle for thin obstacles; (3) Suboptimal path length (sacrificed for clearance). - **Real-World Deployments:** Mars rovers, KIVA warehouse robots, da Vinci surgical systems, autonomous highway lane planners. - **KTU Mark Allocation:** Module 3 typically gives 14 marks to retraction + GVD combined; expect 7 marks for algorithm explanation, 7 marks for GVD computation on a small grid.

topics.title: Searching a Discrete State Space: Graph Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Searching a Discrete State Space: Graph Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In mobile robotics and artificial intelligence, **Searching a Discrete State Space using Graph Search** refers to a systematic algorithmic procedure used to navigate through a finite, discretized representation of a robot's possible configurations in order to find a feasible path from an **initial state** to a **goal state**. The state space is mathematically abstracted as a graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ denotes the set of **vertices (nodes)** representing discrete robot configurations, and $E$ denotes the set of **edges** representing valid transitions between configurations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *state* encapsulates all information necessary to uniquely describe the robot's configuration (position, orientation, velocity, etc.). A *graph search algorithm* explores this state space to determine a sequence of actions (edges) leading to a goal configuration.

### 1.2 Components of a Graph Search Problem

A discrete state-space search problem is formally defined by a **5-tuple** $(S, A, s_0, G, c)$ where:

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| $S$ | Finite set of discrete states (vertices of the graph) |
| $A(s)$ | Set of allowable actions/transitions from state $s$ |
| $s_0 \in S$ | Initial/Start state |
| $G \subseteq S$ | Set of goal states |
| $c(s, a)$ | Cost of applying action $a$ in state $s$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are inside a **maze** in a video game. The maze is divided into discrete square cells (states). From any cell, you can move UP, DOWN, LEFT, or RIGHT (actions), but only if there is no wall (constraints). You start at the entrance ($s_0$) and want to reach the exit ($G$). A *graph search algorithm* is your GPS — it systematically explores the maze either by carefully checking every cell layer-by-layer (**BFS**), by going deep into one path until stuck (**DFS**), or by always moving toward what *looks* closest to the exit (**Greedy/A***). Each cell you visit is a **node**, and each move is an **edge**.

In mobile robotics, this "maze" is replaced by a discretized **occupancy grid** or **roadmap** of the robot's environment, where the robot's position, orientation, and dynamics are encoded into the nodes.

### 1.4 Why Graph Search is Critical in Mobile Robotics

- **Path Planning:** Determines collision-free trajectories from a robot's current pose to a target pose.
- **Localization:** Searches over belief states to identify the robot's most likely position.
- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** Graph-based SLAM treats poses as nodes and relative measurements as edges.
- **Decision Making:** Behavior trees and finite state machines underlying robot autonomy rely on graph traversal.

### 1.5 Types of Search Strategies (High-Level Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Broad Families (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
> 1. **Uninformed (Blind) Search:** No information about the goal beyond its definition. Strategies: BFS, DFS, Uniform-Cost Search, Iterative Deepening DFS.
> 2. **Informed (Heuristic) Search:** Uses an *estimate* of distance to the goal to guide exploration. Strategies: Greedy Best-First Search, A* Search, IDA*, D*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Graph Search Workflow — Operational Logic

Every graph search algorithm follows a unified operational skeleton, differing only in how it selects the *next* node to expand:

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
- Create an empty data structure (queue, stack, or priority queue) called the **Open List** (or *Frontier*).
- Insert the start node $s_0$ into the Open List.
- Initialize a **Closed List** (visited set) as empty.
- Initialize a **Parent Map** $\pi: S \rightarrow S$ such that $\pi(s)$ stores the predecessor of $s$ on the best-known path.

**Step 2 — Selection (Frontier Selection Rule):**
- Remove a node $n$ from the Open List.
  - **FIFO Queue → Breadth-First Search**
  - **LIFO Stack → Depth-First Search**
  - **Priority Queue ordered by $g(n)$ → Uniform-Cost Search**
  - **Priority Queue ordered by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ → A\* Search**

**Step 3 — Goal Test:**
- If $n \in G$, then **terminate** and reconstruct the path by following $\pi$ backward from $n$ to $s_0$.

**Step 4 — Expansion:**
- For every action $a \in A(n)$, generate a successor $n'$.
- If $n'$ is *not* in the Closed List, compute its path cost $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a)$ and insert it into the Open List with parent $\pi(n') = n$.

**Step 5 — Loop:**
- If the Open List is empty, declare **failure** (no path exists). Otherwise, return to Step 2.

### 2.2 Search Tree vs. Search Graph

> [!NOTE]
> A *search tree* enumerates every possible path from $s_0$ (a node can reappear at different depths). A *search graph* keeps only the cheapest known path to each node (a node appears only once). All practical KTU-level algorithms use the **graph-search** variant to avoid redundant work.

### 2.3 Uninformed Search Strategies — Comparison

| Algorithm | Frontier (Data Structure) | Selection Rule | Complete? | Optimal? | Time $O(\cdot)$ | Space $O(\cdot)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BFS** | FIFO Queue | Lowest $g$ (shallowest) | Yes* | Yes** | $b^{d}$ | $b^{d}$ |
| **DFS** | LIFO Stack | Most recent | No | No | $b^{m}$ | $b^{m}$ |
| **UCS** | Priority Queue (by $g$) | Lowest $g(n)$ | Yes* | Yes | $\vert V\vert^{2}$ or $E \log V$ | $\vert V\vert$ |
| **IDDFS** | Iterative DFS | Depth limit $= 0,1,2,\ldots$ | Yes* | Yes** | $b^{d}$ | $b \cdot d$ |

\* Complete only if $b$ is finite and costs are positive.  
\** Optimal for uniform step costs.  
Where: $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = max depth of search tree.

### 2.4 Informed (Heuristic) Search — Core Theory

Informed search introduces a **heuristic function** $h(n)$ that estimates the cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal.

#### 2.4.1 The A* Search Algorithm (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

A* evaluates each node using the **evaluation function**:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = exact cost from $s_0$ to $n$ along the best known path
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of cost from $n$ to the goal
- $f(n)$ = estimated total cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$

#### 2.4.2 Heuristic Properties — Admissibility and Consistency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility:** A heuristic $h$ is *admissible* if it **never overestimates** the true cost to the goal: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true minimum cost.
>
> **Consistency (Monotonicity):** A heuristic $h$ is *consistent* if for every edge $(n, n')$ with cost $c(n, n')$, the triangle inequality holds: $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$.
> If $h$ is consistent, then $h$ is automatically admissible.

If $h$ is admissible, **A\* is optimal** (returns a minimum-cost path). If $h$ is consistent, A\* is also **efficiently optimal** (no node is re-expanded).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Evaluation function (A\*) | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | cost units |
| 2 | Manhattan distance (grid heuristic) | $h_{\text{manh}}(n) = \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$ | grid cells; admissible for 4-connectivity |
| 3 | Euclidean distance | $h_{\text{eucl}}(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_G)^2 + (y_n - y_G)^2}$ | continuous; admissible for $\geq 4$-conn. |
| 4 | Octile distance (8-connected grids) | $h_{\text{oct}}(n) = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | admissible for 8-conn. with diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}$ |
| 5 | BFS time/space complexity | $O(b^{d})$ | exponential in depth |
| 6 | UCS optimality condition | Step costs $\geq \epsilon > 0$ | guarantees finite expansion |
| 7 | A\* optimality condition | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ (admissible) | required for guaranteed optimality |
| 8 | Effective branching factor | $b^{*} = (N+1)^{1/d}$ | empirical measure of heuristic quality; $b^{*}\approx 1$ is ideal |
| 9 | Weighted A\* (suboptimal variant) | $f(n) = g(n) + \varepsilon \cdot h(n), \quad \varepsilon > 1$ | trades optimality for speed |
| 10 | Greedy Best-First | $f(n) = h(n)$ | not optimal, but fast |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A* powers path planning in **ROS Navigation Stack** (`navfn`, `global_planner`), **Google Maps** road routing, **Mars Rover** autonomy (Curiosity/Perseverance use Field D*), and video-game NPC navigation. Choosing a strong admissible heuristic can reduce the effective branching factor $b^{*}$ from $\sim 30$ (blind search) to $\sim 1.2$ (well-designed heuristic) — a **25× speedup** in real-world deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Octile Distance Heuristic (8-Connected Grid)

The octile distance is the canonical admissible heuristic for an 8-connected grid in which cardinal moves cost **1** and diagonal moves cost **$\sqrt{2}$**.

**Step 1 — Define the coordinate deltas:**

$$\Delta x = \vert x_n - x_G \vert, \quad \Delta y = \vert y_n - y_G \vert$$

These are non-negative integers representing how many cells separate $n$ from $G$ in the $x$ and $y$ axes.

**Step 2 — Recognize that diagonal movement reduces both $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ by 1 simultaneously:**

If we make $k = \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ diagonal moves, the remaining Manhattan gap becomes $\max(\Delta x, \Delta y) - k$ in one axis (since the other is now zero).

**Step 3 — Express total cost as a sum of $k$ diagonal moves and the residual cardinal moves:**

$$h_{\text{oct}}(n) = k \cdot \sqrt{2} + \left(\max(\Delta x, \Delta y) - k\right) \cdot 1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $k = \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ and simplify:**

$$h_{\text{oct}}(n) = \sqrt{2} \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y) - \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $\min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ using the identity $\max - \min = \vert \Delta x - \Delta y \vert$:**

$$h_{\text{oct}}(n) = \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) \cdot \sqrt{2} + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y) - \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic reorganization to expose the closed form:**

$$h_{\text{oct}}(n) = \left(\sqrt{2} - 1\right) \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$$

This is the final admissible octile heuristic.

### 3.2 Derivation: Why Admissibility Guarantees A\* Optimality

We prove that if $h$ is admissible, A\* returns an optimal solution.

**Step 1 — Define the optimal goal $G_{\text{opt}}$ with $f(G_{\text{opt}}) = g(G_{\text{opt}}) = C^{*}$, where $C^{*}$ is the true optimal cost.**

**Step 2 — Suppose, for contradiction, that A\* terminates at a non-optimal goal $G_2$ with $f(G_2) = g(G_2) = C_2 > C^{*}$.**

**Step 3 — At the moment of termination, some optimal-path node $n$ (on a path to $G_{\text{opt}}$) is still on the Open List with:**

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + h^{*}(n) \quad \text{(admissibility)}$$

**Step 4 — Since $n$ lies on a path to $G_{\text{opt}}$, we have $g(n) + h^{*}(n) \leq g(G_{\text{opt}}) = C^{*}$. Combining:**

$$f(n) \leq C^{*} < C_2 = f(G_2)$$

**Step 5 — But A\* selects the Open List node with the *minimum* $f$-value. Since $f(n) < f(G_2)$, the algorithm would have expanded $n$ before terminating at $G_2$. Contradiction. ∎**

### 3.3 Effective Branching Factor — Analytical Computation

The **effective branching factor** $b^{*}$ is the empirical measure of search efficiency defined as the solution to:

$$N + 1 = 1 + b^{*} + (b^{*})^{2} + \ldots + (b^{*})^{d}$$

where $N$ is the total number of nodes expanded and $d$ is the depth of the solution. This can be solved numerically via iterative methods or by rewriting in closed form. A **smaller $b^{*}$** indicates a more efficient search. For robotics applications, well-designed heuristics routinely achieve $b^{*} < 1.5$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — A\* on a 2D Grid

**Setup:** Robot on a $5 \times 5$ grid. Start $S = (0,0)$, Goal $G = (4,4)$. 8-connected moves. Move costs: cardinal $= 1$, diagonal $= \sqrt{2}$. Heuristic: Octile distance.

**Iteration-by-iteration summary table (showing Open List $f$-values at each step):**

| Step | Expanded Node $n$ | $g(n)$ | $h_{\text{oct}}(n)$ | $f(n) = g+h$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — (initial) | $S(0,0)$ | $0$ | $4(\sqrt{2}) \approx 5.66$ |
| 1 | $S$ | $0$ | $5.66$ | $5.66$ |
| 2 | $(1,0)$ | $1$ | $4.24$ | $5.24$ |
| 3 | $(1,1)$ | $\sqrt{2}$ | $3.41$ | $4.83$ |
| 4 | $(2,2)$ | $2\sqrt{2}$ | $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ | $5.66$ |
| 5 | $(3,3)$ | $3\sqrt{2}$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ | $5.66$ |
| 6 | $(4,4) = G$ | $4\sqrt{2}$ | $0$ | $5.66$ |

The algorithm finds a path $S \rightarrow (1,1) \rightarrow (2,2) \rightarrow (3,3) \rightarrow G$ with total cost $4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.66$, which is optimal for diagonal-only movement.

### 3.5 Comparative Complexity Matrix for KTU Board Exam

| Strategy | Time | Space | Optimal? | Uses Heuristic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BFS | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b^{d})$ | Yes (unit cost) | No |
| DFS | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ | No | No |
| UCS | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^{*}/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^{*}/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | Yes | No |
| IDDFS | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b \cdot d)$ | Yes (unit cost) | No |
| Greedy | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(b^{m})$ | No | Yes |
| A\* | $O(b^{d})$ (with good $h$, exponential worst case) | $O(b^{d})$ | Yes (admissible $h$) | Yes |
| IDA\* | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(d)$ | Yes (admissible $h$) | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Level State-Space Graph Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS((S = start)) -->|cost 1| nodeA((A))
    nodeS -->|cost 1| nodeB((B))
    nodeA -->|cost 1| nodeC((C))
    nodeA -->|cost sqrt 2| nodeD((D))
    nodeB -->|cost 1| nodeD
    nodeB -->|cost 1| nodeE((E))
    nodeC -->|cost sqrt 2| nodeG((G = goal))
    nodeD -->|cost 1| nodeG
    nodeE -->|cost sqrt 2| nodeG
    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef visited fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    class nodeS start
    class nodeG goal
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeD visited
```

**Figure Explanation:** The diagram depicts a graph with 5 intermediate states (A, B, C, D, E) connecting start $S$ to goal $G$. Edge labels indicate transition costs (1 for cardinal moves, $\sqrt{2}$ for diagonal). A* would explore nodes in order of increasing $f = g + h$ until $G$ is reached.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: A* Search Tree Expansion Flow (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Initialize Open List with S] --> pop{Pop lowest f node n}
    pop -->|n equals Goal| reconstruct[Reconstruct path via parent map]
    pop -->|n not Goal| check{n in Closed List?}
    check -->|Yes| pop
    check -->|No| addClosed[Add n to Closed List]
    addClosed --> gen[Generate all successors n prime of n]
    gen --> loop{For each successor}
    loop --> compute[Compute g prime = g of n + c of n, a]
    compute --> admissible{n prime already in Open with lower g}
    admissible -->|Yes| loop
    admissible -->|No| update[Insert n prime into Open List with parent and f prime]
    update --> loop
    loop -->|All successors done| pop
    reconstruct --> output[Output optimal path]
    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    classDef goalNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    classDef computeNode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B
    class init startNode
    class output goalNode
    class compute,update computeNode
```

**Figure Explanation:** This is the canonical block-level functional architecture of A* search. Each rectangular block is a computational stage; decision diamonds (pop, check, admissible) are branching points. The flow shows the closed loop of expand → generate → update → re-select that defines any best-first graph search.

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Comparative Search Tree Expansion (BFS vs DFS vs A*)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA((S)):::root --> B1((A))
    rootA --> B2((B))
    rootA --> B3((C))
    B1 --> D1((D))
    B1 --> D2((E))
    B2 --> D3((F))
    B2 --> D4((G)):::goal
    B3 --> D5((H))
    B3 --> D6((I))
    B1 -.-> E1((X - DFS ignores)):::pruned
    B2 -.-> E2((Y - DFS ignores)):::pruned
    B3 -.-> E3((Z - DFS ignores)):::pruned
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B7000,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef pruned fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#808080,color:#000
    class rootA root
```

**Figure Explanation:** Solid arrows indicate expansion by BFS or A* (exhaustively explores one level). Dashed arrows show nodes that DFS would explore by going deep along a single branch first, while the bold-italicized "X, Y, Z" labels indicate nodes that DFS *would* consider if its chosen branch fails. A* prunes more aggressively (shown by greyed-out subtrees) because of heuristic guidance.

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Heuristic Function Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose heuristic for grid path planning] --> q1{Movement model?}
    q1 -->|4-connected, unit cost| manh[Use Manhattan Distance]
    q1 -->|8-connected, diag = sqrt 2| oct[Use Octile Distance]
    q1 -->|Continuous / any angle| eucl[Use Euclidean Distance]
    q1 -->|Differential drive, holonomic| cheb[Use Chebyshev Distance]
    manh --> admit[Verify admissibility h ≤ h star]
    oct --> admit
    eucl --> admit
    cheb --> admit
    admit --> cons[Verify consistency if using graph search]
    cons --> deploy[Deploy heuristic in A star]
    classDef heu fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082
    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00
    class manh,oct,eucl,cheb heu
    class admit,cons,deploy step
```

**Figure Explanation:** A decision tree that maps the robot's kinematic/movement constraints to the appropriate admissible heuristic. Practitioners in KTU lab courses and robotics industry use this rubric to pick a heuristic that is both *admissible* (guaranteed optimality) and *informed* (efficient expansion).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**(3 Marks) Define a "state space" in the context of mobile robot path planning. List the five components of a well-defined search problem.**

**Model Answer:**

A **state space** in mobile robotics is the set of all possible discrete configurations (states) the robot can occupy in its environment, represented as nodes in a graph. Each state encodes the robot's pose (position, orientation), and the edges between states represent valid kinematic actions.

The five components of a search problem are:
1. **$S$** — Set of all possible discrete states.
2. **$A(s)$** — Set of allowable actions from state $s$.
3. **$s_0$** — Initial state (where the robot currently is).
4. **$G$** — Set of goal states (where the robot wants to be).
5. **$c(s, a)$** — Cost function that assigns a non-negative cost to each state-action pair.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition of state space, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining the 5-tuple.)*

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**(3 Marks) Differentiate between an admissible heuristic and a consistent heuristic. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Admissible Heuristic | Consistent Heuristic |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Never overestimates true cost: $h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$ | Satisfies triangle inequality: $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ |
| Strength | Weaker condition | Stronger; implies admissibility |
| Example | Manhattan distance for 4-connected grid | Manhattan distance for 4-connected grid with unit costs |

**Examples:** Manhattan distance is *both* admissible and consistent in a 4-connected grid. A non-admissible heuristic would be Euclidean distance squared in a 4-connected grid (it overestimates).

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for definitions, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the A* search algorithm. Define admissible and consistent heuristics and show mathematically that A* returns an optimal path when the heuristic is admissible.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Consider a robot navigating a 5×5 grid with the start node at $S = (0,0)$ and the goal at $G = (4,4)$. The robot can move in 8 directions with cardinal cost = 1 and diagonal cost = $\sqrt{2}$. Use the A* algorithm with the octile distance heuristic to find the optimal path. Show all iterations of the Open List and the parent pointers. Compute the total path cost.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **A* search algorithm** is a best-first graph search that uses the evaluation function:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where $g(n)$ is the cost from the start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is a heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the goal.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2 above for the flowchart).**

**Admissible Heuristic:** A heuristic $h$ is admissible if for every node $n$, $h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$, where $h^{*}(n)$ is the true minimum cost to the goal. Admissible heuristics never *overestimate*.

**Consistent Heuristic:** A heuristic is consistent if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ reachable by action $a$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, a) + h(n')$$

This is the triangle inequality along any edge. Consistency implies admissibility.

**Proof of Optimality of A\* with Admissible $h$:**

Assume contrary that A* terminates at a non-optimal goal node $G_2$ with cost $C_2 > C^{*}$. There must be some node $n$ on the optimal path to $G_{\text{opt}}$ still in the Open List. Then:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + h^{*}(n) \leq g(G_{\text{opt}}) = C^{*} < C_2 = f(G_2)$$

Since A* picks the node with the lowest $f$, it would have expanded $n$ (and reached $G_{\text{opt}}$) before terminating at $G_2$. This contradiction proves A* is optimal with an admissible heuristic. ∎

*(Marks: 2 for the algorithm explanation, 2 for definitions of admissible and consistent, 3 for the proof.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $5 \times 5$ grid, $S = (0,0)$, $G = (4,4)$, 8-connected, cardinal cost $= 1$, diagonal cost $= \sqrt{2}$, heuristic $h_{\text{oct}}(n) = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$.

**Iteration Table:**

| Step | Pop | $g$ | $h$ | $f$ | Closed | Open (parent, $f$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | — | — | $\{\}$ | $\{(0,0): \text{None}, 5.66\}$ |
| 1 | $(0,0)$ | $0$ | $5.66$ | $5.66$ | $\{(0,0)\}$ | $\{(1,0): (0,0), 5.24\}, (0,1): (0,0), 5.24\}, (1,1): (0,0), 4.83\}$ |
| 2 | $(1,1)$ | $\sqrt{2}$ | $3.41$ | $4.83$ | $\{(0,0),(1,1)\}$ | $\{(1,0),(0,1)\}, (2,1): (1,1), 4.66\}, (1,2): (1,1), 4.66\}, (2,2): (1,1), 4.24\}$ |
| 3 | $(2,2)$ | $2\sqrt{2}$ | $2.83$ | $4.24$ | $\{(0,0),(1,1),(2,2)\}$ | $\{(3,2),(2,3): 4.66\}, (3,3): (2,2), 4.24\}$ |
| 4 | $(3,3)$ | $3\sqrt{2}$ | $1.41$ | $4.24$ | $\{(0,0),(1,1),(2,2),(3,3)\}$ | $\{(4,3),(3,4): 4.66\}, (4,4): (3,3), 4.24\}$ |
| 5 | $(4,4) = G$ | $4\sqrt{2}$ | $0$ | $4.24$ | Goal reached! | — |

**Optimal Path:** $(0,0) \rightarrow (1,1) \rightarrow (2,2) \rightarrow (3,3) \rightarrow (4,4)$

**Total Path Cost:**

$$C = \sqrt{2} + \sqrt{2} + \sqrt{2} + \sqrt{2} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.656 \text{ cost units}$$

*(Marks: 2 for setting up heuristic, 3 for the iteration table, 2 for final path and cost.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style — Alternative]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Compare and contrast BFS, DFS, Uniform-Cost Search, and A* search algorithms in terms of (i) data structure used for the frontier, (ii) completeness, (iii) optimality, and (iv) time/space complexity. Provide a real-world robotics application for each.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Derive the octile distance heuristic for an 8-connected grid. Show that it is admissible. Given a 6×6 grid with start at $S = (0,0)$, goal at $G = (5,5)$, cardinal cost = 1, and diagonal cost = $\sqrt{2}$, compute the heuristic value of the cell $n = (2,3)$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Algorithm | Frontier | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space | Robotics Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BFS** | FIFO Queue | Yes (finite $b$) | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b^{d})$ | Exploring all reachable cells for SLAM frontier detection |
| **DFS** | LIFO Stack | No (infinite paths) | No | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(bm)$ | Solving mazes where any path suffices (toy robots, contest bots) |
| **UCS** | Priority Queue (by $g$) | Yes (positive costs) | Yes | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^{*}/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^{*}/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | Minimum-energy path planning where each action has different battery cost |
| **A\*** | Priority Queue (by $f = g+h$) | Yes | Yes (admissible $h$) | $O(b^{d})$ with good $h$ | $O(b^{d})$ | Industrial AGV routing, autonomous vehicle lane-level planning |

**Real-world insights:**
- BFS guarantees finding the *shortest* path in graphs with unit edge weights — used in network topology discovery.
- DFS uses very little memory — favored in embedded robotics with constrained RAM.
- UCS is preferred over BFS when edge costs vary (e.g., sloped terrain).
- A* dominates all three when a good heuristic is available — the industry standard.

*(Marks: 1 mark per algorithm row, 3 marks for applications/discussion.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of Octile Distance (already covered in Section 3.1 — Steps 1 to 6):**

The final form:

$$h_{\text{oct}}(n) = (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$$

**Proof of Admissibility:** In an 8-connected grid with cardinal cost 1 and diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}$, any path from $n$ to $G$ must include at least $k = \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ diagonal moves (since each diagonal reduces both $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ by 1, and you cannot do better). The remaining axis gap is $\max(\Delta x, \Delta y) - k$, which requires at least that many cardinal moves. Thus:

$$h_{\text{oct}}(n) = k \cdot \sqrt{2} + (\max - k) \cdot 1 = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$$

This is the *minimum possible cost* of any path, so $h_{\text{oct}}(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$. Hence octile is **admissible**. ∎

**Numerical Computation for $n = (2, 3)$, $G = (5, 5)$:**

$$\Delta x = \vert 2 - 5 \vert = 3$$

$$\Delta y = \vert 3 - 5 \vert = 2$$

$$\min(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \min(3, 2) = 2$$

$$\max(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \max(3, 2) = 3$$

$$h_{\text{oct}}(2,3) = (\sqrt{2} - 1)(2) + 3 = 2\sqrt{2} - 2 + 3 = 2\sqrt{2} + 1$$

$$h_{\text{oct}}(2,3) \approx 2(1.414) + 1 = 2.828 + 1 = 3.828 \text{ cost units}$$

*(Marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for admissibility proof, 2 for numerical computation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting admissibility proof:** A* optimality *requires* proof that $h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$. Stating "it is admissible" without proof loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing search tree vs. search graph:** Drawing a tree where the same node appears multiple times without explaining why it is a *tree* (not a graph) loses 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Heuristic units:** Manhattan/Euclidean/Octile give *cell counts* or *length units* — failing to specify what they measure loses marks in derivations.
> 4. **Confusing BFS optimality condition:** BFS is optimal *only* for uniform step costs. Students often claim BFS is always optimal — this is a common KTU 2024 scheme error.
> 5. **Skipping iteration tables in A* numericals:** A* must be solved *step-by-step* on the Open List. A direct answer without iterations gets 0.
> 6. **Forgetting parent pointers:** A* solution is not just the cost — it is the *path*. Without the parent map reconstruction, the answer is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **State Space:** Discretized configuration space, abstracted as a graph $G = (V, E)$.
- **5-Tuple Search Problem:** $(S, A, s_0, G, c)$ — must be in your answer for any "define search problem" question.
- **Two Families:** Uninformed (BFS, DFS, UCS, IDDFS) vs. Informed (Greedy, A\*, IDA\*).
- **Data Structures Decide Strategy:** Queue = BFS, Stack = DFS, Priority Queue = UCS/A\*.
- **A\* Evaluation Function:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — commit this to memory.
