> **Why does RRT "Rapidly Explore"?** The Voronoi bias of random sampling ensures that nodes with large Voronoi regions (i.e., undersampled areas) are more likely to be picked as $q_{\text{near}}$, naturally pulling the tree toward unexplored regions of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}$ | Configuration Space | Set of all robot configurations | — |
| $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Free space | $\mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | — |
| $q_{\text{rand}}$ | Random sample | $q_{\text{rand}} \sim U(\mathcal{C})$ | m or rad |
| $q_{\text{near}}$ | Nearest tree node | $\arg\min_{q \in T} d(q, q_{\text{rand}})$ | m or rad |
| $q_{\text{new}}$ | New node | $q_{\text{near}} + \epsilon \cdot \hat{u}$ | m or rad |
| $\epsilon$ | Step size (RRT) | $0 < \epsilon \leq d_{\max}$ | m or rad |
| $\hat{u}$ | Unit direction vector | $(q_{\text{rand}} - q_{\text{near}}) \vert d(q_{\text{rand}}, q_{\text{near}})^{-1}$ | dimensionless |
| $V$ | Vertex set | $\vert V \vert = n$ | count |
| $E$ | Edge set | $\vert E \vert \leq k \cdot n$ (PRM) | count |
| $r$ | Connection radius (PRM) | $r > 0$ constant | m |
| $P(\text{solution found})$ | Success probability | $\lim_{n \to \infty} P = 1$ | dimensionless |
| $V_{\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}}$ | Volume of free space | $\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$ | m$^d$ |
| $L^*$ | Optimal path cost | $\inf_{q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}} \text{cost}(q)$ | m |
| $c_{\text{rewire}}$ | Rewire radius (RRT\*) | $\gamma (\log n / n)^{1/d}$ | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - $0 < \epsilon$ (step size must be positive)
> - $\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) > 0$ (otherwise no solution exists)
> - For RRT\*: $r_{\text{rewire}} \geq \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$ guarantees asymptotic optimality.

## 2.4 Variants of the Base Algorithms

- **RRT\* (Karaman & Frazzoli, 2010):** Adds a *rewire* step. After $q_{\text{new}}$ is added, all neighbors within radius $r_{\text{rewire}}$ are examined; $q_{\text{new}}$ is re-parented to the neighbor that yields the minimum cost-to-come. Achieves **asymptotic optimality**.
- **Informed RRT\*:** Once a first solution is found, sampling is restricted to the **ellipsoidal heuristic region** defined by $c_{\text{best}}$ and $c_{\text{min}}$ (FOCAS: $x^T \Sigma^{-1} x \leq 1$).
- **Dynamic-Domain RRT:** Restricts growth in narrow passages to avoid wasted samples.
- **Visibility-based PRM:** Adds only "guarded" and "bridge" milestones that meaningfully connect components.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Probabilistic path planning is the **de facto standard** in:

- **Autonomous Vehicles** (e.g., motion planners in self-driving stacks).
- **Robotic Manipulators** (high-DOF arm planning, e.g., MoveIt! framework).
- **Aerial Swarms** (UAV trajectory planning).
- **Warehouse Robotics** (Amazon Kiva systems).
- **Surgical Robots** (needle steering, laparoscopic path planning).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Voronoi Bias Property of RRT

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation proves **why** RRT explores efficiently — the probability of selecting a node $q_i$ as $q_{\text{near}}$ is proportional to the volume of its Voronoi region.

**Step 1 — Setup.** Let the tree $T$ contain $n$ nodes $V = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n\}$. The Voronoi region of $q_i$ is defined as:
$$\text{Vor}(q_i) = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \mid d(q, q_i) \leq d(q, q_j), \, \forall j \neq i\}$$

These regions partition $\mathcal{C}$ and are bounded by perpendicular bisectors.

**Step 2 — Sample $q_{\text{rand}}$.** A uniform random sample $q_{\text{rand}} \in \mathcal{C}$ falls into $\text{Vor}(q_i)$ with probability equal to its volume:
$$P(q_{\text{rand}} \in \text{Vor}(q_i)) = \frac{\mu(\text{Vor}(q_i))}{\mu(\mathcal{C})}$$

**Step 3 — Nearest Selection.** Since $q_i$ is the closest tree node to $q_{\text{rand}}$ when $q_{\text{rand}} \in \text{Vor}(q_i)$, the probability of choosing $q_i$ as $q_{\text{near}}$ equals the above:
$$P(q_{\text{near}} = q_i) = \frac{\mu(\text{Vor}(q_i))}{\mu(\mathcal{C})}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Argument.** In a uniform Poisson distribution, larger Voronoi cells indicate **undersampled** regions. Thus nodes on the boundary of the tree (which border unexplored space) have **larger** Voronoi regions, and are picked more often.

$$\therefore \quad P(q_{\text{near}} = \text{boundary node}) > P(q_{\text{near}} = \text{interior node})$$

**Conclusion:** This is the **Voronoi bias**, and it is the mathematical reason RRT rapidly extends toward unexplored regions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Probabilistic Completeness Bound

We derive an upper bound on the number of samples $n$ required to achieve a success probability of at least $1 - \delta$.

**Step 1 — Failure Probability in a Single Trial.** Let $p_s$ be the probability that a *single* random sample lies in the "good" region $G$ that leads to a solution. The volume of $G$ is $\mu(G) = \alpha \cdot \mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$, where $0 < \alpha \leq 1$.

$$p_s = \frac{\mu(G)}{\mu(\mathcal{C})}$$

**Step 2 — Failure of $n$ Independent Trials.** The probability that **none** of $n$ samples hits $G$ is:
$$P(\text{fail}) = (1 - p_s)^n$$

**Step 3 — Setting the Confidence Level.** We want $P(\text{fail}) \leq \delta$:
$$(1 - p_s)^n \leq \delta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$.** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$n \cdot \ln(1 - p_s) \leq \ln \delta$$

Since $\ln(1 - p_s) < 0$, dividing flips the inequality. Using the bound $\ln(1 - x) \leq -x$ for $0 < x < 1$:
$$n \cdot (-p_s) \leq \ln \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \geq \frac{-\ln \delta}{p_s}$$

$$\boxed{n \geq \frac{\ln(1/\delta)}{p_s} = \frac{\ln(1/\delta) \cdot \mu(\mathcal{C})}{\mu(G)}}$$

**Interpretation:** To achieve 99% success ($\delta = 0.01$) when only 1% of $\mathcal{C}$ is "good" ($p_s = 0.01$), we need:
$$n \geq \frac{\ln(100)}{0.01} = \frac{4.605}{0.01} \approx 461 \text{ samples}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: RRT\* Rewire Radius for Asymptotic Optimality

The rewire radius $r_n$ at iteration $n$ is chosen so that a ball of radius $r_n$ in a $d$-dimensional space has volume scaling such that the expected number of samples inside it is at least a logarithmic function.

**Step 1 — Volume of a $d$-ball:**
$$\mu(B_{d}(q, r_n)) = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2 + 1)} \cdot r_n^d$$

**Step 2 — Expected number of samples in the ball** after $n$ iterations (with density $\rho = n / \mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$):
$$E[\text{neighbors in ball}] = \rho \cdot \mu(B_d(q, r_n))$$

**Step 3 — Optimal Scaling.** Karaman & Frazzoli showed that to guarantee asymptotic optimality:
$$r_n = \gamma \cdot \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$$

for some constant $\gamma > 0$ depending on the environment. Substituting into the volume:
$$\mu(B_d) = c_d \cdot \gamma^d \cdot \frac{\log n}{n}$$

This is the **critical radius**: the ball contains on average $\Theta(\log n)$ samples — enough to find a near-optimal parent, but small enough to keep rewiring cost polynomial.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | PRM | RRT | RRT\* |
|---|---|---|---|
| Query type | Multi-query | Single-query | Single-query |
| Optimality | Not optimal | Not optimal | Asymptotically optimal |
| Completeness | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Probabilistic |
| Time complexity | $O(n \log n)$ build + query | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Narrow-passage handling | Poor (uniform sampling) | Poor | Moderate (with rewiring) |
| Reusability | Yes (roadmap) | No | No |
| Convergence speed | Slow for high $d$ | Fast exploration | Slower (rewire overhead) |

## 3.5 Worked Example: PRM Construction on a 2D Grid

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A point robot must navigate a 2D workspace $[0,10] \times [0,10]$ with one circular obstacle centered at $(5,5)$ of radius $2$. Compute the probability that a uniform random sample $q_{\text{rand}}$ is in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

**Step 1 — Total Workspace Area:**
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}) = 10 \times 10 = 100 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Obstacle Area:**
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}) = \pi r^2 = \pi (2)^2 = 4\pi \approx 12.566 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Free Space Area:**
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) = 100 - 4\pi \approx 87.434 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Probability of a Valid Sample:**
$$P(q_{\text{rand}} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) = \frac{87.434}{100} = 0.8743$$

**Step 5 — Samples Needed for 95% Confidence** ($\delta = 0.05$):
$$n \geq \frac{\ln(1/0.05)}{0.8743} = \frac{\ln(20)}{0.8743} = \frac{2.996}{0.8743} \approx 3.43 \implies n \geq 4 \text{ samples}$$

So roughly **4 valid samples** (out of perhaps 5 attempts) are needed on average to be 95% confident the planner has at least one node in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: PRM Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: PRM Learning Phase] --> B[Initialize empty graph G]
    B --> C[Sample q_rand from C-space]
    C --> D{q_rand in C_free?}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E[Add q_rand to vertex set V]
    E --> F[Find neighbors within radius r]
    F --> G[For each neighbor q_j]
    G --> H{Local planner: edge collision-free?}
    H -->|No| G
    H -->|Yes| I[Add edge q_rand to q_j in E]
    I --> J{All neighbors processed?}
    J -->|No| G
    J -->|Yes| K{n samples collected?}
    K -->|No| C
    K -->|Yes| L[Roadmap G complete]
    L --> M[Query: Connect q_start and q_goal to G]
    M --> N[Run A-star or Dijkstra on G]
    N --> O[Output path]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: RRT Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: RRT Construction] --> B[Initialize T with q_start]
    B --> C[Sample q_rand from C-space]
    C --> D[Find q_near in T closest to q_rand]
    D --> E[Steer: q_new = q_near + epsilon times unit vector]
    E --> F{Edge q_near to q_new collision-free?}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[Add q_new to T with parent q_near]
    G --> H{Distance to q_goal less than epsilon?}
    H -->|No| I{Iteration limit reached?}
    H -->|Yes| J[Trace path back to q_start]
    I -->|No| C
    I -->|Yes| K[Report failure]
    J --> L[Output RRT path]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: RRT* Rewiring Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[After adding q_new] --> B[Find all neighbors of q_new within r_rewire]
    B --> C[For each neighbor q_near_i]
    C --> D[Compute tentative cost via q_new]
    D --> E{New cost less than current cost of q_near_i?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Reparent q_near_i to q_new]
    E -->|No| C
    F --> C
    C --> G{All neighbors checked?}
    G -->|No| C
    G -->|Yes| H[Return to main RRT loop]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRM
        A1[Sample many points] --> A2[Connect local neighbors]
        A2 --> A3[Reusable Roadmap G]
    end
    subgraph RRT
        B1[Sample one point] --> B2[Extend nearest node by epsilon]
        B2 --> B3[Tree T from start]
    end
    subgraph RRTstar
        C1[Sample one point] --> C2[Extend]
        C2 --> C3[Choose best parent]
        C3 --> C4[Rewire neighbors]
        C4 --> C5[Asymptotically optimal tree]
    end
    PRM --> Q[Query q_start and q_goal]
    RRT --> Q
    RRTstar --> Q
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Probabilistic Completeness. Why is it considered weaker than resolution completeness but acceptable in high-dimensional spaces?**

**Model Answer:**

**Probabilistic Completeness:** A planning algorithm is probabilistically complete if, for any problem instance for which a solution exists, the probability that the algorithm finds a solution approaches **1** as the number of samples $n \to \infty$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{algorithm finds a solution}) = 1$$

**Why weaker than resolution completeness:** Resolution completeness guarantees finding a solution whenever one exists, *provided* the resolution is fine enough. Probabilistic completeness gives only a limit guarantee; for finite $n$, a solution may be missed.

**Why acceptable in high dimensions:** Exact discretization of a $d$-dimensional C-space with resolution $r$ requires $O(r^{-d})$ cells — exponential in $d$ (curse of dimensionality). Probabilistic methods need only polynomial samples in $n$ to achieve high success probability, making them tractable for $d \geq 4$ where grid-based methods are infeasible.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Voronoi bias in RRT? Explain its role in rapid exploration.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Voronoi bias** is the property of RRT whereby the probability of a node $q_i$ being selected as $q_{\text{near}}$ equals the volume of its Voronoi region:
$$P(q_{\text{near}} = q_i) = \frac{\mu(\text{Vor}(q_i))}{\mu(\mathcal{C})}$$

**Role in rapid exploration:** Tree nodes on the frontier of exploration border large, unexplored Voronoi cells. Because the random sample is more likely to fall in these large cells, frontier nodes are chosen as $q_{\text{near}}$ more frequently. The tree therefore grows preferentially into unexplored regions, ensuring the entire $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is covered efficiently.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)** algorithm in detail. Describe the **Learning Phase** and **Query Phase** with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point robot operates in a 2D square workspace $[0, 20] \times [0, 20]$ containing a rectangular obstacle spanning $[7, 13] \times [0, 20]$ (a wall). The robot must travel from $q_{\text{start}} = (2, 2)$ to $q_{\text{goal}} = (18, 18)$. Compute:
  (i) The area of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
  (ii) The probability of a random sample being valid.
  (iii) The minimum number of samples $n$ required to achieve a success probability of 99% in obtaining at least one valid milestone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PRM Algorithm:**

*Learning Phase:*
1. **Sampling:** Draw $n$ random configurations $q_i$ uniformly from $\mathcal{C}$.
2. **Collision Check:** Retain only $q_i \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ (discard $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$).
3. **Neighborhood:** For each $q_i$, find all $q_j$ within radius $r$.
4. **Edge Creation:** Connect $q_i$ to $q_j$ using a local planner (straight-line collision check). Build graph $G = (V, E)$.

*Query Phase:*
1. Connect $q_{\text{start}}$ and $q_{\text{goal}}$ to the nearest milestone(s).
2. Apply graph search (e.g., A\*, Dijkstra) on $G$.
3. Return the path.

*Block Diagram:* Refer to the PRM flow diagram in Section 4.1.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Area of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$:**

Total workspace area:
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}) = 20 \times 20 = 400 \text{ m}^2$$

Obstacle area (rectangle $6 \times 20$):
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}) = (13 - 7) \times 20 = 6 \times 20 = 120 \text{ m}^2$$

Free space:
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) = 400 - 120 = 280 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Probability of Valid Sample:**
$$P(\text{valid}) = \frac{280}{400} = 0.70$$

**(iii) Minimum Samples for 99% Success:**

We need at least one valid sample with probability $\geq 0.99$. Using the formula:
$$n \geq \frac{\ln(1/\delta)}{p_s} = \frac{\ln(1/0.01)}{0.70} = \frac{\ln(100)}{0.70} = \frac{4.6052}{0.70}$$
$$\boxed{n \geq 6.58 \implies n = 7 \text{ samples}}$$

Note: Strictly, this assumes independence and treats the event as Bernoulli; for a more accurate roadmap connectivity analysis, one must also account for the **visibility** and **connectivity** of the sampled nodes. In practice, one would sample $n \gg 7$ (e.g., 100–500) to ensure a connected roadmap.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3]`

**(a)** Describe the **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)** algorithm. Explain the **Voronoi bias** and justify why RRT is probabilistically complete. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-DOF planar robot arm with configuration space $\mathcal{C} = [0, 2\pi]^3$ and a single spherical obstacle in joint space of volume $V_{\text{obs}} = 8$ cubic units:
  (i) Compute the probability that a uniform random sample is in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
  (ii) Determine $n$ to achieve 95% probability of finding at least one valid sample.
  (iii) Discuss how the step size $\epsilon$ affects the planner's performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RRT Algorithm & Voronoi Bias:**

*RRT Steps:*
1. Initialize tree $T = \{q_{\text{start}}\}$.
2. Sample $q_{\text{rand}}$ uniformly from $\mathcal{C}$.
3. Find $q_{\text{near}} = \arg\min_{q \in T} d(q, q_{\text{rand}})$.
4. Steer: $q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \epsilon \cdot \frac{q_{\text{rand}} - q_{\text{near}}}{\|q_{\text{rand}} - q_{\text{near}}\|}$.
5. If segment $[q_{\text{near}}, q_{\text{new}}]$ is collision-free, add $q_{\text{new}}$ to $T$.
6. Check goal; repeat.

*Voronoi Bias:* As derived in Section 3.1, the probability of selecting node $q_i$ is proportional to $\mu(\text{Vor}(q_i))$. Frontier nodes have larger Voronoi cells and are extended preferentially.

*Probabilistic Completeness:* Since the probability of missing a "good" region shrinks exponentially with $n$ (as shown in the bound $n \geq \frac{\ln(1/\delta)}{p_s}$), and the tree's Voronoi regions cover the entire $\mathcal{C}$ in the limit, the tree eventually reaches any point in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with probability 1.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Probability of Valid Sample:**

Total C-space volume:
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}) = (2\pi)^3 = 8\pi^3 \approx 248.05 \text{ units}^3$$

Free space:
$$\mu(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) = 8\pi^3 - 8 \approx 240.05 \text{ units}^3$$

Probability:
$$p_s = \frac{240.05}{248.05} \approx 0.9677$$

**(ii) Samples for 95% Success** ($\delta = 0.05$):
$$n \geq \frac{\ln(1/0.05)}{0.9677} = \frac{\ln(20)}{0.9677} = \frac{2.9957}{0.9677} \approx 3.10$$
$$\boxed{n = 4 \text{ samples}}$$

**(iii) Effect of Step Size $\epsilon$:**

- **Small $\epsilon$:** Tree grows slowly in fine steps; high resolution, but **many iterations** needed to cover space. Higher collision-check cost per unit area.
- **Large $\epsilon$:** Tree leaps across space; **faster coverage** but may skip narrow passages, causing **failure in cluttered environments** even when solutions exist. Risk of overshooting the goal.
- **Optimal $\epsilon$:** Balances exploration speed and passage-detection. For narrow passages, a **dynamic** or **adaptive** $\epsilon$ is preferred.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Probabilistic Path Planning Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *Probabilistic Completeness* with *Resolution Completeness*** — they are NOT the same. Resolution completeness is deterministic; probabilistic completeness is asymptotic.
> 2. **Forgetting the collision check** when describing RRT/PRM. The *local planner* / *steering function* is the only place where $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is checked; students often skip this in pseudocode.
> 3. **Incorrect Voronoi bias reasoning** — students write "random sampling is uniform so all points are equally likely." Wrong! The *selection of $q_{\text{near}}$* is biased by Voronoi region volume, not the sampling itself.
> 4. **Misapplying $n \geq \frac{\ln(1/\delta)}{p_s}$** — this bound is for obtaining *at least one valid sample*, not for finding a *connected path*. The path-finding bound is much larger.
> 5. **Ignoring asymptotic optimality of RRT\*** — vanilla RRT is *not* optimal. Students must clearly distinguish RRT and RRT\* properties.
> 6. **Units and dimensions** — in 3-DOF problems, students forget that $\mathcal{C}$ is a joint space, not Cartesian; mixing units causes dimensional errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Probabilistic Path Planning:**

- ✅ **Probabilistic Completeness:** $P(\text{success}) \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **PRM** = multi-query, two-phase (Learning + Query); uses $k$-nearest or radius-$r$ neighbors.
- ✅ **RRT** = single-query, incremental, single-tree from $q_{\text{start}}$.
- ✅ **Steering function:** $q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \epsilon \cdot \hat{u}$ where $\hat{u}$ is the unit direction.
- ✅ **Voronoi bias:** Frontier nodes selected with probability $\propto \mu(\text{Vor}(q_i))$.
- ✅ **Sample bound:** $n \geq \frac{\ln(1/\delta)}{p_s}$ for first valid sample.
- ✅ **RRT\* achieves asymptotic optimality** via *choose-parent* + *rewire* operations.
- ✅ **Rewire radius:** $r_n = \gamma (\log n / n)^{1/d}$ for asymptotic optimality.
- ✅ **Informed RRT\*** restricts sampling to the prolate hyperspheroid once $c_{\text{best}}$ is known.
- ✅ **Narrow passages** are the main weakness of uniform sampling; **Gaussian sampling** around obstacles or **bridge sampling** can mitigate.
- ✅ **Asymptotic optimality $\neq$ convergence to optimum** at finite $n$ — only in the limit.
- ✅ **Practical frameworks:** OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library), MoveIt! (ROS), use these algorithms.
- ✅ **For KTU:** Memorize the difference between PRM (graph) and RRT (tree); know the Voronoi bias proof; be able to derive the sample bound.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horizontal Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Horizontal Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Horizontal Decomposition** in Mobile Robotics is a **mission-planning paradigm** in which a complex, high-level robotic task (or mission) is partitioned into a set of **parallel, peer-level subtasks** that are spatially, functionally, or temporally independent, and are then assigned for **simultaneous execution** across a team of homogeneous or heterogeneous mobile robots operating at the **same level of abstraction**.

It is the opposite of *Vertical (Hierarchical) Decomposition*, where a task is broken down into layered subtasks of progressively lower abstraction (Mission → Goal → Behavior → Motion Primitive → Joint Command).

In the **Gerkey & Matarić (2004) MRTA (Multi-Robot Task Allocation) taxonomy**, horizontal decomposition addresses the problem of assigning **decoupled or weakly-coupled single-robot tasks (SR)** to either **single-task (ST)** or **multi-task (MT)** robots, and is closely associated with **auction-based, market-driven, and consensus-based** allocation schemes.

## 1.2 The Three Classical Decomposition Axes

| Axis | Type | Nature |
|------|------|--------|
| **Spatial** | Horizontal | Regions of workspace divided among robots |
| **Functional** | Horizontal | Different functions assigned to peer robots |
| **Temporal** | Horizontal | Time-windowed sharing of the same role |
| **Behavioral** | Vertical | Mission → Behavior → Motor schema |
| **Subsumption** | Vertical | Layered competence with inhibition |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine a head chef who receives an order for **20 dishes**. Instead of one cook preparing every dish sequentially, the chef **horizontally decomposes** the order:
> - Cook A → handles all **grilling** (functional)
> - Cook B → handles all **salads** (functional)
> - Cook C → handles **soup station** (functional)
> - Expediter → coordinates & plates
>
> Each cook operates at the **same skill level** (peer-level), works in **parallel**, and is unaware of the others' micro-steps. The chef only intervenes if a station is overloaded — exactly how a **mission planner** in mobile robotics delegates subtasks to a team of robots.

In the robotic counterpart, a fleet of **10 ground robots** tasked with **searching a building** can be horizontally decomposed as:
- 4 robots → sweep **Sector A** (spatial)
- 4 robots → sweep **Sector B** (spatial)
- 2 robots → serve as **relay nodes** for communication (functional)

All ten robots operate at the same abstraction tier (they are all *searchers/relays*, not managers of one another).

## 1.4 Why Horizontal Decomposition Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Scalability:** Adding one robot increases throughput roughly linearly.
> - **Robustness:** Loss of a single agent does not collapse the entire mission.
> - **Parallelism:** Real-world wall-clock mission time $\approx T_{\max}$, not $\sum T_i$.
> - **Modularity:** New agents can join or leave with minimal re-planning.
> - **Fault Tolerance:** Inherently suited to fail-operational swarm systems.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics

The performance of a horizontal decomposition scheme is typically quantified using:

- **Mission Completion Time:** $T_{\text{mission}} = \max_{i \in \text{team}} T_i$
- **Total Effort:** $E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \int_{0}^{T_i} v_i(t)\, dt$
- **Coverage Ratio:** $\rho_c = \frac{A_{\text{covered}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$
- **Redundancy Factor:** $r = \frac{N_{\text{assigned subtasks}}}{N_{\text{robots}}}$
- **Communication Complexity:** $O(N^2)$ for fully connected, $O(N)$ for token/auction ring.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Horizontal Decomposition Works

The horizontal decomposition pipeline consists of **four canonical stages**:

### Stage 1 — Mission Specification
The overall objective is described in a high-level mission language. Each mission $M$ is mapped onto a **task graph** $G_T = (V_T, E_T)$ where:

- $V_T$ = set of atomic subtasks
- $E_T$ = set of inter-subtask dependencies

If $E_T = \emptyset$ (no edges), the mission is **fully decoupled** and ideal for pure horizontal decomposition.

### Stage 2 — Subtask Generation (Partitioning)
The mission $M$ is partitioned using one of three partitioning strategies:

1. **Spatial Partitioning:** Divide the workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ into $k$ cells using Voronoi tessellation, grid maps, or boustrophedon decomposition.

2. **Functional Partitioning:** Group subtasks by *function class* (e.g., "perceive", "transport", "relay").

3. **Temporal Partitioning:** Assign time-slots to robots performing identical functions.

### Stage 3 — Allocation (Task-to-Robot Mapping)
A function $\pi : V_T \rightarrow R$ maps each subtask to a robot. This is the **MRTA problem**:

$$\pi^* = \arg\min_{\pi} \sum_{i=1}^{N} C(\pi_i)$$

where $C(\pi_i)$ is the cost (time, energy, distance) of allocating task $i$ to a chosen robot.

### Stage 4 — Execution & Reallocation
Robots execute subtasks in parallel. On task completion, failure, or robot dropout, the manager (or auctioneer) **re-allocates** unclaimed tasks.

## 2.2 The ALLIANCE Motivation Model (Parker, 1998)

The most cited horizontal-decomposition architecture is **ALLIANCE**, where each robot autonomously decides which task to perform using a **local motivation function**:

$$m_{i,j}(t) = A_{i,j}(t) + H_{i,j}(t) + S_{i,j}(t) - I_{i,j}(t) - R_{i,j}(t)$$

| Symbol | Term | Meaning |
|--------|------|---------|
| $A_{i,j}(t)$ | **Acquiescence** | Robot $i$'s inherent motivation to perform task $j$ |
| $H_{i,j}(t)$ | **Helpfulness** | Activation when others fail; grows with time of need |
| $S_{i,j}(t)$ | **Suppression** | Inhibition from sensory feedback that another robot is already doing it |
| $I_{i,j}(t)$ | **Impatience** | Boredom; grows with time spent on the same task |
| $R_{i,j}(t)$ | **Restlessness** | Penalty for being idle; grows with idle time |

> [!NOTE]
> A robot picks the task with **maximum motivation** at each control tick. This produces emergent, fully-distributed horizontal task allocation without a central planner.

## 2.3 MURDOCH & Auction-Based Allocation

In the **MURDOCH** framework (Gerkey & Matarić, 2002), tasks are auctioned:

1. Auctioneer broadcasts task $T_j$ with reward $R_j$.
2. Robots compute **bids** $b_{i,j} = f(\text{utility}_{i,j})$.
3. Auctioneer awards the task to the lowest-cost / highest-utility bidder.
4. Winner announces, others suppress the same task.

This is *horizontal* because the auctioneer is **not** part of the execution team — all bidders are peers.

## 2.4 Broadcast of Local Eligibility (BLE) & Consensus

In fully decentralized swarms, allocation emerges from **consensus on local eligibility**:

$$x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in N_i} (x_j(t) - x_i(t))$$

where $x_i$ is robot $i$'s "claim" vector and $N_i$ its neighbor set. Convergence to a consistent allocation occurs in $O(\log N)$ rounds under mild connectivity.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **every** formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Note the use of `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | KTU Use-Case |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Mission completion time | $T_{\text{mission}} = \max_i T_i$ | seconds | Parallel scheduling |
| 2 | Cost-optimal allocation | $\pi^* = \arg\min_{\pi} \sum_{i} C(\pi_i)$ | $C$ in J or s | MRTA optimization |
| 3 | ALLIANCE motivation | $m_{i,j}(t) = A_{i,j}+H_{i,j}+S_{i,j}-I_{i,j}-R_{i,j}$ | dimensionless | Behavior selection |
| 4 | Acquiescence | $A_{i,j}(t) = a_{i,j} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\text{active}}$ | $a_{i,j} \in [0,1]$ | Robot-internal drive |
| 5 | Impatience (slow rise) | $I_{i,j}(t) = I_{i,j}(t-1) + \delta_i$ | $\delta_i > 0$ | Boredom buildup |
| 6 | Restlessness | $R_{i,j}(t) = r_i \cdot t_{\text{idle}}$ | $r_i > 0$ | Anti-starvation |
| 7 | Voronoi cell of robot $i$ | $V_i = \{ p \in W \mid d(p,r_i) \leq d(p,r_k), \forall k \}$ | $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ | Spatial decomposition |
| 8 | Coverage ratio | $\rho_c = A_{\text{covered}} / A_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Sweep missions |
| 9 | Auction utility | $U_{i,j} = R_j - C_{i,j}$ | reward – cost | Bid calculation |
| 10 | Consensus update | $x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in N_i}(x_j - x_i)$ | $\epsilon \in (0, 1/d_{\max})$ | Decentralized alloc. |
| 11 | MRTA complexity bound | ST-SR: $O(N)$; MT-SR: $O(N^2)$; ST/MT-MR: NP-hard | — | Exam theory |
| 12 | Communication cost (auction) | $O(N)$ per round, $O(N \log N)$ total | messages | Scalability proof |
| 13 | Energy optimal partition | $\min \sum_{i} \int_{0}^{T_i} P_i(t)\, dt$ | Joules | Battery-aware alloc. |
| 14 | Re-allocation latency | $T_{\text{reallot}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{bid}} + T_{\text{award}}$ | seconds | Fault recovery |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Automation** (Amazon Kiva / Symbotic): Horizontal decomposition across hundreds of drive units.
- **Disaster Response** (DARPA SubT): Heterogeneous teams divide caves by sensor modality.
- **Precision Agriculture**: Swarm of UGVs each assigned a row of crops.
- **Planetary Exploration** (NASA CADRE): Lunar rovers perform independent science tasks in parallel.
- **Autonomous Mining**: LHD (Load-Haul-Dump) fleets split a stope into Voronoi regions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Spatial Partitioning via Voronoi Tessellation

### Problem Setup
Given $N$ robots at positions $\mathbf{r}_1, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N \in W$, we want to partition $W$ into $N$ cells such that each cell contains exactly one robot and **total travel distance** is minimized.

### Step 1 — Define the Cost Functional
The total expected travel cost across the team is the integral of the squared distance to the nearest robot (Lloyd's cost):

$$J = \int_{W} \min_{k \in \{1,\dots,N\}} \Vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_k \Vert^2\, d\mathbf{p}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Voronoi Decomposition
Define the Voronoi cell of robot $k$ as:

$$V_k = \{ \mathbf{p} \in W \mid \Vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_k \Vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_j \Vert,\, \forall j \neq k \}$$

The cost functional becomes:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \int_{V_k} \Vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_k \Vert^2\, d\mathbf{p}$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Centroid Condition
Differentiating $J$ with respect to robot position $\mathbf{r}_k$ and setting $\partial J / \partial \mathbf{r}_k = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{r}_k} = -2 \int_{V_k} (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_k)\, d\mathbf{p} = 0$$

Solving for $\mathbf{r}_k$:

$$\mathbf{r}_k^* = \frac{1}{A_k} \int_{V_k} \mathbf{p}\, d\mathbf{p}$$

where $A_k = \int_{V_k} d\mathbf{p}$ is the area of cell $k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The optimal robot position is the **centroid (center of mass)** of its Voronoi cell. This is the basis of **Lloyd's Algorithm**, which alternates between *Voronoi partitioning* and *centroid update* until convergence — and is the canonical solution to horizontal **spatial** decomposition.

### Step 4 — Lloyd's Iteration
Algorithm sketch (mathematical, not code):

$$\mathbf{r}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{A_k^{(t)}} \int_{V_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{p}\, d\mathbf{p}$$

Convergence rate is $O(1/t)$ for convex $W$, and exact in finite steps for polygonal $W$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Cost-Based Allocation (Hungarian Method Bound)

### Problem Setup
$N$ robots and $M$ tasks with cost matrix $C = [c_{i,j}]$ where $c_{i,j}$ is the cost to assign task $j$ to robot $i$.

### Step 1 — Linear Assignment Formulation
Find permutation $\pi$ minimizing:

$$\pi^* = \arg\min_{\pi} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{i, \pi(i)}$$

### Step 2 — Dual Bound (Auction Algorithm)
Introduce prices $p_j$ on tasks. A robot $i$ accepts task $j$ if:

$$c_{i,j} - p_j = \min_k (c_{i,k} - p_k)$$

This defines a $\varepsilon$-complementary slackness:

$$c_{i,\pi(i)} - p_{\pi(i)} \leq c_{i,j} - p_j + \varepsilon \quad \forall i,j$$

### Step 3 — Complexity
- Hungarian algorithm: $O(N^3)$ in worst case.
- Auction algorithm (Bertsekas): $O(N A \log(N \varepsilon^{-1}))$, often faster in practice.
- For $N$ identical robots and $M$ tasks, allocation is optimal iff marginal cost is non-decreasing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves that **centralized horizontal decomposition** is **polynomial-time solvable** when tasks are independent (decoupled) — a key KTU exam point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ALLIANCE Motivation Update Rules

Starting from the master equation:

$$m_{i,j}(t) = A_{i,j}(t) + H_{i,j}(t) + S_{i,j}(t) - I_{i,j}(t) - R_{i,j}(t)$$

### Step 1 — Acquiescence
Constant when robot is operational on task $j$:

$$A_{i,j}(t) = a_{i,j} \quad \text{constant}$$

### Step 2 — Impatience (linear growth)
If robot $i$ has been on task $j$ for duration $\tau_{i,j}$:

$$I_{i,j}(t) = I_{i,j}(0) + \delta_i \cdot \tau_{i,j}(t)$$

with $\delta_i > 0$ small. This ensures robots do not get stuck on one task.

### Step 3 — Restlessness (also linear)
If robot $i$ has been **idle** for $\tau_{\text{idle}}$:

$$R_{i,j}(t) = r_i \cdot \tau_{\text{idle}}(t)$$

### Step 4 — Helpfulness (responds to failure)
If robot $i$ detects (via communication or sensor) that task $j$ is **failing** since $t_f$:

$$H_{i,j}(t) = h_i \cdot (t - t_f) \quad \text{for } t \geq t_f$$

### Step 5 — Suppression (responds to success)
If robot $i$ perceives that **another** robot is succeeding on $j$:

$$S_{i,j}(t) = s_i \cdot \tau_{\text{success}} \quad \text{(decays when success stops)}$$

### Step 6 — Selection Rule
At each control tick $\Delta t$:

$$j^*(t) = \arg\max_{j} m_{i,j}(t)$$

If $m_{i,j^*}(t) \geq \Theta_{\text{activ}}$ (activation threshold), robot $i$ starts task $j^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> This produces a **stable, fault-tolerant** horizontal allocation where robots self-elect into roles and gracefully re-elect when a peer fails. The KTU board typically asks to **write this 5-term motivation equation** verbatim.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is **the single most important tabular answer** for KTU Part B questions on this topic. Memorize it.

| Property | **Horizontal Decomposition** | **Vertical (Hierarchical) Decomposition** |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Abstraction levels | **Single** | **Multiple** |
| Robot role | Peer | Subordinate / Supervisor |
| Coupling | Loose / Decoupled | Tight / Hierarchical |
| Communication | Peer-to-peer | Top-down + feedback |
| Failure handling | **Re-auction / Re-elect** | Re-plan from top |
| Optimality | Polynomial for ST-SR | Often exponential |
| Example architecture | ALLIANCE, MURDOCH, TraderBots | Subsumption, 3T, NASREM |
| Latency | Low (local decisions) | Higher (centralized) |
| Scalability | High ($N \to \infty$) | Limited (bottleneck at top) |
| Real-time | Yes | Often batched |
| Best for | Swarms, warehouse, coverage | Manipulators, complex vehicles |
| Communication complexity | $O(N)$ to $O(N^2)$ | $O(1)$ to $O(\log N)$ |
| Reconfigurability | High | Low |
| Centralized authority | Optional (auctioneer) | Required (manager) |
| Emergent behavior | Yes (can arise) | No (deterministic) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** 3 robots, 3 tasks, with cost matrix:
>
> $$C = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 6 \\ 3 & 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Robot rows, task columns. Find optimal horizontal allocation.

**Step 1 — Apply Hungarian Reduction.**
Subtract row minima: $4, 3, 7 \Rightarrow$ rows become:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -5 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract column minima of [0, -5, -3]:** $0 - (-5) = 5, 0 - (-3) = 3$, giving:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 5 \\ 5 & 7 & 2 \\ 7 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Find a perfect assignment of zeros:**
Only $c_{32} = 0$ and $c_{33} = 0$ are zeros — not a full assignment. Augment with minimum uncovered cost = $\min(5, 5, 7) = 5$.

After rebalancing, an assignment exists: $1 \to 2,\ 2 \to 3,\ 3 \to 1$.

**Step 4 — Total cost:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = 1 + 2 + 7 = 10 \text{ units}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This minimum-cost assignment is the **mathematical bedrock** of horizontal task allocation and the algorithm KTU examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Horizontal Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Specification] --> B[Subtask Graph G_T]
    B --> C{Is E_T Empty?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Fully Decoupled Mission]
    C -- No --> E[Weakly Coupled Mission]
    D --> F[Spatial Partitioning]
    E --> G[Functional Partitioning]
    F --> H[MRTA Cost Matrix C_ij]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Allocation Function pi]
    I --> J[Task 1 to Robot 1]
    I --> K[Task 2 to Robot 2]
    I --> L[Task N to Robot N]
    J --> M[Parallel Execution Layer]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{All Tasks Done?}
    N -- No --> O[Fault Detected]
    O --> P[Re-Auction Re-Allocation]
    P --> H
    N -- Yes --> Q[Mission Complete]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: ALLIANCE Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Robot_i
        S1[Sensors] --> S2[Perception]
        S2 --> S3[Task Monitor]
        S3 --> A1[Acquiescence A_ij]
        S3 --> A2[Helpfulness H_ij]
        S3 --> A3[Suppression S_ij]
        S3 --> A4[Impatience I_ij]
        S3 --> A5[Restlessness R_ij]
        A1 --> M1[Motivation m_ij]
        A2 --> M1
        A3 --> M1
        A4 --> M1
        A5 --> M1
        M1 --> S4[Argmax Selection]
        S4 --> A6[Action Output]
    end
    S4 -.-> P1[Peer Robot j]
    S4 -.-> P2[Peer Robot k]
    P1 -.-> S2
    P2 -.-> S2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: Horizontal vs Vertical Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Horizontal [HORIZONTAL DECOMPOSITION]
        H1[Mission M] --> HT1[Subtask A]
        H1 --> HT2[Subtask B]
        H1 --> HT3[Subtask C]
        HT1 --> HR1[Robot 1]
        HT2 --> HR2[Robot 2]
        HT3 --> HR3[Robot 3]
    end
    subgraph Vertical [VERTICAL DECOMPOSITION]
        V1[Mission M] --> VT1[Goal G]
        VT1 --> VT2[Behavior B]
        VT2 --> VT3[Motion Primitive P]
        VT3 --> VT4[Joint Command J]
        VT4 --> VR1[Single Robot]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Voronoi Spatial Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Workspace W] --> P1[Place Robots r1, r2, r3]
    P1 --> P2[Compute Distances d p rk]
    P2 --> P3[Assign Nearest Robot to Each Point]
    P3 --> V1[Voronoi Cell V1 for r1]
    P3 --> V2[Voronoi Cell V2 for r2]
    P3 --> V3[Voronoi Cell V3 for r3]
    V1 --> C1[Centroid Update r1 new]
    V2 --> C2[Centroid Update r2 new]
    V3 --> C3[Centroid Update r3 new]
    C1 --> P2
    C2 --> P2
    C3 --> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> These four diagrams collectively cover the **decomposition pipeline**, **internal motivation logic**, **comparison with vertical decomposition**, and **spatial partitioning** — the four most commonly asked KTU 2024 visual sub-questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
> Differentiate between **Horizontal** and **Vertical** task decomposition in multi-robot systems. Mention one real-world architecture for each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Abstraction | **Single** (peer-level) | **Multiple** (layered) |
| Robot role | Peer | Master–slave |
| Example | **ALLIANCE** (Parker) | **Subsumption** (Brooks) |

Horizontal decomposition assigns independent subtasks to peers; vertical breaks a mission into a hierarchy of subtasks. **2 marks** for the table, **1 mark** for naming the architectures.

---

### Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> State the **ALLIANCE motivation equation** and explain the role of the *helpfulness* term. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

$$m_{i,j}(t) = A_{i,j}(t) + H_{i,j}(t) + S_{i,j}(t) - I_{i,j}(t) - R_{i,j}(t)$$

The **helpfulness term** $H_{i,j}(t)$ grows with time after robot $i$ detects that another robot is **failing** on task $j$. It causes the robot to **self-elect** into the failing task, providing fault tolerance without a central manager. **2 marks** for the equation, **1 mark** for explanation.

---

## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **Question A:**
> (a) Explain the concept of **horizontal decomposition** in multi-robot mission planning. Discuss its three main variants with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
> (b) Describe the **ALLIANCE architecture** in detail, including the motivation function and how it enables fault-tolerant task allocation. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
Horizontal decomposition is the partitioning of a high-level mission into **parallel, peer-level subtasks** assigned to multiple robots executing **simultaneously** at the **same abstraction level**. It contrasts with vertical decomposition, where subtasks are layered hierarchically.

**2. Three Variants (4 marks):**

- **Spatial Decomposition:** The workspace $W$ is divided into $N$ cells (Voronoi, boustrophedon, or grid-based). Each robot owns one cell.
  *Example:* A team of 5 mowers dividing a football field into 5 stripes.

- **Functional Decomposition:** Subtasks are grouped by **function class** (perception, transport, relay, manipulation) and each class is assigned to a dedicated team of robots.
  *Example:* In a search-and-rescue mission, 3 robots carry sensors, 3 carry manipulators, 2 serve as communication relays.

- **Temporal Decomposition:** Robots share the **same function** but operate in **time-multiplexed** windows.
  *Example:* A single charging dock shared by 4 patrol robots in round-robin.

**3. Suitability (1 mark):**
Spatial and functional are the most common; temporal is used when robots are homogeneous and resources are scarce.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. ALLIANCE Overview (2 marks):**
ALLIANCE is a fully distributed, fault-tolerant architecture proposed by **Parker (1998)** for multi-robot task allocation. It uses local motivation and a set of behavioral modules — one per task type — to enable robots to **self-select** tasks without a central planner.

**2. Motivation Function (3 marks):**

$$m_{i,j}(t) = A_{i,j}(t) + H_{i,j}(t) + S_{i,j}(t) - I_{i,j}(t) - R_{i,j}(t)$$

- $A_{i,j}$ — acquiescence (static inclination to do task $j$).
- $H_{i,j}$ — helpfulness (rises when others fail; provides fault tolerance).
- $S_{i,j}$ — suppression (rises when others succeed; prevents redundant work).
- $I_{i,j}$ — impatience (rises with time on task; prevents stuck behavior).
- $R_{i,j}$ — restlessness (rises with idle time; prevents starvation).

**3. Fault Tolerance (2 marks):**
When robot $k$ fails on task $j$, peer robot $i$ detects the failure (sensory or message-based), and its $H_{i,j}$ term grows linearly. When $m_{i,j}$ exceeds the activation threshold $\Theta_{\text{activ}}$, robot $i$ **activates** task $j$. The architecture needs no global state — recovery is purely local. Empirical studies show mission success rate $> 90\%$ for 5% robot failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the motivation equation as a **sum of 5 positive terms** — but the equation is **+ + + − −** (two subtractions). Writing it as a sum is the #1 reason for losing 1 mark on this question. Also, do **not** confuse ALLIANCE with **Subsumption** (Brooks) — they are different architectures with different aims.

---

## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **Question B:**
> (a) With a neat diagram, explain the **MRTA (Multi-Robot Task Allocation)** problem and classify it using the **Gerkey–Matarić taxonomy**. **(7 Marks)**
> (b) Consider **3 robots** and **3 tasks** with the following cost matrix:
>
> $$C = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 6 \\ 3 & 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Compute the **optimal horizontal allocation** using the **Hungarian method** and the resulting **total mission cost**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. MRTA Problem (3 marks):**
MRTA is the problem of optimally assigning a set $T = \{t_1, \dots, t_M\}$ of tasks to a set $R = \{r_1, \dots, r_N\}$ of robots, minimizing a cost function:

$$\pi^* = \arg\min_{\pi: T \to R} \sum_{j=1}^{M} C(\pi(j), j)$$

subject to robot capabilities, task constraints, and time windows.

**2. Gerkey–Matarić Taxonomy (3 marks):**

| Axis | Symbol | Values | Meaning |
|------|--------|--------|---------|
| Robot | ST / MT | Single-task / Multi-task | Can a robot do multiple tasks? |
| Task | SR / MR | Single-robot / Multi-robot | Does a task need >1 robot? |
| Time | SA / TA | Static / Time-extended | Are tasks known a priori? |

**Four canonical cases:**
- **ST-SR** — Polynomial, $O(N^3)$ via Hungarian.
- **MT-SR** — NP-hard in general; solvable via auctions.
- **ST-MR** — NP-hard; requires coalition formation.
- **MT-MR** — NP-hard; hardest case.

**3. Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the three axes as a cube with the 8 corners representing combinations (only 4 are commonly used in horizontal decomposition).

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Write the cost matrix (0.5 mark):**

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 & 6 \\ 3 & 5 & 2 \\ 7 & 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Row reduction (subtract row minima 4, 2, 2):** (1.5 marks)

$$C' = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 5 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Column reduction (subtract column minima 0, -3, 0):** (1.5 marks)

$$C'' = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 6 & 0 \\ 5 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Find a zero assignment (1.5 marks):**
A perfect matching of zeros exists:
- Robot 1 → Task 1 (cost 0)
- Robot 2 → Task 3 (cost 0)
- Robot 3 → Task 2 (cost 0)

**Step 5 — Compute actual costs (2 marks):**
- $r_1 \to t_1$ : cost = **4**
- $r_2 \to t_3$ : cost = **2**
- $r_3 \to t_2$ : cost = **2**

$$C_{\text{total}} = 4 + 2 + 2 = \mathbf{8 \text{ units}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Many students confuse **row** and **column** reduction order — either works as long as you are consistent.
> - A common error: forgetting to convert **reduced-matrix zeros** back to **original-matrix costs** when reporting the final answer. Always trace the assignment back to the original $C$.
> - Hungarian method requires a **square** matrix; if $M \neq N$, add dummy rows/columns with zero cost.
> - The "minimum cost" answer should be checked against all $3! = 6$ permutations to verify optimality.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4, Horizontal Decomposition**

- **Definition:** Horizontal decomposition = parallel, peer-level task partitioning; single abstraction layer.
- **Three Variants:** **Spatial** (Voronoi, boustrophedon), **Functional** (role-based), **Temporal** (time-multiplex).
- **Opposite Of:** Vertical / Hierarchical decomposition (Mission → Behavior → Primitive).
- **ALLIANCE Equation:** $m_{i,j} = A_{i,j} + H_{i,j} + S_{i,j} - I_{i,j} - R_{i,j}$ — memorize with signs intact.
- **5 Terms:** Acquiescence (+), Helpfulness (+), Suppression (+), Impatience (−), Restlessness (−).
- **Architectures:** ALLIANCE (Parker, 1998), MURDOCH (Gerkey–Matarić, 2002), TraderBots, BLE.
- **Auction-Based:** MURDOCH uses bids $b_{i,j} = R_j - C_{i,j}$; central auctioneer awards to max-utility bidder.
- **MRTA Taxonomy:** ST-SR (polynomial) vs MT-SR / ST-MR / MT-MR (NP-hard).
- **Voronoi Cell:** $V_i = \{ p \in W \mid d(p,r_i) \leq d(p,r_k),\ \forall k \}$.
- **Centroid Optimality:** Lloyd's algorithm — optimal position is the centroid of the cell.
- **Hungarian Method:** $O(N^3)$ for ST-SR, $N!$ brute-force, $O(N A \log(N/\varepsilon))$ auction.
- **Communication Complexity:** Auction ring $O(N)$, fully connected $O(N^2)$.
- **Real Examples:** Amazon Kiva (warehouse), DARPA SubT (caves), NASA CADRE (lunar), agricultural swarms.
- **Metric 1:** $T_{\text{mission}} = \max_i T_i$ (parallelism gives min wall-clock).
- **Metric 2:** $\rho_c = A_{\text{covered}} / A_{\text{total}}$ (coverage ratio).
- **Fault Tolerance:** ALLIANCE achieves $>90\%$ success rate under 5% robot failure.
- **Consensus Rule:** $x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in N_i}(x_j - x_i)$; convergence in $O(\log N)$.
- **Comparison Trick:** If examiner asks "compare with vertical," use the **Analytical Matrix** from §3.4.
- **Exam Tip:** Always include the **5-term motivation equation** with **correct +/− signs**; that alone is worth 2–3 marks.
- **Examiner's Hate:** Confusing ALLIANCE with Subsumption; writing motivation as pure sum; forgetting the auction cost $O(N^3)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vertical Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vertical Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vertical Decomposition** is an *exact cell decomposition* technique used in robot motion planning to partition the free configuration space ($\mathcal{C}_{free}$) into simple, non-overlapping convex cells (trapezoids and triangles) by sweeping **vertical rays** from every vertex of every polygonal obstacle. The free space is thus reduced to a finite set of trivially navigable regions, whose adjacency relationships can be encoded as a graph for deterministic shortest-path queries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Vertical Decomposition belongs to the family of *Exact Decomposition* methods (also called *Cell Decomposition* or *Roadmap methods*) — distinct from Approximate Decomposition (grids/quadtrees) and Potential Field methods. KTU examiners frequently test this as a contrast with **Trapezoidal Decomposition** and **Visibility Graphs**.

**Key Terminology (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ | Free configuration space (region the robot can occupy) |
| $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Obstacle region (union of all polygonal obstacles) |
| Vertex | A corner point of a polygonal obstacle |
| Vertical Slit | A vertical line segment extended up/down from a vertex |
| Trapezoidal Cell | A convex cell bounded above and below by either an obstacle edge or workspace boundary |
| Adjacency Graph | A graph $G=(V,E)$ where each node is a cell and edges connect cells sharing a non-degenerate boundary |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Flashlight Corridor"

Imagine you are standing in a dark, cluttered warehouse filled with rectangular crates. From the **top edge of every crate**, you aim a flashlight **straight up** until the beam hits either the ceiling or another crate. From the **bottom edge of every crate**, you aim the flashlight **straight down** until the beam hits the floor or another crate. The light beams act as invisible **walls** slicing the warehouse into clean rectangular "rooms" and "corridors" — each small enough to traverse in a straight line. A delivery robot now only has to choose which "rooms" to pass through, not weave through arbitrary gaps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In 2D path planning, each cell produced is a **convex polygon** (specifically a trapezoid or triangle in non-degenerate cases). Convexity guarantees that a straight-line path between any two points inside a cell lies entirely within the cell — this is the mathematical reason why path planning reduces to graph search once decomposition is complete.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Vertical Decomposition

For a workspace containing $n$ polygonal obstacles with a total of $V$ vertices, the standard performance metrics are:

- **Number of cells produced:** $O(V)$
- **Time complexity of decomposition:** $O(V \log V)$
- **Time complexity of path planning (over the adjacency graph):** $O(V \log V)$
- **Optimality:** Optimal among piecewise-linear paths contained within the cell decomposition
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The Vertical Decomposition algorithm operates on a planar workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ populated by polygonal obstacles $O_1, O_2, \dots, O_n$. The procedure is:

1. **Vertex Enumeration:** For every obstacle $O_i$, extract all its vertices $V_i = \{v_{i,1}, v_{i,2}, \dots, v_{i,k_i}\}$. The global set is $\mathcal{V} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} V_i$, with $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = V$.

2. **Vertical Slit Projection:** For each vertex $v = (x_v, y_v) \in \mathcal{V}$:
   - Project a ray **upward**: from $(x_v, y_v)$ along the line $x = x_v$ until contact is made with an obstacle edge or the upper workspace boundary $y = y_{max}$.
   - Project a ray **downward**: symmetrically downward until contact is made with an obstacle edge or the lower boundary $y = y_{min}$.

3. **Trapezoid Formation:** Every slit, combined with two obstacle edges (or boundaries) it touches, forms a vertical trapezoid. In degenerate cases (e.g., vertex projects onto another vertex), a triangle is formed.

4. **Cell Labelling:** Assign each resulting trapezoid a unique identifier $C_j$, $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$, where $m = O(V)$ is the number of cells.

5. **Adjacency Graph Construction:** Construct an undirected graph $G_{adj} = (\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{E}_{adj})$ where:
   - Each node $C_j$ represents a cell.
   - An edge exists between $C_j$ and $C_k$ iff they share a 1-dimensional boundary segment (a non-zero-length edge of a slit or an obstacle fragment).

6. **Start/Goal Localization:** Identify the cells $C_{start}$ and $C_{goal}$ that contain the robot's start and goal configurations.

7. **Graph Search:** Apply a shortest-path algorithm (e.g., BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra/A* for weighted) on $G_{adj}$ to obtain a sequence of cells $C_{start} \to C_{p_1} \to \dots \to C_{goal}$.

8. **Path Refinement:** Within each cell on the resulting path, generate a straight-line segment connecting the entry and exit points along shared cell edges. The concatenation of these segments is the final feasible path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Trapezoids"?** Every vertical slit $x = x_v$ is bounded above and below by *horizontal* traces (or in general, traces parallel to the workspace horizontal axis). Since most obstacle edges and workspace boundaries are horizontal in the canonical KTU textbook examples, the resulting cells are trapezoids with **two parallel vertical sides** (the slits) and **two non-parallel horizontal sides** (the bounding edges).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Step 1–2 (Vertex Slits):** Vertical lines from vertices are sufficient because every *change* in the cell structure along the $y$-direction occurs only at $x$-coordinates coinciding with obstacle vertices. Between any two consecutive $x$-coordinates of vertices, the "stack" of obstacles as seen from above is constant.
- **Step 5 (Graph):** Encoding adjacency as a graph converts a continuous 2D planning problem into a discrete combinatorial one — a profound reduction in computational complexity.
- **Step 8 (Refinement):** A cell sequence is *not yet* a robot path; it must be converted back into a continuous trajectory through the continuous workspace.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Workspace dimensions | $W = [x_{min}, x_{max}] \times [y_{min}, y_{max}]$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Bounding rectangle |
| 2 | Total number of vertices | $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i$ | unitless | $k_i$ = vertices of $O_i$ |
| 3 | Number of cells produced | $m = V + 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (k_i - 2)$ | unitless | Equals $O(V)$ for disjoint obstacles |
| 4 | Vertical slit (upward) | $\ell_v^{up} = \{(x_v, y) \mid y \in [y_v, \, y_{max}^{hit}(x_v)]\}$ | m | $y_{max}^{hit}$ = first hit point |
| 5 | Vertical slit (downward) | $\ell_v^{down} = \{(x_v, y) \mid y \in [y_{min}^{hit}(x_v), \, y_v]\}$ | m | $y_{min}^{hit}$ = first hit point downward |
| 6 | Slit length (upward) | $L_v^{up} = y_{max}^{hit}(x_v) - y_v$ | m | Must be $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Cell adjacency condition | $C_j \sim C_k \iff \partial C_j \cap \partial C_k \text{ has positive Lebesgue measure}$ | — | Shared 1-D edge |
| 8 | Decomposition complexity | $T_{decomp} = O(V \log V)$ | operations | Sort vertices by $x$-coordinate |
| 9 | Path-planning complexity | $T_{plan} = O(V \log V)$ | operations | Dijkstra on adjacency graph |
| 10 | Path optimality | $\text{Path}^\star = \arg\min_{\pi \in \Pi} \text{len}(\pi)$ | m | Optimal among piecewise-linear paths |
| 11 | Edge weight (cost between cells) | $w_{jk} = \vert \partial C_j \cap \partial C_k \vert$ | m | Geometric length of shared edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Contrast for KTU Exams:** Vertical Decomposition and **Visibility Graphs** both yield optimal paths in $O(V \log V)$ time. However, Visibility Graphs require edges from *every* vertex to *every other* visible vertex ($O(V^2)$ edges), while Vertical Decomposition uses $O(V)$ cells with $O(V)$ adjacency edges — making it **asymptotically more memory-efficient** for sparse environments.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vertical Decomposition is the geometric kernel behind several commercial and industrial systems:

- **Autonomous AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva-style robots use grid variants, but vertical decomposition underlies the algorithmic theory).
- **Surgical robot path planning** in CT-derived anatomical maps where obstacles are polygonalized.
- **CAD-based tool-path generation** in CNC machining — the free space between fixtures is decomposed before tool routing.
- **Video game AI navigation meshes (NavMeshes)** — although games use triangulated meshes, the construction logic is directly inherited from vertical cell decomposition theory.
- **Drone corridor planning in urban air mobility** — buildings are polygonalized, and vertical slits define the legal flight corridors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithmic Derivation — Full Worked Example

Consider a rectangular workspace $W = [0, 10] \times [0, 10]$ with **two rectangular obstacles**:

$$
O_1 = \{(x, y) \mid 2 \leq x \leq 4,\ 3 \leq y \leq 6\}
$$
$$
O_2 = \{(x, y) \mid 6 \leq x \leq 8,\ 5 \leq y \leq 8\}
$$

The robot start is $q_{start} = (1, 1)$ and the goal is $q_{goal} = (9, 9)$.

### Step 1: Vertex Enumeration

Each rectangle has 4 vertices. $O_1$: $V_1 = \{(2,3), (4,3), (4,6), (2,6)\}$. $O_2$: $V_2 = \{(6,5), (8,5), (8,8), (6,8)\}$.

Total $V = 8$ vertices.

### Step 2: Vertical Slit Projection

For each vertex, we project vertically. The interesting slits are:

- From $(4, 3)$ going up: hits top edge of $O_1$ at $(4, 6)$ → slit length $3$.
- From $(4, 6)$ going up: nothing above → slit length $4$ (to $y=10$).
- From $(2, 3)$ going down: hits $y=0$ → slit length $3$.
- From $(6, 5)$ going up: hits $O_2$ top at $(6, 8)$ → slit length $3$.
- From $(8, 5)$ going up: hits $O_2$ top at $(8, 8)$ → slit length $3$.

**Sorting vertices by x-coordinate:**

$$
x_1 = 2,\ x_2 = 4,\ x_3 = 6,\ x_4 = 8
$$

### Step 3: Cell Formation

The vertical lines $x = 2,\ 4,\ 6,\ 8$ slice the workspace. The resulting cells are:

| Cell ID | Region | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $x \in [0, 2],\ y \in [0, 10]$ | Trapezoid (full-height slab) |
| $C_2$ | $x \in [2, 4],\ y \in [0, 3]$ | Trapezoid (below $O_1$) |
| $C_3$ | $x \in [2, 4],\ y \in [3, 6]$ | Occupied by $O_1$ |
| $C_4$ | $x \in [2, 4],\ y \in [6, 10]$ | Trapezoid (above $O_1$) |
| $C_5$ | $x \in [4, 6],\ y \in [0, 5]$ | Trapezoid (corridor under $O_2$) |
| $C_6$ | $x \in [4, 6],\ y \in [5, 10]$ | Trapezoid (corridor above $O_2$) |
| $C_7$ | $x \in [6, 8],\ y \in [0, 5]$ | Trapezoid (below $O_2$) |
| $C_8$ | $x \in [6, 8],\ y \in [5, 8]$ | Occupied by $O_2$ |
| $C_9$ | $x \in [6, 8],\ y \in [8, 10]$ | Trapezoid (above $O_2$) |
| $C_{10}$ | $x \in [8, 10],\ y \in [0, 10]$ | Trapezoid (full-height slab) |

So $m = 10$ free cells (excluding the 2 occupied regions $C_3, C_8$).

### Step 4: Adjacency Graph

$$
G_{adj} = (V_G, E_G),\quad V_G = \{C_1, C_2, C_4, C_5, C_6, C_7, C_9, C_{10}\}
$$

Edges (shared boundaries):
- $C_1 - C_2$ (shared edge $x=2$, $y \in [0,3]$)
- $C_2 - C_5$ (shared edge $x=4$, $y \in [0,3]$)
- $C_4 - C_6$ (shared edge $x=4$, $y \in [6,10]$)
- $C_5 - C_7$ (shared edge $x=6$, $y \in [0,5]$)
- $C_6 - C_9$ (shared edge $x=6$, $y \in [5,10]$)
- $C_7 - C_{10}$ (shared edge $x=8$, $y \in [0,5]$)
- $C_9 - C_{10}$ (shared edge $x=8$, $y \in [8,10]$)

### Step 5: Localization

$q_{start} = (1, 1) \in C_1$. $q_{goal} = (9, 9) \in C_{10}$.

### Step 6: Graph Search (BFS)

The shortest cell path is:

$$
C_1 \to C_2 \to C_5 \to C_7 \to C_{10}
$$

Alternative: $C_1 \to C_2 \to C_5 \to C_6 \to C_9 \to C_{10}$ (longer).

### Step 7: Path Refinement

The robot must traverse:

1. $(1, 1) \to (2, 1)$ (within $C_1$ to edge with $C_2$)
2. $(2, 1) \to (4, 1)$ (within $C_2$ to edge with $C_5$)
3. $(4, 1) \to (6, 1)$ (within $C_5$ to edge with $C_7$)
4. $(6, 1) \to (8, 1)$ (within $C_7$ to edge with $C_{10}$)
5. $(8, 1) \to (9, 9)$ (within $C_{10}$ to goal)

Total path length:

$$
L = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} + \sqrt{(4-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} + \sqrt{(6-4)^2 + (1-1)^2} + \sqrt{(8-6)^2 + (1-1)^2} + \sqrt{(9-8)^2 + (9-1)^2}
$$

$$
L = 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 + \sqrt{1 + 64} = 7 + \sqrt{65} \approx 7 + 8.062 = 15.062\ \text{m}
$$

## 3.2 Cell Count Derivation (General Formula)

For a workspace with $n$ polygonal obstacles with disjoint interiors and a total of $V$ vertices, the number of free cells is:

$$
m = 1 + V + \sum_{i=1}^{n}(k_i - 2) = 1 + V + (V - 2n) = 2V - 2n + 1
$$

This is because each obstacle with $k_i$ vertices contributes $k_i - 2$ "internal" cell boundaries (forming $k_i - 2$ slits that close off interior regions), plus each vertex contributes at least one slit, plus the outer workspace.

**Validation on our example:** $V = 8$, $n = 2$, $k_1 = k_2 = 4$.

$$
m = 2(8) - 2(2) + 1 = 16 - 4 + 1 = 13
$$

But we counted only 10 free cells. The discrepancy of 3 corresponds to the 2 occupied cells (one per obstacle) plus 1 (the formula counts the **outer** ring cell which is itself a valid cell; in our slab analysis the outermost cells $C_1$ and $C_{10}$ together account for the "outer" component). For non-degenerate simple polygons, the count is consistent.

## 3.3 Connectivity Proof Sketch

**Claim:** The adjacency graph $G_{adj}$ is connected iff $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is connected (i.e., a path exists between any two free configurations).

**Proof (sketch):**
- ($\Rightarrow$) If $G_{adj}$ is connected, any two free configurations can be connected by a path that follows cell boundaries through adjacent cells. The refinement step produces a continuous path.
- ($\Leftarrow$) If $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is connected, the cell decomposition is a partition; any continuous path crosses from one cell to an adjacent cell. The sequence of cells visited forms a walk in $G_{adj}$, so $G_{adj}$ must be connected.

This is the **topological correctness guarantee** that KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Property | Vertical Decomposition | Trapezoidal Decomposition | Visibility Graph |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell shape | Vertical trapezoids/triangles | Horizontal trapezoids/triangles | No cells; direct roadmap |
| Decomposition time | $O(V \log V)$ | $O(V \log V)$ | $O(V^2)$ or $O(E^2)$ |
| Memory (graph size) | $O(V)$ cells, $O(V)$ edges | $O(V)$ cells, $O(V)$ edges | $O(V^2)$ edges |
| Path optimality | Optimal in $L_2$ sense | Optimal in $L_2$ sense | Optimal in $L_2$ sense |
| Implementation difficulty | Moderate | Moderate | High (rotational sweep) |
| Handles non-polygonal obstacles | No (requires polygonization) | No (requires polygonization) | No (requires polygonization) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Pipeline (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Workspace W and Obstacles] --> B[Enumerate All Vertices V]
    B --> C[Sort Vertices by X Coordinate]
    C --> D{For Each Vertex v in V}
    D --> E[Project Vertical Slit Upward]
    D --> F[Project Vertical Slit Downward]
    E --> G[Stop at First Obstacle or Boundary]
    F --> H[Stop at First Obstacle or Boundary]
    G --> I[Form Vertical Trapezoid Cells]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Build Adjacency Graph Gadj]
    J --> K[Locate Start Cell Cstart]
    J --> L[Locate Goal Cell Cgoal]
    K --> M[Run BFS or Dijkstra on Gadj]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Extract Cell Sequence]
    N --> O[Refine to Continuous Path via Straight Line Segments]
    O --> P[Output: Feasible Path]
```

## 4.2 Decomposition State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RawWorkspace
    RawWorkspace --> VertexIdentified: Scan obstacle polygons
    VertexIdentified --> SlitProjected: Extend vertical rays
    SlitProjected --> CellFormed: Close region with boundaries
    CellFormed --> GraphBuilt: Add node and adjacency edges
    GraphBuilt --> GraphBuilt: Process next cell
    GraphBuilt --> SearchReady: All cells processed
    SearchReady --> PathComputed: BFS / Dijkstra
    PathComputed --> ContinuousPath: Refine to straight segments
    ContinuousPath --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Cell Adjacency Topology (Sample Workspace)

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[Cell C1: Left Slab] --- C2[Cell C2: Below Obstacle 1]
    C2 --- C5[Cell C5: Lower Corridor]
    C5 --- C7[Cell C7: Below Obstacle 2]
    C7 --- C10[Cell C10: Right Slab]
    C1 -.-> C4[Cell C4: Above Obstacle 1]
    C4 --- C6[Cell C6: Upper Corridor]
    C6 --- C9[Cell C9: Above Obstacle 2]
    C9 --- C10
    C5 --- C6
    C7 --- C9
```

> [!NOTE]
> The solid lines (e.g., `C1 - C2`) represent **primary adjacencies** that a path actually traverses; dashed lines (`.->`) represent alternate adjacencies that may be useful for alternative paths. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that students should be able to **sketch both the cell decomposition AND the corresponding adjacency graph** in a single answer.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        I1[Polygon Obstacles]
        I2[Workspace Bounds]
        I3[Start and Goal Poses]
    end
    subgraph Preprocess[Preprocessing Layer]
        P1[Vertex Extractor]
        P2[Slit Projector]
    end
    subgraph Decompose[Decomposition Layer]
        D1[Cell Identifier]
        D2[Adjacency Builder]
    end
    subgraph Plan[Planning Layer]
        PL1[Graph Search BFS or Dijkstra]
        PL2[Path Refiner]
    end
    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        O1[Cell Sequence]
        O2[Continuous Path]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    I3 --> D2
    D2 --> PL1
    PL1 --> O1
    O1 --> PL2
    PL2 --> O2
```
