$$ \text{pre}(\text{op}) \subseteq W $$

After execution, the world becomes:

$$ W' = (W \setminus \text{del}(\text{op})) \cup \text{add}(\text{op}) $$

**Example — "PickUp(Parcel)" operator:**

$$ \text{op} = (\text{PickUp}, \{ \text{AtRobot}, \text{AtParcel}, \text{HandsFree} \}, \{ \text{HoldingParcel} \}, \{ \text{HandsFree} \}) $$

> **Logic:** The robot can only pick up if it's at the parcel's location and has free hands. After pickup, it holds the parcel (added) but no longer has free hands (deleted).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hierarchical FSM State Count

For a HFSM with $n$ super-states each containing $m_i$ sub-states, the total reachable states are:

$$ \vert S_{total} \vert = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m_i} (k_{ij} - 1) + \ldots $$

where the "+1" accounts for the initial idle state and $k_{ij}$ are nested sub-sub-states. A two-level HFSM simplifies to:

$$ \vert S_{total} \vert = n + \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = n(1 + \bar{m}) $$

> **Logic:** Each super-state adds itself plus its sub-states. The product form comes from assuming a uniform mean $\bar{m}$ of sub-states per super-state.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Comparison

| Property | Deliberative | Reactive (Subsumption) | Hybrid (3-Layer) | Behavior-Based |
|----------|--------------|------------------------|------------------|----------------|
| **World Model** | Yes (explicit) | No | Yes (deliberative only) | Implicit / distributed |
| **Planning** | Global, optimal | None | Hierarchical | Schema fusion |
| **Reaction Speed** | Slow | Fast (ms) | Medium-High | Fast |
| **Optimality** | High | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Robustness in Dynamic Env.** | Low | High | High | High |
| **Computational Cost** | High (exponential in state space) | Low | Medium | Low-Medium |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Good | Good | Good |
| **KTU Exam Popularity** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Industrial Use** | Mission planning | Toy robots, Roomba | Service, AGVs | Research, ASIMO |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Path Cost Optimality (A* Admissibility Proof Sketch)

The A* algorithm (used in HLC for symbolic path planning) is admissible if and only if the heuristic $h(n)$ is admissible:

$$ h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall\, n \in \text{nodes} $$

where $h^*(n)$ is the **true cost-to-go**. The total estimated cost is:

$$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) $$

where $g(n)$ is the cost-so-far. A* is guaranteed to return the optimal path iff $h$ is **admissible** (and **consistent**: $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ for successors $n'$).

> **Proof sketch:** Suppose A* returns a non-optimal path with cost $f(n_{goal}) = g(n_{goal}) + h(n_{goal}) = g(n_{goal}) > C^*$. For any optimal path node $n^*$, $f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*) \leq C^* + 0 = C^*$. So $f(n^*) < f(n_{goal})$, contradiction with A*'s termination rule. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Layer (Hybrid) High Level Control Architecture

This is the **canonical KTU diagram** for Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mission["MISSION GOAL\nGo to Room 204 and Deliver Parcel"]
    HLC["HIGH LEVEL CONTROLLER\nDeliberative Planner\nWorld Model + Task Network"]
    MLC["MID LEVEL CONTROLLER\nSequencer + Path Planner\nFSM / Behavior Arbitrator"]
    LLC["LOW LEVEL CONTROLLER\nReactive Servo Layer\nMotor Velocity Control"]
    Robot["WHEELED MOBILE ROBOT\nDifferential Drive Base"]
    Sensors["LIDAR + IMU + Wheel Encoders + Camera"]
    WM["WORLD MODEL\nSemantic Map + SLAM"]
    KB["KNOWLEDGE BASE\nSTRIPS Operators + Rules"]
    Feedback["Sensory Feedback Loop"]
    
    Mission --> HLC
    HLC -->|Symbolic Plan| MLC
    MLC -->|Numeric Trajectory| LLC
    LLC -->|v and omega| Robot
    Robot -->|Odometry| Feedback
    Sensors -->|Raw Data| Feedback
    Feedback -->|Processed Perception| WM
    WM --> HLC
    KB --> HLC
    
    classDef hlcStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef mlcStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef llcStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hwStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dataStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    
    class HLC hlcStyle
    class MLC mlcStyle
    class LLC llcStyle
    class Mission,Robot hwStyle
    class WM,KB,Feedback,Sensors dataStyle
```

## 4.2 Subsumption Architecture — Layered Competence (Brooks)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LAYER 4: PLAN AND REASON\nMission level planning\nTask scheduling"]
    L3["LAYER 3: MAP BUILDING\nExplore and map environment"]
    L2["LAYER 2: EXPLORE\nWander to discover"]
    L1["LAYER 1: WANDER\nRandom direction motion"]
    L0["LAYER 0: AVOID CONTACT\nReflexive obstacle avoidance"]
    
    Suppress["Inhibitor Signals\nHigher silences Lower"]
    Replace["Suppression Signals\nHigher replaces Lower"]
    
    L4 -.->|Inhibit/Suppress| L3
    L3 -.->|Inhibit/Suppress| L2
    L2 -.->|Inhibit/Suppress| L1
    L1 -.->|Inhibit/Suppress| L0
    
    L4 -.->|Replace| L3
    L3 -.->|Replace| L2
    L2 -.->|Replace| L1
    
    L0 --> MotorOut["MOTOR OUTPUT\nWheel Velocities"]
    L1 --> MotorOut
    L2 --> MotorOut
    L3 --> MotorOut
    L4 --> MotorOut
    
    classDef topLayer fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef midLayer fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lowLayer fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    
    class L4 topLayer
    class L3,L2 midLayer
    class L1,L0 lowLayer
    class MotorOut outStyle
```

## 4.3 HLC Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HLC["HIGH LEVEL CONTROL MODULE"]
        WM["World Model\nSemantic Map"]
        KB["Knowledge Base\nSTRIPS Operators"]
        Planner["Mission Planner\nGenerate Task Network"]
        Scheduler["Task Scheduler\nTemporal + Resource Allocation"]
        Coordinator["Behavior Coordinator\nArbitration + Fusion"]
        Supervisor["Mission Supervisor\nFSM + Exception Handling"]
    end
    
    subgraph In["Inputs"]
        Goal["Mission Goal G"]
        Percept["Perception Updates"]
    end
    
    subgraph Out["Outputs"]
        SubTasks["Sub-Task Sequence\nto MLC"]
        Status["Status Reports\nto Operator"]
    end
    
    Goal --> Planner
    Percept --> WM
    WM --> Planner
    WM --> Supervisor
    KB --> Planner
    Planner --> Scheduler
    Scheduler --> Coordinator
    Coordinator --> SubTasks
    Supervisor --> Status
    Supervisor --> Coordinator
    
    classDef blockStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ioStyle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hlcbg fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:4px,color:#000
    
    class WM,KB,Planner,Scheduler,Coordinator,Supervisor blockStyle
    class Goal,Percept,SubTasks,Status ioStyle
```

## 4.4 HFSM (Hierarchical Finite State Machine) for a Delivery Robot

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Navigating : Receive Mission
    Navigating --> Charging : Battery Low
    Navigating --> Delivering : At Pickup Point
    Navigating --> Error : Localization Lost
    Charging --> Navigating : Battery Sufficient
    Delivering --> Returning : Parcel Delivered
    Delivering --> Error : Drop Failed
    Returning --> Idle : At Dock
    Error --> Idle : Manual Reset
    
    state Navigating {
        [*] --> Planning
        Planning --> Executing : Path Found
        Executing --> Planning : Replan Needed
        Executing --> Avoiding : Obstacle Detected
        Avoiding --> Executing : Clear Path
    }
    
    state Delivering {
        [*] --> Approaching
        Approaching --> Grasping : Within Reach
        Grasping --> Lifting : Grasp Success
        Lifting --> Transporting
        Lifting --> Grasping : Retry
        Transporting --> Releasing : At Destination
        Releasing --> [*]
    }
    
    classDef activeStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef compStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Behavior-Based Control — Motor Schema Fusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Goal["GOAL BEHAVIOR\nMove to target point"]
    Avoid["AVOID BEHAVIOR\nRepel from obstacles"]
    Stay["STAY ON PATH\nAttract to global path"]
    Explore["EXPLORE BEHAVIOR\nWander if lost"]
    
    WGoal["w1 = 0.7"]
    WAvoid["w2 = 1.0"]
    WStay["w3 = 0.5"]
    WExplore["w4 = 0.3"]
    
    V1["Vector V1"]
    V2["Vector V2"]
    V3["Vector V3"]
    V4["Vector V4"]
    
    Sum["SUMMATION\nV_total = sum w_i * V_i"]
    Norm["NORMALIZATION\nScale to V_max"]
    Cmd["OUTPUT\nv and omega"]
    
    Goal --> V1
    Avoid --> V2
    Stay --> V3
    Explore --> V4
    
    WGoal --> V1
    WAvoid --> V2
    WStay --> V3
    WExplore --> V4
    
    V1 --> Sum
    V2 --> Sum
    V3 --> Sum
    V4 --> Sum
    
    Sum --> Norm
    Norm --> Cmd
    
    classDef behStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef weightStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vecStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    
    class Goal,Avoid,Stay,Explore behStyle
    class WGoal,WAvoid,WStay,WExplore weightStyle
    class V1,V2,V3,V4,Sum vecStyle
    class Norm,Cmd outStyle
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define High Level Control in a mobile robot. List its primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** High Level Control (HLC) is the topmost layer of a mobile robot's hierarchical control system, responsible for **decomposing abstract mission goals into sequences of sub-tasks** using a symbolic world model and task-planning algorithms.

**Primary Functions (1.5 marks):**
1. **Mission Planning** — converting high-level goals into executable task networks.
2. **World Modeling** — maintaining a semantic map and knowledge base.
3. **Task Scheduling** — assigning time, resources, and order to sub-tasks.
4. **Behavior Coordination** — arbitrating among competing behaviors.
5. **Reasoning** — applying logical rules to handle exceptions and replan.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between Deliberative, Reactive, and Hybrid control architectures with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Architecture | Key Trait | Example |
|--------------|-----------|---------|
| **Deliberative** | Sense-Plan-Act loop; uses world model; slow but optimal | Shakey the Robot (SRI, 1966) |
| **Reactive** | No planning; subsumption; fast reflexes | Roomba (iRobot) |
| **Hybrid** | Combines deliberative top + reactive bottom | Amazon Kiva warehouse robots |

(1 mark per architecture with correct example.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q. (a) Describe the Three-Layer (Hybrid) Control Architecture of a mobile robot with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of each layer. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Three-Layer Hybrid Architecture divides mobile robot control into three distinct horizontal strata, each operating on a different time-scale and abstraction level.

**Layer 1 — High Level (Deliberative) Controller:**
- **Role:** Accepts the abstract mission goal (e.g., "deliver parcel") and decomposes it into a symbolic plan $\Pi = [T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k]$ using world model and task-planner (STRIPS / A*).
- **Time scale:** Seconds to minutes.
- **Implementation:** Production rules, planning graphs, HFSM.

**Layer 2 — Mid Level (Sequencer) Controller:**
- **Role:** Converts the symbolic plan into a **geometric trajectory** and a **time-ordered sequence of behaviors**. Monitors execution, handles re-planning triggers.
- **Time scale:** 100 ms to seconds.
- **Implementation:** FSMs, Petri nets, behavior trees.

**Layer 3 — Low Level (Reactive / Servo) Controller:**
- **Role:** Real-time closed-loop control of wheel velocities to track the trajectory and avoid immediate obstacles. Outputs $(v, \omega)$.
- **Time scale:** 1 ms to 100 ms.
- **Implementation:** PID controllers, potential fields, subsumption.

**Advantages:** Combines intelligence of deliberative with reflexes of reactive. **Disadvantages:** Complexity in layer interfacing.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for layered roles, 2 marks for advantages/disadvantages.)*

---

**(b) Explain the Subsumption Architecture proposed by Brooks. How does layer inhibition work? State any two limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (3 marks):** Rodney Brooks (1986) proposed the **Subsumption Architecture** as a contrast to traditional deliberative AI. It organizes robot control into **hierarchical layers of competence** $L_0 \prec L_1 \prec \ldots \prec L_n$, where each layer represents an increasingly sophisticated behavior. Lower layers handle urgent reflexes (e.g., *avoid contact*), while higher layers handle complex missions (e.g., *map building, planning*). No central world model is required.

**Layer Inhibition (2 marks):** Higher layers can **suppress** the outputs of lower layers via two mechanisms:
- **Inhibition Signal** $I_{k \to k-1}$: Silences the lower layer completely.
- **Suppression Signal** $S_{k \to k-1}$: Replaces the lower output with a substitute value from the higher layer.

$$ L_{k-1}^{out} = (1 - I_{k \to k-1}) L_{k-1}^{native} + I_{k \to k-1} L_{k}^{override} $$

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **No Optimality** — Layered reflexes are greedy and cannot guarantee globally optimal paths.
2. **Poor Scalability of Behavior Arbitration** — Designing inhibition logic for many layers becomes unmanageable.
3. **No Symbolic Reasoning** — Cannot handle abstract goals or temporal logic.
4. **Difficult Debugging** — Interaction between layers is hard to trace.

*(7 marks: 3 concept + 2 inhibition + 2 limitations.)*

---

### **📗 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) With a suitable example, explain the Motor Schema Theory of behavior-based robot control. Derive the equation for the total behavior output. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (3 marks):** Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1989) is a behavior-based control paradigm where multiple **schemas** (behaviors) run in parallel, each producing a **velocity vector** in 2D space. The robot's actual motion is the **weighted sum** of all active schema outputs. Each schema encapsulates a perceptual-motor coupling (e.g., *move-to-goal*, *avoid-obstacle*, *stay-on-path*).

**Derivation of Total Output (3 marks):**

For $N$ active schemas, the raw vector sum is:

$$ \vec{V}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot \vec{V}_i $$

where $\vec{V}_i = (v_{ix}, v_{iy})$ is the velocity of schema $i$, and $w_i \in [0, 1]$ is its gain.

To prevent saturation beyond the robot's max velocity $V_{max}$, the output is normalized:

$$ \vec{V}_{cmd} = V_{max} \cdot \frac{\vec{V}_{total}}{\vert \vec{V}_{total} \vert + \epsilon} $$

The scalar linear velocity is $v = \vec{V}_{cmd} \cdot \hat{x}_r$ and angular velocity $\omega$ from the heading error.

**Example (1 mark):** A warehouse robot combining *go-to-shelf* ($w=0.8$), *avoid-human* ($w=1.0$), and *stay-in-aisle* ($w=0.6$) schemas.

*(7 marks: 3 concept + 3 derivation + 1 example.)*

---

**(b) Define a Finite State Machine for the high-level controller of a delivery robot. Draw the state transition diagram for states {Idle, Pickup, Navigate, Deliver, Return, Charge, Error}. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Finite State Machine (FSM) is a computational model with a finite set of **states** $S$, **events** $E$, and a **transition function** $\delta: S \times E \to S$. In HLC, FSMs represent mission-level behavior: the robot occupies exactly one state at any time, and transitions are triggered by perception events or internal conditions.

**States Defined (2 marks):**
- $s_0$ = *Idle* — robot at dock, awaiting mission
- $s_1$ = *Navigate* — moving to pickup/destination
- $s_2$ = *Pickup* — grasping the parcel
- $s_3$ = *Deliver* — releasing the parcel
- $s_4$ = *Return* — navigating back to dock
- $s_5$ = *Charge* — recharging battery
- $s_6$ = *Error* — exception handling

**State Transition Table (3 marks):**

| Current State | Event / Condition | Next State |
|---------------|-------------------|------------|
| Idle | Mission received | Navigate |
| Navigate | At pickup point | Pickup |
| Pickup | Grasp success | Navigate (to destination) |
| Navigate | At destination | Deliver |
| Deliver | Release success | Return |
| Return | At dock | Idle |
| Any | Battery low | Charge |
| Charge | Battery full | Idle |
| Any | Localization lost | Error |
| Error | Manual reset | Idle |

*(7 marks: 2 definition + 2 state list + 3 transition table.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 4 — High Level Control:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing HLC with MLC** — Many students incorrectly describe *path planning* as "high level." Path planning is **Mid Level**. HLC deals with **symbolic task planning**, not geometric paths.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the World Model** — In 3-mark definition questions, omitting *"maintains a world model"* costs 0.5 marks. The world model is the **defining feature** of deliberative HLC.
> 
> 3. **Subsumption Direction Confusion** — Saying *"lower layers inhibit higher layers"* is **backwards**. Higher layers inhibit lower. The $\prec$ symbol indicates priority, not dependency direction.
> 
> 4. **Mixing STRIPS Variables** — In STRIPS, $\text{pre}, \text{add}, \text{del}$ are **sets**, not lists. Saying *"add-list"* loosely is fine, but writing them as if order matters will lose marks.
> 
> 5. **Skipping Normalization in Motor Schema** — Writing $\vec{V} = \sum w_i \vec{V}_i$ without the normalization step loses 1 mark in derivations. The **normalization to $V_{max}$ is essential**.
> 
> 6. **No Example in 7-Mark Questions** — Every 7-mark sub-part in KTU ESE **must** have at least one real-world example (e.g., *Roomba, Kiva, Mars Rover*). Skipping it costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 4: High Level Control**

### ✅ Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- ☐ HLC = **deliberative, symbolic, top-of-hierarchy** control
- ☐ HLC outputs **symbolic sub-tasks**, NOT wheel velocities
- ☐ Time scale of HLC: **seconds to minutes**
- ☐ World model is the **defining feature** of deliberative HLC
- ☐ Three-Layer Architecture = **HLC (deliberative) + MLC (sequencer) + LLC (reactive)**

### ✅ Architectures (Compare & Contrast)

- ☐ **Deliberative:** Slow, optimal, brittle. Sense-Plan-Act.
- ☐ **Reactive (Subsumption):** Fast, robust, no planning. Layered competence.
- ☐ **Hybrid:** Best of both. Industry standard.
- ☐ **Behavior-Based:** Parallel schemas, vector fusion.
- ☐ **HFSM / FSM:** Symbolic state-machine task sequencing.

### ✅ Formulas (Must Derive on Demand)

- ☐ Motor Schema: $\vec{V}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \vec{V}_i$
- ☐ Normalized output: $\vec{V}_{cmd} = V_{max} \cdot \vec{V}_{total} / (\vert \vec{V}_{total} \vert + \epsilon)$
- ☐ Subsumption inhibition: $L_{lo}^{out} = (1 - I_{hi \to lo}) L_{lo}^{native} + I_{hi \to lo} L_{hi}^{override}$
- ☐ STRIPS: $W' = (W \setminus \text{del}) \cup \text{add}$ if $\text{pre} \subseteq W$
- ☐ A* optimality: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ with $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$

### ✅ Brooks' Subsumption (Most Asked)

- ☐ Layers: $L_0$ *avoid contact* → $L_1$ *wander* → $L_2$ *explore* → $L_3$ *map* → $L_4$ *plan*
- ☐ Higher inhibits lower (not vice versa)
- ☐ **No world model** is the revolutionary claim
- ☐ **Drawback:** No optimality, no symbolic reasoning

### ✅ Real-World Examples (Mention in Every 7-Mark Answer)

- ☐ Deliberative: **Shakey the Robot (SRI, 1966)**
- ☐ Reactive: **Roomba vacuum cleaner**
- ☐ Hybrid: **Amazon Kiva warehouse robots, MiR, Mars Perseverance**
- ☐ Behavior-Based: **ASIMO (Honda)**
- ☐ HFSM: **Autonomous car driving modes** (Manual, Cruise, AutoPark, etc.)

### ✅ Examiner Triggers (High-Yield Phrases)

- ☐ Always mention the **world model** when describing HLC.
- ☐ Always mention **time scale** when differentiating layers.
- ☐ Always include **one industrial example** in 7-mark answers.
- ☐ Always state **advantages and limitations** of any architecture.
- ☐ Always distinguish **symbolic** (HLC) from **numeric** (LLC) outputs.

### 🎯 Final Exam Mantra

> *"High Level Control is the BRAIN of the robot — it knows the WHY and the WHAT. The MLC and LLC handle the HOW. Never confuse the three."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternative Control Formalisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alternative Control Formalisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Control Formalism" in Mobile Robotics?

A **control formalism** is the mathematical and architectural framework that defines *how a mobile robot maps its sensory inputs and internal state into physical actuator commands* (wheel velocities, steering angles, etc.). It is the "decision-making law" that closes the perception-to-action loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> A *control formalism* is a structured representation of the mapping
> $\mathbf{u} = f(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{E})$
> where $\mathbf{u}$ is the control vector (motor commands), $\mathcal{S}$ is the sensed/estimated state, $\mathcal{G}$ is the goal/task, and $\mathcal{E}$ is the environment representation. "Alternative control formalisms" refer to *non-classical* (non-deterministic-planner-based) approaches that bypass the heavy "sense → model → plan → act" (SMPA) pipeline in favor of tighter, more reactive, or biologically inspired strategies.

## 1.2 Why "Alternative"? The Problem with Classical Control

The classical deliberative paradigm (STRIPS, A*, RRT on a global map) suffers from three weaknesses:
1. **Computational latency** — global planning on large maps can take seconds.
2. **World-model dependency** — any discrepancy between the map and reality can be catastrophic.
3. **Lack of reactivity** — cannot respond to dynamic obstacles in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway**
> Alternative formalisms trade *global optimality* and *deliberation* for *real-time reactivity*, *biological plausibility*, *robustness to sensor/actuator noise*, and *computational tractability*.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Analogy — Driving a Car in Kerala Traffic 🚗**
> Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Thiruvananthapuram.
> - **Deliberative (classical)** approach: study the entire map of Kerala, plan an optimal route, and drive it. But what if a lorry suddenly cuts in front of you in Aroor? You react instantly — swerve, brake — *without* re-planning the whole Kerala map.
> - That *brake-and-swerve reflex* is what alternative control formalisms encode directly into the robot. They are the "instincts" of the machine.

The principal alternative control formalisms covered in Module 4 are:
1. **Behavior-Based Control (Subsumption Architecture — Brooks, 1986)**
2. **Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)**
3. **Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1989)**
4. **Hybrid Deliberative / Reactive Architectures**
5. **Finite State Machines (FSMs)** for high-level task switching

Each of these offers a different philosophy for converting sensor data into motion commands while deliberately avoiding the bottlenecks of the classical SMPA pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Behavior-Based Control — Subsumption Architecture (Rodney A. Brooks, 1986)

### 2.1.1 Core Philosophy
Brooks' seminal paper *"A Robust Layered Control System for a Mobile Robot"* (IEEE JRA, 1986) argued that intelligence can be built from the **bottom-up** by layering simple, task-achieving behaviors. Each layer is a *finite state machine* that subsumes (overrides or inhibits) the layer below it.

### 2.1.2 The Layers (for a typical indoor mobile robot)
- **Level 0 (lowest):** *Avoid obstacles* — sonar-based reflexive avoidance.
- **Level 1:** *Wander* — random heading changes when no goal.
- **Level 2:** *Build a map* — incrementally construct a grid.
- **Level 3:** *Travel to a named place* — use the map.
- **Level 4:** *Notice changes in the environment*.
- **Level 5 (highest):** *Reason about objects and pursue goals.*

### 2.1.3 Operating Mechanism
Each layer continuously produces *output* (motor commands) and *inhibits* (overrides) lower layers only when its trigger condition is met. There is **no central world model** and **no central planner** — intelligence *emerges* from the interaction of the layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Principle:** A subsumption layer does *not* call the layer below it. Higher layers can only **suppress** (`S`) or **inhibit** (`I`) the outputs of lower layers. This guarantees *real-time* response because each layer reads sensors and writes actuators directly.

### 2.1.4 Strengths & Weaknesses
| Aspect | Subsumption | Classical SMPA |
|---|---|---|
| World model | None / minimal | Required |
| Real-time | Yes (parallel FSMs) | Often slow |
| Robustness | High (graceful degradation) | Low (fails on map mismatch) |
| Global optimality | None | Yes |
| Programming complexity | High (wiring of S/I nets) | Moderate |

## 2.2 Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)

The robot moves in an *artificial potential field* $\mathcal{U}(q)$, where $q = (x, y)$ is the robot position. The goal exerts an **attractive** potential and obstacles exert **repulsive** potentials. The negative gradient of $\mathcal{U}$ gives the *force* on the robot, which is converted into a velocity command.

### 2.2.1 Attractive Potential
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{att}} \,\bigl(d(q, q_{\text{goal}})\bigr)^{2}$$

where $d(q, q_{\text{goal}}) = \sqrt{(x - x_{g})^{2} + (y - y_{g})^{2}}$ is the Euclidean distance to the goal and $k_{\text{att}}$ is a positive gain (N/m).

### 2.2.2 Repulsive Potential
$$U_{\text{rep}}(q) = 
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{rep}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right)^{2}, & 0 < \rho(q) \le \rho_{0} \\
0, & \rho(q) > \rho_{0}
\end{cases}$$

where $\rho(q)$ is the distance to the nearest obstacle, $\rho_{0}$ is the influence radius, and $k_{\text{rep}}$ is a positive gain (N·m).

### 2.2.3 Resultant Force
$$\mathbf{F}(q) = -\nabla \mathcal{U}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) - \nabla U_{\text{rep}}(q)$$

The robot velocity is then $\mathbf{v} = \alpha \mathbf{F}(q)$ for some scaling factor $\alpha$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Minima Problem:** A fundamental flaw of pure potential fields is the *local minimum trap* — the robot can settle at a point where $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{0}$ but $q \ne q_{\text{goal}}$ (e.g., equidistant from two obstacles and the goal). KTU students are expected to name this issue and its remedies (random walk, virtual obstacle, harmonic functions by Connolly & Grupen).

## 2.3 Motor Schema Theory (Arkin, 1989)

Ronald Arkin's motor schemas (Georgia Tech) generalize potential fields into *vectors* that represent elementary behaviors, each returning a *velocity vector* in the robot's workspace:

$$\mathbf{v}_{i} = \mathcal{B}_{i}(S, P, G, E)$$

where $\mathcal{B}_{i}$ is schema $i$ (e.g., *move-to-goal*, *avoid-obstacle*, *stay-on-path*, *noise*).

All schemas are *summed* (or weighted-summed) to produce the final command:

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma_{i}\, \mathbf{v}_{i}$$

where $\gamma_{i} \in [0, 1]$ is the *gain* (or *attention*) of schema $i$. Unlike subsumption's hard suppression, motor schemas use *soft summation* — making the system more graceful and biologically analogous to frog or insect nervous systems.

## 2.4 Hybrid (Three-Layer) Architectures

A pragmatic compromise: a **deliberative** planner (top layer) feeds a *qualitative* map of waypoints to a **sequencing/executive** middle layer, which activates a **reactive** controller (bottom layer) that uses potential fields, schemas, or subsumption. The classical examples are:
- **AuRA (Autonomous Robot Architecture)** — Arkin.
- **SFX (Sensor-Fusion eXecution)** — Murphy.
- **CLARAty** — JPL / Carnegie Mellon.

## 2.5 Finite State Machine (FSM) Control

High-level robot behavior is encoded as an FSM with discrete states (`GOTO_DOOR`, `OPEN_DOOR`, `ENTER_ROOM`) and transitions triggered by sensor events. Useful for *mission scripting* and pairing with subsumption or schemas as the executive layer.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only** place you should record these equations when revising — examiners in KTU frequently test these.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attractive potential | $U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} d^{2}$ | J (joules) | $k_{\text{att}}$ in N/m |
| 2 | Repulsive potential | $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} (1/\rho - 1/\rho_{0})^{2}$ | J | Valid only if $\rho \le \rho_{0}$ |
| 3 | Force on robot | $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} - \nabla U_{\text{rep}}$ | N (newtons) | Negative gradient |
| 4 | Attractive force (magnitude) | $F_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} \, d$ | N | Linear in distance |
| 5 | Repulsive force (magnitude) | $F_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}}\right) \dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}}$ | N | $1/\rho^{2}$ blow-up near obstacles |
| 6 | Motor schema summation | $\mathbf{v}_{\text{total}} = \sum \gamma_{i} \mathbf{v}_{i}$ | m/s | Soft blending |
| 7 | Subsumption message | output $= \mathcal{L}_{i}(t)$, $S$ and $I$ signals | binary | Hard override only |
| 8 | FSM transition | $s_{k+1} = \delta(s_{k}, e_{k})$ | — | Deterministic if $\delta$ is total |
| 9 | Hybrid cycle | Deliberate $\rightarrow$ Sequence $\rightarrow$ React | — | Period: tens of ms |
| 10 | Gain tuning rule | $k_{\text{rep}} \rho_{0}^{2} \approx k_{\text{att}} d_{\max}^{2}$ | — | Prevents overshoot at start |

> **Engineering Utility:** Potential fields are used today in real ADAS lane-keeping and parking-assist systems; subsumption ideas live on in modern **ROS 2 BehaviorTree** libraries (`BehaviorTree.CPP`); motor schema summation is mathematically identical to **vector-field histogram (VFH)** obstacle avoidance used in robot vacuum cleaners (iRobot Roomba).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Vector from the Attractive Potential

We start from the standard quadratic attractive potential and take the 2-D gradient.

**Step 1.** Define the goal position vector in the world frame:
$$\mathbf{q}_{g} = (x_{g},\, y_{g})^{T}$$

**Step 2.** Define the current robot position vector:
$$\mathbf{q} = (x,\, y)^{T}$$

**Step 3.** Express the squared Euclidean distance (squared to keep the math clean):
$$d^{2}(q, q_{g}) = (x - x_{g})^{2} + (y - y_{g})^{2}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute into the attractive potential definition:
$$U_{\text{att}}(x, y) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \bigl[(x - x_{g})^{2} + (y - y_{g})^{2}\bigr]$$

**Step 5.** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \cdot 2 (x - x_{g}) = k_{\text{att}} (x - x_{g})$$

**Step 6.** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$ in the same way:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y} = k_{\text{att}} (y - y_{g})$$

**Step 7.** Form the gradient (column vector):
$$\nabla U_{\text{att}}(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} k_{\text{att}} (x - x_{g}) \\ k_{\text{att}} (y - y_{g}) \end{pmatrix} = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{g})$$

**Step 8.** Apply the negative-gradient rule to get the attractive force:
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q}_{g} - \mathbf{q})$$

**Step 9.** Magnitude interpretation: the force is *linear* in distance to the goal. As the robot reaches $q_{g}$, the magnitude vanishes smoothly — desirable for stable stop.

**Step 10.** Engineer's note: because the force is unbounded as $d \to \infty$, the literature sometimes uses a *conical* potential (a constant $\varepsilon$ for $d > d_{c}$) to cap the far-field force and prevent large velocity transients.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Repulsive Force for a Single Circular Obstacle

**Step 1.** Define the obstacle centre and its radius:
$$\mathbf{q}_{o} = (x_{o}, y_{o})^{T}, \quad r_{o}$$

**Step 2.** Define the *minimum* (clearance) distance from the robot to the obstacle boundary:
$$\rho = \sqrt{(x - x_{o})^{2} + (y - y_{o})^{2}} - r_{o}$$

If $\rho > 0$ the robot is outside; if $\rho < 0$ it has penetrated.

**Step 3.** Define the influence radius $\rho_{0}$ beyond which the obstacle is ignored. Write the repulsive potential:
$$U_{\text{rep}} = 
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{\text{rep}} \bigl( \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}} \bigr)^{2}, & 0 < \rho \le \rho_{0} \\
0, & \rho > \rho_{0}
\end{cases}$$

**Step 4.** To differentiate, let $u = 1/\rho$ and $v = 1/\rho_{0}$. Then $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} (u - v)^{2}$.

**Step 5.** Chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial x} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( u - v \right) \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}$$

**Step 6.** Compute $\partial \rho / \partial x$:
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_{o}}{\sqrt{(x - x_{o})^{2} + (y - y_{o})^{2}}} = \frac{x - x_{o}}{\rho + r_{o}}$$

**Step 7.** Combine:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial x} = -k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{x - x_{o}}{\rho + r_{o}}$$

**Step 8.** Vectorise the two axes and assemble the gradient:
$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = -k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2} (\rho + r_{o})}\, (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{o})$$

**Step 9.** Apply the negative-gradient rule:
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2} (\rho + r_{o})}\, (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{o})$$

**Step 10.** Interpretations:
- The direction $(\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{o})$ is the *unit vector* pointing **from the obstacle to the robot** — i.e., the force pushes the robot *away*.
- As $\rho \to 0$ the term $1/\rho^{2}$ dominates, so the force grows without bound — a *safety barrier*.
- As $\rho \to \rho_{0}$ the bracket $(1/\rho - 1/\rho_{0}) \to 0$, so the repulsive influence vanishes continuously.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Force and Velocity Command for a Differential-Drive Robot

**Step 1.** Sum the two force fields:
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} + \mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q}_{g} - \mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the polar components of the force:
$$F_{x} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{i}}, \quad F_{y} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{j}}$$

**Step 3.** Convert to the desired heading angle (the force direction *is* the heading we want):
$$\theta_{\text{des}} = \operatorname{atan2}(F_{y}, F_{x})$$

**Step 4.** Compute the desired translational speed (saturate for safety):
$$v_{\text{des}} = \min\bigl( \alpha \lVert \mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} \rVert,\; v_{\max} \bigr)$$

**Step 5.** Compute the heading error relative to the robot's current heading $\theta$:
$$e_{\theta} = \theta_{\text{des}} - \theta$$

**Step 6.** Apply a simple proportional heading controller (or use the exact unicycle-to-arc mapping):
$$\omega = K_{p} \cdot e_{\theta}, \quad v = v_{\text{des}} \cdot \cos(e_{\theta})$$

**Step 7.** Issue the wheel velocities for a differential drive of wheel-base $L$:
$$v_{R} = v + \tfrac{L}{2}\, \omega, \quad v_{L} = v - \tfrac{L}{2}\, \omega$$

**Step 8.** Sanity check at the goal: as $\mathbf{q} \to \mathbf{q}_{g}$, $F_{\text{att}} \to 0$ and the robot *naturally slows down* — the potential field approach is therefore self-terminating without an extra "goal-reached" routine.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Motor Schema Gain Update (Arkin's Perceptual Schema Coupling)

**Step 1.** Let each schema produce an *unweighted* perceptual vector $\mathbf{p}_{i}(S)$ from sensors.

**Step 2.** Apply a *gain* $\gamma_{i}$ that may be a function of perceptual state (e.g., a schema is "switched on" only if an obstacle is within range).

**Step 3.** Sum the weighted vectors:
$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \gamma_{i}(S)\, \mathbf{p}_{i}(S)$$

**Step 4.** Normalise for actuator saturation:
$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{cmd}} = \frac{v_{\max}}{\lVert \mathbf{v}_{\text{total}} \rVert + \varepsilon}\, \mathbf{v}_{\text{total}}$$

**Step 5.** Properties:
- *Soft* interaction — unlike subsumption, no hard suppression.
- *Linear superposition* — easy to add new behaviours.
- *Gain mediation* — gives the designer explicit control over the *attention* paid to each schema.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Potential Field)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This problem is a *classic KTU numerical* and typically appears as a 7-mark sub-part.

**Given.** Robot at $\mathbf{q} = (2, 3)$. Goal at $\mathbf{q}_{g} = (10, 8)$. Single obstacle at $\mathbf{q}_{o} = (4, 4)$ with $r_{o} = 1$. Gains: $k_{\text{att}} = 1$ N/m, $k_{\text{rep}} = 5$ N·m, $\rho_{0} = 4$ m. Compute the total force.

**Step 1.** Distance to goal:
$$d = \sqrt{(10-2)^{2} + (8-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2.** Attractive force:
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q}_{g} - \mathbf{q}) = (1) \cdot (8, 5) = (8,\, 5) \text{ N}$$

**Step 3.** Clearance to obstacle:
$$\rho = \sqrt{(2-4)^{2} + (3-4)^{2}} - 1 = \sqrt{4 + 1} - 1 = \sqrt{5} - 1 \approx 1.236 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4.** Since $\rho \le \rho_{0}$, repulsive force is active. Compute the scalar coefficient:
$$C = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2} (\rho + r_{o})}$$
$$C = 5 \cdot (0.809 - 0.25) \cdot \frac{1}{(1.236)^{2} \cdot 2.236} = 5 \cdot 0.559 \cdot \frac{1}{3.417} \approx 0.818$$

**Step 5.** Direction vector from obstacle to robot:
$$\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{o} = (-2, -1), \quad \lVert \cdot \rVert = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

**Step 6.** Repulsive force:
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = C \cdot (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{o}) = 0.818 \cdot (-2, -1) = (-1.636,\, -0.818) \text{ N}$$

**Step 7.** Total force:
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} = (8 - 1.636,\; 5 - 0.818) = (6.364,\, 4.182) \text{ N}$$

**Step 8.** Magnitude and heading:
$$\lVert \mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} \rVert = \sqrt{6.364^{2} + 4.182^{2}} \approx 7.62 \text{ N}$$
$$\theta_{\text{des}} = \operatorname{atan2}(4.182, 6.364) \approx 33.3^{\circ}$$

**Step 9.** Engineering conclusion: the robot is steered to the upper-right, but the obstacle successfully reduces the force magnitude and deflects the heading by ~6° from the direct line-of-sight angle of $\operatorname{atan2}(5, 8) \approx 32.0^{\circ}$. This is exactly the *reactive deflection* alternative control formalisms are designed to produce.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["LAYER 0: AVOID OBSTACLES\nSonar Reflex"]
    L1["LAYER 1: WANDER\nRandom Heading Drift"]
    L2["LAYER 2: BUILD MAP\nGrid Incremental"]
    L3["LAYER 3: TRAVEL TO PLACE\nMap Lookup"]
    L4["LAYER 4: NOTICE CHANGE\nDetect Novelty"]
    L5["LAYER 5: REASON\nPursue Object Goal"]
    SONAR["SONAR SENSORS"]
    ODOM["ODOMETRY"]
    MOTL["LEFT WHEEL MOTOR"]
    MOTR["RIGHT WHEEL MOTOR"]
    S10["S  Suppress"]
    S21["S  Suppress"]
    S32["S  Suppress"]
    S43["S  Suppress"]
    S54["S  Suppress"]
    I10["I  Inhibit"]
    I21["I  Inhibit"]
    I32["I  Inhibit"]
    I43["I  Inhibit"]
    I54["I  Inhibit"]
    SONAR --> L0
    ODOM --> L0
    L0 -- output --> MOTL
    L0 -- output --> MOTR
    L1 -- output --> L0
    L2 -- output --> L1
    L3 -- output --> L2
    L4 -- output --> L3
    L5 -- output --> L4
    L5 -. S .-> S54
    S54 -.-> L4
    L4 -. I .-> I43
    I43 -.-> L3
    L4 -. S .-> S43
    S43 -.-> L3
    L3 -. I .-> I32
    I32 -.-> L2
    L3 -. S .-> S32
    S32 -.-> L2
    L2 -. I .-> I21
    I21 -.-> L1
    L2 -. S .-> S21
    S21 -.-> L1
    L1 -. I .-> I10
    I10 -.-> L0
    L1 -. S .-> S10
    S10 -.-> L0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the **strict vertical hierarchy**: each higher layer can `S` (suppress) or `I` (inhibit) outputs of the layer directly below. Sensors feed only the lowest layer by default; higher layers gain access via the same bus.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Potential Field Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GOAL["GOAL POSITION\nq_g"]
    OBS["OBSTACLE POSITIONS\nq_o_i and rho_0"]
    SENSE["ROBOT SENSE\nq current pose"]
    UATTR["ATTRACTIVE POTENTIAL\nU_att = 0.5 k_att d^2"]
    UREP["REPULSIVE POTENTIALS\nU_rep_i piecewise"]
    UTOT["TOTAL POTENTIAL\nU = U_att + Sigma U_rep_i"]
    GRAD["GRADIENT OPERATOR\nnabla U"]
    F["RESULTANT FORCE\nF = - nabla U"]
    CONV["VELOCITY CONVERSION\nv = alpha F"]
    WHEEL["WHEEL VELOCITIES\nv_L  and  v_R"]
    GOAL --> UATTR
    OBS --> UREP
    SENSE --> UATTR
    SENSE --> UREP
    UATTR --> UTOT
    UREP --> UTOT
    UTOT --> GRAD
    GRAD --> F
    F --> CONV
    CONV --> WHEEL
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hybrid Three-Layer Architecture (AuRA / SFX Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MIS["MISSION SPECIFICATION\nGoal and Task List"]
    DEL["DELIBERATIVE LAYER\nGlobal Planner A* / RRT"]
    SEQ["SEQUENCER LAYER\nFSM Executive"]
    REA["REACTIVE LAYER\nPotential Field or Schema"]
    SEN["SENSORS\nLIDAR  Camera  IMU"]
    ACT["ACTUATORS\nWheel Motors"]
    MIS --> DEL
    DEL -- waypoints --> SEQ
    SEQ -- current behavior --> REA
    SEN --> DEL
    SEN --> REA
    REA --> ACT
    SEQ -- behavior status --> DEL
    REA -- exception events --> SEQ
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Motor Schema Summation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["MOVE TO GOAL SCHEMA\nv_1"]
    S2["AVOID OBSTACLE SCHEMA\nv_2"]
    S3["STAY ON PATH SCHEMA\nv_3"]
    S4["NOISE / EXPLORE SCHEMA\nv_4"]
    G1["GAIN gamma_1"]
    G2["GAIN gamma_2"]
    G3["GAIN gamma_3"]
    G4["GAIN gamma_4"]
    SUM["WEIGHTED SUM\nv_total = Sigma gamma_i v_i"]
    NORM["NORMALIZE\nv_cmd = v_max v_total divided by norm v_total"]
    CMD["FINAL VELOCITY COMMAND\nv_cmd  omega_cmd"]
    S1 --> SUM
    S2 --> SUM
    S3 --> SUM
    S4 --> SUM
    G1 --> SUM
    G2 --> SUM
    G3 --> SUM
    G4 --> SUM
    SUM --> NORM
    NORM --> CMD
```

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix of the Three Principal Formalisms

| Dimension | Subsumption | Potential Field | Motor Schema | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coordination | Hard S / I | Vector sum | Weighted sum | Layered |
| World model | None | None / implicit | None / implicit | Yes (top) |
| Real-time | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| Emergent intelligence | Yes | No | Partially | Yes |
| Local-minima issue | No | **Yes** | Reduced | Reduced |
| Programming style | AFSM network | Energy function | Vector fields | Hierarchical |
| Typical platform | MIT Allen, Genghis | Stanford arms | Georgia Tech | NASA Mars rovers |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List any three distinguishing features of the Subsumption Architecture as proposed by Rodney Brooks. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Layered competence** — it is organised into a stack of layers, each adding a higher level of behavioural competence (e.g., avoid → wander → map → navigate).
2. **No central world model** — each layer accesses only the raw sensors it needs; there is no global map-building step in the inner loop.
3. **Task-achieving behaviours** — each layer is itself a complete robot control system producing actuator commands; higher layers can only *suppress* (`S`) or *inhibit* (`I`) the outputs of lower layers.
4. **Bottom-up design** — intelligence is *emergent* from the interaction of layers, not from a top-down planner. *(Any 3 of the above.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the mathematical expression for the attractive potential in the potential-field method and identify the role of $k_{\text{att}}$. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \bigl( d(q, q_{g}) \bigr)^{2}$$
Here $d(q, q_{g})$ is the Euclidean distance between the robot's current position $q$ and the goal $q_{g}$. The constant $k_{\text{att}}$ (units N/m) is a *positive gain* that scales the strength of the attractive force: a larger $k_{\text{att}}$ produces a stronger pull toward the goal and a faster (more aggressive) approach, while a smaller value yields a gentler trajectory.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Subsumption Architecture** of Brooks. Explain the roles of the *Suppress* (`S`) and *Inhibit* (`I`) signals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot is at $q = (1, 2)$. The goal is at $q_{g} = (6, 7)$. A circular obstacle of radius $r_{o} = 0.5$ m is centred at $q_{o} = (3, 3)$. The potential-field gains are $k_{\text{att}} = 1$ N/m, $k_{\text{rep}} = 3$ N·m, and the influence radius is $\rho_{0} = 3$ m. Compute the **total force** on the robot. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- Draw the layered hierarchy (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical diagram).
- Define each layer's purpose (avoid / wander / map / explore / reason).
- Explain the **AFSM** (augmented finite state machine) primitive: timer `T`, output `O`, input `I`, suppression `S`, inhibition `In`.
- Define **Suppress**: a higher layer can *replace* the output of a lower layer with its own (overrides).
- Define **Inhibit**: a higher layer can *block* the output of a lower layer for a fixed time (delays/preempts).
- Note that higher layers cannot call lower layers — they can only suppress or inhibit them. This is the property that guarantees real-time response.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Distance to the goal:**
$$d = \sqrt{(6-1)^{2} + (7-2)^{2}} = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Attractive force:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q}_{g} - \mathbf{q}) = 1 \cdot (5, 5) = (5, 5) \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Clearance to the obstacle boundary:**
$$\rho = \sqrt{(1-3)^{2} + (2-3)^{2}} - 0.5 = \sqrt{4 + 1} - 0.5 = \sqrt{5} - 0.5 \approx 1.736 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Is the obstacle active?** $\rho \le \rho_{0}$ (1.736 ≤ 3), so **yes**, the repulsive field is active.

**Step 5 — Repulsive scalar coefficient:**
$$C = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2} (\rho + r_{o})}$$
$$C = 3 \cdot (0.576 - 0.333) \cdot \frac{1}{(1.736)^{2} \cdot 2.236} = 3 \cdot 0.243 \cdot \frac{1}{6.736} \approx 0.108$$

**Step 6 — Direction from obstacle to robot:**
$$\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{o} = (-2, -1)$$

**Step 7 — Repulsive force:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = C \cdot (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{o}) = 0.108 \cdot (-2, -1) = (-0.216, -0.108) \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Total force:**
$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} = (5 - 0.216,\; 5 - 0.108) = (4.784,\, 4.892) \text{ N}$$

**Step 9 — Magnitude and heading:**
$$\lVert \mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}} \rVert = \sqrt{4.784^{2} + 4.892^{2}} \approx 6.84 \text{ N}$$
$$\theta_{\text{des}} = \operatorname{atan2}(4.892,\, 4.784) \approx 45.6^{\circ}$$

**Step 10 — Engineering interpretation:** The goal-beari­ng angle is $\operatorname{atan2}(5, 5) = 45^{\circ}$. The obstacle deflects the desired heading by $\approx 0.6^{\circ}$ away from it — a small but real effect, because the robot is still relatively far from the obstacle.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**(a)** Explain the **Motor Schema Theory** of Arkin. How does it differ from the pure potential-field approach? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A behaviour-based mobile robot is navigating through a corridor. List and briefly describe the **three layers of competence** that are typically present in a *subsumption-based* indoor mobile robot. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- *Definition:* A motor schema is a generic specification of a behaviour that produces a velocity vector $\mathbf{v}_{i}$ as a function of perceptual stimuli (e.g., `move-to-goal`, `avoid-obstacle`, `stay-on-path`, `noise`, `dodge`).
- *Soft combination:* All active schemas contribute a *weighted* vector; the final command is the normalised sum:
$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{cmd}} = \frac{v_{\max}}{\lVert \sum \gamma_{i} \mathbf{v}_{i} \rVert + \varepsilon} \sum_{i} \gamma_{i}\, \mathbf{v}_{i}$$
- *Differences from pure potential field:*
  1. Schema gains $\gamma_{i}$ allow **on/off switching** of a behaviour based on context (e.g., obstacle-avoidance activates only when $\rho < \rho_{0}$), whereas potential fields are always "on" with continuous gradients.
  2. Schemas may be **heterogeneous** (different mathematical forms) — not restricted to negative gradient of a scalar potential. This makes them *more expressive* than potential fields.
  3. Soft summation gives **graceful degradation**: a weak repulsive vector from a distant obstacle does not over-power a strong goal vector. Potential fields sometimes have *local minima* where the gradients exactly cancel; schema summation rarely produces such traps because gains are context-sensitive.
  4. Schemas are biologically motivated (frogs, insects); potential fields come from physics (mechanical work).

**Model Solution (b):**

The three canonical competence layers of a subsumption-based indoor mobile robot (Brooks, 1986) are:

1. **Layer 0 — Avoid Obstacles (lowest):** Uses real-time sonar/IR readings. If any reading drops below a threshold, the layer commands the wheels to turn away from the closest reading. This layer *never* stops running; it is the safety floor.

2. **Layer 1 — Wander:** When the goal is unknown or the robot is "bored", this layer issues random heading changes and small forward velocities so that the robot explores the environment and can recover from dead ends. It is *inhibited* by Layer 2 (and above) when a definite destination has been chosen.

3. **Layer 2 — Build a Map / Explore (or, in higher systems, *Travel to a Named Place*):** Uses odometry and long-range sensors to incrementally build a grid map of walls and free space. It can re-route the robot to cover un-mapped regions. (In the original 1986 paper, this was the *explore* layer; in the 1991 Allen robot, the next layer *Travel* was the topmost.)

> Each layer *subsumes* the layer below: e.g., when Layer 2 decides the robot should head to a particular unexplored area, it *suppresses* Layer 1's random wander, but Layer 0's obstacle avoidance is **never** overridden — it is the *innate safety reflex* of the robot.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (read this before the exam):**
> 1. **Confusing "suppress" and "inhibit"** in subsumption — *Suppress* replaces the lower-layer output; *Inhibit* blocks it for a fixed time. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\rho > \rho_{0}$ branch** of the repulsive potential — write the piecewise definition *explicitly*; KTU expects to see both cases.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract the obstacle radius $r_{o}$** when computing clearance $\rho$. Many students write $\rho = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2}}$ instead of $\rho = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2}} - r_{o}$. That single mistake costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Local-minimum trap:** Always mention that pure potential fields suffer from local minima and name *one* remedy (random walk, virtual obstacle, or harmonic function). Examiners explicitly look for this awareness.
> 5. **Motor schema ≠ potential field:** Students often write "schema = potential vector" — wrong, because the gain vector $\gamma_{i}$ is not present in a potential field. Stress the **soft, weighted sum** vs. **continuous gradient** distinction.
> 6. **Units:** Always write units (N, m, m/s). KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units in numerical problems.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Alternative Control Formalisms)**

- **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks 1986):** layered FSMs; world-model-free; *S* and *I* are the only inter-layer primitives; "intelligence without representation".
- **Potential Field (Khatib 1986):** $U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} d^{2}$; $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} (1/\rho - 1/\rho_{0})^{2}$ for $\rho \le \rho_{0}$; $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$; **local minima** is the canonical failure mode.
- **Motor Schema (Arkin 1989):** weighted vector sum $\mathbf{v}_{\text{cmd}} = \frac{v_{\max} \sum \gamma_{i} \mathbf{v}_{i}}{\lVert \sum \gamma_{i} \mathbf{v}_{i} \rVert + \varepsilon}$; *softer* than subsumption; context-sensitive gains.
- **Hybrid architectures** combine a slow deliberative planner with a fast reactive controller via a sequencing/executive FSM.
- **FSMs** are the executive glue: $s_{k+1} = \delta(s_{k}, e_{k})$; pair naturally with subsumption or schema layers.
- **Key equations to memorise verbatim:** attractive and repulsive potential expressions, gradient to force conversion, weighted schema summation, FSM transition rule.
- **Key design trade-off:** *reactivity vs. global optimality* — alternative formalisms sacrifice the second to maximise the first.
- **Standard numerical exam pattern:** Compute $\mathbf{F}_{\text{tot}}$ for a 2-D robot with one or two circular obstacles; expect the examiner to test your $\rho$ calculation, the piecewise definition of $U_{\text{rep}}$, and the final vector addition.
- **Bonus awareness:** Vector-Field Histogram (VFH) is the discretised industrial descendant of potential fields; ROS 2 `BehaviorTree.CPP` is the modern reincarnation of subsumption.
- **Real-world link:** Mars rovers (Sojourner, Spirit, Opportunity, Perseverance) use a hybrid CBA (Capability-Based Architecture) that descends directly from AuRA / CLARAty.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Human Robot Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Human Robot Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human-Robot Interface (HRI)** is the interdisciplinary field of study dedicated to understanding, designing, and evaluating robotic systems for use by, or with, humans. It encompasses the communication pathways, control modalities, sensory feedback mechanisms, and interaction protocols that enable a human operator to perceive the state of a robot, issue high-level or low-level commands, and receive feedback about task execution and environmental conditions.

In the context of **Mobile Robotics** (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEBRT522), the Human-Robot Interface is treated as the **bidirectional information conduit** that links the human cognition layer with the autonomous control layer of a mobile platform. Formally, it can be expressed as a tuple:

$$
\text{HRI} = \langle H, R, C, F, M \rangle
$$

Where:
- $H$ = Human operator model (cognitive state, skill level, situational awareness)
- $R$ = Robot state vector $\big( \vec{x}, \dot{\vec{x}}, \theta, \dot{\theta} \big)$
- $C$ = Communication channel characteristics (bandwidth, latency, noise)
- $F$ = Feedback rendering function (visual, auditory, haptic, multimodal)
- $M$ = Mapping/Command translation function (intent $\rightarrow$ actuator signals)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Driving a Car vs. Teleoperating a Mars Rover

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of HRI as the difference between *driving your own car* and *steering a Mars rover from a console on Earth*.
>
> - When you **drive your car**, you have *direct perception* through the windshield, *proprioceptive feedback* from the steering wheel and pedals, and *zero latency* between your brain and the wheels. This is the richest possible human-machine interface.
>
> - When you **teleoperate a Mars rover**, you receive **delayed, degraded video** from 200 million kilometers away, the steering wheel has a **noticeable time-lag** of up to **20 minutes round-trip**, and you must interpret the robot's state from a **2D monitor**. This is a *mediated* HRI.
>
> - In between these extremes are the spectrums of *supervisory control* (you set goals, the robot executes them) and *shared control* (you and the robot co-pilot the task).

The interface is essentially the **translation layer** that compresses, transmits, and re-renders the robot's reality into something the human mind can act upon, while simultaneously expanding the human's high-level intent into low-level robot commands.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Sheridan's 10 Levels of Automation (1992)** — the canonical scale for classifying human-robot control allocation.
- **Fitts's Law Index of Performance** — typically between **4 bits/s** (novice) and **12 bits/s** (expert) for pointing tasks.
- **Round-Trip Teleoperation Latency** — tolerable limit is approximately **< 300 ms** for stable manual control.
- **Haptic Refresh Rate** — minimum **1000 Hz (1 kHz)** for transparent force feedback.
- **Visual Frame Rate** — minimum **30 Hz** for situational awareness; **60 Hz** for teleoperation.
- **ASTM F45 (Standard Guide for HRI) and ISO 13482** — governing safety standards for personal care robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes *The Human-Robot Interface* as a critical component of the autonomous mobile robot stack — sitting between the *Perception* (Module 2) and *Navigation & Planning* (Module 3) layers, and the *Mission Execution* (Module 5) layer. The interface is what makes a robot *useful* to a human.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Paradigms of HRI

Human-Robot Interaction is classified into **three primary paradigms**, distinguished by *physical proximity* and *cognitive engagement* of the human:

#### 2.1.1 Remote Interaction (Teleoperation)
- **Distance**: Kilometers to millions of kilometers (e.g., space, deep-sea, nuclear).
- **Key constraint**: Communication latency and bandwidth.
- **Why it matters**: The human must rely on *synthetic feedback* (telemetry, video, haptic surrogates) because direct perception is impossible.
- **How it works**: The human issues commands through a *master controller*; the *slave robot* executes them; feedback is rendered back to the operator.

#### 2.1.2 Proximate Interaction
- **Distance**: Human and robot share the same physical workspace (collaborative robots / cobots).
- **Key constraint**: Safety, shared situational awareness, physical collision avoidance.
- **Why it matters**: ISO/TS 15066 mandates that *power and force limiting* must be enforced.
- **How it works**: The interface uses *gesture*, *speech*, and *direct manipulation*; sensors monitor human intent and proximity continuously.

#### 2.1.3 Social Interaction
- **Distance**: Co-present in a human social environment.
- **Key constraint**: Natural-language understanding, emotion recognition, social norms.
- **Why it matters**: Service and companion robots must follow human conversational and behavioral conventions.
- **How it works**: Multi-modal fusion of speech, gaze, facial expression, and gesture.

### 2.2 Communication Modalities (Sensory Channels)

| Modality | Information Bandwidth | Typical Use Case | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Visual (video, GUI, AR/HUD)** | Very high (~10⁷ bits/s raw) | Telepresence, status dashboards | Requires attention, susceptible to occlusion |
| **Auditory (speech, sonification, alerts)** | Moderate (~50–100 bits/s intelligible) | Hands-free alerts, command input | Noise interference, ambiguity |
| **Haptic (force, vibrotactile, thermal)** | High (~1000 Hz) | Force-feedback teleoperation, collision warning | Requires special hardware, fatigue |
| **Gestural (hand pose, body)** | Moderate | Proximate/Social HRI | Limited vocabulary, occlusions |
| **Brain-Computer (EEG, fNIRS)** | Low (~25 bits/s) | Assistive robotics for disabled users | Signal noise, training overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multi-modality wins:** The **multimodal advantage theorem** states that combining $N$ independent modalities reduces the probability of communication failure exponentially: $P_{\text{fail}} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_i$, where $p_i$ is the per-modality failure probability. Hence, *redundant* multi-modal interfaces are vastly more robust than single-channel interfaces.

### 2.3 Sheridan's 10 Levels of Automation (The "Gold Standard" Scale)

This is a **frequently tested topic** in KTU ESE. The human-robot control authority transitions from human-dominant (Level 1) to fully autonomous (Level 10):

| Level | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1** | Computer offers **no** assistance; human does it all. |
| **2** | Computer offers a **complete set** of decision/action alternatives. |
| **3** | Computer **narrows** the alternatives down to a few. |
| **4** | Computer suggests **one** alternative. |
| **5** | Computer **executes** that suggestion if the human approves. |
| **6** | Computer allows the human a **limited time** to veto before execution. |
| **7** | Computer **executes automatically**, then **notifies** the human. |
| **8** | Computer executes automatically and **only informs** the human **if asked**. |
| **9** | Computer executes automatically, **ignoring** the human. |
| **10** | Computer **decides everything**, acts autonomously, and ignores the human entirely. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common pitfall is confusing *levels of autonomy* with *levels of intelligence*. Autonomy refers to **who has the authority to act** (human or computer), while intelligence refers to **what the system knows**. A robot can be highly intelligent but still require human authorization (Level 5).

### 2.4 Control Architectures

Three canonical control architectures define how command authority is allocated:

1. **Direct (Manual) Teleoperation** — *Human in the loop, continuous control*. Every actuator command comes from the human. Best for unstructured, time-critical tasks; worst for fatigue and bandwidth.

2. **Shared Control** — *Human and robot co-pilot*. The human provides high-level intent; the robot assists with stability, obstacle avoidance, and compliant motion (e.g., power-steering analogy in cars).

3. **Supervisory Control** — *Human out of the loop, on the loop*. The human sets goals, monitors progress, and intervenes only on exception (e.g., autonomous Mars rovers such as *Curiosity* and *Perseverance*).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of literal `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Fitts's Law** (movement time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T$=move time, $D$=distance to target, $W$=target width, $a,b$=empirical constants | seconds |
| 2 | **Index of Performance (Throughput)** | $TP = \dfrac{\log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)}{T}$ | $TP$ = bits of information conveyed per second | bits/second |
| 3 | **Position error under teleop delay** | $e_p = v \cdot \tau_d$ | $e_p$=position error, $v$=robot velocity, $\tau_d$=one-way delay | meters |
| 4 | **Stability criterion (force-reflecting teleop)** | $\tau_d \cdot K_{fb} < 2$ (bilinear approx.) | $K_{fb}$=force-feedback gain | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Nyquist rate for haptic loop** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (typically $f_s = 1\,\text{kHz}$) | $f_s$=sample rate, $f_{\max}$=highest haptic content | Hz |
| 6 | **Haptic bandwidth budget** | $BW = f_s \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon-Hartley form, $S/N$=signal-to-noise ratio | bits/second |
| 7 | **Multi-modal reliability** | $R = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1-r_i)$ | $r_i$=reliability of modality $i$ | probability (0–1) |
| 8 | **Cognitive workload (NASA-TLX)** | $W = \dfrac{1}{6}\sum_{k=1}^{6} w_k$ | $w_k$=weight of 6 sub-scales | arbitrary (0–100) |
| 9 | **Mean time to intervention** | $MTTI = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_f}$ | $\lambda_f$=fault/intervention rate | seconds |
| 10 | **Communication channel capacity** | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$=bandwidth, $S/N$=signal-to-noise | bits/second |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations underpin the design of surgical robots (da Vinci), search-and-rescue robots, autonomous vehicles, and exoskeletons. For example, **Fitts's Law** is used to optimize the placement and size of HUD elements in an AR-based robot control interface so an operator can issue commands as quickly and accurately as possible.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fitts's Law Applied to HRI Pointing Tasks

> **Background:** Fitts's Law (1954) is an empirical model of human motor performance that is *ubiquitous* in HCI and HRI design. It predicts the time $T$ required to move a pointer (or robot end-effector under teleoperation) to a target of width $W$ at distance $D$.

#### Step 1: The Empirical Observation
Fitts observed that movement time increases linearly with an *index of difficulty* (ID) that depends logarithmically on the ratio $D/W$. The intuition is that a target twice as far away takes longer to reach, but a target twice as wide is twice as easy to hit.

$$
\text{ID} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{(bits)}
$$

The `+1` inside the log prevents division-by-zero and reflects Shannon's channel-capacity analogy (a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ can encode $\log_2(D/W + 1)$ bits of positional information).

#### Step 2: The Linear Empirical Model
Fitts then fitted a straight line between observed movement times and the index of difficulty:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \text{ID}
$$

#### Step 3: Combine Steps 1 and 2
Substituting the ID expression into the linear model yields Fitts's Law as it is universally written:

$$
\boxed{\,T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)\,}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute the Index of Performance
Dividing the ID by $T$ gives the *throughput*, which characterizes the human (or human-interface) channel capacity:

$$
\boxed{\,TP = \frac{1}{T}\,\log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \;\; \left[\frac{\text{bits}}{\text{s}}\right]\,}
$$

#### Step 5: Worked Example (HRI Tablet Touch-Screen)
Suppose a mobile robot's tablet interface has icon-targets of width $W = 1.2$ cm placed $D = 6$ cm from a typical touch point. With $a = 0.05\,\text{s}$ and $b = 0.10\,\text{s/bit}$:

$$
\text{ID} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{1.2} + 1\right) = \log_2(6) = 2.585 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
T = 0.05 + 0.10 \times 2.585 = 0.3085\,\text{s}
$$

$$
TP = \frac{2.585}{0.3085} \approx 8.38\,\text{bits/s}
$$

This throughput of ~8 bits/s is consistent with a moderately skilled human operator — strong evidence the interface is well designed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Position Error Accumulation in Delayed Teleoperation

> **Background:** In any teleoperation loop, the time delay $\tau_d$ between command issuance and visual feedback causes the human to *over-steer* or *lag-steer*. The accumulated position error must be quantified to set safe velocity limits.

#### Step 1: Define the Open-Loop Error
If a robot moves at constant velocity $v$ and the round-trip communication delay is $\tau_{rt}$, then in the time it takes for a corrective command to be applied, the robot has already traveled a distance:

$$
e_p = v \cdot \tau_{rt}
$$

#### Step 2: Replace Round-Trip with One-Way Delay
Often, the human's *mental model* updates only when visual feedback arrives, so the effective delay is one-way: $\tau_d = \tau_{rt}/2$ for symmetric links, giving:

$$
e_p = v \cdot \tau_d
$$

#### Step 3: Solve for Safe Velocity
Rearranging to find the maximum safe speed given a tolerated error $e_p^{\max}$ (say, $0.05$ m for indoor mobile robots):

$$
\boxed{\,v_{\max} = \frac{e_p^{\max}}{\tau_d}\,}
$$

#### Step 4: Numerical Example
For a deep-sea ROV with one-way acoustic delay $\tau_d = 1.5$ s and tolerable error $e_p^{\max} = 0.05$ m:

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{0.05}{1.5} = 0.0333\,\text{m/s} \approx 3.3\,\text{cm/s}
$$

This is consistent with operational ROV speeds, justifying the slow, deliberate teleoperation style of underwater vehicles.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multimodal Reliability Calculation

> **Background:** A safety-critical HRI (e.g., a surgical robot) must *never* fail to communicate a stop command. Using redundant modalities dramatically improves reliability.

#### Step 1: Define Per-Modality Failure Probability
Let $p_v, p_a, p_h$ be the failure probabilities of the visual, auditory, and haptic channels respectively. Suppose $p_v = 0.02$, $p_a = 0.05$, $p_h = 0.01$ for a particular stop-command link.

#### Step 2: Compute Joint Failure (Assuming Independence)
The probability that *all* modalities fail simultaneously is:

$$
P_{\text{fail}} = p_v \cdot p_a \cdot p_h = 0.02 \times 0.05 \times 0.01 = 10^{-5}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Reliability

$$
\boxed{\,R = 1 - P_{\text{fail}} = 1 - 10^{-5} = 0.99999\,}
$$

This **"five-nines" reliability** is the gold standard for safety-critical HRI.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping HRI Theory to Engineering Practice

| Engineering Question | Theoretical Tool | Equation Used | Resulting Design Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| How fast can a human teleoperate safely? | Fitts's Law | $T = a + b \log_2(D/W+1)$ | Set $W$ (button size) for target $T$ |
| What is the max safe speed under delay? | Position-error bound | $v_{\max} = e_p^{\max}/\tau_d$ | Set top-speed limiter |
| Will the haptic loop go unstable? | Bilinear-stability criterion | $\tau_d K_{fb} < 2$ | Adjust force-feedback gain |
| Is my interface safe enough? | Multimodal reliability | $R = 1 - \prod (1-r_i)$ | Add redundancy |
| How heavy is operator workload? | NASA-TLX | $W = \tfrac{1}{6}\sum w_k$ | Reduce task complexity |
| Is my video feed bandwidth-bound? | Shannon-Hartley | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Compress, increase SNR, or raise $B$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic HRI Architecture (Teleoperation)

The following block diagram shows the closed-loop information flow of a canonical bilateral teleoperation system. Every block is a functional unit, and the arrows indicate the direction of information/energy flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeH[Human Operator]
    nodeB[Human Brain Cognition]
    nodeM[Master Controller Joystick or Haptic Device]
    nodeE[Encoder of Master Pose]
    nodeT[Transmitter and Communication Channel]
    nodeD[Decoder and Demultiplexer]
    nodeC[Robot Controller Slave CPU]
    nodeA[Robot Actuators Motors and Drivers]
    nodeR[Mobile Robot in Environment]
    nodeSE[Robot Sensors Cameras LIDAR IMU]
    nodeFB[Feedback Encoder Channel]
    nodeDISP[Visual Display Monitor or HMD]
    nodeHAUD[Auditory Speaker or Buzzer]
    nodeHAP[Haptic Motor Force Feedback]
    nodeCON[Constraint Layer Safety Filter]

    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeCON
    nodeCON --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeSE
    nodeSE --> nodeFB
    nodeFB --> nodeDISP
    nodeFB --> nodeHAUD
    nodeFB --> nodeHAP
    nodeDISP --> nodeH
    nodeHAUD --> nodeH
    nodeHAP --> nodeH
    nodeHAP --> nodeM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The forward path (left to right) carries the human's intent through the master controller, communication channel, and safety filter down to the robot's actuators. The backward path (right to left, lower half) carries sensor data back through display, audio, and haptic devices to the human. The **Constraint Layer** is a modern addition that enforces ISO safety standards by overriding commands that would violate a safety envelope (e.g., force, speed, or proximity limits).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Levels of Autonomy in HRI

The following diagram visualizes how control authority migrates from the human (top) to the robot (bottom) as we descend through Sheridan's 10 levels of automation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeL1[Level 1 Manual Full Human Control]
    nodeL2[Level 2 Computer Suggests Alternatives]
    nodeL3[Level 3 Computer Narrows Choices]
    nodeL4[Level 4 Computer Suggests One Option]
    nodeL5[Level 5 Human Approves then Robot Acts]
    nodeL6[Level 6 Robot Acts Unless Vetoed in Time Window]
    nodeL7[Level 7 Robot Acts and Informs Human]
    nodeL8[Level 8 Robot Acts and Informs Only on Request]
    nodeL9[Level 9 Robot Acts and Ignores Human]
    nodeL10[Level 10 Full Autonomy Robot Decides Everything]

    nodeL1 --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4
    nodeL4 --> nodeL5
    nodeL5 --> nodeL6
    nodeL6 --> nodeL7
    nodeL7 --> nodeL8
    nodeL8 --> nodeL9
    nodeL9 --> nodeL10
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Human-Robot Communication Modalities and Their Bandwidths

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVIS[Visual Channel Monitors AR HUD]
    nodeAUD[Auditory Channel Speech Beeps]
    nodeHAP2[Haptic Channel Vibrotactile Force]
    nodeGES[Gestural Channel Hand Pose Gaze]
    nodeBCI[Brain Computer Interface EEG]

    nodeVIS --> nodeFUS[Fused Multimodal Command Stream]
    nodeAUD --> nodeFUS
    nodeHAP2 --> nodeFUS
    nodeGES --> nodeFUS
    nodeBCI --> nodeFUS
    nodeFUS --> nodeROB[Mobile Robot Actuator Stack]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design takeaway:** In real systems, *no single channel is sufficient*. A well-designed HRI for a mobile robot uses visual for primary status, auditory for urgent alerts, haptic for proximity/contact warnings, and gestural for natural proximate interaction. The fusion node is typically an AI-driven intent classifier (often a probabilistic Bayesian or deep-learning model).

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Supervisory Control Loop for an Autonomous Mobile Robot

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeOP[Human Supervisor Sets High Level Goal]
    nodePLAN[Mission Planner Decomposes Goal into Subtasks]
    nodeEXE[Robot Executes Subtasks Autonomously]
    nodeMON[Monitoring Subsystem Compares Progress to Plan]
    nodeEXC[Exception Detector Identifies Anomalies]
    nodeREQ[Requests Human Intervention If Needed]
    nodeUPD[Updates Operator Situation Awareness]

    nodeOP --> nodePLAN
    nodePLAN --> nodeEXE
    nodeEXE --> nodeMON
    nodeMON --> nodeEXC
    nodeEXC --> nodeREQ
    nodeREQ --> nodeOP
    nodeMON --> nodeUPD
    nodeUPD --> nodeOP
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the modern paradigm used by NASA's Mars rovers, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), and drone swarms. The human is "on the loop" rather than "in the loop".
