# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a servomechanism. List any two applications of servo control in mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

A **servomechanism** is an automatic closed-loop negative-feedback control system in which the output (position, velocity, or torque) is continuously compared with a reference input, and the error is used to drive a power amplifier that actuates the motor so as to reduce the error to zero. It is a power-amplifying device because the controller signal is small (milliwatts) while the motor delivers large mechanical power (watts to kilowatts).

**Two applications in mobile robotics:**
1. **Wheel velocity control** in differential-drive mobile robots — an encoder-mounted DC servo maintains commanded speed for straight-line motion and odometry.
2. **Steering actuation** in autonomous cars — a torque/position servo turns the steering rack to a precise angle commanded by the path planner.
3. *Joint position control* in robotic manipulator arms mounted on mobile bases.

> [!NOTE]
> For full **3 marks**, the student must write the *definition with the key phrase "negative feedback"*, give *two distinct robotic applications*, and *not* confuse a servomotor (plant) with a servomechanism (system).

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of feedback in a servo control system. Why is negative feedback preferred over positive feedback?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Feedback:** Feedback is the mechanism that *closes the loop*. The output (actual position or velocity) is sensed by a transducer — potentiometer, encoder, or tachogenerator — and returned to the summing junction. The error $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$ is computed and used to drive the actuator. Without feedback, the system is *open-loop* and cannot correct for disturbances, load variations, or parameter drift.

**Why Negative Feedback?**
1. **Stability** — Negative feedback subtracts the output from the input, automatically opposing any deviation. Positive feedback would *amplify* deviations, leading to saturation or oscillation.
2. **Disturbance rejection** — External loads (gravity, friction) are compensated without operator intervention.
3. **Reduced sensitivity to plant parameter variations** — The closed-loop response depends more on the feedback element than on the motor constants.
4. **Linearization and bandwidth extension** — Negative feedback makes a non-linear plant behave more linearly over a wider range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common board trap: students sometimes say "feedback increases accuracy" — that is correct, but the **deep reason** is the *error-correcting action* in the time domain, not the *gain* in the frequency domain. Phrase it that way for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (ESE):** *Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE full question from the given pair of 14-mark questions. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Choose **A** or **B**)

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC servomotor, clearly stating the assumptions. Obtain the simplified first-order form by neglecting armature inductance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the servomotor obtained in (a), a PD controller $G_c(s) = K_p + K_d s$ and an amplifier of gain $K_a = 4$ are used. The motor constants are $K_m = 20$ rad/s/V, $\tau_m = 0.2$ s. Find the values of $K_p$ and $K_d$ so that the closed-loop system has a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.707$ and natural frequency $\omega_n = 20$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Assumption 1:** Constant field flux (separately excited or permanent-magnet motor).

**Assumption 2:** The motor is operating in its linear region (no magnetic saturation).

**Assumption 3:** All initial conditions are zero for transfer-function analysis.

**Step 1:** Write the armature circuit equation using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law.

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

The back-EMF is proportional to shaft angular velocity:

$$e_b(t) = K_b \, \omega_m(t)$$

**Step 2:** Write the mechanical equation using Newton's second law for rotation.

$$J_m \frac{d\omega_m(t)}{dt} + B_m \omega_m(t) = T_m(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)$$

The load torque is neglected because the motor is on no-load for transfer-function derivation.

**Step 3:** Apply the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions.

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_b \Omega_m(s)$$

$$(J_m s + B_m) \Omega_m(s) = K_t I_a(s)$$

**Step 4:** From the second equation, $I_a(s) = (J_m s + B_m) \Omega_m(s) / K_t$. Substitute into the first equation.

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot \frac{(J_m s + B_m) \Omega_m(s)}{K_t} + K_b \Omega_m(s)$$

**Step 5:** Collect $\Omega_m(s)$ terms.

$$V_a(s) = \Omega_m(s) \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the transfer function.

$$\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_t K_b}$$

**Step 7 (Engineering Simplification):** For typical robotics servos, the electrical time constant $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$ is much smaller (often by a factor of 10 or more) than the mechanical time constant $\tau_m = J_m / B_m$. Therefore, $L_a$ is neglected, and the transfer function becomes first-order.

$$\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} \approx \frac{K_t}{R_a J_m \, s + (R_a B_m + K_t K_b)} = \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$$

where

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B_m + K_t K_b} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_m = \frac{R_a J_m}{R_a B_m + K_t K_b}$$

This is the **standard first-order velocity transfer function** used in all KTU numerical problems. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Write the open-loop transfer function with the PD controller.

$$G_{OL}(s) = (K_p + K_d s) \cdot K_a \cdot \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

Substitute $K_a = 4$, $K_m = 20$, $\tau_m = 0.2$:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{4 \times 20 \, (K_p + K_d s)}{s(0.2 s + 1)} = \frac{80 (K_p + K_d s)}{s(0.2 s + 1)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the negative feedback formula.

$$T(s) = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{80 (K_d s + K_p)}{s(0.2 s + 1) + 80 (K_d s + K_p)}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the denominator.

$$T(s) = \frac{80 K_d s + 80 K_p}{0.2 s^2 + s + 80 K_d s + 80 K_p} = \frac{80 K_d s + 80 K_p}{0.2 s^2 + (1 + 80 K_d) s + 80 K_p}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to standard second-order form by dividing by 0.2.

$$T(s) = \frac{400 K_d s + 400 K_p}{s^2 + \left(\frac{1 + 80 K_d}{0.2}\right) s + 400 K_p}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2$.

$$\omega_n^2 = 400 K_p \implies 20^2 = 400 K_p \implies K_p = 1.0$$

$$2\zeta \omega_n = \frac{1 + 80 K_d}{0.2} = 5 (1 + 80 K_d)$$

With $\zeta = 0.707$ and $\omega_n = 20$:

$$2 \times 0.707 \times 20 = 28.28 = 5 (1 + 80 K_d)$$

$$1 + 80 K_d = 5.656 \implies 80 K_d = 4.656 \implies K_d = 0.0582 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

**Step 6:** Final answer — $K_p = 1.0$ and $K_d \approx 0.058$ V·s/rad. **(7 marks awarded)**

**Verification:** Substitute back to check the closed-loop poles. The denominator becomes $s^2 + 28.28 s + 400 = 0$, giving roots $s = -14.14 \pm j \, 14.14$, confirming $\zeta = 0.707$.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop position servo system. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity-feedback position servo has the open-loop transfer function $G_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+5)(s+10)}$. Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

```
  R(s) ──►(+)──────►[ Gc(s) ]──►[ Ka ]──►[ K_m / (s(τm s+1)) ]──► C(s)
           ▲(-)                                                         │
           │                                                           │
           │                                                           │
           └────────────────────[ H(s)=1 ]◄──────────────────────────┘
```

**Function of each block:**

1. **Reference Input $R(s)$** — The desired position, typically a step or ramp function. **(1 mark)**
2. **Summing Junction** — Computes the error $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$ by comparing reference and feedback. **(1 mark)**
3. **Controller $G_c(s)$** — Compensates the error. Usually PID. **(1 mark)**
4. **Power Amplifier $K_a$** — Boosts the controller's low-power signal to a level that can drive the motor armature. **(1 mark)**
5. **DC Servomotor $G_m(s)$** — Plant that converts electrical energy to mechanical rotation with transfer function $K_m / [s(\tau_m s + 1)]$. **(1 mark)**
6. **Feedback Sensor $H(s) = 1$** — Potentiometer/encoder that senses the output and returns it to the summing junction. **(1 mark)**
7. **Output $C(s)$** — The actual mechanical position of the load. **(1 mark)**

> **Working:** A reference $R(s)$ is applied. The error $E(s)$ is computed, amplified, and fed to the motor. The motor drives the load. The sensor measures the output and feeds it back. The loop continues until $E(s) \to 0$, at which point $C(s) \approx R(s)$.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Write the closed-loop characteristic equation $1 + G_{OL}(s) = 0$.

$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+5)(s+10)} = 0$$

$$s(s+5)(s+10) + K = 0$$

**Step 2:** Expand the cubic.

$$s(s^2 + 15s + 50) + K = 0$$

$$s^3 + 15 s^2 + 50 s + K = 0$$

**Step 3:** Build the Routh array.

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 50 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | 15 | $K$ | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{15 \times 50 - 1 \times K}{15}$ | 0 | |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | | |

**Step 4:** Simplify the $s^1$ entry.

$$\frac{750 - K}{15}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Routh stability criterion — all first-column entries must be strictly positive.

$$1 > 0 \;\; \checkmark$$

$$15 > 0 \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\frac{750 - K}{15} > 0 \implies K < 750$$

$$K > 0$$

**Step 6:** Combine the inequalities.

$$\boxed{0 < K < 750}$$

**Step 7 (Interpretation):** For marginal stability, $K = 750$. The closed-loop system is stable only for $K$ strictly between 0 and 750. Beyond $K = 750$, two roots cross into the right-half plane and the servo oscillates unboundedly. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Servo Control Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "servomotor" with "servomechanism"** — A servomotor is a *component* (the DC motor). A servomechanism is the *whole system* including feedback. (1–2 marks lost)
> 2. **Forgetting units** — $K_p$ is dimensionless, $K_i$ has units of 1/s, $K_d$ has units of seconds. Always quote units.
> 3. **Sign error in feedback loop** — A positive feedback at the summing junction (instead of negative) destabilises almost every servo. Always show the minus sign.
> 4. **Neglecting back-EMF $K_t K_b$ term** — In mobile robotics, this term is *not* negligible and must be retained in the denominator.
> 5. **Routh array sign** — A single negative entry in the first column means *instability*, not "marginal stability". Marginal stability occurs when the entry equals *exactly zero*.
> 6. **RC servo pulse width confusion** — Standard is 1 ms = one extreme, 2 ms = other extreme, 1.5 ms = centre, **not** 0–100% duty cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Servo Control:**

- A **servomechanism** is a *closed-loop, negative-feedback, power-amplifying* control system.
- A **servomotor** is the *plant* (actuator) inside a servomechanism.
- The **three classical control variables** in a servo are **position, velocity, and torque**.
- The **armature-controlled DC servomotor** transfer function (with $L_a$ neglected) is:

$$\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}, \qquad \frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

- The **PID** controller is $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
- **P-action** reduces rise time but causes steady-state error.
- **I-action** eliminates steady-state error but may cause oscillation.
- **D-action** damps oscillations and improves stability but amplifies noise.
- A **Type-1 system** (one pole at the origin) tracks a step with zero error and a ramp with error $1/K_v$.
- The **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** gives the range of a gain parameter for closed-loop stability.
- A standard **RC servo** uses a **50 Hz PWM** signal with pulse widths of **1.0–2.0 ms** mapped to mechanical angles of **−90° to +90°**.
- **Industrial servos** use **encoders or resolvers** for position feedback and **tachogenerators** for velocity feedback.
- The **order of the closed-loop system** equals the order of the open-loop denominator; a second-order system is fully described by $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$.
- The **steady-state error** for a unit step input to a unity-feedback system is $e_{ss} = 1/(1+K_p)$ for a proportional-only controller.
- **Negative feedback** is preferred because it provides stability, disturbance rejection, reduced sensitivity, and bandwidth extension.
- For the **PD-controlled position servo**, the relationship between gains and second-order parameters is:

$$K_p = \frac{\omega_n^2 \tau_m}{K_a K_m}, \qquad K_d = \frac{2 \zeta \omega_n \tau_m - 1}{K_a K_m}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursive Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recursive Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Recursive Filtering** in mobile robotics is a class of probabilistic state estimation algorithms that recursively estimate the hidden state of a dynamic system (the robot) from a sequence of noisy sensor measurements and control inputs. The defining property is **recursion**: the current belief about the state is computed purely from the *previous* belief and the *current* measurement/control, without requiring access to the full history of past data. The most general formalization is the **Bayes Filter** (also called the **Recursive State Estimator**), which forms the mathematical backbone of three foundational algorithms in mobile robotics:

1. **Kalman Filter (KF)** — for linear-Gaussian systems.
2. **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** — for mildly non-linear systems.
3. **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)** — for highly non-linear / multi-modal systems.

Mathematically, recursive filtering propagates a probability distribution called the **belief** $bel(x_t)$ over the robot state $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$ up to time $t$:

$$bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme (NEP 2020), Module 4 of *PEBRT522 – Mobile Robotics* mandates study of **Robot Localization**, and recursive filtering forms its *theoretical foundation*. A student is expected to know the **Bayes Filter derivation**, and the **algorithmic and mathematical differences** between KF, EKF, and Particle Filter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded and standing in your house**, trying to guess your exact location.

- **At every second**, you take a step (this is your **control input** $u_t$).
- **At every second**, you also *feel* the wall (this is your **measurement** $z_t$, but it's noisy — maybe your hand is sweaty).
- You maintain a *mental map* of where you might be. This mental map is your **belief** $bel(x_t)$.
- You **do not remember your every step from the day you were born**; you only update your belief based on the *previous belief*, the *new step*, and the *new sensation*.

That is **recursion** in plain English: **"I update what I know now using only what I knew a moment ago and what I just observed/acted upon."** The Bayes Filter is the mathematician's formalization of this everyday reasoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "filter"?** In signal processing, a *filter* extracts a clean signal from noise. A **recursive** filter is one that does this *iteratively* — each new sample updates the previous estimate, like a low-pass filter that smooths jittery GPS readings in real time.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Recursive Filtering

| Pillar | Core Idea | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bayes Filter** (General) | Recursive application of Bayes' rule under Markov assumption | Theoretical foundation; not a usable algorithm |
| **Kalman Filter** | Closed-form Gaussian propagation (mean + covariance) | Linear systems, Gaussian noise |
| **Extended Kalman Filter** | Linearize non-linear models using Jacobians | Mildly non-linear systems (e.g., range-bearing SLAM) |
| **Particle Filter** | Monte Carlo sample-based belief representation | Highly non-linear, multi-modal beliefs (global localization) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayes Filter — Conceptual Breakdown

The Bayes filter operates in **two alternating phases** at every time step $t$:

### Step 1 — **Prediction (or "Control Update")**
The belief from the previous time $bel(x_{t-1})$ is propagated forward through the **motion model** $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$, which encodes the physics of robot actuation. Because of the **Markov assumption** (the next state depends only on the current state and the current control), this becomes an integral:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

> The bar $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ denotes the *predicted* (prior) belief, *before* the new measurement is incorporated.

### Step 2 — **Measurement Update (or "Correction")**
The prediction is then multiplied by the **measurement model** $P(z_t \mid x_t)$, which expresses the likelihood of the observed sensor reading given a hypothetical state. The result is normalized by a constant $\eta$ to produce a valid probability:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

Here, $\eta$ is a normalizer that ensures the belief integrates to 1 over the state space.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Recursion:** Notice that $bel(x_t)$ depends *only* on $\overline{bel}(x_t)$, which in turn depends *only* on $bel(x_{t-1})$. The full history $z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}$ has been **summarized** into the single distribution $bel(x_{t-1})$. This is the essence of *recursion* and what makes Bayes filters computationally tractable for online robotics.

## 2.2 The Markov Assumption — The "Why" Behind Recursion

The **Markov assumption** is the philosophical underpinning of recursive filtering. It states that the past is irrelevant if the present is known:

$$P(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without the Markov assumption, the belief at time $t$ would require keeping the *entire history* of states, controls, and measurements — making online estimation intractable for any real robot.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bayes Rule** | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Foundation of all recursive filters |
| 2 | **Markov Assumption** | $P(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Enables recursion |
| 3 | **Prediction Step** | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \displaystyle\int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Propagates belief via motion model |
| 4 | **Update Step** | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Incorporates measurement $z_t$ |
| 5 | **Normalizer** | $\eta = \left[ \displaystyle\int P(z_t \mid x_t') \, \overline{bel}(x_t') \, dx_t' \right]^{-1}$ | Ensures $bel$ integrates to **1** |
| 6 | **KF State Prediction** | $\bar{x}_t = A x_{t-1} + B u_t$ | Linear-Gaussian motion model |
| 7 | **KF Covariance Prediction** | $\bar{P}_t = A P_{t-1} A^{\top} + Q$ | Propagates uncertainty ($Q$ = process noise) |
| 8 | **KF Kalman Gain** | $K_t = \bar{P}_t C^{\top} \left( C \bar{P}_t C^{\top} + R \right)^{-1}$ | Optimal weighting of prediction vs. measurement |
| 9 | **KF State Update** | $x_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t \left( z_t - C \bar{x}_t \right)$ | Corrects prediction with innovation |
| 10 | **KF Covariance Update** | $P_t = (I - K_t C) \bar{P}_t$ | Reduces uncertainty after measurement |
| 11 | **EKF Linearization** | $A_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$, $\quad C_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{\bar{x}_t}$ | Jacobians of non-linear $g, h$ |
| 12 | **Particle Filter Resampling** | $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Importance weights for sample-based belief |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Recursive filtering is the workhorse behind nearly every modern autonomous system:

- **GNSS / GPS Receivers:** Tracking the position of a satellite signal in noise.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Fusing LiDAR, camera, IMU, and GPS to localize in real time.
- **Drone Navigation:** Fusing IMU + barometer + optical flow.
- **Spacecraft Attitude Control:** Estimating orientation from star trackers and gyroscopes.
- **AR/VR Headsets:** Tracking headset pose (position + orientation) at **≥ 90 Hz** using IMU + cameras.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Context:** The KTU 2024 syllabus (NEP 2020-aligned) emphasizes **outcome-based learning**. In lab courses linked to this module, students implement a *Kalman filter* on an Arduino/ROS-based mobile robot to fuse wheel odometry with ultrasonic sensor readings — directly mirroring the equations above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Bayes Filter

We begin from the definition of the posterior belief:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 1 — Condition on the latest measurement $z_t$.** Using the definition of conditional probability and Bayes' rule:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; P(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov assumption to the measurement model.** The probability of observing $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ (not on past measurements or controls), so:

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; P(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}), \quad \text{where } \eta = \frac{1}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov assumption to the prior.** The current state $x_t$ depends only on the previous state $x_{t-1}$ and the current control $u_t$:

$$
P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

$$
= \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the result as the predicted belief.** By definition, $\overline{bel}(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$, so:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Final Combined Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}\;}
$$

This is the **Bayes Filter equation** — the *mother of all recursive filters* in mobile robotics.

## 3.2 Kalman Filter Derivation (from Bayes Filter under Gaussian Assumptions)

We assume the state distribution is **Gaussian**, parameterized by mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$:

$$
bel(x_t) \;=\; \mathcal{N}(x_t; \mu_t, \Sigma_t)
$$

**Step 1 — Linear-Gaussian motion model.** Let $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.

The mean of the predicted belief is:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t \;=\; A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

Since the covariance transforms as $\Sigma \mapsto A \Sigma A^{\top}$ under a linear map $x \mapsto A x$, and we add independent process noise $R_t$:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t \;=\; A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^{\top} + R_t
$$

**Step 2 — Linear-Gaussian measurement model.** Let $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$ with $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.

The joint distribution of $\begin{pmatrix} x_t \\ z_t \end{pmatrix}$ is Gaussian. Conditioning a joint Gaussian on $z_t$ yields a Gaussian posterior with mean and covariance:

$$
K_t \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top} \left( C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^{\top} + Q_t \right)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t \;=\; \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t \;=\; (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Step 3 — Why is $K_t$ called the "Kalman Gain"?** The matrix $K_t$ optimally blends the prediction $C_t \bar{\mu}_t$ with the actual measurement $z_t$. When the measurement noise is large ($Q_t$ large), $K_t \to 0$ and the filter trusts the prediction. When the prediction uncertainty is large ($\bar{\Sigma}_t$ large), $K_t \to I$ and the filter trusts the measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common derivation mistake is forgetting that the covariance prediction is $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A \Sigma A^{\top} + R$, not $A \Sigma A^{\top}$ alone. The $+R$ term represents the *process noise injected at every step* — without it, the filter would converge to zero uncertainty (an obviously false, overconfident state).

## 3.3 Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) — Linearization via Taylor Expansion

When the motion or measurement model is non-linear, $x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1})$ and $z_t = h(x_t)$, we linearize them using a **first-order Taylor expansion** around the current estimate:

$$
g(u_t, x_{t-1}) \;\approx\; g(u_t, \mu_{t-1}) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})
$$

$$
h(x_t) \;\approx\; h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

where $G_t$ and $H_t$ are the **Jacobians** of $g$ and $h$ with respect to $x$:

$$
G_t \;=\; \left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} \right\vert_{(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)}, \qquad H_t \;=\; \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}
$$

Substituting these linearized forms into the KF equations:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t \;=\; g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t \;=\; G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^{\top} + R_t
$$

$$
K_t \;=\; \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{\top} \left( H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{\top} + Q_t \right)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t \;=\; \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t \;=\; (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Extended" not "Unscented"?** The EKF approximates non-linearity with a *local linear* model, which introduces approximation error when the system is highly non-linear. The **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** and **Particle Filter** are alternatives that handle this without explicit Jacobians.

## 3.4 Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization) — Sample-Based Recursion

When the belief is **multi-modal** (e.g., the robot has been kidnapped and doesn't know where it is — its belief is a uniform distribution over all possible locations), the Kalman / EKF assumption of a single Gaussian fails. The **Particle Filter** represents the belief by a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles):

$$
bel(x_t) \;\approx\; \left\{ x_t^{[m]}, w_t^{[m]} \right\}_{m=1}^{M}
$$

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Sampling:** For each particle $m$, draw a new state from the proposal distribution (typically the motion model): $x_t^{[m]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[m]})$.
2. **Importance Weighting:** Evaluate how well each particle explains the measurement:

$$
w_t^{[m]} \;\propto\; w_{t-1}^{[m]} \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]})
$$

3. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles from the set $\left\{ x_t^{[m]} \right\}$ with probabilities proportional to $\left\{ w_t^{[m]} \right\}$, then reset all weights to $w_t^{[m]} = 1/M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why resampling?** Without it, after a few iterations, almost all weight concentrates on a few particles ("particle deprivation"). Resampling focuses computational resources on the high-probability regions of the state space — making the filter self-adaptive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bayes Filter — Top-Level Recursive Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Previous Belief bel xt-1] --> B[Apply Motion Model: P xt ut xt-1]
    B --> C[Predicted Belief: bel bar xt]
    D[New Control ut] --> B
    E[New Measurement zt] --> F[Apply Measurement Model: P zt xt]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Bayes Rule: Multiply by Likelihood]
    G --> H[Normalize by eta]
    H --> I[Posterior Belief: bel xt]
    I --> J[Output: bel xt and Prediction]
    J --> K[Store as Previous Belief for Next Step]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Kalman Filter — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRED[Prediction Stage]
        P1[Previous Mean mut-1] --> P2[Multiply by State Matrix A]
        P3[Previous Cov Sigmat-1] --> P4[Compute A Sigmat-1 AT]
        P2 --> P5[Predicted Mean bar mut]
        P4 --> P6[Add Process Noise Q]
        P6 --> P7[Predicted Cov bar Sigmat]
        P5 --> P7
    end
    subgraph UPD[Measurement Update Stage]
        P7 --> U1[Compute Kalman Gain Kt]
        P5 --> U2[Innovation: zt minus C bar mut]
        U1 --> U3[Correction: bar mut plus Kt times Innovation]
        U2 --> U3
        U1 --> U4[Update Cov: I minus Kt C times bar Sigmat]
        U3 --> U5[New Mean mut]
        U4 --> U6[New Cov Sigmat]
    end
    U5 --> OUT[Output: Updated State Estimate]
    U6 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Extended Kalman Filter — Linearization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Non-linear System: x = g u x, z = h x] --> J1[Compute Jacobian Gt: partial g / partial x]
    S --> J2[Compute Jacobian Ht: partial h / partial x]
    J1 --> L1[Linearized Motion: A replaced by Gt]
    J2 --> L2[Linearized Measurement: C replaced by Ht]
    L1 --> KF[Apply Standard Kalman Filter Equations]
    L2 --> KF
    KF --> OUT[EKF Estimate: mut, Sigmat]
```

## 4.4 Particle Filter — Resampling Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Particles xt-1 with weights wt-1] --> B[Sample New States from Motion Model]
    B --> C[Predicted Particles xt]
    C --> D[Evaluate Measurement Likelihood P zt xt]
    D --> E[Compute Importance Weights wt proportional to wt-1 times P zt xt]
    E --> F{Resampling Triggered?}
    F -- YES --> G[Resample M Particles with Replacement proportional to wt]
    F -- NO --> H[Keep Particles and Weights]
    G --> I[Normalized Particles xt wt = 1 over M]
    H --> J[Output Posterior Sample Set]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Use as Input for Next Time Step]
    K --> A
```

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture of the Three Filters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BF[Bayes Filter: General Recursive Framework] --> KF[Kalman Filter: Linear Gaussian]
    BF --> EKF[Extended Kalman Filter: Linearized Non-Linear]
    BF --> PF[Particle Filter: Sample Based Non-Gaussian]
    KF --> U1[Single Gaussian: mean and covariance]
    EKF --> U2[Jacobians and Gaussian approximation]
    PF --> U3[Set of weighted samples]
    U1 --> APP1[Tracking, GPS, INS]
    U2 --> APP2[SLAM, Mobile Robot Localization]
    U3 --> APP3[Global Localization, Kidnapped Robot Problem]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]`

> **State and explain the Bayes Filter algorithm for recursive state estimation in mobile robotics. Why is the Markov assumption critical to its recursive nature? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Bayes Filter recursively estimates the robot's belief $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over its state $x_t$ using two steps:

1. **Prediction:** $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$
2. **Measurement Update:** $bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$

The **Markov assumption** — that the future state depends only on the current state and current control — is critical because it lets the entire history be **summarized into the single previous belief** $bel(x_{t-1})$, making recursion (and hence real-time computation) possible.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]`

> **Differentiate between the Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, and Particle Filter. State one application domain of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Filter | System Type | Representation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kalman Filter** | Linear-Gaussian | Single Gaussian (mean + covariance) | GPS receiver tracking, INS |
| **Extended Kalman Filter** | Mildly non-linear | Linearized Gaussian (Jacobians) | Mobile robot EKF-SLAM, indoor localization |
| **Particle Filter** | Highly non-linear, multi-modal | Set of weighted samples | Global localization, kidnapped robot recovery, terrain mapping |

The KF uses closed-form matrix equations; the EKF linearizes non-linear models via Jacobians; the Particle Filter uses Monte Carlo sampling to represent arbitrary, multi-modal belief distributions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and are typically divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[Adapted from KTU Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Derive the **Bayes Filter algorithm** from first principles, clearly stating the Markov assumption at each step. Explain each term in the final equation. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A differential drive mobile robot is localized using a **Kalman Filter** with state vector $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^{\top}$ and measurement matrix $C = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Given the following values at time $t$, compute the **Kalman Gain** $K_t$, the **updated state** $\mu_t$, and the **updated covariance** $\Sigma_t$. **(7 Marks)**

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 2.0 \\ 3.0 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad z_t = \begin{pmatrix} 2.2 \\ 2.9 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad Q_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Bayes Filter Derivation (7 Marks)

The belief is defined as $bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Step 1** — Condition on the latest measurement and apply Bayes' rule:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 2** — Apply Markov assumption to measurement model:

$$
P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

**Step 3** — Apply total probability and Markov assumption to the prior:

$$
P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

$$
= \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 4** — Final equation:

$$
\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}
$$

**Term Explanations:**
- $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$: **Motion model** (probability of new state given control and previous state).
- $bel(x_{t-1})$: **Previous posterior belief** (the recursive input).
- $P(z_t \mid x_t)$: **Measurement model** (likelihood of measurement $z_t$ given state $x_t$).
- $\eta$: **Normalizer** ensuring the belief integrates to 1.

#### Part (b) — Kalman Filter Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the innovation** $\nu_t = z_t - C \bar{\mu}_t$:

$$
C \bar{\mu}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2.0 \\ 3.0 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.0 \\ 3.0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\nu_t = \begin{pmatrix} 2.2 \\ 2.9 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 2.0 \\ 3.0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the innovation covariance** $S_t = C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^{\top} + Q_t$:

$$
C \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
S_t = C \bar{\Sigma}_t C^{\top} + Q_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Kalman Gain** $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C^{\top} S_t^{-1}$:

$$
S_t^{-1} = \frac{1}{(0.6)(0.6) - (0.1)(0.1)} \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & -0.1 \\ -0.1 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.35} \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & -0.1 \\ -0.1 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 1.714 & -0.286 \\ -0.286 & 1.714 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
K_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1.714 & -0.286 \\ -0.286 & 1.714 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1.714 & -0.286 \\ -0.286 & 1.714 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8286 & -0.1714 \\ 0.0 & 0.8286 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Updated state** $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$:

$$
K_t \nu_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8286 & -0.1714 \\ 0.0 & 0.8286 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1829 \\ -0.0829 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \begin{pmatrix} 2.0 \\ 3.0 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.1829 \\ -0.0829 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.183 \\ 2.917 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Updated covariance** $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C) \bar{\Sigma}_t$:

$$
K_t C = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8286 & -0.1714 & 0 \\ 0.0 & 0.8286 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
I - K_t C = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1714 & 0.1714 & 0 \\ 0.0 & 0.1714 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C) \bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0857 & 0.0686 & 0 \\ 0.0171 & 0.0857 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse $Q_t$ (process noise) and $R_t$ (measurement noise) when applying the Kalman equations. In our problem, $Q_t$ is the **measurement noise** covariance (size 2×2 matching $z_t$), and the **process noise** $R_t$ is implicitly zero in the prediction step. Mismatching these will lose **up to 3 marks**. Also, the **orientation $\theta$** is unchanged because the measurement model $C$ does not observe it — a key insight.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** algorithm. With a suitable block diagram, describe how it linearizes the non-linear motion and measurement models. State the role of Jacobians $G_t$ and $H_t$. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** With reference to the **Particle Filter**, explain the three main steps: (i) Sampling, (ii) Importance Weighting, and (iii) Resampling. Why is resampling necessary? Mention one scenario where a particle filter outperforms an EKF. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Extended Kalman Filter (7 Marks)

The EKF is an extension of the Kalman Filter to non-linear systems. It applies the KF equations to **locally linearized** versions of the non-linear models.

**Linearization:** Let $x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \epsilon_t$ and $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$. We approximate these via first-order Taylor expansion around the current estimate:

$$
g(u_t, x_{t-1}) \approx g(u_t, \mu_{t-1}) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})
$$

$$
h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

The **Jacobians** are the matrices of partial derivatives:

$$
G_t = \left. \frac{\partial g(u_t, x)}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x = \mu_{t-1}}, \qquad H_t = \left. \frac{\partial h(x)}{\partial x} \right\vert_{x = \bar{\mu}_t
$$

These are used in place of $A$ and $C$ in the standard KF equations. The **role of Jacobians** is to capture the local sensitivity of the non-linear functions to changes in the state — effectively giving a *tangent-line approximation* of the true non-linear dynamics at each time step.

#### Part (b) — Particle Filter (7 Marks)

The Particle Filter represents the belief $bel(x_t)$ as a set of $M$ weighted samples:

$$
bel(x_t) \approx \left\{ x_t^{[m]}, w_t^{[m]} \right\}_{m=1}^{M}, \quad \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_t^{[m]} = 1
$$

**Step 1 — Sampling (Prediction):** For each particle, draw a hypothetical next state from the motion model:

$$
x_t^{[m]} \sim P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[m]})
$$

**Step 2 — Importance Weighting (Update):** For each particle, compute the likelihood of the actual measurement:

$$
w_t^{[m]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[m]} \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]})
$$

Particles that "agree" with the measurement receive higher weight.

**Step 3 — Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles with replacement, where particle $x_t^{[m]}$ is chosen with probability $w_t^{[m]}$. After resampling, all weights are reset to $w_t^{[m]} = 1/M$.

**Why resampling is necessary:** After a few iterations, the weight distribution becomes highly skewed — a few particles dominate the total weight while the rest contribute negligibly ("**particle deprivation**" or "**sample impoverishment**"). Resampling eliminates low-weight particles and duplicates high-weight ones, ensuring computational effort is spent on the relevant regions of the state space.

**Scenario where Particle Filter outperforms EKF:** The **kidnapped robot problem** — where a robot with no prior knowledge is placed in an arbitrary location in a known map. The resulting belief is a *uniform* distribution over all map locations (highly multi-modal), which a single-Gaussian EKF cannot represent. A Particle Filter naturally handles this by spreading samples over the entire state space.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake in EKF questions is to say that the EKF "**converts**" a non-linear system into a linear one. The correct phrasing is that it "**approximates**" or "**linearizes locally**" the system — the original system remains non-linear, and large deviations from the linearization point cause error. Similarly, for the particle filter, students often forget to **reset weights to $1/M$ after resampling** — losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ **Bayes Filter** is the *most general* recursive estimator; KF, EKF, and PF are *instantiations* of it under different assumptions.
- ✅ **Two key equations**: Prediction $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ and Update $bel(x_t) = \eta P(z_t \mid x_t) \overline{bel}(x_t)$.
- ✅ **Markov Assumption** is the philosophical reason recursion works — it summarizes history into the current state.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter** assumes linear-Gaussian models; uses mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$.
- ✅ **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ optimally trades off prediction vs. measurement trust based on relative uncertainties.
- ✅ **Covariance prediction** always includes **process noise** $R_t$: $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A \Sigma_{t-1} A^{\top} + R_t$. Forgetting $+R_t$ is a common mark-losing mistake.
- ✅ **EKF** uses **Jacobians** $G_t$ and $H_t$ to linearize non-linear models; first-order Taylor approximation.
- ✅ **Particle Filter** uses **Monte Carlo sampling** with importance weights; ideal for **multi-modal** beliefs.
- ✅ **Resampling** prevents particle deprivation; resets weights to $1/M$.
- ✅ **Kidnapped robot problem** = canonical scenario where Particle Filter >> EKF.
- ✅ All recursive filters share the same skeleton: **(1) Predict using motion model → (2) Update using measurement model → (3) Normalize.**
- ✅ **Units reminder:** $x_t$ is in SI units (m, rad); $\Sigma_t$ has units of $[x]^2$ (e.g., $\text{m}^2$); $Q_t, R_t$ are noise covariances matching the dimensions of $z_t$ and $x_t$ respectively.
- ✅ The **normalizer** $\eta$ ensures $\int bel(x_t) dx_t = 1$ — a fundamental property of any valid probability distribution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Geometric Methods: Perceptual Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Geometric Methods: Perceptual Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Non-Geometric / Perceptual Methods

In the context of mobile robotics, **Non-Geometric Methods (also called Perceptual Methods or Appearance-Based Methods)** are localization and mapping techniques that identify and represent a robot's position using **perceptual signatures** derived directly from sensor data, rather than by estimating the robot's precise **Cartesian (x, y, θ) pose** in a metric reference frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Perceptual Structure* is the representation of a robot's environment as a set of distinct, recognizable **"places"**, where each place is characterized by an abstract sensor signature (a histogram, image, feature vector, or histogram-of-gradients). Two observations are considered to belong to the same place if and only if their perceptual signatures are statistically similar — a property called **Perceptual Equivalence**.

The fundamental hypothesis is that the **physical structure of a place produces a stable, repeatable pattern in sensor data**, which can be used as a "fingerprint" for recognition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Tourist"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped in an unfamiliar city. When the blindfold is removed, you do not first declare *"My GPS coordinates are 9.9815° N, 76.2923° E."* Instead, you look around and think:
> - *"I see a red postbox, a fountain, and a glass dome — this is the same square I walked through 5 minutes ago."*
> - *"I hear the hum of the metro and smell croissants — I am back at the bakery street."*
>
> You are performing **perceptual localization** — recognizing places by their **sensory appearance** (visual, auditory, olfactory signatures), not by their **metric coordinates**. Non-geometric robot localization works on exactly this principle.

## 1.3 Why Non-Geometric Methods Matter in Mobile Robotics

| Geometric (Metric) Methods | Non-Geometric (Perceptual) Methods |
|---|---|
| Estimate pose $(x, y, \theta)$ | Estimate **place identity** $L_i$ |
| Sensitive to odometry drift | Robust to odometry errors |
| Require accurate landmark extraction | Use raw sensor signatures |
| High computational cost for large maps | Compressed / compact signatures |
| Ineffective in feature-poor environments | Works in any environment that produces stable sensor readings |
| Output continuous estimates | Output **discrete** place labels |

## 1.4 Key Constituents of a Perceptual Structure System

A complete non-geometric / perceptual localization pipeline consists of four components, which will be the focus of this module:

1. **Signature Extraction** — converting raw sensor data (range scans, camera images, Wi-Fi RSSI) into a compact descriptor.
2. **Signature Storage / Learning** — building a database of place signatures.
3. **Signature Matching** — comparing a live signature with stored signatures to recognize a place.
4. **Temporal / Sequential Inference** — using **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)** or similar probabilistic models to enforce temporal consistency over sequences of observations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The two most important sub-topics for examination are **(a) Histogram-based perceptual signatures** and **(b) HMM-based sequence-based place recognition.** Both are almost guaranteed to appear in the KTU University Exam.

## 1.5 Standard Perceptual Signature Metrics Used in the Literature

The following metrics are considered **canonical** in KTU Mobile Robotics boards and the broader research community:

- **Beam/Range Histograms** — distributions of obstacle distances.
- **Intensity/Color Histograms** — distribution of pixel values in a camera view.
- **Fourier Signatures** — frequency-domain representation of sensor scans.
- **Principal Component (Eigenplace) Coefficients** — low-dimensional PCA projections of panoramic images.
- **Global Image Descriptors** — GIST, HOG, SIFT-bag-of-words.
- **Wi-Fi / RFID Fingerprints** — Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) vectors.
- **Dimensionality (Fractal dimension) and Scaling signatures.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Perceptual Structure Framework

The non-geometric / perceptual framework is built on five layered stages. Each stage has a clear "why" and "how", which we will dissect in sequence.

### Stage 1 — Sensor Data Acquisition

**Why:** Every perceptual structure begins with a *raw observation* $o_t$ at time $t$.

**How:** The robot captures data from one or more sensors:
- 2D/3D **range finders** (LIDAR, sonar arrays) producing a vector of distances $z_t = [z_t^{(1)}, z_t^{(2)}, \ldots, z_t^{(k)}]$.
- **Cameras** producing an image $I_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$.
- **Wi-Fi / Bluetooth / RFID** sensors producing an RSSI vector $r_t = [r_t^{(1)}, \ldots, r_t^{(m)}]$.

### Stage 2 — Signature Extraction

**Why:** Raw sensor data is high-dimensional, noisy, and varies with viewpoint. A *signature* compresses the observation into a low-dimensional, noise-tolerant descriptor.

**How:** A deterministic function $f$ maps an observation to a signature vector $s_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$:

$$s_t = f(o_t)$$

Common choices for $f$:

| Method | Signature Equation | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Range Histogram** | $h_t(k) = \text{card}\{i : z_t^{(i)} \in \text{bin}_k\}$ | Indoor corridor recognition |
| **Color Histogram** | $h_t(k) = \text{card}\{(x,y) : I_t(x,y) \in \text{bin}_k\}$ | Visual place recognition |
| **Fourier Signature** | $F(\omega) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} z_t^{(n)} e^{-j 2\pi \omega n / N}$ | Rotation-invariant scan matching |
| **PCA / Eigenplaces** | $s_t = W^T (I_t - \mu)$ | Compact visual SLAM |
| **RSSI Fingerprint** | $r_t$ itself (normalised) | Wi-Fi indoor localization |

### Stage 3 — Place Definition & Perceptual Equivalence

**Why:** A "place" is a *cluster* of observations that look alike. Without this, the robot has no labels to assign.

**How:** Two observations $o_i$ and $o_j$ are **perceptually equivalent** iff:

$$d(s_i, s_j) \le \tau$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a similarity/distance metric and $\tau$ is a predefined threshold. The standard distance metrics are:

$$d_{\text{Euclid}}(s_i, s_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d} (s_i^{(k)} - s_j^{(k)})^2}$$

$$d_{\text{Manhattan}}(s_i, s_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert s_i^{(k)} - s_j^{(k)} \vert$$

$$d_{\text{Bhat}}(s_i, s_j) = -\ln \sum_{k=1}^{d} \sqrt{s_i^{(k)} \cdot s_j^{(k)}}$$

$$d_{\chi^2}(s_i, s_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{d} \frac{(s_i^{(k)} - s_j^{(k)})^2}{s_i^{(k)} + s_j^{(k)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bhattacharyya and Chi-Squared are preferred for histograms:** Range and color histograms are **probability distributions**, not Euclidean vectors. The Bhattacharyya and chi-squared metrics are **distribution-aware** and are invariant under bin re-parameterization, which is critical for noisy robot sensors.

### Stage 4 — Storage & Indexing

**Why:** The robot must remember what it has seen.

**How:** A **perceptual map** $\mathcal{M} = \{(L_1, s_1), (L_2, s_2), \ldots, (L_N, s_N)\}$ where $L_i$ is a place label. In topological maps, places are connected by edges representing navigable transitions.

### Stage 5 — Sequential / Probabilistic Inference (HMM)

**Why:** A single signature match is **noisy**. A robot looking at a kitchen from one angle may look like a robot looking at a corridor. To disambiguate, the robot exploits the fact that **places occur in sequences** (kitchen → hallway → bedroom, never kitchen → basement → sky).

**How:** Use a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$:
- **States** $Q = \{L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_N\}$ = place labels.
- **Observations** $O = \{o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_T\}$ = sensor signatures.
- **Transition probabilities** $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = L_j \mid q_t = L_i)$ — how often place $L_i$ is followed by $L_j$.
- **Emission probabilities** $b_j(o_t) = P(o_t \mid q_t = L_j)$ — how often a signature $o_t$ is observed at place $L_j$.
- **Initial distribution** $\pi_i = P(q_1 = L_i)$.

The three canonical HMM problems (from the standard HMM theory of Rabiner, 1989) apply directly to perceptual localization:

| Problem | Notation | Algorithm | Mobile Robotics Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Evaluation | $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Forward algorithm | "How likely is this observation sequence given my map?" |
| Decoding | $\arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$ | **Viterbi algorithm** | "What is the most likely sequence of places the robot visited?" |
| Learning | $\arg\max_\lambda P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Baum–Welch (EM) | "Learn the place-transition probabilities from data." |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **complete high-yield formula sheet** for this topic. Memorize every entry.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Perceptual equivalence | $d(s_i, s_j) \le \tau$ | Threshold-based clustering |
| 2 | Euclidean distance | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum_k (s_i^{(k)} - s_j^{(k)})^2}$ | For PCA / Fourier |
| 3 | Bhattacharyya distance | $d_B = -\ln \sum_k \sqrt{s_i^{(k)} s_j^{(k)}}$ | For histograms |
| 4 | Chi-squared distance | $d_{\chi^2} = \sum_k \frac{(s_i^{(k)} - s_j^{(k)})^2}{s_i^{(k)} + s_j^{(k)}}$ | For histograms |
| 5 | Range histogram bin | $h(k) = \text{card}\{i : z^{(i)} \in \text{bin}_k\}$ | $k=1,\ldots,B$ |
| 6 | Fourier signature | $F(\omega) = \sum_n z^{(n)} e^{-j 2\pi \omega n / N}$ | Rotation invariant with $\vert F(\omega) \vert$ |
| 7 | PCA projection | $s = W^T (I - \mu)$ | $W$ = top $k$ eigenvectors of $\Sigma$ |
| 8 | HMM Forward variable | $\alpha_t(i) = P(o_1, \ldots, o_t, q_t = L_i \mid \lambda)$ | Initialised: $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i b_i(o_1)$ |
| 9 | HMM Forward recursion | $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$ | Computes $P(O \mid \lambda)$ |
| 10 | HMM likelihood | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$ | Termination step |
| 11 | HMM Viterbi variable | $\delta_t(i) = \max_{q_1,\ldots,q_{t-1}} P(q_1,\ldots,q_t, o_1,\ldots,o_t \mid \lambda)$ | $\delta_1(i) = \pi_i b_i(o_1)$ |
| 12 | Viterbi recursion | $\delta_{t+1}(j) = \max_i [\delta_t(i) a_{ij}] b_j(o_{t+1})$ | Backtrace stores $\psi_{t+1}(j)$ |
| 13 | Viterbi termination | $P^* = \max_i \delta_T(i)$ | Most likely final state |
| 14 | Backward variable | $\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1},\ldots,o_T \mid q_t = L_i, \lambda)$ | $\beta_T(i) = 1$ |
| 15 | Backward recursion | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | Used in Baum–Welch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** HMM-based perceptual localization is used in industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), autonomous wheelchairs, and museum tour-guide robots. PCA-based "Eigenplace" recognition is used in low-power embedded robots where storage and computation are limited.

## 2.3 Why the HMM Formulation Is the "Heart" of Perceptual Structure

A purely nearest-neighbor signature match suffers from **perceptual aliasing** — two distinct places producing identical signatures (e.g., two identical hospital corridors). The HMM solves this through **temporal context**: even if signature $s_t$ matches both place $L_i$ and place $L_j$, the Viterbi algorithm will choose whichever is consistent with $L_{t-1}$ and $L_{t+1}$. This is the most examination-relevant insight in the entire module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Forward Algorithm for HMM-Based Place Recognition

The forward algorithm computes $P(O \mid \lambda)$, the probability of observing the perceptual-signature sequence $O = (o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_T)$ given a learned HMM $\lambda$.

### Step 1 — Define the Forward Variable

We define:

$$\alpha_t(i) = P(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_t,\ q_t = L_i \mid \lambda)$$

This is the joint probability that the robot has **seen signatures $o_1$ through $o_t$ AND is currently at place $L_i$**.

### Step 2 — Initialization (t = 1)

At the very first time step, the robot is at place $L_i$ with probability $\pi_i$ and observes $o_1$ with probability $b_i(o_1)$. By the chain rule of probability:

$$\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

### Step 3 — Induction (Recursive Step)

Suppose $\alpha_t(i)$ is known for all $i$. To find $\alpha_{t+1}(j)$:

**Sub-step 3.1:** To end up at place $L_j$ at time $t+1$ and have produced the observation sequence so far, the robot must have been at **some** place $L_i$ at time $t$ and then transitioned to $L_j$. By the law of total probability:

$$P(q_{t+1} = L_j,\ o_1,\ldots,o_t \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij}$$

**Sub-step 3.2:** Now account for the new observation $o_{t+1}$ at place $L_j$:

$$\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$$

### Step 4 — Termination

After processing the entire sequence of length $T$, the robot is at some place $L_i$. The total likelihood is:

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (2 Places, 2 Observations)

Let the HMM be:
- States: $L_1, L_2$
- Initial: $\pi_1 = 0.6, \pi_2 = 0.4$
- Transition matrix: $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$
- Emission matrix: $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$ (columns are observations $o_a, o_b$)
- Observation sequence: $O = (o_a, o_b)$

Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$.

**Initialization:**
- $\alpha_1(1) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$
- $\alpha_1(2) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$

**Recursion (t = 1 → t = 2, target $o_2 = o_b$):**
- $\alpha_2(1) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21}] \cdot b_1(o_b)$
- $\alpha_2(1) = [0.54 \times 0.7 + 0.08 \times 0.4] \times 0.1$
- $\alpha_2(1) = [0.378 + 0.032] \times 0.1 = 0.410 \times 0.1 = 0.041$

- $\alpha_2(2) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22}] \cdot b_2(o_b)$
- $\alpha_2(2) = [0.54 \times 0.3 + 0.08 \times 0.6] \times 0.8$
- $\alpha_2(2) = [0.162 + 0.048] \times 0.8 = 0.210 \times 0.8 = 0.168$

**Termination:**
$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \alpha_2(1) + \alpha_2(2) = 0.041 + 0.168 = 0.209$$

## 3.2 Derivation: The Viterbi Algorithm for Place-Sequence Decoding

Viterbi finds the most likely place sequence: $Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$.

### Step 1 — Define the Viterbi Variable

$$\delta_t(i) = \max_{q_1,\ldots,q_{t-1}} P(q_1,\ldots,q_{t-1}, q_t = L_i, o_1,\ldots,o_t \mid \lambda)$$

### Step 2 — Initialization

$$\delta_1(i) = \pi_i b_i(o_1), \quad \psi_1(i) = 0$$

where $\psi_t(i)$ is the *backpointer* that stores which previous state produced the maximum.

### Step 3 — Recursion

$$\delta_{t+1}(j) = \max_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \delta_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$$

$$\psi_{t+1}(j) = \arg\max_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \delta_t(i) a_{ij} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Termination and Backtrace

$$P^* = \max_{i=1}^{N} \delta_T(i)$$

$$q_T^* = \arg\max_{i=1}^{N} \delta_T(i)$$

$$q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*), \quad t = T-1, T-2, \ldots, 1$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (Viterbi)

Using the same HMM, $O = (o_a, o_b)$:

**Initialization:**
- $\delta_1(1) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$, $\psi_1(1) = 0$
- $\delta_1(2) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$, $\psi_1(2) = 0$

**Recursion (t = 2, target $o_b$):**
- $\delta_2(1) = \max[0.54 \times 0.7, \ 0.08 \times 0.4] \times 0.1 = \max[0.378, 0.032] \times 0.1 = 0.378 \times 0.1 = 0.0378$
- $\psi_2(1) = 1$ (path came from $L_1$)
- $\delta_2(2) = \max[0.54 \times 0.3, \ 0.08 \times 0.6] \times 0.8 = \max[0.162, 0.048] \times 0.8 = 0.162 \times 0.8 = 0.1296$
- $\psi_2(2) = 1$ (path came from $L_1$)

**Termination:**
- $P^* = \max[0.0378, 0.1296] = 0.1296$
- $q_2^* = 2$ (robot ended at $L_2$)

**Backtrace:**
- $q_1^* = \psi_2(q_2^*) = \psi_2(2) = 1$

**Most likely place sequence:** $Q^* = (L_1, L_2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Forward Algorithm:** The Forward algorithm **sums** over all paths (yielding total probability $0.209$). Viterbi **takes the max** (yielding the best path probability $0.1296$). The ratio $\frac{0.1296}{0.209} \approx 0.62$ tells us the most likely path carries 62% of the total likelihood mass — a high-confidence single-path answer.

## 3.3 Derivation: Histogram Signature Extraction

Given a 2D range scan $Z = [z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_M]$ where $z_i$ is the distance of the $i$-th beam, the histogram signature is constructed as:

$$h(k) = \text{card}\{i \mid z_i \in \text{bin}_k\}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, B$$

**Normalization step (to convert to a probability distribution):**

$$p(k) = \frac{h(k)}{\sum_{k'=1}^{B} h(k')}$$

**Bhattacharyya distance** between two normalized histograms $p$ and $q$:

$$d_B(p, q) = -\ln \sum_{k=1}^{B} \sqrt{p(k) q(k)}$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Scan 1 histogram (normalised): $p = [0.4, 0.4, 0.1, 0.1]$
- Scan 2 histogram (normalised): $q = [0.45, 0.35, 0.1, 0.1]$

Compute:
- $\sqrt{pq} = [\sqrt{0.18}, \sqrt{0.14}, \sqrt{0.01}, \sqrt{0.01}] = [0.4243, 0.3742, 0.1000, 0.1000]$
- $\sum \sqrt{pq} = 0.4243 + 0.3742 + 0.1000 + 0.1000 = 0.9985$
- $d_B = -\ln(0.9985) = 0.0015$

This very small distance confirms the two scans belong to the same place.

## 3.4 Derivation: PCA (Eigenplace) Projection

Given $N$ training panoramic images $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_N$ each of size $m \times n$ flattened to vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{mn}$:

**Step 1 — Mean image:** $\mu = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} I_j$

**Step 2 — Centered data matrix:** $X = [I_1 - \mu, I_2 - \mu, \ldots, I_N - \mu]$, size $(mn \times N)$.

**Step 3 — Covariance matrix:** $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N} X X^T$, size $(mn \times mn)$. (For $N \ll mn$, use the dual form $X^T X$ instead.)

**Step 4 — Eigendecomposition:** $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$. Sort eigenvalues in descending order.

**Step 5 — Projection matrix:** $W = [v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k]$, size $(mn \times k)$, where $k$ is chosen to retain 95% of variance.

**Step 6 — Signature (Eigenplace coefficients) of a new image $I_{new}$:**

$$s_{new} = W^T (I_{new} - \mu) \in \mathbb{R}^k$$

The signature is a $k$-dimensional vector, typically with $k \in [10, 50]$ — a massive compression from the original $mn$ pixels.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Perceptual Localization Pipeline (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Sensor Data Acquisition] --> nodeB[Signature Extraction f o]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Signature Database M]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Similarity Metric d s1 s2 le tau}
    nodeD -->|Match| nodeE[Place Label Li]
    nodeD -->|No Match| nodeF[New Place Candidate]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Add to Map]
    nodeE --> nodeH[HMM Sequential Inference]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Final Localized Place Sequence]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — HMM Topology for Place Recognition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> L1
    L1 --> L1 : a11
    L1 --> L2 : a12
    L1 --> L3 : a13
    L2 --> L1 : a21
    L2 --> L2 : a22
    L2 --> L3 : a23
    L3 --> L1 : a31
    L3 --> L2 : a32
    L3 --> L3 : a33
    L1 : Place L1 emission b1
    L2 : Place L2 emission b2
    L3 : Place L3 emission b3
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Node L1 Kitchen] --- B[Node L2 Hall]
    B --- C[Node L3 Bedroom]
    B --- D[Node L4 Bathroom]
    C --- E[Node L5 Balcony]
    A -.Signature s1.-> A
    B -.Signature s2.-> B
    C -.Signature s3.-> C
    D -.Signature s4.-> D
    E -.Signature s5.-> E
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1L1[delta 1 L1] --> s2L1[delta 2 L1]
    s1L1 --> s2L2[delta 2 L2]
    s1L1 --> s2L3[delta 2 L3]
    s1L2[delta 1 L2] --> s2L1
    s1L2 --> s2L2
    s1L2 --> s2L3
    s1L3[delta 1 L3] --> s2L1
    s1L3 --> s2L2
    s1L3 --> s2L3
    s2L1 --> s3L1[delta 3 L1]
    s2L1 --> s3L2[delta 3 L2]
    s2L1 --> s3L3[delta 3 L3]
    s2L2 --> s3L1
    s2L2 --> s3L2
    s2L2 --> s3L3
    s2L3 --> s3L1
    s2L3 --> s3L2
    s2L3 --> s3L3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SensingLayer[Sensing Layer]
        sens1[LIDAR]

    end

    end

    end

        inf1[Forward Algorithm]

    end

    end
```
