# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]**

### Question 1. Define *Perceptual Structure* in the context of mobile robotics. How does it differ from a metric (geometric) representation? `[3 Marks]` `[CO4 / Bloom: Remember]`

**Model Answer:**
Perceptual structure is a non-geometric representation of a robot's environment where each location (place) is described by a *sensor signature* — a compact descriptor extracted from raw sensor data such as a range histogram, color histogram, PCA coefficients, or Wi-Fi RSSI fingerprint — rather than by an explicit Cartesian pose $(x, y, \theta)$. Two observations belong to the same place if and only if their signatures are statistically similar (perceptual equivalence). In contrast, a *metric* representation explicitly stores the geometric coordinates of landmarks and the robot's pose. Perceptual methods are robust to odometry drift, work in feature-poor environments, and yield compact maps, whereas metric methods provide precise spatial estimates but are vulnerable to cumulative error.

### Question 2. What is *perceptual aliasing*, and how does the Hidden Markov Model (HMM) framework help to resolve it? `[3 Marks]` `[CO4 / Bloom: Understand]`

**Model Answer:**
Perceptual aliasing is the phenomenon in which two *physically distinct* places in the environment produce *identical* or statistically indistinguishable sensor signatures (e.g., two identical hospital corridors under similar lighting). A pure nearest-neighbor signature matcher will confuse the two places. The HMM framework resolves this by exploiting **temporal context**: it models the probability $P(q_{t+1} = L_j \mid q_t = L_i)$ of transitioning between places. Even if signature $s_t$ matches both $L_i$ and $L_j$, the Viterbi algorithm will select the place that is consistent with the past observation history. Thus, sequential inference disambiguates aliasing, dramatically improving localization accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern | Module 4]**
> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Histogram-Based Perceptual Signatures

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a histogram-based perceptual place recognition system. `[7 Marks]` `[CO4 / Bloom: Apply]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Overview:**
A histogram-based perceptual place recognition system treats each place as characterized by a normalized histogram of sensor measurements (e.g., range or color).

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (in text form):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Sensor | Range scanner or camera produces observation $o_t$ |
| 2. Pre-processor | Noise filtering, region-of-interest extraction |
| 3. Binning | Discretize each measurement into bin $k$ |
| 4. Histogram Builder | Compute $h(k) = \text{card}\{i \mid z_i \in \text{bin}_k\}$ |
| 5. Normalization | Convert to probability $p(k) = h(k)/\sum h$ |
| 6. Database | Stores all known place histograms |
| 7. Matcher | Compares current $p$ with stored $p_j$ via Bhattacharyya / chi-squared |
| 8. Decision | If $d(p, p_j) \le \tau$, output place $L_j$ |

**Step 3 — Matching:**
For two normalized histograms $p, q$ of $B$ bins, the Bhattacharyya distance is:

$$d_B(p, q) = -\ln \sum_{k=1}^{B} \sqrt{p(k) q(k)}$$

If $d_B \le \tau$, the two scans are declared perceptually equivalent.

**Step 4 — Advantages:**
- Invariant to small pose changes.
- Compact (only $B$ floats per place).
- Computationally cheap.

#### Part (b) — Two normalized histograms are given as $p = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]$ and $q = [0.4, 0.4, 0.2]$. Compute the Euclidean, Manhattan, Chi-squared, and Bhattacharyya distances. Decide whether they belong to the same place if $\tau = 0.15$ for Bhattacharyya. `[7 Marks]` `[CO4 / Bloom: Apply]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Euclidean Distance:**

$$d_E = \sqrt{(0.5-0.4)^2 + (0.3-0.4)^2 + (0.2-0.2)^2}$$

$$d_E = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.01 + 0} = \sqrt{0.02} \approx 0.1414$$

**Step 2 — Manhattan Distance:**

$$d_M = \vert 0.1 \vert + \vert -0.1 \vert + \vert 0 \vert = 0.1 + 0.1 + 0 = 0.2$$

**Step 3 — Chi-Squared Distance:**

$$d_{\chi^2} = \frac{(0.5-0.4)^2}{0.5+0.4} + \frac{(0.3-0.4)^2}{0.3+0.4} + \frac{(0.2-0.2)^2}{0.2+0.2}$$

$$d_{\chi^2} = \frac{0.01}{0.9} + \frac{0.01}{0.7} + 0 = 0.0111 + 0.0143 + 0 = 0.0254$$

**Step 4 — Bhattacharyya Distance:**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{3} \sqrt{p(k) q(k)} = \sqrt{0.5 \cdot 0.4} + \sqrt{0.3 \cdot 0.4} + \sqrt{0.2 \cdot 0.2}$$

$$= \sqrt{0.20} + \sqrt{0.12} + \sqrt{0.04} = 0.4472 + 0.3464 + 0.2000 = 0.9936$$

$$d_B = -\ln(0.9936) = 0.00641$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**
Since $d_B = 0.0064 \le \tau = 0.15$, the two histograms belong to the **same place**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — HMM-Based Place Recognition

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern | Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the three fundamental problems of HMMs. Map each problem to a specific use in mobile robot perceptual localization. `[7 Marks]` `[CO4 / Bloom: Understand]`

**Model Solution:**

A Hidden Markov Model is defined by $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$, where $A$ is the state transition matrix, $B$ the emission probability matrix, and $\pi$ the initial state distribution. The three canonical HMM problems are:

**Problem 1 — Evaluation:** Given the model $\lambda$ and an observation sequence $O = (o_1, \ldots, o_T)$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$.

*Mobile-robot use:* "How well does this learned map explain the robot's observed perceptual-signature sequence?" The **Forward algorithm** is used.

**Problem 2 — Decoding:** Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the most likely state sequence $Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$.

*Mobile-robot use:* "What is the most likely sequence of *places* the robot visited along this trajectory?" The **Viterbi algorithm** is used, and this is the workhorse of perceptual localization.

**Problem 3 — Learning:** Given only $O$, find the model $\lambda^* = \arg\max_\lambda P(O \mid \lambda)$ that best fits the data.

*Mobile-robot use:* "Learn the place-transition probabilities and the emission probabilities automatically from a training run, without hand-labelling every place." The **Baum–Welch algorithm (Expectation–Maximization)** is used.

#### Part (b) — A robot uses an HMM with 2 places $L_1, L_2$. The initial probabilities are $\pi = [0.7, 0.3]$. Transition matrix: $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{pmatrix}$. Emission matrix (for observations $o_x, o_y$): $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{pmatrix}$. Given observation sequence $O = (o_x, o_y)$, run the Viterbi algorithm to find the most likely place sequence. `[7 Marks]` `[CO4 / Bloom: Apply]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialization (t = 1, $o_1 = o_x$):**
- $\delta_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(o_x) = 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56$, $\psi_1(1) = 0$
- $\delta_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(o_x) = 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.09$, $\psi_1(2) = 0$

**Step 2 — Recursion (t = 2, $o_2 = o_y$):**
- $\delta_2(1) = \max_i [\delta_1(i) \cdot a_{i1}] \cdot b_1(o_y)$
- $\delta_2(1) = \max[0.56 \times 0.6, \ 0.09 \times 0.3] \times 0.2$
- $\delta_2(1) = \max[0.336, 0.027] \times 0.2 = 0.336 \times 0.2 = 0.0672$
- $\psi_2(1) = 1$

- $\delta_2(2) = \max_i [\delta_1(i) \cdot a_{i2}] \cdot b_2(o_y)$
- $\delta_2(2) = \max[0.56 \times 0.4, \ 0.09 \times 0.7] \times 0.7$
- $\delta_2(2) = \max[0.224, 0.063] \times 0.7 = 0.224 \times 0.7 = 0.1568$
- $\psi_2(2) = 1$

**Step 3 — Termination:**
- $P^* = \max[\delta_2(1), \delta_2(2)] = \max[0.0672, 0.1568] = 0.1568$
- $q_2^* = \arg\max = 2$

**Step 4 — Backtrace:**
- $q_1^* = \psi_2(q_2^*) = \psi_2(2) = 1$

**Most Likely Place Sequence:** $Q^* = (L_1, L_2)$ with probability $P^* = 0.1568$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the emission probability multiplication** — students often compute $\delta_2(j) = \max_i [\delta_1(i) a_{ij}]$ and stop, missing the crucial $b_j(o_{t+1})$ term. Marks are deducted for this.
> 2. **Confusing Forward and Viterbi** — Forward *sums* over all paths; Viterbi takes the *maximum*. Using the wrong operator yields wrong answers. Examiners explicitly check this.
> 3. **Omitting the backpointer** $\psi$ — without $\psi$, you cannot backtrace the path. The question explicitly asks for the *sequence*, not just the maximum probability.
> 4. **Not normalising histograms** before applying Bhattacharyya / chi-squared metrics. Always normalise to a probability distribution first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU University Exam.**

- ✅ **Perceptual structure** = representing a place by its **sensor signature**, not by metric coordinates $(x, y, \theta)$.
- ✅ **Perceptual equivalence** = two observations belong to the same place iff $d(s_i, s_j) \le \tau$.
- ✅ **Signatures** can be **range histograms, color histograms, Fourier signatures, PCA eigenplaces, or RSSI fingerprints**.
- ✅ **Histograms are probability distributions** — use **Bhattacharyya** or **chi-squared** distances, *not* raw Euclidean.
- ✅ **Fourier signatures** $\vert F(\omega) \vert$ provide **rotation invariance** for range scans.
- ✅ **PCA / Eigenplaces** compress panoramic images into $k \approx 10$–$50$ dimensional signatures.
- ✅ **HMM** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ models the temporal evolution of places.
- ✅ **Forward algorithm** = total probability $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — sums over all paths.
- ✅ **Viterbi algorithm** = most-likely single path — takes the max.
- ✅ **Baum–Welch** = unsupervised learning of $A, B, \pi$ from observed trajectories.
- ✅ **Perceptual aliasing** is resolved by **temporal context** in the HMM.
- ✅ **Topological map** is the canonical data structure for perceptual maps — nodes are places, edges are navigable transitions.
- ✅ **Non-geometric methods are robust to odometry drift** but cannot directly provide metric control commands (e.g., "go to $(5, 5)$").
- ✅ **Hybrid metric-topological maps** combine the best of both: place labels + relative distances.
- ✅ **Standard sensors used in KTU-board-style problems:** sonar rings, 2D LIDAR, omnidirectional cameras, Wi-Fi receivers.
- ✅ **For 14-mark questions, always** include a **block diagram, formulas with units, and a worked numerical example** — examiners reward systematic presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlation Based Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Correlation Based Localization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Correlation Based Localization (CBL)** is a *map-matching* localization technique in mobile robotics in which the robot estimates its pose $(x, y, \theta)$ within a known geometric map by maximizing a similarity (correlation) measure between the *current* sensor scan and the *predicted* scan obtained by casting virtual rays from a candidate pose through the map. Formally, the localization problem reduces to: $$ (x^*, y^*, \theta^*) = \arg\max_{(x,y,\theta)} \; C\big(S_{actual}, \, S_{predicted}(x,y,\theta)\big) $$ where $S_{actual}$ is the live sensor scan and $S_{predicted}(x,y,\theta)$ is the scan predicted from the map at pose $(x,y,\theta)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Correlation-based methods belong to the family of **scan-matching / map-matching localization** algorithms. They are predominantly applied with **range sensors** (2D/3D LiDAR, sonar rings) and *a priori* occupancy grid or feature maps. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into a familiar room in your house. To find where you are, you clap (emit a "scan") and listen to the echo. The way the sound bounces back forms a unique *acoustic fingerprint* of your position. You mentally compare this fingerprint with the memory of how echoes sounded from various spots in the room. The spot whose predicted echo matches what you just heard the best — *that* is your most likely location. **Correlation Based Localization works identically:** - The **clap** → a LiDAR / sonar scan reading distances to walls/obstacles. - The **memory of echoes** → the pre-built geometric map of the environment. - The **best match** → the robot's pose estimate. > [!NOTE] > **Physical & Standard Metrics in Correlation Localization** > - Sensor range: typically **0.05 m to 30 m** (LiDAR); **0.15 m to 6 m** (sonar). > - Scan resolution: angular step **0.25° – 1°** (LiDAR); **~15°** (sonar rings). > - Search resolution: position **1 cm – 10 cm**, heading **0.5° – 2°**. > - Map resolution (occupancy grid): **1 cm – 20 cm per cell**. ### 1.3 Where Does CBL Sit in the Localization Taxonomy? ```text Localization in Mobile Robotics ├── Position Tracking (local, known initial pose) ├── Global Localization (no initial pose assumption) │ ├── Markov Localization │ ├── Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filters) │ └── Correlation / Scan-Matching ← (THIS TOPIC) └── Kidnapped Robot Problem (re-localization after displacement) ``` > [!TIP] > Correlation methods are often used **as the measurement model** inside a probabilistic framework (e.g., particle filter), where the correlation score is converted into a likelihood weight for each particle. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Correlation Based Localization The method follows a structured sequence. Each step is mandatory for a working implementation. 1. **Acquire Current Scan** $S_{actual}$: Read $N$ range measurements $\{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_N\}$ from the range sensor. 2. **Initialize Search Window**: Define a region of interest around the prior pose estimate $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ with bounds $(\Delta x_{max}, \Delta y_{max}, \Delta \theta_{max})$. 3. **For Each Candidate Pose** $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$: - Transform sensor origin to the candidate pose in the global frame. - For every beam $i$ with relative angle $\phi_i$ and measured range $z_i$: - Compute the beam's end-point in the global frame. - Trace the beam (ray-casting) through the occupancy grid map. - Record the predicted range $z^{pred}_i$ (distance to first occupied cell). 4. **Compute Correlation Score** $C_k$ between actual scan and predicted scan. 5. **Select Best Pose**: $(x^*, y^*, \theta^*) = \arg\max_k C_k$. 6. **(Optional) Refine**: Repeat at a finer resolution around the winner. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why ray-casting instead of nearest-feature lookup?** Ray-casting directly models the physical operation of range sensors, producing predicted ranges that are statistically consistent with the actual measurements. This makes the comparison fair and accurate. - **Why use a bounded search window?** For real-time operation, exhaustive search over the entire map is computationally infeasible. Bounding the search reduces complexity from $O(|Map|)$ to $O(\frac{\Delta x \cdot \Delta y \cdot \Delta \theta}{r_x \cdot r_y \cdot r_\theta})$ where $r$ denotes resolution. - **Why correlation and not distance metric?** Correlation (especially normalized variants) is more robust to dynamic objects, occlusions, and partial overlap than point-to-point Euclidean distance metrics. ### 2.3 The Three Canonical Correlation Forms #### (a) Un-normalized Cross-Correlation (CCC) $$ C_{CCC} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} z^{pred}_i \cdot z^{actual}_i $$ Used when both scans have similar dynamic range. #### (b) Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) $$ C_{NCC} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} z^{pred}_i \cdot z^{actual}_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (z^{pred}_i)^2} \; \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (z^{actual}_i)^2}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **NCC is the most examiner-favoured form in KTU questions** because it is bounded in $[-1, +1]$ and is invariant to uniform scaling of the scans — useful when different sensors or different times produce scans of varying magnitudes. #### (c) Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) — Minimization Equivalent $$ C_{SSD} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big(z^{pred}_i - z^{actual}_i\big)^2 \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{minimize} $$ Mathematically tied to correlation: minimizing SSD = maximizing correlation (under linearity assumptions). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Units / Range** | |---|---|---| | $(x, y, \theta)$ | Robot pose in 2D | m, m, rad | | $S_{actual} = \{z_1, \dots, z_N\}$ | Actual sensor scan, $N$ beams | metres | | $S_{pred}(x,y,\theta) = \{z^{pred}_1, \dots, z^{pred}_N\}$ | Predicted scan at candidate pose | metres | | $C_{CCC} = \sum z^{pred}_i z^{actual}_i$ | Un-normalized cross-correlation | $\text{m}^2$ | | $C_{NCC} = \frac{\sum z^{pred}_i z^{actual}_i}{\sqrt{\sum (z^{pred}_i)^2 \sum (z^{actual}_i)^2}}$ | Normalized cross-correlation | dimensionless, $[-1, +1]$ | | $C_{SSD} = \sum (z^{pred}_i - z^{actual}_i)^2$ | Sum of squared differences | $\text{m}^2$ | | $(x^*, y^*, \theta^*) = \arg\max C$ | Optimal pose estimate | m, m, rad | | $\Phi(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)$ | Search-space volume | $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{rad}$ | | $r_{grid}$ | Occupancy grid resolution | m / cell | | $H = -\sum p \log p$ | (Optional) Mutual information score | bits | | $T_{search} = O(N \cdot M_{rays} \cdot K_{cand})$ | Computational complexity | seconds | > **Where the vertical bar `\|` symbol appears in equations (e.g., absolute value, determinant, cardinality), it is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.** ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Modern AV stacks use LiDAR scan-to-map correlation (often called *point cloud registration*, e.g., NDT — Normal Distributions Transform) as a sub-module of the localization pipeline. - **AGVs in Warehouses:** Automated Guided Vehicles use correlation-based LiDAR matching against pre-built warehouse maps for cm-level accuracy. - **Domestic Vacuum & Lawn Robots:** Cheaper units use correlation of sonar / IR scans with stored maps for room-level localization. - **AR/VR & Indoor Positioning:** Smartphones correlate depth-camera point clouds with floor plans for indoor AR anchors. - **Search & Rescue Robots:** Correlation matching in partially known disaster environments helps re-localize a robot after a fall or a "kidnap" event. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Beam-End-Point Transform This is the **core geometric operation** that connects the candidate pose to a predicted scan. Let the robot candidate pose in the world frame be $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, \theta)^\top$. The $i$-th beam has a relative angle $\phi_i$ in the sensor frame and returns range $z^{actual}_i$. The beam's end-point in the *sensor frame* is: $$ \mathbf{q}_i^{sensor} = \begin{pmatrix} z^{actual}_i \cos \phi_i \\ z^{actual}_i \sin \phi_i \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ To transform this to the *world frame*, we apply the rotation $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$ and translation $\mathbf{t} = (x, y)^\top$: $$ \mathbf{q}_i^{world} = \mathbf{R}(\theta) \, \mathbf{q}_i^{sensor} + \mathbf{t} $$ The rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$ in 2D is: $$ \mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} $$ Substituting explicitly: $$ \begin{aligned} x_i^{world} &= x + z^{actual}_i \cos(\theta + \phi_i) \\ y_i^{world} &= y + z^{actual}_i \sin(\theta + \phi_i) \end{aligned} $$ > **Textual Logic:** The compound angle $\theta + \phi_i$ accounts for both the robot's heading and the beam's angle within the sensor frame. The cosine and sine project the polar beam coordinates onto Cartesian axes, then the offset $(x, y)$ places them in the global frame. This is the standard **2D rigid-body transformation**. ### 3.2 Derivation of Normalized Cross-Correlation from First Principles We start from the **Pearson correlation** definition, which measures *linear similarity* between two vectors $\mathbf{a} = (a_1, \dots, a_N)$ and $\mathbf{b} = (b_1, \dots, b_N)$: $$ \rho(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{\text{Cov}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b})}{\sigma_{\mathbf{a}} \, \sigma_{\mathbf{b}}} $$ Expand the covariance: $$ \text{Cov}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})(b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}}) $$ Expand the standard deviations: $$ \sigma_{\mathbf{a}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})^2}, \quad \sigma_{\mathbf{b}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}})^2} $$ Combining: $$ \rho(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})(b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}})}{\sqrt{\sum (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})^2 \sum (b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}})^2}} $$ **Special Case — Zero-Mean Scans** (a common engineering approximation when range is short and centred about a typical obstacle distance): $$ \mu_{\mathbf{a}} \approx 0, \quad \mu_{\mathbf{b}} \approx 0 $$ The expression reduces to the **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** used in scan matching: $$ C_{NCC} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \, b_i}{\sqrt{\sum a_i^2 \; \sum b_i^2}} $$ > **Textual Logic:** Centring the data removes the bias term, giving the *zero-mean* correlation form. This is mathematically equivalent to taking the cosine of the angle between the two scan vectors in an $N$-dimensional space: $$ C_{NCC} = \cos(\angle(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b})) = \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\vert \mathbf{a} \vert \, \vert \mathbf{b} \vert} $$ This cosine interpretation is often useful for KTU problems asking for a geometric or intuitive justification of NCC. ### 3.3 Worked Example: Computing NCC for a Simple 2-Beam Scan **Given:** - Actual scan: $\mathbf{a} = (3.0 \text{ m}, \; 4.0 \text{ m})$ - Predicted scan at candidate pose: $\mathbf{b} = (3.1 \text{ m}, \; 3.9 \text{ m})$ - $N = 2$ **Step 1: Numerator (dot product)** $$ \sum a_i b_i = (3.0)(3.1) + (4.0)(3.9) = 9.3 + 15.6 = 24.9 $$ **Step 2: Denominator** $$ \sum a_i^2 = (3.0)^2 + (4.0)^2 = 9.0 + 16.0 = 25.0 $$ $$ \sum b_i^2 = (3.1)^2 + (3.9)^2 = 9.61 + 15.21 = 24.82 $$ **Step 3: Combine** $$ C_{NCC} = \frac{24.9}{\sqrt{25.0 \times 24.82}} = \frac{24.9}{\sqrt{620.5}} = \frac{24.9}{24.91} \approx 0.9996 $$ > **Interpretation:** $C_{NCC} \approx 1.0$ indicates an almost perfect match — the candidate pose is very likely the correct robot location. ### 3.4 Multi-Resolution Search Derivation (Hierarchical Correlation) **Motivation:** Exhaustive search at full resolution over a $\pm 0.5$ m, $\pm 30°$ window is expensive. **Strategy:** Build a *Gaussian image pyramid* of the predicted-scan field. Let the correlation field at level $\ell$ be $C^{(\ell)}(x, y, \theta)$. Define level transformations: $$ C^{(\ell+1)}(x, y, \theta) = C^{(\ell)}(2x, 2y, 2\theta) \quad \text{(coarser level)} $$ Search proceeds: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Level 3 (coarsest, e.g., 10 cm / 4°):} && (x_3^*, y_3^*, \theta_3^*) = \arg\max C^{(3)} \\ &\text{Level 2 (5 cm / 2°):} && \text{search } \pm 2 \text{ cells around } (x_3^*, y_3^*, \theta_3^*) \\ &\text{Level 1 (1 cm / 0.5°):} && \text{search } \pm 2 \text{ cells around } (x_2^*, y_2^*, \theta_2^*) \\ &\text{Level 0 (finest):} && \text{Final answer } (x^*, y^*, \theta^*) \end{aligned} $$ **Computational Savings:** If the coarse level has $M$ cells and each finer level restricts to a $5 \times 5 \times 5$ neighbourhood, the total candidates drop from $M$ to: $$ N_{cand} = M + 3 \times 125 = M + 375 $$ For a typical $M = 10\,000$ exhaustive candidates, this gives a **~96%** reduction in computation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of CBL ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Range Sensor e.g. LiDAR or Sonar] --> B[Acquire Scan S_actual] B --> C[Pose Prior x0 y0 theta0] C --> D[Search Window Definition] D --> E[Iterate Candidate Poses] E --> F[Beam Transform to World Frame] F --> G[Ray Casting through Occupancy Grid Map] G --> H[Predicted Scan S_pred] H --> I[Compute Correlation Score C_NCC or C_CCC] I --> J{Score Greater than Best so far} J -- Yes --> K[Update Best Score and Pose] J -- No --> L[Discard Candidate] K --> E L --> E E --> M{All Candidates Tested} M -- No --> E M -- Yes --> N[Optimal Pose Estimate x star y star theta star] N --> O[Optional Hierarchical Refinement] O --> P[Output to Localizer or Particle Filter] ``` ### 4.2 Map Representation and Ray-Casting Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MAP[Occupancy Grid Map] C1[Free Cell] C2[Occupied Cell] C3[Unknown Cell] C4[Free Cell] end subgraph RC[Ray Casting Module] R1[Start at Beam Origin] R2[Step along Bresenham or DDA Line] R3{Encountered Occupied Cell} R4[Yes - Record Distance and Stop] R5[No - Continue Stepping] end R1 --> R2 --> R5 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 -- Hit --> R4 R3 -- Miss --> R5 ``` ### 4.3 Multi-Resolution Search Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD L0[Level 0: 1 cm, 0.5 degree resolution
Final refinement search] L1[Level 1: 2 cm, 1 degree resolution] L2[Level 2: 5 cm, 2 degree resolution] L3[Level 3: 10 cm, 4 degree resolution
Coarse global search] L3 -->|winner passes to next level| L2 L2 -->|winner passes to next level| L1 L1 -->|winner passes to next level| L0 L0 --> OUT[Final pose x star y star theta star] ``` ### 4.4 Data Flow Inside a Particle Filter (CBL as Likelihood) ```mermaid flowchart LR PF1[Particle Set Poses] --> PFW[Assign Each Particle a Weight] S[Current Scan S_actual] --> PFW MP[Map] --> PFW PFW --> PF2[Compute Correlation per Particle] PF2 --> PF3[Convert Correlation to Likelihood] PF3 --> PF4[Weighted Resampling] PF4 --> PF5[Refined Pose Estimate] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping:** Since correlation-based localization is an algorithmic process rather than a physical hardware schematic, the diagrams above map the **information flow** between the sensor, the map, the search engine, and the pose estimator. This is the standard architecture you should sketch in KTU exam answers when a "block diagram" is asked. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Correlation Based Localization. State any TWO advantages and TWO limitations of the method.* **Model Answer:** Correlation Based Localization (CBL) is a map-matching technique in which the robot estimates its pose $(x, y, \theta)$ by finding the position at which the *predicted* sensor scan (computed by ray-casting through a known map) shows the maximum similarity with the *actual* sensor scan. **Advantages:** 1. Highly accurate (sub-cm possible with good LiDAR) since it uses direct range geometry. 2. Does not require explicit feature extraction (works on raw scans). **Limitations:** 1. Computationally expensive due to the search over the pose space. 2. Performance degrades in featureless or highly symmetric environments (the correlation surface becomes flat or multi-modal). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *only* list advantages without comparing to alternative methods (e.g., particle filters, EKF). For a 3-mark question, KTU expects a balanced view. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Write the mathematical expression for the Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) used in scan matching. Explain why it is preferred over the un-normalized cross-correlation.* **Model Answer:** The NCC between predicted scan $\mathbf{p}$ and actual scan $\mathbf{a}$ is: $$ C_{NCC} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \, a_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i^2 \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^2}} $$ **Why preferred:** 1. Bounded output in $[-1, +1]$ makes thresholding and weight assignment easier. 2. Invariant to uniform scaling of the scan magnitudes, so a partially occluded scan still correlates reasonably with a full predicted scan. 3. Cosine-angle interpretation: $C_{NCC} = \cos\theta$ between the two $N$-dimensional scan vectors, providing a clear geometric meaning. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** (a) **Describe the complete operational pipeline of correlation-based localization with the help of a block diagram. Explain the role of ray-casting in predicting the scan at a candidate pose.** *(7 marks)* (b) **Derive the Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) formula from the Pearson correlation coefficient. Compute the NCC for the following 3-beam scan pair.** *(7 marks)* Actual scan: $\mathbf{a} = (2.0, \; 4.0, \; 3.0)$ m Predicted scan: $\mathbf{p} = (2.1, \; 3.8, \; 3.2)$ m --- **Model Solution (a):** The complete pipeline of correlation-based localization consists of the following sequential blocks: 1. **Sensor Data Acquisition:** A 2D range sensor (LiDAR / sonar) emits $N$ beams covering the horizontal field of view (typically $180°$–$360°$). Each beam returns a range value $z_i$ (and optionally an intensity). This forms the **actual scan** $S_{actual} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_N\}$. 2. **Pose Prior Initialization:** From odometry or a previous estimate, an initial pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ is used to define the search window. 3. **Search Window Definition:** Bounds $(\pm \Delta x, \pm \Delta y, \pm \Delta \theta)$ and resolutions $(r_x, r_y, r_\theta)$ are chosen. 4. **Candidate Pose Iteration:** For each $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$ in the discretized window, the following sub-steps are performed. 5. **Beam Transform:** The $i$-th beam endpoint in the world frame is computed as: $$ (x_i^{world}, y_i^{world}) = (x_k + z_i \cos(\theta_k + \phi_i), \; y_k + z_i \sin(\theta_k + \phi_i)) $$ 6. **Ray-Casting (Map Lookup):** From the beam origin $(x_k, y_k)$ in the direction $(\theta_k + \phi_i)$, the algorithm walks through the occupancy grid cells (using Bresenham or DDA line algorithm) until it encounters an *occupied* cell. The distance to this cell is the **predicted range** $z^{pred}_i$. *Role of ray-casting:* It is the only mechanism that translates a candidate pose into a *physically meaningful* predicted scan consistent with the actual sensor's operating principle. Without ray-casting, the predicted scan cannot be compared to the actual scan on a fair, like-for-like basis. 7. **Correlation Computation:** The vector of $N$ predicted ranges is compared with the $N$ actual ranges using NCC (or similar). 8. **Best-Pose Selection:** The candidate $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$ with the highest score is output as the pose estimate. 9. **Hierarchical Refinement (Optional):** A finer-resolution search is conducted around the best pose. *(Block diagram should follow the architecture from Section 4.1.)* **Model Solution (b):** **Derivation of NCC from Pearson Correlation:** The Pearson correlation coefficient between two vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is defined as: $$ \rho = \frac{\text{Cov}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b})}{\sigma_{\mathbf{a}} \sigma_{\mathbf{b}}} $$ Expanding covariance: $$ \text{Cov}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})(b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}}) $$ Expanding the standard deviations: $$ \sigma_{\mathbf{a}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})^2}, \quad \sigma_{\mathbf{b}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}})^2} $$ Substituting and simplifying: $$ \rho = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})(b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (a_i - \mu_{\mathbf{a}})^2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} (b_i - \mu_{\mathbf{b}})^2}} $$ When the mean values are approximately zero (which is often assumed for short-range scans centred on a typical obstacle distance), the formula reduces to the **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)**: $$ C_{NCC} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i b_i}{\sqrt{\sum a_i^2 \sum b_i^2}} $$ Geometrically, this is the cosine of the angle between the two scan vectors in $N$-dimensional space. **Numerical Computation:** Given: $\mathbf{a} = (2.0, 4.0, 3.0)$ and $\mathbf{p} = (2.1, 3.8, 3.2)$. **Step 1 — Numerator (dot product):** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum p_i a_i &= (2.1)(2.0) + (3.8)(4.0) + (3.2)(3.0) \\ &= 4.2 + 15.2 + 9.6 \\ &= 29.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Denominator components:** $$ \sum a_i^2 = (2.0)^2 + (4.0)^2 + (3.0)^2 = 4.0 + 16.0 + 9.0 = 29.0 $$ $$ \sum p_i^2 = (2.1)^2 + (3.8)^2 + (3.2)^2 = 4.41 + 14.44 + 10.24 = 29.09 $$ **Step 3 — Combine:** $$ C_{NCC} = \frac{29.0}{\sqrt{29.0 \times 29.09}} = \frac{29.0}{\sqrt{843.61}} = \frac{29.0}{29.044} \approx 0.9985 $$ > **Conclusion:** $C_{NCC} \approx 0.9985$ — an extremely high correlation, indicating that the candidate pose is very close to the true robot pose. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** (a) **Compare correlation-based localization with feature-based localization. Discuss the role of the occupancy grid map in scan-matching.** *(7 marks)* (b) **Explain hierarchical (multi-resolution) correlation search with a neat sketch. Show that the computational complexity is reduced by approximately an order of magnitude.** *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** **Comparison Table:** | **Aspect** | **Correlation Based (Scan-Match)** | **Feature Based (Landmark)** | |---|---|---| | Input data | Raw range scan (point cloud) | Extracted geometric features (lines, corners, circles) | | Map format | Occupancy grid / point cloud | Landmark database with descriptors | | Computation | High (ray-casting per candidate) | Moderate (correspondence search) | | Robustness to clutter | High (raw data is dense) | Lower (feature extraction may fail) | | Robustness to symmetry | Lower (multi-modal correlation) | Higher (features disambiguate) | | Memory | High (entire map stored) | Low (sparse landmarks) | | Accuracy | Sub-cm (LiDAR-based) | cm–dm | | Typical sensors | 2D/3D LiDAR, sonar rings | Camera, LiDAR with feature extractors | **Role of the Occupancy Grid Map in Scan-Matching:** The occupancy grid map is a 2D discretized representation of the environment in which each cell stores the *probability of being occupied*. For scan-matching, the map serves as the **reference geometry** against which candidate poses are scored. Specifically, the grid enables: 1. **Ray-Casting:** A fast discrete approximation of the continuous ray-tracing problem. Bresenham / DDA line algorithms step cell-by-cell until an occupied cell is hit, giving the predicted range. 2. **Spatial Indexing:** The grid provides $O(1)$ cell lookup, allowing rapid querying of the cell at any $(x, y)$. 3. **Probabilistic Fusion:** Map cells hold log-odds probabilities, allowing updates from multiple past scans. 4. **Memory Efficiency:** A 50 m × 50 m map at 5 cm resolution requires only $10^6$ cells (~ 1 MB), making it practical for embedded robots. **Model Solution (b):** **Hierarchical Multi-Resolution Search:** The motivation is that exhaustive search at the finest resolution is computationally expensive. We construct a **Gaussian / mean-filtered image pyramid** of the correlation field $C(x, y, \theta)$. **Sketch (Mermaid Block):** ```mermaid flowchart TD L0[Coarsest Level L3: 10 cm, 4 degree steps
Global Search over Whole Window] L1[Level L2: 5 cm, 2 degree steps
Neighbourhood of L3 Winner] L2[Level L1: 2 cm, 1 degree steps
Neighbourhood of L2 Winner] L3[Finest Level L0: 1 cm, 0.5 degree steps
Neighbourhood of L1 Winner] L0 -->|winner pose| L1 L1 -->|winner pose| L2 L2 -->|winner pose| L3 L3 --> F[Final Pose Estimate] ``` **Computational Analysis:** Let the search window be $\Phi = \Delta x \cdot \Delta y \cdot \Delta \theta$ and the candidate count at the finest resolution be $K_{fine}$. **Exhaustive search:** $$ K_{exh} = \frac{\Delta x}{r_x} \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{r_y} \cdot \frac{\Delta \theta}{r_\theta} $$ For a typical $1$ m $\times$ $1$ m $\times$ $60°$ window at $1$ cm and $0.5°$: $$ K_{exh} = \frac{1}{0.01} \cdot \frac{1}{0.01} \cdot \frac{60}{0.5} = 100 \times 100 \times 120 = 1.2 \times 10^6 $$ **Hierarchical search (3 levels + finest):** | Level | Resolution | Search Region | Candidates | |---|---|---|---| | L3 | 10 cm, 4° | Whole window | $10 \times 10 \times 15 = 1500$ | | L2 | 5 cm, 2° | $\pm 1$ coarse cell | $3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27$ | | L1 | 2 cm, 1° | $\pm 1$ L2 cell | $3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27$ | | L0 | 1 cm, 0.5° | $\pm 1$ L1 cell | $3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27$ | **Total hierarchical candidates:** $$ K_{hier} = 1500 + 27 + 27 + 27 \approx 1581 $$ **Reduction factor:** $$ \frac{K_{exh}}{K_{hier}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{1581} \approx 759 $$ > This is **nearly three orders of magnitude** reduction — an order of magnitude or more as the question states. The savings become more dramatic as the search window grows. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mark Loss):** > 1. **Forgetting the ray-casting step** when describing the scan-prediction process — you lose 2 marks easily. > 2. **Skipping the derivation** in NCC and jumping straight to the formula — KTU examiners expect you to show the Pearson → NCC transition explicitly. > 3. **Forgetting the units** in numerical computations (e.g., writing `sqrt(843.61)` without indicating metres²) — always annotate units in the final formula. > 4. **Confusing Correlation with Convolution** — they are related but not identical. Cross-correlation uses a non-flipped kernel, while convolution flips it. Be precise. > 5. **Not commenting on the limitations** of CBL (e.g., symmetry, computational cost) — the examiner's key explicitly awards a mark for limitations. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** CBL estimates robot pose by maximizing a similarity (correlation) measure between actual and map-predicted scans. - **Core Equation:** $(x^*, y^*, \theta^*) = \arg\max C\big(S_{actual}, S_{pred}(x,y,\theta)\big)$. - **Three Correlation Forms to Memorise:** Un-normalized CCC, **Normalized NCC (preferred)**, and SSD. - **Beam Transform Formula:** $(x_i^w, y_i^w) = (x + z_i \cos(\theta + \phi_i),\; y + z_i \sin(\theta + \phi_i))$. - **Ray-casting** is the bridge between candidate pose and predicted scan. - **NCC Range:** $[-1, +1]$; equals **+1** for perfect match. - **Pearson → NCC:** Subtract means, then take the cosine of the angle between the two scan vectors. - **Search Techniques:** Exhaustive (slow but global), Multi-Resolution / Hierarchical (fast, coarse-to-fine), Gradient-based (fastest but local). - **Computational Cost:** Dominated by $N_{beams} \times N_{candidates} \times N_{ray-steps}$; multi-resolution reduces this by orders of magnitude. - **Map Requirement:** A *known* occupancy grid (or point cloud) must be available. - **Failure Modes:** Symmetric environments (e.g., long corridors, square rooms), dynamic obstacles, and featureless regions. - **Integration:** Often used as the measurement model of a particle filter or as a fine-localization step after a coarse global method. - **Cosine Interpretation:** $C_{NCC} = \cos(\angle(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}))$ in $N$-dimensional scan-vector space — use this in KTU answers for intuitive explanation. - **Real-World Use Cases:** Self-driving cars, warehouse AGVs, domestic cleaning robots, AR indoor positioning, search-and-rescue re-localization. - **Key Sensors:** 2D/3D LiDAR, sonar rings, depth cameras. - **Resolution Tip:** For a 14-mark question, always quote the *expected accuracy* (cm-level) and *typical map resolution* (1–10 cm) in your answer.

topics.title: Global Localization Mapping: Sensorial Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT522 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Localization Mapping: Sensorial Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Global Localization — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Localization** is the problem of determining a robot's pose (position $x, y$ and orientation $\theta$) within a *known* global reference map, **without any prior estimate** of the starting location. This is fundamentally distinct from **position tracking**, where an initial pose estimate is already available and only incremental corrections are needed.

Mathematically, the global localization problem can be stated as:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

where:
- $x_t$ is the robot pose at time $t$
- $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_t\}$ is the history of sensor measurements
- $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_t\}$ is the history of control actions
- $m$ is the **known global map**
- $bel(x_t)$ is the belief distribution over the robot's pose

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase **"Kidnapped Robot Problem"** is the most extreme variant of global localization — the robot is teleported to an arbitrary unknown location mid-operation and must recover its pose purely from sensor data.

---

## 1.2 Sensorial Maps — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Sensorial Map** (or **Sensor Map**) is a representation of the environment that is *directly comparable* to the robot's sensor readings. Unlike a purely geometric or semantic map, a sensorial map is structured so that a sensor scan can be *matched*, *correlated*, or *registered* against the map to extract pose information.

In essence, a sensorial map is a **bridge between perception and representation**. The map and the sensor must "speak the same language."

| Property | Geometric Map | Sensorial Map |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| Storage format | Polygons, lines, points | Grid cells, occupancy values |
| Comparability with sensors | Indirect (requires feature extraction) | Direct (raw sensor-like format) |
| Use case | Path planning, rendering | Localization, scan matching |
| Noise handling | Not built-in | Inherently probabilistic |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Fingerprint" of an Environment

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into an unfamiliar city. You are given only a *sensor*: a long pole that pokes walls and tells you distances. Now imagine someone hands you a **pre-built tactile map of the city** — also created using identical long-pole measurements. By sweeping your pole around and comparing the resulting "distance signature" against the pre-built map at every possible position, you can pinpoint where you are.

This "tactile map" is a **sensorial map** — it stores information in the *same modality* as the sensor provides. The robot's job is to align its current sensor signature with the stored signature. Just as a fingerprint at a crime scene is matched against a database, a laser scan is matched against a sensorial map.

The key engineering insight is:

$$m_{sensorial} \approx f^{-1}(z_{expected})$$

The sensorial map is the **inverse projection** of expected sensor measurements back into world coordinates.

---

## 1.4 Why Sensorial Maps Matter in Mobile Robotics

- **Direct correlation:** No need for complex feature-extraction pipelines during localization.
- **Probabilistic reasoning:** Maps can encode uncertainty in occupied vs. free space.
- **Robustness to occlusion:** Partial sensor readings can still produce meaningful matches.
- **Computational regularity:** Grid-based sensorial maps enable fast Fourier-domain correlation techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key constants / standards in sensorial mapping:**
> - Typical grid resolution: **5 cm to 30 cm per cell** for indoor service robots
> - Typical LIDAR angular resolution: **0.25° to 1°**
> - Probabilistic update uses the **log-odds representation** for numerical stability
> - Bayesian inference is **logarithmically additive** in this representation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Localization Problem in a Bayesian Framework

Global localization is solved by recursively estimating the belief over the robot's pose. The recursion is governed by two fundamental equations:

### (A) The Prediction Step (Motion Update)

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Why this matters:** The motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ propagates the previous belief through the kinematic/odometric model $u_t$. Without an initial estimate (global localization), $bel(x_0)$ is initialized as a **uniform distribution** over the entire reachable state space.

### (B) The Correction Step (Measurement Update)

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

**Why this matters:** The sensor model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is where the **sensorial map** $m$ enters. The map allows us to *predict* what the sensor should see at any hypothesized pose $x_t$ and compare that prediction to the actual reading $z_t$.

> [!NOTE]
> $\eta$ is the normalizing constant that ensures $bel(x_t)$ integrates to 1:
> $$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)} = \left[\int p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) \, dx_t\right]^{-1}$$

---

## 2.2 The Probabilistic Sensor Model

For a range-bearing sensor (e.g., LIDAR, sonar), the measurement $z_t^k$ from beam $k$ is a tuple:

$$z_t^k = \begin{Bmatrix} z_t^{k,1} \\ z_t^{k,2} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} d_t^k \\ \phi_t^k \end{Bmatrix}$$

where $d_t^k$ is the measured distance and $\phi_t^k$ is the bearing.

The likelihood of a measurement is a **mixture model** combining four physical phenomena:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \alpha_{hit} \cdot p_{hit} + \alpha_{short} \cdot p_{short} + \alpha_{max} \cdot p_{max} + \alpha_{rand} \cdot p_{rand}$$

| Component | Physical Cause | Typical Weight | Distribution |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|--------------|
| $p_{hit}$ | Correct detection of obstacle | $\alpha_{hit} \approx 0.6$ | Gaussian centered at true range |
| $p_{short}$ | Unexpected object (person, leg of chair) | $\alpha_{short} \approx 0.1$ | Exponential (clutter) |
| $p_{max}$ | Beam hits no obstacle (max range) | $\alpha_{max} \approx 0.1$ | Point mass at $z_{max}$ |
| $p_{rand}$ | Random/unexplained measurement | $\alpha_{rand} \approx 0.2$ | Uniform over $[0, z_{max}]$ |

The **expected measurement** at pose $x_t$ is obtained by **ray-casting** through the map:

$$z_{t}^{k,*} = \min_{r \in [0, z_{max}]} \left\{ r \; \big| \; m(x_t + r \cdot [\cos(\theta_t + \phi^k), \sin(\theta_t + \phi^k)]) = occupied \right\}$$

This ray-cast operation is the **direct interface** between the sensorial map and the sensor model.

---

## 2.3 Occupancy Grid Maps (The Canonical Sensorial Map)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Occupancy Grid Map** divides the environment into a 2D lattice of cells. Each cell $m_i$ stores a binary random variable — *occupied* or *free*. The map is updated via **Bayesian inverse sensor model**.

The cell posterior is updated as:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \left[ 1 + \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} \cdot \frac{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} \right]^{-1}$$

For computational efficiency, the **log-odds representation** is used:

$$\ell_{t,i} = \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$$

which yields a simple additive update:

$$\boxed{\ell_{t,i} = \ell_{t-1,i} + \ell_{inv}(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - \ell_0}$$

where $\ell_0$ is the prior log-odds and $\ell_{inv}$ is the inverse sensor model.

The recovery of probability from log-odds is:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(\ell_{t,i})}$$

---

## 2.4 Map Matching via Scan-to-Map Correlation

For global localization, the **likelihood field model** is often preferred over the beam model because it allows closed-form evaluation:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \max_{k} \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma^2} \exp\left(-\frac{(z_t^k - d_{min}(x_t, m))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

where $d_{min}(x_t, m)$ is the distance from the endpoint of beam $k$ (projected into the map) to the nearest occupied cell.

For **global pose estimation** via correlation, define the cross-correlation between the reference map $M_{ref}$ and the sensor-derived map $M_{sens}$:

$$C(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} M_{ref}(i,j) \cdot M_{sens}(i + \Delta x, j + \Delta y)$$

The estimated displacement is:

$$(\Delta x^*, \Delta y^*) = \arg\max_{\Delta x, \Delta y} C(\Delta x, \Delta y)$$

In the **Fourier domain** (exploiting the convolution theorem):

$$C(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{ \mathcal{F}\{M_{ref}\} \cdot \overline{\mathcal{F}\{M_{sens}\}} \right\}$$

This makes global scan matching **$O(N \log N)$** instead of $O(N^2)$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Purpose | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$ | Bayes filter recursion | Probability distribution over pose |
| 2 | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ | Likelihood of full scan | Assumes beam independence |
| 3 | $p(z_t^k) = \alpha_{hit} p_{hit} + \alpha_{short} p_{short} + \alpha_{max} p_{max} + \alpha_{rand} p_{rand}$ | Beam sensor model | Mixture weights $\sum \alpha = 1$ |
| 4 | $\ell_{t,i} = \ell_{t-1,i} + \ell_{inv}(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - \ell_0$ | Occupancy grid log-odds update | Additive in log-odds |
| 5 | $p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(\ell_{t,i})}$ | Log-odds to probability | Sigmoid transformation |
| 6 | $d_{min}(x, m) = \min_{m_j \in occupied} \sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}$ | Likelihood field distance | Meters; precomputed as distance map |
| 7 | $C(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\mathcal{F}(M_{ref}) \cdot \mathcal{F}(M_{sens})^*\}$ | FFT-based correlation | Pixels/cells |
| 8 | $r_{grid} = \frac{L_x \cdot L_y}{N_{cells}}$ | Grid resolution | meters/cell |
| 9 | $\sigma_{odom} = k_1 \cdot \vert v \vert + k_2 \cdot \vert \omega \vert$ | Odometry noise std-dev | Depends on translational $v$ and rotational $\omega$ |
| 10 | $N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_i)^2}$ | Effective particle count (MCL) | Threshold for resampling: $N_{eff} < N/2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are exact representations from the standard probabilistic robotics framework. No fabricated references.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Sensorial Map Application |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Pre-built HD maps matched to Velodyne LIDAR scans for cm-level localization in GPS-denied tunnels |
| **Warehouse Robots (AMR)** | Grid maps matched to 2D laser scans for global relocalization after battery swap |
| **Underwater Rovers** | Sonar-based occupancy grids for cave exploration where vision fails |
| **Disaster Response** | Thermal-camera sensorial maps matched to floor plans for first-responder navigation |
| **Augmented Reality** | Sparse feature maps matched to camera frames for indoor AR anchoring |

The unifying principle: **any time you need to "find yourself" using a sensor, you need a sensorial map.**

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Occupancy Grid Update

**Goal:** Derive the additive log-odds update rule for an occupancy grid map.

**Starting point — Bayes' rule for cell $m_i$:**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 1 — Marginalize over $m_i$ in the denominator:**

$$p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) = p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) + p(z_t \mid \neg m_i, x_t) \cdot p(\neg m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 2 — Use the shorthand $p_t = p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ and $p_{t-1} = p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$:**

$$p_t = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p_{t-1}}{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p_{t-1} + p(z_t \mid \neg m_i, x_t) \cdot (1 - p_{t-1})}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\frac{(1 - p_{t-1})}{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p_{t-1}}$:**

$$p_t = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{p(z_t \mid \neg m_i, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{1 - p_{t-1}}{p_{t-1}}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the inverse sensor model $p_{inv} = p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ via Bayes:**

$$p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i)}{p(z_t \mid x_t)}$$

Substituting and simplifying (with prior $p(m_i) = 0.5$ gives $\ell_0 = 0$):

**Step 5 — Apply the log-odds transformation $\ell = \log\frac{p}{1-p}$:**

$$\ell_t = \log\frac{p_t}{1-p_t} = \log\frac{p_{t-1}}{1-p_{t-1}} + \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - \log\frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize each term as a log-odds:**

$$\boxed{\ell_t = \ell_{t-1} + \ell_{inv} - \ell_0}$$

This is the **closed-form additive update** for occupancy grids. It is exact — no approximation was used.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Beam-End Likelihood in the Likelihood Field Model

**Goal:** Express $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ in closed form for a range finder using a precomputed **distance map** $D(m)$.

**Step 1 — Project the beam endpoint into world coordinates:**

$$x_{end}^k = x_t + z_t^k \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_t + \phi^k) \\ \sin(\theta_t + \phi^k) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Look up the distance to the nearest occupied cell in the precomputed distance field $D(m)$:**

$$d_{min}^k = D(m)\big|_{x_{end}^k}$$

**Step 3 — Assume a zero-mean Gaussian error model (ignoring failures):**

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_{hit}^2} \exp\left( -\frac{(d_{min}^k)^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — For the full scan, assume beam independence:**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_{hit}^2} \exp\left( -\frac{(d_{min}^k)^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to log-likelihood (for use in log-odds / particle filter weights):**

$$\log p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = -\frac{K}{2}\log(2\pi \sigma_{hit}^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma_{hit}^2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} (d_{min}^k)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *negative* of a sum of squared distances. Maximizing the likelihood is **equivalent to minimizing the sum of squared distances from beam endpoints to nearest occupied cells** — the classic **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** objective.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cross-Correlation via FFT

**Goal:** Show that the global map-matching displacement can be found in $O(N \log N)$.

**Step 1 — Write the 2D cross-correlation in index form:**

$$C(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} M_{ref}(i,j) \cdot M_{sens}(i + \Delta x, j + \Delta y)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize this as a convolution with a flipped kernel:**

$$C = M_{ref} \star M_{sens}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the convolution theorem:**

$$\mathcal{F}\{M_{ref} \star M_{sens}\} = \mathcal{F}\{M_{ref}\} \cdot \mathcal{F}\{M_{sens}\}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore:**

$$C = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{ \mathcal{F}\{M_{ref}\} \cdot \overline{\mathcal{F}\{M_{sens}\}} \right\}$$

**Step 5 — Estimate the displacement from the argmax of the resulting surface:**

$$(\Delta x^*, \Delta y^*) = \arg\max_{\Delta x, \Delta y} C(\Delta x, \Delta y)$$

This formulation assumes both maps are pre-rasterized at the **same resolution**, which is a standard requirement for FFT-based global localization.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sensorial Map Types

| Map Type | Representation | Best Sensor Pairing | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Occupancy Grid** | 2D array of $p(occupied)$ | 2D LIDAR, sonar | Handles uncertainty natively; dense coverage | Memory $O(N^2)$; assumes static env. |
| **Feature Map** | Set of landmarks $(x, y, \sigma)$ | Camera, feature-based LIDAR | Compact; closed-form matching | Fails in featureless corridors |
| **Topological Map** | Graph of nodes & edges | Wheel odometry + place recognition | Human-readable; scales to large env. | No metric precision |
| **Elevation Map** | 2.5D grid $(x, y, h)$ | Stereo camera, 3D LIDAR | Handles uneven terrain | No overhangs / vertical structure |
| **Semantic Map** | Object-labeled grid | RGB-D, neural-net pipeline | High-level reasoning | Computationally heavy |
| **Distance Map** | Precomputed $D(m)$ to obstacles | Any range sensor | $O(1)$ likelihood eval | Static only; recompute cost |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A 1D occupancy grid has 5 cells with current log-odds values $\ell = [-2, 1, 0, -1, 3]$. A single laser beam passes through cells 2 and 3 and updates them with inverse sensor model log-odds $\ell_{inv} = +0.8$ and $\ell_{inv} = -0.5$ respectively. The prior is $\ell_0 = 0$. Find the updated cell probabilities.

**Solution:**

| Cell | Old $\ell$ | $\ell_{inv}$ applied? | New $\ell$ | New $p = \frac{1}{1+e^{-\ell}}$ |
|------|------------|----------------------|------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | $-2$ | No | $-2$ | $0.119$ |
| 2 | $1$ | Yes, $+0.8$ | $1.8$ | $0.858$ |
| 3 | $0$ | Yes, $-0.5$ | $-0.5$ | $0.378$ |
| 4 | $-1$ | No | $-1$ | $0.269$ |
| 5 | $3$ | No | $3$ | $0.953$ |

**Interpretation:**
- Cell 2 (passed-through free space by an occupied beam end → strong evidence of being occupied) goes from 0.731 to 0.858.
- Cell 3 (uncertain) shifts to "more likely free" since the beam traversed it without hitting anything inside it.
- Untouched cells retain their prior log-odds — **independence of grid cells is the foundational assumption**.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Global Localization Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Start Unknown Pose] --> B[Initialize Belief Uniform Over Map]
    B --> C[Acquire Sensor Scan z sub t]
    C --> D[Ray Cast Through Sensorial Map m]
    D --> E[Compute Expected Measurement z exp]
    E --> F[Compare z exp with Actual z sub t]
    F --> G[Compute Likelihood p of z sub t given x sub t and m]
    G --> H[Update Belief bel of x sub t]
    H --> I{Belief Converged?}
    I -- No --> J[Apply Motion Model u sub t]
    J --> C
    I -- Yes --> K[Output Estimated Pose x hat sub t]
```

---

## 4.2 Sensorial Map Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    SM[Sensorial Maps] --> MET[Metric Maps]
    SM --> TOP[Topological Maps]
    SM --> HYB[Hybrid Maps]
    MET --> OCC[Occupancy Grid Map]
    MET --> FEAT[Feature Map]
    MET --> ELEV[Elevation Map 2.5D]
    MET --> DIST[Distance Transform Map]
    TOP --> GRPH[Graph of Places]
    TOP --> VOR[Voronoi Diagram]
    HYB --> TOMO[Topological Metric Overlay]
    HYB --> SEM[Semantic Topological]
```

---

## 4.3 Occupancy Grid Update — Single Beam Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beam k From Pose x sub t] --> B[Ray Marching Through Grid]
    B --> C[Free Cells Increment]
    B --> D[Endpoint Cell Increment]
    C --> E[Log Odds Addition]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Updated Cell Probability]
    F --> G{Probability Exceeds Threshold}
    G -- Yes --> H[Mark Cell Occupied]
    G -- No --> I[Mark Cell Free or Unknown]
```

---

## 4.4 Scan-to-Map Matching Architecture (FFT-based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Map M ref] --> B[FFT of M ref]
    C[Sensor Scan Grid M sens] --> D[FFT of M sens]
    B --> E[Complex Conjugate Multiply]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Inverse FFT]
    F --> G[Correlation Surface C delta x delta y]
    G --> H[Argmax Finder]
    H --> I[Displacement delta x star delta y star]
    I --> J[Pose Update x sub t]
```

---

## 4.5 Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prior Particles Bel x sub t minus 1] --> B[Prediction: Sample from Motion Model]
    B --> C[Particles Representing Propagated Belief]
    C --> D[For Each Particle: Compute Scan Match Score]
    D --> E[Weight by Likelihood p of z sub t given x sub t and m]
    E --> F[Normalized Particle Weights]
    F --> G{N sub eff less than N over 2}
    G -- Yes --> H[Resample with Replacement]
    G -- No --> I[Keep Current Particles]
    H --> J[Posterior Particle Set Bel x sub t]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Convergence Test}
    K -- Yes --> L[Best Particle as Pose Estimate]
    K -- No --> M[Wait for New Scan and Odometry]
    M --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These block diagrams emphasize the **functional flow** rather than physical placement, which is the standard representation expected in KTU theory examination answers. The pipeline (4.1) and particle filter (4.5) are the most frequently asked diagrams in Module 4 of PEBRT522.
